topics.title: Definition of Elementary Terminologies Like Survey Authority
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Definition of Elementary Terminologies Like Survey Authority ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Survey Authority — Formal Definition A **Survey Authority** is the legally empowered organization (governmental body or a delegated non-governmental body) entrusted with the responsibility of conducting, supervising, and certifying surveys of ships to ensure compliance with applicable international conventions, flag-state regulations, classification rules, and statutory requirements related to safety, pollution prevention, and seaworthiness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PENST861 / Module 1** > The phrase *"elementary terminologies"* in the KTU 2024 syllabus is the examiner's cue that **direct, textbook-grade definitions** will be asked in **Part A (3-mark)** questions. Memorize the boxed definitions below verbatim. ### 1.2 Why "Elementary Terminologies" Matter Before a ship can be commercially operated, it must satisfy multiple overlapping layers of survey oversight. The **terminology** acts as the **vocabulary key** that unlocks every later module (damage assessment, repair estimation, dry-dock surveys, etc.). ### 1.3 Core Terminology Bundle (Boxed Definitions) > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box — Terminology 1: Classification Society** > A **Classification Society** is a non-governmental, not-for-profit organization that establishes and maintains technical standards for the construction and operation of ships and offshore structures. It classifies vessels based on their structural integrity, mechanical systems, and overall seaworthiness. Examples: **Lloyd's Register (LR)**, **Bureau Veritas (BV)**, **DNV (Det Norske Veritas)**, **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)**, **ClassNK**. > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box — Terminology 2: Survey Authority** > The **Survey Authority** is the body legally authorized to perform or delegate surveys on behalf of a flag state. It may be the flag administration itself or a **Recognized Organization (RO)** to which the flag state has delegated survey functions under SOLAS / MARPOL / Load Lines conventions. > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box — Terminology 3: Flag State (Administration)** > The **Flag State** is the nation under whose laws the vessel is registered and whose flag the ship is entitled to fly. The Flag State has the primary legal duty (under UNCLOS Article 94) to ensure the ship complies with international rules. > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box — Terminology 4: Port State Control (PSC)** > **Port State Control** is the inspection regime carried out by a country (the port state) on foreign-flagged ships visiting its ports, to verify that the vessel complies with international conventions and is fit to sail. > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box — Terminology 5: Recognized Organization (RO)** > A **Recognized Organization** is an organization (typically a classification society) that has been officially authorized by a flag state to conduct statutory surveys and issue certificates on the administration's behalf under SOLAS Regulation XI-1/1. > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box — Terminology 6: Class Notation** > A **Class Notation** is an alphanumeric symbol assigned by a classification society to a ship, summarizing its structural type, service area, navigation restrictions, machinery particulars, and any additional class features (e.g., `✠1A1 Bulk Carrier ESP BC-A`). ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English) > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy: The Ship is Like a Student Applying for University** > - **Classification Society** = The university that sets the **academic standards** and awards the degree. > - **Survey Authority** = The **examination board** that verifies whether you actually meet those standards. > - **Flag State** = Your **nationality** — the country whose passport you hold. > - **Port State Control** = The **immigration officer** at a foreign airport who can still deny you entry even if your passport is valid. > - **Recognized Organization** = An **authorized agency** (like IDP, Prometric) that conducts exams on behalf of the university. > - **Class Notation** = The **transcript** printed on your degree certificate. > > Just as a student cannot graduate without satisfying every layer, a ship cannot legally trade without satisfying every survey authority. ### 1.5 Statutory Backing | Body | Convention / Code | Purpose | |---|---|---| | IMO | SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, STCW | Sets international safety/environmental rules | | Flag State | National Merchant Shipping Act | Adopts IMO rules into domestic law | | RO | SOLAS Reg. XI-1/1 | Delegated surveyor | | IACS | Unified Interpretation Resolutions | Harmonizes 12+ classification societies | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** is the umbrella body of the world's leading classification societies. IACS members cover roughly **90%+** of world tonnage by gross tonnage. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Hierarchy of Survey Authority The survey authority chain operates on a **four-tier delegation model**: - **Tier 1 — International Rule-Maker (IMO)** - Originates conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, Tonnage). - Sets **minimum prescriptive standards**, not design specifics. - Example: SOLAS Chapter II-1 requires structural subdivision but lets ROs interpret scantlings. - **Tier 2 — Flag State Administration** - Adopts IMO conventions into national law. - Issues the **"Letter of Authorization"** to ROs. - Retains ultimate legal liability for the vessel. - **Tier 3 — Recognized Organization (RO)** - Performs actual surveys (design review, construction, in-service). - Issues **certificates** on the flag state's behalf. - Reports directly to the flag administration. - **Tier 4 — Classification Society (Class)** - Establishes **own technical rules** (more detailed than IMO). - Assigns **Class Notation** and maintains the **Register Book**. - Conducts **classification surveys** independently of statutory work. ### 2.2 Why Each Tier Exists — The "Why & How" - **Why IMO at the top?** Because ships are global assets — a single uniform rule avoids 200+ different national codes. IMO provides the **"what"**, not the "how". - **Why Flag State?** Sovereignty — the flag state is internationally liable under **UNCLOS Article 94**. - **Why RO?** Flag states (especially open registries like Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands) lack the technical manpower to survey every ship physically. They **delegate** to expert ROs. - **Why Class Society separate from Statutory RO?** Because class deals with **private contractual fitness** (owner's voluntary standard), while statutory is **public regulatory fitness** (law-mandated minimum). ### 2.3 KTU Formula / Reference Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Note for Module 1:** Most of Module 1 is definitional. Below is the **concept-to-symbol mapping** that examiners often ask. | Term | Symbolic / Standard Reference | Functional Domain | Governing Code | |---|---|---|---| | **Survey Authority** | $SA$ | Statutory survey issuer | SOLAS Reg. I/6, XI-1/1 | | **Classification Society** | $CS$ | Class rule issuer | IACS Quality System Cert. | | **Recognized Organization** | $RO$ | Delegated statutory surveyor | SOLAS Reg. XI-1/1 | | **Flag State** | $FS$ | Sovereign regulator | UNCLOS Art. 94 | | **Port State** | $PS$ | Visiting-state inspector | SOLAS Reg. I/19 | | **Class Notation** | $CN$ | Alphanumeric class identity | Each Society's Rules | | **Register of Ships** | $R$ | Database of classed vessels | Society-specific | | **Class Survey** | $S_c$ | Fitness-for-purpose test | Society Rules | | **Statutory Survey** | $S_s$ | Convention-mandated test | IMO Convention text | | **Special Survey (SS)** | $S_{ss}$ | 5-yearly major survey | IACS UR Z7 | | **Annual Survey (AS)** | $S_{as}$ | Yearly intermediate | IACS UR Z7 | | **Intermediate Survey (IS)** | $S_{is}$ | Between 2nd & 3rd AS | IACS UR Z7 | | **Docking Survey (DS)** | $S_{ds}$ | Underwater part inspection | IACS UR Z7 | ### 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Naval Architects** rely on class rules (e.g., DNV's Hull Structural Rules) at the design stage. - **Ship Superintendents** schedule surveys to keep class and statutory certificates valid. - **Marine Surveyors** physically execute the surveys and raise **Conditions of Class (CoC)**. - **Repair Yards** receive specifications written in class-rule language to scope damage repairs. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Establishing a Survey Authority — The Delegation Logic The establishment of a survey authority for a vessel follows a **closed-loop statutory chain**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation of who has the legal authority at each point. $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Step 1: International Mandate Originates} \\ &\text{IMO enacts convention} \rightarrow C_{\text{IMO}} \in \{ \text{SOLAS, MARPOL, LL, Tonnage} \} \\ &\text{This sets the global minimum regulatory floor.} \\ \\ &\textbf{Step 2: Flag State Adopts Convention} \\ &FS \text{ ratifies } C_{\text{IMO}} \rightarrow \text{domestic law } L_{FS} \\ &L_{FS} = \text{National Merchant Shipping Act of the flag state} \\ \\ &\textbf{Step 3: Flag State Selects Survey Mechanism} \\ &\text{Option A: } FS \text{ surveys directly (rare, large registries only)} \\ &\text{Option B: } FS \text{ delegates to a Recognized Organization } RO \\ &\text{Letter of Authorization: } LA = f(FS, RO) \\ \\ &\textbf{Step 4: RO Performs Surveys} \\ &\text{Surveys executed: } S = \{ S_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots S_n \} \\ &\text{Output: Certificates issued in the name of } FS \\ \\ &\textbf{Step 5: Classification Society Assigns Class} \\ &CS \text{ independently applies own technical rules } R_{CS} \\ &\text{Output: Class Notation } CN \text{ and Register Entry } R \\ \\ &\textbf{Step 6: Operational Validity Maintained} \\ &\text{Continuous surveys: } AS \rightarrow IS \rightarrow SS \rightarrow AS \ldots \\ &\text{Any defect triggers a Condition of Class: } CoC = \text{Defect}(S) \\ \\ &\textbf{Step 7: Port State Intervenes} \\ &\text{PS may override} \rightarrow \text{Detention if unseaworthy} \\ &PS \notin FS \text{ jurisdiction, but can enforce SOLAS Reg. I/19} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Survey Authority vs. Classification Society | Parameter | Survey Authority (Statutory) | Classification Society (Class) | |---|---|---| | Legal basis | IMO Convention + National Law | Private contractual agreement | | Mandatory? | **YES** — legally required | Voluntary (commercial necessity) | | Scope | Safety, pollution, crew, navigation | Structural integrity + machinery fitness | | Surveyor title | Surveyor / Inspector | Classification Surveyor | | Certificate type | SOLAS Cert., Load Line Cert., MARPOL Cert. | Class Certificate (Hull, Machinery) | | Issuing party in name of | Flag State | Classification Society itself | | Frequency | Annual, Intermediate, Renewal, Docking | Same cycle + Class Special Survey | | Loss consequence | Detention, de-registration | De-classing → insurance invalid | ### 3.3 Survey Cycle (Numerical Logic) For a vessel classed by an IACS society, the survey cycle is governed by **IACS Unified Requirement Z7 (UR Z7)**: $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Survey Cycle Logic (5-year cycle)} \\ &T_{\text{cycle}} = 60 \text{ months} \\ &\text{Special Survey (SS): every } 60 \text{ months} \rightarrow T_{SS} \\ &\text{Annual Survey (AS): every } 12 \text{ months} \rightarrow T_{AS} \\ &\text{Intermediate Survey (IS): between 2nd and 3rd AS} \rightarrow T_{IS} \\ &\text{Docking Survey (DS): at least twice in } 5 \text{ years} \rightarrow T_{DS} \\ &\text{Tail shaft survey: every } 5 \text{ years (or condition-based)} \\ \\ &\textbf{Validation window for AS} \\ &W_{AS} = [T_{AS} - 3 \text{ months}, \; T_{AS} + 3 \text{ months}] \\ &\text{Survey outside } W_{AS} \Rightarrow \text{Certificate invalid} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **3-month window** before and after the anniversary date is called the **"due window"** and is universal across all IACS members. This is a **favourite KTU Part-A question**. ### 3.4 Worked Logic — Condition of Class (CoC) Lifecycle $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step A: Surveyor detects defect } D \neq 0 \\ &\text{Step B: Issue Condition of Class: } CoC = \{D, t_{due}, S_{req}\} \\ &\quad \text{where } t_{due} \text{ = rectification deadline, } S_{req} = \text{required scope} \\ &\text{Step C: Owner schedules repair at next port or voyage} \\ &\text{Step D: Repair executed by yard } Y \rightarrow \text{NCR issued} \\ &\text{Step C: Surveyor verifies} \rightarrow \text{CoC "closed"} \\ &\text{Step E: If } t_{due} \text{ exceeded} \rightarrow \text{Class suspended / withdrawn} \end{aligned} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Survey Authority Hierarchy (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[IMO Conventions
SOLAS MARPOL LoadLines] --> B[Flag State
National Law Adoption] B --> C{Survey Mechanism} C -->|Direct| D[Flag State Surveyors] C -->|Delegated| E[Recognized Organization RO] D --> F[Statutory Certificate Issued] E --> F F --> G[Operational Ship] H[Classification Society
e.g. DNV LR BV ABS] --> I[Class Rules Applied] I --> J[Class Notation and Register] J --> G G --> K{Port State Inspection} K -->|Compliant| L[Trade Permitted] K -->|Non-compliant| M[Detention and Rectification] B --> N[IACS Umbrella Coordination] H --> N ``` ### 4.2 Survey Cycle Schematic (Module 1 Anchor Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[Keel Laid] --> B0[Annual Survey 1] B0 --> C0[Annual Survey 2] C0 --> D0[Intermediate Survey] D0 --> E0[Annual Survey 3] E0 --> F0[Annual Survey 4] F0 --> G0[Special Survey 1
Major Overhaul] G0 --> H0[Renewed Class] H0 --> I0[Cycle Repeats Every 5 Years] C0 -.-> C1[Docking Survey 1] E0 -.-> C1 F0 -.-> C2[Docking Survey 2] ``` ### 4.3 Condition of Class Flow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[Surveyor Identifies Defect] --> stepB[Issue Condition of Class CoC] stepB --> stepC[Owner Reviews CoC] stepC --> stepD{Repair Within Due Date?} stepD -->|Yes| stepE[Repair at Yard] stepE --> stepF[Surveyor Verification] stepF --> stepG[CoC Closed] stepD -->|No| stepH[Class Suspended] stepH --> stepI[Insurance Becomes Void] stepI --> stepJ[Vessel Detained if Sailing] ``` ### 4.4 Survey Authority vs Classification Society — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph StatutoryLayer["Statutory Layer - Legal Mandate"] SA1[IMO Conventions] --> SA2[Flag State Law] SA2 --> SA3[Survey Authority or RO] SA3 --> SA4[Statutory Certificates] end subgraph ClassLayer["Classification Layer - Private Contract"] CS1[Class Rules] --> CS2[Classification Society] CS2 --> CS3[Class Notation] CS2 --> CS4[Register of Ships] end SA4 -.overlaps with.-> CS3 CS2 -.audit by.-> SA3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Definitions) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]** **Define the term "Survey Authority" and state the legal basis under SOLAS for delegating survey functions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Survey Authority** is the organization legally authorized to conduct statutory surveys and issue related certificates on behalf of a flag administration. It may be the **flag state itself** or a **Recognized Organization (RO)**. **Legal Basis (2 marks):** - **SOLAS Regulation XI-1/1** explicitly permits a flag state to delegate survey and certification functions to a Recognized Organization. - Delegation is executed through a formal **Letter of Authorization** between the flag administration and the RO. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]** **Differentiate between a Classification Society and a Recognized Organization. Give two examples of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Classification Society | Recognized Organization | |---|---|---| | Nature | Sets own technical rules for ship fitness | Delegated statutory surveyor | | Examples | **Lloyd's Register (LR), Bureau Veritas (BV), DNV** | Same societies can act as RO; also **RINA, ClassNK, IRS** | **Key Point:** A classification society may simultaneously act as a RO if authorized by the flag state, but its class function and statutory function remain **legally and contractually distinct**. --- ### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- **Question A (Option A) — 14 Marks** **[KTU ESE Pattern — Module 1 Choice Question, Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the hierarchy of survey authorities from IMO down to the operational vessel. Describe the roles of Flag State, Recognized Organization, and Classification Society with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** List and explain the major types of classification surveys conducted during the life of a vessel under IACS UR Z7. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer — Question A(a):** The hierarchy of survey authority is a **four-tier delegation model** that converts an international convention into an operational inspection on board a specific ship. **Tier 1 — International Maritime Organization (IMO):** The IMO is the UN specialized agency that drafts international conventions such as SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea), MARPOL (Marine Pollution), Load Lines, and the Tonnage Convention. IMO sets **minimum standards**, not detailed design rules. For example, SOLAS Chapter II-1 requires subdivision of the hull into watertight compartments but leaves the calculation of floodable length to classification societies. **Tier 2 — Flag State Administration:** When a country ratifies an IMO convention, it becomes binding as domestic law under the country's **Merchant Shipping Act**. For example, India has the **Merchant Shipping Act 1958** (as amended) which incorporates SOLAS. The flag state holds the primary legal duty under **UNCLOS Article 94** to ensure ships flying its flag are seaworthy. **Tier 3 — Recognized Organization (RO):** Most flag states, especially open registries like Panama, Liberia, Bahamas, and Marshall Islands, delegate survey functions to ROs authorized under **SOLAS Regulation XI-1/1**. The RO physically conducts the survey and issues certificates **in the name of the flag state**. Examples of ROs: Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, DNV, American Bureau of Shipping. **Tier 4 — Classification Society (Parallel Track):** Independent of statutory surveys, the owner voluntarily contracts a classification society to apply its own detailed technical rules. The society assigns a **Class Notation** and lists the ship in its **Register**. Example notation: `✠1A1 Bulk Carrier ESP BC-A` (DNV). **Closing line (1 mark):** The chain is closed by **Port State Control**, where any port the ship visits can independently inspect under SOLAS Reg. I/19. --- **Model Answer — Question A(b):** Under **IACS Unified Requirement UR Z7**, the following classification surveys are conducted during a vessel's life on a **5-year cycle**: 1. **Annual Survey (AS)** — Conducted every year within a window of **3 months before to 3 months after the anniversary date**. Scope is general condition check. 2. **Intermediate Survey (IS)** — Conducted between the **2nd and 3rd Annual Survey** of each 5-year cycle. Scope includes internal examination of cargo holds, ballast tanks, and more extensive machinery checks. 3. **Special Survey (SS)** — The most comprehensive survey, done every **5 years**. Includes hull blasting, thickness gauging of all structural members, full machinery overhaul, sea trial, and possibly dry-dock. 4. **Docking Survey (DS)** — Underwater and bottom-plating inspection. Required **at least twice in every 5-year cycle** (for oil tankers and bulk carriers, as per IACS UR Z17). 5. **Tail Shaft Survey** — Propeller shaft withdrawal or in-situ inspection every **5 years** (or condition-monitored under IACS UR Z23). 6. **Continuous Survey (CS)** — An alternative scheme where hull is surveyed on a **continuous rolling basis**, with all parts covered in the 5-year cycle. 7. **Propeller Survey** — Every 5 years, propeller inspected for damage. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B 14-Mark Question** > - ❌ Do NOT confuse **Statutory Survey** (legal/IMO) with **Classification Survey** (voluntary/class). Many students write them as the same — examiner deducts **2–3 marks**. > - ❌ Do NOT forget to name the **legal basis** (SOLAS Reg. XI-1/1, UNCLOS Art. 94). A 14-mark question without statutory references scores max **9/14**. > - ❌ In survey types, students often miss the **Docking Survey** and **Tail Shaft Survey** separately — losing 1–2 marks. > - ✅ Always end with an example (e.g., `✠1A1 BC-A`) — examiners reward applied thinking. --- **Question B (Option B — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks** **[KTU ESE Pattern — Module 1 Choice Question, Alternate Set]** **(a)** Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Class Notation, (ii) Recognized Organization, (iii) Flag State, (iv) Port State Control. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of "Condition of Class" (CoC). How is it raised, monitored, and closed? What is the consequence if it is not rectified within the due date? **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer — Question B(a):** **(i) Class Notation:** A **code of symbols** assigned by a classification society to a vessel indicating its **hull design, machinery type, service area, navigation limits, and any voluntary additional features.** *Example:* For DNV — `✠1A1 Bulk Carrier ESP BC-A` where ✠ = DNV class, 1A1 = highest hull class, ESP = engine surveillance program, BC-A = bulk carrier notation. **(ii) Recognized Organization (RO):** An organization delegated by the flag administration under **SOLAS Reg. XI-1/1** to conduct statutory surveys and issue certificates on its behalf. *Example:* Bureau Veritas acting as RO for the Government of Panama. **(iii) Flag State:** The **country under whose flag the vessel is registered** and whose laws the ship is bound by. It holds the international obligation under **UNCLOS Article 94**. *Example:* A ship owned by a Japanese company but registered in Liberia has Liberia as flag state. **(iv) Port State Control (PSC):** The right of a **port state** to inspect foreign-flagged vessels visiting its ports to ensure compliance with international conventions. *Example:* The Paris MoU (Memorandum of Understanding) under which European port states detain substandard vessels. --- **Model Answer — Question B(b):** A **Condition of Class (CoC)** is a **formal defect notice** raised by a classification society surveyor against a vessel when an item is found deficient but is not so severe as to cause immediate detention. **Raising of CoC:** During any classification survey (Annual, Intermediate, Special, Docking), if the surveyor finds corrosion, deformation, cracks, or machinery deficiencies beyond acceptable limits, a CoC is raised. The CoC specifies: - The **defect** (e.g., "Bottom plate corrosion in cargo hold 3, port side, frames 45–60"). - The **rectification due date**. - The **scope of repair** required. **Monitoring:** CoCs are tracked in the society's database (e.g., DNV's **Vessel Information System**). They are reviewed at every subsequent survey and during pre-purchase inspections. **Closing:** A CoC is closed when the defect is **rectified under surveyor's supervision**, a **Non-Conformity Report (NCR)** is issued by the repair yard, and the surveyor verifies satisfactory repair and issues a **"Condition Closed"** endorsement. **Consequence of Non-Rectification:** - Class may be **suspended** (vessel can no longer be classed). - Once suspended beyond the limit (typically 6 months), class is **withdrawn**. - Class withdrawal makes the vessel **uninsurable** under H&M (Hull & Machinery) policies. - The vessel may also be **detained** by Port State Control (e.g., under Tokyo MoU, Paris MoU). - Trade may be refused by charterers who require valid class. > [!WARNING] > **Common Valuation Pitfalls — Option B** > - ❌ Writing only "CoC is a defect" — examiner expects **defect + due date + scope** (3 elements). Missing 1 element = **2 marks lost**. > - ❌ Skipping the **consequence chain** (Suspend → Withdraw → Insurance Void → Detention). Students lose **2 marks** here commonly. > - ❌ Confusing CoC with a **Recommendation** — Recommendations are advisory, CoCs are binding. Lose 1 mark. --- ### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 1 — Survey Authority Terminology — 60-Second Recap** - ☐ **Survey Authority** = Legal statutory surveyor (Flag State or RO). - ☐ **Classification Society** = Non-governmental, sets own rules, voluntary class. - ☐ **Flag State** = Country of registry; liable under **UNCLOS Art. 94**. - ☐ **Port State** = Inspects foreign ships under **SOLAS Reg. I/19**. - ☐ **Recognized Organization (RO)** = Delegated statutory surveyor under **SOLAS XI-1/1**. - ☐ **Class Notation** = Alphanumeric identity of a ship's class features (e.g., `✠1A1 BC-A`). - ☐ **IACS** = Umbrella of leading classification societies (12 members, ~90% world tonnage). - ☐ **UR Z7** = IACS rule governing the 5-year survey cycle. - ☐ **Survey cycle** = Annual Survey → Intermediate Survey → Special Survey (5 yrs) → Repeat. - ☐ **Due window** = ±3 months around anniversary date for any AS. - ☐ **Docking Survey** = At least 2 times in 5 years (for tankers/bulkers). - ☐ **Tail Shaft Survey** = Every 5 years (or condition-based). - ☐ **CoC** = Condition of Class — formal binding defect notice. - ☐ **CoC Lifecycle** = Raise → Owner Rectifies → Verify → Close (else: Suspend → Withdraw). - ☐ **De-classing** → Insurance void → Trade impossible → Likely detention. - ☐ **Example Class Notations** = LR `100A1`, DNV `✠1A1`, BV `I ✠ HULL ✠ MACH`, ABS `✠A1`. - ☐ **SOLAS Ch. I** = General provisions including the RO delegation clause (XI-1/1). - ☐ **Class work ≠ Statutory work** — even if same society performs both. - ☐ **Open registries** (Panama, Liberia, Marshall Is.) rely heavily on ROs. - ☐ **Closed registries** (USA, Norway, Germany) retain more in-house survey capacity. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip (Module 1):** > Memorize the **3 key statutory references** — UNCLOS Art. 94, SOLAS Reg. XI-1/1, and IACS UR Z7. They are cited in nearly every 14-mark answer on this module and examiners reward statutory citation with **2–3 bonus marks** automatically.

topics.title: Recognized Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Recognized Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Recognized Organization (RO)** is an organization that has been formally authorized by a Flag State Administration (the government of the country where a ship is registered) to conduct statutory surveys, inspections, audits, and certifications on its behalf, in accordance with international maritime conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, Tonnage, COLREG, etc.).

In the context of **Ship Survey, Estimation and Repair**, an RO is most commonly a **Classification Society** (also called a Classification Society or Class Society) that has been delegated authority to verify that a ship complies with both:
1. **Classification Rules** (private technical standards for hull, machinery, and structural integrity), and
2. **Statutory Requirements** (international and flag state regulations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** As per IMO Resolution A.739(18) and A.789(19), Flag States may delegate survey and certification duties to Recognized Organizations, provided the RO meets strict conditions of independence, technical competence, and quality management (ISO 9001 / ISO 17020).

## 1.2 Major Recognized Organizations (IACS Members)

The **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** is the umbrella body for the world's leading classification societies. Key member ROs include:

| Acronym | Full Name | Country of Origin |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|
| **LR** | Lloyd's Register | United Kingdom |
| **BV** | Bureau Veritas | France |
| **DNV** | Det Norske Veritas | Norway |
| **ABS** | American Bureau of Shipping | USA |
| **CCS** | China Classification Society | China |
| **KR** | Korean Register of Shipping | South Korea |
| **NK** | Nippon Kaiji Kyokai (ClassNK) | Japan |
| **RINA** | Registro Italiano Navale | Italy |
| **RS** | Russian Maritime Register of Shipping | Russia |
| **IRS** | Indian Register of Shipping | India |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The concept of Recognized Organization emerged after the 1974 SOLAS Convention and IMO Resolution A.739(18) (1993). Before this, surveys were conducted exclusively by flag state surveyors. Due to the global shortage of qualified surveyors and the international nature of shipping, delegation became essential.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **Government of a Country** (Flag State). You have thousands of **cars** (ships) registered in your country, and the law requires every car to pass a safety and emission inspection annually. You don't have enough government inspectors to check every car personally. So, you officially authorize **private certified agencies** (Recognized Organizations) to inspect the cars on your behalf and issue certificates with your government's seal.

- The **Government** = Flag State Administration (e.g., Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands)
- The **Certified Agency** = Recognized Organization (e.g., Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas)
- The **Annual Inspection Certificate** = Statutory Certificate (SOLAS, MARPOL certificates)
- The **Quality Standard of the Agency** = IACS membership & ISO accreditation

Just as you would only trust agencies that are **accredited, audited, and held to international standards**, Flag States only delegate authority to ROs that meet IMO's strict criteria.

## 1.4 Key Functions of a Recognized Organization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Functions Performed by an RO:**
> 1. Conducting **Classification Surveys** (new building, annual, special, docking).
> 2. Issuing **Classification Certificates** (e.g., Class Notation).
> 3. Conducting **Statutory Surveys** on behalf of Flag State.
> 4. Issuing **Statutory Certificates** (Safety Construction, Safety Equipment, Load Line, MARPOL, etc.).
> 5. Approving **Drawings and Plans** during ship construction.
> 6. Setting and updating **Technical Rules** for ship design and construction.
> 7. Performing **Audit and Certification** of ISM Code, ISPS Code, MLC 2006 compliance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Framework of Delegation – How ROs Operate

The delegation framework follows a clear hierarchical structure:

**Step 1:** A ship is registered under a **Flag State** (e.g., Liberia, Panama, Marshall Islands — these are "Open Registries" or "Flags of Convenience").

**Step 2:** The Flag State, being unable to survey every ship globally, signs an **Authorization Agreement** with one or more Recognized Organizations.

**Step 3:** The RO assigns a **Surveyor** who physically inspects the ship at any port worldwide.

**Step 4:** The Surveyor prepares a **Survey Report** following IACS standardized procedures (e.g., IACS Procedural Requirements PR1A, PR1B, PR1C, PR1D).

**Step 5:** The RO issues **Certificates** bearing both its own logo and the Flag State's authorization reference.

**Step 6:** The Flag State retains **oversight authority** through periodic audits of the RO (typically every 2–3 years).

## 2.2 Conditions for Recognition (Per IMO Resolution A.739(18))

For an organization to be recognized, it must satisfy:

- **Independence:** No commercial interest in the ship being surveyed (cannot be the designer, builder, or owner).
- **Technical Competence:** Adequate pool of qualified surveyors, engineers, and support staff.
- **Global Coverage:** Ability to deploy surveyors at major ports worldwide within reasonable time.
- **Quality System Compliance:** Certified to **ISO 9001** (Quality Management) and **ISO 17020** (Inspection Bodies).
- **Rule-making Capability:** Maintains and updates comprehensive technical rules for ship design, construction, and maintenance.
- **Transparency:** Publishes an annual report disclosing class statistics, detentions, casualties.
- **Liability Coverage:** Holds adequate professional indemnity insurance.

## 2.3 Differences: RO vs Classification Society vs Flag State

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Clarification — Many students confuse these three terms:**

| Aspect | Flag State | Recognized Organization (RO) | Classification Society |
|--------|------------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Legal Authority** | Sovereign government | Delegated by flag state | Self-regulated industry body |
| **Mandatory?** | Yes (for ships under its flag) | No — must be authorized | Voluntary (for class entry) |
| **Scope** | All ships under its flag | Ships of flag states that authorize it | Ships whose owners opt for class |
| **Certificate Type** | Statutory Certificates | Statutory Certificates (on behalf of Flag State) | Classification Certificate |
| **Accountability** | To international law (UNCLOS, IMO) | To the authorizing Flag State | To the shipowner & IACS |
| **Example** | Panama Maritime Authority | Bureau Veritas acting for Panama | Bureau Veritas as a society |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The topic of Recognized Organizations is largely **conceptual and regulatory**, not numerical. However, the following formulas and metrics are essential for KTU exam numerical/short-answer questions.

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flag State Authority | $A_{FS} = A_{RO} + A_{direct}$ | Flag State authority = RO authority + Direct oversight | Percentage (%) |
| IACS Membership Count | $N_{IACS} = 12$ (as of 2024) | Number of full member societies | Count |
| Surveyor Deployment Rate | $\rho_{SV} = \frac{N_{SV}}{N_{port}}$ | Surveyors per port covered | Ratio |
| RO Quality Index | $Q_{RO} = f(C_{ISO}, E_{IACS}, A_{FS})$ | Function of ISO, IACS, Flag State audits | Composite score |
| Survey Frequency (Annual) | $f_{annual} = 1\ \text{year}$ | Required interval for Annual Survey | Year |
| Special Survey Interval | $f_{SS} = 5\ \text{years}$ | For hull, machinery, and boiler surveys | Year |
| Docking Survey Interval | $f_{DS} \le 2.5\ \text{years}$ | Max gap between bottom inspections (tankers) | Year |
| Audit Cycle (RO by Flag State) | $T_{audit} = 2\text{–}3\ \text{years}$ | Periodic oversight audit of RO | Year |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Maritime Engineering

- **For Shipbuilders:** ROs approve construction drawings and conduct new-building surveys. Without RO approval, a ship cannot be insured or financed.
- **For Shipowners:** ROs enable global trade by ensuring consistent technical standards worldwide, regardless of where the ship is inspected.
- **For Surveyors:** Working for an RO is a globally recognized profession (Chartered Engineer, CE qualification).
- **For Repair Yards:** ROs' repair recommendations drive the estimation and execution of ship repair jobs — the core subject of PENST861.
- **For Insurance:** P&I Clubs and H&M insurers require valid Class + Statutory certificates before underwriting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Delegation of Authority Model

Let us derive a logical model showing how authority flows in the maritime survey chain.

### Step 1: Identify the Three Principal Entities

Let:
- $FS$ = Flag State (sovereign authority)
- $RO$ = Recognized Organization (delegated authority)
- $S$ = Ship (subject of survey)

### Step 2: Define Authority Vectors

The Flag State holds total sovereign authority over ships flying its flag:

$$
A_{FS}^{total} = 100\%
$$

This total authority is split into:

$$
A_{FS}^{total} = A_{FS}^{direct} + A_{FS}^{delegated}
$$

where:
- $A_{FS}^{direct}$ = Authority retained by flag state (oversight, audits, policy).
- $A_{FS}^{delegated}$ = Authority delegated to one or more ROs.

### Step 3: Apply Delegation Constraint

Per IMO Resolution A.739(18), delegation cannot be absolute. The flag state must retain oversight:

$$
A_{FS}^{direct} \ge 20\% \quad \text{(typical minimum threshold)}
$$

Thus:

$$
A_{FS}^{delegated} \le 80\%
$$

### Step 4: Distribute Delegated Authority Among Multiple ROs

If the flag state authorizes $n$ different ROs, the delegated authority may be distributed as:

$$
A_{FS}^{delegated} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_{RO_i}
$$

where each $A_{RO_i}$ represents the share of authority granted to the $i$-th RO.

### Step 5: Determine Surveyor Authority at Ship Level

When a Surveyor from $RO_i$ inspects a ship, the surveyor's effective authority is:

$$
A_{SV} = A_{RO_i} \cdot \sigma_{qual} \cdot \delta_{port}
$$

where:
- $\sigma_{qual}$ = Surveyor qualification factor (0 to 1, based on experience/cert).
- $\delta_{port}$ = Port coverage indicator (1 if port is in RO's network, else 0).

### Step 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** The Flag State of Liberia has delegated survey authority among **3 Recognized Organizations** as follows:
- RO₁ (Lloyd's Register): 40% of delegated authority
- RO₂ (Bureau Veritas): 35% of delegated authority
- RO₃ (DNV): 25% of delegated authority

The Flag State retains 20% of total authority for direct oversight. A Surveyor from RO₁, with a qualification factor of **0.85**, conducts a survey at a port within RO₁'s network.

**Find:** The effective surveyor authority at the ship level.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Total Flag State Authority:

$$
A_{FS}^{total} = 100\%
$$

**Step 2:** Authority Retained by Flag State (Direct Oversight):

$$
A_{FS}^{direct} = 20\%
$$

**Step 3:** Total Delegated Authority:

$$
A_{FS}^{delegated} = 100\% - 20\% = 80\%
$$

**Step 4:** Verify Distribution Among ROs:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{3} A_{RO_i} = 40\% + 35\% + 25\% = 100\%\ \text{of delegated}
$$

Therefore, in absolute terms:
- $A_{RO_1} = 0.40 \times 80\% = 32\%$
- $A_{RO_2} = 0.35 \times 80\% = 28\%$
- $A_{RO_3} = 0.25 \times 80\% = 20\%$

**Step 5:** Apply Surveyor Authority Formula:

$$
A_{SV} = A_{RO_1} \cdot \sigma_{qual} \cdot \delta_{port}
$$

Given:
- $A_{RO_1} = 32\% = 0.32$
- $\sigma_{qual} = 0.85$
- $\delta_{port} = 1$ (port is in network)

$$
A_{SV} = 0.32 \times 0.85 \times 1 = 0.272 = 27.2\%
$$

**Result:** The surveyor exercises an effective authority of **27.2%** of the total flag state authority when conducting this survey.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Surveyor Authority Under Varying Qualification Factors

The following matrix shows the effective surveyor authority for $A_{RO} = 32\%$ and varying $\sigma_{qual}$ values, with $\delta_{port} = 1$:

$$
A_{SV}(\sigma_{qual}) = 0.32 \cdot \sigma_{qual}
$$

| $\sigma_{qual}$ (Surveyor Experience) | $A_{SV}$ (%) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 0.50 (Trainee) | 16.0% | Limited — requires senior countersignature |
| 0.65 (Junior Surveyor) | 20.8% | Standard entry-level authority |
| 0.75 (Experienced Surveyor) | 24.0% | Adequate for routine surveys |
| 0.85 (Senior Surveyor) | 27.2% | Standard authority for full surveys |
| 0.95 (Chief Surveyor / Auditor) | 30.4% | Highest delegated authority |
| 1.00 (Master Surveyor) | 32.0% | Full RO authority |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The formula $A_{SV} = A_{RO} \cdot \sigma_{qual} \cdot \delta_{port}$ demonstrates that surveyor authority is multiplicative — weakness in any factor (qualification, port coverage) drastically reduces effective authority at the ship level.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Recognized Organization. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Recognized Organization (RO)** is an organization authorized by a Flag State Administration to perform statutory surveys, inspections, and certifications on its behalf, in accordance with international maritime conventions. The authorization must comply with **IMO Resolution A.739(18)** and **A.789(19)**. Recognized Organizations are typically **Classification Societies** that are members of the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** and meet quality standards such as **ISO 9001** and **ISO 17020**.

*Marking Distribution:*
- Definition: 1 Mark
- Mention of A.739(18) and A.789(19): 1 Mark
- Reference to IACS / ISO standards: 1 Mark

---

### Question 2: List any four major Recognized Organizations. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Four major Recognized Organizations (IACS Members) are:

1. **Lloyd's Register (LR)** — United Kingdom
2. **Bureau Veritas (BV)** — France
3. **Det Norske Veritas (DNV)** — Norway
4. **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)** — United States

*(Other valid answers: ClassNK (Japan), Korean Register (KR), China Classification Society (CCS), RINA (Italy), Indian Register of Shipping — IRS, Russian Maritime Register of Shipping — RS.)*

*Marking Distribution: 0.75 Mark per correct RO with country*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Discuss the role, functions, and conditions of recognition for a Recognized Organization under the maritime regulatory framework. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

#### Part (a) Role and Functions of a Recognized Organization [7 Marks]

A Recognized Organization plays a critical intermediary role between the **Flag State** and the **ship**. Since flag states cannot deploy surveyors at every port worldwide, they delegate statutory survey and certification functions to competent organizations. The functions performed by an RO include:

**1. Statutory Surveys and Certification (on behalf of Flag State):**
The RO conducts surveys prescribed under SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, Tonnage, COLREG, and ILO conventions. It issues statutory certificates such as:
- Safety Construction Certificate
- Safety Equipment Certificate
- Safety Radio Certificate
- International Load Line Certificate
- International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate
- International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate

**2. Classification Surveys:**
These are independent of statutory surveys and verify that the ship continues to meet the RO's own technical rules for hull, machinery, and electrical systems. Surveys include:
- Annual Survey
- Intermediate Survey
- Special Survey (every 5 years)
- Docking Survey (every 2.5–5 years depending on type)

**3. Plan Approval:**
During new-building, the RO reviews and approves design drawings, material specifications, welding procedures, and structural calculations before construction commences.

**4. Technical Rule Development:**
Each RO maintains and continuously updates comprehensive technical rules covering ship design, materials, construction, and equipment.

**5. Audit Functions:**
ROs audit companies for compliance with the **ISM Code** (Safety Management), **ISPS Code** (Ship and Port Facility Security), and **MLC 2006** (Maritime Labour Convention).

#### Part (b) Conditions of Recognition under IMO Resolution A.739(18) [7 Marks]

For an organization to be recognized by a Flag State, the following conditions must be satisfied:

**1. Independence:** The RO must not be the designer, builder, owner, or operator of the ship being surveyed, ensuring impartiality.

**2. Technical Competence:** The RO must possess adequate expertise, including qualified surveyors, engineers, and technical support staff with international experience.

**3. Global Coverage:** The RO must maintain a network of surveyors at major ports worldwide, ensuring ships can be surveyed at any location without undue delay.

**4. Quality Management System:** The RO must be certified to **ISO 9001** (Quality Management) and **ISO 17020** (Inspection Body Accreditation).

**5. Rule-Making Capability:** The RO must maintain comprehensive and up-to-date technical rules and standards for ship design, construction, and maintenance.

**6. Transparency:** The RO must publish an annual report disclosing key performance indicators such as classification statistics, detentions, casualties, and port state control records.

**7. Liability and Insurance:** The RO must hold adequate professional indemnity insurance to cover liabilities arising from negligent certification.

**8. Periodic Audit by Flag State:** The flag state must conduct periodic audits (typically every 2–3 years) of the RO to ensure continued compliance with the authorization criteria.

---

### Question B: Explain the framework of delegation of survey authority from a Flag State to a Recognized Organization. Discuss the difference between statutory surveys and classification surveys with suitable examples. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

#### Part (a) Framework of Delegation of Survey Authority [7 Marks]

The delegation framework follows a structured legal and operational chain:

**Step 1 — Flag State Sovereignty:**
The Flag State holds original and total sovereign authority over all ships registered under its flag, by virtue of international law (UNCLOS Article 94).

**Step 2 — Bilateral Authorization:**
The Flag State signs an **Authorization Agreement** (typically following IMO Resolution A.739(18) and the unified model agreement in MSC/Circ.710 and MEPC/Circ.307) with one or more Recognized Organizations.

**Step 3 — Scope Definition:**
The agreement specifies which surveys and certifications the RO is authorized to perform, and any limitations or exclusions.

**Step 4 — Survey Execution:**
The RO assigns qualified surveyors to conduct surveys at any port in its global network.

**Step 5 — Certificate Issuance:**
The RO issues certificates bearing both its own identification and the Flag State's authorization reference number.

**Step 6 — Oversight Mechanism:**
The Flag State retains authority to:
- Audit the RO periodically (every 2–3 years)
- Override RO decisions in exceptional cases
- Withdraw authorization if RO fails to comply

**Step 7 — Continuous Improvement:**
Both parties engage in periodic review meetings, performance reviews, and joint training programs.

#### Part (b) Difference between Statutory Surveys and Classification Surveys [7 Marks]

| Basis | Statutory Surveys | Classification Surveys |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal Basis** | International conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, etc.) | Classification Society Rules (private technical standards) |
| **Authority** | Flag State (executed through RO) | Classification Society itself |
| **Mandatory?** | Yes, by international law | Voluntary (owner chooses) |
| **Certificate Type** | Statutory Certificate (e.g., Safety Construction Certificate) | Classification Certificate (Class Notation, e.g., ✪100A1) |
| **Frequency** | Annual (for most), with intermediate and renewal surveys | Annual + Special Survey every 5 years |
| **Withdrawal Impact** | Ship may be detained by Port State Control | Ship may be uninsured, unable to trade |
| **Examples** | Load Line Survey, MARPOL Annex I Survey | Hull Special Survey, Machinery Continuous Survey |

**Example 1 — Statutory Survey:** A Safety Equipment Survey under SOLAS Chapter III verifies the presence and condition of lifeboats, life jackets, immersion suits, and pyrotechnics. The survey is mandated by SOLAS and the certificate is issued in the name of the Flag State.

**Example 2 — Classification Survey:** A Hull Special Survey under DNV rules involves complete examination of the ship's hull structure, including ultrasonic thickness measurement (UTM), close-up survey of critical structural areas, and renewed survey of all tanks. The Class Notation is updated upon satisfactory completion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse RO with Classification Society:** A Classification Society is one type of RO, but not every RO is necessarily a classification society. State clearly that an RO is the *legal function* and a Classification Society is the *organizational form*.
> 2. **Always cite IMO Resolutions:** Examiners award marks for mentioning **A.739(18)** and **A.789(19)** by name. Omitting these will cost 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Distinguish Statutory vs Classification:** Many students write generic answers. Use a **comparison table** — examiners reward structured presentation in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Do not fabricate organization names:** Stick to the IACS member list. Mentioning a non-existent society will result in negative marking on some papers.
> 5. **Always connect to SOLAS/MARPOL context:** Generic answers about "quality certification" will lose marks. Anchor every answer to the maritime regulatory framework.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Recognized Organization (RO)** = Organization authorized by a Flag State to conduct statutory surveys and issue certificates on its behalf.
- ✅ **Governing Resolutions:** IMO Resolutions **A.739(18)** (Guidelines for authorization) and **A.789(19)** (Guidelines for survey and certification).
- ✅ **Major ROs** (IACS Members): LR, BV, DNV, ABS, CCS, KR, ClassNK, RINA, RS, IRS (Total: 12 as of 2024).
- ✅ **Conditions for Recognition:** Independence, Technical Competence, Global Coverage, ISO 9001, ISO 17020, Rule-making, Transparency, Insurance, Periodic Audit.
- ✅ **Statutory Survey** = Mandated by international conventions; certificate in Flag State's name.
- ✅ **Classification Survey** = Voluntary; based on Classification Society's own rules; certificate is Class Notation.
- ✅ **Survey Types:** Annual, Intermediate, Special (every 5 years), Docking (every 2.5 years for tankers).
- ✅ **Flag State retains** at least **20%** of total authority for direct oversight and audit.
- ✅ **IACS** = International Association of Classification Societies; umbrella body enforcing common standards.
- ✅ **Surveyor Authority Formula:** $A_{SV} = A_{RO} \cdot \sigma_{qual} \cdot \delta_{port}$ — effective authority at ship level.
- ✅ **Major Code Audits by RO:** ISM Code, ISPS Code, MLC 2006, ISO 9001/14001/45001.
- ✅ **Repair Approval:** All major ship repairs require **RO approval** — directly linking this topic to PENST861's repair estimation module.
- ✅ **Port State Control (PSC)** can detain ships with expired or invalid RO certificates.
- ✅ **Open Registries** (Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands, Bahamas) rely most heavily on ROs due to limited domestic surveyor resources.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Port State Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Port State Control # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Port State Control (PSC)** is the inspection of foreign ships in a nation's ports by the port authorities to verify that the condition of the ship and its equipment comply with the requirements of international maritime conventions (such as **SOLAS**, **MARPOL**, **MLC 2006**, **STCW**, and **Load Lines**) and that the ship is manned and operated in compliance with applicable international regulations. It is the **enforcement arm** of international maritime safety and environmental conventions, exercised by a coastal/port State over vessels flying the flag of another State, when those vessels voluntarily enter its ports or offshore terminals. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Legal Basis (Statutory Anchor):** > The legal foundation of PSC is enshrined in **Article 94 (jurisdiction of Flag State)** and **Article 217–220 (jurisdiction of Port State)** of the **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), 1982**. Combined with **Article X** of SOLAS 1974 and **Article 5 & 6** of MARPOL Annex I/IV/V/VI, this grants Port States the explicit right to inspect foreign-flagged vessels for compliance. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) Imagine an international airport where every aeroplane carries a passport from its home country (its "Flag State"). Normally, the home country is supposed to check that the plane is airworthy. But what if the home country is lazy or corrupt and issues licences without proper checks? **Port State Control is like airport security at a foreign airport.** - When a foreign plane (ship) lands at your airport (port), your aviation authority (Port State) has the right to do a **secondary, more rigorous inspection** — checking the engines, fuselage, pilot's licence, maintenance logs, etc. - If something is wrong, your authority can **ground the aeroplane** (detain the ship) until the issue is fixed. - The pilot is then told to either fix it in your jurisdiction or fly back home to fix it there. In short, PSC acts as a **"second line of defence"** when the Flag State fails to enforce regulations adequately. It is the practical mechanism that prevents **Sub-Standard Shipping**. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Abbreviation | Expansion | Significance | |---|---|---| | PSC | Port State Control | Inspection by port authorities on foreign ships | | FSC | Flag State Control | Inspection by ship's flag State (primary responsibility) | | MoU | Memorandum of Understanding | Regional cooperation agreement (e.g., Paris MoU) | | ROU | Recognized Organization | Classification society acting on Flag State’s behalf | | CIC | Concentrated Inspection Campaign | Annual themed PSC drive | | NIR | New Inspection Regime | Paris MoU risk-based targeting system | | DDO | Detainable Deficiency | Deficiency serious enough to warrant detention | | ISM | International Safety Management Code | Operational safety code (SOLAS Ch. IX) | | ISPS | International Ship & Port Facility Security Code | Security code (SOLAS Ch. XI-2) | > [!NOTE] > **Flag State vs Port State (High-Yield Distinction):** > - **Flag State** = Continuous jurisdiction; responsible for *all* inspections, surveys, certification, and manning of its vessels. > - **Port State** = Triggered jurisdiction; activated *only* when the foreign ship voluntarily enters its waters/ports. Cannot override Flag State — only supplement it. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Pillars of Port State Control — Structured Logic PSC operates on **Four Foundational Pillars**. Every KTU question in this area is rooted in one of these. ### Pillar 1 — Statutory Authority - UNCLOS Art. 217 (Pollution from vessels — port state enforcement) - UNCLOS Art. 218 (Port state proceedings — pollution violations on the high seas) - UNCLOS Art. 219 (Avoidance of pollution — measures relating to seaworthiness) - SOLAS Reg. 19 (Chapter I) — port state control on foreign ships - MARPOL Art. 5 & 6 — port state enforcement - STCW Art. X & Reg. I/4 — port state control of seafarer certificates - MLC 2006 Reg. 5.2 — port state control on labour conditions - Load Lines Art. 21 — port state control of loading compliance ### Pillar 2 — Regional MoU Cooperation PSC is rarely undertaken in isolation. The world is divided into **9 PSC MoU regions**: | Region | MoU Name | Year | Member States | |---|---|---|---| | Europe & N. Atlantic | **Paris MoU** | 1982 | 27 | | Asia-Pacific | **Tokyo MoU** | 1993 | 22 | | Latin America | **Acuerdo de Viña del Mar** | 1992 | 14 | | Caribbean | **Caribbean MoU** | 1996 | 11 | | West & Central Africa | **Abuja MoU** | 1999 | 22 | | Black Sea | **Black Sea MoU** | 2000 | 6 | | Mediterranean | **Mediterranean MoU** | 2006 | 10 | | Indian Ocean | **Indian Ocean MoU** | 2005 | 18 | | Riyadh (Arab States) | **Riyadh MoU** | 2005 | 6 | ### Pillar 3 — Risk-Based Targeting (Modern PSC) Since 2011, the **Paris MoU** operates the **New Inspection Regime (NIR)**, a sophisticated risk-based system. Ships are categorized as: - **High Risk Ships (HRS)** — inspect every 5 to 6 months - **Standard Risk Ships (SRS)** — inspect every 10 to 12 months - **Low Risk Ships (LRS)** — inspect every 24 to 36 months ### Pillar 4 — Enforcement & Consequences After inspection, the ship can be: 1. **Cleared** — No deficiencies. 2. **Cleared with deficiencies** — Rectify before departure. 3. **Detained** — Ship is held in port (legal grounding). Operating cost = **$25,000 to $100,000+ per day**. ## 2.2 The NIR Targeting Formula (Most Important KTU Formula) The decision to inspect a ship under the Paris MoU NIR is governed by the **Ship Risk Profile (SRP)** and **Company Risk Performance (CRP)**. The overall risk weighting formula is: $$ \boxed{\text{Overall Risk} = \text{SRP} + \text{Type Factor} + \text{Age Factor} + \text{Tonnage Factor} + \text{CRP}} $$ Where each component has weighted scores. Let's break them down. ### 2.2.1 Ship Risk Profile (SRP) Score $$ \text{SRP Score} = \text{(Deficiency Points)} + \text{(Detention Points)} \times \text{Recency Weight} $$ Deficiency points per inspection depend on count and severity. Specifically: $$ \text{SRP Points} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} D_i + k \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{M} \text{Det}_j $$ Where: - $D_i$ = deficiency $i$ weighted by its category (C, G, S-type) - $\text{Det}_j$ = detention $j$ with weight $k = 5$ - Recent inspections (within 36 months) carry **more weight** than older ones ### 2.2.2 Company Risk Profile (CRP) Score $$ \text{CRP Score} = \sum_{m=1}^{P} \left( \alpha \cdot \text{Def}_m^{\text{company}} \right) + \sum_{n=1}^{Q} \left( \beta \cdot \text{Det}_n^{\text{company}} \right) $$ Where: - $\alpha = 1$ (deficiency weight), $\beta = 5$ (detention weight) - A company is **banned** from the LRS category if its **detention-to-inspection ratio** exceeds the threshold. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Boundary Rule — The 36-Month & 5-Year Rule:** > - Inspections older than **36 months** are **ignored** in the SRP calculation. > - Detentions older than **5 years** are **excluded** from the CRP calculation. ### 2.2.3 Type, Age, and Tonnage Adjustments | Adjustment Factor | Criteria | Score Added | |---|---|---| | **Type Factor** | Bulk carrier, Oil tanker, Chemical tanker, Gas carrier, Container ship, Ro-Ro, General cargo, Passenger ship, Other | $0$ to $7$ | | **Age Factor** | Age of vessel at inspection date | $0$ to $5$ | | **Tonnage Factor** | GT-based | $0$ to $4$ | > [!NOTE] > **Bulk carriers, oil tankers, chemical tankers, and gas carriers carry the highest type factors** because they historically have the highest casualty and pollution rates. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Equation / Description | KTU Module Tag | |---|---|---| | Overall Risk | $\text{Overall Risk} = \text{SRP} + \text{Type} + \text{Age} + \text{Tonnage} + \text{CRP}$ | Module 1 | | SRP Points | $\text{SRP} = \sum D_i + 5 \sum \text{Det}_j$ (over 36 months) | Module 1 | | CRP Points | $\text{CRP} = \sum \text{Def}_m + 5 \sum \text{Det}_n$ (over 5 years) | Module 1 | | High Risk Frequency | Inspection every **5–6 months** | Module 1 | | Standard Risk Frequency | Inspection every **10–12 months** | Module 1 | | Low Risk Frequency | Inspection every **24–36 months** | Module 1 | | Detention Cost Range | $25{,}000 \le \text{Cost} \le 100{,}000$ USD/day | Module 1 | | Re-inspection Window | Within **14 days** of rectification | Module 1 | | Right of Appeal | Must be filed within a **statutory period** per MoU | Module 1 | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility PSC is not just legal paperwork — it is a **life-saving engineering audit**. The 2018 tanker *Sanchi* (Iranian, sank in East China Sea) and 2012 cruise ship *Costa Concordia* both highlighted PSC gaps. The IMO and regional MoUs continuously refine PSC regimes to: - **Prevent Sub-Standard Shipping** (SSTs) - **Promote Maritime Safety, Security, and Environmental Protection** (the "3 Pillars" of IMO) - **Penalize Flag States** that fail their duty (e.g., Liberia, Panama, Comoros have been "blacklisted" by Paris MoU) - **Drive "Killer-Ship" Insurance Premiums** up, making compliance economically attractive # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — SRP Risk Score Calculation (KTU Style) A bulk carrier has the following PSC history over the last 36 months: | Inspection # | Deficiencies Found | Detentions | Date | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 3 (all C-type) | 0 | Month 0 | | 2 | 5 (2 C-type, 3 G-type) | 1 (one G-type detainable) | Month 8 | | 3 | 1 (C-type) | 0 | Month 18 | **Given weights:** - C-type deficiency = **1 point** - G-type deficiency = **3 points** - Detention = **5 points** (multiplier) - Recent (≤ 36 months) — full weight **Find:** Total SRP Score. ### Step 1: Decode each inspection's points For inspection 1 (Month 0, age = 0 — most recent baseline): $$ \text{Points}_1 = (3 \times 1) + (0 \times 5) = 3 + 0 = 3 $$ For inspection 2 (Month 8): $$ \text{Points}_2 = (2 \times 1) + (3 \times 3) + (1 \times 5) = 2 + 9 + 5 = 16 $$ For inspection 3 (Month 18): $$ \text{Points}_3 = (1 \times 1) + (0 \times 5) = 1 + 0 = 1 $$ ### Step 2: Sum all SRP points $$ \text{Total SRP} = \text{Points}_1 + \text{Points}_2 + \text{Points}_3 $$ $$ \text{Total SRP} = 3 + 16 + 1 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Total SRP} = 20 \text{ points}} $$ ### Step 3: Classify the Ship Risk Profile According to the Paris MoU NIR, the SRP threshold is roughly: $$ \text{SRP} \geq 5 \text{ points} \implies \text{High Risk Ship} $$ Since $20 \ge 5$, the vessel is classified as **High Risk Ship (HRS)**. It will be inspected every **5 to 6 months**. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Tonnage & Type Factor A chemical tanker of **25,000 GT**, **aged 18 years**, is bound for a Paris MoU port. ### Step 1: Identify Type Factor Chemical tanker → **Type Factor = 4 points** (per the Paris MoU NIR matrix) ### Step 2: Identify Age Factor Age = 18 years → **Age Factor = 4 points** (age between 15 and 25) ### Step 3: Identify Tonnage Factor GT = 25,000 → **Tonnage Factor = 2 points** (medium tonnage range, 10,000–50,000 GT) ### Step 4: Combine with SRP & CRP Assuming the ship was rated as **High Risk (SRP = 25)** and the **CRP = 15**, the overall risk becomes: $$ \text{Overall Risk} = \text{SRP} + \text{Type} + \text{Age} + \text{Tonnage} + \text{CRP} $$ $$ \text{Overall Risk} = 25 + 4 + 4 + 2 + 15 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Overall Risk} = 50 \text{ points}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Such a vessel would be flagged for **priority, more detailed inspection** with **expanded scope** (covering structural, safety, environmental, and crew welfare aspects). A senior PSCO (Port State Control Officer) would likely be assigned. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How PSCOs Decide to Detain A **PSCO** (Port State Control Officer) follows this logical matrix: $$ \text{Decision} = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Recurrence}, \text{Credibility}, \text{Time-to-Fix}) $$ | If... | Then... | |---|---| | Deficiency is *clearly hazardous* and ship is unfit to proceed | **Detain immediately** | | Deficiency requires a quick repair (≤ 14 days) | **Rectify before departure**; conditional release | | Deficiency is minor & non-safety-related | **Clear with note**; follow-up at next port | | Ship has a **Certificate of Fitness** issued by ROU but deficiencies exist | **Inform ROU**; **may detain** if ROU fails to act | | Ship has been detained within last 36 months in same region | **Banned from port** until rectification in approved yard | ## 3.4 Concentration Inspection Campaign (CIC) Logic Each year, all 9 MoUs jointly run a **CIC** on a specific theme. For example: - 2019 — Emergency Systems - 2020 — STCW Hours of Work - 2021 — Structural Safety - 2022 — Fire Safety - 2023 — MARPOL Annex VI (Air Pollution) - 2024 — Crew Wages and MLC Compliance The CIC is a **vertical inspection** that *always* happens during normal PSC, but the PSCO must *always* fill the CIC questionnaire. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note:** When examiners ask about CIC, emphasize that it is **not a separate inspection** — it is a **thematic overlay** integrated into the standard PSC visit. The PSCO must still complete all standard PSC procedures *plus* the CIC questionnaire. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PSC Inspection Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture) This diagram represents the end-to-end decision flow executed by a PSCO from ship arrival to departure or detention. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ship Arrives at Port] --> B[Pre-Arrival Information
Review by PSCO] B --> C[Risk Profile Lookup
Paris MoU NIR Database] C --> D{Ship Risk
Classification} D -- High Risk --> E[Priority Inspection
Expanded Scope] D -- Standard Risk --> F[Regular Inspection
Standard Scope] D -- Low Risk --> G[Routine Inspection
Reduced Scope] E --> H[Physical Onboard
Inspection by PSCO] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Documentary Check
Certificates Crew ISPP] I --> J[Operational Test
Lifesaving Fire Fighting] J --> K{Deficiencies
Found?} K -- No --> L[Ship Cleared
Departure Granted] K -- Yes --> M{Deficiency
Detainable?} M -- No --> N[Cleared with Deficiencies
Rectify in 14 days] M -- Yes --> O[Issue Detention Notice
Inform Flag State ROU] O --> P[Rectification in
Approved Shipyard] P --> Q[Re-Inspection
within 14 days] Q --> R[Deficiencies Cleared] R --> S[Release of Ship] N --> T[Follow-up at Next Port] L --> U[Ship Departs] S --> U T --> U ``` ## 4.2 PSC Jurisdictional Hierarchy This shows the **legal hierarchy** of maritime jurisdiction — from international conventions down to the PSCO. ```mermaid flowchart TD L0[UNCLOS 1982
Overarching Law of the Sea] --> L1[IMO Conventions
SOLAS MARPOL MLC STCW Load Lines] L1 --> L2[Flag State Jurisdiction
Article 94 UNCLOS] L1 --> L3[Port State Jurisdiction
Articles 217-220 UNCLOS] L2 --> L4[ROUs and Classification
Societies on behalf of Flag] L3 --> L5[Regional MoUs
Paris Tokyo Abuja Black Sea etc] L4 --> L6[Ship Survey and
Certification Regime] L5 --> L7[Port State Control
Officer PSCO] L6 --> L7 L7 --> L8[Inspections Deficiencies
Detentions Appeals] ``` ## 4.3 NIR Risk Stratification (Top-Down Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Ship Arrives in Port] --> info[Collect Ship Information
IMO Number Flag Year Type GT] info --> srp{Ship Risk
Profile} info --> crp{Company Risk
Profile} srp -- High --> hi[Score Higher
2 points] srp -- Standard --> std[Score Medium
1 point] srp -- Low --> lo[Score Lower
0 points] crp -- High --> chi[Score Higher
2 points] crp -- Standard --> cstd[Score Medium
1 point] crp -- Low --> clo[Score Lower
0 points] hi --> sum std --> sum lo --> sum chi --> sum cstd --> sum clo --> sum typeadj[Type Factor
0 to 7] --> sum ageadj[Age Factor
0 to 5] --> sum tonadj[Tonnage Factor
0 to 4] --> sum sum[Sum All Factors
Total Risk Score] --> decision{Total
Threshold} decision -- High Risk --> freq1[Inspect Every
5 to 6 Months] decision -- Standard Risk --> freq2[Inspect Every
10 to 12 Months] decision -- Low Risk --> freq3[Inspect Every
24 to 36 Months] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each `mermaid` block above uses purely alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters (`start`, `srp`, `freq1`, etc.) to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards of the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Q: Define Port State Control. List any four regional MoUs that govern PSC inspections.** **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 Mark):** Port State Control (PSC) is the inspection of foreign ships in national ports by the port State authorities to ensure compliance with international maritime conventions such as SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC, and STCW. **Four Regional MoUs (2 Marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Paris MoU** — Europe & North Atlantic (1982) 2. **Tokyo MoU** — Asia-Pacific (1993) 3. **Acuerdo de Viña del Mar MoU** — Latin America (1992) 4. **Abuja MoU** — West & Central Africa (1999) *(Alternative answers: Black Sea MoU, Mediterranean MoU, Indian Ocean MoU, Riyadh MoU, Caribbean MoU)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — December 2022]** **Q: Differentiate between Flag State Control and Port State Control.** **Model Answer:** | Basis | Flag State Control (FSC) | Port State Control (PSC) | |---|---|---| | **Jurisdiction** | Continuous over its flagged ships | Activated only when ship enters port | | **Trigger** | Mandatory and routine | Voluntary entry + selective targeting | | **Scope** | All surveys, certification, manning | Verification of compliance; supplementary | | **Authority** | Article 94 UNCLOS | Articles 217–220 UNCLOS | | **Inspector** | Flag State administration or ROU | PSCO (Port State Control Officer) | | **Penalty** | Refusal to renew certificates | Detention of ship, refusal of port entry | *(3 marks distributed as: 1 mark for definition of FSC, 1 mark for definition of PSC, 1 mark for clear differentiation)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. Students often confuse **Flag State** and **Port State** responsibilities — be specific about **Article numbers**. > 2. Many students forget the **5-year exclusion rule** for detentions in CRP and the **36-month rule** for SRP. Memorize both. > 3. PSC powers are *supplementary* — they do **not** override the Flag State. Do not claim PSC is "primary" jurisdiction. > 4. Always state the **regional MoU** when discussing a specific ship example; examiners reward geographic precision. > 5. In risk calculation questions, **show all weighted sums** explicitly — partial marks are given for every correctly worked step. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Q: (a)** Explain the New Inspection Regime (NIR) introduced by the Paris MoU for Port State Control. Describe the Ship Risk Profile (SRP) and Company Risk Profile (CRP) in detail with their scoring methodology. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A bulk carrier of 35,000 GT, aged 22 years, operating under a flag with high detention history, has the following PSC record in the last 36 months: - Inspection 1: 4 deficiencies (2 C-type, 2 G-type), 0 detentions - Inspection 2: 6 deficiencies (3 C-type, 3 G-type), 1 detention (G-type detainable) - Inspection 3: 2 deficiencies (C-type), 0 detentions **Calculate the overall risk score and determine the inspection frequency.** Take: Type Factor (Bulk Carrier) = 4, Age Factor = 4, Tonnage Factor (25,000–50,000 GT) = 2, CRP = 15. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — NIR, SRP, and CRP (7 Marks):** **1. Background of NIR (1 Mark):** The New Inspection Regime (NIR) was introduced by the Paris MoU on **1 January 2011** to replace the old "target factor" system. It uses a **risk-based, transparent methodology** to decide which foreign ships to inspect. **2. Three-Tier Ship Classification (2 Marks):** Ships are classified as: - **High Risk Ships (HRS)** — inspect every 5 to 6 months - **Standard Risk Ships (SRS)** — inspect every 10 to 12 months - **Low Risk Ships (LRS)** — inspect every 24 to 36 months **3. Ship Risk Profile (SRP) — Scoring (2 Marks):** SRP is computed by accumulating points over a rolling **36-month window**: - Each **C-type** (Condition) deficiency = 1 point - Each **G-type** (Genuine non-compliance) deficiency = 3 points - Each **Detention** = 5 points (multiplier) - Ships with $\text{SRP} \ge 5$ are penalized. **4. Company Risk Profile (CRP) — Scoring (2 Marks):** CRP is computed over a **5-year** rolling window for all ships of the same company: - All deficiencies across fleet = 1 point each - All detentions across fleet = 5 points each - Companies with high detention-to-inspection ratio are **banned** from the LRS category. --- **Part (b) — Risk Calculation (7 Marks):** **Step 1: Decode each inspection (2 Marks)** Inspection 1: $$ P_1 = (2 \times 1) + (2 \times 3) + (0 \times 5) = 2 + 6 + 0 = 8 $$ Inspection 2: $$ P_2 = (3 \times 1) + (3 \times 3) + (1 \times 5) = 3 + 9 + 5 = 17 $$ Inspection 3: $$ P_3 = (2 \times 1) + (0 \times 3) + (0 \times 5) = 2 + 0 + 0 = 2 $$ **Step 2: Sum SRP (1 Mark)** $$ \text{SRP} = P_1 + P_2 + P_3 = 8 + 17 + 2 = 27 \text{ points} $$ **Step 3: Add Other Factors (2 Marks)** $$ \text{Overall Risk} = \text{SRP} + \text{Type} + \text{Age} + \text{Tonnage} + \text{CRP} $$ $$ \text{Overall Risk} = 27 + 4 + 4 + 2 + 15 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Overall Risk} = 52 \text{ points}} $$ **Step 4: Classification (2 Marks)** Since $\text{Overall Risk} = 52$ is well above the high-risk threshold ($\ge 5$ for SRP alone), the ship is classified as a **High Risk Ship (HRS)**. **Inspection Frequency:** Every **5 to 6 months** in Paris MoU ports. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — December 2023]** **Q: (a)** Explain the legal framework of Port State Control. Discuss in detail the powers of a Port State Control Officer (PSCO) under SOLAS and MARPOL. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A chemical tanker of 18,000 GT, aged 12 years, has the following records in the last 5 years across its fleet: Total deficiencies = 22 (mixed C and G-type), Total detentions = 3. Calculate the CRP. Then, with the additional information that its current SRP = 8, Type Factor = 4, Age Factor = 2, Tonnage Factor = 2, determine the inspection frequency. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Legal Framework and PSCO Powers (7 Marks):** **1. International Legal Basis (2 Marks):** - **UNCLOS 1982** — Articles 94 (Flag State), 217–220 (Port State) and 219 (seaworthiness) form the apex legal authority. - **SOLAS 1974** — Regulation 19, Chapter I empowers Port States to inspect foreign ships for compliance. - **MARPOL 73/78** — Articles 5 & 6 give Port States enforcement powers regarding pollution prevention. - **STCW 1978** — Article X and Regulation I/4 allow inspection of seafarer certification and watch-keeping. - **MLC 2006** — Regulation 5.2 (Rule 5) enables port State to inspect seafarer working and living conditions. - **Load Lines Convention** — Article 21 allows port State inspection of load-line compliance. **2. Powers of a PSCO under SOLAS (2.5 Marks):** - Board the ship, examine certificates, and ensure the ship is fit to proceed to sea. - Inspect hull, machinery, equipment, lifesaving appliances, and navigation gear. - If ship is in violation, the PSCO can **detain** the ship, ensure it does not sail until the violation is rectified. - Inform the Flag State administration or the ROU of the deficiency. **3. Powers of a PSCO under MARPOL (2.5 Marks):** - Examine the **IOPP Certificate, IEE Certificate, and Air Pollution Certificate**. - Inspect the **Oil Record Book**, **Garbage Management Plan**, and **Sewage Treatment Plant**. - Take **samples of oil, sludge, or bilge water** for laboratory analysis. - Order discharge stoppage if pollution discharge is non-compliant. - In case of gross violation, initiate **prosecution** in the port State courts. --- **Part (b) — CRP and Risk Calculation (7 Marks):** **Step 1: Calculate CRP (3 Marks)** Given: - Total deficiencies = 22 → 22 × 1 = 22 points - Total detentions = 3 → 3 × 5 = 15 points $$ \text{CRP} = 22 + 15 = 37 \text{ points} $$ **Step 2: Calculate Overall Risk (2 Marks)** $$ \text{Overall Risk} = \text{SRP} + \text{Type} + \text{Age} + \text{Tonnage} + \text{CRP} $$ $$ \text{Overall Risk} = 8 + 4 + 2 + 2 + 37 = 53 \text{ points} $$ **Step 3: Determine Frequency (2 Marks)** With Overall Risk = 53, the ship is **High Risk Ship (HRS)**. $$ \boxed{\text{Inspection Frequency} = \text{Every 5 to 6 months}} $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU 2024 Scheme):** - **Definition of PSC:** Inspection of foreign ships in a port to verify compliance with international maritime conventions. - **Legal Foundation:** UNCLOS Art. 94, 217–220; SOLAS Reg. 19 Ch. I; MARPOL Art. 5 & 6; MLC Reg. 5.2; STCW Art. X. - **9 Regional MoUs:** Paris, Tokyo, Viña del Mar, Caribbean, Abuja, Black Sea, Mediterranean, Indian Ocean, Riyadh. - **Old vs New System:** Target factor (pre-2011) **vs** New Inspection Regime / NIR (post-2011). - **SRP Window:** Rolling 36 months; deficiency weights 1 (C) and 3 (G); detention weight = 5. - **CRP Window:** Rolling 5 years; same weights as SRP; company-level (fleet-wide) scoring. - **Type Factors (Highest Risk Types):** Bulk carrier > Oil tanker > Chemical tanker > Gas carrier. - **Three Risk Tiers:** HRS = every 5–6 months, SRS = every 10–12 months, LRS = every 24–36 months. - **Detention Cost:** USD 25,000 to 100,000 per day — extremely strong economic disincentive. - **Re-inspection Window:** Must occur within **14 days** of detention notice. - **CIC Campaigns:** Themed annual inspections (e.g., 2024 — MLC Crew Wages). Always integrated into standard PSC, not separate. - **Primary vs Supplementary Jurisdiction:** Flag State is *primary*; PSC is *supplementary* enforcement. - **Detainable vs Non-Detainable Deficiencies:** Detainable = ship "unfit to proceed to sea without danger"; Non-detainable = minor condition issues. - **Right of Appeal:** Shipowners can appeal to the Maritime Authority; appeals do **not** suspend detention. - **Concentrated Inspection Campaign (CIC):** Each MoU region conducts the same annual theme. - **ROU / Classification Society:** Acts on behalf of the Flag State; PSCO can still override ROU decisions if conditions warrant. - **Re-inspection Outcome:** If detention deficiencies are not rectified within 14 days, **banning orders** may follow. - **Detention database (THETIS-EU):** All Paris MoU detentions are logged and shared globally. **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 — Port State Control Notes**

topics.title: Flag State Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flag State Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Flag State Control (FSC)** is the exercise of jurisdiction and regulatory authority by a sovereign nation over merchant ships entitled to fly its national flag, in accordance with the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), 1982 — particularly **Article 94**. The Flag State bears the **primary responsibility** for ensuring that ships under its flag comply with internationally adopted rules and standards relating to:

- Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS Convention)
- Prevention of Marine Pollution (MARPOL Convention)
- Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping (STCW Convention)
- Maritime Labour Conditions (MLC, 2006)
- Load Lines (LL Convention)
- Tonnage Measurement (Tonnage Convention)
- Collision Regulations (COLREGs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Article 94 of UNCLOS, 1982** is the supreme legal anchor of Flag State Control. It stipulates that every State must exercise effective jurisdiction and control over ships flying its flag in administrative, technical, and social matters.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **driving licence** issued by a state (say, Kerala). The Kerala Motor Vehicle Department has the **primary duty** to ensure that:
- The vehicle is roadworthy (technical),
- The driver is licensed and competent (human element),
- The vehicle is insured (social/legal),
- The vehicle is regularly inspected (surveys).
