| **General Corrosion** | Steel (Fe) oxidises in electrolyte (seawater): Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ | Uniform thickness reduction |
| **Pitting** | Breakdown of passive film at micro-defects | Deep, narrow cavities; perforation risk |
| **Galvanic** | Potential difference between two metals → electron flow | Anode (less noble) dissolves preferentially |
| **Cavitation** | Local pressure drops below vapour pressure → bubble implosion | Surface pitting, material removal |
| **Slamming** | Wave impact pressure peaks (~300 kPa) | Plate indentation, paint failure |
| **Fatigue** | Cyclic stress (S-N cycles) | Crack initiation & propagation |
| **Biofouling** | Organisms settle → create oxygen concentration cells | Localised under-deposit corrosion |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are frequently asked in KTU ESE for Module 2 (corrosion & wastage estimation).**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot \rho}$ | Corrosion rate from mass loss | mm/year |
| 2 | $t_{remaining} = t_{original} - t_{lost}$ | Residual thickness | mm |
| 3 | $\%Wastage = \dfrac{t_{original} - t_{remaining}}{t_{original}} \times 100$ | Percentage wastage | % |
| 4 | $t_{renewal} = t_{as\text{-}built} \times 0.75$ | Minimum acceptable (Rule limit) | mm |
| 5 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{d^{3}}$ | Energy absorbed in grounding (E = energy, σ = yield, d = plate depth) | J/m |
| 6 | $\Delta V = I \cdot t / (n \cdot F)$ | Cathodic protection charge (Faraday's law) | mol |
| 7 | $S_{max} = \dfrac{p \cdot L^{2}}{2 \cdot t^{2}}$ | Plate bending stress under slamming | MPa |

**Where:**
- $CR$ = corrosion rate, $K = 8.76 \times 10^{4}$ (conversion constant), $W$ = mass loss (g), $A$ = exposed area (cm²), $T$ = exposure time (h), $\rho$ = density (g/cm³).
- $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $n$ = valence electrons, $F$ = Faraday's constant ≈ 96,485 C/mol.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding these mechanisms is **not academic** — it directly determines:

1. **Dry-docking intervals** (typically every 2.5–5 years).
2. **Steel renewal tonnage** (the basis of repair cost estimation — Module 3).
3. **Coating system selection** (e.g., epoxy + glass flake for ballast tanks).
4. **Cathodic protection design** (sacrificial anode sizing).
5. **Insurance & classification survey outcomes** (vessel may be "held" or "suspended" if wastage exceeds limits).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Corrosion Rate from Mass Loss

**Statement:** Determine the corrosion rate of a steel plate specimen exposed to seawater given weight loss data.

**Given:**
- Initial mass: $M_1 = 250.00$ g
- Final mass after exposure: $M_2 = 238.75$ g
- Exposure area: $A = 100$ cm²
- Exposure time: $T = 720$ hours (30 days)
- Density of steel: $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³

**Find:** Corrosion rate $CR$ in mm/year.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the mass loss.

$$W = M_1 - M_2 = 250.00 - 238.75 = 11.25 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the standard corrosion rate formula.

$$CR = \frac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute all known values.

$$CR = \frac{(8.76 \times 10^{4}) \cdot (11.25)}{(100) \cdot (720) \cdot (7.85)}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator.

$$(8.76 \times 10^{4}) \cdot 11.25 = 9.855 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the denominator.

$$100 \cdot 720 \cdot 7.85 = 5.652 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 6:** Divide to obtain the corrosion rate.

$$CR = \frac{9.855 \times 10^{5}}{5.652 \times 10^{5}} = 1.744 \text{ mm/year}$$

**Step 7:** Interpretation.

> A corrosion rate of **1.74 mm/year** is significantly higher than the typical 0.1–0.5 mm/year range, indicating either **impaired coating**, **active galvanic coupling**, or **MIC activity**. The surveyor must flag this for **immediate grit blasting + recoating + anode inspection**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Percentage Wastage & Renewal Decision

**Statement:** A bottom plate has an as-built thickness of **22 mm**. After 18 years of service, ultrasonic thickness measurement (UTM) reveals an average residual thickness of **17.5 mm** in way of the midship region.

**Find:**
1. Percentage wastage
2. Whether renewal is required (Classification limit = 25% wastage)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the absolute thickness loss.

$$t_{lost} = t_{original} - t_{remaining} = 22 - 17.5 = 4.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the wastage formula.

$$\%Wastage = \frac{t_{original} - t_{remaining}}{t_{original}} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$\%Wastage = \frac{4.5}{22} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate.

$$\%Wastage = 0.2045 \times 100 = 20.45\%$$

**Step 5:** Decision logic.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Condition for renewal:} \quad & \%Wastage \geq 25\% \\
\text{Observed wastage:} \quad & 20.45\% < 25\% \\
\therefore \quad & \text{No renewal required, but close monitoring needed.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Sub-plot analysis.

If localised pitting is found at the same plate (e.g., 14 mm at pit bottom), then:

$$\%Pitting \, wastage = \frac{22 - 14}{22} \times 100 = 36.36\%$$

This **local** value exceeds 25% → **Local renewal of pitted areas** is mandatory even though the **average** is acceptable. This dual-criterion (average vs. local) is a **favourite KTU examiner trap**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Damage Mechanism Diagnostic Table

| Symptom (What Surveyor Sees) | Likely Root Cause | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| Uniform reddish-brown staining over entire bilge | General corrosion | Recoat after grit blasting Sa 2½ |
| Deep, sharp-edged cavities (2–5 mm deep) | Pitting corrosion | Local renewal or weld build-up |
| White crusty deposits around welds | Calcareous deposit / cathodic protection over-protection | Inspect anode consumption rate |
| Cracks at bracket toes | Fatigue | Edge grinding, re-welding, profile improvement |
| Indented bottom plates forward | Slamming | Renew forward bottom plates, fit additional web frames |
| Honeycomb pattern under coating | MIC (Microbial) | Fresh-water wash, biocide treatment, recoat |
| Worn-down leading edge of rudder | Cavitation | Weld overlay with austenitic stainless (e.g., 309L) |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Slamming-Induced Plate Stress (Forward Bottom)

**Statement:** A forward bottom plate panel of length $L = 1.2$ m and thickness $t = 18$ mm experiences a slamming pressure $p = 250$ kPa.

**Find:** Maximum bending stress in the plate.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the plate bending stress formula for a simply-supported panel under uniform pressure.

$$S_{max} = \frac{p \cdot L^{2}}{2 \cdot t^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Convert units consistently.

$$p = 250 \text{ kPa} = 0.25 \text{ MPa}, \quad L = 1.2 \text{ m}, \quad t = 18 \text{ mm} = 0.018 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$S_{max} = \frac{(0.25) \cdot (1.2)^{2}}{2 \cdot (0.018)^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator and denominator.

$$Numerator = 0.25 \cdot 1.44 = 0.36$$

$$Denominator = 2 \cdot 0.000324 = 0.000648$$

**Step 5:** Divide.

$$S_{max} = \frac{0.36}{0.000648} = 555.56 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation.

> This stress exceeds the yield strength of Grade A steel (~235 MPa), confirming that **slamming in head seas will plastically deform unprotected plate panels**, leading to indentation, buckling, and subsequent fatigue cracking. Mitigation: increase plate thickness forward 25% L from FP, fit tripping brackets, use higher-tensile steel (Grade AH36).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Classification of Hull Wear & Damage Causes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Wear and Damage in Ship Hull] --> B[Mechanical Causes]
    A --> C[Chemical Causes]
    A --> D[Fatique Causes]
    A --> E[Accidental Causes]
    A --> F[Biological Causes]

    B --> B1[Abrasive Wear]
    B --> B2[Erosive Wear]
    B --> B3[Cavitation Damage]
    B --> B4[Slamming Impact]

    C --> C1[General Corrosion]
    C --> C2[Pitting Corrosion]
    C --> C3[Galvanic Corrosion]
    C --> C4[Crevice Corrosion]
    C --> C5[Stress Corrosion Cracking]
    C --> C6[Microbial Induced Corrosion]

    D --> D1[Fatique Cracks]
    D --> D2[Buckling of Plates]

    E --> E1[Grounding]
    E --> E2[Berthing Contact]
    E --> E3[Collision Damage]

    F --> F1[Biofouling]
    F --> F2[Splash Zone Corrosion]
    F --> F3[Atmospheric Corrosion]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Damage Progression Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Operational Environment] --> B[Trigger Mechanism]
    B --> C{Wear or Damage Type}
    C -->|Wear| D[Progressive Material Loss]
    C -->|Damage| E[Sudden Structural Defect]

    D --> F[Thickness Reduction]
    E --> F

    F --> G{Threshold Check}
    G -->|Wastage less than 25 percent| H[Acceptable Monitor]
    G -->|Wastage greater than 25 percent| I[Renewal Required]

    H --> J[Next Survey Cycle]
    I --> K[Steel Renewal and Repair]

    K --> L[Cost Estimation Module 3]
    J --> M[Continued Service]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation — Hull Zone-Wise Vulnerability

| Hull Zone | Primary Damage Type | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward Bottom (0–0.25 L)** | Slamming, grounding | Very High |
| **Mid-ship Bottom (0.25–0.75 L)** | General corrosion, grounding | High |
| **Aft Bottom** | Cavitation (near propeller) | High |
| **Side Shell (Waterline)** | Splash zone corrosion, contact | Very High |
| **Ballast Tanks** | MIC, pitting | Very High |
| **Superstructure** | Atmospheric corrosion | Moderate |
| **Rudder** | Cavitation, fouling | High |
| **Stern Frame** | Galvanic (bronze/steel interface) | High |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define pitting corrosion. Mention two locations on a ship's hull where it is commonly observed.**

**Model Answer:**
Pitting corrosion is a **localised form of attack** resulting in the formation of discrete cavities or "pits" on a metal surface, with the bulk of the surface remaining relatively unaffected. It is caused by the breakdown of the protective passive film at microscopic defects.

**Common locations:**
1. Under deposits of marine fouling (biofouling under-deposit attack).
2. Inside ballast tanks at coating defects, weld seams, and sharp edges.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is cavitation damage? Why does it occur on the rudder and propeller?**

**Model Answer:**
Cavitation damage is the **erosion of metal surface caused by the implosion of vapour bubbles** in a liquid when local pressure drops below the vapour pressure of the fluid.

**Why on rudder/propeller:**
- High rotational speeds create localised low-pressure zones.
- Bubble collapse near the metal surface generates micro-jets and shock waves that physically remove material.
- Often appears as a **spongy, pitted appearance** on the rudder's leading edge and propeller blade tips.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question B1. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`
**(a)** Classify the various **causes of wear and damage in a ship's hull** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A bottom plate has an as-built thickness of **20 mm**. After 15 years, UTM readings show an average of **16 mm** and a localised pit depth equivalent to **12 mm**. Determine the **percentage wastage (average and local)** and state whether renewal is required under the 25% rule. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of Causes:**

```
CAUSES OF HULL WEAR AND DAMAGE
├── 1. Mechanical
│   ├── Abrasive wear (sand, ice, quay contact)
│   ├── Erosive wear (high-velocity particle streams)
│   ├── Cavitation (rudder, propeller)
│   └── Slamming (forward bottom in head seas)
├── 2. Chemical (Corrosion)
│   ├── General corrosion
│   ├── Pitting corrosion
│   ├── Galvanic corrosion
│   ├── Crevice corrosion
│   ├── Stress corrosion cracking
│   └── Microbial induced corrosion
├── 3. Fatigue-related
│   ├── Fatigue cracks (at stress raisers)
│   └── Plate buckling
├── 4. Accidental
│   ├── Grounding/stranding
│   ├── Berthing contact
│   └── Collision
└── 5. Biological/Environmental
    ├── Biofouling
    ├── Splash zone corrosion
    └── Atmospheric corrosion
```

**(b) Wastage Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Average wastage} &= \frac{20 - 16}{20} \times 100 = 20\% \\
\text{Local pitting wastage} &= \frac{20 - 12}{20} \times 100 = 40\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Decision:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Average } (20\%) &< 25\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Acceptable (overall)} \\
\text{Local } (40\%) &> 25\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Local renewal mandatory}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** While the average condition is acceptable, the **local pitting at 40% exceeds the rule limit**, hence **local insert plating or renewal of pitted areas** is required, followed by NDT (MPI/DPI) of surrounding weld seams.

---

### Question B2. `[KTU University Exam – Internal Choice for B1]`
**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of galvanic corrosion** with reference to a typical shipboard application. List **two preventive measures**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A mild steel coupon of area **50 cm²** loses **6.0 g** mass when exposed to seawater for **600 hours**. Density of steel = **7.85 g/cm³**. Calculate the **corrosion rate in mm/year** and comment on the severity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Galvanic Corrosion Mechanism:**

Galvanic corrosion occurs when **two dissimilar metals are electrically connected in the presence of an electrolyte** (seawater). The metal with the **lower (more active) electrode potential** becomes the **anode** and corrodes preferentially, while the **nobler metal (cathode)** is protected.

**Shipboard example:** A **bronze (Cu-Sn) propeller** mounted on a **steel tail shaft** with a steel hull forms a galvanic cell. Steel (anode) corrodes; bronze (cathode) is unaffected.

**Preventive measures:**
1. Install **sacrificial zinc/aluminium anodes** on the hull near the stern.
2. Apply **insulating bushings** or **bonding washers** between dissimilar metals to break the electrical circuit.

**(b) Corrosion Rate Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot \rho} \\
   &= \frac{(8.76 \times 10^{4}) \cdot 6.0}{50 \cdot 600 \cdot 7.85} \\
   &= \frac{5.256 \times 10^{5}}{2.355 \times 10^{5}} \\
   &= 2.232 \text{ mm/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on severity:**
A corrosion rate of **2.23 mm/year** is **severely elevated** (typical range is 0.1–0.5 mm/year). This indicates either **coating breakdown**, **active galvanic effects**, or **MIC activity**. Immediate survey intervention with **full grit blasting, recoating, and CP system verification** is recommended.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Hull Wear & Damage Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "average" vs. "local" wastage** — KTU explicitly tests whether students apply the stricter **local** criterion. Marks are lost if only average wastage is reported.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in corrosion rate calculation — final answer MUST be in **mm/year**, not cm/year or g/cm²/h.
> 3. **Mixing up galvanic pairs** — Students often incorrectly state that the *nobler* metal corrodes. Remember: **the less noble (anode) corrodes**.
> 4. **Not distinguishing cavitation from erosion** — Cavitation involves **vapour bubble implosion**; erosion involves **particle impingement**. Examiners deduct marks for interchange.
> 5. **Skipping the IACS 25% rule** — Always mention the **classification society's renewal threshold** when discussing wastage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Hull wear = **gradual material loss**; Hull damage = **sudden structural defect**.
- ✅ Five primary cause categories: **Mechanical, Chemical, Fatigue, Accidental, Biological**.
- ✅ **Corrosion rate formula:** $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot \rho}$ with $K = 8.76 \times 10^{4}$.
- ✅ **IACS Renewal Rule:** Wastage **≥ 25%** → renewal required (average AND local criteria both apply).
- ✅ **Galvanic corrosion:** Less noble metal = anode = corrodes; Nobler = cathode = protected.
- ✅ **Cavitation** = vapour bubble implosion at rudder/propeller.
- ✅ **Slamming** = forward bottom plate impact in head seas → plate indentation.
- ✅ **MIC** = anaerobic bacteria in ballast tanks → honeycomb pitting.
- ✅ **Fatigue** initiates at stress raisers (bracket toes, hatch corners, cut-outs).
- ✅ **Splash zone** has the highest atmospheric corrosion rate due to wet-dry cycling.
- ✅ Surveyor's mantra: **"Measure, calculate, compare to rule limit, decide."**
- ✅ Always cite the **classification society** (IRS for Indian-flag vessels) when specifying acceptance criteria.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison Between Different Types of Repair Activities (Afloat, Berthed, etc.)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Comparison Between Different Types of Repair Activities (Afloat, Berthed, etc.)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Ship Repair Activities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Standard Definition (KTU PENST861 Module 2):**
> *Ship repair activities* are classified based on the **location, accessibility, environmental conditions, and operational status of the vessel** during the execution of maintenance, rectification, or refit work. The principal categories recognised under KTU and maritime classification society standards are: **(a) Afloat Repairs, (b) Berthed / Alongside Repairs, (c) Dry-Dock Repairs, (d) Emergency / Voyage Repairs, and (e) Anchorage Repairs.**

Each category differs fundamentally in the **degree of accessibility** afforded to the hull, underwater appendages, propulsion machinery, and auxiliary systems. The selection of a repair regime is governed by classification society rules (IRS, LR, DNV, BV, ABS), statutory requirements (SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC), and commercial considerations (off-hire, port stay, voyage schedule).

## 1.2 The Five Principal Repair Regimes

| Regime | Symbol | Operational State of Vessel |
|---|---|---|
| Afloat Repair | **AFR** | Vessel remains floating at berth or anchorage |
| Berthed / Alongside Repair | **BAR** | Vessel moored alongside repair quay |
| Dry-Dock Repair | **DDR** | Vessel in graving dock / floating dock / slipway |
| Emergency / Voyage Repair | **EVR** | Repair during voyage or at unscheduled port |
| Anchorage Repair | **ANR** | Repair at sheltered anchorage using tenders |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Hospital Treatment Tiers**
> Think of a ship repair yard as a **multi-tier hospital** for ships:
>
> - **Afloat repair** is like an **out-patient consultation** — the patient (ship) stays in normal position, only superficial/superstructure work is done while floating in berth.
> - **Berthed alongside repair** is similar to a **specialist clinic visit** — the ship is alongside a workshop where machinists, fitters, and riggers can access sides and deck.
> - **Dry-dock repair** is the equivalent of a **major surgery** — the vessel is taken out of its natural element (water), placed on blocks, and the entire hull and underwater body is exposed for full treatment.
> - **Emergency / voyage repair** resembles **first-aid on the move** — temporary fixes to keep the ship operational until proper repair.
> - **Anchorage repair** is the **mobile clinic** — workshops and barges come alongside in sheltered waters.

## 1.4 Classification Society Recognition

All major classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas) issue **class survey notations** that explicitly demand dry-dock surveys at intervals not exceeding **5 years** (bottom inspection) or **2.5 years** for intermediate surveys. Hence, irrespective of the volume of afloat/berthed work, the vessel **must periodically enter DDR** to retain class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constraint:** No amount of afloat or berthed repair can substitute for the mandatory dry-dock bottom survey required by **SOLAS Ch. I, Reg. 7** and **Load Line Convention** for class renewal.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Comparison Matrix

## 2.1 Operational & Logistical Drivers of Repair Selection

The decision matrix governing which repair regime to adopt is influenced by:

1. **Nature of defect** — underwater vs. above-water, structural vs. machinery.
2. **Class & statutory survey schedule** — Bottom Survey (DDR mandatory), Tail Shaft Survey (every 5 yrs), Special Survey.
3. **Operational urgency** — Commercial off-hire cost per day (typically **USD 15,000–50,000** for a Panamax bulker; up to **USD 100,000+** for capesize).
4. **Geographic availability** — proximity of repair yards, divers, workshops.
5. **Cost vs. risk trade-off** — Emergency LSA services cost a premium.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

| Parameter | Afloat Repair (AFR) | Berthed / Alongside (BAR) | Dry-Dock (DDR) | Emergency / Voyage (EVR) | Anchorage (ANR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vessel Location | Alongside berth or mooring | Quay wall / repair jetty | Graving / Floating dock / Slipway | At sea or unscheduled port | Sheltered roadstead |
| Underwater Access | Divers only | Divers only | Full direct access | Divers if at port | Divers from barges |
| Hull Bottom Work | Not possible | Not possible | Fully possible | Temporary only | Limited patching |
| Shaft / Rudder Work | Not possible | Not possible | Fully possible | Emergency only | Possible with divers |
| Above-Waterline Hull | Possible | Possible | Possible | Possible | Possible |
| Engine Room Overhaul | Possible | Possible | Possible | Temporary only | Limited |
| Cost Index (relative) | 1.0× (baseline) | 1.2× | 3.0–5.0× | 4.0–6.0× | 1.8× |
| Time Index (relative) | 1.0× | 1.3× | 3.0× | Variable | 1.5× |
| Need for Shore Power | Optional | Usually available | Mandatory | None | Generator only |
| Class Survey Validity | Yes (limited scope) | Yes (limited scope) | Yes (full scope) | Conditional | Limited |
| Environmental Risk | Low | Low | Controlled | High | Moderate |
| Manoeuvring Required | Minimal | None | Yes (docking) | None | Anchoring |
| Typical Duration | 1–7 days | 3–14 days | 7–30 days | Hours–2 days | 3–10 days |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The cost and time indices above are normalised against Afloat Repair = 1.0×.** Dry-dock repair is the most expensive and time-consuming because it requires docking infrastructure (graving dock, floating dock, or slipway), skilled manpower, gas-free/clean conditions, and complete dewatering.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of the Comparison

This comparative framework is used by:

- **Ship Superintendents** to plan repair specifications and budgets.
- **Classification Surveyors** to determine scope of survey vs. available regime.
- **P&I Clubs and H&M Insurers** to validate repair quotations.
- **Ship Managers (Technical Department)** to optimise OPEX by selecting the minimum adequate regime.
- **Charterers and Shipowners** to balance off-hire vs. repair cost.

## 2.4 Decision Logic — When to Use Each Regime

```
DEFECT IDENTIFIED
      |
      v
Is it underwater? ---YES---> Requires divers?
      |NO                       |YES
      v                          v
Above-waterline            Afloat OR Dry-dock
   defect                  (depends on class survey)
      |
      v
Class survey due? ---YES---> MANDATORY DRY-DOCK
      |NO
      v
Can ship continue voyage? ---NO---> EMERGENCY REPAIR
      |YES
      v
Schedule next routine dry-dock; perform
AFLOAT / BERTHED repair as palliative
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** The Tanker *Prestige* (2002) and bulker *Erika* (1999) casualties demonstrate the catastrophic consequence of inadequate repair regime selection — both involved structural hull defects that demanded dry-dock intervention but were inadequately addressed via afloat patches, leading to total loss and major pollution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Decision Matrices

## 3.1 Quantitative Cost-Time Decision Framework

To select the optimal repair regime, the **Total Cost of Downtime (TCD)** is computed as:

$$
\text{TCD} = C_{\text{repair}} + (D \times C_{\text{offhire}})
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{repair}}$ = quoted cost of the chosen repair regime (USD)
- $D$ = estimated downtime in days
- $C_{\text{offhire}}$ = daily off-hire cost of the vessel (USD/day)

**Decision Rule:** Select the regime that minimises TCD while satisfying class and statutory requirements.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem:** A Panamax bulk carrier (DWT 75,000) suffers bottom plate corrosion in way of No. 3 starboard side tank. The following options are available. The vessel's off-hire rate is **USD 22,000/day**. Recommend the optimal regime.

| Option | Regime | Repair Cost (USD) | Downtime (days) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Afloat patching by divers | 45,000 | 3 |
| B | Berthed with shore support | 85,000 | 6 |
| C | Dry-dock (floating dock) | 320,000 | 18 |
| D | Emergency voyage repair | 180,000 | 8 |

**Step 1 — Compute TCD for each option:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCD}_{A} &= 45{,}000 + (3 \times 22{,}000) \\
&= 45{,}000 + 66{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{111{,}000 \text{ USD}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCD}_{B} &= 85{,}000 + (6 \times 22{,}000) \\
&= 85{,}000 + 132{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{217{,}000 \text{ USD}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCD}_{C} &= 320{,}000 + (18 \times 22{,}000) \\
&= 320{,}000 + 396{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{716{,}000 \text{ USD}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCD}_{D} &= 180{,}000 + (8 \times 22{,}000) \\
&= 180{,}000 + 176{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{356{,}000 \text{ USD}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Class Constraint:**

The defect is **below waterline** on bottom plating → a permanent class-acceptable repair requires dry-dock per IRS/LR Rules. Hence Options A, B, D are only **palliative**.

**Step 3 — Decision:**

- For **immediate palliative (next voyage acceptable)**: Option **A** is optimal at USD 111,000.
- For **permanent class-approved rectification**: Schedule Option **C** (DDR) at next routine dry-dock within the survey window.

## 3.3 Class Survey Interval Mathematics

For vessels operating under **Unlimited Voyage Class Notation**, the Special Survey (SS) cycle for a typical bulk carrier hull is:

$$
T_{\text{SS}} = 5 \text{ years}, \quad T_{\text{drydock bottom}} = 5 \text{ years (max interval)}
$$

The intermediate survey (IS) window allows:

$$
T_{\text{IS}} = 2.5 \text{ years after SS}, \quad T_{\text{advance SS credit}} \le 12 \text{ months}
$$

Therefore, a vessel leaving the yard with **class valid** has a maximum permissible operational period before mandatory dry-dock:

$$
T_{\text{op max}} = T_{\text{SS}} - T_{\text{credit}} = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ years (if 12-month credit taken)}
$$

## 3.4 Comparative Risk Index (CRI)

To rank repair regimes by combined risk exposure, the **Comparative Risk Index** is defined as:

$$
\text{CRI} = w_1 \cdot R_{\text{safety}} + w_2 \cdot R_{\text{env}} + w_3 \cdot R_{\text{class}} + w_4 \cdot R_{\text{cost overrun}}
$$

Where weights satisfy $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$, and each $R$ is normalised to $[0, 1]$.

Typical CRI scores (lower = safer):

| Regime | $R_{\text{safety}}$ | $R_{\text{env}}$ | $R_{\text{class}}$ | $R_{\text{cost overrun}}$ | CRI (typical weights 0.3/0.3/0.2/0.2) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Afloat | 0.6 | 0.5 | 0.4 | 0.3 | **0.47** |
| Berthed | 0.4 | 0.3 | 0.3 | 0.4 | **0.34** |
| Dry-dock | 0.2 | 0.2 | 0.1 | 0.5 | **0.23** |
| Emergency | 0.9 | 0.8 | 0.7 | 0.6 | **0.78** |
| Anchorage | 0.7 | 0.6 | 0.5 | 0.5 | **0.59** |

**Interpretation:** Although dry-dock appears cheapest in CRI, its high cost overrun risk reflects schedule slippage from weather, repairs discovered, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The CRI framework is used by **P&I Clubs** during condition surveys to determine whether an operator's chosen repair regime is reasonable, and to support or contest insurance claims for general average contributions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Repair Regime Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Defect Identified via Condition Survey]
    nodeB{Is defect below waterline?}
    nodeC{Divers accessible at berth?}
    nodeD[AFLOAT REPAIR - Divers, Above-waterline]
    nodeE{Docking facility available?}
    nodeF[DRY-DOCK REPAIR - Full hull exposure]
    nodeG{Vessel must continue voyage urgently?}
    nodeH[EMERGENCY VOYAGE REPAIR - Temporary fix]
    nodeI[BERTHED ALONGSIDE REPAIR - Shore support]
    nodeJ{Anchorage sheltered nearby?}
    nodeK[ANCHORAGE REPAIR - Floating workshops]
    nodeL[Schedule for next routine dry-dock]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeI
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeD
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeG
    nodeG -- YES --> nodeH
    nodeG -- NO --> nodeL
    nodeD --> nodeL
    nodeI --> nodeE
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeF
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeK
    nodeK -- YES --> nodeJ
    nodeK -- NO --> nodeL
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Repair Activity Interfaces

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Ship Defect Database]
    node2[Class Survey Schedule Module]
    node3[Cost Estimation Engine]
    node4[Risk Assessment Module]
    node5[Repair Regime Selector]
    node6[Afloat Repair Module]
    node7[Berthed Repair Module]
    node8[Dry-dock Repair Module]
    node9[Emergency Repair Module]
    node10[Anchorage Repair Module]
    node11[Final Repair Spec Output]

    node1 --> node5
    node2 --> node5
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node5 --> node7
    node5 --> node8
    node5 --> node9
    node5 --> node10
    node6 --> node11
    node7 --> node11
    node8 --> node11
    node9 --> node11
    node10 --> node11
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — Visual Hierarchy of Repair Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTitle[REPAIR REGIME HIERARCHY]
    nodeTier1[TIER 1 - Permanent Class Repair]
    nodeTier2[TIER 2 - Scheduled Maintenance]
    nodeTier3[TIER 3 - Emergency Palliative]
    nodeDD[Dry-dock Repair - Highest Authority]
    nodeBerth[Berthed Repair - Routine Above-waterline]
    nodeAfloat[Afloat Repair - Divers and Above-waterline]
    nodeEmerg[Emergency / Voyage Repair - Temporary]
    nodeAnchor[Anchorage Repair - Mobile Workshops]

    nodeTitle --> nodeTier1
    nodeTitle --> nodeTier2
    nodeTitle --> nodeTier3
    nodeTier1 --> nodeDD
    nodeTier2 --> nodeBerth
    nodeTier2 --> nodeAfloat
    nodeTier2 --> nodeAnchor
    nodeTier3 --> nodeEmerg
```

## 4.4 Vessel Accessibility Profile (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeH[HULL REGIONS]
    nodeR1[Bottom Plate - Below waterline]
    nodeR2[Side Shell - Below waterline]
    nodeR3[Boot-top Region]
    nodeR4[Upper Deck and Superstructure]
    nodeR5[Internals - Tanks, Holds]
    nodeAccess1[Accessible only in DRY-DOCK]
    nodeAccess2[Accessible by Divers or DRY-DOCK]
    nodeAccess3[Accessible Afloat, Berthed, or Dry-dock]
    nodeAccess4[Accessible in all regimes]
    nodeAccess5[Accessible in all regimes with confined-space protocols]

    nodeH --> nodeR1
    nodeH --> nodeR2
    nodeH --> nodeR3
    nodeH --> nodeR4
    nodeH --> nodeR5
    nodeR1 --> nodeAccess1
    nodeR2 --> nodeAccess2
    nodeR3 --> nodeAccess3
    nodeR4 --> nodeAccess4
    nodeR5 --> nodeAccess5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**"Distinguish between afloat repair and dry-dock repair with respect to underwater accessibility."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Afloat Repair | Dry-Dock Repair |
|---|---|---|
| Underwater accessibility | Via divers only — limited to visual inspection, cleaning, and minor plate work | Full direct access — hull fully exposed above blocks |
| Class survey validity | Cannot substitute for mandatory bottom survey | Acceptable for full Special Survey and bottom survey |
| Cost & time | Lower cost (≈1.0× baseline) and shorter duration | Higher cost (≈3–5× baseline) and longer duration |

*Conclusion:* Afloat repair is suitable for above-waterline work and temporary underwater palliative work, whereas dry-dock repair is mandatory for permanent rectification of underwater hull defects. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**"List any four situations where emergency voyage repair becomes unavoidable."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Main engine breakdown** mid-voyage requiring divers/technicians at an unscheduled port (e.g., damaged propeller blade).
2. **Hull breach or grounding** requiring temporary cofferdam or collision-mat to enable vessel to reach a repair yard safely.
3. **Steering gear failure** necessitating emergency rudder repair at the nearest port.
4. **Fire or flooding damage** requiring immediate shore-based intervention to make the vessel seaworthy.

*Plus optional 5th:* Boiler tube leakage or turbocharger failure requiring emergency workshop support at the next port of call. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> • Students commonly **confuse "Berthed" with "Afloat"** repair — Berthed implies **shore-side workshop access via quay**, while Afloat can be at any mooring (including anchorage).
> • Students often **omit the Total Cost of Downtime (TCD) calculation** — the KTU 2024 Scheme rewards quantitative decision-making, not just descriptive comparison.
> • Students forget to cite the **class survey interval** (5 years for bottom survey) — losing 2 marks on completeness.
> • Drawing a **comparison table with at least 6 parameters** is mandatory for full marks in Part B questions on repair regimes.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (CHOICE 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a comparative table, explain the salient differences between **Afloat Repair, Berthed Alongside Repair, and Dry-Dock Repair** of a merchant ship, covering at least six technical parameters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bulk carrier (DWT 90,000) requires propeller blade cropping and underwater stern seal renewal. The vessel's daily off-hire cost is **USD 28,000**. The following repair options are quoted by the shipyard:

| Option | Regime | Cost (USD) | Downtime (days) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Afloat — divers only | 60,000 | 4 |
| 2 | Berthed alongside | 110,000 | 7 |
| 3 | Dry-dock (floating) | 380,000 | 20 |

Calculate the **Total Cost of Downtime (TCD)** for each option and recommend the most economical regime. Justify with reference to class survey obligations.

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

##### (a) Comparative Table [7 marks]

| Parameter | Afloat Repair | Berthed Alongside | Dry-Dock Repair |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Vessel Position** | Moored at berth, anchorage, or buoy | Secured alongside repair quay | In graving/floating dock or on slipway |
| **2. Underwater Access** | Commercial divers only | Divers from quay | Full direct access — hull on blocks |
| **3. Scope of Work** | Above-waterline; minor underwater palliative | Above-waterline; engine/machinery; some underwater via divers | Full hull, propeller, shaft, rudder, sea chests, anodes |
| **4. Class Survey Scope** | In-water survey (IWS) only | Partial survey | Complete Special Survey, Bottom Survey, Tail Shaft Survey |
| **5. Relative Cost Index** | 1.0× | 1.2× | 3.0–5.0× |
| **6. Relative Duration** | 1–7 days | 3–14 days | 7–30 days |
| **7. Shore Support** | Limited; via workboats | Direct crane, power, workshop access | Comprehensive yard support |
| **8. Environmental Risk** | Low | Low | Controlled (gas-free, clean conditions) |
| **9. Typical Examples** | Hull cleaning, anode replacement | Boiler overhaul, engine room work, superstructure repairs | Hull plating renewal, propeller polishing, rudder overhaul |

**(Full marks for at least 6 parameters clearly tabulated with class implications.)**

##### (b) TCD Calculation [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Total Cost of Downtime formula:**

$$
\text{TCD} = C_{\text{repair}} + (D \times C_{\text{offhire}})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute TCD for each option:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCD}_{1} &= 60{,}000 + (4 \times 28{,}000) \\
&= 60{,}000 + 112{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{172{,}000 \text{ USD}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCD}_{2} &= 110{,}000 + (7 \times 28{,}000) \\
&= 110{,}000 + 196{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{306{,}000 \text{ USD}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCD}_{3} &= 380{,}000 + (20 \times 28{,}000) \\
&= 380{,}000 + 560{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{940{,}000 \text{ USD}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recommendation:**

The **lowest TCD is Option 1 (Afloat — Divers) at USD 172,000**.

**Justification with class survey obligations:**

- Propeller cropping and stern seal (stern gland) work is a **mandatory Tail Shaft Survey item** under IRS/LR rules, requiring the propeller to be removed for gauging.
- **Afloat diver work alone is NOT class-approved** for permanent propeller cropping — divers can perform temporary in-situ polishing but cannot certify propeller removal.
- **Berthed alongside** allows shore support but **cannot expose the propeller shaft** for class-approved withdrawal.
- **Dry-dock (Option 3)** is the **only class-acceptable regime** for permanent propeller cropping and stern seal renewal.

**Final recommendation:** Schedule **Option 3 (Dry-dock)** at the next routine docking within the 5-year SS window. If the defect is urgent and the vessel can complete the next voyage safely, perform **Option 1 (Afloat)** as a palliative measure only. **(7 marks)**

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (CHOICE 2)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Emergency Voyage Repair** and **Anchorage Repair**. State their respective operational constraints and typical applications in merchant ship operations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vessel is at a remote anchorage with a damaged sea chest valve requiring underwater emergency closure. Compare the suitability of (i) emergency voyage repair by divers, and (ii) anchorage repair using a floating workshop barge. Recommend the optimum approach using a cost-risk analysis with the following data:

| Parameter | Emergency Voyage Repair | Anchorage Repair |
|---|---|---|
| Repair Cost (USD) | 95,000 | 70,000 |
| Downtime (days) | 3 | 5 |
| Risk Score (0–1) | 0.75 | 0.45 |
| Class Acceptability | Temporary only | Conditional |

Off-hire cost = **USD 18,000/day**. Weights for CRI: $w_1 = 0.4$, $w_2 = 0.4$, $w_3 = 0.2$ (cost overrun, environmental risk, class compliance).

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

##### (a) Emergency Voyage & Anchorage Repair [7 marks]

**Emergency Voyage Repair (EVR):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A temporary, expedited repair performed during voyage or at an unscheduled port call to restore minimum seaworthiness, allowing the vessel to proceed to a proper repair facility.

**Operational Constraints:**
- Limited spares, tools, and skilled manpower at remote ports.
- Time-critical — vessel may be at risk of total loss if repair is delayed.
- Classification society typically issues a **"Condition of Class"** with a time limit (e.g., 3 months) for permanent repair.

**Typical Applications:**
- Main engine lube-oil system failure — temporary bypassing of cooler.
- Hull breach — collision mat or cofferdam installation.
- Steering gear hydraulic leak — temporary seal kit replacement.

**Anchorage Repair (ANR):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Repair carried out while the vessel is at anchor in a sheltered roadstead, using floating workshops, workboats, and crew transported from shore.

**Operational Constraints:**
- Weather dependency — significant wave height limit ≈ 1.0 m for safe barge operations.
- Limited heavy-lift capability compared to a dry-dock.
- Requires port authority permission and customs/immigration clearance for foreign repair teams.

**Typical Applications:**
- Underwater hull cleaning, anode replacement.
- Propeller polishing in-situ by divers.
- Sea chest blanking, overboard valve repairs.
- Minor side-shell plate patching using cofferdams.

**(7 marks)**

---

##### (b) Cost-Risk Analysis [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute TCD for each option:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCD}_{\text{EVR}} &= 95{,}000 + (3 \times 18{,}000) \\
&= 95{,}000 + 54{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{149{,}000 \text{ USD}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCD}_{\text{ANR}} &= 70{,}000 + (5 \times 18{,}000) \\
&= 70{,}000 + 90{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{160{,}000 \text{ USD}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Comparative Risk Index (CRI) with given weights:**

Assuming normalised $R_{\text{cost overrun}}$, $R_{\text{env}}$, $R_{\text{class}}$ are approximated by the given Risk Score, weighted equally for simplicity:

$$
\text{CRI} = 0.4 \cdot R_{\text{cost}} + 0.4 \cdot R_{\text{env}} + 0.2 \cdot R_{\text{class}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CRI}_{\text{EVR}} &= (0.4 \times 0.75) + (0.4 \times 0.75) + (0.2 \times 0.75) \\
&= 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.15 \\
&= \mathbf{0.75}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CRI}_{\text{ANR}} &= (0.4 \times 0.45) + (0.4 \times 0.45) + (0.2 \times 0.45) \\
&= 0.18 + 0.18 + 0.09 \\
&= \mathbf{0.45}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Combined decision score (lower = better):**

$$
\text{Score} = \frac{\text{TCD}}{10^5} + \text{CRI}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{\text{EVR}} &= \frac{149{,}000}{100{,}000} + 0.75 \\
&= 1.49 + 0.75 = \mathbf{2.24}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{\text{ANR}} &= \frac{160{,}000}{100{,}000} + 0.45 \\
&= 1.60 + 0.45 = \mathbf{2.05}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Recommendation:**

**Anchorage Repair (ANR)** is the optimum choice:

- TCD is only USD 11,000 higher (negligible at 6.9% premium).
- **CRI is 40% lower** (0.45 vs 0.75) — significantly safer.
- Anchorage repair with a floating workshop barge can provide a **class-acceptable temporary repair** with proper cofferdam, whereas emergency voyage repair by divers carries higher environmental and class-compliance risk.

**Final recommendation:** **Option (ii) — Anchorage Repair** is recommended on a combined cost-risk basis, with a permanent dry-dock repair scheduled for the next class survey window. **(7 marks)**

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2 — Comparison of Repair Activities)**

- **Five regimes:** Afloat (AFR), Berthed Alongside (BAR), Dry-Dock (DDR), Emergency Voyage (EVR), Anchorage (ANR).
- **DDR is mandatory** every **5 years** for Special Survey / Bottom Survey (per IRS, LR, DNV rules) — cannot be substituted by other regimes.
- **Afloat repair = divers only** for underwater work; above-waterline work possible via staging.
- **Berthed alongside = shore support** (cranes, power, workshops) without dry-docking.
- **Emergency voyage repair = palliative only**; triggers Condition of Class with limited validity.
- **Anchorage repair = floating workshops & barges** at sheltered roadsteads.
- **Cost index (relative):** AFR 1.0× → BAR 1.2× → ANR 1.8× → DDR 3–5× → EVR 4–6×.
- **TCD formula:** $\text{TCD} = C_{\text{repair}} + (D \times C_{\text{offhire}})$.
- **CRI formula:** $\text{CRI} = w_1 R_1 + w_2 R_2 + w_3 R_3 + w_4 R_4$ with weights summing to 1.
- **Decision rule:** Minimise TCD subject to class & statutory constraints.
- **Examples to memorise:** Prestige (2002), Erika (1999) — casualties from inadequate repair regime selection.
- **Examiner expects:** A **comparison table with at least 6 parameters** for full marks on Part B questions.
- **Numerical questions** require TCD computation and class-survey justification, not just cost comparison.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing for Water Tightness and Hull Continuity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Testing for Water Tightness and Hull Continuity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water Tightness** is the property of a ship's hull, bulkheads, decks, tanks, and associated structural boundaries to resist the passage of water under specified hydrostatic or pneumatic pressure conditions, without leakage, seepage, or permanent deformation.

**Hull Continuity** is defined as the uninterrupted structural, metallurgical, and geometric integrity of the hull plating, stiffeners, brackets, and welded/sealed joints, ensuring there are no discontinuities such as cracks, porosity, undercuts, lack of penetration, or structural separations that could compromise strength or sealing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** These tests are governed by Classification Society Rules (IACS members: Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV, KR, IRS, RINA) and SOLAS Chapter II-1 / IACS S21/S22 Unified Requirements. Ship surveyors must certify **watertight integrity** before a vessel is recommissioned after repair.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **leaking steel water tank** in your home. Before you fill it with drinking water, you want to be absolutely sure that:
1. The welded seams will not drip water (this is **water tightness**).
2. There are no hidden cracks, hairline fractures, or pinholes in the metal sheet itself (this is **hull continuity**).

In a ship, these tests are performed in much the same way — we pressurize compartments with water or air and look for leaks, drops, or pressure decay. It is exactly like the **air-pressure test a mechanic performs on a car radiator** to find a small leak, but scaled up to thousands of tonnes of steel plating.

### 1.3 Key Standards and Constants

- **Test fluid:** Fresh water (ρ ≈ **1000 kg/m³**) or sea water (ρ ≈ **1025 kg/m³**)
- **Gravitational acceleration:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard test head:** Varies from **0.3 m to 2.4 m above the top of the tank** (per IACS S21)
- **Standard test pressure for air tests:** Typically **0.02 N/mm² (0.2 bar)** to **0.04 N/mm² (0.4 bar)**
- **Acceptable leakage rate during hose test:** **Zero visible leakage** over **15 minutes** minimum

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Terminology Alert:** In KTU answer scripts, always use the exact phrase — *"Test for watertightness"* (for hydrostatic/hose tests) and *"Test for structural continuity"* (for NDT methods). These are the exact terms used in IACS UR Z17 and Z23.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Water Tightness Tests

The water tightness of a ship's compartments is verified through a **tiered hierarchy of tests**, chosen based on the compartment's location, function, and risk:

| Tier | Test Type | Application | Medium | Typical Pressure |
|------|-----------|-------------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | **Hose Test** | Decks, bulkheads, superstructures | Pressurized water jet | ~ 0.1 MPa (ambient) |
| 2 | **Chalk Test** | Boundary joints, weld seams | Slaked lime/chalk + kerosene | Atmospheric |
| 3 | **Hydrostatic Test** | Tanks, double bottoms, cofferdams | Water filling | ρ·g·h |
| 4 | **Pneumatic (Air) Test** | Void spaces, cofferdams (where water test is impractical) | Compressed air | 0.02–0.04 N/mm² |
| 5 | **Ultrasonic Test (UT)** | Plate integrity, thickness | High-frequency sound | N/A (NDT) |
| 6 | **Dye Penetrant Test (DPT)** | Surface cracks | Dye + developer | N/A (NDT) |

### 2.2 Theoretical Basis — Why Pressure?

The driving principle is **Pascal's Law of Fluid Statics**: any compartment under hydrostatic load experiences a pressure that increases linearly with depth.

For a tank filled with water to a height `h` above its bottom, the pressure at the bottom is given by:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where:
- **P** = Hydrostatic pressure (Pa or N/m²)
- **ρ** = Density of test fluid (kg/m³)
- **g** = Gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- **h** = Head of water above the point of interest (m)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---------------|-------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth `h` | Pa (N/m²) | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $P_{\text{test}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{test}}$ | Required test pressure for a tank | Pa | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $h_{\text{test}} = h_{\text{max service}} + \text{0.3 to 0.9 m}$ | Standard surcharge over service head | m | ★★★★ |
| 4 | $P_{\text{air}} = 0.02$ to $0.04\ \text{N/mm}^2$ | Pneumatic test pressure range | N/mm² | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $\Delta P \le 5\%$ in 15 min | Acceptable air-pressure decay in pneumatic test | — | ★★★ |
| 6 | $V_{\text{leak}} \le 0$ | Acceptable leakage in hose test (zero visible leak) | — | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | $c = \frac{v}{2f}$ | Ultrasonic wave velocity relation (for UT) | m/s | ★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion rule for exam:** $1\ \text{N/mm}^2 = 1\ \text{MPa} = 10\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa}$. Always convert test pressures to **N/mm²** when stating them, as classification society rules use this unit.

### 2.4 Why Hydrostatic Pressure is Used for Tanks (Engineering Utility)

- **Self-loading:** Water's weight naturally pressurizes the structure, simulating worst-case service load.
- **Safety:** Water is incompressible — leaks are slow and safe, unlike air which is compressible and stores energy (rupture risk).
- **Defect magnification:** Even micro-leaks become visible as drips or wet patches on the outer surface.
- **Residual strength check:** Hydrostatic loading simultaneously verifies the **yield and buckling strength** of the compartment.

### 2.5 Why Hull Continuity is Tested Separately (Engineering Utility)

Hull continuity deals with **metallurgical and geometric soundness** — not just leaks. A perfectly water-tight compartment may still have a **sub-surface crack** waiting to propagate under cyclic wave loading. This is why **NDT methods (UT, DPT, MPI)** are mandatory in addition to leakage tests.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world parallel:** In the aviation industry, pressurized fuselages are tested pneumatically (not hydrostatically) because water is too heavy. In shipbuilding, the opposite is true — ships are designed to carry water (ballast, cargo), so **hydrostatic testing is the gold standard**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Test Pressure for a Ballast Tank (Full Worked Example)

**Problem Statement:**
A ballast tank of a bulk carrier has its highest point at **12.0 m** above the keel and its lowest point at **2.0 m** above the keel. The tank is to be tested hydrostatically to the top of the air pipe (located at **14.0 m** above the keel). Sea water is used as the test fluid.

**Step 1 — Identify the test head (h_test):**

The test head is the vertical height of water column above the **bottom of the tank** (lowest point of compartment).

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{\text{test}} &= (\text{Top of air pipe height}) - (\text{Bottom of tank height}) \\
h_{\text{test}} &= 14.0\ \text{m} - 2.0\ \text{m} \\
h_{\text{test}} &= 12.0\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the tank:**

We use sea water density ρ = 1025 kg/m³.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{bottom}} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{test}} \\
P_{\text{bottom}} &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 12.0 \\
P_{\text{bottom}} &= 120{,}651\ \text{Pa} \\
P_{\text{bottom}} &= 120.65\ \text{kPa} \\
P_{\text{bottom}} &= 0.1206\ \text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure at the top of the tank (reference for leakage check):**

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{\text{top}} &= 12.0\ \text{m} - 10.0\ \text{m} = 2.0\ \text{m} \\
P_{\text{top}} &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2.0 = 20{,}110\ \text{Pa} \\
P_{\text{top}} &= 0.0201\ \text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Result Summary:**

| Location | Test Head (m) | Pressure (kPa) | Pressure (N/mm²) |
|----------|---------------|----------------|------------------|
| Bottom of tank | 12.0 | 120.65 | 0.1206 |
| Mid of tank | 6.0 | 60.32 | 0.0603 |
| Top of tank | 2.0 | 20.11 | 0.0201 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for surveyor:** The maximum design stress occurs at the bottom of the tank. The surveyor should visually inspect all welded seams, particularly **butt welds and T-joints at the bottom plates**, for any seepage during the test.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Acceptance Criteria for Pneumatic Test

**Problem Statement:**
A forepeak void space is tested pneumatically at a pressure of **0.04 N/mm²** (40 kPa). After 15 minutes, the pressure drops to **0.0385 N/mm²**. Determine if the test is acceptable per IACS S21.

**Step 1 — Calculate the absolute pressure drop:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= P_{\text{initial}} - P_{\text{final}} \\
\Delta P &= 0.0400 - 0.0385 \\
\Delta P &= 0.0015\ \text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the percentage pressure drop:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \Delta P &= \left(\frac{\Delta P}{P_{\text{initial}}}\right) \times 100 \\
\% \Delta P &= \left(\frac{0.0015}{0.0400}\right) \times 100 \\
\% \Delta P &= 3.75\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with acceptance criterion (≤ 5% in 15 minutes):**

Since 3.75% < 5%, the test **passes** the acceptance criterion.

**Step 4 — Note on safety:**
Even though the test passes numerically, the surveyor must perform a **soapy water spray** on all joints to detect small leaks that may not have caused a measurable pressure drop.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to convert kPa to N/mm² or vice-versa. Always check units **before** substituting into the formula. IACS rules universally use **N/mm²**, not bar or kPa.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Choosing the Right Test

| Compartment Type | Primary Test | Secondary Test | Reason |
|------------------|--------------|----------------|--------|
| **Double bottom tank** | Hydrostatic | UT thickness gauging | High hydrostatic load; check for wastage |
| **Forepeak / Afterpeak** | Hydrostatic or Pneumatic | Hose test at boundaries | Limited access for filling/draining |
| **Cofferdam** | Pneumatic | Hose test | Narrow space, water test impractical |
| **Cargo hold (bulk carrier)** | Hose test | Visual + DPT | Open to atmosphere, not a pressure boundary |
| **Bunker tank** | Hydrostatic | UT | Diesel ingress hazard; structural check |
| **Superstructure deck** | Hose test | Visual | Watertight integrity check, not strength |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key exam tip:** When a question asks *"Suggest a suitable test for..."*, immediately identify whether the compartment is a **pressure boundary** (use hydrostatic/air) or an **open boundary** (use hose test).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Testing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compartment under Survey] --> B{Is it a pressure boundary?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is water filling practical?}
    B -- No --> D[Hose Test on Decks/Bulkheads]

    C -- Yes --> E[Hydrostatic Test]
    C -- No --> F[Pneumatic Air Test]

    E --> G{Leakage observed in 15 min?}
    F --> G

    G -- No Leak --> H[Test PASS - Mark OK]
    G -- Leak Found --> I[Identify defect location]
    I --> J[Repair and Re-test]

    H --> K{Hull continuity also required?}
    J --> K
    K -- Yes --> L[NDT - UT or DPT]
    K -- No --> M[Sign off Survey Report]

    L --> N{Defects found in NDT?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Repair / Renew Plate]
    N -- No --> M
    O --> J
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Start at the top with the compartment to be tested.
- Branch left for pressure boundaries (tanks, cofferdams) and right for non-pressure boundaries (decks, bulkheads).
- All pressure tests end in a **leakage check**, which loops back to repair if failed.
- Hull continuity (NDT) is a **parallel requirement**, not a substitute for water tightness.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hydrostatic Test Setup

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1[Water Source] --> P1[Hydrostatic Pump]
    P1 --> V1[Inlet Valve]
    V1 --> TK[Tank under Test]
    TK --> VG[Pressure Gauge at Bottom]
    TK --> V2[Air Vent at Top]
    TK --> V3[Drain Valve]
    TK --> EXT[External Surface under Inspection]
    EXT --> OBS[Surveyor Visual Check]
    OBS --> DEC{Leakage Found?}
    DEC -- Yes --> REP[Mark and Repair]
    DEC -- No --> SIGN[Sign Hydrostatic Test Certificate]
```

**Functional interpretation:**
- The **pump** delivers water to the tank under controlled rate.
- The **pressure gauge at the bottom** records the maximum hydrostatic pressure.
- The **air vent at the top** ensures complete filling (no trapped air pockets).
- The **surveyor** inspects the **external surface** of the test compartment, including adjacent compartments, for any seepage.

### 4.3 Test Pressure Reference Map (Compartment vs. Test)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph PS[Pressure Boundary Tests]
        T1[Double Bottom Tank] -->|Hydrostatic 0.10-0.20 N/mm²| PASS1[Test Pass]
        T2[Wing Tank] -->|Hydrostatic 0.08-0.15 N/mm²| PASS2[Test Pass]
        T3[Cofferdam] -->|Pneumatic 0.02-0.04 N/mm²| PASS3[Test Pass]
        T4[Forepeak] -->|Hydro or Pneumatic| PASS4[Test Pass]
    end

    subgraph OS[Non-Pressure Boundary Tests]
        T5[Weather Deck] -->|Hose Test 0.1 MPa| PASS5[Test Pass]
        T6[Superstructure Bulkhead] -->|Hose Test| PASS6[Test Pass]
        T7[Watertight Door Frame] -->|Chalk + Hose| PASS7[Test Pass]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual cue for answer scripts:** When drawing diagrams in the KTU exam, always label the **pressure values** on the arrows. The examiner looks for two things: (1) the correct choice of test, and (2) the correct pressure range.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "water tightness" as applied to ship hull. State the standard test methods used for verifying water tightness of a ship's tank.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Water Tightness** is the property of a ship's hull, bulkheads, decks, and tank structures to prevent the ingress or egress of water under specified pressure conditions, without any leakage, seepage, or structural deformation.

The standard test methods for verifying water tightness of a ship's tank are:
1. **Hydrostatic Test** — filling the tank with water to the test head and inspecting for leaks.
2. **Pneumatic (Air) Test** — pressurizing the tank with air and measuring pressure decay.
3. **Hose Test** — directing a pressurized water jet on joints and seams.

(Definition 1 mark, three tests 2 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between "water tightness test" and "hull continuity test". State two NDT methods used for hull continuity.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Water Tightness Test | Hull Continuity Test |
|--------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Objective | Verify absence of leakage | Verify structural/metallurgical integrity |
| Detects | Leaks, seepage, drip-through | Cracks, porosity, lack of fusion, wastage |
| Methods | Hose, Hydrostatic, Pneumatic | UT, DPT, MPI, RT |
| Standard | IACS S21 | IACS S22 / S23 |

**Two NDT methods for hull continuity:**
1. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — uses high-frequency sound waves to detect internal flaws and measure plate thickness.
2. **Dye Penetrant Test (DPT)** — uses liquid dye to reveal surface-breaking cracks and porosity.

(Difference 2 marks, NDT 1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse hydrostatic test pressure with operational pressure. Always add a **safety surcharge** (typically 0.3–0.9 m head above the highest point) to the service head.
> 2. **Do NOT** write "air test" without specifying the pressure in **N/mm²**. The examiner will deduct 1 mark for missing units.
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse IACS S21 (watertightness) with IACS S22 (hull continuity). Mixing them up is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Always** state the **test medium density** (fresh or sea water) explicitly in calculations.
> 5. **Always** include a **safety observation** in long answers — KTU examiners award 1 mark for safety awareness.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Compartment Test Selection

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

(a) **A double bottom ballast tank of an oil tanker is to be tested for water tightness after structural repair work. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the procedure for conducting the hydrostatic test for this tank. List the acceptance criteria.** **(7 marks)**

(b) **A forepeak tank of a bulk carrier cannot be filled with water due to the absence of sounding pipes. Propose a suitable alternative test procedure and state the standard pressure values to be applied. Justify your selection.** **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hydrostatic Test for Double Bottom Tank (7 marks):**

**Procedure:**
1. **Pre-test preparation:** Ensure all structural repair welding is complete, NDT (UT/DPT) has been passed, and the tank is clean and dry. Close all valves, blank off the vents and openings except one air vent.
2. **Filling:** Fill the tank gradually with **sea water** (ρ = 1025 kg/m³) through a calibrated inlet, ensuring air is vented from the top.
3. **Test head:** Fill the tank to a level **0.9 m above the top of the tank** (or to the overflow pipe, whichever is higher), as per IACS S21.
4. **Hold period:** Maintain the test head for a minimum of **15 minutes** (or 30 minutes for class requirements) to allow any leakage to manifest.
5. **Inspection:** The surveyor and shipyard QA team inspect:
   - All welded seams (butt and fillet welds)
   - Sleeve connections, sounding pipe penetrations
   - Adjacent compartments (double bottom tanks are checked for seepage into adjacent tanks)
   - Boundaries at the bilge keel and bracket toes
6. **Recording:** Any leak is marked, photographed, and recorded with location, time, and rate.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- **No visible leakage** through any part of the compartment boundary.
- **No permanent deformation** of plating or stiffeners.
- **No pressure drop** (in case of closed pressurized test).
- All **NDT reports** for the repair welds must be on file and approved.

(Marks distribution: Procedure 4 marks, Sketch 1 mark, Acceptance 2 marks)

---

**(b) Alternative Test for Forepeak Tank (7 marks):**

**Suitable Test: Pneumatic (Air) Test**

**Justification:**
- The forepeak is a **narrow, conical space** at the bow, making complete water filling difficult and slow.
- It is **not normally filled with cargo or ballast** in service, so high hydrostatic loading is not representative.
- Water in the forepeak adds significant weight, which may **overload the forward structural members** during the test.
- An air test is **faster, cleaner, and safer** if properly controlled.

**Procedure:**
1. Seal all openings (manholes, sounding pipes, vent connections) with blank flanges and gaskets.
2. Connect a calibrated **low-pressure air supply** with a pressure regulator and a high-accuracy gauge.
3. Pressurize the forepeak gradually to **0.04 N/mm² (40 kPa)** — the maximum value per IACS S21.
4. Hold the pressure for **15 to 30 minutes**.
5. Spray all boundary joints, weld seams, and penetrations with **soapy water**.
6. Bubbles will appear at any leak point.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- **Pressure decay ≤ 5%** over 15 minutes.
- **No bubble formation** at any joint under soapy water test.
- All NDT reports approved.

**Safety Note:** Pneumatic tests store energy and can be hazardous. The test area must be **barricaded**, and no personnel should enter the compartment during the test.

(Marks distribution: Justification 2 marks, Procedure 3 marks, Pressure values 1 mark, Safety 1 mark)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Hull Continuity and Test Pressure Calculation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

(a) **Define hull continuity. With a neat diagram, describe the ultrasonic thickness measurement procedure for a ship's hull plate. State the formula for sound velocity and identify the factors affecting it.** **(7 marks)**

(b) **A deep ballast tank of length 30 m, breadth 20 m, and depth 12 m is to be tested hydrostatically with fresh water to a head of 1.5 m above the top of the tank. Calculate the test pressure at the bottom of the tank in N/mm² and the total hydrostatic force on the bottom plate.** **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hull Continuity and UT Procedure (7 marks):**

**Definition:**
**Hull continuity** is the property of the hull structure (plating, stiffeners, frames, welds) to be free of metallurgical, geometric, or mechanical discontinuities that may compromise strength, fatigue life, or sealing integrity.

**Ultrasonic Thickness Measurement (UTM) Procedure:**
1. **Surface preparation:** Remove rust, paint, and marine growth from the test area using a grinder to obtain a smooth, clean surface.
2. **Couplant application:** Apply a couplant gel (typically **water-based gel, glycerin, or oil**) on the probe and plate surface to eliminate air gaps.
3. **Probe placement:** Press the **ultrasonic probe (transducer)** firmly against the plate surface.
4. **Calibration:** Use a **calibration block** of known thickness to verify the velocity setting and zero-offset.
5. **Measurement:** The instrument sends an ultrasonic pulse; the **time of flight** is measured and converted to thickness.
6. **Recording:** Multiple readings are taken in a grid pattern (typically **every 5–10 plate intersections** in way of highly stressed areas) and compared with the **original build plate thickness** to compute **wastage %**.

**Formula for Sound Velocity in a Material:**

$$v = 2 \cdot f \cdot c$$

Or, in terms of wavelength:

$$c = f \cdot \lambda$$

Where:
- $c$ = Velocity of sound in the material (m/s)
- $f$ = Frequency of ultrasonic wave (Hz)
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength (m)

**For mild steel:** $c \approx 5920\ \text{m/s}$ (standard reference value).

**Factors affecting sound velocity:**
1. **Material density** — denser materials have higher velocity (in general).
2. **Elastic modulus (E)** — higher E gives higher velocity.
3. **Poisson's ratio (ν)** — affects the velocity.
4. **Temperature** — velocity decreases with rising temperature.
5. **Grain size and microstructure** — coarse grains cause scattering and attenuation.

(Marks: Definition 1, Procedure 3, Diagram 1, Formula + factors 2)

---

**(b) Test Pressure Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Length L = 30 m
- Breadth B = 20 m
- Depth (height of tank) = 12 m
- Surcharge head above top of tank = 1.5 m
- Test fluid: Fresh water, ρ = 1000 kg/m³
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Total test head (h_test):**

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{\text{test}} &= \text{Tank depth} + \text{Surcharge head} \\
h_{\text{test}} &= 12.0 + 1.5 \\
h_{\text{test}} &= 13.5\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Test pressure at the bottom of the tank:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{test}} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{test}} \\
P_{\text{test}} &= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 13.5 \\
P_{\text{test}} &= 132{,}435\ \text{Pa} \\
P_{\text{test}} &= 132.435\ \text{kPa} \\
P_{\text{test}} &= 0.1324\ \text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Area of the bottom plate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= L \times B = 30 \times 20 = 600\ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Total hydrostatic force on the bottom plate:**

The pressure on a horizontal surface equals the pressure at its centroid. For the bottom plate at depth 13.5 m:

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= P_{\text{test}} \times A \\
F &= 132{,}435\ \text{Pa} \times 600\ \text{m}^2 \\
F &= 79{,}461{,}000\ \text{N} \\
F &= 79.461\ \text{MN} \\
F &= 79{,}461\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Result Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Test Head | 13.5 m |
| Test Pressure (bottom) | 0.1324 N/mm² (132.4 kPa) |
| Bottom Plate Area | 600 m² |
| Total Hydrostatic Force | 79,461 kN (≈ 79.46 MN) |

**Note for the surveyor:** The bottom plate experiences a uniform pressure of 132.4 kPa. The local plate thickness must be verified using UT to ensure it can withstand this load with adequate safety margin (typically 1.5–2.0 mm additional corrosion addition on top of the required thickness).

(Marks: Head calculation 1, Pressure 2, Area 1, Force 2, Units 1)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- **Water tightness** = leakage check (hydrostatic, pneumatic, hose test).
- **Hull continuity** = structural integrity check (UT, DPT, MPI, RT).
- **Test pressure formula:** $P = \rho g h$ — never forget this.
- **Standard surcharge head above tank top:** **0.3 m (for fresh water) to 0.9 m (for sea water)**.
- **Pneumatic test pressure range:** **0.02–0.04 N/mm²**.
- **Acceptance for pneumatic test:** Pressure decay **≤ 5%** in 15 minutes.
- **Acceptance for hydrostatic test:** **Zero visible leakage**, no deformation.
- **Speed of sound in mild steel:** $c \approx 5920\ \text{m/s}$ (use this value unless specified).
- **Density values:** Fresh water = 1000 kg/m³; Sea water = 1025 kg/m³.
- **g = 9.81 m/s²** — always.
- **Unit conversion:** $1\ \text{N/mm}^2 = 1\ \text{MPa} = 10\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa}$.
- **Classification society rules to cite:** **IACS S21 (watertightness), S22 (hull continuity), UR Z17 (welding).**
- **Safety in pneumatic test:** Always barricade the area; do not enter the compartment under test.
- **Cofferdams and voids** → pneumatic test (water impractical).
- **Tanks, cargo holds, double bottoms** → hydrostatic test (primary).
- **Decks, superstructure bulkheads, flats** → hose test.
- **Always state ρ, g, h, and final units** in calculations — losing units means losing 1 mark.
- **Drawing neat diagrams** of the test setup earns 1 mark in long answers.
- **Mention NDT (UT/DPT)** as a complementary check, not a substitute for water tightness test.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme has a strong focus on **safety + calculations + IACS standards**. A complete answer must include: (1) the **rule reference** (IACS S21/S22), (2) the **test pressure value with units**, (3) the **acceptance criterion**, and (4) at least one **safety observation**. Missing any of these costs marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repair of Hull and Other Parts While Afloat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Repair of Hull and Other Parts While Afloat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Repair of Hull and Other Parts While Afloat** refers to the comprehensive set of structural, mechanical, and maintenance interventions carried out on a ship's hull, appendages, propulsion machinery, and auxiliary systems while the vessel remains **waterborne in its operational floating condition**, without the need for dry-docking or dry-dock equivalent arrangements such as graving docks, floating docks, or slipways.

This encompasses all forms of corrective and preventive maintenance performed on submerged, waterline, and above-waterline components of a vessel while it is moored at a berth, anchored in a sheltered harbor, or even during transit at slow speeds. The discipline forms a critical sub-branch of **naval architecture and marine engineering practice**, governed by classification society rules (ABS, DNV, LR, BV, IRS), IMO conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL), and flag state regulations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine your car develops a serious oil leak in the engine. Taking it to a garage for a full repair would normally require lifting the car on a hoist. But if you are in the middle of a long highway journey and cannot stop, you might use a temporary sealant or a quick patch to reach the next service station. **Afloat repair is exactly this concept applied to ships** — except the stakes are exponentially higher because the "fluid" is the ocean, the "sealant" must withstand immense hydrostatic pressure, and the "patch" is often applied by commercial divers working in zero-visibility, high-pressure underwater environments.

### 1.3 Why Afloat Repairs Are Necessary

Ships earn revenue only when they are operational. A vessel placed in dry-dock ceases trading immediately. Therefore, afloat repairs are undertaken when:

1. **Commercial Imperative** — The shipping company cannot afford trading loss during a scheduled voyage.
2. **Emergency Situations** — Collision damage, grounding, hull breaches, or machinery failure at sea.
3. **Classification Survey Constraints** — Certain intermediate surveys and bottom inspections can be performed by diving inspection instead of dry-docking (subject to Class approval).
4. **Unavailability of Docking Facilities** — Remote ports without graving/floating docks.
5. **Cost Optimization** — Underwater hull cleaning, anode replacement, and propeller polishing extend the interval between dry-dockings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly stresses the difference between *temporary afloat repairs* (emergency, restore seaworthiness) and *permanent afloat repairs* (durable, Class-accepted solutions that may extend dry-dock intervals). Students must clearly distinguish these in examinations.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Hydrostatic Pressure at 10 m draft:** approximately **1 bar (100 kPa)** or **10.13 m of seawater head**.
- **Sea Water Density:** **1.025 tonnes/m³** (standard reference value for naval architecture calculations).
- **Standard Diving Depth Limit (Surface-Supplied Air):** **50 m** (deeper requires saturation diving systems).
- **Underwater Visibility (typical harbor):** **0.3 – 3 m**; in turbid estuaries: less than **0.1 m**.
- **Corrosion Allowance (mild steel hull):** typically **1 – 3 mm** per side per 5-year cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categorization of Afloat Repairs

Afloat repairs can be systematically classified into the following categories:

#### A. By Urgency

| Category | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Emergency / Temporary** | Immediate response to restore seaworthiness | Collision patch, leak stopping |
| **Voyage Repair** | Short-term fix to complete current voyage | Temporary pipe repair, shaft seal |
| **Permanent / Planned** | Class-approved durable repair while afloat | Cofferdam plate replacement, propeller blade welding |

#### B. By Location on Hull

| Zone | Method | Diver Access |
|------|--------|--------------|
| **Above waterline** | Direct access from deck, staging, or boatswain's chair | No diver required |
| **Boot-top / Waterline** | Floating platform, pontoon staging, or temporary cofferdam | Optional diver |
| **Below waterline (flat bottom)** | Divers with surface-supplied air | Mandatory diver |
| **Bilge / Double bottom tanks** | Internal access via manholes; divers only for external shell plating | Mostly internal |
| **Rudder / Propeller area** | Divers with specialized rigging | Specialist diver team |

### 2.2 Core Engineering Principles

#### 2.2.1 Hydrostatic Pressure Management

When a hull breach occurs, the surrounding sea pressure pushes water into the ship. Any repair must resist this differential pressure. The relationship is:

$$P_{hydrostatic} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

where:
- $P_{hydrostatic}$ = hydrostatic pressure in Pascals (Pa)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = density of seawater = **1025 kg/m³**
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $h$ = depth below waterline in metres (m)

#### 2.2.2 Patch Design Philosophy

An afloat patch must satisfy three simultaneous conditions:

1. **Mechanical Strength** — The patch plate thickness and stiffener spacing must withstand hydrostatic load plus a safety factor (typically **1.5 – 2.5** for temporary, **2.5 – 4.0** for permanent repairs).
2. **Watertight Seal** — Gasket material (rubber, neoprene, or wooden wedges) must compensate for hull curvature irregularities.
3. **Load Transfer** — Forces must be transmitted back to intact hull structure, not just the damaged plate.

#### 2.2.3 The "Why" Behind Cofferdams

A cofferdam is a temporary watertight enclosure built around the damaged hull section. It is pumped dry, allowing workers to enter and execute conventional welding/fitting work in dry conditions. The cofferdam itself is the "patch" — it bears the hydrostatic load while the actual repair is performed inside.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all quantitative formulas critical to solving KTU-level numerical problems on afloat hull repair.

| Formula | Description | Units | Typical KTU Application |
|---------|-------------|-------|--------------------------|
| $P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa (N/m²) | Patch plate thickness calculation |
| $F_{hydro} = P \cdot A$ | Total hydrostatic force on patch | N | Bolt/stud sizing |
| $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_{plate}}$ | Bending stress in patch plate | Pa (N/m²) | Allowable stress check |
| $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{n}$ | Allowable stress using safety factor $n$ | Pa | Permanent repair validation |
| $M = \dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{8}$ | Bending moment (simply supported plate strip) | N·m | Stiffener design |
| $w = P \cdot s$ | Distributed load on stiffener | N/m | Load transfer path |
| $I_{req} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Required moment of inertia | m⁴ | Section modulus check |
| $S_{corr} = \dfrac{t_{nom} - t_{measured}}{t_{nom}} \times 100$ | Corrosion wastage percentage | % | Survey wastage assessment |
| $W_{pump} = A_{leak} \cdot v_{leak} \cdot \rho_{sw}$ | Pump capacity to control flooding | kg/s | Emergency pumping |
| $T_{hold} = \dfrac{V_{flood}}{Q_{pump}}$ | Time to hold water level | seconds | Stability assessment |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Afloat repair methodology underpins:
- **Emergency Response Towing Vessels (ERTVs)** that perform hull stabilization at sea.
- **Military salvage operations** where warships are patched afloat in forward operating bases.
- **Offshore oil & gas** where FPSO hulls and submerged structures are routinely maintained by saturation divers.
- **Commercial shipping** where intermediate Class surveys can be deferred 2.5–5 years via approved diving inspections (per IACS UR Z17).
- **Insurance claim minimization** — temporary afloat repairs allow vessels to reach the nearest repair yard without total loss declaration.

### 2.5 Decision Logic Flow for Afloat vs Dry-Dock Repair

```
STEP 1: Assess damage extent and location
```
