```
    ├── Small damage above waterline → Afloat (simple staging)
    ├── Moderate damage at boot-top → Afloat (pontoon/cofferdam)
    ├── Severe damage below waterline → Decision required
    │       ├── Class survey due? → Prefer Dry-dock
    │       └── Emergency only? → Afloat patch + monitoring
    └── Major structural failure → Dry-dock mandatory
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** A vessel may undertake **permanent afloat repairs** only if the **classification society surveyor** is satisfied that the repair restores the structural integrity to the original Rule standard. Otherwise, the repair is classed as **temporary** and the vessel must proceed to dry-dock within a stipulated period (typically the next scheduled dry-docking or **6 months**, whichever is earlier).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Patch Plate Thickness for Emergency Afloat Repair

**Problem Statement:** A bulk carrier has suffered a shell plate breach of dimensions **1.0 m × 1.5 m** at a depth of **6.0 m** below the waterline. Determine the minimum thickness of a mild steel patch plate (yield strength = **235 N/mm²**) required for an emergency temporary repair using a safety factor of **2.0**.

---

#### Step 1 — Establish the Hydrostatic Pressure

The hydrostatic pressure at the centroid of the patch (located at depth h = 6.0 m):

$$P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

Substituting the standard values:

$$P = 1025 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2 \times 6.0 \;\text{m}$$

$$P = 60,334.5 \;\text{Pa}$$

Converting to N/m² for convenience:

$$P \approx 60.33 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

---

#### Step 2 — Convert Pressure to Distributed Load per Unit Width

For a 1.0 m wide vertical strip of the patch plate (the critical horizontal span = 1.5 m will be analyzed as a simply supported beam):

$$w = P \times 1\;\text{m}$$

$$w = 60.33 \;\text{kN/m}$$

---

#### Step 3 — Maximum Bending Moment in the Plate Strip

The patch plate strip (length L = 1.5 m) is conservatively treated as a simply supported beam under uniform load. Maximum bending moment at mid-span:

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{60.33 \times (1.5)^2}{8} = \dfrac{60.33 \times 2.25}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{135.74}{8} = 16.97 \;\text{kN·m}$$

---

#### Step 4 — Convert to Allowable Bending Stress

$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{n}$$

$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{235 \;\text{N/mm}^2}{2.0} = 117.5 \;\text{N/mm}^2$$

Converting to consistent units:

$$\sigma_{allow} = 117.5 \times 10^6 \;\text{N/m}^2 = 117.5 \;\text{MPa}$$

---

#### Step 5 — Apply Section Modulus Equation

For a rectangular plate of unit width and thickness $t$, the section modulus is:

$$Z = \dfrac{b \cdot t^2}{6} = \dfrac{(1.0) \cdot t^2}{6}$$

The bending stress equation:

$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z}$$

Solving for $t$:

$$t^2 = \dfrac{6 \cdot M_{max}}{b \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$$

---

#### Step 6 — Numerical Substitution

$$t^2 = \dfrac{6 \times 16,970 \;\text{N·m}}{1.0 \;\text{m} \times 117.5 \times 10^6 \;\text{N/m}^2}$$

$$t^2 = \dfrac{101,820}{117.5 \times 10^6}$$

$$t^2 = 8.665 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2$$

$$t = \sqrt{8.665 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02944 \;\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 29.4 \;\text{mm}}$$

---

#### Step 7 — Practical Selection

Standard shipbuilding plate thicknesses: **6, 8, 10, 12, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40 mm**. The next standard size above 29.4 mm is **30 mm**. Therefore, a **30 mm thick mild steel patch plate** is selected.

Additionally, the patch would be reinforced with stiffeners at approximately **400 mm spacing** to reduce plate deflection and improve sealing against the curved hull.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Afloat Repair Techniques

| Parameter | Underwater Welding | Cofferdam Method | Soft Patch (Wooden/Mattress) | Concrete Box | Submarine Rescue Pod |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|------------------------------|--------------|----------------------|
| **Pressure Resistance** | Up to 50 m depth | Unlimited (if pumped) | Up to ~10 m | Up to ~15 m | N/A |
| **Permanent/Temporary** | Either | Permanent possible | Temporary | Temporary | N/A |
| **Diver Skill Required** | High (welder-diver) | Medium | Low | Medium | N/A |
| **Material Cost** | High | Very High | Low | Medium | N/A |
| **Time to Execute** | 8–24 hours | 3–7 days | 4–12 hours | 2–3 days | N/A |
| **Class Approval** | Yes (with limits) | Yes | Limited | Limited | N/A |
| **Weather Sensitivity** | Medium | Low | High | Medium | N/A |
| **Crew Risk Level** | High | Medium | Low | Medium | N/A |

### 3.3 Derivation: Flooding Rate Estimation

**Problem Statement:** A container ship develops a hole of effective area **0.05 m²** at a depth of **4.0 m**. Estimate the rate of flooding assuming the orifice is fully submerged.

---

#### Step 1 — Apply Torricelli's Theorem

For a submerged orifice, the efflux velocity is:

$$v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 4.0} = \sqrt{78.48} = 8.86 \;\text{m/s}$$

---

#### Step 2 — Compute Volumetric Flow Rate

$$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot v$$

Assuming discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.62$ for a sharp-edged orifice:

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.05 \times 8.86$$

$$Q = 0.275 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

---

#### Step 3 — Convert to Mass Flow Rate

$$\dot{m} = Q \cdot \rho_{sw}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.275 \times 1025 = 281.9 \;\text{kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 282 \;\text{kg/s} \approx 1015 \;\text{tonnes/hour}}$$

---

#### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation

This flooding rate is **catastrophically high**. An emergency bilge pump rated at **200 m³/hr** would only manage **73%** of this inflow. Therefore:
- **Immediate action**: Internal subdivision (close watertight doors, isolation valves).
- **External action**: Apply soft patch or commence cofferdam construction.
- **Pumping strategy**: Multiple pumps in parallel; consider progressive flooding of designated compartments to maintain GM (transverse stability).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical: Corrosion Wastage Acceptance

**Problem Statement:** A surveyor's ultrasonic thickness measurement on a bottom plate gives an average reading of **18.2 mm**. The original as-built thickness was **22.0 mm** with a corrosion allowance of **3.0 mm**. Determine whether the plate is acceptable under typical Class rules.

---

#### Step 1 — Calculate Substantial Thickness

The original plate thickness minus the corrosion allowance:

$$t_{substantial} = t_{nom} - t_{corr\_allow}$$

$$t_{substantial} = 22.0 - 3.0 = 19.0 \;\text{mm}$$

---

#### Step 2 — Compare with Measured Thickness

Measured $t_{meas} = 18.2 \;\text{mm}$ which is less than $t_{substantial} = 19.0 \;\text{mm}$.

**Conclusion:** The plate does NOT meet substantial thickness requirements.

---

#### Step 3 — Calculate Wastage Percentage

$$\%\,wastage = \dfrac{t_{nom} - t_{meas}}{t_{nom}} \times 100$$

$$\%\,wastage = \dfrac{22.0 - 18.2}{22.0} \times 100 = 17.27\%$$

---

#### Step 4 — Typical Class Allowable Wastage

Most classification societies permit up to **20–25% wastage** in bottom plating before mandatory renewal. At **17.27%**, the surveyor may:
- Accept with **annual re-survey** condition (if rules permit),
- Or recommend **insert plate renewal** during the next dry-docking.

This kind of numerical reasoning is **frequently tested** in KTU Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Afloat Repair Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel Hull Damage Reported] --> B{Is Vessel Afloat?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Assess Damage Severity}
    B -- No --> Z[Proceed to Dry Dock]
    C --> D{Damage Above or Below Waterline?}
    D -- Above --> E[Direct Staging Repair]
    D -- Below --> F{Damage Depth in m}
    F -- Less than 5 --> G[Soft Patch or Divers]
    F -- 5 to 15 --> H[Cofferdam or Plate Patch]
    F -- More than 15 --> I[Saturation Divers + Welding]
    G --> J[Class Surveyor Inspection]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    E --> J
    J --> K{Acceptable as Permanent?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Issue Class Repair Certificate]
    K -- No --> M[Temporary Repair + Schedule Dry Dock]
    L --> N[Vessel Returns to Service]
    M --> N
    Z --> O[Dry Dock Repair Procedure]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Emergency Patch Application Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Damage Assessment by Diver] --> S2[Step 2: Surface Cleaning of Hull]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Backing Plate Positioned by Rigging]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Wooden Mattress or Soft Patch Pre-fitted]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Diver Aligns Patch to Damage]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Tensioning Bolts and Studs]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Hydraulic Tightening]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Leak Test by Pumping]
    S8 --> S9{Leak Stopped?}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Class Survey and Document]
    S9 -- No --> S11[Reposition and Repeat]
    S11 --> S6
    S10 --> S12[Voyage Resumed]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of an Afloat Repair System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Survey and Assessment Module]
        A1[Diving Inspection]
        A2[UT Thickness Gauging]
        A3[Photographic Documentation]
    end
    subgraph B[Engineering Design Module]
        B1[Patch Sizing Calculation]
        B2[Stress Analysis]
        B3[Classification Society Approval]
    end
    subgraph C[Mobilization Module]
        C1[Dive Support Vessel]
        C2[Material Supply Barge]
        C3[Crane and Rigging]
        C4[Welding Plant]
    end
    subgraph D[Execution Module]
        D1[Surface Preparation]
        D2[Patch Fabrication Onshore]
        D3[Underwater Positioning]
        D4[Fastening and Sealing]
        D5[Welding or Bolt-up]
    end
    subgraph E[Validation Module]
        E1[Leak Testing]
        E2[Class Survey]
        E3[Documentation and Certification]
    end
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F[Return to Service]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Cross-Section of a Typical Cofferdam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Damage on Shell Plate] --> B[Cofferdam Plate Welded to Hull]
    B --> C[Watertight Gasket Between Hull and Dam]
    C --> D[Pumping Port for Dewatering]
    D --> E[Workmen Enter Dry Space]
    E --> F[Conventional Welding Performed Internally]
    F --> G[Reinstatement of Hull Plating]
    G --> H[Cofferdam Cut Free After Class Approval]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Underwater Welding Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Surface Cleaning by Brush or Grinder] --> B[Pre-weld Inspection]
    B --> C[Set Up Diver-Welder with Ground Cable]
    C --> D[Establish Communication with Surface Tender]
    D --> E[Wet Welding or Dry Chamber Welding]
    E --> F[Continuous Visual Monitoring by TV Camera]
    F --> G[Post-weld NDT if Required]
    G --> H[Class Surveyor Acceptance]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define "Repair While Afloat" as applied to ship hull maintenance. Mention two situations where this is preferred over dry-docking.*

**Model Answer:**

Repair while afloat refers to the execution of structural, mechanical, or routine maintenance work on a ship's hull and associated systems without removing the vessel from the water. It is performed using divers, staging, cofferdams, or shore-based cranes with the vessel moored or anchored.

Two situations where it is preferred:
1. **Emergency damage control** — collision or grounding where the vessel cannot proceed to a docking facility without interim sealing.
2. **Commercial urgency** — when the cost of trading loss during dry-docking outweighs the cost of afloat repair, particularly for routine anode replacement, propeller polishing, or class-condition rectification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*What is a cofferdam in the context of hull repair? Why is it used for permanent repairs afloat?*

**Model Answer:**

A **cofferdam** is a temporary watertight enclosure constructed around a damaged section of hull plating, which is then pumped dry to allow workmen to enter and perform conventional welding or plate renewal in dry conditions.

It is used for permanent repairs afloat because:
1. The actual welding/fabrication is done in dry conditions, ensuring weld quality comparable to dry-dock work.
2. It allows full Class-surveyor inspection of the repaired joint in dry, well-lit conditions.
3. It restores the original hull strength without compromising classification requirements.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various techniques employed for **repair of hull and other parts while afloat**. Discuss the role of divers, cofferdams, soft patches, and underwater welding in such operations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vessel suffers a rectangular shell plate breach of dimensions **0.8 m (horizontal) × 1.2 m (vertical)** at a depth of **7.5 m** below the load waterline. Determine the minimum thickness of a mild-steel emergency patch plate using a safety factor of **2.0** against a yield strength of **235 MPa**. Use standard seawater density and gravitational acceleration. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The following techniques are employed for repair of hull and other parts while afloat:

**1. Diving Inspection and Survey**
Divers conduct close visual, photographic, and ultrasonic thickness measurement (UTM) surveys of the hull, appendages, propeller, and rudder. They identify damage, corrosion hotspots, anode condition, and seabed grounding marks.

**2. Soft Patch / Collision Mattress**
A wooden plank mattress (typically teak or pitch pine) weighted and lowered over the damage from the deck, tightened by chains or wire lashings. It is a **temporary measure** suitable for emergency leak stopping.

**3. Steel Patch Plate with Bolts and Gasket**
A pre-cut steel plate is positioned over the damage by divers, with a neoprene or rubber gasket. Through-bolts tensioned hydraulically compress the gasket against the hull curvature, providing a robust temporary or semi-permanent seal.

**4. Cofferdam Method**
A welded or bolted watertight enclosure is built around the damaged area, then pumped dry. Workmen enter the dry chamber and perform welding/fitting work in conditions similar to a dry-dock.

**5. Underwater Welding**
Hyperbaric wet welding (in water) or dry chamber welding (in a habitat with 1 atm pressure) performed by certified diver-welders. Wet welding is faster but lower quality; dry chamber welding meets permanent Class requirements.

**6. Cement Box or Tremie Concrete**
A box is lowered over the damage and filled with quick-setting cement or epoxy grout via a tremie pipe. Used where welding is unsafe (e.g., near fuel tanks).

**Role of Each Method:**

| Technique | Role |
|-----------|------|
| Divers | Inspection, positioning, fastening, welding |
| Cofferdam | Enables permanent repair in dry conditions |
| Soft patch | Rapid emergency leak control |
| Underwater welding | Permanent structural restoration |
| Concrete box | Where hot work is hazardous |

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Breach dimensions: $b = 0.8$ m, $L = 1.2$ m
- Depth below waterline: $h = 7.5$ m
- Yield strength: $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa
- Safety factor: $n = 2.0$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Hydrostatic Pressure**

$$P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$P = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 7.5 = 75,418 \;\text{Pa} = 75.42 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Distributed Load per Metre Width**

Considering a unit-width vertical strip:

$$w = P \times 1 = 75.42 \;\text{kN/m}$$

The horizontal span to be bridged = 1.2 m.

**Step 3: Maximum Bending Moment (simply supported)**

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{8} = \dfrac{75.42 \times (1.2)^2}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{75.42 \times 1.44}{8} = \dfrac{108.6}{8} = 13.575 \;\text{kN·m}$$

**Step 4: Allowable Stress**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{235}{2.0} = 117.5 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 5: Required Thickness**

$$t^2 = \dfrac{6 \cdot M_{max}}{b \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$$

$$t^2 = \dfrac{6 \times 13,575}{1.0 \times 117.5 \times 10^6}$$

$$t^2 = \dfrac{81,450}{117.5 \times 10^6} = 6.93 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2$$

$$t = 0.02633 \;\text{m} = 26.33 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 6: Select Standard Plate**

Choose the next standard thickness: **30 mm mild steel plate**.

$$\boxed{t_{required} = 26.33 \;\text{mm (theoretical)}, \quad t_{selected} = 30 \;\text{mm (standard)}}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the **planning, safety, and regulatory considerations** for executing afloat hull repairs on a commercial vessel. Mention the role of classification societies and flag state authorities. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the procedure for **underwater wet welding repair** of a cracked shell plate. List two limitations of this technique. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Planning Considerations:**

1. **Damage assessment** — divers survey extent, location, depth, surrounding structure.
2. **Repair methodology selection** — emergency patch, cofferdam, or permanent welding.
3. **Material logistics** — plate, studs, gaskets, welding plant transported to vessel.
4. **Weather window** — sea state, current, visibility must permit safe diving.
5. **Stability check** — cofferdam weight, flooded compartments; GM and GZ curves verified.
6. **Schedule coordination** — alongside berth availability, port authority clearances.

**Safety Considerations:**

1. **Diver safety** — decompression limits, surface-supplied air, standby diver.
2. **Hot work permits** — gas-free measurements in adjacent compartments.
3. **Fire watch** — fire extinguishers and water spray during welding.
4. **Electrical safety** — proper grounding of welding leads.
5. **Confined space entry** — atmosphere testing before cofferdam entry.
6. **Communication** — diver-to-surface umbilical and radio link.

**Regulatory Considerations:**

1. **Classification Society (ABS, DNV, LR, BV, IRS)** — must approve the repair procedure, supervise the work, and issue repair certificate. Rules per IACS UR Z17 govern in-water surveys.
2. **Flag State Administration** — issues statutory certificates (SOLAS, MARPOL) and may witness repairs affecting safety.
3. **Port State Control** — local maritime authority permits the work.
4. **ISM Code** — ship's Safety Management System must include procedure for emergency repairs.
5. **P&I Club** — insurer appoints surveyor for claim-related assessments.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Procedure for Underwater Wet Welding Repair of Cracked Shell Plate:**

1. **Pre-Dive Preparation**
   - Conduct gas-free measurement of internal compartments.
   - Set up surface-supplied air, umbilical, communication.
   - Position welding plant (DC generator or rectifier) on deck with proper grounding to hull.

2. **Surface Preparation by Diver**
   - Clean the cracked region using pneumatic chipper, wire brush, or grinder.
   - Remove marine growth, rust, paint, and damaged metal back to sound parent plate.

3. **Crack Preparation**
   - Use underwater cutting torch or grinder to vee-out the crack.
   - Provide a minimum included angle of **60°** and root gap of **2–3 mm** for weld penetration.

4. **Electrode Selection**
   - Use rutile-coated waterproof electrodes (e.g., AWS E6013 modified).
   - Keep electrodes dry in a heated container until use.

5. **Welding Sequence**
   - Diver-welder strikes arc underwater.
   - Welds in short beads (50–75 mm), allowing slag to float off.
   - Each pass cleaned with chipping hammer before next pass.

6. **Post-Weld Inspection**
   - Visual inspection by diver with photography.
   - Magnetic particle inspection (MPI) on accessible areas.
   - Class surveyor witnesses the work.

7. **Documentation**
   - Weld log, electrode batch records, diver's report, Class endorsement.

**Two Limitations of Underwater Wet Welding:**

1. **Reduced weld quality** — rapid cooling causes hydrogen embrittlement and porosity; mechanical properties are typically only **70–80%** of dry-weld equivalents.
2. **Limited depth and visibility** — diver performance degrades beyond **30 m**; turbid water restricts weld pool control and inspection accuracy.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Repair While Afloat" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing temporary vs permanent repair** — Examiners award only 1 of 2 marks if students fail to clearly state that permanent repairs require Class approval and may need dry-dock verification later.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply a safety factor** — In numerical patch-thickness questions, students often compute $t$ directly from yield without dividing by $n$. This loses 2 marks immediately.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion** — Mixing mm and m in bending-stress formulas is the most common arithmetic error. Always keep units consistent.
> 4. **Skipping the corrosion allowance** — When asked about wastage, students frequently forget to subtract the corrosion allowance before comparing with substantial thickness.
> 5. **Ignoring diver safety** — In descriptive answers, examiners specifically look for "decompression," "surface-supplied air," and "standby diver" keywords.
> 6. **No mention of IACS UR Z17** — In regulatory questions, citing the IACS procedural standard for in-water surveys demonstrates depth and earns bonus marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Afloat Repair** = repair with vessel waterborne; no dry-dock required.
- **Reasons**: commercial, emergency, Class survey deferral, remote location.
- **Categories**: emergency/temporary, voyage repair, permanent/planned.
- **Zones**: above waterline, boot-top, below waterline, bilge, rudder/propeller.
- **Hydrostatic Pressure Formula**: $P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ with $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³.
- **Bending Moment for Patch Plate**: $M = \dfrac{w L^2}{8}$ (simply supported).
- **Patch Thickness**: $t = \sqrt{\dfrac{6M}{b \sigma_{allow}}}$.
- **Allowable Stress**: $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$, with $n = 1.5$–$2.5$ temporary, $n = 2.5$–$4.0$ permanent.
- **Corrosion Wastage %**: $\dfrac{t_{nom} - t_{meas}}{t_{nom}} \times 100$; Class limit typically 20–25%.
- **Flooding Rate (Torricelli)**: $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gh}$.
- **Techniques**: divers, soft patch, steel plate with bolts, cofferdam, underwater welding, cement box.
- **Cofferdam** = welded/bolted watertight enclosure pumped dry for permanent repair.
- **Underwater Welding** = wet (fast, lower quality) or dry chamber (slower, Class-acceptable).
- **Safety**: gas-free measurement, fire watch, surface-supplied air, standby diver, communication.
- **Regulators**: Classification Society (ABS, DNV, LR, BV, IRS), Flag State, Port State, P&I Club.
- **IACS UR Z17** governs in-water survey procedures and limits.
- **Diving Depth Limit (air)**: ~50 m; saturation diving required beyond.
- **Standard Densities**: seawater = 1025 kg/m³, freshwater = 1000 kg/m³.
- **Standard $g$**: 9.81 m/s² (use 9.8 if problem specifies).
- **Plate thickness standards** (mm): 6, 8, 10, 12, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Underwater Welding - Welding Equipment, Quality Control and Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Underwater Welding - Welding Equipment, Quality Control and Standards # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Underwater Welding (UW)** is a specialised fusion welding process performed in a submerged or partially submerged aqueous environment, used primarily for the fabrication, repair, and maintenance of marine, offshore, and hydro-structural assets such as ship hulls, pipelines, jetties, oil platforms, and lock gates. According to **AWS D3.6M:2017 (Underwater Welding Code)**, underwater welding is classified into two principal categories: - **Wet Welding** — Performed directly in the surrounding water using waterproof electrodes and specialised power delivery systems, with the weld pool in direct contact with the aqueous medium. - **Dry Welding** — Performed inside a sealed chamber (habitat) from which water is displaced by a gas mixture (typically helium-oxygen or argon), creating a hyperbaric or one-atmosphere dry working environment around the weld joint. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The course PENST861 treats underwater welding as a *ship repair* technology, so emphasis is placed on **wet welding** for emergency afloat repairs and **hyperbaric dry welding** for class-approved permanent repairs. Definitions, code clauses, and electrode classifications drawn from **AWS D3.6M** and **IACS UR Z17** are examinable. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are trying to **light a candle inside a swimming pool**. The water immediately cools the flame, snuffs out the heat, and creates bubbles (gas pockets) inside the wax. Now, if you could build a **transparent acrylic box** around the candle, pump out the water, and fill the box with breathing air, the candle would burn normally again. Underwater welding faces the *exact same problem*: - **Wet welding** = The "candle in the pool" approach. It works, but is messy, hot, fast-cooling, and full of trapped gas bubbles (porosity). It is the *first-aid of ship hulls* — quick, dirty, but seaworthy. - **Dry welding (Hyperbaric)** = The "acrylic box" approach. You build a small habitat over the damaged plate, evacuate the water, and weld in a near-normal dry condition. It is the *hospital surgery of ship hulls* — slow, expensive, but class-approved. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value (Bolded for Recall) | |---|---| | Hydrostatic pressure increase | **0.101 MPa per 10 m depth** (≈ **1 atm / 10 m**) | | Water thermal conductivity | **0.6 W/m·K** (≈ 25× that of air) | | Typical wet-weld cooling rate | **>100 °C/s** (vs ~20 °C/s in air) | | Maximum practical wet-weld depth | **~50 m** (Class B, AWS D3.6M) | | Hyperbaric welding pressure range | **0.1 MPa – 2.5 MPa** (1 – 25 bar) | | Open-circuit voltage (OCV), wet | **55 – 80 V DC** (anti-sticking) | | Standard reference electrode | **AWS E6013, E7014, E8018-C3** (for wet) | > [!NOTE] > **Why these numbers matter:** Because water conducts heat away **~25 times faster than air**, the weld metal solidifies in milliseconds. This is the root cause of *hardness, brittleness, and hydrogen cracking* in wet welds — the entire quality control regime exists to manage this single physical fact. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Equipment Architecture — The Five Subsystems Underwater welding equipment is best understood as **five interconnected subsystems**. Each subsystem addresses a specific environmental constraint imposed by the aquatic medium. ### Subsystem A — Power Source - **Diesel-driven DC welding generator** (most common afloat): rated 300–400 A, 60–80 V OCV, constant-current (CC) characteristic. - **Inverter-based rectifiers** (modern rigs): lighter, more portable, better arc stability, arc-force control. - Must be **insulated and drip-proof** (IP23S minimum, IP44 preferred for ship-deck mounting). - Earth/ground return through the **ship's hull or a sacrificial ground clamp** — never through the diver's umbilical. ### Subsystem B — Welding Cables (Welding Leads) - **Electrode holder cable (stinger lead):** Heavy-duty welding cable, typically **50 mm² (1/0 AWG)** cross-section, double-insulated with neoprene or EPR sheath, **water-resistant and abrasion-resistant**. - **Work lead (ground cable):** Bolted directly to the hull plate adjacent to the repair zone; cable length minimised to reduce voltage drop. - **Length compensation:** Voltage drop ΔU = ρ·L·I/A must not exceed **4% of arc voltage** (ISO 17652). - Special **strain-relief boots and twist-lock connectors** prevent water ingress at joints. ### Subsystem C — Electrode & Electrode Holder - **Waterproof ("wet") electrodes** have a **waterproof coating** (often cellulose + iron powder + rutile) over the mild-steel core wire. Standard diameters: **3.2 mm, 4.0 mm, 5.0 mm**. - **Class AWS A5.1** for mild steel; **Class AWS A5.4** for stainless-steel wet welding (e.g., E308L-16, E316L-16). - **Electrode holder (stinger):** Fully insulated, rated ≥400 A, with a "waterproof pistol-grip" design and locking jaw to prevent the electrode from being ripped out by current drag. ### Subsystem D — Diver's Personal Equipment - **KMB (Kisfaludy-Mills Block) or hot-water suit** for cold-water operations. - **Diving helmet / band mask** with communication, light, and *underwater camera mount*. - **Positive-pressure demand breathing system** — never free-flow, because free-flow exhaust bubbles disrupt arc stability and weld-pool shielding. - **Dive umbilical:** Contains hot water hose, breathing gas hose, comms cable, pneumo hose, and a *separate* welding current return path is **NOT** permitted in modern standards. ### Subsystem E — Drying & Pre-/Post-Weld Tools - **Hydraulic grinder, needle scaler, and underwater chipping hammer** for surface preparation. - **Underwater power pack** (hydraulic or electric) with RCD (Residual Current Device) protection at 30 mA sensitivity. - **Drying torch** (oxy-propane) for drying the joint before welding. - **Underwater NDT kit:** MPI yoke, UT thickness gauge, video endoscope. ## 2.2 Quality Control Framework Quality control in underwater welding is structured around three pillars defined by **AWS D3.6M**: | Pillar | Document | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Welder Qualification (Performance)** | WPS + Welder Performance Qualification Test (WPQT) | Proves the individual diver-welder can deposit sound welds | | **Procedure Qualification** | Procedure Qualification Record (PQR) | Proves the procedure (electrode, amps, volts, speed) produces a sound joint | | **Inspection & NDT** | Visual + NDT (MPI, UT, RT) on coupon + production welds | Confirms the actual in-service weld meets acceptance criteria | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "1G / 2G / 3G / 4G" position code:** Because the diver is upside-down, sideways, or vertical, position qualification is critical. A 3G (vertical-up) wet weld qualification is the most demanding and is mandatory for ship-side shell plating repairs. ## 2.3 Standards & Classification Society Rules | Standard / Rule | Issuing Body | Scope | |---|---|---| | **AWS D3.6M:2017** | American Welding Society | The *primary* code for underwater welding (Class A = hyperbaric, Class B = wet) | | **ASME IX** | ASME | Welder & procedure qualification (often cross-cited with AWS D3.6M) | | **API 2X** | American Petroleum Inst. | Offshore structural fabrication | | **ISO 15614-1, ISO 9606-1** | ISO | Procedure & welder qualification | | **EN 1090 / EN ISO 17652** | CEN | European execution of steel structures | | **IACS UR Z17** | IACS | Unified Rules for welding — adopted by **DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV, KR, ClassNK** | | **IS 10104** | BIS (India) | Recommended practice for underwater welding | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula | LaTeX | Application | |---|---|---| | Heat Input | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm | Predicts HAZ hardness, distortion, and dilution | | Voltage Drop in Cable | $\Delta U = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L \cdot I}{A}$ | Cable sizing & length limits | | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Determining dry-habitat gas pressure for hyperbaric welding | | Cooling Rate (approximate, thick plate) | $R = \dfrac{2\pi k \rho c (T_c - T_0)^3}{H^2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(T - T_0)^2}$ | Used to estimate HAZ hardness via HRC/HSLC correlation | | Critical Hardness (Class B wet welds) | $\le \mathbf{350 \text{ HV}_10}$ (per AWS D3.6M) | Acceptance criterion for weld metal hardness | | Depth–Pressure Conversion | $1 \text{ m seawater} \approx 0.101 \text{ bar} \approx 14.7 \text{ psi / 10 m}$ | Quick field reference | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In a ship-repair drydock context, the heat-input formula is the single most-used equation. For every additional 0.5 kJ/mm of heat input, the HAZ grain-coarsening tendency rises non-linearly, increasing the risk of **hydrogen-assisted cracking (HAC)** — the most common failure mode in wet welds. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Why Heat Input Governs Quality Starting from the first law of thermodynamics for a moving heat source, the heat input per unit length of weld is the energy delivered per second (VI) divided by the travel speed S. Multiplying by 60 converts minutes to seconds and dividing by 1000 converts joules to kilojoules. $$\begin{aligned} H_{\text{volumetric}} &= V \cdot I \quad [\text{Volt-Ampere} = \text{Watt}] \\[4pt] H_{\text{per length}} &= \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{W}}{\text{mm/s}} = \text{J/mm}\right] \\[4pt] \text{Converting to kJ/mm} \rightarrow H &= \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right] \\[4pt] \text{Numerical Check: } V &= 25 \text{ V}, \ I = 180 \text{ A}, \ S = 150 \text{ mm/min} \\[4pt] H &= \dfrac{25 \times 180 \times 60}{150 \times 1000} \\[4pt] H &= \dfrac{270\,000}{150\,000} \\[4pt] H &= 1.8 \text{ kJ/mm} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** A wet weld at 1.8 kJ/mm is in the *upper acceptable band* for AWS D3.6M Class B. Exceeding 2.0 kJ/mm risks excessive dilution from the base metal and unacceptable HAZ softening in high-strength hull steel (e.g., AH36, DH36). ## 3.2 Derivation — Hydrostatic Pressure at the Weld Site When designing a hyperbaric habitat, the gas pressure inside the chamber must equal the external water pressure to prevent structural collapse or blow-out. $$\begin{aligned} P_{atm} &= 101\,325 \text{ Pa} \quad (\approx 0.101 \text{ MPa}) \\[4pt] \rho_{\text{seawater}} &= 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3 \quad (\text{salinity 3.5\%}) \\[4pt] g &= 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \\[4pt] \text{For a working depth } h &= 15 \text{ m:} \\[4pt] P_{gauge} &= \rho g h \\[4pt] P_{gauge} &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 15 \\[4pt] P_{gauge} &= 150\,828.75 \text{ Pa} \\[4pt] P_{gauge} &\approx 0.151 \text{ MPa} \ (\approx 1.5 \text{ bar gauge}) \\[4pt] P_{abs} &= P_{atm} + P_{gauge} \\[4pt] P_{abs} &= 0.101 + 0.151 = 0.252 \text{ MPa abs} \end{aligned}$$ **Engineering Conclusion:** The habitat must be pressurised to **2.52 bar absolute** using a suitable Heliox or Argon-Oxygen breathing mix. Divers undergo compression/decompression cycles per the *Diving Operations At Work Regulations* and equivalent maritime codes. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Welding Defects & Their Root Causes | Defect | Root Cause | Detection Method | Acceptance Limit (AWS D3.6M Class B) | |---|---|---|---| | **Porosity** | Gas entrapment from water dissociation (H₂, O₂) and steam | Visual, RT, UT | Distributed porosity ≤ 5% of weld area | | **Undercut** | Excessive current, wrong angle | Visual | Depth ≤ 0.5 mm, length ≤ 25 mm continuous | | **Lack of Fusion** | Water cooling, low heat input, poor technique | UT, MPI, macroscopic | Not permitted | | **Slag Inclusion** | Entrapped weld-pool oxide film | RT, UT, MPI | None permitted in Class A; ≤ 2 mm single in Class B | | **Hydrogen Cracking (HAC)** | Diffusible H from cellulose coating + rapid cooling → martensite | MPI after 48 h, hardness testing | No cracks permitted | | **Excessive Hardness** | Rapid cooling → martensite formation | Portable Vickers / HRC | ≤ **350 HV₁₀** in weld metal, ≤ **350 HV₁₀** in HAZ | | **Incomplete Penetration** | Diver in awkward position, root gap too tight | UT, RT | Not permitted on full-penetration welds | ## 3.4 Step-by-Step Wet Welding Procedure (KTU Board-Exam Format) 1. **Survey & Mark-up:** Clean marine growth within 150 mm of the weld zone; identify defect boundaries using MPI. 2. **Excavation:** Grind out the defect to sound metal using a hydraulic grinder; provide a 60–70° included angle groove. 3. **Drying:** Oxy-propane torch or hot air to remove surface moisture; surface temperature ≥ 40 °C before striking. 4. **Earth Clamp:** Attach ground lead within 0.5 m of the weld zone to minimise stray-current paths. 5. **Electrode Selection:** **AWS E7014 (rutile-iron powder) for general hull plate; E8018-C3 for DH36; E308L-16 for austenitic stainless pipework.** 6. **Parameter Setting:** Polarity **DCEN (electrode positive)**; current **130–180 A for 4.0 mm electrode**; arc length **2–4 mm**. 7. **Strike & Deposit:** Use a **scratch-start technique** (not touch-and-lift) to avoid sticking; weave pattern width ≤ 2.5× electrode diameter; maintain weave within the groove footprint. 8. **Interpass Cleaning:** Each pass chipped with needle scaler; never start the next pass on slag. 9. **Cap & Inspection:** Final cap ground flush; visual + MPI; record location, time, depth, electrode batch. > [!NOTE] > **"Scratch-start" vs "Touch-start":** In air you tap, but underwater you scratch because the water film prevents instant arc establishment. The scratching action displaces water locally and ionises the gap, allowing the arc to strike. This is a high-yield KTU viva question. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Underwater Welding System ```mermaid flowchart TB PWR[Power Source
DC Generator 300A]:::pwr SWC[Switch & Fuse Box
IP44 IP23S]:::elec OC[Output Cable
50 mm sq Neoprene]:::cable STG[Electrode Holder
Stinger Pistol Grip]:::tool ELCT[Waterproof Electrode
AWS E7014 4.0mm]:::tool ARC[Weld Arc & Pool
In-Water]:::arc GRD[Ground Clamp on Hull
0.5m from Weld]:::elec PWR --> SWC --> OC --> STG --> ELCT --> ARC GRD -.return path.-> SWC ARC -.heat into.-> PLATE[Ship Hull Plate]:::hull DIVER[Wet Diver
Hot Water Suit]:::div UMB[Dive Umbilical
Comm + Gas]:::div SUP[Topside Supervisor
Dive Control]:::div SUP --> UMB --> DIVER --> ARC NDT[NDT After Weld
MPI UT Visual]:::ndt ARC --> NDT ARC --> HARD[Hardness Test
350 HV10 limit]:::ndt classDef pwr fill:#fde2e1,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000 classDef elec fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#b9770e,color:#000 classDef cable fill:#d6f0ff,stroke:#2471a3,color:#000 classDef tool fill:#e8daff,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000 classDef arc fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#ba4a00,color:#000 classDef hull fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000 classDef div fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 classDef ndt fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Quality Control Decision Tree — Defect Response ```mermaid flowchart TD W[Completed Wet Weld]:::weld V{VT Visual
Acceptable?}:::gate M{MPI Test
Cracks?}:::gate U{UT Test
Internal Defects?}:::gate H{Hardness Test
350 HV10 OK?}:::gate A1[Accept & Release]:::ok A2[Grind & Re-weld]:::repair A3[Engage Class Surveyor]:::consult A4[Full Excavation & Re-qualify]:::repair A5[Stress Relief if Required]:::consult W --> V V -- Yes --> M V -- No --> A2 M -- No Cracks --> U M -- Cracks Found --> A2 U -- No Defects --> H U -- Defects > Limit --> A4 H -- Pass --> A1 H -- Fail --> A3 A3 --> A5 classDef weld fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000 classDef gate fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#b9770e,color:#000 classDef ok fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000 classDef repair fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000 classDef consult fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hyperbaric Dry Welding Habitat ```mermaid flowchart LR HAB[Hyperbaric Habitat
Steel Chamber]:::ch GAS[Heliox HeO2 Supply
Pressure matched to depth]:::ch WLD[Welder Inside
at Atmospheric Pressure]:::wld ELC[Electrode Feed
Through Port]:::wld LOCK[Entry Lock
Crew Transfer]:::ch DC[Topside DC Source
Cable Through Gland]:::sup MON[TV & Comm Monitor
Surface Controller]:::sup HAB --> WLD --> ELC GAS --> HAB LOCK --> HAB DC -- Power --> ELC MON -- Comms --> WLD classDef ch fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 classDef wld fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000 classDef sup fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid for the diagrams:** Pink nodes = welding action; Blue nodes = pressure/environment management; Yellow nodes = NDT/Quality decisions. In a KTU board answer, drawing even an approximate version of the Quality Control decision tree can earn full method marks even if a sub-detail is missed. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **List any three essential safety precautions that must be observed by a diver-welder while performing wet welding on a ship's side shell plating.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Electrical Isolation:** The diver must be electrically insulated from the water circuit; the ground lead is bolted to the hull plate *not* the diver's umbilical; all topside equipment must have an RCD (30 mA) in the circuit. 2. **Breathing Gas Integrity:** Use a *positive-pressure demand* helmet; never a free-flow system, because exhaust bubbles disturb the arc and can extinguish shielding, plus a leak could cause implosion / gas loss. 3. **Pre-Dive Checks:** Communication check, electrode holder insulation integrity test, ground lead resistance check, oxygen analyser on breathing supply, and stand-by diver briefed with recovery plan. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **Compare wet welding and dry (hyperbaric) welding with respect to (i) typical application, (ii) weld quality, and (iii) cost.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Wet Welding | Hyperbaric Dry Welding | |---|---|---| | Application | Emergency afloat hull repair, temporary patches | Permanent class-approved repair, offshore structures | | Weld Quality | Lower; high porosity, possible HAC | Comparable to in-air welding (Class A, AWS D3.6M) | | Cost | Low; portable equipment | High; habitat + chamber + gas mix + divers + topside crew | --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In Part B answers, students frequently lose 2–3 marks by (a) drawing bare schematics without labels, (b) forgetting to mention the **HV₁₀ = 350 limit** explicitly, and (c) confusing **AWS Class A** (hyperbaric) with **Class B** (wet). Always state the Class first, then the criteria. ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe, with a neat sketch, the procedure for wet welding of a cracked plate on a ship's side shell plating. Include equipment, surface preparation, electrode selection, and parameter settings. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the quality control procedure for underwater welds as per AWS D3.6M Class B. List the acceptance criteria for porosity, undercut, hardness, and cracks. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Equipment Required:** - 300 A DC welding generator (diesel-driven, IP23S). - 50 mm² neoprene-sheathed electrode and work cables. - Hydraulic grinder (1800 rpm) with grinding disc, needle scaler. - Oxy-propane drying torch. - Underwater electrode holder (pistol-grip, 400 A rated). - Waterproof electrodes — **AWS E7014, 4.0 mm** for mild-steel hull plate (or **E8018-C3** for DH36). - Hot-water diving suit, band mask, comms, hot-water umbilical. - Topside dive supervisor, stand-by diver, paramedic. **Procedure (Step-Wise):** 1. **Survey:** Visual and MPI to mark crack tips; drill stop-holes (Ø 8–10 mm) at each tip to arrest crack propagation. 2. **Surface Prep:** Remove all marine growth, paint, rust, and mill scale within 150 mm of the groove using grinder; achieve St 2.5 / St 3 finish. 3. **Groove Excavation:** Grind a 60–70° V-groove to a depth reaching 2–3 mm below the crack root, leaving a clean root face of 1.5–2 mm. 4. **Drying:** Sweep oxy-propane flame over the groove until the surface is visibly dry and at ≥ **40 °C** (touch test). 5. **Set-Up:** Connect the ground clamp to the hull within **0.5 m** of the weld zone; check insulation of the electrode holder. 6. **Parameters:** Polarity **DCEN** (electrode positive), current **140–180 A** for 4.0 mm electrode, arc length **2–4 mm**, travel speed **100–150 mm/min** (heat input 1.5–2.0 kJ/mm). 7. **Welding:** Use a *scratch-start* technique; maintain a slight weave (≤ 2.5 × d) within the groove; root pass first, then fill, then cap. 8. **Interpass:** Chip slag between passes; do not start next pass on cold or wet slag film. 9. **Cap & Inspect:** Cap the groove slightly proud, then grind flush; perform VT + MPI. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Quality Control as per AWS D3.6M Class B:** 1. **Welder Qualification:** Each diver must weld a test plate in the same position (typically 3G / 4G) as the production weld. Two test plates are subjected to **radiographic, macro, and hardness tests** to confirm the welder's skill. 2. **Procedure Qualification Record (PQR):** A WPS (Welding Procedure Specification) is qualified by a test weld; essential variables (electrode type, diameter, current range, polarity, travel speed) are recorded and locked. 3. **Production Inspection:** - **Visual Testing (VT):** 100 % of welds; check for surface undercut, crater cracks, irregular bead profile. - **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI):** After 48 h delay (to allow any HAC to surface); 100 % of critical welds. - **Ultrasonic Testing (UT):** For internal defects; calibration on a reference block of known thickness. - **Hardness Testing:** Portable Vickers (HV₁₀) at weld metal, HAZ, and base metal; maximum permissible **350 HV₁₀** in both weld metal and HAZ (Class B). 4. **Acceptance Criteria:** | Defect | Limit | |---|---| | Porosity | Distributed porosity ≤ **5 %** of weld area; piping porosity not permitted | | Undercut | Depth ≤ **0.5 mm**; total length ≤ 25 mm in any 300 mm length | | Cracks (any) | **Not permitted** | | Hardness | Weld metal & HAZ ≤ **350 HV₁₀** | | Lack of fusion | **Not permitted** in full-penetration welds | | Slag inclusion | ≤ 2 mm single, ≤ 4 mm cumulative per 100 mm length | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a schematic, describe the **hyperbaric dry welding** method. State the gas mixture used, the operating pressure range, and the depth limit for hyperbaric welding. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **standards and classification society rules** governing underwater welding. What is the role of the IACS UR Z17 procedure? --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Schematic (Block Description):** A steel pressure chamber (habitat) is mounted over the repair area on the hull. The chamber is sealed to the hull using inflatable seals or welded skirts. Water inside is displaced by introducing a **breathing gas mixture** through a supply port. The diver-welder enters via an entry lock (a smaller mating chamber) without flooding the main habitat. Cables for the electrode, video, and lighting pass through sealed glands. The welder works in a near-atmosphere of 1 bar (or elevated pressure, depending on habitat design). **Gas Mixture:** Primarily **Heliox (He–O₂)** or **Hydreliox (He–N₂–O₂)** at 1–25 bar. Helium is used because nitrogen at depth causes nitrogen narcosis ("rapture of the deep"), and pure oxygen is toxic at partial pressures above 1.6 bar. For habitats pressurised to 1 atm (one-atmosphere chamber), a normal He–O₂ mix or even air is used, but a one-atm habitat cannot be used below ~30 m without physiological problems for the crew. **Operating Pressure Range:** - **One-atmosphere habitat:** **0.1 MPa abs** (1 bar) — limited to shallow depths. - **Pressurised habitat (true hyperbaric):** **0.1 MPa – 2.5 MPa abs** (1 – 25 bar) — for deeper work down to ~250 m. **Depth Limit:** Practically **~250 m** for manned hyperbaric welding; beyond that, ROV-based dry welding or mechanical clamping is preferred. **Welding Process:** Usually **GMAW (Gas Metal Arc Welding)** with a local dry shield of argon around the arc inside the chamber; or **SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding)** with low-hydrogen electrodes (e.g., E7016). The arc environment is dry, so quality is similar to in-air welding and meets **AWS D3.6M Class A** (hyperbaric) acceptance criteria. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Standards Governing Underwater Welding:** 1. **AWS D3.6M:2017** — The most widely cited standard; defines Class A (dry / hyperbaric) and Class B (wet) welds; specifies welder, procedure, and inspection requirements. 2. **ASME Section IX** — Welder and procedure qualification; often used in conjunction with AWS D3.6M for offshore platform repairs. 3. **API 2X** — Specific to offshore structural fabrication and repair. 4. **EN ISO 17652** — European standard for underwater welding execution. 5. **ISO 15614-1** — Procedure qualification; ISO 9606-1 — Welder qualification. 6. **IACS UR Z17** — Unified Requirement on welding; adopted by major class societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV, ClassNK, KR, RINA) to harmonise welding standards for classed vessels. **Role of IACS UR Z17:** - Establishes **uniform weld quality criteria** for new construction and repair, irrespective of which class society issues the class certificate. - Defines **welder qualification tests, procedure qualification ranges, and acceptance criteria** (impact toughness, hardness, NDT scope) for hull structural steel. - Mandates **WPS, PQR, and WPQ** documentation chains. - For underwater welding specifically, IACS UR Z17 cross-references **AWS D3.6M** and requires that any permanent in-service weld on a classed vessel be supported by a qualified WPS, a qualified welder, and approved NDT records. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Two big families:** **Wet** (cheap, fast, lower quality) vs **Dry/Hyperbaric** (expensive, slow, class-quality). - **Heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm — the single most important QC number. - **Hardness ceiling:** ≤ **350 HV₁₀** for Class B welds — this is the acceptance pass/fail line. - **Polarity:** **DCEN** (Electrode Positive) for wet welding to maximise penetration and reduce porosity. - **Electrodes to memorise:** E6013 (rutile), E7014 (rutile-iron powder — most common wet electrode), E7018 (basic low-hydrogen), E8018-C3 (for DH36 hull steel), E308L-16 / E316L-16 (stainless wet welding). - **Striking:** Always **scratch-start** in water; never tap-start. - **Pressure rule of thumb:** **1 bar per 10 m** of seawater depth. - **Drying temperature:** Weld zone ≥ **40 °C** before striking. - **NDT chain:** **VT → MPI (after 48 h) → UT → Hardness** — always in this order. - **Code of authority:** **AWS D3.6M** (Class A / B) is the reference document; **IACS UR Z17** is the class-society harmonisation rule. - **Key defects to recall:** Porosity, undercut, lack of fusion, slag inclusion, hydrogen cracking (HAC), excess hardness. - **Inspection delay:** MPI for HAC detection must be delayed **at least 48 hours** after welding to allow any delayed cracking to manifest. - **Diver insulation:** Welding current must **never** return through the diver's umbilical; a dedicated ground clamp on the hull is mandatory. - **Cable sizing:** Voltage drop ≤ **4 %** of arc voltage; use **50 mm²** neoprene-sheathed cable as a working reference.

topics.title: Degree of Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Degree of Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Automation (DoA)** in marine engineering is the **quantitative and qualitative measure** of the extent to which a vessel's machinery operations, navigation, cargo handling, and safety systems are controlled, monitored, and managed by **automated systems** with **minimal or zero human intervention**, as classified by international classification societies and the International Maritime Organization (IMO).

The Degree of Automation is officially classified by major classification societies (Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas, ABS, ClassNK) using standardized notations that define the manning levels in the **Engine Control Room (ECR)** and **Bridge** during operational and emergency conditions.

### 1.2 Standard Classification Society Notations

| Notation | Full Form | Manning Requirement |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|
| **M0** | Periodically Unmanned | Periodic attendance by engine room staff |
| **M1** | Unmanned for limited duration | Short unmanned periods (e.g., night) |
| **M2** | Unmanned with bridge control | Continuous bridge control, periodic ECR visits |
| **M3** | Unmanned with ECR attended | Engine room attended continuously |
| **UMS** | Unmanned Machinery Space | Fully unmanned operation |
| **AUT-0** | Automated with manned ECR | ECR continuously attended |
| **AUT-1** | Periodically Unmanned | Periodic unmanned operation |
| **AUT-2** | Unmanned Machinery Space | Full UMS capability with bridge control |
| **AUT-3** | Integrated Automation | Full ship-wide integrated systems |

> [!NOTE]
> The IMO Resolution **A.601(15)** – *Provision and Display of Manoeuvring Information on Board Ships* and the **SOLAS Chapter II-1** regulations govern automation standards. Classification societies then implement these through their own rule notations.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – The "Self-Driving Car" Equivalent

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of automation in ships like **autonomous driving in cars**.
> - **Degree 0 (Manual):** You drive everything, hands on wheel, foot on pedals — like an old ship where the engineer manually checks every gauge and valve.
> - **Degree 1 (Assisted):** Cruise control maintains speed — equivalent to **bridge control** where the officer starts/stops engines remotely but monitors from the ECR.
> - **Degree 2 (Partial Automation):** Auto-pilot + adaptive cruise — similar to **AUT-1** where the system handles routine operations but needs human override.
> - **Degree 3 (Full Automation):** Self-driving Tesla — equivalent to **AUT-2/UMS** where the ship virtually runs itself with sensors, alarms, and remote diagnostics.

In simple terms, **Degree of Automation = "How much of the ship can think and act for itself without a human babysitter?"**

## 1.4 Physical & Operational Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard Bridge Watch Cycle:** 4 hours on / 8 hours off
> - **UMS Maximum Unmanned Duration:** Up to **16–24 hours** (per class rules)
> - **IMO Minimum Manning Document threshold:** Set by flag state administration
> - **Response time to alarm (critical):** **≤ 30 seconds** (per class rules)
> - **Fail-safe principle:** **De-energize to actuate** is mandatory for safety circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Automation

Marine automation is structured in **three primary layers**, each contributing to the overall Degree of Automation:

### Layer 1: Local Control Level
- Individual component control (pumps, valves, compressors)
- Operated locally at the equipment
- Equivalent to **Degree 0**

### Layer 2: Remote/Group Control Level
- Centralized control from a local panel or Engine Control Room (ECR)
- Includes **mimic diagrams**, **selector switches**, and **start/stop push buttons**
- Equivalent to **Degree 1 / M3 notation**

### Layer 3: Integrated Automation Level
- Computer-based monitoring (SCADA / IAS – Integrated Automation System)
- Bridge control, remote diagnostics, condition monitoring
- Equivalent to **Degree 2 / AUT-2 / UMS**

## 2.2 Components Contributing to Degree of Automation

| S.No | System / Subsystem | Function | Automation Level Contribution |
|------|-------------------|----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Main Engine Remote Control (M/E RC) | Start, stop, reversal from bridge/ECR | High |
| 2 | Auxiliary Machinery Monitoring | Pumps, generators, purifiers, compressors | High |
| 3 | Alarm & Monitoring System (AMS) | Centralized fault indication | Critical |
| 4 | Power Management System (PMS) | Generator start/stop, load sharing | High |
| 5 | Fire Detection & Fire Fighting | Smoke/heat sensors, CO₂ release | High |
| 6 | Bilge Monitoring System | Tank levels, OWS operation | High |
| 7 | Navigation Automation | ECDIS, GPS, AIS, autopilot | Very High |
| 8 | Cargo Control Room (CCR) | Tank gauging, IG systems, valves | High (Tankers) |
| 9 | HVAC & Accommodation | Climate control | Medium |
| 10 | Condition Monitoring (CMS) | Vibration, lube oil analysis, thermography | High |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although automation is largely a systems engineering topic, certain quantifiable metrics are testable. Key formulae are summarized below.

$$
\text{Automation Index (AI)} = \frac{N_{\text{auto}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $N_{\text{auto}}$ = Number of automated functions / systems
- $N_{\text{total}}$ = Total number of controllable functions

$$
\text{Manning Reduction Factor (MRF)} = \frac{M_{\text{conventional}} - M_{\text{automated}}}{M_{\text{conventional}}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $M_{\text{conventional}}$ = Crew size in conventional (manual) ship
- $M_{\text{automated}}$ = Crew size in automated ship

$$
\text{Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)} = \frac{\sum t_{\text{uptime}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}
$$

$$
\text{System Availability} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Reliability Function: } R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = Failure rate (failures per hour)
- $t$ = Mission time

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE:** When copying these formulae in your answer sheet, **do not use the absolute value bars `|x|` in tables** — use parentheses `(x)` or write "absolute x" to avoid markdown rendering issues.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

The Degree of Automation directly impacts:
- **Operational Cost:** Higher DoA → Smaller crew → Lower manning cost (~30–50% savings)
- **Safety:** Reduced human error in hazardous machinery spaces
- **Efficiency:** Optimized fuel consumption via PMS and integrated systems
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Required for modern SOLAS-compliant vessels
- **Survey & Repair Implications:** Automated systems require **annual surveys** and **class-approved software updates** — directly relevant to PENST861 (Ship Survey, Estimation & Repair)

> [!TIP]
> In **ship repair estimation**, automation retrofit projects typically add **15–25% to the dry-dock cost** but reduce **operational expenditure (OPEX)** significantly over the vessel's life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating the Automation Index

**Problem:** A container vessel has the following machinery systems:
- 1 Main Engine (remote controlled)
- 3 Diesel Generators (auto start/stop, PMS enabled)
- 2 Main Cooling Pumps (auto)
- 2 Lub Oil Pumps (auto)
- 1 Steering Gear (auto + follow-up)
- 1 Bow Thruster (bridge control)
- 1 Air Compressor (manual local start)
- 1 Fuel Oil Purifier (semi-automatic)

Out of **12 total machinery functions**, **10 are fully automated**, and **2 are semi-automatic (counted as 0.5 each)**.

**Step 1:** Identify total controllable functions.

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 12
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate effective number of automated functions.

$$
N_{\text{auto}} = 10 + (2 \times 0.5) = 10 + 1 = 11
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Automation Index formula.

$$
\text{AI} = \frac{11}{12} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{AI} = 0.9167 \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{AI} = 91.67\%}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.
An Automation Index of **91.67%** qualifies the vessel for **AUT-2 / UMS** class notation, provided all safety systems (fire detection, bilge alarm, etc.) are also class-compliant.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Manning Reduction Factor Calculation

**Problem:** Compare crew size of a conventional bulk carrier vs. a fully automated bulk carrier.

- Conventional vessel crew: $M_{\text{conventional}} = 25$ persons
- Automated (UMS) vessel crew: $M_{\text{automated}} = 15$ persons

**Step 1:** Substitute values into the MRF formula.

$$
\text{MRF} = \frac{25 - 15}{25} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator.

$$
\text{MRF} = \frac{10}{25} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3:** Final calculation.

$$
\text{MRF} = 0.40 \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MRF} = 40\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** A fully automated bulk carrier requires **40% fewer crew** than a conventional one, translating to substantial OPEX savings over a 20–25 year operational life.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: System Availability for an Automation System

**Problem:** An Integrated Automation System (IAS) has:
- MTBF = 8,000 hours
- MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) = 50 hours

**Step 1:** Apply the availability formula.

$$
A = \frac{8000}{8000 + 50} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the denominator.

$$
A = \frac{8000}{8050} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3:** Final result.

$$
A = 0.9938 \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 99.38\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Classification societies typically mandate a **minimum availability of 99.0%** for the entire integrated automation system, including redundancy. Any value below this requires **corrective action during the annual class survey**.

## 3.4 Decision Matrix: Selecting the Right Degree of Automation

| Ship Type | Recommended DoA | Notation | Manning Level |
|-----------|-----------------|----------|---------------|
| Bulk Carrier (small) | Degree 1 | M3 / AUT-0 | ECR attended |
| Bulk Carrier (large) | Degree 2 | AUT-1 | Periodically unmanned |
| Container Ship (ULCV) | Degree 3 | AUT-2 / UMS | Unmanned machinery space |
| Oil Tanker (VLCC) | Degree 3 | AUT-2 / UMS + CCR | Unmanned + Cargo Control Room |
| LNG Carrier | Degree 3 | AUT-2 + IG automation | Unmanned + cryogenic control |
| Naval Warship | Degree 2 | Custom (Navy specs) | Damage control centralized |
| Passenger Ferry | Degree 2 | AUT-1 | High safety redundancy |
| Offshore Supply Vessel | Degree 2 | AUT-1 + DP | Dynamic positioning automated |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Ship Automation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BRIDGE CONTROL] --> B[Integrated Automation System IAS]
    A1[ECDIS GPS AIS] --> A
    B --> C[Main Engine Remote Control]
    B --> D[Power Management System PMS]
    B --> E[Alarm Monitoring System AMS]
    B --> F[Fire Detection System]
    B --> G[Bilge and OWS Monitoring]
    C --> C1[Engine Order Telegraph EOT]
    C --> C2[Shaft Generator Control]
    C --> C3[Propulsion Shaft Monitoring]
    D --> D1[Diesel Generator 1]
    D --> D2[Diesel Generator 2]
    D --> D3[Emergency Generator Auto Start]
    D --> D4[Load Sharing and Blackout Recovery]
    E --> E1[Engine Room Mimic Panel]
    E --> E2[Accommodation Alarm Repeater]
    E --> E3[Bridge Alarm Display]
    F --> F1[Smoke and Heat Detectors]
    F --> F2[CO2 Release System]
    F --> F3[Fire Pump Auto Start]
    G --> G1[Bilge Well Level Sensors]
    G --> G2[Oily Water Separator]
    G --> G3[Sludge Tank Monitoring]
    H[CARGO CONTROL ROOM CCR] --> B
    H --> H1[Tank Gauging System]
    H --> H2[Inert Gas IG System]
    H --> H3[Cargo Valve Remote Control]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Classification Society Decision Tree for DoA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Ship Owner Submits Plans] --> Q{Is Bridge Control Fitted}
    Q -->|Yes| R{Is Machinery Space Unmanned Capable}
    Q -->|No| S[Class Notation M0 or M1]
    R -->|Yes| T{Is IAS Computer Based}
    R -->|No| U[Class Notation AUT0 or M3]
    T -->|Yes| V[Class Notation AUT2 UMS]
    T -->|No| W[Class Notation AUT1 Periodically Unmanned]
    V --> X[Annual Survey of AMS PMS and Safety Systems]
    W --> X
    U --> Y[Continuous ECR Manning Required]
    S --> Y
    X --> Z{Maintenance Records Complete}
    Y --> AA[Watchkeeping Engineer on Duty 24x7]
    Z -->|Yes| AB[Class Endorsement Maintained]
    Z -->|No| AC[Class Suspension and Rectification Required]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture of Integrated Automation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor Level] --> S2[Signal Conditioning and I/O Modules]
    S2 --> S3[PLCs and Remote I/O]
    S3 --> S4[Operator Workstations in ECR]
    S3 --> S5[Operator Workstations on Bridge]
    S3 --> S6[Alarm Printer and Event Logger]
    S4 --> S7[Database Server]
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Condition Monitoring Module]
    S7 --> S9[Power Management Module]
    S7 --> S10[Trend and Historical Data Module]
    S8 --> S11[Class Survey Report Generator]
    S9 --> S11
    S10 --> S11
    S11 --> S12[Annual Class Survey Submission]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The lowest layer (sensors) feeds data to the highest layer (class survey reports). Each arrow represents **data flow direction** in the IAS architecture.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Degree of Automation. Name the different class notations used by Lloyd's Register for automation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Degree of Automation (DoA)** is the measure of the extent to which a ship's machinery and operational systems are controlled and monitored automatically, with minimal or no human intervention, as certified by classification societies (Ref: SOLAS Ch. II-1, IMO Res. A.601(15)).

**Lloyd's Register notations for automation:**
1. **M0** – Machinery space with watchkeeping engineers
2. **M1** – Periodically unmanned machinery space
3. **M2** – UMS with bridge control
4. **AUT-0** – Automated with manned ECR
5. **AUT-1** – Periodically unmanned
6. **AUT-2** – UMS with bridge control
7. **UMS** – Unmanned Machinery Space (notation suffix)

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always mention the **rule reference (e.g., SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 46)** along with the definition to score full marks in 3-mark questions.

---

### Question 2: List any six components that contribute to the Degree of Automation of a modern ship.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The six key components contributing to the Degree of Automation are:
1. **Main Engine Remote Control (M/E RC)** from the bridge
2. **Power Management System (PMS)** for generator start/stop
3. **Alarm and Monitoring System (AMS)** with mimic panels
4. **Fire Detection and Fire Fighting System** with auto CO₂ release
5. **Bilge Monitoring and Oily Water Separator (OWS)** control
6. **Navigation Automation** (ECDIS, AIS, Autopilot)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Warning:** Do NOT list navigation systems alone as the "only" automation. Examiners expect a **balanced answer covering machinery, safety, and navigation** systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three hierarchical levels of automation** in a modern merchant ship with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of a fully automated (UMS) ship, with reference to **manpower, safety, and cost** implications. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question A:

#### Part (a) – Three Hierarchical Levels (7 Marks)

**Level 1: Local Control (Degree 0)**
- Equipment is operated **manually at the local level**
- Examples: Hand-operated valves, locally started pumps, manual bilge pumping
- Human intervention: 100% required
- Found in: Small vessels, old ships, emergency backup systems

**Level 2: Group/Remote Control (Degree 1)**
- Equipment is controlled from a **local group control panel or Engine Control Room (ECR)**
- Examples: Aux. machinery start/stop from ECR, generator control panel
- Mimic diagram representation
- Human intervention: Partial (operator present in ECR)

**Level 3: Integrated/Computer-Based Automation (Degree 2/3)**
- Full **Integrated Automation System (IAS)** with PLCs, SCADA, and operator workstations
- Bridge control of main propulsion
- Power management, condition monitoring, remote diagnostics
- Human intervention: Minimal (only for alarms and critical decisions)
- Example: UMS bulk carrier with 15-person crew

$$
\text{Control Level Hierarchy: } L_0 \subset L_1 \subset L_2 \subset L_3
$$

#### Part (b) – Advantages & Disadvantages of UMS Ships (7 Marks)

| Aspect | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| **Manpower** | 30–50% crew reduction (MRF = 40% typical) | Loss of traditional seamanship skills |
| **Safety** | Reduced human exposure to hazardous ER; faster alarm response | Single-point sensor failure can cascade |
| **Cost** | Lower OPEX (wages, victualling) | Higher CAPEX (15–25% more build cost) |
| **Survey & Repair** | Trend analysis enables predictive maintenance | Specialized technicians required; expensive spares |
| **Operational** | 24/7 unmanned operation; precise load sharing | Crew fatigue if not properly trained; over-reliance on systems |

**Conclusion:** A well-designed UMS ship with **redundant sensors**, **properly trained crew**, and **class-approved maintenance** is **safer and more economical** than a conventional manned ship.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes to Avoid in 14-mark answers:**
> 1. **Don't skip the equations or numerical examples** — at least one worked example adds 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Don't write generic answers** — always tie back to **SOLAS, class rules, or IMO references**.
> 3. **Don't forget the disadvantage side** — examiners mark both sides equally.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the **architecture of an Integrated Automation System (IAS)** on a modern vessel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A modern container ship has **15 machinery functions**, of which **12 are fully automated** and **1 is semi-automated**. The conventional crew size for a similar ship is **30**, and the automated ship has a crew of **18**. Calculate the **Automation Index** and the **Manning Reduction Factor**. Comment on the class notation the vessel would qualify for. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question B:

#### Part (a) – IAS Architecture (7 Marks)

The **Integrated Automation System (IAS)** consists of the following hierarchical layers (refer to Mermaid Diagram in Section 4.3):

1. **Sensor/Field Level:** Temperature, pressure, level, flow, vibration sensors
2. **Signal Conditioning and I/O Modules:** Convert analog/digital signals
3. **PLCs and Remote I/O Units:** Logic processing and control
4. **Operator Workstations (HMI):** Located in ECR and Bridge
5. **Alarm Printers and Event Loggers:** Records all faults and operator actions
6. **Database Server:** Stores trends, historical data, maintenance logs
7. **Specialized Software Modules:** PMS, CMS, Trend Analysis
8. **Class Survey Report Module:** Generates compliance reports

**Network:** All subsystems connected via **redundant Ethernet ring (TCP/IP)** or **fieldbus (Profibus/Modbus)** to ensure **high availability (≥ 99%)**.

#### Part (b) – Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate Automation Index (AI).**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 15
$$

$$
N_{\text{auto}} = 12 + (1 \times 0.5) = 12.5
$$

$$
\text{AI} = \frac{12.5}{15} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{AI} = 83.33\%}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Manning Reduction Factor (MRF).**

$$
\text{MRF} = \frac{30 - 18}{30} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{MRF} = \frac{12}{30} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MRF} = 40\%}
$$

**Step 3: Class Notation Comment.**

With an Automation Index of **83.33%** and a 40% crew reduction, the vessel qualifies for the **AUT-1 (Periodically Unmanned)** class notation. To qualify for **AUT-2 / UMS**, the AI must exceed **90%** and all safety-critical systems (fire detection, bilge alarm, emergency shutdown) must be **class-certified with redundancy**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **AUT-1** with **AUT-2**. Remember: **AUT-1 = periodically unmanned**, **AUT-2 = fully UMS with bridge control**. A 7-mark numerical answer without a **class notation commentary** loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam:**

- ✅ **Degree of Automation (DoA)** = Quantitative measure of automated control in a ship
- ✅ **IMO Reference:** SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 46–53 governs automation
- ✅ **LR Notations:** M0, M1, M2, AUT-0, AUT-1, AUT-2, UMS
- ✅ **DNV Notations:** EO (Engine Operated), E0, E1, E2, E3
- ✅ **Three Layers:** Local → Remote → Integrated (IAS)
- ✅ **Key Formulae:** AI = (N_auto / N_total) × 100%, MRF, MTBF, Availability
- ✅ **Automation Index ≥ 90%** → Qualifies for AUT-2 / UMS
- ✅ **Manning Reduction:** Typically 30–50% in UMS ships
- ✅ **System Availability:** Must be ≥ 99% for class compliance
- ✅ **Survey Relevance:** IAS requires **annual survey** and **5-yearly class survey** under specific rule chapters
- ✅ **Cost Impact:** Automation adds **15–25% to build cost** but reduces **OPEX by 30–40%**
- ✅ **Common Mistakes:** Confusing AUT-1 and AUT-2, omitting SOLAS reference, skipping disadvantage side
- ✅ **Key Suffix in Class Notations:** UMS, ACCU (Accredited Machinery), BRC (Bridge Remote Control)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always start a 14-mark answer with a **definition**, include a **diagram or formula**, and end with a **conclusion tied to class rules or IMO regulations**. This structure guarantees ≥ 12/14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dry Docking and Repairing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dry Docking and Repairing

# Dry Docking and Repairing — Comprehensive Module Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Dry Docking** is a scheduled maritime engineering operation in which a vessel is removed from its operational aquatic environment and supported on a rigid structural system in order to expose the underwater hull, appendages, propulsion units, and steering gear for the purpose of statutory survey, classification society inspection, hull treatment, machinery overhaul, and damage repair in accordance with **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)**, **MARPOL (Marine Pollution) Annex VI**, and **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** unified requirements.

In simple naval architecture terminology, dry docking is the controlled transition of a ship from a **floating equilibrium condition** (Archimedes' principle governs buoyancy) to a **grounded/support condition** (where the keel blocks and side shores bear the entire vessel weight).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mechanical Elephant Lift"

Think of a ship as a giant floating mechanical elephant weighing **50,000 to 400,000 tonnes**. Imagine you need to:

1. Inspect its feet (propeller, rudder, sea valves)
2. Paint its belly (anti-fouling system)
3. Repair its tusks (bow thruster, stabilizer fins)

You cannot roll it over in the water. So you build a giant mechanical lift (the dry dock), hoist it out, prop it up on wooden blocks (keel and bilge blocks), and let engineers work underneath. When finished, you refill the dock with water and let the ship float away again.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** 
> - **Wet Docking (Afloat Repairs):** Divers perform work while the ship remains in water.
> - **Dry Docking (In-Dock Repairs):** Ship is physically removed from water — required for any work below the **load waterline (LWL)** or for thickness gauging of plates.

### 1.3 Why Dry Docking is Statutorily Mandatory

| Regulation Body | Requirement | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **SOLAS Ch. I, Reg. 10** | Hull inspection of passenger ships | Every 12 months |
| **IACS UR Z7 / Z10** | Special Survey (Class Renewal) | Every 5 years (max) |
| **MARPOL Annex I** | Renewal of IOPP Certificate (Oil Record Book audit) | Every 5 years |
| **IMO Resolution A.1156(32)** | ESP (Enhanced Survey Programme) — Bulk carriers & tankers | Throughout operational life |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Docking

- **Docking Draft (Tₐ):** The forward or aft draft of the vessel at the moment of entering the dock — typically limited by dock sill depth.
- **Maximum Beam (B_max):** The dock's clear internal width must exceed the ship's maximum beam (including fenders).
- **LOA (Length Overall):** Length over alles, including bulbous bow and any protrusions.
- **Deflection (hogging/sagging):** The vessel's elastic curve when grounded — must be controlled via block arrangement.
- **Block Load (kN/m²):** Bearing pressure on each dock block — typically limited to **300–400 kN/m²** for timber blocks, **500–800 kN/m²** for steel keel blocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dry Dock Sill Depth Formula:** A ship's maximum entry draft $T_{entry}$ must satisfy:
> $$T_{entry} \leq D_{sill} + S_{rise}$$
> where $D_{sill}$ is the dock sill depth below chart datum and $S_{rise}$ is the tidal rise at the time of docking.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Dry Docks

Docks are broadly classified into **four architectural families**:

#### A. Graving Dock (Permanent / Dry Dock)
A rectangular basin with one end open to the sea, fitted with a **floating caisson gate** or **hinged gate**. Water is pumped out after the vessel enters.

**Sub-features:**
- **Docking blocks:** Fixed timber or steel supports along the centerline.
- **Side shores:** Adjustable timber or hydraulic shores from dock walls.
- **Bilge blocks:** V-shaped supports at the turn-of-bilge region.
- **Pump well:** Sumps at low points for drainage.

#### B. Floating Dock
A pontoon-based U-shaped or L-shaped floating structure that submerges itself, receives the ship, then deballasts to lift the vessel clear of water.

**Operational Phases:**
1. Submerge to required waterline.
2. Position vessel over pontoon deck.
3. Ballast control to lift ship clear of water.
4. Shore the ship on the wing walls/pontoon.

#### C. Slipway (Patent Slip)
An inclined rail system using a cradle hauled by wire ropes and winches (or in modern versions, hydraulic cylinders).

**Modern Variants:**
- **Shiplift / Synchrolift:** Multiple synchronized hoists lift the vessel from the water onto a transfer system, then onto a land-based berth.

#### D. Marine Railway
A cradle running on rails, hauled by chains/winch from a fixed onshore winch station — historically common for smaller vessels and fishing fleets.

### 2.2 Dry Docking Procedure — Twelve Logical Phases

The docking operation follows a strict, internationally codified sequence:

| Phase | Operation | Critical Engineering Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Pre-Docking Planning** | Review docking plan, stability book, prior docking reports | Displacement Δ, LCG, VCG, TPC |
| **2. Docking Plan Approval** | Class surveyor + dock master approve block layout | Block load ≤ 400 kN/m² |
| **3. Approach & Mooring** | Ship maneuvers using tugs, moored with springs | Approach velocity ≤ 0.05 m/s |
| **4. Caisson Closure** | Floating caisson positioned and flooded into seat | Gate seal pressure ≥ design |
