| **5. De-Watering** | Dock pumps evacuate water in stages | Rate ≈ 50–150 mm/min lowering |
| **6. First Line / Land Position** | Vessel settles on initial blocks | Even bearing contact |
| **7. Centerline Adjustment** | Centering using side shores and shores | Centerline deviation ≤ 25 mm |
| **8. Deflection Monitoring** | Trigonometric leveling of hull | Hog/Sag ≤ 50 mm typical |
| **9. Full Blocking & Shoring** | Bilge blocks, side shores, poppets engaged | Full keel line contact |
| **10. Inspection & Survey** | Hull gauging, NDT (UTM), class survey | Class thickness vs. renewal |
| **11. Repair Work** | Steel renewal, blasting, painting, machinery | Per work list |
| **12. Re-Floating (Undocking)** | Re-flooding and ship departure | Trim, list monitored |

### 2.3 Hull Support System — The Block & Shore Architecture

The support system must distribute the vessel's lightweight ($W_L$) evenly over the dock floor while accommodating the hull's curvature.

**Component Definition:**
- **Keel Block:** Centerline vertical timber/steel block, height typically **1.6 m to 2.4 m**.
- **Bilge Block:** Diagonal V-shaped block located where the hull transitions from flat bottom to vertical side.
- **Side Shore:** Horizontal timber brace from dock wall to hull side.
- **Poppet (Dolphin):** Auxiliary vertical shore used in floating docks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Empirical):** A ship's lightweight $W_L$ (in tonnes) is approximately:
> $$W_L \approx \Delta - DWT \approx 0.10 \text{ to } 0.18 \times \Delta$$
> where $\Delta$ is the full-load displacement and $DWT$ is deadweight.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU exam-relevant and mathematically verifiable. Memorize the boxed ones.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_B = \rho g V$ | Archimedes' Buoyancy (Floating condition) | N or kN |
| 2 | $W = \Delta \cdot g$ | Weight = Displacement × gravity | kN |
| 3 | $T_{entry} \leq D_{sill} + S_{rise}$ | Maximum entry draft condition | m |
| 4 | $\sigma_{block} = \dfrac{W_L}{A_{contact}}$ | Block bearing pressure | kN/m² |
| 5 | $\delta_{hog} = \dfrac{q \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot EI}$ | Elastic deflection of grounded hull | m |
| 6 | $q = \dfrac{W_L}{L_{block}}$ | Distributed load per meter on keel | kN/m |
| 7 | $P_{pump} = \rho g Q H / \eta$ | Dock pump power requirement | kW |
| 8 | $t_{corrosion} = t_{as-built} - t_{measured}$ | Corrosion allowance consumed | mm |
| 9 | $t_{renewal} = t_{scantling} + t_{corrosion}$ | Renewal thickness target | mm |
| 10 | $S_{dock} = W_L / (n_{blocks} \cdot A_{block})$ | Block load per unit | kN/m² |
| 11 | $W_L \approx 0.15 \Delta$ | Approximate lightweight rule | tonnes |
| 12 | $T_{mean} = \dfrac{T_{forward} + T_{aft}}{2}$ | Mean draft at docking | m |

### 2.5 Undocking Calculation Logic

When the dock re-floods and the ship becomes buoyant, the **first line / land cut** clearance must be checked:

$$\Delta_{floating} = \rho \cdot V_{submerged}$$

For the ship to refloat off the blocks:
$$F_B > W \implies \rho g V_{submerged} > mg$$

The **under-keel clearance (UKC)** during undocking must satisfy:
$$UKC \geq 0.5 \text{ m (minimum safety)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This same logic is used in **semi-submersible rigs**, **jack-up platforms**, and **diving support vessels (DSVs)** for load-out and float-off operations in offshore engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Block Load Calculation (Full Worked Example)

**Problem Statement:** A bulk carrier with a lightweight $W_L = 18,500$ tonnes is to be dry-docked. The keel line is supported on **40 main keel blocks**, each of plan area $A_{block} = 1.2 \text{ m} \times 0.6 \text{ m} = 0.72 \text{ m}^2$. Calculate the bearing pressure on each block.

**Step 1 — Convert weight to force:**
$$W = W_L \cdot g = 18{,}500 \times 9.81 = 181{,}485 \text{ kN}$$

Here, $W_L$ is in metric tonnes (1000 kg), so multiplying by $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ converts mass to force in kilonewtons.

**Step 2 — Total contact area:**
$$A_{total} = n \cdot A_{block} = 40 \times 0.72 = 28.8 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Average bearing pressure:**
$$\sigma_{block} = \dfrac{W}{A_{total}} = \dfrac{181{,}485}{28.8} \approx 6{,}302 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

This is unrealistically high; let us reconsider.

**Step 4 — Realistic Loading (Assuming 70% of W on keel, 30% on bilge shores):**
$$W_{keel} = 0.70 \times 181{,}485 = 126{,}940 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Corrected per-block load:**
$$\sigma_{block,corrected} = \dfrac{W_{keel}}{A_{total}} = \dfrac{126{,}940}{28.8} \approx 4{,}407 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with allowable timber block capacity:**
$$\sigma_{allow} \approx 2{,}500 \text{ to } 3{,}000 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Conclusion:** The assumed 40 blocks are **insufficient**. Recalculate the required number of blocks:

$$n_{required} = \dfrac{W_{keel}}{\sigma_{allow} \cdot A_{block}} = \dfrac{126{,}940}{2{,}800 \times 0.72} \approx 63 \text{ blocks}$$

**Result:** Use **64 keel blocks minimum** to safely support the vessel.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Hull Deflection on the Blocks (Hogging/Sagging Control)

When a vessel sits on discrete keel blocks, the unsupported span between blocks creates a bending moment analogous to a simply-supported beam.

**Problem:** A vessel of length between perpendiculars $L_{BP} = 150$ m is supported on **n = 60 keel blocks** spaced uniformly. Assume the lightweight is uniformly distributed along the keel.

**Step 1 — Distributed load:**
$$q = \dfrac{W_L \cdot g}{L_{BP}} = \dfrac{18{,}500 \times 9.81}{150} \approx 1{,}210 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 2 — Effective span between blocks:**
$$L_{span} = \dfrac{L_{BP}}{n} = \dfrac{150}{60} = 2.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Moment of inertia of hull girder (approximate):**
For a typical bulk carrier:
$$I \approx \dfrac{B \cdot D^3}{12} \approx \dfrac{32 \times 18^3}{12} \approx 15{,}552 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 4 — Young's Modulus for steel:**
$$E = 210 \text{ GPa} = 210 \times 10^6 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Deflection between two adjacent blocks (treat as fixed-end-beam segment):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \dfrac{q \cdot L_{span}^4}{384 \cdot EI} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}210 \times (2.5)^4}{384 \times 210 \times 10^6 \times 15{,}552} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}210 \times 39.0625}{1.253 \times 10^{12}} \\
&\approx 3.77 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} \\
&\approx 0.038 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The inter-block deflection is negligible because the hull acts as a continuous girder. Local deflections at the bilge are more critical and are controlled by **poppet shores** rather than block spacing alone.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Corrosion Allowance and Steel Renewal Decision

**Background:** Class rules require that the minimum hull plate thickness $t_{min}$ is maintained throughout the service life. Steel renewal is mandated when the measured thickness falls below the renewal threshold.

**Step 1 — As-built thickness:**
$$t_{as\text{-}built} = t_{scantling} + t_{corrosion}$$

For a bottom plate, IACS typically specifies:
$$t_{corrosion} = 1.0 \text{ to } 2.5 \text{ mm (depending on coating type)}$$

**Step 2 — Measured thickness after 20 years of service:**
$$t_{measured} = t_{as\text{-}built} - C \cdot T$$
where $C$ = corrosion rate (mm/year) and $T$ = elapsed years.

**Step 3 — Renewal criterion (IACS UR Z7):**
$$t_{measured} < t_{ren} = t_{scantling} + 0.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Numerical Example:**
Given $t_{scantling} = 18$ mm, $t_{as\text{-}built} = 20$ mm, $C = 0.12$ mm/year, $T = 20$ years.

$$t_{measured} = 20 - (0.12 \times 20) = 20 - 2.4 = 17.6 \text{ mm}$$

$$t_{ren} = 18 + 0.5 = 18.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $17.6 < 18.5$ mm, the plate is **substandard** — **renewal is required.**

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Criteria for Dock Type

| Parameter | Graving Dock | Floating Dock | Synchrolift |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Max vessel size** | Up to VLCC | Up to Panamax+ | Up to Panamax |
| **Capital cost (USD)** | $100–400 M | $50–150 M | $20–60 M |
| **Operating cost** | Moderate | High (ballast) | Low |
| **Turnaround time** | 8–14 days | 3–5 days | 1–2 days |
| **Docking draft limit** | Sill depth | Pontoon depth | Hoist capacity |
| **Best application** | Capesize, VLCC | Repair docks | Smaller vessels, newbuild |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Dry Docking Operational Flow — End-to-End Process Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pre-Docking Planning] --> B[Block Layout Design]
    B --> C[Docking Plan Approval]
    C --> D[Ship Approaches Dock]
    D --> E[Caisson Gate Closed]
    E --> F[Pump Out Dock Water]
    F --> G[Ship Settles on Blocks]
    G --> H[Centerline Deflection Check]
    H --> I[Full Shoring Applied]
    I --> J[Hull Survey and Gauging]
    J --> K[Steel Renewal Work]
    K --> L[Blasting and Painting]
    L --> M[Machinery Inspection]
    M --> N[Re-flood Dock]
    N --> O[Undocking and Departure]
    O --> P[Post-Docking Report]
```

### 4.2 Dry Dock Cross-Section — Block and Shore Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hull Side] -->|Side Shore| B[Dock Wall]
    C[Keel Line] -->|Keel Block| D[Dock Floor]
    E[Turn of Bilge] -->|Bilge Block| D
    F[Dock Pumps] --> G[Pump Sump]
    G --> H[Sea Outfall]
```

### 4.3 Floating Dock Sub-system Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Floating Dock Pontoon] --> B[Ballast Tanks]
    A --> C[Wing Walls]
    A --> D[Main Deck Keel Blocks]
    B --> E[Bilge Pump]
    B --> F[Main Pump]
    F --> G[Sea Chest]
    E --> H[Overboard Discharge]
    D --> I[Vessel Support Line]
    C --> J[Side Support Brackets]
```

### 4.4 Hull Survey Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thickness Gauging Complete] --> B{All t >= t_ren}
    B -->|Yes| C[No Renewal - Pass]
    B -->|No| D{Is plate in critical area?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Mandatory Renewal]
    D -->|No| F[Local Repair with Class Approval]
    E --> G[Steel Renewal Drawing]
    G --> H[Work Execution]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Re-survey and Closure]
```

### 4.5 Dock Type Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Dry Docks] --> B[Permanent Graving Dock]
    A --> C[Floating Dock]
    A --> D[Slipway Synchrolift]
    B --> E[Used for VLCC Bulk Carriers]
    C --> F[Used for Medium Sized Vessels]
    D --> G[Used for Smaller Vessels and Newbuilds]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**Define the term "Dry Docking" and list any three mandatory reasons why a ship must undergo dry docking during its operational life.**

**Model Answer:**
Dry docking is the operation of removing a vessel from water by supporting it on keel blocks and shores inside a graving or floating dock so that underwater hull, appendages, and machinery may be inspected and repaired in dry condition.

The three mandatory reasons are:
1. **Statutory Survey** — Special Survey required by Classification Society every **5 years** maximum (IACS UR Z7/Z10).
2. **Hull Treatment** — Surface preparation (sandblasting Sa 2.5 standard) and re-application of anti-fouling paint, which cannot be performed in water.
3. **Underwater Appendage Inspection** — Visual and NDT (UTM) inspection of propeller, rudder, sea valves, and stern seal.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]**
**What is a "block load" in dry docking? State the typical allowable bearing pressure on a timber keel block.**

**Model Answer:**
Block load is the load transmitted per unit area from the hull of the vessel to an individual support block (keel, bilge, or side shore) during dry docking.

The typical allowable bearing pressure on a timber keel block is **2,500 to 3,000 kN/m² (≈ 25 to 30 kg/cm²)** depending on block material grade, orientation, and grain direction.

---

### Part B Question — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the step-by-step procedure for dry docking a vessel in a graving dock with the help of a neat sketch. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The dry docking procedure in a graving dock comprises the following steps:

**Step 1 — Pre-Docking Preparation:**
- Review vessel's docking plan, stability data, and prior docking history.
- Inspect and align keel blocks, bilge blocks, and side shores to the prescribed heights.
- Verify dock sill depth vs. vessel entry draft.

**Step 2 — Docking Plan Approval:**
The class surveyor and dock master jointly approve the block layout based on the vessel's structural arrangement and lightweight distribution.

**Step 3 — Vessel Approach:**
The vessel enters the dock under tug assistance at a controlled approach velocity (≤ 0.05 m/s) and is secured with mooring lines and springs at both ends.

**Step 4 — Caisson Closure:**
The floating caisson is positioned across the dock entrance and flooded into its seat to seal the dock from the sea.

**Step 5 — De-Watering:**
Dock pumps evacuate water in controlled stages. As the water level drops, the vessel settles onto the keel blocks under gravity.

**Step 6 — First Line Contact and Centering:**
The dock master checks centerline alignment using shores and winches, ensuring even contact at all blocks.

**Step 7 — Full Shoring:**
Bilge blocks, side shores, and poppets are engaged to fully support the vessel, transferring the entire lightweight to the dock floor.

**Step 8 — Survey and Repairs:**
Hull survey, thickness gauging, and repair work proceed as per approved work scope.

**(b) A bulk carrier of lightweight 22,000 tonnes is dry-docked with 60 keel blocks of size 1.5 m × 0.6 m. Calculate the average bearing pressure on the keel blocks. Assume 70% of weight is borne on keel blocks. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute total weight (force):**
$$W = 22{,}000 \times 9.81 = 215{,}820 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Weight on keel blocks:**
$$W_{keel} = 0.70 \times 215{,}820 = 151{,}074 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Area of one block:**
$$A_{block} = 1.5 \times 0.6 = 0.90 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Total keel block area:**
$$A_{total} = 60 \times 0.90 = 54.0 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Average bearing pressure:**
$$\sigma_{block} = \dfrac{151{,}074}{54.0} \approx 2{,}797.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Conclusion:** This is within the typical allowable bearing pressure of timber blocks (**2,500–3,000 kN/m²**), confirming the layout is acceptable.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Compare the features of a graving dock and a floating dock. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Graving Dock | Floating Dock |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Excavated basin with caisson gate | Modular pontoon with wing walls |
| **Mobility** | Permanent, fixed location | Can be towed between sites |
| **Capital Cost** | Very high | Moderate |
| **Operational Cost** | Moderate (pumping) | High (ballast handling) |
| **Vessel Size Limit** | Up to VLCC / ULCC | Up to Panamax size typically |
| **Sill Depth Issue** | Limited by sill depth | Limited by pontoon deck height |
| **Stability Risk** | Minimal | Sensitive to tide and trim |
| **Best Use** | Newbuild, capesize vessels | Repair yards, medium tonnage |

**(b) The thickness of a hull plate was 22 mm when built. After 18 years of service, the measured thickness is 18.5 mm. The corrosion rate is 0.13 mm/year. Determine if steel renewal is required as per IACS UR Z7 if $t_{scantling} = 17.5$ mm. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Theoretical wastage:**
$$\Delta t = C \times T = 0.13 \times 18 = 2.34 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Expected thickness after 18 years:**
$$t_{expected} = t_{as\text{-}built} - \Delta t = 22 - 2.34 = 19.66 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Actual measured thickness:**
$$t_{measured} = 18.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with renewal threshold (IACS UR Z7.1):**
$$t_{ren} = t_{scantling} + 0.5 = 17.5 + 0.5 = 18.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**
Since $t_{measured} = 18.5 \text{ mm} > t_{ren} = 18.0 \text{ mm}$, the plate is **acceptable** and **steel renewal is NOT required** at this survey, though enhanced gauging at the next annual survey is recommended.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss Points — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students frequently forget to multiply by $g = 9.81$ when computing force in kN — always show the conversion step.
> 2. **Block Distribution Logic:** Marks are lost if you assume 100% of weight is on the keel — Class rules mandate **70% on keel, 30% on side shores**.
> 3. **Tidal Allowance:** Forgetting to add tidal rise $S_{rise}$ to sill depth $D_{sill}$ in entry-draft problems.
> 4. **Renewal Threshold:** Using $t_{scantling}$ alone instead of $t_{scantling} + 0.5$ mm as the renewal criterion (UR Z7.1).
> 5. **Sketch Quality:** Board examiners **deduct up to 2 marks** if the dock sketch has no labels on the caisson, keel blocks, side shores, or pump sump.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3, Dry Docking & Repairing**

- **Dry docking** = removing ship from water for survey, repair, or maintenance.
- **Four dock types:** Graving, Floating, Slipway (Synchrolift), Marine Railway.
- **Block spacing** determined by hull structural arrangement — not arbitrary.
- **Block load allowable:** 2,500–3,000 kN/m² for timber; 500–800 kN/m² for steel.
- **Lightweight rule:** $W_L \approx 0.10$ to $0.18 \times \Delta$.
- **Renewal threshold (IACS UR Z7):** $t_{ren} = t_{scantling} + 0.5$ mm.
- **Entry draft condition:** $T_{entry} \leq D_{sill} + S_{rise}$.
- **Class surveys:** Special Survey every **5 years**; Intermediate Survey at **2.5 years**.
- **Anti-fouling paint standard:** Surface preparation **Sa 2.5** (ISO 8501-1).
- **Undocking UKC:** Minimum **0.5 m** safety under-keel clearance.
- **Mandatory dry-docking reasons:** Class renewal, hull coating renewal, underwater appendage inspection.
- **Deflection formula:** $\delta = q L^4 / 384 EI$ — applies between adjacent keel blocks.
- **70/30 rule:** 70% of weight on keel blocks, 30% on side shores.
- **Caisson gate** is the watertight closure at the entrance of a graving dock.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dry-docking - Steps to be Taken Before, During and After Dry-docking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dry-docking - Steps to be Taken Before, During and After Dry-docking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dry-docking** is the deliberate operation of removing a ship from the water by supporting it on a rigid structural platform (the docking blocks of a graving dock, floating dock, or marine railway) so that the underwater hull, appendages, propellers, rudders, sea valves, and the entire shell plating below the waterline can be inspected, maintained, repaired, surveyed, painted, and certified in accordance with the Class Rules of classification societies and the IMO regulatory framework.

A ship must typically undergo **dry-docking at least twice in any 5-year Special Survey cycle** (the Intermediate Survey and the Special Survey Hull), with intermediate in-water surveys using divers/ROVs permitted under IACS Unified Requirements **Z7** and **Z7.1** between dockings. The statutory interval between consecutive dry-dockings must not exceed **36 months** for most commercial vessels, although class-approved extensions (CAS - Condition Assessment Program) may extend this up to a maximum of **60 months** subject to satisfactory in-water survey results.

### Classification of Docking Facilities

| Dock Type | Principle | Typical Capacity |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| Graving Dock | Ship enters flooded dock; gates close; water pumped out; ship settles on keel blocks | Up to VLCC sizes |
| Floating Dock | Pontoon submerges, ship enters, pontoon lifts ship clear of water | Medium to large vessels |
| Marine Railway | Ship hauled up an inclined slipway on wheeled cradles | Small to medium vessels |
| Syncrolift | Platform lifted by synchronized hoists | Medium vessels |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy - The Ship Doctor's Operating Theatre**
>
> Imagine a patient (the ship) needs surgery on its legs and abdomen (the underwater hull, propellers, and keel). Normally the patient is standing in a swimming pool (the sea), and the doctor cannot access the submerged parts. To operate, the patient is rolled into a special room (the **dry dock**), the door is sealed (dock gates closed), the pool water is pumped out, and the patient is gently lowered onto a custom-built padded bed (the **keel blocks and side blocks**). Now the doctor has full 360-degree access to the legs (hull bottom) and belly (bilge keels). After surgery and a clean bandage (fresh anti-fouling paint), the room is refilled with water, the door opens, and the patient walks back into the pool (the sea).
>
> The whole choreography — preparing the patient, transferring the bed, monitoring vitals, surgery, recovery — must follow a strict sequence. Skip a step, and you risk broken bones (hull damage), infection (corrosion), or even death (capsizing in dock).

## 1.3 Why Dry-Docking is Statutorily Mandatory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Favourite Line:**
> *"Why cannot we do everything by in-water survey by divers?"*

Answer:
- **Cathodic Protection (sacrificial anode) replacement** requires physical access to welded anodes.
- **Hull plate thickness measurements** with ultrasonic probes need clean, dry, de-scaled steel.
- **Propeller polishing, sea chest cleaning, stern seal servicing** cannot be reliably performed underwater.
- **Class surveyors require physical inspection** — the IACS UR Z7 explicitly demands "close-up survey" which is impractical in water due to visibility and tactile limitations.
- **Renewal of anti-fouling paint** requires a controlled, dry, dust-free environment.

### Key Terminology Students Must Memorize

- **Keel blocks** — Wooden or concrete blocks laid along the centreline of the dock floor on which the ship's keel rests.
- **Side blocks / Bilge blocks** — Bilge shores fitted at the bilge to take the side weight of the vessel.
- **Docking plan** — A scaled drawing showing the arrangement of blocks under the ship.
- **Shores / Shores** — Adjustable timber or steel supports bracing the ship's sides during docking.
- **Survey conditions** — "Clean, gas-free, well-lit and safe" — the four mandatory conditions for any class survey.
- **Docking draft / Air draft** — Forward, after, and trim drafts at the moment of docking.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy of Dry-Docking

The dry-docking operation is governed by a **tri-phasic protocol**: *Pre-Docking (Planning)*, *During Docking (Execution)*, and *Post-Docking (Re-commissioning)*. Each phase has multiple sub-stages, all of which must be completed in sequence. Skipping any sub-stage may lead to:

1. Structural damage to the ship's hull (e.g., slamming on blocks).
2. Toppling / instability of the dock or ship.
3. Class survey failure, leading to detention.
4. Port State Control (PSC) deficiencies.
5. Costly rework and time overruns.

## 2.2 The Three-Phase Master Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **The Master Dry-Docking Protocol (the 3 × 7 = 21 Rule)**
>
> For KTU exam answers, the student must remember:
> - **Phase 1 (Pre-Docking):** 7 critical preparatory steps.
> - **Phase 2 (During Docking):** 7 critical operational steps.
> - **Phase 3 (Post-Docking):** 7 critical re-commissioning steps.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the technical formulas, parameters, and limits a KTU student must know for dry-docking problems.

| Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Units | Engineering Utility |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------|---------------------|
| Ship's displacement at docking | $\Delta_{\text{dock}} = L \times B \times T \times C_b \times \rho_w$ | tonnes | Determines block loading |
| Block load per unit area | $q_b = \dfrac{W_{\text{ship}}}{n_b \times A_b}$ | $\text{tonnes/m}^2$ | Block design / bearing pressure check |
| Maximum allowable bearing pressure on keel block | $q_{\text{allow}} \leq 25 \text{ tonnes/m}^2$ (typical hardwood) | $\text{tonnes/m}^2$ | Hull girder shear stress limit |
| Keel block length (typical) | $L_b = 0.6 \times L_{pp}$ | metres | Covers mid 60% of Lpp |
| Side block spacing | $s_b = 1.5 \text{ m to } 3.0 \text{ m}$ | metres | Prevents hull plate local buckling |
| Bilge block height | $h_b = T_{\text{air-draft}} - T_{\text{dock}} - \text{freeboard}$ | metres | Sets bilge clearance |
| Trim at docking | $\text{trim} = \text{draft}_{\text{aft}} - \text{draft}_{\text{fwd}}$ (typical < 0.5 m by stern) | metres | Prevents stern slamming |
| Dock water filling rate | $Q = A_{\text{dock}} \times v_{\text{rise}}$ with $v_{\text{rise}} \leq 0.3 \text{ m/min}$ | m³/min | Controls hydrostatic load on gates |
| Number of shore positions (rule of thumb) | $n_{\text{shores}} \geq \dfrac{L_{pp}}{6}$ | dimensionless | Lateral stability |
| Underkeel clearance (during undocking) | $\text{UKC} \geq 0.5 \text{ m}$ | metres | Safe refloat |

> **Notation legend:** $L$ = Length overall, $L_{pp}$ = Length between perpendiculars, $B$ = Breadth, $T$ = Draft, $C_b$ = Block coefficient, $\rho_w$ = Water density (typically **1.025 t/m³** for seawater), $n_b$ = Number of blocks, $A_b$ = Contact area of each block.

## 2.4 Why Each Phase Matters — The Engineering Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5 Pillars of Dry-Docking Success:**
> 1. **Safety** — No injuries to personnel or damage to ship or dock.
> 2. **Survey Compliance** — Class survey completed and certified.
> 3. **Time Efficiency** — On-schedule docking minimizes off-hire (lost revenue).
> 4. **Cost Control** — Avoiding rework saves money.
> 5. **Environmental Protection** — No oil spill, no hazardous discharge.

The **off-hire cost** of a vessel is typically:
$$
C_{\text{off-hire}} = \text{Daily TCE rate} \times \text{Days in dock}
$$

A single extra day in dock for a Panamax bulker can cost the owner **USD 25,000–40,000**. Hence the engineering imperative for **meticulous pre-docking planning**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedures — The Three Phases

## 3.1 PHASE 1 — STEPS TO BE TAKEN **BEFORE** DRY-DOCKING

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pre-docking preparation typically starts **3 to 6 months before** the scheduled docking date. Class societies, owners, dockyards, and the Master must coordinate through a **Dock Plan Document**.

### Step 1.1 — Notification and Statutory Approvals
- Notify the Classification Society (e.g., IRS, LR, DNV, BV, ABS, KR) at least **3 months in advance** to schedule attendance of the Class Surveyor.
- Apply to the port authority for a **docking slot / allocation**.
- Inform the **P&I Club** and **H&M (Hull & Machinery) insurers** for record and joint survey if required.
- Obtain **flag-state** concurrence (e.g., through the Maritime Administration).

### Step 1.2 — Selection of the Repair Yard / Dry Dock
- Compare docks based on: **length, breadth, lifting capacity, dock water depth, water density at site, lifting capacity, crane capacity, location relative to trading routes**.
- Sign the **Dockyard Repair Specification (DRS)** contract with scope of work, prices, and timelines.

### Step 1.3 — Docking Plan Preparation
- The yard prepares a **docking plan** based on the ship's general arrangement drawing, showing:
  - Position of **keel blocks** along the centreline.
  - Position of **bilge blocks** under the bilge.
  - Position of **side shores** on both sides.
  - Position of **bilge shores** at the bilge radius.
- Plan must be approved by **Class Surveyor and the Master's representative (Superintendent)**.

### Step 1.4 — Ship's Preparation
- **Defuel / de-ballast / debunker** to the **lightest safe draft** that still allows safe navigation to the dock.
  - Typical docking draft: **50% to 70% of design draft**.
  - Deballasting prevents excessive **block loads** (which could buckle the keel).
- **Trim** the ship slightly **by the stern** (about 0.3 m to 0.5 m) so the stern enters the dock first, and the propeller clears the blocks.
- Remove or secure loose items on deck — boats, lashings, gangways.
- **Close all overboard discharges, sea valves, and hull openings** to prevent ingress of dockyard debris.
- Rig the **docking pilot ladders, mooring ropes, and fenders**.

### Step 1.5 — Pre-Docking Survey by Master
- The Master, Chief Officer, and Chief Engineer jointly conduct a **pre-docking survey** covering:
  - Soundings of bilges, cofferdams, and tanks.
  - Condition of propeller, rudder, sea chest grids.
  - Marking of hull for **fouling intensity** (to plan cleaning).
  - Identification of any **underwater appendages** (drain plugs, anodes, echo sounder transducers) that may be damaged by blocks.
- The Master issues a **Docking Memo** to the dockyard.

### Step 1.6 — Safety Preparations
- **Gas-free the tanks** (especially if hot work is planned in adjacent spaces).
- Inform crew on **fire-fighting, man-overboard, and emergency dock procedures**.
- Provide **lifeboats** for crew transfer between ship and quay.
- Check that the **dock gate, caisson, or dock sill** are intact.

### Step 1.7 — Towage and Approach Planning
- For ocean-going vessels, engage **tugboats** (typically 2–3 tugs) for the docking maneuver.
- Brief the **docking master** (yard representative) on ship particulars: LOA, beam, draft, displacement, GM, freeboard, bow/stern thrusters, propeller direction.

## 3.2 PHASE 2 — STEPS TO BE TAKEN **DURING** DRY-DOCKING

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The actual docking maneuver is the **most hazardous single operation** in the ship's life-cycle (apart from launch). The dockyard's Docking Master has overriding authority over the ship's navigation.

### Step 2.1 — Ship Entering the Dock
- The ship enters either **bow-first or stern-first** depending on the **direction of the prevailing current, wind, and propeller accessibility**.
- Mooring lines (head, stern, breast, spring) are passed to the dockyard bollards.
- Position is adjusted using **winches, capstans, and tugs** until the ship is **centrally aligned** above the keel blocks.

### Step 2.2 — Centering and Aligning
- Use **docking hawsers** (heavy wires) attached to the ship's bow and stern and led to **docking winches on the dock floor**.
- Visual confirmation by docking master and Master from elevated positions.
- Confirm **centreline alignment** using plumb lines and laser alignment devices.
- The ship's centreline must coincide with the **centreline of the keel blocks** within a tolerance of **±25 mm** for most vessels, **±50 mm** for VLCCs.

### Step 2.3 — De-watering the Dock
- The dock is de-watered using **dock pumps**.
- Pumping rate is controlled to keep **water level drop ≤ 0.3 m/minute** to avoid sudden loading on blocks.
- As the water level drops:
  - First the **keel** touches the keel blocks.
  - Then the **bilges** rest on bilge blocks.
  - Then the **sides** are supported by side shores.

### Step 2.4 — Settling on the Blocks
- As the ship settles, the **block pressure** increases gradually.
- Continuous monitoring by **strain gauges and pressure sensors** (modern yards).
- Listen and watch for **creaking noises** indicating uneven loading — if detected, **immediately flood the dock partially** and re-align.

### Step 2.5 — Rigging Shores
- **Side shores** (heavy timbers or screw-jacks) are placed between the ship's side and the **dock side walls** at intervals of about **1.5–3 m**.
- **Bilge shores** are placed at the bilge radius to support the curved part of the hull.
- **Top shores / Deck shores** may be added to prevent rolling.
- The number of shores must satisfy:
  $$
  n_{\text{shores}} \geq \frac{L_{pp}}{6}
  $$

### Step 2.6 — Survey, Cleaning, and Repairs
- **High-pressure water jetting** removes marine fouling (typically 250–350 bar).
- **Sandblasting / grit blasting** removes old paint and rust down to bare metal (Sa 2.5 standard).
- **Class Surveyor** carries out **close-up survey, thickness measurement (UTM), and NDT (ultrasonic, magnetic particle, dye penetrant)** as per UR Z7.1.
- Repairs: steel renewal, plate cropping and welding, anode replacement, propeller polishing, stern gland overhaul.

### Step 2.7 — Painting
- Apply **anti-corrosive primer** (typically zinc-rich epoxy).
- Apply **anti-fouling paint** (biocide-based, e.g., cuprous oxide or newer silicone-based foul-release coatings).
- Paint thickness verified with **wet film and dry film thickness gauges**.

## 3.3 PHASE 3 — STEPS TO BE TAKEN **AFTER** DRY-DOCKING

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The post-docking phase is also called **"re-commissioning"** and is equally critical. Errors here can result in sinking, capsize, machinery failure, or class detentions.

### Step 3.1 — Final Hull Survey and Class Endorsement
- Class Surveyor issues the **Class Certificate endorsement** confirming hull and machinery are in satisfactory condition.
- All survey reports, thickness measurement reports, and **Conditions of Class** are documented.

### Step 3.2 — Inspection of Underwater Fittings
- Re-fit **sea chest grids, sea valves, anode bolts, transducers (echo sounder, speed log)**.
- Check **rudder, propeller, stern seal** for tightness.
- Plug all temporary **drain plugs** used during dewatering.

### Step 3.3 — Removal of Shores
- Side shores, bilge shores, and any rigging are **removed in a controlled sequence** — typically symmetrically to avoid sudden load shifts.
- All **timbers, ropes, debris** are removed from the dock floor.

### Step 3.4 — Re-floating (Undocking)
- **Dock caisson / gate** is sealed.
- Dock is **re-flooded**.
- As water rises, the ship **lifts off the blocks** and floats free.
- Trim and list monitored continuously.
- UKC (under-keel clearance) monitored as ship is undocked.

### Step 3.5 — Mooring Out and Trials
- Ship is moored at the **fitting-out berth** alongside the dockyard.
- Conduct **sea trials** if extensive machinery work was done:
  - **Engine trials** — main engine, generators, steering gear.
  - **Speed trial** to verify performance.
  - **Navigation equipment trials**.
- Conduct **anchoring and mooring trials** if required.

### Step 3.6 — Documentation and Records
- Update the ship's **Maintenance Plan and Survey Status File**.
- File the **Dry-Docking Report** with the Class Society.
- Update the **Continuous Survey File (CSF)** if applicable.
- Close out all **Conditions of Class** within the stipulated due dates.

### Step 3.7 — Departure and Operational Re-entry
- Re-bunker to operational draft.
- Take on stores, spares, fresh water.
- Complete **port clearance formalities**.
- Pilot and tug escort the ship out of the dockyard.
- Master signs the **Dockyard Completion Certificate** acknowledging satisfactory re-delivery.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Handymax bulk carrier of $L_{pp} = 180$ m, breadth $B = 30$ m, draft $T = 8.5$ m, block coefficient $C_b = 0.78$ is to be docked at a dock with seawater density 1.025 t/m³. Estimate (a) the ship's displacement at docking, (b) the minimum number of keel blocks assuming each block carries an area of $0.4 \text{ m} \times 0.4 \text{ m}$ and the safe bearing pressure on the blocks is **20 tonnes/m²**.

### Solution

**(a) Displacement:**
$$
\Delta = L_{pp} \times B \times T \times C_b \times \rho_w
$$
$$
\Delta = 180 \times 30 \times 8.5 \times 0.78 \times 1.025
$$

$$
\Delta = 180 \times 30 = 5400
$$

$$
5400 \times 8.5 = 45900
$$

$$
45900 \times 0.78 = 35802
$$

$$
35802 \times 1.025 = 36697.05 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**(b) Minimum number of keel blocks:**

Total allowable block load:
$$
W_{\text{allow}} = q_{\text{allow}} \times A_b = 20 \times (0.4 \times 0.4) = 20 \times 0.16 = 3.2 \text{ tonnes/block}
$$

Number of blocks:
$$
n_b = \frac{\Delta}{W_{\text{allow}}} = \frac{36697.05}{3.2} \approx 11468 \text{ blocks}
$$

Since the keel blocks are placed in rows of 2 (port and starboard), the block pairs required are:
$$
n_{\text{pairs}} = \frac{11468}{2} = 5734 \text{ pairs}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the block coefficient $C_b$ and use 1.0, leading to massive over-estimation of displacement.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Dry-Docking Workflow (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PHASE 1 PRE-DOCKING] --> A1[Class Notification 3 months prior]
    A1 --> A2[Dock Selection and Contract]
    A2 --> A3[Docking Plan Preparation]
    A3 --> A4[Ship Defuel and Deballast]
    A4 --> A5[Pre-Docking Survey by Master]
    A5 --> A6[Safety and Gas-Free Preparations]
    A6 --> A7[Towage and Docking Master Briefing]
    A7 --> B[PHASE 2 DURING DOCKING]
    B --> B1[Ship Entry Bow or Stern First]
    B1 --> B2[Centering Using Docking Hawsers]
    B2 --> B3[De-Watering the Dock]
    B3 --> B4[Settling on Keel and Bilge Blocks]
    B4 --> B5[Rigging Side and Bilge Shores]
    B5 --> B6[Cleaning Sandblasting and Class Survey]
    B6 --> B7[Repairs and Anti-Fouling Painting]
    B7 --> C[PHASE 3 POST-DOCKING]
    C --> C1[Final Class Survey and Endorsement]
    C1 --> C2[Inspection of Underwater Fittings]
    C2 --> C3[Removal of Shores]
    C3 --> C4[Re-Floating the Dock]
    C4 --> C5[Mooring Out and Sea Trials]
    C5 --> C6[Documentation and Records Update]
    C6 --> C7[Departure and Operational Re-Entry]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional View of a Graving Dock (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GRAVING_DOCK_CROSS_SECTION
    direction LR
    K1[Keel Block Centerline] --> K2[Ship Hull in Section]
    K2 --> B1[Bilge Block Port]
    K2 --> B2[Bilge Block Starboard]
    K2 --> S1[Side Shore Port from Dock Wall]
    K2 --> S2[Side Shore Starboard from Dock Wall]
    K2 --> A1[Anti-Fouling Paint Layer]
    K2 --> A2[Steel Renewal Patch]
    K2 --> P1[Propeller for Inspection]
    K2 --> R1[Rudder for Inspection]
    K2 --> V1[Sea Chest and Valve]
    end
    W1[Seawater Before Dewatering] -.floods.-> GRAVING_DOCK_CROSS_SECTION
    PUMP1[Dock Pump] -.empties.-> GRAVING_DOCK_CROSS_SECTION
    GATE1[Dock Gate or Caisson] -.seals.-> GRAVING_DOCK_CROSS_SECTION
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Hull Cleaning Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Fouling Inspection After Settling on Blocks] --> Q1{Is Hull Lightly Fouled?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Use High Pressure Water Jet 200 bar]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is Hull Medium to Heavy Fouled?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Use Ultra High Pressure Water Jet 350 bar plus Manual Scraping]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is Heavy Calcareous or Shell Growth Present?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Use Mechanical Scraping plus Grit Blasting]
    Q3 -->|No| R4[Standard Cleaning Protocol]
    R1 --> NEXT[Move to Surface Preparation]
    R2 --> NEXT
    R3 --> NEXT
    R4 --> NEXT
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of Re-Commissioning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RC[Re-Commissioning Phase Start] --> R1[Hull Survey Endorsement by Class]
    R1 --> R2[Fit Sea Chest Grids and Anodes]
    R2 --> R3[Plug Drain Plugs]
    R3 --> R4[Remove Shores Symmetrically]
    R4 --> R5[Re-Flood Dock]
    R5 --> R6[Ship Floats Off Blocks]
    R6 --> R7[Tow to Fitting Out Berth]
    R7 --> R8[Sea Trials]
    R8 --> R9[Documentation Update]
    R9 --> R10[Operational Departure]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

### Question A.1
**Q: Define the term "Dry-docking" and state any two statutory occasions when it becomes mandatory.**

**Model Answer:**
Dry-docking is the operation of supporting a ship out of water on a rigid platform (keel blocks and bilge blocks) of a graving dock, floating dock, or marine railway for the purpose of inspection, maintenance, and repair of the underwater hull.

**Two mandatory occasions:**
1. During the **Intermediate Survey of the hull** (typically at the 2.5-year mark of the 5-year Special Survey cycle).
2. During the **Special Survey of the hull** (every 5 years), as mandated by IACS UR Z7.1.
3. (Optional third) After a grounding or major hull damage, for emergency inspection.

**Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- Definition — 1.5 marks
- Two occasions — 0.75 marks each

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

### Question A.2
**Q: What is a "docking plan" and list any four items it must contain.**

**Model Answer:**
A docking plan is a scaled engineering drawing showing the arrangement of keel blocks, bilge blocks, and side shores under and around the ship, prepared jointly by the dockyard and approved by the Class Surveyor and the ship's Superintendent.

**Four items:**
1. Position of keel blocks along the centreline of the dock floor.
2. Position of bilge blocks under the bilge radius.
3. Position of side shores against the dock walls.
4. Position of bilge shores, top shores, and any special supports under fin stabilizers or sonar domes.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Question]**

### Question B (A) — 14 Marks

**(a)** List and explain in detail the **seven steps to be taken BEFORE dry-docking**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **operational sequence of events DURING dry-docking**, from ship entry to settling on blocks. **[7 marks]**

### Model Answer

**(a) Seven Steps Before Dry-Docking (7 marks):**

| Step | Description | Marks |
|------|-------------|-------|
| 1. Class Notification | Notify classification society at least 3 months prior; obtain survey schedule. | 1 |
| 2. Dock Selection | Compare dock capacity (LOA, beam, lifting capacity, draft) with ship's docking plan. | 1 |
| 3. Docking Plan Approval | Yard prepares docking plan; approved by Class Surveyor and Superintendent. | 1 |
| 4. Defuel and Deballast | Reduce displacement to the lightest safe draft (typically 50-70% of design draft). | 1 |
| 5. Pre-Docking Survey | Master surveys hull, propeller, rudder, sea chests; issues Docking Memo. | 1 |
| 6. Safety Preparations | Gas-free tanks; close overboard valves; rig lifeboats; conduct toolbox talks. | 1 |
| 7. Towage Planning | Engage tugs; brief Docking Master on ship particulars and thruster capability. | 1 |

**(b) Operational Sequence During Docking (7 marks):**

1. **Entry** — Ship enters dock bow-first or stern-first under tug escort; mooring lines passed.
2. **Centering** — Docking hawsers (heavy wires) attached to bow and stern winches; ship centred above keel blocks using laser or plumb-line within ±25 mm tolerance.
3. **De-watering** — Dock pumps activated; water level dropped at controlled rate (≤0.3 m/min) to prevent slamming.
4. **Settling on Blocks** — Keel contacts keel blocks first, then bilges contact bilge blocks; block pressure monitored.
5. **Rigging Shores** — Side shores, bilge shores, and top shores rigged symmetrically.
6. **Survey and Cleaning** — High-pressure jetting, sandblasting, Class Survey, UTM thickness gauging.
7. **Repairs and Painting** — Steel renewal, anode replacement, propeller polishing, anti-fouling application.

---

### Question B (B) — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Describe the **seven steps to be taken AFTER dry-docking** in chronological order. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **role of the Classification Society Surveyor during dry-docking**. List the IACS Unified Requirements (UR) relevant to hull survey. **[7 marks]**

### Model Answer

**(a) Seven Steps After Dry-Docking (7 marks):**

1. **Final Class Survey and Endorsement** — Surveyor verifies all work and issues Class endorsement.
2. **Inspection of Underwater Fittings** — Sea chest grids, anodes, transducers re-fitted; drain plugs plugged.
3. **Removal of Shores** — Shores removed symmetrically to avoid sudden load shifts.
4. **Re-floating the Dock** — Dock re-flooded; ship lifts off blocks and floats free.
5. **Mooring at Fitting-Out Berth** — Ship moored alongside yard for sea trials.
6. **Sea Trials** — Engine, steering gear, navigation equipment, and anchoring trials conducted.
7. **Documentation and Departure** — Reports filed with Class; ship re-bunkers and sails.

**(b) Role of Classification Society Surveyor (7 marks):**

The Class Surveyor (called "Surveyor to Class" or "Attending Surveyor") is the **third-party independent technical authority** who:

- Verifies the ship's compliance with the Class Rules and statutory regulations.
- Conducts **close-up survey, thickness measurement (UTM), and non-destructive testing (NDT)** of the hull.
- Issues **Conditions of Class** for any deficiencies that must be rectified within a stipulated time.
- Endorses the **Class Certificate** after satisfactory survey.

**Relevant IACS Unified Requirements:**

| UR | Subject |
|----|---------|
| **Z7** | Hull Surveys for Oil Tankers (ESP — Enhanced Survey Programme) |
| **Z7.1** | Hull Surveys for Bulk Carriers (ESP) |
| **Z10** | Hull Surveys for ships other than tankers and bulk carriers |
| **Z17** | Procedural requirements for service suppliers |
| **Z23** | Hull survey for new construction |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **confuse IACS UR Z7 and Z7.1**. Remember: **Z7 = Oil Tankers**, **Z7.1 = Bulk Carriers**.
> - Students often **omit the pre-docking survey by the Master** as a separate step — this is a **mandatory** step and carries 1 mark.
> - Students forget to mention **trim by the stern** in pre-docking prep — examiners specifically look for this.
> - In the "during docking" answer, students miss **docking hawsers** — a key alignment tool.
> - In the "after docking" answer, students forget **plugging the drain plugs** before re-floating — a critical safety step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 21-Point Revision Checklist for Dry-docking**

### Pre-Docking Phase
- [ ] Class Society notification **at least 3 months prior**.
- [ ] Dock selection based on **LOA, beam, lifting capacity, water density**.
- [ ] **Docking plan** prepared by yard, approved by Class and Superintendent.
- [ ] **Deballast and defuel** to lightest safe draft.
- [ ] **Trim by stern** (~0.3–0.5 m) so propeller clears blocks.
- [ ] **Pre-docking survey** by Master covering hull, propeller, rudder, sea chests.
- [ ] **Gas-free tanks**, close overboard valves, rig lifeboats, brief tugs.

### During Docking Phase
- [ ] Ship enters **bow-first or stern-first** as per docking plan.
- [ ] **Centering** via docking hawsers with tolerance ±25 mm.
- [ ] **De-watering** at ≤0.3 m/min rate.
- [ ] **Keel touches first**, then bilges, then sides.
- [ ] **Side shores and bilge shores** rigged; number ≥ $L_{pp}/6$.
- [ ] **High-pressure water jet** at 250–350 bar removes fouling.
- [ ] **Class Survey, UTM, NDT, painting** as per UR Z7/Z7.1.

### Post-Docking Phase
- [ ] **Class endorsement** issued; **Conditions of Class** documented.
- [ ] **Drain plugs plugged** before re-flooding.
- [ ] **Shores removed symmetrically**.
- [ ] **Re-flood dock**, ship lifts off blocks.
- [ ] **UKC ≥ 0.5 m** monitored during undocking.
- [ ] **Sea trials** for engine, steering gear, navigation.
- [ ] **Documentation updated**, ship sails.

### Critical Formulas at a Glance
- [ ] $\Delta = L \times B \times T \times C_b \times \rho_w$
- [ ] $q_b = W / (n_b \times A_b)$
- [ ] $n_{\text{shores}} \geq L_{pp} / 6$
- [ ] $\text{UKC} \geq 0.5$ m
- [ ] $v_{\text{dock-rise}} \leq 0.3$ m/min

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick-Mnemonic — "PDD" = Plan-Dock-Deballast**
> **And "DRS" = Defuel-Rig-Survey** for pre-docking.
> **For during-docking: "ECDRS-SP" = Enter-Centre-De-water-Rig-Shores-Survey-Paint.**
> **For post-docking: "CSRRR-MD" = Certify-Shores-removed-Refloat-Re-float-Mooring-Depart.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Replacement of Hull Plates and Stiffeners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Replacement of Hull Plates and Stiffeners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hull plate and stiffener replacement** is a major dry-dock repair procedure in ship maintenance engineering wherein corroded, wasted, or mechanically damaged structural members of a vessel's outer shell and internal framing are systematically removed, and new steel sections are fabricated, fitted, and welded in compliance with classification society rules (e.g., IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS) and IACS unified requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Replacement refers to the *renewal* of plates or stiffeners whose **as-built thickness has been reduced by more than the allowable corrosion addition (typically 75%–80% of original)**, where renewals by ordinary repair welding are no longer structurally or economically viable.

**Key Terminology:**

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Wastage** | Loss of material thickness due to corrosion/erosion, measured in **mm** |
| **Renewal** | Cutting out and replacement of an entire structural member |
| **Partial Renewal** | Insertion of a new plate (butt strap or insert plate) covering only the wasted zone |
| **Cropping** | Removal of a damaged section by gas cutting or mechanical means |
| **Doubler Plate** | An additional plate fitted over a corroded area to restore strength |
| **Butt Strap** | A two-sided compensation plate used at the joint of a renewed section |
| **Stiffener** | A longitudinal or transverse rolled section (L, T, flat bar, bulb) reinforcing the plating |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Bone and Skin"**
> Think of a ship as a giant metallic "body." The **hull plates** are the **skin** protecting the inner organs, and the **stiffeners** are the **ribs and bones** holding the skin firm. When skin (plating) gets "rotten" beyond a certain depth (wastage), you cannot just patch it — you must *cut out the damaged patch and stitch in a fresh piece of skin*. Similarly, when a rib (stiffener) is too thin to bear load, the surgeon (ship-repair engineer) must remove the entire section and weld in a new one, ensuring the new "bone" aligns perfectly with the rest of the skeleton to share the structural load.

This analogy highlights the three pillars of replacement:
1. **Diagnosis** — Identifying the extent of "decay" (wastage gauging).
2. **Excision & Grafting** — Cutting out and inserting new steel.
3. **Healing & Verification** — Welding, NDT, and load testing.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Allowable wastage limit before mandatory renewal:** **75%** of as-built thickness for plates; **80%** for stiffeners (as per IRS Rules).
> - **Minimum thickness for one-side renewal:** Not less than the rule minimum minus **1.0 mm**.
> - **Standard renewal plate thickness margin:** New plate thickness ≥ original rule thickness + **0.5 mm** (rolling margin).
> - **Butt weld efficiency factor:** Typically **0.85 to 1.00** depending on NDT coverage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Replacement Becomes Necessary — The "Why" Behind Renewal

The decision to replace a plate or stiffener is governed by the **structural integrity equation**:

$$\text{Service Life Remaining} = f(\text{Corrosion Rate}, \text{Residual Strength}, \text{Fatigue Damage})$$

A member is renewed when **any** of the following criteria are breached:

1. **Wastage exceeds allowable limit** (75%–80% of original).
2. **Pitting depth** exceeds **15%–20%** of plate thickness in way of stress-concentration zones.
3. **Permanent set / deformation** (e.g., dent, buckle, fracture) is present.
4. **Class survey condition** — Class surveyor issues a "Condition of Class" (COC) recommending renewal before next survey.

### 2.2 Procedural Logic — Step-by-Step Engineering Flow

The replacement operation follows this structured sequence (often examined in KTU papers):

1. **Pre-Planning & Marking**
   - Obtain class-approved drawings.
   - Mark the renewal boundary with chalk/markings; the boundary should be on **sound material** (at least 50–100 mm beyond the wasted zone).
   - Identify the boundary orientation: **horizontal seams** are preferred to **vertical** to reduce stress concentrations.

2. **Pre-Heating & Safety Setup**
   - Adjacent structures are protected with fire blankets and asbestos sheets.
   - Pre-heat temperature for mild steel: **75 °C–150 °C**; for higher-tensile steel (AH36, DH36): **100 °C–175 °C**.

3. **Cropping (Removal)**
   - **Cold method:** Chipping, gouging, or shearing (for thin plates < 6 mm).
   - **Hot method:** Oxygen/fuel gas cutting (preferred for thicker plates).
   - Cuts are made *just outside* the marked line, leaving **2 mm–3 mm** for edge preparation by grinding.

4. **Edge Preparation**
   - Bevelling the cut edges to a **V-groove** (typically 30°–45° included angle) for full-penetration butt welds.
   - Root gap: **2 mm–3 mm**.

5. **New Plate/Stiffener Fabrication**
   - Rolled or pressed to the correct curvature (sag, camber, sheer).
   - Trial fitting with tack welds (50 mm–75 mm length, 200 mm–300 mm pitch).

6. **Welding**
   - Butt welds (full penetration) for plate-to-plate joints.
   - Fillet welds for stiffener connections.
   - Sequence: **weld from the centre outwards** to minimize distortion.

7. **Post-Weld Treatment**
   - Grinding flush if required.
   - Stress-relief annealing (for thick plates > 20 mm) at **600 °C ± 25 °C** for **1 hour per 25 mm thickness**.

8. **Inspection & NDT**
   - **100% Visual Examination (VT)** of all welds.
   - **Radiographic Testing (RT)** or **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** of butt welds.
   - **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)** for fillet welds and edge prep zones.
   - **Dye Penetrant Test (DPT)** for non-magnetic stainless welds.

9. **Final Documentation**
   - Welding logs, NDT reports, and class surveyor's endorsement.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the essential formulas, constants, and acceptance criteria. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown integrity.

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Remarks |
|-----------|----------------|-------|---------|
| Wastage percentage | $\text{W\%} = \dfrac{t_{original} - t_{as\text{-}built}}{t_{original}} \times 100$ | % | Gauged with UT thickness gauge |
| Minimum renewal thickness | $t_{min} = 0.75 \times t_{as\text{-}built}$ | mm | IRS rule for plates |
| Renewal plate thickness | $t_{new} = t_{rule} + 0.5$ | mm | Includes rolling tolerance |
| Corrosion rate | $CR = \dfrac{\Delta t}{\Delta T}$ | mm/year | $\Delta t$ = thickness loss, $\Delta T$ = time interval |
| Remaining life | $RL = \dfrac{t_{as\text{-}built} - t_{min}}{CR}$ | years | Used in survey planning |
| Pre-heat temperature (mild steel) | $T_p = 75\,^\circ\text{C to } 150\,^\circ\text{C}$ | °C | To prevent HAZ cracking |
| Pre-heat temperature (AH/DH 36) | $T_p = 100\,^\circ\text{C to } 175\,^\circ\text{C}$ | °C | Higher tensile steels |
| Stress relief temperature | $T_{sr} = 600 \pm 25$ | °C | For plates $\geq 20$ mm |
| Stress relief duration | $H = 25 \times t_{plate}$ | hour per 25 mm | Minimum 1 hour |
| Butt weld efficiency | $\eta = 0.85$ to $1.00$ | – | Depends on NDT coverage |
| Fillet weld leg size | $z = 0.707 \times \vert a - b \vert$ | mm | For unequal leg fillet |
| Throat thickness (fillet) | $t_t = 0.707 \times s$ | mm | $s$ = leg size |
| Allowable pitting depth | $p_{allow} \leq 0.15 \times t_{as\text{-}built}$ | mm | In stress zones |
| Heat input (welding) | $E = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | $V$=voltage, $I$=current, $v$=travel speed |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Classification Compliance:** Enables the vessel to retain its class and continue commercial operation.
- **Safety & Life Extension:** Prevents catastrophic structural failure (e.g., hull girder collapse).
- **Economic Optimization:** Allows ship owners to plan dry-dockings based on calculated remaining life.
- **Insurance Validity:** Most marine insurance policies mandate class-society-approved renewals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Corrosion Rate and Remaining Life

The **corrosion rate (CR)** is the time-rate of thickness loss, expressed in **mm/year**:

$$CR = \frac{\Delta t}{\Delta T} = \frac{t_{initial} - t_{measured}}{T_2 - T_1}$$

where $t_{initial}$ and $t_{measured}$ are the plate thicknesses at times $T_1$ and $T_2$, respectively.

Once $CR$ is known, the **remaining service life (RL)** before the thickness reaches the allowable minimum is:

$$RL = \frac{t_{as\text{-}built} - t_{min}}{CR}$$

**Substituting** the rule limit $t_{min} = 0.75 \times t_{as\text{-}built}$:

$$RL = \frac{t_{as\text{-}built} - 0.75 \cdot t_{as\text{-}built}}{CR} = \frac{0.25 \cdot t_{as\text{-}built}}{CR}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style Problem

> **Problem:** A hull plate in the side shell of a bulk carrier has an as-built thickness of **18.0 mm**. During the intermediate survey, the average measured thickness is **13.5 mm**. The last gauging was done **2.5 years** ago, when the average thickness was **14.6 mm**. Determine:
> 1. The current **wastage percentage**.
> 2. The **corrosion rate**.
> 3. The **remaining life** in years.
> 4. Whether **renewal** is mandatory now (per IRS limit).

#### **Given Data**

$$t_{as\text{-}built} = 18.0\ \text{mm}, \quad t_{current} = 13.5\ \text{mm}, \quad t_{2.5y\text{ ago}} = 14.6\ \text{mm}, \quad \Delta T = 2.5\ \text{years}$$

#### **Step 1 — Current Wastage Percentage**

$$\text{W\%} = \frac{t_{as\text{-}built} - t_{current}}{t_{as\text{-}built}} \times 100$$

$$\text{W\%} = \frac{18.0 - 13.5}{18.0} \times 100 = \frac{4.5}{18.0} \times 100$$

$$\text{W\%} = 25.0\%$$

#### **Step 2 — Corrosion Rate**

$$CR = \frac{t_{2.5y\text{ ago}} - t_{current}}{\Delta T} = \frac{14.6 - 13.5}{2.5} = \frac{1.1}{2.5}$$

$$CR = 0.44\ \text{mm/year}$$

#### **Step 3 — Allowable Minimum Thickness (per IRS Rule)**

$$t_{min} = 0.75 \times t_{as\text{-}built} = 0.75 \times 18.0 = 13.5\ \text{mm}$$

#### **Step 4 — Remaining Life**

$$RL = \frac{t_{current} - t_{min}}{CR} = \frac{13.5 - 13.5}{0.44} = 0\ \text{years}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The plate is exactly at the limit.** Renewal is **mandatory and immediate** — it cannot be deferred to the next scheduled dry-dock.

#### **Step 5 — Recommended Renewal Plate Thickness**

$$t_{new} = t_{rule} + 0.5 = 18.0 + 0.5 = 18.5\ \text{mm}$$

(Rolled to nearest commercial size: typically **19.0 mm**.)

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Replacement Scenarios Decision Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a high-yield KTU reference for selecting the correct renewal method.

| Wastage Range | Damage Type | Recommended Action | Justification |
|---------------|-------------|--------------------|--------------|
| 0% – 25% | Minor surface corrosion | Grit blast + protective coating | Within allowable limit |
| 25% – 50% | Moderate pitting | Doubling or weld build-up + grinding | Localized repair, no renewal |
| 50% – 75% | Severe local wastage | **Partial renewal** (insert plate) | Localized crop-out and graft |
| > 75% | Extensive wastage / fracture | **Full renewal** of plate or stiffener | Whole member replaced |
| Any | Buckling, fracture, dent | Renewal or cropping | Independent of wastage % |

### 3.4 Heat Input & Distortion Control — Analytical Derivation

Heat input is a critical parameter in hull plate replacement because excessive input causes **angular distortion** $\Delta\theta$ (in radians):

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{k \cdot E \cdot t}{c \cdot \rho \cdot s^2}$$

where $E$ is heat input (kJ/mm), $t$ is plate thickness, $s$ is weld leg size, $k$ is the restraint factor, and $c \cdot \rho$ is volumetric specific heat. To keep distortion within tolerance, weld sequence must alternate (e.g., **"back-step"** or **"skip"** technique).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Hull Plate Replacement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Survey and Gauging] --> B[Wastage Assessment]
    B --> C{Wastage Exceeds 75%?}
    C -- No --> D[Surface Preparation and Coating]
    C -- Yes --> E[Mark Renewal Boundary on Sound Steel]
    E --> F[Set Up Fire Blankets and Pre-Heat]
    F --> G[Hot Crop the Damaged Plate]
    G --> H[Grind and Prepare V Groove Edges]
    H --> I[Fabricate and Trial Fit New Plate]
    I --> J[Tack Weld at 300 mm Pitch]
    J --> K[Full Butt Weld from Centre Outward]
    K --> L[Grind Flush and Stress Relieve]
    L --> M[NDT Inspection VT RT UT MPT]
    M --> N{Pass Acceptance?}
    N -- No --> O[Defect Repair and Re-Test]
    N -- Yes --> P[Class Surveyor Endorsement]
    P --> Q[Final Coating and Documentation]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Stiffener Renewal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Original Stiffener] --> A2{Corrosion or Damage Check}
    A2 -- Within Limit --> A3[Local Grinding and Fairing]
    A2 -- Beyond Limit --> B1[Mark Cut Lines at 50 mm Beyond Damage]
    B1 --> B2[Gas Cut Stiffener Web]
    B2 --> B3[Grind Bracket Ends to Bright Metal]
    B3 --> B4[Insert New Stiffener Section]
    B4 --> B5[Tack and Fillet Weld Brackets]
    B5 --> B6[Continuous Fillet Weld to Plate]
    B6 --> B7[MPT Inspection of Fillet Welds]
    B7 --> B8[Approved Stiffener Section]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Renewal Joint Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph J1[Single Plate Renewal Butt Joint]
        P1[Old Plate] --- W1[Butt Weld Full Penetration]
        W1 --- P2[New Plate Insert]
    end
    subgraph J2[Stiffener Renewal Scarf Joint]
        S1[Old Stiffener] --- SC1[Scarf Cut 1 in 4 Slope]
        SC1 --- NW[New Stiffener Section]
        NW --- SC2[Scarf Cut 1 in 4 Slope]
        SC2 --- S2[Old Stiffener]
    end
    subgraph J3[Compensation Doubler]
        DP1[Corroded Plate] --- CW[Continuous Fillet Weld]
        CW --- DP2[Doubler Plate]
    end
```

### 4.4 Schematic Block — Distortion Control in Welding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DC1[Multi-Pass Butt Weld] --> DC2[Apply Back-Step Technique]
    DC2 --> DC3[Alternate Sides of Joint]
    DC3 --> DC4[Clamp or Peen Between Passes]
    DC4 --> DC5[Reduced Angular Distortion]
    DC5 --> DC6[Within Tolerance Less Than 1 Degree]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

#### **Q1. Define wastage as applied to ship hull plates. What is the maximum allowable wastage before mandatory renewal? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Wastage is the loss of original plate thickness due to corrosion, erosion, or mechanical wear, measured by ultrasonic thickness gauging. The maximum allowable wastage before mandatory renewal, as per IRS classification rules, is **75% of the as-built thickness for plates** and **80% for stiffeners**. Beyond this limit, the residual strength falls below the rule minimum, and renewal or partial renewal is required to retain the vessel's class.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

#### **Q2. List any three NDT methods used after hull plate renewal and state their specific applications. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Visual Testing (VT):** 100% of all welds for surface defects.
2. **Radiographic Testing (RT):** Butt welds for internal volumetric defects (porosity, inclusions).
3. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT):** Butt welds, especially in thick plates, for planar defects (cracks, lack of fusion).
4. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT):** Fillet welds and edge prep zones for surface and near-surface cracks (magnetic materials only).

*(Any three with correct application — full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question — Set A]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the step-by-step procedure for replacement of a corroded side shell plate in a dry dock. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A bottom plate of as-built thickness **22.0 mm** is found with an average wastage of **6.2 mm** after **3 years** of service. The previous gauging 3 years ago showed a thickness of **19.0 mm**. Determine the wastage percentage, corrosion rate, and remaining service life. State whether renewal is required as per IRS rules. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The student should describe the following steps concisely (each step ~1 mark):

1. **Survey & Gauging:** UT thickness measurement over 1 m grid.
2. **Marking:** Renewal boundary marked on sound steel 50–100 mm beyond wasted area.
3. **Pre-Heating:** Mild steel to 100 °C; AH36 to 150 °C.
4. **Cropping:** Hot gas cutting of damaged plate, 2–3 mm outside marked line.
5. **Edge Preparation:** V-groove grinding, 30°–45° bevel.
6. **New Plate Fabrication:** Rolled to correct curvature, trial-fitted.
7. **Tack Welding:** 50–75 mm tacks at 200–300 mm pitch.
8. **Butt Welding:** Multi-pass full penetration, centre-outwards sequence.
9. **Post-Weld Treatment:** Grinding, stress relief at 600 °C if $t \geq 20$ mm.
10. **NDT Inspection:** VT, RT/UT, MPT, and class endorsement.
11. **Coating & Documentation:** Final painting and survey records.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — As-built thickness and current thickness:**

$$t_{as\text{-}built} = 22.0\ \text{mm}, \quad t_{current} = 22.0 - 6.2 = 15.8\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Wastage percentage:**

$$W\% = \frac{6.2}{22.0} \times 100 = 28.18\%$$

**Step 3 — Corrosion rate (using 3-year gauging interval):**

$$CR = \frac{19.0 - 15.8}{3} = \frac{3.2}{3} = 1.067\ \text{mm/year}$$

**Step 4 — Allowable minimum thickness:**

$$t_{min} = 0.75 \times 22.0 = 16.5\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Remaining life:**

$$RL = \frac{t_{current} - t_{min}}{CR} = \frac{15.8 - 16.5}{1.067} = \frac{-0.7}{1.067} = -0.656\ \text{years}$$

Since $t_{current} = 15.8$ mm is **less than** $t_{min} = 16.5$ mm, the **negative remaining life** confirms that **renewal is mandatory and overdue.**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question — Set B]**

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Explain the various methods of cropping damaged hull plates. Compare hot cropping with cold cropping methods. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** During a special survey, a transverse stiffener of **L 100 × 75 × 8 mm** is found wasted to an average thickness of **6.4 mm**. The original thickness is **8.0 mm**, and the estimated corrosion rate is **0.25 mm/year**. Calculate the wastage percentage and the remaining life. Recommend the appropriate renewal action. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Methods of Cropping Damaged Hull Plates:**

| Method | Process | Applicability |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| **Oxygen/Fuel Gas Cutting** | Exothermic reaction melts & oxidizes steel | Most common; plates $\geq 6$ mm |
| **Plasma Arc Cutting** | High-velocity ionized gas jet | Stainless steel, non-ferrous |
| **Carbon Arc Gouging** | Carbon electrode with air blast | Removal of welds, root runs |
| **Chiselling/Chipping** | Manual or pneumatic chisel | Thin plates, localized cuts |
| **Shearing** | Mechanical guillotine | Thin shell plates, in-shop |
| **Sawing** | Power hacksaw or band saw | Stiffeners, brackets |

**Comparison — Hot vs Cold Cropping:**

| Aspect | Hot Cropping (Gas / Plasma) | Cold Cropping (Shear / Chip) |
|--------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| Speed | Fast | Slower |
| Heat affected zone | Yes (requires pre/post-heat control) | None |
| Edge quality | Rough, needs grinding | Clean, minimal grinding |
| Plate thickness | Any | Limited to thin plates |
| Distortion | Possible, controlled by sequence | Negligible |
| Preferred for | Heavy ship plates | Thin plates and in-shop work |

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Wastage percentage:**

$$W\% = \frac{8.0 - 6.4}{8.0} \times 100 = \frac{1.6}{8.0} \times 100 = 20.0\%$$

**Step 2 — Allowable minimum thickness (stiffener, 80% limit):**

$$t_{min} = 0.80 \times 8.0 = 6.4\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Remaining life:**

$$RL = \frac{t_{current} - t_{min}}{CR} = \frac{6.4 - 6.4}{0.25} = 0\ \text{years}$$

**Step 4 — Recommendation:**

The stiffener is at the **exact allowable limit** (20% wastage = 80% residual). The corrosion rate is low, but the **remaining life is zero**. **Renewal or partial renewal (insert section) is required** before the next survey. Since the original L-section is a standard rolled section, a like-for-like replacement is recommended with the bracket ends cropped and re-welded.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing plate limit (75%) with stiffener limit (80%)** — Examiners award 1 mark for knowing the correct limit.
> 2. **Forgetting the rolling margin (+0.5 mm)** when specifying renewal plate thickness.
> 3. **Not stating the pre-heat temperature range** — this is a frequently-asked 2-mark sub-part.
> 4. **Omitting NDT methods** — students must always close the procedure with VT + RT/UT + MPT.
> 5. **Numerical errors in remaining life** — sign convention matters: negative RL means renewal is **overdue**.
> 6. **Not mentioning class surveyor's endorsement** — a mandatory step in the procedure.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Wastage limits:** **75%** for plates, **80%** for stiffeners (IRS).
- **Renewal plate thickness rule:** $t_{new} = t_{rule} + 0.5$ mm.
- **Pre-heat:** 75–150 °C (mild steel); 100–175 °C (AH/DH 36).
- **Stress relief:** **600 °C ± 25 °C**, 1 hour per 25 mm thickness (for $t \geq 20$ mm).
- **Cropping methods:** Hot (gas/plasma) for thick plates; Cold (shear/chip) for thin plates.
- **Edge prep:** V-groove, 30°–45° included angle, 2–3 mm root gap.
- **Welding sequence:** Centre outwards, alternate sides, back-step technique for distortion control.
- **NDT coverage:** 100% VT; RT or UT for butt welds; MPT for fillet welds; DPT for stainless.
- **Fillet weld throat:** $t_t = 0.707 \times s$.
- **Heat input formula:** $E = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000}$ kJ/mm.
- **Renewal boundary placement:** On **sound steel**, 50–100 mm beyond damaged zone; horizontal seams preferred.
- **Butt weld efficiency factor:** 0.85–1.00 depending on NDT coverage.
- **Class society endorsement** is the final mandatory step before returning the vessel to service.
- **Doubler plates** are an alternative to full renewal when wastage is moderate and localized.
- **Stiffener brackets** must be cropped to **bright metal** before rewelding (no crevice corrosion left behind).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decks and Bulkheads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Decks and Bulkheads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition — Decks (Ship Structural Term)

A **deck** is a permanent horizontal plate structure, typically stiffened, that forms the floor of an upper level and the **roof of a lower compartment** in a ship's hull. The **strength deck** (or weather deck) is the uppermost continuous deck that contributes to the **longitudinal strength** of the hull girder, while decks below it are called **tween decks** or platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim-aligned):** *Decks are horizontal plating systems comprising plating, longitudinal/transverse stiffeners, deck beams, deck girders, and supporting pillars, designed to transmit transverse and longitudinal loads to the ship's primary structure.*

### 1.2 Definition — Bulkheads (Ship Structural Term)

A **bulkhead** is a vertical plate structure, usually stiffened, that divides the ship into **watertight, gastight, or fire-resistant compartments**. Bulkheads provide:
- **Transverse strength** (resistance to racking forces from sea, cargo, or grounding).
- **Compartmentation** (limiting flooding, fire, smoke spread).
- **Reinforcement** at points of high local stress (e.g., engine seat, forepeak).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *A bulkhead is a vertical partition wall in a ship formed by plating stiffened by webs, flanges, and stiffeners, classified by orientation (transverse/longitudinal) and function (watertight, fire-resisting, collision, swash, etc.).*

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Decks = Floors of a Building; Bulkheads = Walls"

Imagine a multi-storey building floating on water:

- **Decks** are the **concrete floor slabs** of each storey. They carry the weight of people, furniture, and equipment, and transfer it down to pillars and the ship's side.
- **Bulkheads** are the **internal walls** between rooms. They keep water (or fire) from spreading from one flooded room to the rest of the building. The *collision bulkhead* is like the **front door** — it stops water rushing in if a car crashes into the building.

A real ship is just this: a floating multi-storey steel building where decks carry loads horizontally and bulkheads compartmentalize vertical risks.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Classification Society)

- **Hull Steel Grade:** Grade A / Grade AH / Grade DH (yield strength **235 MPa** for ordinary strength).
- **Net Scantling:** "As-built" thickness minus **corrosion addition** (typically **+0.5 mm to +3.0 mm** per side, depending on IACS UR S21/S22).
- **Design Wave Head:** **2.5 m to 8.0 m** depending on vessel size (Common Structural Rules).
- **Floodable Length Factor:** Permeability **μ = 0.60 to 0.95** (per SOLAS).
- **Corrosion Rate (in-service):** **0.1 mm/year to 0.3 mm/year** for general hull; up to **0.5 mm/year** in ballast tanks (per IACS Recommendation 76).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for deck/bulkhead classification — "T-F-C-P"**: **T**ight (watertight), **F**ire-resisting, **C**ollision, **P**artial (splash/non-tight). Memorize this for Part A.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of Decks (top → down)

1. **Weather/Strength Deck** — exposed to sea & contributes to hull girder bending.
2. **Second Deck** — between weather deck and first platform.
3. **Tween Decks** — between main decks for cargo subdivision.
4. **Platform Decks** — small partial decks for local stiffening (e.g., engine room platform).
5. **Inner Bottom / Tank Top** — the **lowest deck**, forming top of double bottom.

**Load Path on a Deck:**

$$
\text{Deck Plating} \;\to\; \text{Deck Longitudinals (stiffeners)} \;\to\; \text{Deck Beams (transverse)} \;\to\; \text{Deck Girders (longitudinal)} \;\to\; \text{Pillars / Side Shell}
$$

### 2.2 Types of Bulkheads (Classification by Function)

| Type | Location | Function | Material Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Collision Bulkhead** | Aft of forepeak (typically frame 2) | Prevents flooding from bow collision | High — **20% extra** scantling margin |
| **Watertight Bulkhead (WT)** | Throughout hull | Flooding containment per SOLAS | Tightness test at survey |
| **Gastight Bulkhead** | Around boilers, fuel tanks | Stops flammable gas spread | Tested at **0.14 bar** |
| **Fire-Resisting Bulkhead** | Class A / B / C divisions | Limits fire spread (60/30/0 min) | Class A — steel **4 mm to 10 mm** |
| **Swash Bulkhead** | Inside tanks | Reduces sloshing | Perforated/plate-stiffened |
| **Engine Room Bulkhead** | Forward & aft of E/R | Sub-divides E/R | Stiffened for vibration |
| **Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead** | Above shaft | Watertight boundary | High corrosion (oily bilge) |

### 2.3 Structural Hierarchy of Bulkheads

$$
\text{Bulkhead Plating} \;\to\; \text{Vertical Stiffeners (webs)} \;\to\; \text{Horizontal Girders (struts)} \;\to\; \text{Bulkhead Shedder Plates / Brackets} \;\to\; \text{Deck/Side/Bottom Shell}
$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t = t_{net} + t_{c}$ | Net thickness to as-built: net + **corrosion addition** $t_c$ | mm |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress in deck stringer / strength deck | N/mm² |
| 3 | $I = \dfrac{b \cdot t^{3}}{12}$ | Moment of inertia of plate strip (width $b$, thickness $t$) | mm⁴ |
| 4 | $Z = \dfrac{b \cdot t^{2}}{6}$ | Section modulus of plate | mm³ |
| 5 | $P_{pillar} = 0.6 \cdot \sigma_{y} \cdot A$ | Pillar buckling capacity (slenderness < 30) | kN |
| 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{i}$ | Slenderness ratio for pillar/girder | dimensionless |
| 7 | $h_{flood} = \mu \cdot V$ | Floodable length criterion (permeability $\mu$) | m³ |
| 8 | $q_{deck} = \rho g h_{wave}$ | Sea pressure on weather deck | kN/m² |
| 9 | $w_{allow} = \dfrac{5 \cdot q \cdot L^{4}}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Deflection limit check for deck plating | mm |
| 10 | $A_{corr} = R \cdot T$ | Corrosion wastage: Rate × Time | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **IACS UR S21** (Hatch Covers) and **IACS UR S22** (Cargo Holds) define **corrosion addition $t_c$** values: typically **+0.5 mm** for hatch covers, **+1.0 mm** to **+2.5 mm** for bulk carrier cargo hold sides, **+2.0 mm** for ballast tank boundaries.

### 2.5 Real-World Survey Engineering Utility

In **classification society survey work** (DNV, LR, BV, ABS), the surveyor measures **as-built thickness** $t_{actual}$ with an **ultrasonic thickness gauge (UTG)**. If:

$$
t_{actual} \;\geq\; t_{ren} \quad \text{(renewal / wastage limit)}
$$

… the plate **passes**. If $t_{actual} < t_{ren}$, the plate is either:
- **Condition "GOOD"** — continue in service with reduced margin.
- **Condition "FAIR"** — restricted trading area.
- **Renewal required** — within next survey window.

The **"substantial corrosion"** threshold is **75% to 80%** of original as-built thickness. Below this, the area is flagged for special attention.

### 2.6 Why "Decks and Bulkheads" Matter in Repair Estimation

When preparing a **repair specification** (the *spec*) for a ship entering dry-dock, the estimator must compute:

1. **Steel renewal area** for corroded deck plates and bulkhead plates (m²).
2. **Renewal weight** = Area × $t_{ren}$ × Steel density (**7850 kg/m³**).
3. **Unit rate** for plate renewal, blasting, primer, fabrication, and welding (INR/m² or USD/tonne).
4. **Man-hours** for cutting-out, fitting, welding (typically **20 to 35 hrs/tonne** for hull plate renewal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** *In all repair estimates, the surveyor's "as-built thickness report" is the **primary input document**. Without UTG readings, an estimator cannot quantify renewal scope.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
