## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Deck Plate Thickness (IACS CSR-style, simplified)

The minimum required net thickness for a deck plate is governed by the **panel** between stiffeners and the **combined** plate-stiffener system.

**Step 1 — Identify the panel:**

For a deck plate between longitudinal stiffeners, the panel is rectangular with:
- Length $s$ = stiffener spacing (e.g., **600 mm** to **900 mm**).
- Width $b$ = plate breadth = 1000 mm (per metre strip).

**Step 2 — Apply the panel yield criterion:**

For one-way bending of the plate strip under design pressure $p$:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{p \cdot b \cdot s^{2}}{8} \quad \text{(N·mm per mm width)}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate to plastic moment capacity of plate:**

$$
M_{p} = \frac{b \cdot t^{2}}{4} \cdot \sigma_{y}
$$

**Step 4 — Set $M_{max} = M_p$ and solve for $t$:**

$$
\frac{p \cdot b \cdot s^{2}}{8} = \frac{b \cdot t^{2}}{4} \cdot \sigma_{y}
$$

Cancelling $b$ from both sides:

$$
t^{2} = \frac{p \cdot s^{2}}{2 \cdot \sigma_{y}}
$$

$$
t = s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{2 \cdot \sigma_{y}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Add corrosion addition $t_c$:**

$$
t_{ren} = s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{2 \cdot \sigma_{y}}} + t_{c}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **simplified closed-form** for understanding. In actual CSR rules, the formula is non-dimensional and includes **k** (boundary condition factor) and **m₁, m₂** (moment factors), and uses **$p_{d} = p \cdot 10^{4}$** in kN/m². KTU 2024 questions on this topic test the **logic** of the derivation, not the full CSR constants.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Deck Plate Renewal

**Given:**
- Stiffener spacing $s$ = **750 mm**.
- Design pressure $p$ = **35 kN/m²** = 0.035 N/mm².
- Steel yield $\sigma_y$ = **235 N/mm²**.
- Corrosion addition $t_c$ = **1.5 mm**.

**Step 1 — Convert pressure:**

$$
p = 35 \text{ kN/m}^{2} = \frac{35 \times 10^{3}}{10^{6}} = 0.035 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply net thickness formula:**

$$
t_{net} = s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{2 \cdot \sigma_{y}}}
$$

$$
t_{net} = 750 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.035}{2 \times 235}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracket:**

$$
\dfrac{0.035}{470} = 7.447 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\sqrt{7.447 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.008630
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$
t_{net} = 750 \times 0.008630 = 6.47 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Add corrosion addition:**

$$
t_{ren} = 6.47 + 1.5 = 7.97 \text{ mm} \approx 8.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** Use **8 mm** plate for renewal.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Deck/Bulkhead Survey Findings

| Finding | Probable Cause | Surveyor Action | Repair Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pitting (groove pitting)** | Coating breakdown, tankage water | Measure pit depth, % area | Grit blast, coat, renewal if > 75% |
| **Grooving at bulkhead-stiffener intersection** | Stress concentration | UT gauge, crack dye-pen | Crop & renew plate insert |
| **Buckled deck plating** | Slamming load, over-pressure | Visual + UT | Renew panel + check pillars |
| **Corroded bulkhead plating** | Cargo sweat, tank water | UT sweep, % wastage | Renew plate insert |
| **Cracks at hatch corners** | Shear lag, fatigue | MPI / UT | Doubler plate renewal |
| **Loose/rusted pillars** | Coating failure, condensation | Visual + hammer tap | Renew pillar + shoe |
| **Frayed/broken stiffener web** | Local impact, corrosion | Visual, dimensional | Renew stiffener |
| **Hole in tank top** | Pitting coalescence | Visual, UT | Insert plate renewal |

### 3.4 Derivation: Renewal Steel Weight Estimate

For **150 m²** of deck plate renewal at 8 mm thickness:

$$
W = A \cdot t \cdot \rho
$$

$$
W = 150 \text{ m}^{2} \times 0.008 \text{ m} \times 7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}
$$

$$
W = 9.42 \text{ tonnes}
$$

At a typical dry-dock rate of **INR 1,20,000 per tonne** for hull plate renewal (2024 rates), the cost is:

$$
C = 9.42 \times 1.2 \text{ lakh} = \text{INR } 11.3 \text{ lakh (approx.)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Always show **units** in every calculation step. Examiners deduct marks when units are missing in intermediate steps.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Deck Cross-Section (Mermaid Block Diagram — Transverse View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Weather Deck Plate\n10 mm to 14 mm] --> A2[Deck Longitudinals\nT 100x12 every 600 mm]
    A2 --> A3[Deck Beam Transverse\nT 200x9 at every frame]
    A3 --> A4[Deck Girder Longitudinal\nT 300x10 + face plate]
    A4 --> A5[Pillar Vertical Support\nOD 100 to 200 mm pipe]
    A5 --> A6[Inner Bottom / Tank Top\n8 mm to 12 mm]
    A6 --> A7[Double Bottom Plate]
    A7 --> A8[Keel Plate]
    A1 -.-> A9[Sheer strake and side shell]
    A6 -.-> A9
```

### 4.2 Bulkhead Layout (Mermaid Flow — Classification by Function)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Collision Bulkhead\nat frame 2] --> B2[Watertight Bulkheads\nat frames 6, 12, 18, ...]
    B2 --> B3[Engine Room\nBulkhead]
    B3 --> B4[Aft Peak\nBulkhead]
    B2 --> B5[Swash Bulkheads\ninside DB tanks]
    B2 --> B6[Fire Resisting\nClass A or B]
    B1 --> B7[Forepeak\nTank]
    B4 --> B8[Steering Gear\nCompartment]
```

### 4.3 Load Path Block Diagram (Survey & Repair View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Cargo or Sea Load] --> L2{Deck Plating}
    L2 --> L3[Longitudinals]
    L2 --> L4[Beams]
    L3 --> L5[Deck Girder]
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6{Pillar}
    L6 --> L7[Inner Bottom]
    L6 --> L8[Double Bottom Plate]
    L7 --> L9[Hull Girder Bottom]
    L8 --> L9
    L2 -.Corrosion.-> L10[Renewal Scope]
    L4 -.Crack.-> L10
    L6 -.Buckling.-> L10
    L10 --> L11[Repair Specification\nand Cost Estimate]
```

### 4.4 Bulkhead Stiffener Detail (Section View — ASCII Schematic)

```
       |--|-- Deck Plate ----|------------|------------|
          |                   |  Stiffener |
          |      Z-Section    |   Web      |
          |      ┌───┐        |  ┌───┐     |
          |      │   │ flange |  │   │     |
          |      │   │ ───────|──│   │─────|
          |      │   │        |  │   │     |
          |      │   │        |  │   │     |
          |      │   │        |  │   │     |
          |      │   │ ───────|──│   │─────|
          |      └───┘ flange |  └───┘     |
          |                   |            |
       |--|-------------------|------------|----------|
       Bulkhead Plating (continuous)
```

### 4.5 Decision Flow — When to Renew a Deck/Bulkhead Plate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[UTG Reading Obtained] --> D2{t_actual >= t_ren?}
    D2 -- Yes --> D3[Acceptable\nNo action]
    D2 -- No --> D4{t_actual >= 75% t_original?}
    D4 -- Yes --> D5[Substantial Corrosion\nMeasure adjacent plates]
    D4 -- No --> D6[Renewal Required\nInsert plate]
    D5 --> D7[Coating renewal\nSurface prep + paint]
    D6 --> D8[Crop out defective plate\nFit new plate\nWeld + NDT + Blast + Coat]
    D8 --> D9[Update Thickness Report\nand Class file]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
Define a *collision bulkhead*. State its typical location and the regulation governing its position.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A collision bulkhead is a **watertight transverse bulkhead** fitted in the forepeak of a ship to prevent flooding of the entire hull in the event of a bow collision. As per **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 12**, the collision bulkhead must be located:
- Not less than **5% of the ship's length (L)** from the forward perpendicular, and
- Not more than **3 m + 5% of L** from the forward perpendicular.
(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — location, 1 mark — SOLAS rule.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between the **strength deck** and a **platform deck** with respect to their structural role.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **strength deck** (or weather deck) is the uppermost continuous deck that **participates in the longitudinal hull girder bending**. It carries compressive and tensile stresses together with the bottom shell during hogging/sagging. A **platform deck** is a partial, secondary deck that does **not** contribute to hull girder strength; it is used for **local stiffening** (e.g., engine room platform) and to support machinery or subdivide a tank. (1.5 marks each aspect.)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of bulkheads** based on function. For each type, state the location, purpose, and survey/inspection requirement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship’s strength deck plating has the following data: stiffener spacing $s = 800$ mm, design pressure $p = 40$ kN/m², yield stress $\sigma_y = 235$ N/mm², and corrosion addition $t_c = 2.0$ mm. Calculate the **renewal (as-built) thickness** of the deck plate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to the table in Section 2.2 above. The full answer must list **6 to 7 types** (Collision, WT, Gastight, Fire-resisting, Swash, Engine Room, Shaft Tunnel) with:
- Location (1.5 marks)
- Purpose (1.5 marks)
- Survey requirement (1.5 marks)
- Presentation/coherence (1 mark)
- Reference to SOLAS / Class rules (1.5 marks)

**(b) Step-by-step Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert pressure to N/mm²:

$$
p = \frac{40 \times 10^{3}}{10^{6}} = 0.040 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

Step 2 — Apply the net thickness formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$
t_{net} = s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{2 \cdot \sigma_{y}}}
$$

Step 3 — Substitute:

$$
t_{net} = 800 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.040}{2 \times 235}}
$$

Step 4 — Compute the fraction:

$$
\dfrac{0.040}{470} = 8.511 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Step 5 — Square root:

$$
\sqrt{8.511 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.009226
$$

Step 6 — Multiply:

$$
t_{net} = 800 \times 0.009226 = 7.38 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 7 — Add corrosion addition:

$$
t_{ren} = 7.38 + 2.0 = 9.38 \text{ mm} \approx 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer:** The renewal thickness of the strength deck plate is **10 mm** (next standard plate size above 9.38 mm).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe the **structural hierarchy of a ship's deck**, from plating down to the hull girder, with a labeled sketch. Explain the **load path** during sea or cargo loading. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a special survey, the UT gauge on a **bulkhead plate** in a cargo hold reads **6.0 mm** actual. Original as-built thickness was **10.0 mm**. The class renewal limit $t_{ren}$ is **7.5 mm** (net 5.0 mm + 2.5 mm corrosion addition). Classify the plate and state the required action. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Deck Hierarchy (Section 2.1):**
- Plating → Longitudinals → Beams → Deck Girders → Pillars → Inner Bottom → Double Bottom.
- Each step should include **sectional shape** (e.g., T-bulb for longitudinals, T-flat for beams, I-girder for deck girders).
- Load path: lateral sea/cargo load → plate → longitudinals → beams → girders → pillars → bottom.

Marks: 3 for hierarchy listing, 2 for sketch/flow, 2 for load path explanation.

**(b) Plate Classification:**

Step 1 — Compare $t_{actual}$ with $t_{ren}$:

$$
t_{actual} = 6.0 \text{ mm} < t_{ren} = 7.5 \text{ mm}
$$

The plate is **below the renewal limit** — **Renewal Required**.

Step 2 — Compute % wastage:

$$
\% \text{ wastage} = \frac{10.0 - 6.0}{10.0} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

Step 3 — Apply IACS criteria:

Since wastage is **40%** (well above the **25% "substantial corrosion" threshold**), and below $t_{ren}$, the plate must be **cropped and renewed with a 10 mm insert plate** at the next docking.

Step 4 — Required actions:
- Crop out defective plate by **gas or plasma cutting** (1.5 m × 1.5 m typical insert).
- Fit and weld new **10 mm plate** (or upgrade to **11 mm** to gain 1 mm margin).
- **NDT 100% on welds** (UT or RT for butt welds, MPI for fillet).
- Grit blast to **Sa 2.5** and apply **epoxy primer (75 µm DFT)**.
- Update class file with new as-built thickness.

Marks: 2 for condition, 2 for calculation, 2 for procedure, 1 for documentation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not skip the "add corrosion addition" step** — a common mistake is to report only $t_{net}$ as the answer. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for omitting $t_c$.
> 2. **Always state units** in intermediate steps. A unitless number = 0.5 mark deduction.
> 3. **For bulkhead classification questions**, do NOT write only 2 types. Class Society examiners expect **at least 4 to 5 types** with **SOLAS / IACS reference** for full marks.
> 4. **Do not confuse IACS UR S21 (Hatch Covers) with S22 (Cargo Holds)** — they have different $t_c$ values.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Strength deck** = topmost continuous deck; contributes to hull girder bending.
- [ ] **Platform deck** = local, secondary; does **not** contribute to hull girder.
- [ ] **Tween deck** = intermediate cargo deck.
- [ ] **Inner bottom / tank top** = lowest deck; forms top of double bottom.
- [ ] **Collision bulkhead** = at frame 2, per **SOLAS Reg II-1/12**, 5% L to 3 m + 5% L from FP.
- [ ] **Watertight bulkheads** — tested at **0.2 to 0.35 bar** during survey.
- [ ] **Fire-resisting bulkheads** — Class A (**60 min**), Class B (**30 min**), Class C (**0 min**).
- [ ] **Corrosion addition $t_c$** — IACS UR S22 for cargo holds: **+1.0 to +2.5 mm**; UR S21 for hatch covers: **+0.5 mm**.
- [ ] **Substantial corrosion threshold** = **25% wastage** of original.
- [ ] **Renewal trigger** when $t_{actual} < t_{ren} = t_{net} + t_c$.
- [ ] **Load path** = Plate → Longitudinals → Beams → Girders → Pillars → Hull Girder.
- [ ] **Steel density** = **7850 kg/m³** for weight estimation.
- [ ] **UT gauge** is the **primary survey tool** for thickness measurement.
- [ ] **NDT methods** for deck/bulkhead repair: MPI for fillets, UT/RT for butt welds.
- [ ] **Surface preparation** standard: **Sa 2.5** (ISO 8501-1) for repair coatings.

---

**End of Module 3 — Decks and Bulkheads Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repair of Stem and Stern Frames and Shaft Bracket
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Repair of Stem and Stern Frames and Shaft Bracket

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Ship's Stem – Formal KTU Definition
The **stem** is the forward-most vertical (or near-vertical) structural member of a ship's hull, forming the prow of the vessel. It connects the forecastle deck plating to the keel plate and provides the leading edge that parts the water as the ship moves forward. The stem is subject to extreme hydrodynamic and impact loading, making it one of the most heavily reinforced components in the hull.

### 1.2 The Stern Frame – Formal KTU Definition
The **stern frame** is a heavy cast or fabricated steel structure fitted at the after end of a ship. It consists of a **propeller post (inner member)**, a **rudder post (outer member)**, and a **sole piece (horizontal connecting member)**. Its function is to:
- Support the **propeller shaft** and its bearings
- Provide a strongback for the **rudder** and its gudgeons
- Transfer thrust and hydrodynamic forces from the propeller to the main hull structure.

### 1.3 The Shaft Bracket – Formal KTU Definition
A **shaft bracket** (also called a **propeller shaft bracket** or **A-bracket**) is a V-shaped or arched structure—either **cast steel** or **fabricated plate**—that supports the propeller shaft (intermediate shaft or tail shaft) externally as it exits the hull, before reaching the stern tube and propeller. The shaft bracket usually has one arm fixed to the hull shell plating and the other to a hull girder or transverse bulkhead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** Repair of the stem, stern frame, and shaft bracket is governed by the Rules of **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)**, **IACS**, and **Classification Society** guidelines. These components are **Primary Structural Members (PSMs)** and any renewal or major repair must be approved by the attending Surveyor.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Think of the ship as a **swimming athlete**:
- The **stem** is the athlete's **nose and forehead**, which leads and breaks through water resistance; if it gets damaged, the whole body (hull) is compromised.
- The **stern frame** is the athlete's **hips and pelvis**, anchoring the powerful **legs (propeller)** and providing the pivot point for steering.
- The **shaft bracket** is the athlete's **knee joint**, holding the leg (shaft) in proper alignment with the body so power is transmitted efficiently.

Just as a doctor would carefully repair a broken bone, ship surveyors must restore these critical appendages with strict tolerances, because any misalignment causes vibration, fatigue, and eventual failure at sea.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Glossary:**
> - **Bar Stem** – built from a solid rectangular steel bar (older ships)
> - **Plate Stem** – built from welded plate sections (modern ships)
> - **Cast Stem** – a single forging or casting (warships, icebreakers)
> - **Rudder Gudgeon** – the bearing on the stern frame carrying the rudder pintle
> - **Stern Tube** – the watertight passage carrying the propeller shaft through the hull.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Failure Modes Requiring Repair
Repair of the stem, stern frame, and shaft bracket is necessitated by the following damage types:

- **Impact Damage** – from collision, contact with quay, or striking submerged objects.
- **Grounding Damage** – the stem, being the leading edge, frequently bears the brunt of grounding incidents; forefoot damage is common.
- **Corrosion / Erosion** – pitting, wastage, and groove corrosion at welded joints, especially in the splash zone.
- **Fatigue Cracking** – at fillet radii, shaft bracket arms, and weld toes due to cyclic propeller-induced vibration.
- **Cavitation Erosion** – damage to shaft brackets caused by collapse of vapor bubbles near the propeller wash.
- **Wastage Beyond Allowable Limits** – the section modulus falls below classification society minimums.

### 2.2 Theoretical Basis of Repair Design
The repair of these appendages must satisfy three engineering conditions:

1. **Static Strength Condition** – the renewed section must equal or exceed the **original rule scantling** in plate thickness and modulus.
2. **Fatigue Strength Condition** – the weld profile, transition radius, and NDT quality must limit the **stress concentration factor (SCF)** to within IRS/IACS bounds.
3. **Alignment & Geometric Condition** – propeller shaft alignment must remain within **0.05 mm per 100 mm** of shaft diameter (TIR – Total Indicated Reading).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Section Modulus of Renewed Plate | $Z = \dfrac{b \cdot t^2}{6}$ | cm³ | Required for plate stem renewal verification |
| Weld Throat Thickness | $t_t = 0.707 \cdot a$ | mm | For full-penetration butt weld leg `a` |
| Scantling Rule (IRS Pt.3) | $t = s \cdot \sqrt{L} + C$ | mm | Plate thickness of stem `s` is a coefficient, `L` is LBP, `C` is corrosion addition |
| Allowable Wastage (50% rule) | $t_{min} = 0.5 \cdot t_{as\text{-}built}$ | mm | Renewal mandatory when actual thickness < 50% of original |
| Shaft Bracket Arm Thickness | $t_{arm} = 1.5 \cdot t_{shell\,plate}$ | mm | Empirical rule for A-bracket arm renewal |
| Fillet Weld Leg Size | $a = 0.707 \cdot t_{thinner\,plate}$ | mm | Single-side fillet weld for bracket arm |
| Rudder Pintle Bearing Pressure | $p = \dfrac{W}{d \cdot L_b}$ | N/mm² | Pintle load `W`, pintle dia `d`, bearing length `L_b` |
| Propeller Thrust Reaction | $F_t = \dfrac{P_{eff}}{V - 0.7 \cdot V_{s}}$ | kN | Approx. thrust on stern frame |
| Welding Heat Input | $E = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S}$ | kJ/mm | Heat input, where `V` is voltage, `I` is current, `S` is travel speed |
| Interpass Temperature Limit (HSLA) | $T_{ip} \le 250°\text{C}$ | °C | Critical for stern frame fabrication welds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for Examiners:** Whenever a calculation of plate renewal is asked, always state the **original rule thickness**, subtract measured wastage, compare with the **50% allowable wastage limit**, and conclude whether **renewal, partial renewal, or doubler plate** is required.

### 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in the Field
- The **section modulus equation** confirms whether the new plate restores hull girder strength.
- The **heat input equation** is critical because excessive heat input on a cast or fabricated stern frame causes **HAZ softening**, distortion, and lamellar tearing.
- The **bearing pressure equation** determines if the **pintle bush** requires re-metalling or replacement during stern frame repair.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation – Heat Input for a Stern Frame Weld

The **heat input per unit length of weld** is a key parameter controlled during repair to avoid HAZ cracking. It is derived from electrical power dissipated at the arc:

**Step 1 – Define Electrical Power**

$$P = V \cdot I$$

where `V` is arc voltage in volts and `I` is welding current in amperes.

**Step 2 – Energy Per Unit Time**

Energy supplied per second equals the electrical power, so for a weld travel time of `Δt` seconds, total heat delivered is:

$$E_{total} = V \cdot I \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 – Convert Time to Travel Speed**

Let `S` be the welding travel speed in mm/min. Then the time to weld 1 mm of joint is:

$$\Delta t = \dfrac{60}{S} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4 – Substitute Into Heat Equation**

Heat input per millimetre of weld is therefore:

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}}$$

The factor `1/1000` converts joules to kilojoules. This is the **standard heat input formula** quoted in IRS welding procedure specifications.

**Step 5 – Worked Numerical Example**
A welder is repairing the propeller post of a stern frame using SMAW with `V = 24 V`, `I = 220 A`, and travel speed `S = 180 mm/min`. Calculate the heat input.

$$E = \dfrac{60 \times 24 \times 220}{1000 \times 180}$$

$$E = \dfrac{316{,}800}{180{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{E = 1.76 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

This value is within the typical range of **0.8 – 2.5 kJ/mm** for low-hydrogen electrodes on HSLA stern frame steels, satisfying IRS Pt.3 Ch.6 weld procedure rules.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Selection of Repair Method

| Damage Type | Component | Extent | Recommended Repair | Surveyor Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Localized pitting < 25% area | Stem shell plating | Surface | Grit blast, fair with weld, recoat | Annual survey, no renewal |
| Wastage 25% – 50% | Stem plate | Mid-zone | Doubler plate welded insert | Special survey, NDT 100% |
| Wastage > 50% | Stem plate | Extensive | Full crop & renewal | Class approval, dock trial |
| Cracks at forefoot | Stem-keel joint | Any | Welding using low-hydrogen electrodes, peening, NDT 100% | Immediate repair, hot work permit |
| Cracks at gudgeon | Stern frame | Any | Repair welding, post-weld UT | Class-approved WPS mandatory |
| Worn pintle bush | Stern frame | Beyond limit | Re-metalling or bushing renewal | Workshop repair, bench test |
| Bent shaft bracket arm | Shaft bracket | Visible | Heat straightening or renewal | Drydock, alignment check |
| Cracked bracket arm | Shaft bracket | Any | Renewal of complete arm | Class-approved, dynamic balance recheck |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Repair Procedure – Shaft Bracket Arm Renewal
1. **Stage 1 – Survey and Marking:** Mark cut line 50 mm beyond visible crack tip; UT 100% to confirm extent.
2. **Stage 2 – Support and Pre-Heating:** Shore up the propeller shaft; pre-heat casting to **150 °C** minimum.
3. **Stage 3 – Cutting Out:** Oxy-arc or plasma gouge out the damaged arm; grind back to bright metal.
4. **Stage 4 – Preparation of New Arm:** Fabricate new arm from **rolled steel plate** to IRS-approved forging/scantling; profile match the original.
5. **Stage 5 – Fit-Up and Welding:** Use approved WPS; root run with low-hydrogen electrode, fill, cap.
6. **Stage 6 – Post-Weld NDT:** 100% MPI on fillet welds, 100% UT on butt welds; hardness survey on HAZ.
7. **Stage 7 – Alignment Check:** Re-measure shaft alignment; TIR within 0.05 mm per 100 mm.
8. **Stage 8 – Class Trials:** Sea trial with attending surveyor; final survey endorsement.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram – Stem Repair Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Surveyor Inspects Stem] --> B{Damage Type?}
    B -- Pitting Minor --> C[Grit Blast and Recoat]
    B -- Wastage 25 to 50% --> D[Insert Doubler Plate]
    B -- Wastage over 50% --> E[Full Crop and Renewal]
    B -- Crack at Forefoot --> F[Low Hydrogen Weld Repair]
    C --> G[Annual Survey Endorsement]
    D --> H[Class Approved WPS]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I[100 percent NDT MPI and UT]
    I --> J[Sea Trial Verification]
    J --> K[Continuous Survey Entry]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram – Stern Frame Component Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Transom Plate] --> S2[Sole Piece]
    S2 --> S3[Propeller Post]
    S3 --> S4[Rudder Post]
    S4 --> S5[Rudder Gudgeon]
    S3 --> S6[Propeller Shaft Support]
    S5 --> S7[Rudder Pintle]
    S6 --> S8[Stern Tube Bearing]
    S8 --> S9[Propeller]
    S7 --> S10[Rudder Blade]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram – Shaft Bracket Stress and Repair Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SA1[Top Arm Connection to Hull] --> SA2[Mid Arm Casting Zone]
    SA2 --> SA3[Bottom Strum Palm]
    SA3 --> SA4[Shaft Bearing Liner]
    SA2 -- Fatigue Crack --> SA5[Repair Weld with Peening]
    SA1 -- Corrosion --> SA6[Doubler Plate Renewal]
    SA3 -- Cavitation Erosion --> SA7[Stainless Overlay Welding]
    SA4 -- Bearing Wear --> SA8[White Metal Re-Babbit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The Mermaid blocks above are functional schematics. The **Top Arm Connection** suffers corrosion; the **Mid Arm** is most prone to fatigue; the **Strum Palm** is exposed to propeller cavitation. This zoning guides surveyors in planning targeted repairs.

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram – Stern Frame Weld Repair Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[Crack Detected on Stern Frame] --> W2{Crack Depth?}
    W2 -- Less than 25 percent t --> W3[Grind Out and Weld]
    W2 -- 25 to 50 percent t --> W4[Pre-Heat and Full Pen Weld]
    W2 -- More than 50 percent t --> W5[Cut Out and Insert Plate]
    W3 --> W6[Post Weld MPI and UT]
    W4 --> W6
    W5 --> W6
    W6 --> W7{Acceptance Criteria Met?}
    W7 -- Yes --> W8[Class Endorsement]
    W7 -- No --> W9[Rework and Re Inspect]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the components of a typical stern frame of a ship and briefly explain the function of each.*

**Model Answer:**
A typical stern frame consists of the following four components:
- **Propeller Post (inner member):** the vertical post that supports the propeller shaft and the forward end of the stern tube.
- **Rudder Post (outer member):** the vertical post to which the rudder gudgeons are attached.
- **Sole Piece (bottom horizontal member):** the lower transverse member connecting propeller post and rudder post, forming the lower frame.
- **Gudgeons and Pintle Bosses:** the bearing supports for the rudder pintle.

Together they transmit the propeller thrust, support the rudder, and provide the rearmost structural strength of the hull. (3 marks: 1 for naming, 1 for functions, 1 for diagram)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any six probable causes of damage to a ship's stem and stern frame during service.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Collision damage** with quay, floating objects, or another vessel at the bow.
2. **Grounding damage** at the forefoot, leading to stem buckling or fracture.
3. **Slamming loads** causing fatigue cracks in the forepeak region.
4. **Corrosion** at welded seams and bilge keel terminations.
5. **Propeller-induced vibration** leading to fatigue cracks in the stern frame.
6. **Cavitation erosion** of the shaft bracket from the propeller wash.
7. **Improper dry-docking support** causing the ship to settle unevenly.
(3 marks: ½ mark each, list any six)

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a) With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction of a typical stern frame of a ship. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A typical stern frame is a heavy built-up structure fitted at the after end of a ship. It is generally of **cast steel construction**, although older vessels used riveted fabricated plate construction.

**Construction Details:**
- **Propeller Post:** A vertical post aligned with the centreline of the propeller shaft. It carries the forward end of the stern tube and supports the weight and thrust of the propeller.
- **Rudder Post:** A vertical post located aft of the propeller post, to which the rudder is hung using **gudgeons** (fixed lugs) and **pintles** (rotating lugs on the rudder).
- **Sole Piece:** A horizontal casting joining the bottom ends of the propeller post and rudder post, shaped to provide a smooth water flow into the propeller.
- **Gudgeons and Bosses:** Bearing surfaces for the rudder pintle, often fitted with **white-metal bushes** for low-friction operation.
- **Shaft Strut or Bracket:** Sometimes an external arm extends from the hull to provide intermediate support to the propeller shaft.

[Sketch Description: Side elevation showing transom, sole piece, propeller post with stern tube entry, rudder post with three gudgeons, rudder hung on the post, propeller at the rear, and shaft bracket arm extending from the hull.]

(7 marks: 2 for sketch, 3 for component description, 2 for function explanation)

---

**(b) Explain in detail the procedure for the repair of a cracked propeller post of a stern frame. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 – Survey and Identification:** The crack is identified during dry-dock survey by visual inspection, MPI, or UT. The full extent of cracking is mapped by 100% NDT around the affected zone.

**Step 2 – Classification Society Approval:** A repair procedure is submitted to the attending surveyor and classification society (IRS) for approval before hot work begins.

**Step 3 – Preparation:** The area is cleaned by grit blasting, all paint, rust, and oil removed within a 200 mm radius of the crack.

**Step 4 – Pre-Heating:** The casting is pre-heated uniformly to a minimum of **150 °C** using resistance heating mats or oxy-propane torches, monitored by temperature crayons.

**Step 5 – Crack Removal:** The crack is removed by **air-arc gouging** or grinding, going **5 – 10 mm beyond the visible crack tip**. A U- or J-groove is prepared on the casting.

**Step 6 – Welding:** An **approved WPS** is used with **low-hydrogen electrodes** (e.g., AWS E7018). The weld is deposited in multi-pass stringer beads, with **interpass temperature ≤ 250 °C** and **controlled heat input 1.0 – 1.8 kJ/mm**.

**Step 7 – Peening:** Each weld pass is lightly peened to relieve residual stress, particularly at the root and cap.

**Step 8 – Post-Weld NDT:** 100% MPI and 100% UT are carried out 24 hours after welding. A hardness survey of the HAZ is conducted to ensure values are below **350 HV10** for normal-strength steel castings.

**Step 9 – Post-Weld Heat Treatment (PWHT):** For thicker sections (>50 mm) and critical castings, PWHT at **600 ± 25 °C** for stress relief may be required.

**Step 10 – Surveyor Endorsement and Sea Trial:** The repair is recorded in the class file, and a dock trial is carried out to confirm no leakage from the stern tube.

(7 marks: 1 for survey, 1 for approval, 1 for pre-heat, 1 for weld procedure, 1 for NDT, 1 for documentation, 1 for alignment/trial)

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a) Describe with a sketch the construction of a typical **shaft bracket** of a ship. Discuss the various materials used. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **shaft bracket** (or **A-frame bracket**) is an external support fitted to the shell plating to support the propeller shaft between the engine-room bulkhead and the stern tube.

**Construction Details:**
- The bracket is shaped like the letter **"A"** (or "V"), with the apex pointing downward to cradle the shaft.
- It usually consists of **two arms**:
  - **Top arm** welded to the hull shell plating (above the waterline).
  - **Bottom arm** (strum) connected to a longitudinal girder or a transverse bulkhead (below the waterline).
- The shaft passes through a **bearing housing** at the apex, lined with **white metal, lignum vitae, or synthetic rubber**.
- Modern designs may have a **single-arm strum** for reduced drag.

**Materials Used:**
- **Cast Steel:** Traditional choice for its strength and ability to form complex hydrodynamic shapes. Grades per IRS Pt.3 are **ASTM-A27 Grade 60-30 or equivalent**.
- **Fabricated Steel (Plate):** Welded construction from rolled plates, allowing for replacement of individual arms.
- **Bronze or Stainless Steel Bushes:** Used for the bearing liner to resist corrosion in seawater.
- **Composite Materials:** Modern high-speed vessels use GRP-reinforced brackets.

[Sketch Description: Side view of a hull, A-shaped bracket with two arms joining at the shaft bearing, propeller shaft passing through bearing, propeller at the rear, and labels for top arm, bottom arm, strum palm, and bearing liner.]

(7 marks: 2 for sketch, 3 for component description, 2 for materials)

---

**(b) Explain the procedure for the repair of a damaged **stem** of a ship by crop and renewal method. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **crop and renewal** method is used when the wastage or damage to the stem exceeds **50% of the original plate thickness**, or when there is severe structural cracking.

**Step 1 – Survey and Scope Definition:** A complete survey is conducted including UT thickness gauging. The boundaries of the renewal are marked at locations where the original thickness is intact (i.e., beyond the wasted zone).

**Step 2 – Class Approval and WPS:** A detailed repair plan with sketches, material specifications (IRS-graded steel), and WPS is submitted to the **attending Surveyor of IRS (or IACS member society)** for approval.

**Step 3 – Scaffolding and Rigging:** Scaffolding is erected around the stem. The ship is positioned to allow access from outside; shore supports are checked.

**Step 4 – Pre-Heating and Marking:** Pre-heat adjacent plates to 75 – 150 °C depending on plate grade. Mark cut lines using templates matched to the original stem profile.

**Step 5 – Cutting Out:** The damaged portion is cut using **oxy-fuel or plasma arc**, leaving a **50 mm step** for welding continuity. Edges are ground to bright metal and U-grooved for full penetration.

**Step 6 – Fabricating the New Section:** A new plate section is **cold-formed to the original stem profile** using a hydraulic press or rollers. The new plate conforms to the **IRS Pt.3 scantling** and may be one grade higher (e.g., Grade A → Grade D) to extend fatigue life.

**Step 7 – Fit-Up and Welding:** The new section is tack-welded in position and welded using the approved WPS, with low-hydrogen electrodes, controlled heat input, and interpass temperature control. **Submerged arc welding (SAW)** is preferred for long vertical seams.

**Step 8 – Post-Weld NDT:** 100% MPI of fillet welds, 100% UT of butt welds, and hardness testing of the HAZ.

**Step 9 – Fairing and Surface Protection:** The stem is **fair-ground** to a smooth hydrodynamic profile. A full **anti-corrosive paint system** (e.g., epoxy-zinc primer, anti-fouling) is applied.

**Step 10 – Class Endorsement and Sea Trial:** The repair is documented, surveyor endorses the form, and a sea trial is carried out to confirm performance.

(7 marks: 1 for survey/scope, 1 for class approval, 1 for cut-out, 1 for fabrication, 1 for welding, 1 for NDT, 1 for finishing)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not** forget to mention **pre-heating and interpass temperature** for cast stern frame welding – this is a recurring 2-mark deduction.
> - **Always** mention **NDT methods** (MPI, UT, dye penetrant) – examiners specifically look for "100% NDT" in the answer.
> - **Avoid generic answers** like "the stem is repaired by welding." You must mention **preparation, electrode, heat input, NDT, and class approval**.
> - **Diagrams** are mandatory for 7-mark sub-questions on construction; failing to draw a sketch costs a minimum of **2 marks**.
> - **Units** must accompany all numerical answers. A correct value without units is treated as incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap – Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **stem** is the foremost structural member; repair is required due to **collision, grounding, and corrosion**.
- ✅ The **stern frame** consists of **propeller post, rudder post, sole piece, and gudgeons**.
- ✅ The **shaft bracket** (A-frame) supports the propeller shaft outside the hull.
- ✅ **Material of construction:** Cast steel, fabricated plate, or bronze bushes.
- ✅ **Renewal threshold:** when wastage > **50%** of original rule thickness.
- ✅ **Heat input formula:** $E = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot S}$ kJ/mm – a high-yield KTU formula.
- ✅ **Pre-heat temperature:** ≥ **150 °C** for cast stern frames; **75 – 100 °C** for mild steel stems.
- ✅ **Interpass temperature:** ≤ **250 °C** for HSLA steels.
- ✅ **NDT after repair:** **100% MPI + 100% UT** is the gold standard.
- ✅ **Class approval** is mandatory before any hot work on these **Primary Structural Members (PSMs)**.
- ✅ **Shaft alignment** tolerance: **0.05 mm TIR per 100 mm** of shaft diameter.
- ✅ **Repair methods:** minor damage → **doubler plate**, severe → **crop and renewal**.
- ✅ **Welding electrodes:** **low-hydrogen type (e.g., E7018)** are mandatory.
- ✅ **Cavitation erosion** affects shaft bracket strum palms; **stainless steel overlay** is a recognized repair.
- ✅ **Documentation:** All repairs must be entered in the **class survey file** with **Form 27B / REP report**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propeller Shaft Sealing Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Propeller Shaft Sealing Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Propeller Shaft Sealing Equipment** refers to the complete assembly of components designed to provide a controlled, leak-proof interface between the rotating propeller shaft and the stationary hull of a ship, while the shaft penetrates the aft peak bulkhead and stern tube. The primary functional objectives of this equipment, as defined in the KTU 2024 Scheme naval architecture syllabus, are:

1. **Prevention of seawater ingress** into the engine room and aft peak compartment.
2. **Containment of lubricating oil** (in oil-lubricated stern tubes) or **sealing water** (in water-lubricated stern tubes) within the stern tube assembly.
3. **Maintenance of hydrodynamic bearing film** between the shaft and the stern tube bearings.
4. **Transmission of axial thrust** from the propeller to the hull structure (in some designs).
5. **Compliance with classification society rules** (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS) regarding environmental protection (e.g., MARPOL Annex I oil pollution prevention).

The propeller shaft sealing system is broadly classified into two technological generations:
- **Conventional Packed Stuffing Box** (traditional hemp/graphite packing with lantern ring)
- **Modern Mechanical Face Seal** (Simplex, Tandem, or Double Seal arrangements using precision-lapped faces)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 3 emphasis is on the **operational principles, survey requirements, maintenance procedures, and repair methodologies** of these sealing systems as encountered during statutory and class survey work onboard commercial vessels.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of the propeller shaft sealing equipment as the **"rotating watertight lid"** of a submarine. Imagine you have a horizontal pipe (the propeller shaft) spinning at 100-250 rpm, and this pipe must pass through the wall of a water-filled compartment (the sea) without letting the water leak inside, while also keeping the lubricating oil from escaping out. This is analogous to trying to seal the **spinning axle of a washing machine** where water must be kept inside the drum but the axle must rotate freely.

A closer analogy is the **kitchen mixer tap**: the rotating spindle of the tap has rubber O-rings and graphite packing that prevent water from leaking out along the spindle. The shipboard sealing system is essentially a heavy-duty, marine-grade, dynamically loaded version of this same principle — but operating under **seawater hydrostatic pressure of 2-6 bar** at the stern, with shaft speeds of 60-300 rpm, and continuous service for 5-10 years between dry-dockings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** The propeller shaft sealing equipment is the "gasket" around a spinning shaft that enters the sea — it stops oil from leaking out and stops seawater from leaking in, while allowing the shaft to rotate smoothly and transfer thrust.

## 1.3 Critical Engineering Metrics (Bolded Standards)

The following physical constants and engineering standards govern the design and survey of propeller shaft sealing equipment:

- **Maximum design shaft speed (n): 60–300 rpm** (typical for slow-speed / medium-speed marine propulsion)
- **Stern tube operating pressure (P): 0.2–0.6 MPa** (2–6 bar hydrostatic + dynamic)
- **Sealing water pressure margin: 0.05–0.15 MPa** above external seawater pressure
- **Friction face PV limit: 1.5–3.5 MPa·m/s** (Pressure-Velocity product for face materials)
- **Maximum allowable leakage: < 2 litres/hour** (under normal service, class requirement)
- **Oil-lubricated stern tube oil pressure: 0.15–0.40 MPa** above external water pressure
- **Stern tube bearing material temperature limit: 70°C** (white metal) / **55°C** (rubber)
- **Classification rule references: SOLAS Ch. II-1, MARPOL Annex I Reg. 12A, IACS S79**

## 1.4 Functional Block Diagram of Sealing System (Conceptual Hook)

The propeller shaft sealing system, in its complete form, performs **five concurrent functions**, each addressed by a specific sub-assembly:

| Function | Responsible Sub-Assembly | Failure Consequence |
|----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Radial shaft support | Stern tube bearings (white metal / rubber / lignum vitae) | Shaft whip, bearing seizure |
| Axial thrust transmission | Thrust bearing (often integrated with main engine) | Forward propeller movement |
| Sealing against water ingress | Aft seal (mechanical face seal / stuffing box) | Flooding of engine room |
| Sealing against oil egress | Forward seal (in oil-lubricated systems) | Oil pollution (MARPOL violation) |
| Lubricant supply and cooling | Gravity tank / pressurizing unit / cooling system | Bearing failure, overheating |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Propeller Shaft Sealing Arrangements

### 2.1.1 By Lubrication Philosophy

**(A) Water-Lubricated Stern Tube (Conventional Packed Stuffing Box)**

- Bearings: Lignum vitae, rubber, or synthetic composite (e.g., Thordon)
- Lubricant: Sea water (ambient)
- Sealing: Hemp or PTFE packing in a stuffing box, with sealing water injection at the lantern ring
- Typical application: Small to medium vessels, older designs, simple installations

**(B) Oil-Lubricated Stern Tube (Mechanical Face Seal)**

- Bearings: White metal (tin-based babbitt) or bronze
- Lubricant: Mineral or synthetic lubricating oil (ISO VG 68–100)
- Sealing: Mechanical face seal (Simplex or Tandem/Double)
- Typical application: Modern vessels, high-power installations, large propellers

### 2.1.2 By Seal Technology Generation

**Generation 1 — Packed Stuffing Box (Greased / Water-Flushed)**
**Generation 2 — Single Mechanical Face Seal (Simplex)**
**Generation 3 — Tandem Mechanical Seal (Primary + Secondary with barrier fluid)**
**Generation 4 — Double Mechanical Seal (Two independent seals with independent barrier circuits)**

## 2.2 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 Conventional Packed Stuffing Box Operation

The packed stuffing box operates on the principle of **progressive pressure drop across multiple soft packing rings**, in series:

1. **Shaft entry zone**: Propeller shaft passes through the gland with a clearance of 0.5–1.0 mm radial.
2. **Packing stack**: Typically 4–6 rings of square-section braided packing (graphite-impregnated PTFE or flax/hemp with tallow).
3. **Lantern ring**: A perforated brass or bronze ring positioned centrally in the packing stack. Sealing water is fed into the lantern ring from a gravity tank.
4. **Pressure logic**: Sealing water pressure at the lantern ring is maintained **0.05–0.10 MPa above** the external seawater hydrostatic head. This ensures any leakage is **outward** (seawater cannot enter the stern tube).
5. **Gland follower**: A forged brass / bronze gland compresses the packing axially via studs. Torque-controlled tightening produces radial expansion of the packing against the shaft and the stuffing box bore.
6. **Lubrication and cooling**: The sealing water also provides cooling to the packing, preventing charring of graphite or melting of PTFE.
7. **Leakage control**: A controlled drip of mixed sealing water and oil is permitted (3–30 drops per minute) — this is the "controlled leakage" design philosophy.

### 2.2.2 Mechanical Face Seal Operation

The mechanical face seal operates on the principle of **two precision-lapped flat surfaces in sliding contact**, normal to the shaft axis:

1. **Primary ring (rotating face)**: Usually silicon carbide or tungsten carbide, mounted on the shaft via an O-ring secondary seal.
2. **Mating ring (stationary face)**: Usually carbon-graphite (resilient, self-lubricating), mounted in the gland housing via a secondary elastomer O-ring.
3. **Contact band**: A narrow annular contact zone of width **b = 1.5–4.0 mm** is established between the two faces.
4. **Face loading**: Axial force (from spring + hydraulic pressure differential) loads the faces together at a specific pressure of **0.3–0.7 MPa**.
5. **Fluid film**: A hydrodynamic lubricating film of thickness **0.1–1.0 µm** (mixed / boundary lubrication regime) separates the faces, preventing dry contact.
6. **Wear compensation**: The elastomer O-rings (or garter springs in older designs) maintain face contact as the seal faces wear over time.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind mechanical seals:** Packed stuffing boxes have high friction (5–15% of bearing friction losses) and require constant adjustment. Mechanical seals offer **lower friction torque**, **zero visible leakage**, and **longer service intervals** (typically 25,000–50,000 hours), justifying their higher initial cost and precision.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **examined formulas** for Module 3, Propeller Shaft Sealing Equipment. Every KTU ESE and internal assessment problem in this topic maps to one of these equations.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Use | Typical Units |
|---|--------------|------------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Sealing water pressure requirement | $P_{sw} \ge P_{ext} + \Delta P_{margin}$ | Minimum sealing water pressure = external seawater pressure + 0.05–0.10 MPa margin | MPa |
| 2 | External seawater hydrostatic head | $P_{ext} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at stern tube depth | Pa |
| 3 | Gland specific pressure on packing | $\sigma_{p} = \dfrac{F_{gland}}{A_{gland}} = \dfrac{4 F_{gland}}{\pi (D_o^2 - D_i^2)}$ | Axial gland force per unit packing area | N/mm² |
| 4 | Mechanical seal face contact pressure | $P_c = \dfrac{F_{spring} + F_{hydraulic}}{A_{contact}} = \dfrac{F_{spring} + \Delta P \cdot A_{eff}}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$ | Face loading pressure on sliding interface | MPa |
| 5 | Friction torque on seal face | $T_{f} = f \cdot F_{N} \cdot r_{m}$ where $r_{m} = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ | Sliding friction torque resisting shaft rotation | N·m |
| 6 | PV factor (wear indicator) | $PV = P_c \cdot v$ where $v = \pi D_{m} n / 60$ | Pressure-Velocity product — material wear limit | MPa·m/s |
| 7 | Mean sliding velocity at seal face | $v_{m} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_{m} \cdot n}{60}$ | Circumferential velocity at mean diameter | m/s |
| 8 | Mechanical seal friction power loss | $P_{loss} = T_{f} \cdot \omega = T_{f} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi n}{60}$ | Power dissipated as heat in seal faces | W |
| 9 | Heat dissipation by sealing water | $Q_{cool} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat absorbed by sealing/lubricating water flow | W |
| 10 | Reynolds number for film flow | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v_{m} \cdot b}{\mu}$ | Confirms laminar/turbulent film regime | dimensionless |
| 11 | Thin-film leakage through seal face | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_m \cdot h^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \mu \cdot L}$ | Leakage through the gap of thickness h | m³/s |
| 12 | Stuffing box gland bolt tightening torque | $T_{bolt} = \dfrac{F_{bolt}}{k_d} \cdot \dfrac{d_{bolt}}{2}$ | Torque to achieve required gland force | N·m |
| 13 | Stern tube bearing projected load | $W = P_{oil} \cdot L_{bearing} \cdot D_{journal}$ | Bearing reaction from oil film pressure | N |
| 14 | Oil-lubricated stern tube oil flow rate | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{Q_{gen}}{\rho_{oil} \cdot c_{oil} \cdot \Delta T_{max}}$ | Required oil circulation to remove frictional heat | m³/s |
| 15 | Sealing water tank head requirement | $h_{tank} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{margin}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g}$ | Gravity tank height for sealing water pressure | m |

> **Where:**
> - $\rho_{sw}$ = seawater density (≈ **1025 kg/m³**)
> - $g$ = gravitational acceleration (≈ **9.81 m/s²**)
> - $h$ = depth of stern tube below waterline
> - $f$ = coefficient of friction at seal face (≈ 0.05–0.12 for lubricated face contact)
> - $D_m$ = mean diameter of seal face
> - $n$ = shaft rotational speed in rpm
> - $c_p$ = specific heat capacity of water (≈ **4186 J/(kg·K)**)
> - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of lubricant
> - $h$ (in Eq. 11) = face flatness / film thickness (µm)
> - $L$ = contact band length along shaft axis
> - $k_d$ = nut factor (typically 0.15–0.25)

## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Application

The propeller shaft sealing system is the **only dynamically loaded penetration of the hull** below the waterline (excluding rudder stocks). Its failure consequences are catastrophic:

- **Loss of stern tube oil** (typically 5,000–15,000 litres in a large vessel) → **MARPOL Annex I oil pollution** violation, fines up to **USD 1 million+**, vessel detention.
- **Sea water ingress** into engine room → loss of propulsion, **blackout of auxiliary systems**, possible sinking.
- **Survey and class implications** → Detention by Port State Control, loss of class, insurance premium escalation.

**Industry adoption rate:** Approximately **85% of new-build vessels** (post-2010) use oil-lubricated stern tubes with mechanical face seals. The remaining 15% are smaller coastal vessels, naval auxiliaries, or specialized vessels using water-lubricated systems with rubber bearings.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **fully worked derivations** for the key equations used in propeller shaft sealing equipment design and survey calculations. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown — no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Derivation: Sealing Water Pressure Requirement

### Problem Statement
A vessel's stern tube is located **4.5 m below the load waterline (LWL)**. The classification society rules require a sealing water pressure margin of **0.08 MPa** above external seawater pressure. Determine the required sealing water gauge pressure at the stuffing box inlet.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Define the engineering requirement.**

The sealing water must be at a higher pressure than the external seawater to ensure any leakage is **outward** (preventing seawater ingress into the stern tube).

$$P_{sw} \ge P_{ext} + \Delta P_{margin}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the external seawater hydrostatic pressure at stern tube depth.**

Using the hydrostatic pressure equation $P_{ext} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$:

$$P_{ext} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 4.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerical value.**

$$P_{ext} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4.5$$

$$P_{ext} = 1025 \times 44.145$$

$$P_{ext} = 45{,}248.625 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{ext} = 0.04525 \text{ MPa} \approx 0.0453 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Apply the safety margin.**

$$\Delta P_{margin} = 0.08 \text{ MPa (as per classification rule)}$$

**Step 5: Compute minimum required sealing water pressure.**

$$P_{sw} = P_{ext} + \Delta P_{margin}$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.0453 + 0.08 = 0.1253 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Express in practical units.**

$$P_{sw} \approx 0.125 \text{ MPa} \approx 1.253 \text{ bar (gauge)}$$

**Step 7: Determine gravity tank head height requirement.**

$$h_{tank} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{margin}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \dfrac{0.08 \times 10^6}{1025 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_{tank} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{10{,}055.25} = 7.956 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** A gravity tank mounted **at least 7.96 m above the stuffing box** is required to deliver the mandatory pressure margin. Most vessels install the gravity tank **8.5–10.0 m** above the shaft centerline for a safety buffer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mechanical Seal Friction Torque and Power Loss

### Problem Statement
A mechanical face seal on a vessel's propeller shaft has the following specifications:
- Shaft diameter: **D = 600 mm**
- Seal face outer diameter: **R_o = 320 mm**
- Seal face inner diameter: **R_i = 280 mm**
- Face contact pressure: **P_c = 0.50 MPa**
- Coefficient of friction (lubricated faces): **f = 0.08**
- Shaft speed: **n = 120 rpm**

Calculate: (a) Mean sliding velocity, (b) PV factor, (c) Friction torque, (d) Power loss.

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part (a): Mean Sliding Velocity

**Step 1: Compute the mean diameter of the seal face.**

$$D_m = R_o + R_i = 320 + 280 = 600 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(in radius terms, mean } r_m = 300 \text{ mm)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the circumferential velocity formula.**

$$v_m = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_m \cdot n}{60}$$

where $D_m$ is in metres = 0.600 m.

$$v_m = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.600 \times 120}{60}$$

**Step 3: Numerical evaluation.**

$$v_m = \dfrac{3.14159 \times 0.600 \times 120}{60} = \dfrac{226.195}{60}$$

$$v_m = 3.770 \text{ m/s}$$

#### Part (b): PV Factor (Wear Indicator)

**Step 4: Apply the PV formula.**

$$PV = P_c \cdot v_m$$

$$PV = 0.50 \text{ MPa} \times 3.770 \text{ m/s}$$

$$PV = 1.885 \text{ MPa·m/s}$$

**Step 5: Check against material limit.**

For **carbon vs. silicon carbide faces** (typical pairing), the PV limit is **2.5–3.5 MPa·m/s**.

**Conclusion:** PV = 1.885 MPa·m/s is **well within the design limit** — no risk of premature face wear.

#### Part (c): Friction Torque

**Step 6: Compute the contact (normal) force on the seal face.**

$$F_N = P_c \cdot A_{contact} = P_c \cdot \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$$

$$F_N = 0.50 \times \pi \times (320^2 - 280^2) \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F_N = 0.50 \times \pi \times (102{,}400 - 78{,}400) \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F_N = 0.50 \times \pi \times 24{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F_N = 0.50 \times 75{,}398.2 \text{ mm}^2 = 37{,}699.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7: Compute the friction torque.**

Using the formula $T_f = f \cdot F_N \cdot r_m$ where $r_m = 0.300$ m:

$$T_f = 0.08 \times 37{,}699.1 \times 0.300$$

$$T_f = 904.78 \text{ N·m}$$

#### Part (d): Power Loss

**Step 8: Compute angular velocity in rad/s.**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi n}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 120}{60} = 4 \pi = 12.566 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 9: Compute power loss.**

$$P_{loss} = T_f \cdot \omega = 904.78 \times 12.566$$

$$P_{loss} = 11{,}370 \text{ W} \approx 11.37 \text{ kW}$$

**Conclusion:** The mechanical face seal dissipates approximately **11.4 kW** of frictional heat, which must be removed by the sealing water or stern tube oil circulation system to prevent thermal damage to the seal faces and elastomer secondary seals.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Thin-Film Leakage Rate Through Seal Face

### Problem Statement
Estimate the leakage rate through a mechanical seal face with the following parameters:
- Mean diameter: **D_m = 0.600 m**
- Face gap (film thickness): **h = 0.5 µm = 0.5 × 10⁻⁶ m**
- Pressure differential across faces: **ΔP = 0.40 MPa = 4 × 10⁵ Pa**
- Dynamic viscosity of sealing oil: **μ = 0.05 Pa·s**
- Effective axial length of contact band: **L = 0.004 m** (4 mm)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the thin-film laminar flow equation (Reynolds equation simplified for parallel faces).**

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_m \cdot h^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.600 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-6})^3 \times 4 \times 10^5}{12 \times 0.05 \times 0.004}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the numerator.**

Numerator = $\pi \times 0.600 \times (1.25 \times 10^{-19}) \times (4 \times 10^5)$

Numerator = $\pi \times 0.600 \times 5 \times 10^{-14}$

Numerator = $3.14159 \times 3 \times 10^{-14}$

Numerator = $9.4248 \times 10^{-14}$ m⁴·Pa

**Step 4: Evaluate the denominator.**

Denominator = $12 \times 0.05 \times 0.004 = 0.0024$ Pa·s·m

**Step 5: Compute leakage rate.**

$$Q = \dfrac{9.4248 \times 10^{-14}}{0.0024}$$

$$Q = 3.927 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6: Convert to litres per hour.**

$$Q = 3.927 \times 10^{-11} \times 1000 \text{ (to litres)} \times 3600 \text{ (to per hour)}$$

$$Q = 3.927 \times 10^{-11} \times 3{,}600{,}000$$

$$Q = 1.414 \times 10^{-4} \text{ litres/hour}$$

**Conclusion:** The calculated leakage is **0.00014 litres/hour**, which is essentially zero. This explains why mechanical face seals are classified as **"zero-leakage"** seals in service. The dominant leakage path in a real seal is **not through the faces** (which are lapped to optical flatness), but through the **secondary O-ring elastomer seals** if they are damaged or aged.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Seal Selection Decision Table

The following analytical matrix is used by **Chief Engineers and Class Surveyors** to select the appropriate sealing system during new-build specification or repair assessment:

| Design Parameter | Packed Stuffing Box | Simplex Mechanical Seal | Tandem Mechanical Seal | Double Mechanical Seal |
|------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Initial cost | Low (1×) | Medium (3–5×) | High (6–10×) | Very High (10–15×) |
| Service interval | 6–12 months | 3–5 years | 5–7 years | 7–10 years |
| Visible leakage | Yes (controlled drip) | None | None (with monitoring) | None (redundant) |
| Friction loss | High (5–15 kW) | Low (3–8 kW) | Low (4–10 kW) | Medium (6–12 kW) |
| Maintenance skill | Low (manual) | Medium (specialist) | High (OEM-trained) | High (OEM-trained) |
| Environmental risk | Medium | Low | Very low | Negligible |
| Class acceptance | Universal | Universal | Universal | Universal (preferred for new) |
| Typical vessel size | < 30,000 DWT | All sizes | > 30,000 DWT | > 80,000 DWT, tankers |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "stuffing box" as used in marine propeller shaft sealing. List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stuffing box** is a sealing assembly fitted at the inboard end of the stern tube where the propeller shaft enters the engine room, designed to prevent seawater ingress into the vessel and to control leakage of sealing water from the stern tube.

**Main Components (1 mark each for 3 components):**
1. **Stuffing box body** — a cast iron or bronze housing bolted to the stern tube or aft peak bulkhead, with a central bore matching the shaft diameter.
2. **Packing rings** — 4 to 6 rings of square-section braided packing (graphite-impregnated PTFE or flax with tallow) that seal against the shaft.
3. **Lantern ring** — a perforated brass ring positioned centrally in the packing stack, which distributes sealing water uniformly around the shaft circumference.
4. **Gland follower** — a removable component that compresses the packing axially when tightened via studs.
5. **Gland studs and nuts** — provide the controlled axial force to compress the packing to the correct specific pressure (typically 1.5–3.0 N/mm²).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is a mechanical face seal? State its two main advantages over a conventional packed stuffing box.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **mechanical face seal** is a precision-engineered sealing device in which two flat, lapped surfaces (one rotating, one stationary) are held in sliding contact perpendicular to the shaft axis, with the seal interface lubricated by a thin hydrodynamic film of oil or water. (2 marks)

**Two Main Advantages (1/2 mark each for clearly stated advantage):**
1. **Zero visible leakage** — Unlike packed stuffing boxes that require controlled drip leakage (3–30 drops per minute), mechanical face seals operate with essentially zero leakage (theoretically < 0.001 litres/hour), which is critical for **MARPOL Annex I compliance** on oil-lubricated stern tubes.
2. **Lower friction and longer service life** — Mechanical face seals have friction coefficients of 0.05–0.12 (vs. 0.15–0.30 for packed stuffing boxes), resulting in **50–70% lower friction losses** and service intervals of **25,000–50,000 hours** (vs. 6–12 months for repacking).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, students must answer **one full question (14 marks)** from Module 3, with **internal choice** between two alternatives. The pattern below follows the exact KTU paper structure.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**Q: With the aid of a functional diagram, explain the construction and working of a tandem mechanical seal used in an oil-lubricated stern tube arrangement. State the materials of construction for the seal faces and the secondary seals. Discuss the survey requirements during a special survey.**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working [7 marks]

**Constructional Details (3 marks):**

A **tandem mechanical seal** consists of two independent mechanical face seals arranged **in series along the shaft axis**, separated by a **barrier fluid reservoir** that is pressurized with nitrogen or a compatible buffer fluid. The construction includes:

1. **Primary (inboard) seal pair**: The first line of defence against stern tube oil leakage. The rotating face is keyed to the shaft via a **Viton or PTFE O-ring** (secondary seal), and the stationary face (carbon-graphite) is held in the gland housing via another elastomer O-ring.
2. **Secondary (outboard) seal pair**: The backup seal, facing the seawater side. The rotating face is typically **silicon carbide** (hard, wear-resistant), and the stationary face is **carbon-graphite** (resilient, self-lubricating).
3. **Barrier fluid reservoir**: A small pressure vessel (1–3 litres capacity) mounted above the stuffing box, pressurized to **0.05–0.10 MPa above the higher of the two adjacent fluid pressures**. It contains a **buffer fluid** (typically a synthetic oil compatible with both stern tube oil and seawater).
4. **Springs**: Helical or wave springs that apply a controlled axial load (typically 50–200 N per spring) to maintain face contact.
5. **Gland flange**: Bolted to the stern tube or shaft bracket, providing axial restraint.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

1. The propeller shaft is supported by white metal bearings within the oil-filled stern tube.
2. The stern tube oil pressure is maintained at **0.15–0.40 MPa above external seawater pressure** by the gravity tank.
3. The **primary seal** contains the stern tube oil. Any oil that bypasses the primary seal enters the barrier fluid reservoir, where it is detected by an **oil content meter (OCM)** — alarm set at **15 ppm** of oil in the barrier water.
4. The **secondary seal** then contains the barrier fluid against seawater ingress. If the primary seal leaks, the barrier fluid pressure rises slightly, triggering an OCM alarm.
5. The barrier fluid is monitored continuously; the system allows operation with a degraded primary seal until the next port, but immediate dry-docking is required if the **secondary seal** also shows leakage.
6. The **forward seal pair** at the inboard end of the stern tube prevents oil leakage into the engine room and uses a similar tandem arrangement.

**Materials of Construction (0.5 marks each, included in the 7):**
- **Rotating face**: Tungsten carbide or silicon carbide (WC-Co or SiC)
- **Stationary face**: Carbon-graphite (resin-impregnated, antimony-impregnated, or metal-impregnated)
- **Secondary O-rings**: Viton (FKM) for oil service, EPDM for water service
- **Springs**: Stainless steel (AISI 316)
- **Gland housing**: Naval brass or Ni-Al bronze

#### Part (b) — Survey Requirements [7 marks]

**During Special Survey (every 5 years), the following checks are mandated by classification societies:**

1. **Stern tube oil analysis** (2 marks):
   - Sample stern tube oil and test for **FEGT (Four-Ball Extreme Pressure Test)** value — minimum 250 kg weld point.
   - Test for **water content** (Karl Fischer titration) — alarm at **0.5%** by mass.
   - Test for **particulate contamination** (spectrographic analysis) — wear metals indicate bearing or seal condition.
   - **Viscosity check** — must remain within ISO VG 68–100 spec.

2. **Shaft-to-bearing clearance measurement** (1.5 marks):
   - Use **dial bore gauge** or **lead-wire method** at forward and aft bearings.
   - **Maximum allowable clearance**: 0.3% of shaft diameter (e.g., 1.8 mm for D = 600 mm).
   - If clearance exceeds 0.4% D, **bearing replacement** is required.

3. **Seal face inspection** (2 marks):
   - Extract mechanical seal faces, examine under **10× magnification** for:
     - **Uniform light band wear** (acceptable, reuse)
     - **Chipping, grooving, hot spots** (unacceptable, replace faces)
     - **Pattern asymmetry** (indicates misalignment)
   - Replace all **elastomer O-rings** irrespective of visual condition.
   - Check spring tension with calibrated gauge.

4. **Hydrostatic test** (1.5 marks):
   - Pressurize the stern tube to **0.4 MPa** for **30 minutes**.
   - Verify **zero leakage** past all seals using a graduated collection vessel.
   - Class surveyor must witness the test.

5. **Oil content meter (OCM) calibration** — functional test of all alarms and shutdowns.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern] — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q: A vessel's propeller shaft passes through a conventional packed stuffing box. The shaft diameter is 450 mm, the stuffing box bore is 460 mm, and the gland is tightened to a total axial force of 35 kN distributed over 4 packing rings, each 12 mm thick. The shaft speed is 90 rpm. Calculate: (a) the specific pressure on the packing, (b) the mean sliding velocity, and (c) the friction power loss assuming a packing-shaft friction coefficient of 0.20. Discuss the limitations of packed stuffing boxes in modern shipping.**

#### Part (a) — Specific Pressure on the Packing [5 marks]

**Step 1: Calculate the effective contact area of the packing.**

The packing seals against the shaft along its **circumferential contact length**. The radial interference between shaft (450 mm) and bore (460 mm) is 10 mm total, split between radial expansion and compression.

The effective bearing area of the packing (cylindrical contact) is:

$$A_{contact} = \pi \cdot D_{shaft} \cdot L_{pack}$$

where $L_{pack}$ = total packing length (4 rings × 12 mm) = 48 mm.

$$A_{contact} = \pi \times 0.450 \times 0.048$$

$$A_{contact} = 0.0679 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply the specific pressure formula.**

$$\sigma_p = \dfrac{F_{gland}}{A_{contact}}$$

$$\sigma_p = \dfrac{35{,}000 \text{ N}}{0.0679 \text{ m}^2}$$

$$\sigma_p = 515{,}464 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 0.515 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Compare with the design range.**

**Recommended packing specific pressure: 1.5–3.0 MPa** for graphite-impregnated PTFE packing in marine service.

**Observation:** The calculated value of 0.515 MPa is **significantly below the recommended range**, indicating that the gland is **under-tightened**. The packing will leak excessively and the sealing water will not be properly distributed.

**Corrective action:** Increase gland force to achieve $\sigma_p \approx 2.0$ MPa:

$$F_{gland,corrected} = 2.0 \text{ MPa} \times 0.0679 \text{ m}^2 = 135.8 \text{ kN}$$

#### Part (b) — Mean Sliding Velocity [4 marks]

**Step 1: Apply the circumferential velocity formula at the shaft surface.**

$$v_m = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot n}{60}$$

$$v_m = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.450 \times 90}{60}$$

**Step 2: Numerical evaluation.**

$$v_m = \dfrac{127.235}{60} = 2.121 \text{ m/s}$$

**Conclusion:** The shaft surface sliding velocity is **2.12 m/s** — well within the design limit of **5 m/s** for conventional packing materials.

#### Part (c) — Friction Power Loss [5 marks]

**Step 1: Compute the normal (radial) force on the packing-shaft interface.**

The packing exerts a normal force on the shaft equal to the gland force times the packing friction angle (assumed equal to the gland force for the conservative friction calculation):

$$F_N = F_{gland} = 35{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Compute the friction force on the shaft surface.**

$$F_{friction} = f \cdot F_N = 0.20 \times 35{,}000 = 7{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Compute the friction torque at the shaft radius.**

$$T_{friction} = F_{friction} \cdot r_{shaft} = 7{,}000 \times 0.225 = 1{,}575 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Compute the angular velocity.**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi n}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 90}{60} = 9.425 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Compute the power loss.**

$$P_{loss} = T_{friction} \times \omega = 1{,}575 \times 9.425$$

$$P_{loss} = 14{,}844 \text{ W} \approx 14.84 \text{ kW}$$

**Conclusion:** The packed stuffing box dissipates **14.84 kW** of frictional heat — this is approximately **3–4× the friction loss of an equivalent mechanical face seal** for the same service. The frictional heat must be removed by the sealing water flow; otherwise, the packing will char and fail.

**Limitations of Packed Stuffing Boxes in Modern Shipping (1 mark for the discussion, included in the 5):**

1. **High friction losses** (3–4× mechanical seal) reduce propulsive efficiency.
2. **Continuous leakage** of sealing water creates an oil-contaminated effluent requiring OWS (Oily Water Separator) processing.
3. **Frequent maintenance** — repacking required every 6–12 months, with shaft wear inspection.
4. **Environmental risk** — any seal failure results in oil pollution (MARPOL violation).
5. **Not suitable for high-power installations** — limited to vessels below ~30,000 DWT in modern new-build designs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Mark Deductions to Watch For):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up units**: The PV factor must use **MPa·m/s** — students often calculate PV in **Pa·m/s**, giving a value 10⁶ too large. Always convert pressure to MPa **before** multiplication.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to add the safety margin** in sealing water pressure calculations. The formula $P_{sw} \ge P_{ext} + \Delta P_{margin}$ is non-negotiable — class surveyors WILL fail the calculation if the margin is missing.
>
> 3. **Using shaft radius instead of mean seal radius** in the friction torque calculation. For seals with a wide contact band (e.g., $R_o = 320$ mm, $R_i = 280$ mm), the mean radius $r_m = (R_o + R_i)/2 = 300$ mm MUST be used, not the shaft outer radius.
>
> 4. **Confusing the lantern ring function**: Students often state that the lantern ring "seals" the shaft — it does not. Its function is to **distribute sealing water uniformly** around the circumference. The actual sealing is done by the packing rings on either side of it.
>
> 5. **Omitting units in intermediate steps**: KTU examiners deduct **0.5 marks per step** for unit inconsistency. Always write units in every line of the calculation.
>
> 6. **Mechanical seal face material pairings**: The correct pairing is **hard face vs. soft face** — typically **silicon carbide (rotating) vs. carbon-graphite (stationary)**. The reverse pairing (graphite rotating against SiC stationary) is mechanically incorrect and will fail quickly.
>
> 7. **Survey question trap**: When asked about "survey requirements," students often give **operational maintenance** steps (oil top-up, gland adjustment) instead of **class survey** steps (FEGT test, clearance measurement, hydrostatic test). The KTU paper specifically tests the **statutory/class survey** perspective.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Propeller Shaft Sealing Equipment**

### Core Concepts (Must Memorize)
- [ ] **Two main seal types**: (1) Packed stuffing box, (2) Mechanical face seal (Simplex / Tandem / Double).
- [ ] **Two lubrication philosophies**: (1) Water-lubricated (seawater, lignum vitae/rubber bearings), (2) Oil-lubricated (white metal bearings, mineral oil).
- [ ] **Lantern ring** = perforated distributor for sealing water, **NOT a sealing element itself**.
- [ ] **Packing material of choice today**: Graphite-impregnated PTFE (replaced older flax/hemp with tallow).
- [ ] **Mechanical seal face pairing**: Silicon carbide (rotating) vs. carbon-graphite (stationary).
- [ ] **Sealing water pressure rule**: $P_{sw} = P_{ext} + 0.05$ to $0.10$ MPa minimum.
- [ ] **MARPOL Annex I Reg. 12A**: This regulation requires oil-lubricated stern tubes to be fitted with **OCMs** and **sealing fluid arrangements** that prevent oil discharge overboard.

### Critical Numerical Values (Exam-Ready)
- [ ] Seawater density: $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³
- [ ] Specific pressure on packing (graphite-PTFE): 1.5–3.0 MPa
- [ ] Mechanical seal face contact pressure: 0.3–0.7 MPa
- [ ] PV limit for SiC/Carbon: 2.5–3.5 MPa·m/s
- [ ] Friction coefficient (lubricated seal face): 0.05–0.12
- [ ] Friction coefficient (packed stuffing box): 0.15–0.30
- [ ] Maximum shaft-to-bearing clearance: 0.3% of D
- [ ] OCM alarm threshold: 15 ppm oil in barrier water
- [ ] FEGT minimum weld point for stern tube oil: 250 kg
- [ ] Hydrostatic test pressure for stern tube: 0.4 MPa for 30 minutes

### Survey and Class Triggers
- [ ] **Special survey** (every 5 years) → full oil analysis, clearance measurement, seal face inspection.
- [ ] **Annual survey** → oil sample, OCM function test, gland adjustment (for packed boxes).
- [ ] **Continuous monitoring** → OCM readings, sealing water flow, stern tube oil pressure, oil temperature, bearing temperature.

### Real-World Failure Modes (Examiner's Favourite)
- [ ] **Catastrophic seal failure** → loss of 5,000–15,000 litres of stern tube oil overboard.
- [ ] **Primary seal wear, secondary intact** → OCM alarm, slow degradation, schedule dry-dock.
- [ ] **Both seals fail** → mandatory emergency dry-dock, possible MARPOL prosecution.
- [ ] **Packing char** → caused by insufficient sealing water flow; remedy: check gravity tank level and pipe blockages.
- [ ] **Excessive shaft wear** → caused by abrasive contamination in stern tube oil; remedy: full oil change, filter replacement, source investigation.

### Diagram-Linked Knowledge
- [ ] Be able to **draw a labeled cross-section** of a packed stuffing box (8 labels minimum).
- [ ] Be able to **draw a labeled cross-section** of a tandem mechanical seal (10 labels minimum).
- [ ] Be able to **sketch the oil circulation circuit** for an oil-lubricated stern tube (gravity tank → pump → cooler → stern tube → return line → sump).

### Common Exam Question Formats
- [ ] **Numerical** (8–10 marks): Given shaft diameter, speed, gland force, etc., calculate specific pressure, velocity, PV factor, friction torque, or power loss.
- [ ] **Descriptive with diagram** (10–14 marks): "Explain the construction and working of [X] with a labeled diagram. State materials of construction. Discuss survey requirements."
- [ ] **Comparison** (7 marks): "Compare packed stuffing box vs. mechanical face seal in terms of [friction, leakage, maintenance, environmental risk]."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rudder - Pintle Clearances
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rudder - Pintle Clearances

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pintle Clearance** in shipboard engineering refers to the *radial and axial gap* allowed between the **Pintle** (the vertical cylindrical pin forged/cast at the lower end of the rudder) and the **Gudgeon** (the matching bush/socket fitted on the rudder horn or stern frame) which together form the *lower bearing assembly of the rudder*.

The pintle-gudgeon arrangement is the **primary load-bearing lower pivot** of a balanced or semi-balanced rudder. Surveyors measure these clearances during *annual, intermediate, and special (dry-dock) surveys* to determine wear, alignment integrity, and the remaining operational life of the rudder bearing system.

The two principal clearances monitored by classification surveyors are:

1. **Radial Pintle Clearance** — the diametric gap between the pintle bearing surface and the gudgeon bush inner diameter, measured at multiple angular positions (typically 4 points: 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°).
2. **Axial Pintle Clearance (Vertical Play)** — the upward/downward free movement of the pintle within the gudgeon when the rudder is un-supported, indicating wear of the bearing surfaces and shim pack condition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Door Hinge Test:** Imagine a heavy steel gate (your rudder) that swings on a vertical pin-and-socket hinge (pintle-gudgeon). If you grip the gate and try to lift it upward, it should normally have *almost zero movement*. If it lifts more than a hairline gap, the pin or its bush has worn oval. Now grab the gate and try to wobble it sideways — if you feel *rocking*, the pin has worn round in the bush. Both these "gaps" are exactly what a ship surveyor measures on the rudder pintle!
