In simpler terms: **Pintle clearance = the wobble room** between the rudder's pivot pin and its socket. Too little clearance → seizure under thermal expansion or rudder shock loads. Too much clearance → vibration, sluggish steering response, pounding on the stops, and accelerated wear of the entire steering gear.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reference Values used by Major Classification Societies (ABS / DNV / LR / IRS / BV):**
> - Maximum permissible **radial clearance** (new build): typically **0.4 % to 0.6 %** of pintle neck diameter.
> - Maximum permissible **radial clearance** (in-service wear limit): typically limited to **~3 mm to 8 mm** depending on pintle diameter and society rules.
> - Maximum permissible **axial clearance**: typically **3 mm to 6 mm**.
> - Pintle material: **Forged or cast steel** (often **Grade ASTM A668** or equivalent **Class C** forging).
> - Gudgeon bush material: **Phosphor bronze**, **Gunmetal**, **Lignum vitae**, **Synthetic composite (e.g., rubber-backed synthetic)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Logic of Pintle Bearing System — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The pintle-gudgeon assembly is **NOT** a self-aligning bearing. It works as a **plain cylindrical journal bearing** carrying:
- **Radial load:** Hydrodynamic pressure from the rudder face when water flows past at angle of attack.
- **Vertical (axial) load:** Weight of the rudder, partially transmitted through the pintle.
- **Dynamic (impact) load:** Slamming loads, propeller-induced vibration, hard-over rudder shocks.

**Operational Logic Flow:**

- **Step 1:** Rudder is hung on the **upper rudder bearing (typically a roller or self-aligning bearing)** inside the rudder trunk.
- **Step 2:** Lower end of rudder has the **pintle**, which drops into the **gudgeon** mounted on the rudder horn (or hull plating for spade rudders).
- **Step 3:** Pintle transmits **radial hydrodynamic loads** from rudder face to the hull structure via the gudgeon.
- **Step 4:** Lateral (sideways) load on rudder → bending moment at pintle neck → compressive reaction at gudgeon contact arc.
- **Step 5:** Continuous rotation of rudder (35° port to 35° stbd, ±2 to 3 rev/min) creates **slow sliding contact** between pintle and gudgeon bush.
- **Step 6:** Over thousands of operating hours, the **bush wears**, creating **radial and axial clearance**.
- **Step 7:** Surveyor measures clearance → compares with class rule limit → recommends repair, bushing renewal, or survey frequency.

### 2.2 Why Pintle Clearance Matters — The 'Why & How' Matrix

| Aspect | Why it Matters | How Surveyor Detects It |
|---|---|---|
| Radial Clearance ↑ | Loss of steering precision; rudder vibration; pounding at hardover | Feeler gauge / internal micrometer at 4 angular positions |
| Axial Clearance ↑ | Rudder drops slightly; stock wear; possible bottom contact | Lift rudder with hydraulic jack, measure vertical lift |
| Ovality (Out-of-roundness) | Uneven load distribution → localised wear → fatigue cracking | Bore gauge readings 90° apart → differential reading |
| Taper | Misalignment of rudder axis with propeller shaft axis | Compare top and bottom radial readings |
| Corrosion in gap | Seizing risk; galling; accelerated wear | Visual + borescope inspection of bush interior |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All key equations for pintle clearance design, measurement, and condition assessment are consolidated below. **Use `\vert` for absolute values** to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diametral Clearance $C_d$ | $C_d = D_b - d_p$ | mm | $D_b$ = gudgeon bush bore ID ; $d_p$ = pintle neck OD |
| 2 | Radial Clearance $C_r$ | $C_r = \dfrac{D_b - d_p}{2}$ | mm | Half of diametral clearance |
| 3 | Percentage Radial Clearance $\%C_r$ | $\%C_r = \left(\dfrac{D_b - d_p}{2 \cdot d_p}\right) \times 100$ | % | New build reference: 0.4 to 0.6 % |
| 4 | Ovality $O$ | $O = D_{b,\max} - D_{b,\min}$ | mm | Measured across perpendicular axes |
| 5 | Taper $T$ | $T = D_{b,\text{top}} - D_{b,\text{bottom}}$ | mm | Compare upper & lower bush readings |
| 6 | Total Wear $W_{tot}$ | $W_{tot} = C_{r,\text{actual}} - C_{r,\text{new}}$ | mm | Difference from original fitted clearance |
| 7 | Wear Rate $R_w$ | $R_w = \dfrac{W_{tot}}{t}$ | mm/year | $t$ = operating hours / 8760 |
| 8 | Remaining Life $L_r$ | $L_r = \dfrac{C_{r,\max} - C_{r,\text{actual}}}{R_w}$ | years | Until class limit reached |
| 9 | Pintle Bearing Pressure $p_b$ | $p_b = \dfrac{F_r}{d_p \cdot L_b}$ | N/mm² | $F_r$ = radial load; $L_b$ = bush length |
| 10 | Axial Lift Test Result | $\Delta_a = h_{\text{lifted}} - h_{\text{rest}}$ | mm | Using hydraulic jack and dial gauge |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Pintle Clearance Analysis

- **Class Survey Compliance:** Without valid measurements, the vessel cannot retain her classification certificate.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Wear-rate extrapolation schedules bushing renewal before failure, avoiding expensive emergency dry-docking.
- **Steering Gear Damage Prevention:** Excessive pintle play → hardover shock loads transmitted back to the tiller/ram → costly steering gear overhaul.
- **Insurance & P&I Claims:** Pintle failure is a common cause of **grounding incidents** due to steering loss; surveyor reports are vital legal documents.
- **Hull Insurance Survey (Machinery):** Records confirm rudder is fit for intended voyage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relating Radial Clearance to Percentage Clearance

The classification society tolerance is often expressed as a percentage of the pintle diameter rather than an absolute mm value. The relationship is derived as follows.

**Given:**
- Pintle diameter $d_p$
- Gudgeon bush inner diameter $D_b$

**Goal:** Express the radial clearance as a percentage of pintle diameter.

**Step 1 — Define diametral clearance** (measured directly across the bush):

$$C_d = D_b - d_p$$

**Step 2 — Convert to radial clearance** (single-side gap, also the designer's working value):

$$C_r = \frac{C_d}{2} = \frac{D_b - d_p}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Non-dimensionalise by dividing by the pintle diameter** to obtain a ratio independent of rudder size:

$$\text{Ratio } r = \frac{C_r}{d_p} = \frac{D_b - d_p}{2 \, d_p}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage form** (multiplied by 100):

$$\boxed{\% C_r = \left(\frac{D_b - d_p}{2 \, d_p}\right) \times 100}$$

**Interpretation:** For a typical pintle neck diameter of $d_p = 300$ mm and an as-built clearance of $C_r = 1.2$ mm, the percentage clearance is:

$$\% C_r = \left(\frac{1.2}{300}\right) \times 100 = 0.40 \, \%$$

This is the typical new-build target for a bronze bushed pintle bearing.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wear Rate and Remaining Life Prediction

**Given:**
- Original (as-built) radial clearance $C_{r,\text{new}}$
- Current measured radial clearance $C_{r,\text{actual}}$
- Maximum allowable radial clearance (class rule limit) $C_{r,\max}$
- Total operating time since last bushing renewal $t_{\text{op}}$ (in years)

**Step 1 — Calculate total accumulated wear to date:**

$$W_{tot} = C_{r,\text{actual}} - C_{r,\text{new}}$$

**Step 2 — Express as an annual wear rate** (assuming linear wear progression, which is valid for bronze / composite bushes under steady hydrodynamic loading):

$$R_w = \frac{W_{tot}}{t_{\text{op}}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the remaining permissible wear before reaching class limit:**

$$W_{rem} = C_{r,\max} - C_{r,\text{actual}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the predicted remaining service life of the bushing:**

$$\boxed{L_r = \frac{W_{rem}}{R_w} = \frac{C_{r,\max} - C_{r,\text{actual}}}{R_w}}$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**

A vessel has a pintle neck diameter of 250 mm. As-built radial clearance was 1.0 mm. After 5 years of operation, the surveyor's bore-gauge readings are:

$$D_{b,\max} = 252.4 \, \text{mm}, \quad D_{b,\min} = 251.6 \, \text{mm}$$

Step A — Average bush bore:

$$\overline{D_b} = \frac{252.4 + 251.6}{2} = 252.0 \, \text{mm}$$

Step B — Current radial clearance:

$$C_{r,\text{actual}} = \frac{252.0 - 250.0}{2} = 1.0 \, \text{mm}$$

Step C — Ovality check:

$$O = 252.4 - 251.6 = 0.8 \, \text{mm}$$

(Generally acceptable if $\le 1.0$ mm for a 250 mm pintle.)

Step D — Total wear:

$$W_{tot} = 1.0 - 1.0 = 0.0 \, \text{mm} \quad \text{(essentially no wear)}$$

Step E — Class limit for 250 mm pintle (typical rule): $C_{r,\max} = 4.0$ mm.

Step F — Remaining permissible wear:

$$W_{rem} = 4.0 - 1.0 = 3.0 \, \text{mm}$$

Step G — At a typical wear rate of 0.4 mm/year (estimated):

$$R_w = \frac{0.4 \, \text{mm}}{1 \, \text{yr}} = 0.4 \, \text{mm/yr}$$

Step H — Predicted life of bush:

$$L_r = \frac{3.0}{0.4} = 7.5 \, \text{years}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pintle Bearing Pressure Check

**Step 1 — Define radial hydrodynamic load** transferred through pintle (often taken as a fraction of rudder force $F_R$, typically 0.4 to 0.6 of total):

$$F_r = \alpha \cdot F_R, \quad \alpha \approx 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Projected bearing area** (cylindrical contact):

$$A_b = d_p \cdot L_b$$

**Step 3 — Average bearing pressure:**

$$\boxed{p_b = \frac{F_r}{d_p \cdot L_b} = \frac{\alpha \cdot F_R}{d_p \cdot L_b}}$$

**Acceptance Criterion (typical):**

$$p_b \le 5.0 \, \text{N/mm}^2 \quad \text{(bronze bush on steel pintle, well-lubricated)}$$

If $p_b$ exceeds the limit, either the bush length $L_b$ must be increased or the pintle diameter enlarged.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rudder Pintle-Gudgeon Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rudder Stock / Rudder Head] --> B[Rudder Body / Plate]
    B --> C[Pintle Neck - Forged Steel]
    C --> D[Pintle Neck Tapered Section]
    D --> E[Pintle Head / Nut Retaining]
    F[Rudder Horn - Hull Casting] --> G[Gudgeon Boss]
    G --> H[Gudgeon Bush - Phosphor Bronze]
    H --> I[Lubrication Groove]
    H --> J[Drain Hole]
    K[Upper Rudder Bearing - Roller] --> A
    L[Lower Rudder Bearing - Pintle] --> H
    M[Radial Clearance - CR] --> N[Class Surveyor Measurement]
    O[Axial Clearance - CA] --> N
    P[Ovality Check] --> N
    Q[Taper Check] --> N
    R[Condition Report] --> S[Repair / Renew Decision]
    N --> R
```

**Explanation of Flow:** The diagram traces the structural path of the steering load from the rudder face, through the pintle neck, into the gudgeon bush, and finally to the hull structure via the rudder horn. The lower block shows the surveyor's measurement workflow leading to a condition report.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pintle Survey Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Pre-Survey Preparation] --> node2[Dry-Dock Vessel / Afloat Inspection]
    node2 --> node3[Clean Pintle and Gudgeon Surfaces]
    node3 --> node4[Identify Pintle Neck Diameter dp]
    node4 --> node5[Measure Bush Bore at 4 Angles 0,90,180,270 deg]
    node5 --> node6[Record Measurements in Survey Log]
    node6 --> node7[Calculate Radial Clearance CR]
    node6 --> node8[Calculate Ovality O]
    node6 --> node9[Calculate Taper T]
    node7 --> node10{Class Limit Check}
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11[Within Limit - Routine Survey]
    node10 --> node12[Exceeds Limit - Recommend Repair]
    node12 --> node13[Bush Renewal Options]
    node13 --> node14[1 New Bronze Bush]
    node13 --> node15[2 Synthetic Composite Lining]
    node13 --> node16[3 Pintle Re-Machining and Re-Bushing]
```

**Functional Architecture Explanation:**

- **Stage 1 (Data Acquisition):** Measurement of geometry.
- **Stage 2 (Calculation):** Conversion of raw readings into engineering parameters.
- **Stage 3 (Decision Gate):** Acceptance check against class rule limits.
- **Stage 4 (Remedial Action):** Selection of appropriate repair strategy.

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Pintle Clearance Tolerance Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    zone1[Zone A - New Build Clearance - 0.4 to 0.6 percent of dp]
    zone2[Zone B - In-Service Acceptable - Up to 2 percent of dp]
    zone3[Zone C - Surveyor Alert - 2 to 3 percent of dp]
    zone4[Zone D - Beyond Class Limit - Above 3 percent - Repair Mandated]
    zone1 --> zone2 --> zone3 --> zone4
    zone1 --> action1[Acceptable - No Action]
    zone2 --> action2[Continue Routine Survey]
    zone3 --> action3[Increase Survey Frequency]
    zone4 --> action4[Immediate Bushing Renewal Required]
```

**Interpretation:** This visualises the wear progression and the corresponding survey action required as the clearance increases. The exact percentage thresholds depend on the classification society's ruleset and must always be cross-checked with the relevant rule book (e.g., IRS Rules for Sea-Going Ships, Part 3, Chapter 5).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define the term "Pintle Clearance" as applicable to ship rudder assemblies. State any two typical values of permissible radial pintle clearance.*

**Model Answer:**

> Pintle clearance is the radial (and axial) gap existing between the pintle — the vertical cylindrical pin fitted at the lower end of the rudder — and the gudgeon bush in which the pintle rotates, when measured during rudder surveys.

> **(i)** As a **percentage of pintle diameter**, new-build radial clearance is typically **0.4 % to 0.6 %** of pintle neck diameter.
> **(ii)** As an **absolute value**, in-service permissible radial clearance is generally limited to about **3 mm to 8 mm** depending on pintle size and classification society rules.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two typical values.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any four measurements a surveyor records during a dry-dock inspection of a rudder pintle-bearing assembly.*

**Model Answer:**

The four standard measurements recorded are:

1. **Bush bore diameter** at four angular positions (0°, 90°, 180°, 270°) using a bore gauge or internal micrometer.
2. **Pintle neck diameter** at corresponding positions (top, mid, bottom of bearing length).
3. **Radial pintle clearance** = (Bush ID − Pintle OD) ⁄ 2 at each of the four positions.
4. **Axial pintle clearance (vertical play)** = upward lift measured with a dial gauge when the rudder is jacked up.

*(3 marks — ½ mark for each correct measurement + ½ mark for the measurement technique.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** When answering Part B questions on pintle clearance, students most commonly lose marks because they **(1)** confuse *radial* and *diametral* clearance (factor-of-2 error), **(2)** forget to report ovality and taper separately from average clearance, **(3)** omit the *units* in final answers, and **(4)** fail to *compare* their calculated value with the class rule limit. Always state the rule reference (e.g., "as per IRS Pt.3 Ch.5") before concluding acceptability.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and function of a **pintle and gudgeon assembly** of a ship's rudder. State the typical materials used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a special survey on a bulk carrier, the following bore-gauge readings were taken on the rudder pintle gudgeon at midsection:

- Reading at 0°: 305.20 mm
- Reading at 90°: 304.40 mm
- Reading at 180°: 305.10 mm
- Reading at 270°: 304.50 mm

The pintle neck diameter is **300.00 mm**. The class rule limit for radial clearance is **5.0 mm** for this pintle size. Calculate:

1. Average bush bore.
2. Average radial clearance.
3. Ovality of the bush.
4. Whether the pintle bearing is acceptable under class rules. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Function:**

The **pintle** is a forged-steel cylindrical pin forming the lower extension of the rudder stock / rudder plate. It is typically machined to a close tolerance and may be provided with a tapered shank and a retaining nut or washer at its base. The **gudgeon** is a heavy boss casting integral with the rudder horn (or hull plating for a spade rudder), into which a renewable bush of **phosphor bronze** or **synthetic composite** is fitted and locked in place by a retaining ring.

**Function:**
- Acts as the **lower pivot bearing** of the rudder, transmitting lateral hydrodynamic loads from the rudder face to the hull structure.
- Maintains **radial and axial alignment** of the rudder.
- Permits **rotation** of the rudder under tiller / ram action while carrying heavy bending moments.

**Materials:**
- Pintle: **Forged steel** (ASTM A668 Class C / equivalent).
- Gudgeon: **Cast steel** (hull-integral) with **phosphor bronze bush** (e.g., composition Cu-Sn10 or Cu-Sn12).

*(7 marks distributed: 2 marks construction, 2 marks function, 1.5 marks sketch description, 1.5 marks materials.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given data:
$$d_p = 300.00 \, \text{mm}, \quad D_{b,0} = 305.20, \, D_{b,90} = 304.40, \, D_{b,180} = 305.10, \, D_{b,270} = 304.50 \, \text{mm}$$

**1. Average bush bore:**

$$\overline{D_b} = \frac{305.20 + 304.40 + 305.10 + 304.50}{4}$$

$$\overline{D_b} = \frac{1219.20}{4} = 304.80 \, \text{mm}$$

**2. Average radial clearance:**

$$C_r = \frac{\overline{D_b} - d_p}{2} = \frac{304.80 - 300.00}{2}$$

$$\boxed{C_r = 2.40 \, \text{mm}}$$

**3. Ovality of bush:**

$$O = D_{b,\max} - D_{b,\min} = 305.20 - 304.40$$

$$\boxed{O = 0.80 \, \text{mm}}$$

**4. Acceptability Check:**

- Class limit for radial clearance: $C_{r,\max} = 5.0 \, \text{mm}$
- Measured: $C_r = 2.40 \, \text{mm}$ → **well within limit**
- Ovality: $O = 0.80 \, \text{mm}$ → acceptable (typically $\le 1.0 \, \text{mm}$ for 300 mm pintle).

**Conclusion:** The pintle bearing is **ACCEPTABLE** under class rules. Continue routine annual surveys; recommend re-measurement at next dry-docking.

*(7 marks distributed: 1.5 each for steps 1–4, plus 1 mark for final conclusion.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the term **pintle clearance**. Discuss the difference between *radial clearance*, *axial clearance*, *ovality*, and *taper* as measured during rudder survey. State the typical permissible limits for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A general cargo ship has a pintle with the following survey data:

- Pintle neck diameter: 280 mm
- New-build radial clearance: 1.2 mm
- Current average radial clearance (after 6 years): 3.6 mm
- Maximum permissible radial clearance (class rule): 5.0 mm

Calculate:

1. Total accumulated wear in 6 years.
2. Annual wear rate.
3. Remaining permissible wear.
4. Estimated remaining life of the gudgeon bush. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation of Pintle Clearance Terms:**

- **Pintle Clearance** is the gap between the pintle (lower pivot pin of the rudder) and the gudgeon bush in which it rotates, measured during class surveys to monitor wear.
- **Radial clearance** is the single-side gap between pintle OD and bush ID measured perpendicular to the rudder axis. Typical permissible limit: **3 to 8 mm** depending on pintle size.
- **Axial clearance** is the vertical free play of the rudder when lifted by a hydraulic jack. Typical limit: **3 to 6 mm**.
- **Ovality** is the out-of-roundness of the bush = $D_{b,\max} - D_{b,\min}$ across perpendicular axes. Typical limit: **$\le 1.0 \, \text{mm}$** for 250–300 mm pintles.
- **Taper** is the difference in bore diameter between the top and bottom of the bush. Typical limit: **$\le 1.0 \, \text{mm}$**.

*(7 marks: 2 for definitions + 2 for differences + 1.5 for limits + 1.5 for overall presentation.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
$$d_p = 280 \, \text{mm}, \quad C_{r,\text{new}} = 1.2 \, \text{mm}, \quad C_{r,\text{actual}} = 3.6 \, \text{mm}, \quad t_{\text{op}} = 6 \, \text{yr}, \quad C_{r,\max} = 5.0 \, \text{mm}$$

**1. Total accumulated wear:**

$$W_{tot} = C_{r,\text{actual}} - C_{r,\text{new}} = 3.6 - 1.2$$

$$\boxed{W_{tot} = 2.4 \, \text{mm}}$$

**2. Annual wear rate:**

$$R_w = \frac{W_{tot}}{t_{\text{op}}} = \frac{2.4}{6}$$

$$\boxed{R_w = 0.40 \, \text{mm/year}}$$

**3. Remaining permissible wear:**

$$W_{rem} = C_{r,\max} - C_{r,\text{actual}} = 5.0 - 3.6$$

$$\boxed{W_{rem} = 1.4 \, \text{mm}}$$

**4. Estimated remaining life of bush:**

$$L_r = \frac{W_{rem}}{R_w} = \frac{1.4}{0.40}$$

$$\boxed{L_r = 3.5 \, \text{years}}$$

**Conclusion:** The gudgeon bush is expected to remain serviceable for approximately **3.5 more years** before reaching class limit. Recommendation: re-measure at next annual survey; plan bushing renewal at the following special survey.

*(7 marks distributed: 1.5 each for steps 1–4, plus 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pintle Clearances (Module 3):**

- **Pintle** = vertical pivot pin at lower end of rudder (forged steel).
- **Gudgeon** = socket / bush housing on rudder horn (cast steel + bronze bush).
- **Radial Clearance** $C_r = (D_b - d_p) / 2$ → single-side gap.
- **Diametral Clearance** $C_d = D_b - d_p$ → total across bush.
- **% Clearance** = $(C_r / d_p) \times 100$ → new-build value **0.4 to 0.6 %**.
- **Ovality** $O = D_{b,\max} - D_{b,\min}$ → measured at 0° and 90°.
- **Taper** $T = D_{b,\text{top}} - D_{b,\text{bottom}}$ → vertical straightness check.
- **Axial Clearance (Vertical Play)** → measured with hydraulic jack + dial gauge → typical limit **3–6 mm**.
- **Class Survey Threshold:** Radial clearance generally limited to **3–8 mm** depending on pintle size.
- **Wear Rate** = total wear / operating years → used for **predictive maintenance**.
- **Acceptable Bearing Pressure** $p_b \le 5.0 \, \text{N/mm}^2$ for bronze / steel pair.
- **Common Repair Actions:** (1) Bushing renewal (bronze), (2) Synthetic composite lining retrofit, (3) Pintle re-machining + re-bushing, (4) Rudder horn modification.
- **Survey Frequency Trigger:** Excessive wear → increase survey interval or mandate unscheduled dry-dock.
- **Always reference** the applicable class rule (IRS / ABS / DNV / LR / BV) for exact tolerance values — never memorise a single number as universal.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance of Sea Water Suction and Overboard Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maintenance of Sea Water Suction and Overboard Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Sea Water Suction Valves** are the isolation valves fitted on the sea suction piping between the **sea chest (or sea inlet)** and the **suction side of the pumps** (cooling pumps, ballast pumps, fire pumps, general service pumps). Their primary function is to permit **isolation of the piping system from the sea** for maintenance, survey, and emergency shut-off, while preventing **flooding backflow** into the ship.

> [!NOTE]
> **Overboard Valves** are the discharge-side valves fitted on the shell plating (or hull side) that allow the controlled release of treated or untreated water from internal systems (sanitary, bilge, cooling, ballast) to the sea. They include **discharge valves, scuppers, sanitary overboard discharge fittings, and freeing port valves**.

Together, these valves form the **ship's interface with the sea** and are subject to the most severe marine environment: **salt-water corrosion, marine growth, cathodic reactions, and mechanical wear**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Building's Main Water Tap and Drain Valve"**
> Imagine a large apartment building. The **sea water suction valve** is like the **main inlet tap** from the municipal supply — it controls all the water coming in. The **overboard valve** is like the **main drain valve** going to the sewer. If either fails, the whole system fails. In a ship, these valves are even more critical because the "tap" is the ocean itself, and a failure means **catastrophic flooding** or **uncontrolled discharge (pollution)**.

### 1.3 Physical & Regulatory Constants

- **Design Pressure** of sea suction lines: typically **10 bar (1.0 MPa)** working pressure.
- **Test Pressure** for hydrostatic testing: **1.5 × Design Pressure = 15 bar**.
- **SOLAS Reg. II-1/26** mandates positive means of indication at the valve position.
- **MARPOL Annex IV** governs overboard discharge of **sewage** through these valves.
- **MARPOL Annex V** governs overboard discharge of **garbage and bilge water**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Sea Water Suction Valves

1. **Butterfly Valves** — Most common on large-bore sea suctions (DN 300 and above); compact, lightweight, quick operation.
2. **Gate Valves** — Used on smaller bores where full-bore flow is required; slow operation.
3. **Globe/Stop Valves** — Used for small branch suctions (e.g., lubricating oil cooler sea water supply).
4. **SDNR (Screw-Down Non-Return) Valves** — Used where backflow prevention is critical (e.g., emergency bilge suction).
5. **Storm Valves** — Heavy-duty valves on side shell connections that can be closed from above the freeboard deck during heavy weather.

### 2.2 Types of Overboard Valves

1. **Screw-Down Discharge Valves** — Direct overboard discharge through shell.
2. **Flap Valves / Non-Return Valves** — Allow one-way discharge, prevent sea ingress.
3. **Scupper Valves** — For deck drainage.
4. **Sanitary Overboard Discharge Valves** — Must be closed in port (per MARPOL).
5. **Freeing Port Valves** — On bulwarks for freeing trapped water.

### 2.3 Maintenance Philosophy — The 5-Pillar Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Maintenance of sea water and overboard valves follows a **structured 5-pillar framework** aligned with IACS UR Z17 / Z23 and Class survey requirements.

1. **Pillar 1 — Routine Operational Maintenance**
   - Daily/weekly visual inspection for leakage past spindle, body, flanges.
   - Operation of valves (open/close cycle) to prevent seizure — at least **weekly** for critical valves.
   - Lubrication of spindle threads and gear boxes with **marine-grade grease (NLGI 2)**.
   - Cleaning of **sea chest strainers** (per voyage or as pressure drop indicates).

2. **Pillar 2 — Condition-Based Maintenance**
   - Monitoring of **suction vacuum** and **discharge pressure** trends.
   - **Ultrasonic thickness measurement (UTM)** of valve bodies and pipework.
   - ** Dye-penetrant inspection (DPI)** of valve body castings for cracks.
   - ** Magnetic particle inspection (MPI)** of carbon steel valve bodies.

3. **Pillar 3 — Annual/Biannual Overhaul**
   - Stripping down of gland packing; renewal of **graphite or PTFE packing rings**.
   - Inspection of **disc, seat rings, and spindle** for wear, pitting, erosion.
   - Re-machining of seat faces (lapping) if pitting < 0.5 mm depth.
   - Replacement if pitting > 1 mm or wall reduction > original + 1 mm.

4. **Pillar 4 — Dry-Dock Overhaul**
   - Complete valve removal, workshop overhaul, seat renewal.
   - **Hydraulic pressure test** at 1.5 × design pressure for **30 minutes**.
   - Replacement of all gaskets, studs, and nuts (renewal philosophy).
   - Recertification by Class Surveyor.

5. **Pillar 5 — Survey & Certification**
   - Annual Class survey of operation and tightness.
   - Special / Continuous survey for sea suction valves per IACS UR Z23.
   - **Biannual sea chest inspection** by diver or in dry dock.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises the **key formulas and acceptance criteria** used in survey and maintenance of these valves. All formulas are **mathematically verifiable** standard engineering relations.

| Parameter | Formula / Criterion | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrostatic Test Pressure | $$P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design}$$ | bar | Per IACS UR Z17 |
| Allowable Wall Reduction | $$\% \, wear = \frac{t_{original} - t_{measured}}{t_{original}} \times 100$$ | % | Limit: typically **15–20 %** |
| Minimum Wall Thickness | $$t_{min} = t_{original} - (C_{corr} + C_{mech})$$ | mm | $C_{corr}$ = corrosion addition, $C_{mech}$ = mechanical allowance |
| Pitting Acceptance | $p_{depth} < 0.5 \, t_{min}$ (for isolated pits) | mm | Pits must be blended to 1:3 slope |
| Gland Torque (Empirical) | $$T_{gland} \approx 0.2 \times d_{spindle} \, (N \cdot m)$$ | N·m | For graphite packing |
| Seat Leakage Rate | $Q_{leak} < 0.01 \times Q_{rated}$ | m³/h | Tightness Class IV minimum |
| Flange Bolt Torque | $$T_{bolt} = \frac{k \times d^2 \times \sigma_y}{n}$$ | N·m | $k$ = nut factor (0.18–0.22), $d$ = bolt dia, $n$ = number of bolts |
| Spindle Stem Speed | $$N_{spindle} = \frac{Lead \, (mm)}{Handle \, radius \, (mm)}$$ | rev | Determines operating effort |
| Corrosion Rate (Steel in Seawater) | $0.1 - 0.5 \, mm/year$ | mm/yr | Depends on coating, cathodic protection |
| Cathodic Protection Current | $$I = i_c \times A_{wetted}$$ | A | $i_c$ = current density (10–30 mA/m² for steel) |
| Pressure Drop (Valve) | $$\Delta P = \zeta \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$ | Pa | $\zeta$ = loss coefficient, $\rho$ = density, $v$ = velocity |
| Stress in Spindle | $$\sigma = \frac{4F}{\pi d_{spindle}^2}$$ | MPa | $F$ = axial force on stem |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These valves are the **last line of defence against catastrophic flooding**. Their failure on a passenger ship can result in loss of life (per the **Herald of Free Enterprise** disaster, 1987, where bow-door failure — similar philosophy of hull integrity — caused 193 deaths). The **Costa Concordia** disaster (2012) also highlighted the need for proper hull penetration and overboard integrity. Survey and maintenance of these valves is therefore a **life-safety and pollution-prevention** activity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Hydrostatic Test Pressure Acceptance

The hydrostatic test pressure is derived from the design pressure multiplied by a **safety factor of 1.5** as per IACS and ASME standards.

$$
P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design}
$$

**Step 1:** Identify the design pressure rating of the valve from the nameplate.
- For a sea suction valve on a cooling line: $P_{design} = 10$ bar.

**Step 2:** Apply the safety factor.
- $P_{test} = 1.5 \times 10 = 15$ bar.

**Step 3:** Conduct the test for **30 minutes** holding pressure.
- The pressure must hold steady without any drop (other than the thermal compensation of ~1–2 %).

**Step 4:** Acceptance criteria.
- No visible leakage, no pressure drop > 2 % of test pressure.
- No permanent deformation of valve body (verified by re-measurement of dimensions post-test).

### 3.2 Derivation — Corrosion Allowance and Minimum Wall Thickness

The **minimum allowable wall thickness** of a valve body or sea suction pipe is calculated as:

$$
t_{min} = t_{original} - C_{corr} - C_{mech} - C_{manuf}
$$

Where:
- $t_{original}$ = original as-built thickness (mm)
- $C_{corr}$ = corrosion allowance, typically **2.0 mm** for sea water systems (higher than freshwater)
- $C_{mech}$ = mechanical allowance (for threading, bending) = **0.5 mm**
- $C_{manuf}$ = manufacturing tolerance = **0.5 mm**

**Example Walk-through:**

Given: A sea suction pipe of $t_{original} = 8.0$ mm with measured thickness $t_{measured} = 5.5$ mm.

**Step 1:** Calculate percentage wear.
$$
\% \, wear = \frac{8.0 - 5.5}{8.0} \times 100 = 31.25 \%
$$

**Step 2:** This exceeds the typical **20 % limit** for sea water service.

**Step 3:** Compute minimum acceptable thickness.
$$
t_{min} = 8.0 - 2.0 - 0.5 - 0.5 = 5.0 \, mm
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with measured.
$$
t_{measured} = 5.5 \, mm > t_{min} = 5.0 \, mm \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{ACCEPTABLE}
$$

**Step 5:** But the wear rate is high; the pipe should be **renewed at the next dry-dock** as a precautionary measure.

### 3.3 Derivation — Pressure Drop Across a Valve (Bernoulli-Based)

The pressure drop across an open valve in a sea suction line determines the **NPSH available** to the pump.

$$
\Delta P = \zeta \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}
$$

**Step 1:** Identify flow conditions.
- Sea water density: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³
- Velocity in pipe (DN 200, flow 200 m³/h): $v = \frac{200/3600}{(\pi/4)(0.2)^2} = 1.77$ m/s

**Step 2:** Loss coefficient for a fully open butterfly valve: $\zeta = 0.5$ (approximate).

**Step 3:** Compute pressure drop.
$$
\Delta P = 0.5 \times \frac{1025 \times (1.77)^2}{2} = 0.5 \times \frac{3211}{2} = 803 \, Pa \approx 0.008 \, bar
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to head loss.
$$
h_f = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \frac{803}{1025 \times 9.81} = 0.08 \, m
$$

This is the additional head loss introduced by the valve when fully open — a critical input to **NPSH calculation**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defects vs. Rectification

| Defect Type | Detection Method | Acceptance Limit | Rectification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pitting corrosion on body | Visual + UTM | Isolated pit < 0.5 × t_min | Grind out, blend 1:3 slope |
| Deep pitting (> 2 mm) | UTM grid scan | Not acceptable | Weld repair + NDT |
| Cracks in body casting | DPI / MPI | Any length | Weld repair or renewal |
| Spindle wear | Micrometer | < 0.2 mm | Re-machine or renew |
| Seat face erosion | Visual + leakage test | No visible leak at 1.1 × P_design | Re-lap or renew seat |
| Gland packing leakage | Visual | Zero leakage at design P | Re-pack with graphite/PTFE |
| Sea chest strainer clog | Pressure drop indicator | ΔP < 0.3 bar | Clean by diver or in dry-dock |
| Flange gasket leak | Visual | Zero visible leak | Renew gasket, re-torque bolts |
| Valve seizure | Operational test | Smooth operation < 30 s | Strip, clean, lubricate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sea Water Suction System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SEA CHEST] --> B[STRAINER GRID]
    B --> C[SEA SUCTION VALVE 1 - BUTTERFLY]
    B --> D[SEA SUCTION VALVE 2 - GATE]
    C --> E[COOLING PUMP SUCTION MANIFOLD]
    D --> F[BALLAST PUMP SUCTION MANIFOLD]
    C --> G[FIRE PUMP SUCTION MANIFOLD]
    E --> H[MAIN COOLING PUMP]
    F --> I[BALLAST PUMP]
    G --> J[FIRE PUMP]
    H --> K[FRESH WATER COOLER]
    H --> L[LUB OIL COOLER]
    K --> M[OVERBOARD DISCHARGE VALVE 1]
    L --> N[OVERBOARD DISCHARGE VALVE 2]
    I --> O[BALLAST TANK]
    O --> P[BALLAST OVERBOARD DISCHARGE VALVE]
    J --> Q[FIRE MAIN]
    Q --> R[FIRE OVERBOARD TEST VALVE]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Overboard Valve Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SANITARY TANK] --> B[SEWAGE TREATMENT PLANT]
    B --> C[OVERBOARD VALVE A - MARPOL ANNEX IV]
    B --> D[PORT DRAIN VALVE - LOCKED CLOSED]
    A --> E[DECK SCUPPER VALVES]
    F[BILGE WELL] --> G[BILGE PUMP]
    G --> H[OVERBOARD VALVE B - OWS COMPLIANT]
    G --> I[SLUDGE TANK OVERFLOW VALVE]
    J[COOLING WATER DISCHARGE] --> K[OVERBOARD VALVE C - FREE DISCHARGE]
    L[HULL SIDE PENETRATIONS] --> M[STORM VALVES - 4 NOS]
    M --> N[WEATHER DECK ACCESS]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Maintenance Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DAILY VISUAL INSPECTION] --> B[WEEKLY OPERATIONAL TEST]
    B --> C[MONTHLY STRAINER CHECK]
    C --> D[QUARTERLY LUBRICATION]
    D --> E[ANNUAL CLASS SURVEY]
    E --> F[BIANNUAL INTERNAL INSPECTION]
    F --> G[DRY DOCK OVERHAUL 5 YEARS]
    G --> H[HYDROSTATIC TEST 1.5xP]
    H --> I[CLASS RECERTIFICATION]
    I --> A
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Defect Rectification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DEFECT DETECTED] --> B{DEFECT TYPE}
    B -->|PITTING| C{PIT DEPTH}
    C -->|less than 0.5mm| D[GRIND AND BLEND]
    C -->|0.5 to 1mm| E[WELD REPAIR]
    C -->|greater than 1mm| F[RENEW COMPONENT]
    B -->|CRACK| G[DR MPI DYE PEN]
    G -->|CRACK FOUND| H[WELD OR RENEW]
    B -->|LEAKAGE| I{TEST PRESSURE}
    I -->|PASS| J[RE PACK GLAND]
    I -->|FAIL| K[OVERHAUL SEAT]
    B -->|SEIZURE| L[STRIP AND CLEAN]
    L --> M[RE LUBRICATE]
    M --> N[OPERATIONAL TEST]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State any three maintenance activities to be carried out on sea water suction valves during the annual survey of a ship.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Operational test — open and close the valve to confirm smooth operation within the specified time (typically < 30 seconds for hand-operated valves).
2. Visual and dimensional inspection — check for external leakage at the gland, body, and flange joints; measure spindle and body for wear.
3. Internal inspection of the sea chest strainer — clean marine growth and debris; check for corrosion of the strainer grid and surrounding shell plating.

*(1 mark per activity — 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a sea suction valve and a storm valve. List two locations where storm valves are fitted.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sea Suction Valve | Storm Valve |
|---|---|
| Used on the **suction side of pumps** for normal cooling/ballast duty. | Used on **side shell connections** above the freeboard deck for emergency closure. |
| Operated **frequently** (daily/weekly) in normal service. | Operated only in **heavy weather or emergency**. |
| Usually butterfly or gate type. | Heavy-duty, positive-closure type with remote operation from above freeboard deck. |

**Two locations of storm valves:**
1. On the **shell side air pipes** for bunker tanks, ballast tanks, and cofferdams (per SOLAS Reg. II-1/26).
2. On the **discharge lines** of emergency bilge suctions and emergency fire pumps that penetrate the shell below the freeboard deck.

*(2 marks for differentiation + 1 mark for locations)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Describe with the aid of a sketch the procedure for the complete overhaul of a sea water suction butterfly valve during a special survey in dry dock.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Explain the survey and certification requirements for overboard valves under the IACS framework, citing relevant UR (Unified Requirements).*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Overhaul Procedure of a Sea Water Suction Butterfly Valve**

1. **Isolation and Preparation (1 mark):**
   - Isolate the line by closing upstream and downstream valves; depressurise; drain the line.
   - Obtain permits: Permit to Work, Cold Work Permit, Confined Space Entry if entering the sea chest.

2. **Removal and Dismantling (1.5 marks):**
   - Remove the valve from the line by unbolting the flange joints.
   - Mark the orientation for re-installation.
   - Place the valve on a workshop bench; remove the gearbox cover and actuator.
   - Open the body — typically by removing the body bolts on a wafer-type butterfly valve.
   - Extract the disc, spindle, and seat rings.

3. **Inspection of Components (2 marks):**
   - **Disc:** Check for pitting, erosion, distortion; measure thickness; check sealing edge condition.
   - **Spindle:** Check for straightness (run-out < 0.05 mm), wear, and corrosion at the gland area.
   - **Seat rings:** Check for scoring, pitting, hardness loss; perform leakage test on the seat.
   - **Body:** Dye-penetrant or magnetic particle inspection for cracks; UTM for wall thickness.

4. **Rectification and Reassembly (1.5 marks):**
   - Re-lap the seat faces using grinding paste (180 to 600 grit).
   - Renew gland packing, O-rings, and gaskets.
   - Replace the disc if wear exceeds 10 % of original thickness.
   - Reassemble with new fasteners (studs and nuts) — Torque to $T_{bolt} = 0.2 \times d^2$ for steel studs.

5. **Pressure Testing and Re-installation (1 mark):**
   - Hydrostatic test at $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design} = 15$ bar for 30 minutes.
   - Re-install in the line, torque flange bolts in a star pattern, witness by Class Surveyor.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Survey and Certification of Overboard Valves (IACS Framework)**

1. **IACS UR Z17 — Procedural Requirements for Service Suppliers (1.5 marks):**
   - Defines the qualifications required for personnel performing survey of overboard valves.
   - Specifies the Underwater Inspection in Lieu of Dry-Docking (UWILD) requirements for divers.

2. **IACS UR Z23 — Continuous Survey of Sea Suction Valves (2 marks):**
   - Mandates that sea suction valves and sea chests be inspected at intervals not exceeding **5 years**.
   - Requires **diver inspection** between dry-dockings, with the interior of sea chests examined by TV camera or direct visual means.

3. **IACS UR Z7 / Z7.1 — Hull Surveys (1.5 marks):**
   - Annual survey: confirm all sea suction and overboard valves operate and are leak-free.
   - Special survey (every 5 years): complete internal examination; seat face inspection; pressure test.

4. **SOLAS and MARPOL Requirements (1.5 marks):**
   - **SOLAS Reg. II-1/26:** All sea inlets and discharges must be fitted with **positive means of indication at the position from which the valve is controlled**.
   - **MARPOL Annex IV:** Sanitary overboard valves must be **locked closed** in port and within special areas; records in the Sewage Treatment Plant logbook.
   - **MARPOL Annex V:** Garbage overboard discharge prohibited; certain types of overboard discharge (e.g., comminuted food waste) allowed only beyond specified distances.

5. **Class Certification (0.5 mark):**
   - On satisfactory survey, the Class Society issues a **Survey Status Report** and endorses the Continuous Survey Plan.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a screw-down non-return (SDNR) valve used on sea water suctions. Why is it preferred for emergency bilge suctions?*

**(b) [7 marks]** *A sea suction pipe has an original thickness of 10 mm. After 8 years of service, UTM gives an average remaining thickness of 7.0 mm with isolated pits of 2.0 mm depth. The corrosion allowance is 2.0 mm and mechanical allowance is 0.5 mm. Determine (i) percentage wear, (ii) effective thickness, and (iii) acceptability of the pipe with justification.*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Construction and Working of SDNR Valve (5 marks):**

An SDNR valve is a combination of a **globe valve (screw-down)** and a **non-return (check) valve** in a single body. It has:
- A **main valve disc** that can be lowered onto its seat by turning the handwheel (screw-down action).
- A **auxiliary non-return disc** that closes automatically under backflow pressure.
- A **central spindle** with internal threads engaged with a disc holder.

**Working:**
- In normal forward flow, the screw-down disc is raised, and the non-return disc is also lifted by flow.
- In reverse flow or pump shut-down, the non-return disc snaps shut, preventing backflow.
- For isolation, the screw-down disc is lowered onto its seat, providing positive shut-off.

**Why preferred for emergency bilge suction (2 marks):**
1. The **non-return function prevents flooding back into the bilge well** if the main sea inlet is breached — a critical safety feature.
2. The **screw-down function provides positive isolation** for maintenance, while the non-return disc maintains protection even with the valve open.
3. Required by **SOLAS Reg. II-1/35-1** for emergency bilge suction arrangements.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $t_{original} = 10$ mm
- $t_{measured} = 7.0$ mm (average)
- Pit depth = 2.0 mm
- $C_{corr} = 2.0$ mm
- $C_{mech} = 0.5$ mm

**(i) Percentage wear (2 marks):**
$$
\% \, wear = \frac{10.0 - 7.0}{10.0} \times 100 = 30 \%
$$

**(ii) Effective thickness (2 marks):**
Effective thickness considering deepest pit:
$$
t_{eff} = 7.0 - 2.0 = 5.0 \, mm
$$

Or using minimum thickness formula:
$$
t_{min} = 10.0 - 2.0 - 0.5 = 7.5 \, mm
$$

**(iii) Acceptability (3 marks):**
- The percentage wear of **30 %** exceeds the typical Class acceptance limit of **20 %** for sea water service.
- The effective thickness $t_{eff} = 5.0$ mm is **less than** the minimum acceptable $t_{min} = 7.5$ mm.
- The pit depth of 2.0 mm is significant (≥ 20 % of $t_{min}$) and isolated pitting criterion is $p < 0.5 \times t_{min} = 3.75$ mm — this is acceptable for isolated pitting, but the **general wastage is unacceptable**.

**Conclusion:** The pipe is **NOT acceptable** for continued service. It must be **renewed** at the next opportunity, or the affected section must be cropped and renewed. Class Surveyor will issue a **Recommendation / Condition of Class** for renewal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Do not confuse "sea suction valve" with "sea chest."** The sea chest is the hull recess; the valve is the isolation fitting on the pipe. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> - **In calculation questions, always show units.** Writing $t = 7.0$ without "mm" will cost 0.5 marks per occurrence.
> - **Do not forget the SOLAS / MARPOL citation.** In the 14-mark question, quoting the regulation (e.g., "per SOLAS Reg. II-1/26") is worth at least 1 mark and distinguishes a good answer from a great one.
> - **For SDNR valves, do not describe only a globe valve or only a non-return valve** — you must explain the **dual function** to score full marks.
> - **In defect assessment, state both the criterion AND the conclusion.** Simply calculating 30 % wear without stating "exceeds 20 % limit, hence renewal" loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] Sea water suction valves isolate the **pump suction** from the **sea chest** for maintenance and emergency.
- [ ] Overboard valves control **discharge** to the sea and are subject to **MARPOL** regulations.
- [ ] Main types of sea suction valves: **butterfly, gate, globe, SDNR, storm**.
- [ ] Overboard valves include **flap, scupper, screw-down discharge, and freeing port** types.
- [ ] Maintenance follows a **5-pillar framework**: routine, condition-based, annual overhaul, dry-dock, survey/certification.
- [ ] Hydrostatic test pressure = **1.5 × Design Pressure**, held for **30 minutes**.
- [ ] Maximum acceptable wastage: typically **20 %** of original wall thickness.
- [ ] Isolated pit acceptance: depth < **0.5 × t_min**, blended to 1:3 slope.
- [ ] **SOLAS Reg. II-1/26** mandates position indication for all hull penetration valves.
- [ ] **IACS UR Z23** governs continuous survey of sea suction valves and sea chests (5-year cycle).
- [ ] **MARPOL Annex IV** governs sanitary overboard discharge — **locked closed in port**.
- [ ] **MARPOL Annex V** governs bilge and garbage overboard discharge via Oily Water Separator.
- [ ] **SDNR valves** are mandatory on **emergency bilge suctions** per SOLAS Reg. II-1/35-1.
- [ ] **Storm valves** must be operable from **above the freeboard deck** in heavy weather.
- [ ] **Gland packing** is renewed during annual overhaul; common materials: **graphite, PTFE**.
- [ ] **Flange bolt torque** follows the **star pattern** to ensure even gasket loading.
- [ ] **Cathodic protection** with sacrificial zinc/aluminium anodes reduces sea water valve corrosion to **0.1 mm/yr** or less.
- [ ] **Sea chest strainer** cleaning is done **per voyage** or when **ΔP > 0.3 bar**.
- [ ] The **Herald of Free Enterprise (1987)** and **Costa Concordia (2012)** are landmark cases for hull penetration integrity.
- [ ] Always quote the **IACS UR** and **SOLAS Regulation** numbers in exam answers for high marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety During Repair - Various Operations Involving Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety During Repair - Various Operations Involving Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Safety During Ship Repair – Various Operations Involving Risk

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety During Repair (SDR)** is the systematic application of engineering controls, administrative procedures, and personal protective measures to eliminate or mitigate hazards arising during the docking, undocking, and overhaul phases of a vessel's operational life cycle. It encompasses identification, evaluation, and control of risks associated with **high-risk repair operations** such as hot work, enclosed space entry, work at height, electrical isolation, lifting operations, and tank cleaning.

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST861), this topic is mapped to **Course Outcome CO3** – *"Apply safety protocols and risk mitigation strategies during ship repair operations in compliance with international maritime conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, ISM Code) and classification society rules."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Human Surgery Principle**
> Think of a ship undergoing repair exactly like a patient undergoing major surgery. Just as a surgeon cannot operate without a sterile field, a qualified anaesthetist, proper instruments, and emergency backup, a ship repair yard cannot commence hot work, tank entry, or structural modifications without a **Permit-to-Work (PTW)**, gas testing, fire watch, and rescue arrangement. The "patient" (ship) is incapacitated, the "operating theatre" (dock/berth) is full of unknowns (residues, gases, structural weaknesses), and the "surgical team" (yard workers) must follow a strict checklist. Skipping any step can lead to **fatality, fire, explosion, or pollution** – just as skipping surgical protocol can kill a patient.

### 1.3 Standard Maritime Safety Metrics

The following **mandatory safety thresholds** govern all ship repair operations worldwide:

| Parameter | Critical Value | Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen (O₂) in enclosed space | **19.5% – 22%** | SOLAS Ch. XI-1, OSHA 29 CFR 1915.84 |
| Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) | **< 1% LEL** mandatory | IMO Resolution A.1050(27) |
| Hydrogen Sulphide (H₂S) | **< 10 ppm** (8-hr TWA) | ACGIH / IMO |
| Carbon Monoxide (CO) | **< 25 ppm** (8-hr TWA) | SOLAS / SOLAS Ch. II-2 |
| Benzene exposure | **< 1 ppm** (8-hr TWA) | MARPOL Annex II |
| Noise exposure limit | **< 85 dB(A)** (8-hr) | IMO Res. A.468(XII) |
| Scaffolding inspection | **Every 7 days** | ILO-152 / OSHA 1915.71 |
| Hot work fire watch retention | **Minimum 30 minutes** post-job | SOLAS / IACS UR Z17 |
| Rescue team stand-by | **Throughout confined space entry** | IMO Circ. 1083 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **International Safety Management (ISM) Code** and **IACS Unified Requirements (UR Z17 / Z23)** form the legal backbone. Any non-compliance can lead to detention, class withdrawal, criminal prosecution of the Master, and insurance voidance.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Risk Assessment Matrix (RAM) for Ship Repair

The cornerstone of repair safety is the **5 × 5 Risk Matrix** prescribed by **ISO 12100:2010** and adopted by IACS. Risk is a function of:

$$R = f(S, P)$$

where **S** = Severity of consequence, **P** = Probability of occurrence.

**Severity Categories (S):**
1. *Negligible* – Minor first aid, no lost time.
2. *Minor* – Medical treatment, restricted work.
3. *Moderate* – Lost time injury, serious illness.
4. *Major* – Severe injury, permanent disability, significant fire.
5. *Catastrophic* – Fatality, vessel loss, major explosion.

**Probability Categories (P):**
1. *Rare* – Almost never occurs in industry.
2. *Unlikely* – Could occur in a career.
3. *Possible* – Could occur in a project.
4. *Likely* – Will probably occur.
5. *Almost Certain* – Expected to occur, possibly multiple times.

**Risk Score:** $R = S \times P$ (range 1 – 25)

| Risk Level | Score | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| Low | 1 – 4 | Manage by routine procedures. |
| Medium | 5 – 9 | Specific monitoring, supervisor approval. |
| High | 10 – 15 | Senior management approval, controls mandatory. |
| Very High | 16 – 25 | **STOP WORK**. Operation prohibited. |

### 2.2 The Permit-to-Work (PTW) Hierarchy

A PTW is a **formal written authority** to perform a job that has been assessed as having significant risk. It is **not a clearance for the job itself** – it is a **control document** that authorises work only when all preconditions are met.

The standard PTW types in ship repair are:

1. **Hot Work Permit** – Welding, burning, grinding, soldering.
2. **Cold Work Permit** – Non-spark producing activities.
3. **Enclosed Space Entry Permit** – Tanks, cofferdams, double bottoms, duct keels.
4. **Electrical Isolation Permit (LOTO)** – Lockout / Tagout / Try-out.
5. **Working at Height Permit** – Scaffolding, staging, mast work.
6. **Over-the-Side Permit** – Painting, hull cleaning, lifeboat drills at berth.
7. **Lifting Operations Permit** – Crane lifts > 1 tonne or critical lifts.
8. **Radiography Permit** – NDT using X-ray or gamma sources.
9. **Hazardous Substance Permit** – Asbestos, lead, PFAS, isocyanates.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are examination-grade and frequently tested in ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Application in Repair |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = S \times P$ | Risk Score | Risk assessment, RAM scoring |
| 2 | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{22.4 \times \text{Vol\%}}{M} \times 10^6$ | Gas concentration conversion (M = molar mass g/mol) | Gas testing interpretation |
| 3 | $\text{O}_2 \text{ required} = \dfrac{21\% \times V_{tank}}{100}$ | Oxygen volume needed for human entry | Ventilation calculation |
| 4 | $Q_{vent} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{t_{purge}}$ | Required purge flow rate (m³/min) | Tank purging before entry |
| 5 | $\text{SWL} = \dfrac{UTS \times A_{net}}{n_{sf}}$ | Safe Working Load of sling/shackle | Crane lift planning |
| 6 | $\theta_{crit} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{v^2}{g \cdot r}\right)$ | Crane slewing critical angle (swing) | Lift dynamics |
| 7 | $\text{FOS} = \dfrac{\text{Ultimate Load}}{\text{Working Load}}$ | Factor of Safety (typically 4:1 for slings) | Lifting gear certification |
| 8 | $\text{NPD}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level (P₀ = 20 µPa) | Noise mapping, hearing protection |
| 9 | $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \times P_{atm}$ | Partial pressure of O₂ at sea level | Confined space ventilation |
| 10 | $\text{LEL}_{margin} = \dfrac{\text{Actual}}{\text{LEL}} \times 100\%$ | % of Lower Explosive Limit – must be **< 1%** | Hot work gas test threshold |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This risk framework is not academic – it is enforced in:
- **Classification Societies** (LR, DNV, BV, ABS, ClassNK) during repair surveys.
- **Port State Control (PSC)** under Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, USCG.
- **P&I Club** claims handling – uninsured losses can exceed **USD 50 million** for a single repair fire.
- **MARPOL Annex VI** – Air pollution from repair coatings (VOC).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Risk Mapping for Each High-Risk Operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner typically awards **3 marks for correct hazard identification** and **4 marks for control measures**. A 7-mark sub-part on a high-risk operation requires **at least 4 distinct hazards** and **their specific controls**.

### 3.2 Hot Work Operations (Welding, Cutting, Grinding)

**Definition:** Hot work is any operation that produces sparks, heat, flame, or molten metal capable of initiating combustion or explosion.

**Step-by-Step Risk Analysis:**

**Step 1 – Hazard Identification**
- *Ignition source* in fuel-rich atmosphere (cargo residues, paint, oil).
- *Slag / sparks* falling into unprotected openings (falling up to **10 m vertically**).
- *Toxic fume generation* (Zn, Cd, Be, Cr, Ni from coatings and plating).
- *Burns* from UV radiation (arc eye / photokeratitis).
- *Electric shock* from welding equipment (secondary voltage **60–100 V** open circuit, lethal if wet).
- *Backfire / flashback* in gas welding.

**Step 2 – Pre-Work Controls**
1. **Issue Hot Work Permit** – valid for maximum **8 hours / one shift**.
2. **Gas Test** – within **30 minutes** of work, at multiple levels (top, middle, bottom).
3. **Remove combustibles** within **11 m (35 ft)** horizontally and **5 m** vertically.
4. **Cover openings** with fire-resistant blankets.
5. **Isolate adjacent pipelines** carrying fuel oil, lube oil, or gas.
6. **Position fire watch** with extinguisher (CO₂ / dry powder, **9 kg minimum**).
7. **Establish fire watch retention** of **minimum 30 minutes** after job completion.
8. **Provide fire hoses** charged and ready.

**Step 3 – During-Work Controls**
- Continuous gas monitoring at **30-minute intervals**.
- Communication protocol (radio / whistle).
- PPE: Welding helmet (shade **9–13**), leather gloves, fire-retardant clothing, safety boots.

**Step 4 – Post-Work Controls**
- Fire watch retained for **30 min – 4 hours** depending on adjacent fire load.
- Final walkthrough by Safety Officer.
- Permit closure, sign-off, archival (minimum **3 years** for ISM).

**Derivation – Safe Slag Drop Distance:**

The terminal velocity of a falling spark / slag particle is given by:

$$
v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 m g}{\rho_{air} C_d A}}
$$

where **m** = particle mass, **C_d** ≈ 0.5 (spherical), **A** = cross-sectional area, **ρ_air** = 1.225 kg/m³.

For a typical **0.5 g** slag particle of diameter **2 mm**:

$$
v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.0005 \times 9.81}{1.225 \times 0.5 \times \pi (0.001)^2}}
$$

Solving the numerator: $2 \times 0.0005 \times 9.81 = 0.00981$

Solving the denominator: $1.225 \times 0.5 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-6} = 1.924 \times 10^{-6}$

$$
v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.00981}{1.924 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{5098.75} \approx 71.4 \text{ m/s}
$$

This confirms that sparks can travel **laterally 10–11 m** with residual temperature > **1000 °C**, justifying the **11 m exclusion zone** under SOLAS and IACS.

### 3.3 Enclosed Space Entry (Tanks, Cofferdams, Duct Keels)

**Definition:** An enclosed space is any space with limited openings, not designed for continuous occupancy, where dangerous atmospheres may accumulate.

**Step-by-Step Risk Analysis:**

**Step 1 – Recognise the "Toxic Trio"**
- **Oxygen deficiency** (< 19.5%) – asphyxiation.
- **Flammable gas** (> 1% LEL) – explosion.
- **Toxic gas** (H₂S, CO, benzene, phosgene) – poisoning.

**Step 2 – Pre-Entry Procedure (SOLAS Reg. XI-1/7)**
1. **Risk Assessment** by competent person.
2. **Issue Enclosed Space Entry Permit** valid **max 8 hours**.
3. **Initial gas test** by trained person using **calibrated 4-gas detector** (O₂, LEL, CO, H₂S).
4. **Ventilation** – mechanical, **20 air changes per hour minimum**.
5. **Establish rescue plan** – tripod + winch, SCBA, rescue team of **minimum 2 persons** outside.
6. **Communication** – radio or hardwired talk-back.
7. **Log entry/exit** – logbook at the entrance.

**Step 3 – Atmosphere Testing Calculation**

For a tank of volume **V = 500 m³**, the oxygen volume at atmospheric conditions is:

$$
V_{O_2} = 0.21 \times 500 = 105 \text{ m}^3
$$

If the O₂ reading drops to **18%** (i.e., **3% below safe**), the oxygen deficit is:

$$
V_{deficit} = 0.03 \times 500 = 15 \text{ m}^3
$$

The required purge volume to restore 20.9% (assuming 100% displacement by fresh air) is:

$$
V_{purge} = 15 \div 0.209 \approx 71.8 \text{ m}^3
$$

The time required at a fan capacity of **Q = 50 m³/min**:

$$
t = \dfrac{71.8}{50} = 1.44 \text{ min (theoretical)}
$$

In practice, **3 to 5 exchanges** are required to ensure homogeneity, hence:

$$
t_{practical} = \dfrac{4 \times 500}{50} = 40 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 – PPE and Rescue**
- SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus) – **30 min air supply**.
- Lifeline of **minimum 6 m clearance**.
- Rescue tripod with mechanical advantage winch.
- Hospital alert + ambulance on standby.

> [!WARNING]
> **Rescue must NEVER be attempted without proper PPE.** Statistics show that **> 60% of confined-space fatalities are would-be rescuers** entering without protection. The KTU examiner will deduct marks if "rescue team wears SCBA" is not stated.

### 3.4 Work at Height (Scaffolding, Staging, Rope Access)

**Definition:** Work at height is any work where the worker can fall a distance greater than **2 m (6 ft)** – the threshold under OSHA 29 CFR 1915.72 and IMO Resolution A.1050(27).

**Step-by-Step Risk Analysis:**

**Step 1 – Hierarchy of Fall Protection**
1. **Eliminate** – do the job at ground level where possible (e.g., pre-fabrication).
2. **Prevent** – guardrails, edge protection, hole covers.
3. **Mitigate** – safety nets, personal fall arrest systems (PFAS).
4. **Administrative** – training, supervision, exclusion zones.

**Step 2 – Scaffolding Design**

For a tube-and-coupler scaffold of height **H** and bay length **L**, the base plate load on a standard 4-leg scaffold carrying a uniformly distributed load of **w (kg/m²)** is:

$$
P_{leg} = \dfrac{w \times A_{tributary}}{n_{legs}} = \dfrac{w \times (L_1 \times L_2)}{4}
$$

For **w = 1.5 kN/m²**, **L₁ = 2 m**, **L₂ = 2 m**:

$$
P_{leg} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 4}{4} = 1.5 \text{ kN per leg}
$$

The factor of safety against tube buckling (BS 5973) requires:

$$
\text{FOS} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{P_{leg}} \geq 2.0
$$

For a 48.3 mm OD × 4 mm wall tube (i ≈ 1.24 cm), effective length **L_e = 2 m**:

$$
P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 2.1 \times 10^{11} \times 1.24 \times 10^{-8}}{2^2} \approx 6.42 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\text{FOS} = \dfrac{6.42}{1.5} \approx 4.28 \;\;(> 2.0 \text{ ✓ Safe})
$$

**Step 3 – Inspection Regime**
- **Before use** – every shift.
- **Weekly** – formal inspection by competent person (tag system: **green = safe, red = do not use**).
- **After modification, weather event, or impact**.

### 3.5 Electrical Work and LOTO

**Definition:** Lockout / Tagout / Try-out is a procedure to ensure that hazardous energy sources are isolated and rendered inoperable before maintenance.

**Step-by-Step Procedure (6 Steps):**
1. **Identify** all energy sources (mains, generators, batteries, hydraulic accumulators, pneumatic reservoirs).
2. **Notify** affected personnel.
3. **Isolate** the energy source (open breaker, close valve).
4. **Lockout** – apply personal padlock and danger tag.
5. **Try-out** – operate controls to confirm zero energy; test with multimeter.
6. **Verify** – secondary test before commencing work.

**Reverse Procedure** for re-energisation: Remove tools, remove locks, notify personnel, re-energise, confirm operation.

### 3.6 Lifting Operations and Crane Safety

**Step-by-Step Risk Analysis:**

**Step 1 – Lift Plan Categories**
- *Standard lift* – routine, low risk.
- *Critical lift* – > 75% SWL, personnel transfer, tandem, or near sensitive areas.
- *Heavy lift* – typically > 100 t.

**Step 2 – Sling Tension Calculation**

For a 2-leg sling lifting a load **W** at an angle **θ** from vertical, the tension in each leg is:

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{2 \cos \theta}
$$

For **W = 10 t**, **θ = 30°** (measured from horizontal):

$$
T = \dfrac{10}{2 \cos 30°} = \dfrac{10}{2 \times 0.866} = 5.77 \text{ t per leg}
$$

The sling is stressed, so the **larger the angle from vertical, the higher the tension**. Vertical lifts are most efficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** Never use a sling at an angle less than **30° from horizontal** (60° from vertical) – the load multiplier grows dramatically and the sling may fail.

**Step 3 – SWL Verification**

The Safe Working Load of a 2-leg wire rope sling of diameter **d** (mm) is approximately:

$$
\text{SWL} \approx 0.4 \times d^2 \;\;(\text{kg, vertical hitch})
$$

For **d = 20 mm**:

$$
\text{SWL} \approx 0.4 \times 400 = 160 \text{ kg}
$$

In the above 10 t lift at 30° from horizontal, the **required sling diameter** would be:

$$
d = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{0.4}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{5770}{0.4}} = \sqrt{14425} \approx 120 \text{ mm}
$$

This shows that a **120 mm diameter wire rope sling** is needed for a 10 t lift – well beyond standard 20 mm rigging.

### 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix – High-Risk Operations

| Operation | Primary Hazard | Critical Control | Permit Required | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hot Work | Fire / Explosion | Gas test + Fire watch | Yes | High (frequent in ESE) |
| Enclosed Space | Asphyxiation | SCBA + Rescue tripod | Yes | Very High |
| Work at Height | Fall from height | Harness + Guardrail | Yes | High |
| Electrical | Electrocution | LOTO + Try-out | Yes | Medium |
| Lifting | Falling load | Lift Plan + SWL check | Yes (>1 t) | Very High |
| Tank Cleaning | Toxic exposure | PPE + Ventilation | Yes | High |
| Grit Blasting | Silicosis | Supplied-air hood | Yes | Medium |
| Asbestos Removal | Mesothelioma | Class H enclosure | Yes | Medium |
| Radiography (NDT) | Radiation | Distance + Shielding | Yes | Low-Medium |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Risk Management Flow for Repair Operations

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end risk governance architecture** used in modern ship repair yards, mapped to IMO Resolution A.1050(27) and ISM Code Section 7.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Arrives at Repair Yard] --> B[HSE Induction of All Personnel]
    B --> C[Job Scope Definition by Technical Dept]
    C --> D[Job Hazard Analysis JHA Walkdown]
    D --> E{Is Risk Score R = S x P greater than 9?}
    E -- No --> F[Standard Operating Procedure SOP]
    E -- Yes --> G[Permit to Work PTW Required]
    G --> H[Pre Work Gas Test by Competent Person]
    H --> I[Atmosphere Safe]
    I -- No --> J[STOP WORK - Continue Ventilation]
    J --> H
    I -- Yes --> K[Issue Valid PTW max 8 hours]
    K --> L[Implement Control Measures]
    L --> M[Continuous Monitoring every 30 min]
    M --> N[Job Execution with PPE]
    N --> O{Incident or Near Miss?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Stop Work - Investigate - Corrective Action]
    P --> K
    O -- No --> Q[Job Completion Sign Off]
    Q --> R[Post Work Fire Watch 30 min to 4 hours]
    R --> S[PTW Closure and Archival 3 years]
    S --> T[HSE Audit and Lessons Learned]
```

## 4.2 Permit-to-Work Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Planned Repair Activity]) --> Q1{Is it Hot Work?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> HW[Hot Work Permit]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Confined Space Entry?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CS[Enclosed Space Entry Permit]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Height above 2 m?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> WH[Working at Height Permit]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Electrical Isolation?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> LOTO[LOTO Permit]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Critical Lift above 1 t?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> LF[Lifting Operations Permit]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Hazardous Substance?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> HS[Hazardous Substance Permit]
    Q6 -- No --> CW[Cold Work Permit Standard]
    HW --> GAS[Gas Test within 30 min]
    CS --> GAS
    GAS --> Execute[Execute Job]
    LF --> LiftPlan[Detailed Lift Plan]
    LiftPlan --> Execute
    Execute --> Close[Permit Closure and Audit]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram – Confined Space Rescue Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Entrant inside Tank] -- Radio + Lifeline --> B[Attendant at Entry Point]
    B -- Communication --> C[Rescue Team Standby]
    C -- Equipped with --> D[SCBA 30 min]
    C -- Equipped with --> E[Tripod and Winch]
    C -- Equipped with --> F[First Aid and AED]
    C -- Coordinated with --> G[Emergency Services 108]
    B -- Records --> H[Entry Logbook]
    C -- Reports --> I[Site Safety Officer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Attendant must NEVER enter the space**. Their role is to maintain communication, monitor the entrant, summon rescue, and operate retrieval equipment. This is a frequent **1-mark trap question** in KTU papers.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A – 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. Define "Permit-to-Work" in the context of ship repair. List any four types of PTW.

**Model Answer:**
> A **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** is a formal written authorisation document issued by a competent person after verifying that all necessary precautions have been taken, authorising specific work to be carried out safely within a defined timeframe.
>
> **Four types of PTW:**
> 1. Hot Work Permit
> 2. Enclosed Space Entry Permit
> 3. Working at Height Permit
> 4. Lifting Operations Permit / Electrical LOTO Permit
>
> *(Accept any 4 of the 9 listed in Section 2.2.)*

---

### Q2. What is the significance of measuring LEL before issuing a Hot Work Permit?

**Model Answer:**
> LEL stands for **Lower Explosive Limit** – the minimum concentration of a flammable gas/vapour in air below which the mixture is too lean to ignite.
>
> **Significance:**
> - Hot work (welding/cutting) creates ignition sources of temperature > 1000 °C.
> - If the atmosphere contains flammable gas above **1% of its LEL**, an ignition can cause deflagration or detonation.
> - SOLAS and IACS UR Z17 mandate that the **measured LEL must be < 1%** before issuing the Hot Work Permit.
> - The gas test is performed by a **competent, calibrated 4-gas detector** within **30 minutes** of work and at multiple elevations (top, middle, bottom of the space).

---

## Part B – 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the safety precautions to be observed during **Hot Work Operations** in a tanker undergoing repair at a shipyard. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution – 7 marks:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Hot work on a tanker is the most high-risk repair activity because tankers carry petroleum products, and any residual flammable atmosphere can cause catastrophic explosion. Compliance with **SOLAS Ch. II-2, IACS UR Z17, and the OCIMF/ICS Hot Work Guide** is mandatory.

**Pre-Work Precautions (3 marks):**
1. **Hot Work Permit** issued by competent person; valid for one shift (max 8 hours).
2. **Gas Testing:** Atmosphere tested at multiple levels – O₂ between 19.5–22%, LEL < 1%, toxic gases below TLV, performed within 30 minutes of work.
3. **Isolation:** Adjacent pipelines carrying fuel oil, cargo, or gas blanked and blinded.
4. **Removal of combustibles** within 11 m horizontally and 5 m vertically.
5. **Coverage** of all openings, vents, and scuppers with fire-resistant blankets.
6. **Fire watch** posted at each level with extinguisher (CO₂ 9 kg or dry powder) and charged fire hose.
7. **Equipment check** – welding machine earthing, gas cylinder securing, flashback arrestors on oxy-fuel torches.

**During-Work Precautions (2 marks):**
- Continuous gas monitoring every 30 minutes.
- Spark shields and fire blankets.
- Communication via radio or whistle.
- PPE: Welding helmet (shade 9–13), fire-retardant coveralls, leather gloves, safety boots, respiratory protection if coatings contain Cr/Zn.

**Post-Work Precautions (1 mark):**
- Fire watch retained for **30 min to 4 hours** depending on fire load.
- Final walkthrough, permit closure, archive for 3 years.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **Enclosed Space Entry Procedure** as per SOLAS Reg. XI-1/7, with reference to the atmospheric testing requirements and rescue arrangements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution – 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** SOLAS Reg. XI-1/7 defines an enclosed space as one with limited openings, not designed for continuous worker occupancy, where toxic or flammable atmospheres may accumulate. Examples: cargo tanks, cofferdams, duct keels, chain lockers, sewage tanks.

**Atmospheric Testing (3 marks):**
- **4-Gas Detector** calibrated, used by trained person.
- **Oxygen:** 19.5% – 22% (deficiency = asphyxiation; excess = fire risk).
- **LEL:** < 1% (to prevent explosion).
- **Toxic Gases:** H₂S < 10 ppm, CO < 25 ppm, benzene < 1 ppm.
- **Sequence:** Test top, middle, bottom; heavier gases settle at bottom.
- **Re-test every 30 minutes** during work.

**Pre-Entry Procedure (1 mark):** Issue Entry Permit, conduct JHA, ensure mechanical ventilation (≥ 20 air changes/hour), establish communication, log entry/exit.

**Rescue Arrangements (2 marks):**
- **Attendant** stationed at the entry point (must NOT enter the space).
- **Rescue team** of at least 2 persons on standby with:
  - SCBA (30 min air supply).
  - Tripod + winch with retrieval line.
  - First aid kit, AED.
  - Hospital/ambulance pre-alerted.
- Rescue team wears full PPE and uses the buddy system.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Describe the **hierarchy of fall protection** to be applied for work at height during ship repair. With a neat sketch, explain the design and inspection of a typical **scaffolding system**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution – 7 marks:**

**Hierarchy of Fall Protection (3 marks):** In descending order of preference:
1. **Eliminate** – perform work at ground level (e.g., prefabricate, then lift).
2. **Prevent** – guardrails (top rail 1.0–1.2 m, mid-rail 0.5 m, toe board 0.1 m), hole covers, edge protection.
3. **Mitigate** – safety nets, personal fall arrest systems (PFAS: anchor + harness + lanyard + connector; arrest force ≤ 6 kN).
4. **Administrative** – training, supervision, exclusion zones, signage.

**Scaffolding Design (3 marks):**
- Tube-and-coupler or system scaffold (e.g., Cuplok, Ringlock).
- Standards, ledgers, transoms, base plates on sole boards.
- Ties to the vessel at intervals; bracing for lateral stability.
- Working platforms with full planking; guardrails on all open sides.

**Inspection (1 mark):**
- Pre-shift visual check by user.
- Weekly formal inspection by competent person (tag system: green/red).
- After modification, weather, or impact.

---

**Q. (b)** With the help of a numerical example, explain how the **Safe Working Load (SWL) of a 2-leg sling** is calculated for a lifting operation. Mention the precautions to be taken before and during a critical lift. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution – 7 marks:**

**Theory (2 marks):** Sling tension in a 2-leg sling at sling angle θ from vertical is:

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{2 \cos \theta}
$$

For a **10 t load at θ = 30° from vertical (i.e., 60° from horizontal):**

$$
T = \dfrac{10}{2 \cos 30°} = \dfrac{10}{1.732} = 5.77 \text{ t per leg}
$$

Required sling diameter (using empirical rule SWL = 0.4 d² in kg):

$$
d = \sqrt{\dfrac{5770}{0.4}} \approx 120 \text{ mm diameter wire rope}
$$

**Pre-Lift Precautions (3 marks):**
1. **Lift Plan** prepared by competent rigger and approved by engineer.
2. **SWL verification** of crane, slings, shackles (each must be certified with valid test certificate).
3. **Ground stability** – outrigger pads on firm ground; crane on level surface.
4. **Exclusion zone** – radius = 1.5 × boom radius.
5. **Weather limits** – wind < 35 km/hr; no lifting in lightning/fog.
6. **Tag lines** to control swing; banksman appointed.

**During-Lift Precautions (2 marks):**
- Test lift to 100–200 mm, hold for 30 seconds, check stability.
- Continuous communication between operator and banksman.
- No personnel under suspended load.
- Slow, controlled motion; no sudden slewing/lifting.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. Writing "gas test" without specifying **< 1% LEL** – loses 1 mark.
> 2. Saying "rescue team enters to help" – **deduct 1 mark**; attendant must NOT enter.
> 3. Confusing **LFL (Lower Flammable Limit)** with **LEL (Lower Explosive Limit)** – both are correct but conventionally LEL is used.
> 4. Forgetting the **30-minute retention** of fire watch – very common omission.
> 5. In sling calculations, using θ from horizontal instead of vertical – formula becomes wrong, full marks lost.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- [x] **Permit-to-Work** is mandatory for 9 categories of work.
- [x] **Hot Work Permit** – gas test < 1% LEL, 11 m exclusion, 30-min fire watch retention.
- [x] **Enclosed Space** – O₂ 19.5–22%, LEL < 1%, SCBA mandatory, attendant outside, tripod winch rescue.
- [x] **Work at Height** – threshold 2 m; hierarchy: Eliminate → Prevent → Mitigate → Administer.
- [x] **Scaffolding** – inspection every 7 days; tag system green/red; FOS ≥ 2.0 on tube buckling.
- [x] **LOTO** – 6-step procedure: Identify → Notify → Isolate → Lockout → Try-out → Verify.
- [x] **Lift Plan** – required for any lift > 1 t or > 75% SWL; Sling tension T = W / (2 cos θ).
- [x] **Risk Matrix** – R = S × P; scores 1–25; > 15 = stop work.
- [x] **Fire watch** – must be **dedicated, trained, equipped**, and have **clear escape route**.
- [x] **Gas testing sequence** – Top, Middle, Bottom (heavier gases settle at bottom).
- [x] **PPE Selection** – task-specific; e.g., welding helmet shade 9–13, SCBA for confined space, Class H hood for asbestos.
- [x] **Rescue team composition** – minimum 2 persons; **never** enter without SCBA.
- [x] **Hot work fire watch extinguisher** – minimum **9 kg CO₂ or dry powder**.
- [x] **ISM Code Section 7** – mandates SMS for all repair activities.
- [x] **PTW archival** – minimum 3 years (ISM audit requirement).
- [x] **Lifting angle** – sling angle < 30° from horizontal is **prohibited**.
- [x] **Sound pressure level** – > 85 dB(A) requires hearing protection (IMO Res. A.468(XII)).
- [x] **Toxic exposure** – benzene < 1 ppm TWA (MARPOL Annex II).
- [x] **Asbestos** – Class H enclosure, supplied air, certified removal contractor.
- [x] **Stop Work Authority (SWA)** – every worker has the legal right to refuse unsafe work.

**Mnemonic for PTW Types:** "**H-E-W-E-L-H-R**" – **H**ot, **E**nclosed, **W**ork at height, **E**lectrical (LOTO), **L**ifting, **H**azardous substance, **R**adiography.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 6. Additional Engineering Context for KTU Board Preparation

## 6.1 International Regulatory Framework – The "Big Five"

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to know the hierarchy of regulations that govern ship repair safety:

1. **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)** – IMO Convention, Chapter II-2 (Fire Safety) and Chapter XI-1/7 (Enclosed Space Entry).
2. **MARPOL** – Marine Pollution, especially Annex I (oil) and Annex VI (air/VOC from coatings).
3. **ISM Code** – International Safety Management Code (mandatory SMS for shipyards servicing SOLAS vessels).
4. **MLC 2006** – Maritime Labour Convention, including crew repatriation and occupational safety during repair.
5. **Class Rules** – IACS Unified Requirements (UR Z17 – Procedures for Repair), Lloyd's Register, DNV, BV, ABS rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **A frequent KTU question (2–3 marks):** *"Which IACS Unified Requirement governs ship repair procedures?"* — Answer: **UR Z17**.

## 6.2 Common High-Risk Operations – Examiner's Favourite List

| Operation | Frequency in KTU Papers | Examiner's "Trap" |
|---|---|---|
| Hot work on tankers | Very High | Forgetting 1% LEL threshold |
| Enclosed space entry | Very High | "Attendant enters to help" |
