| Scaffolding design | High | Forgetting weekly inspection |
| Crane lift | High | Sling angle confusion |
| Electrical LOTO | Medium | Missing the "try-out" step |
| Tank cleaning | Medium | Toxic gas identification |
| Asbestos work | Low-Medium | Class H enclosure requirement |

## 6.3 KTU 2024 Scheme – Mark Distribution Pattern

| Question Type | Marks | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | Time Allocation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Part A Q1 (Definition) | 3 | Remember / Understand | 7 min |
| Part A Q2 (Significance) | 3 | Understand / Apply | 7 min |
| Part B Q(a) | 7 | Apply / Analyse | 20 min |
| Part B Q(b) | 7 | Analyse / Evaluate | 20 min |
| **Total** | **20** | — | **~55 min** |

## 6.4 Sample Self-Test – Quick Recall Questions

1. What is the **minimum oxygen percentage** for safe entry into an enclosed space? *(Answer: 19.5%)*
2. What is the **maximum sling angle from horizontal**? *(Answer: 60°, i.e., not less than 30° from horizontal)*
3. What is the **fire watch retention period** after hot work on a tanker? *(Answer: 30 min to 4 hours depending on fire load)*
4. Which **SOLAS regulation** governs enclosed space entry? *(Answer: SOLAS Reg. XI-1/7)*
5. What is the **noise exposure threshold** for mandatory hearing protection? *(Answer: 85 dB(A) TWA)*
6. **Define LEL.** *(Answer: Minimum concentration of a flammable gas in air below which propagation of a flame does not occur on contact with a source of ignition.)*
7. What **4 gases** does a typical 4-gas detector measure? *(Answer: O₂, LEL, CO, H₂S)*
8. What is the **factor of safety** for steel wire rope slings? *(Answer: Minimum 4:1 for general lifting; 5:1 for personnel lifting)*
9. How frequently must **scaffolding** be inspected? *(Answer: Weekly by competent person, plus pre-shift by user)*
10. What is the **first action** on discovering a missing entrant from a confined space? *(Answer: Raise alarm; do NOT enter; summon rescue team with SCBA)*

## 6.5 KTU Examiner's Final Tips

> [!TIP]
> **Top 5 Strategies to Score 14/14 in Part B:**
> 1. **Use bullet points** – examiners reward clarity.
> 2. **Quote regulations** – SOLAS Chapter, IACS UR number, IACS Z17.
> 3. **Include numerical examples** where possible (sling angle, O₂ calculation).
> 4. **End with a "compliance" sentence** – e.g., "This procedure complies with SOLAS Reg. XI-1/7 and ISM Code Sec. 7."
> 5. **Draw simple diagrams** – even a sketch of a scaffold with guardrail dimensions can fetch 1 extra mark.

---

topics.title: Safety Devices and Plans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMAND AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety Devices and Plans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Devices and Plans** refer to the comprehensive suite of life-saving, fire-fighting, navigational, communication, and pollution-control equipment, together with the mandatory onboard documentation (plans, lists, and procedures), that are required on a merchant ship to ensure the safety of life at sea (SOLAS), protection of the marine environment (MARPOL), and compliance with the International Safety Management (ISM) Code, as enforced under the flag state administration and verified during statutory and class surveys.

In the language of classification societies and the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, *safety devices* are the hardware items — from a 6 kg portable dry-powder extinguisher to a 100-person totally enclosed lifeboat — whose presence, condition, and certification are physically verified during the **Annual Safety Equipment Certificate (SEC) survey** and the **Cargo Ship Safety Equipment Certificate (CSEC)** renewal survey.

*Safety plans* are the paper/electronic documents that communicate, at a glance, **where** the devices are located, **how** to use them, **who** is responsible, and **what** to do in an emergency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The High-Rise Building**
> Think of a ship as a 20-storey floating high-rise. The "Safety Devices" are the same items you would find in any premium office tower: fire extinguishers on every floor, sprinkler risers in the ceiling, life jackets under each seat, evacuation chairs, a public-address system, and a master fire-control panel in the lobby. The "Safety Plans" are the laminated diagrams glued inside the lift and the Fire Action notices pinned to every door.
> The only difference? On a ship, the building is **moving**, **rocking**, **cut off from rescue services**, and the staircase (the escape route) is replaced by a survival craft that is *lowered to the water from a height of 20+ metres*. That single fact is why the SOLAS rules are so strict — and why the **Annual Safety Equipment Survey** is one of the most rigorous of all statutory surveys.

## 1.3 The Regulatory Framework — Why These Devices Exist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The need for these devices was crystallised by three maritime catastrophes:
> 1. **RMS Titanic (1912)** → SOLAS Convention (1914, revised 1974, 2024 amendments).
> 2. **MV Torrey Canyon (1967)** → MARPOL Convention (1973/78).
> 3. **Herald of Free Enterprise (1987)** → ISM Code (1993) and the operational requirement for **Damage Control Plans**.

The current regulatory stack that a ship surveyor must verify is:

| Layer | Instrument | Survey Cert. Issued |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | SOLAS Ch. II-1, II-2 | Cargo Ship Safety Construction Certificate (CSSC) |
| **Equipment** | SOLAS Ch. III, LSA Code | Cargo Ship Safety Equipment Certificate (CSEC) |
| **Radio** | SOLAS Ch. IV, GMDSS | Cargo Ship Safety Radio Certificate (CSRC) |
| **Pollution** | MARPOL Annex I-VI | International Oil Pollution Prevention (IOPP) Cert. |
| **Operations** | ISM Code, STCW | Document of Compliance (DOC) / SMC |
| **Tonnage** | ITC 1969 | International Tonnage Certificate |

## 1.4 Categories of Safety Devices — A Bird's-Eye View

Safety devices onboard a merchant ship fall into **five** functional families, all of which must be examined and certified under **CSEC/Annual SEC survey**:

1. **Life-Saving Appliances (LSA)** — regulated by **SOLAS Chapter III** and the **LSA Code (Resolution MSC.48(66))**.
2. **Fire-Fighting Appliances (FFA)** — regulated by **SOLAS Chapter II-2** and the **FSS Code (MSC.45(65))**.
3. **Navigational Safety Devices** — regulated by **SOLAS Chapter V** and **COLREGS**.
4. **Radio & Communication Devices (GMDSS)** — regulated by **SOLAS Chapter IV**.
5. **Pollution Prevention Devices (SOPEP, ODME, OWS, Sewage Plant)** — regulated by **MARPOL**.

## 1.5 Mark Distribution Snapshot (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> As a KTU board-priority topic, *Safety Devices and Plans* is typically tested as:
> - **Module 3 — Part A**: 1–2 direct questions on the function, location, and survey of LSA / FFA.
> - **Module 3 — Part B**: a 14-mark full-choice question on the **Muster List**, **Fire & Safety Plan**, **Damage Control Plan**, or the **LSA Code requirements** for a specific ship type.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Life-Saving Appliances (LSA) — Operational Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in survey:** During the **Annual SEC Survey**, the surveyor must personally witness the *liferaft hydrostatic release* drop test, the *lifeboat load (1.1 × SWL) test*, and the *lifejacket donning drill*. Memorise the SOLAS Ch. III percentages below — they appear in KTU questions every year.

### 2.1.1 Lifeboats — Totally Enclosed (TELB) vs. Partially Enclosed (PELB)

| Parameter | SOLAS III/31 Requirement |
|---|---|
| Capacity per boat | ≤ **150 persons** (post-1986 SOLAS) |
| Number of boats per side | At least **one** on each side OR one free-fall at the bow (≥ 30 % complement may be carried in davit-launched) |
| Construction | **Fibreglass** (most common) or aluminium — fire-retardant per FTP Code |
| Propulsion | Outboard petrol engine **OR** inboard diesel with **independent fuel tank** |
| Fuel endurance | **24 hours** continuous running |
| Speed | ≥ **6 knots** in calm water with full load |
| Release mechanism | **On-load** release per MSC.1/Circ.1392 |
| FFD (fire-fighting) pump | Diesel-driven, **5000 L/h** at **0.7 bar** minimum |
| Air supply | 4 hours supply for **TELB**; 10 min for **PELB** |

### 2.1.2 Liferafts — Inflatable, Throw-Overboard or Davit-Launched

**SOLAS Ch. III/31.1** prescribes that **100 %** of the complement must be accommodated in liferafts, in addition to the lifeboats.

**Liferaft packing capacities (SOLAS III/39):**
- **6-person**, **8-person**, **10-person**, **12-person**, **15-person**, **20-person**, **25-person** are standard.
- The maximum allowed is **25 persons per raft** (except in limited cases with MSC approval).

### 2.1.3 Lifejackets (LJs) and Immersion Suits

- **Lifejackets** — one per person onboard, plus **5 % spare** (e.g., 22 LJs for 20 persons).
- **Additional** for the watchkeepers on the bridge: **at least 6 LJs** must be stowed on the bridge.
- **Children's LJs** — at least **10 %** of the complement OR sufficient for all children onboard, whichever is greater.
- **Immersion Suits (IS)** — one per person for ships operating in **cold-water regions** (sea surface temp ≤ −2 °C to +10 °C, depending on flag).

### 2.1.4 Life Buoys (Ring Buoys)

| Item | Quantity (typical 20,000 GT bulker) | SOLAS III/7.1 |
|---|---|---|
| Total life buoys | 8 | Min 8, including 2 with **buoyant lifeline (≥ 30 m)** |
| With self-igniting light | 6 | 1/2 of total, min 6, with 1 from bridge wing |
| With smoke marker | 2 | Min 2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"State the SOLAS III/7 requirements for life buoys on a passenger ship of 200 pax."* — answer must be **2 × the values above** (i.e., 16 life buoys, etc.).

### 2.1.5 EPIRBs and SARTs

- **EPIRB (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon)** — **406 MHz**, COSPAS-SARSAT satellite, with **121.5 MHz** homing and **GPS**; 48-hour battery life; hydrostatically released at depth **1.5–4 m** OR manually from bridge.
- **SART (Search and Rescue Transponder)** — **9 GHz** (X-band) radar transponder, 3-cm wavelength, placed in survival craft; battery endurance **96 hours** standby + **8 hours** active.

## 2.2 Fire-Fighting Appliances (FFA) — FSS Code, Chapter 2–5

### 2.2.1 The Three-Tier Fire-Fighting Philosophy

1. **Tier 1 — Fixed High-Pressure Water Mist / Sprinkler / Water-Spray (FSS 8/7/2)** — suppression.
2. **Tier 2 — Portable + Semi-Portable Extinguishers (FSS 4)** — first aid.
3. **Tier 3 — Personal Protective Equipment (PPE), breathing apparatus (BA) sets** — escape.

### 2.2.2 Fire Pumps (FSS 12)

- **Number of main pumps:** 2 main + 1 emergency (sea-water or independent sea-water) for cargo ships.
- **Total capacity:** **(0.7 × Q₁) + Q₂** where:
  - **Q₁** = capacity of the second main pump
  - **Q₂** = capacity of the emergency pump
  - The total must deliver water to any hydrant at **≥ 0.35 N/mm² (3.5 bar)**.
- Each pump must have a **sea inlet** separate from the others, typically with non-return valves.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** **"Second-Main × 0.7 + Emergency"** — this is THE most-tested formula in Module 3.

### 2.2.3 Fire Hydrants, Hoses, and Nozzles

- **Hose diameter:** **38 mm** (small), **51 mm** (large), or **64 mm** (very large).
- **Hose length:** **10 m**, **15 m**, or **20 m**.
- **Number of hoses:** one per hydrant + 3 spares, each with a **dual-purpose nozzle** (spray/jets) of **12 mm** or **19 mm** diameter.

### 2.2.4 Portable Extinguishers (FSS Code 4)

| Media | Colour Band | Use On | Capacity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Water** | Red | Class A (wood/paper/textile) | 9 L |
| **Foam** | Cream | Class A & B | 9 L |
| **CO₂** | Black | Electrical / Class C | 5 kg |
| **Dry Powder (DCP)** | Blue | Class A, B, C, electrical | 6 kg / 9 kg |
| **Wet Chemical** | Yellow | Cooking oil / Class F | 3 L |
| **Clean Agent (HFC-227)** | — | Server rooms, electronic spaces | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A 100,000 GT bulk carrier must carry at least **24 portable extinguishers** spread across engine rooms, deck, accommodation, and bridge — but **CO₂ must NEVER be used in a manned space** because of the asphyxiation risk.

## 2.3 Navigational Safety Devices (SOLAS V/18–V/19)

- **Radar:** 9 GHz (X-band) **and** 3 GHz (S-band) for ships ≥ 3,000 GT.
- **ECDIS** (Electronic Chart Display & Information System): mandatory from **2012 onwards** for new build and progressively for existing vessels per SOLAS V/27.
- **AIS** (Automatic Identification System): Class A, 161.975/162.025 MHz, VHF.
- **VDR** (Voyage Data Recorder) / **S-VDR** (Simplified): "Black box" — 12-hour recording.
- **BNWAS** (Bridge Navigational Watch Alarm System): for ships ≥ 150 GT.
- **Heading/Track Control System (HCS/TCS).**

## 2.4 The "Plans" — Mandatory Onboard Safety Documentation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Plans" is a Board Favourite.** A KTU 14-marker on *Safety Plans* will almost always ask you to either **(a)** list the contents of the **Fire & Safety Plan** OR **(b)** draw the **Muster List** structure. Memorise the structure below.

| Plan / Document | SOLAS Reg. | Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Muster List** | SOLAS III/8 | Bridge, mess rooms, alleyways, each cabin |
| **Fire & Safety Plan** (FSP) | SOLAS II-2/15.2.4 (also called "Fire Control Plan") | Permanently exhibited in alleyways, bridge, E/R, and a copy in a weathertight container on the bridge wing |
| **Damage Control Plan** (DCP) | SOLAS II-1/19 (for passenger ships) and IMO Res. A.601(15) | Bridge, E/R, CO₂ room, Officer's cabins |
| **Signalling Lamp** (Day/night) | SOLAS V/19.2.2 | Bridge |
| **Garbage Management Plan** | MARPOL Annex V | Each ship ≥ 100 GT or carrying ≥ 15 crew |
| **SOPEP** (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan) | MARPOL Annex I Reg. 26 | For oil tankers and other ships ≥ 150 GT |
| **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** | ISPS Code | Protected/confidential |
| **Fire Drill & Boat Drill Records** | SOLAS III/19.5 | Master's log / Crew Training Manual |

### 2.4.1 Muster List — Structural Decomposition

The Muster List must show:
- **General alarm signal** (7 short + 1 long blasts, or 5+1 for SOLAS III/50).
- **Public-address system call** ("Mr. XYZ, your station please").
- **Duties of each crew member** at the **boat stations** and **fire stations**.
- **Substitutes** for key persons.
- **Duties of crew for operating the survival craft** and their location.
- **Lifejacket donning drill** order.

### 2.4.2 Fire & Safety Plan (FSP) — Contents

A FSP is a permanent coloured, simplified plan (typically **A3 size, 0.5 mm plastic coated**) that shows:
- **Fire stations** with extinguisher types and locations.
- **Means of access** to each compartment.
- **Ventilation shut-offs** (fire flaps).
- **Fire hydrant layout.**
- **Fire-detection and fire-alarm panel** location.
- **Fixed system** (CO₂ room location, release station, line diagram).
- **Escape routes** to assembly stations and lifeboat embarkation points.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single table covers **~80 %** of the numerical questions a KTU board paper can throw at you on Module 3. Print it mentally.

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbolic Form | Governing Reg. |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total fire-pump capacity** (cargo ship) | $Q_{total} = (0.7 \times Q_{second}) + Q_{emergency}$ | FSS 12.2.2 |
| 2 | **Minimum hose pressure at any hydrant** | $P_{hydrant} \ge 0.35 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;(\text{or } 3.5 \text{ bar})$ | FSS 12.2.2 |
| 3 | **Number of lifejackets** | $N_{LJ} = 1.05 \times N_{crew}$ | SOLAS III/7.2 |
| 4 | **Children's lifejackets** | $\max(10\% \text{ of complement}, \text{ all children})$ | SOLAS III/7.2 |
| 5 | **Liferaft capacity** (≥ 25 % on each side) | $\sum N_{raft} \ge 1.00 \times N_{crew}$ | SOLAS III/31.1.4 |
| 6 | **Liferaft hydrostatic release depth** | $d = 1.5 \text{ to } 4.0 \text{ m}$ (typically 4.5 m) | LSA 4.2.6 |
| 7 | **Lifeboat engine fuel endurance** | $t \ge 24 \text{ h}$ continuous | SOLAS III/31.3.3 |
| 8 | **Lifeboat FFD pump** | $Q_{FFD} \ge 5000 \text{ L/h} @ 0.7 \text{ bar}$ | LSA 4.4.7 |
| 9 | **EPIRB battery endurance** | $t \ge 48 \text{ h}$ | SOLAS IV/14.1 |
| 10 | **SART endurance** | $t \ge 96 \text{ h standby} + 8 \text{ h active}$ | LSA 4.2.13 |
| 11 | **Pyrotechnic parachute flare altitude** | $h \ge 300 \text{ m}$, burning $\ge 40 \text{ s}$ | SOLAS III/6.3 |
| 12 | **Number of life buoys** (cargo ship) | $N_{LB} \ge 8$ (with $1/2$ self-igniting lights) | SOLAS III/7.1.1 |
| 13 | **Number of life buoys** (passenger ship) | $N_{LB} \ge 8 \times \text{(length factor)}$ | SOLAS III/7.1.1 |
| 14 | **Boat drill frequency** | Once every **3 months** for each lifeboat | SOLAS III/19.5 |
| 15 | **Fire drill frequency** | Once every **3 months** for fire stations | SOLAS III/19.4 |
| 16 | **Portable extinguisher spacing (engine room)** | One every **10 m** of walk | FSS 4.2.3 |
| 17 | **CO₂ release time for engine room** | $t \le 2 \text{ min}$ for full discharge | FSS 5.2.1.3 |
| 18 | **BA set endurance (Bord-class / Compressed air)** | $t \ge 30 \text{ min @ 200 bar}$ | FSS 3.1.1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Trick:** When the question says *"Lifeboat engine endurance"*, the answer is **24 hours**, not 12. When it says *"CO₂ discharge time"*, the answer is **120 seconds maximum** — not 3 minutes. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per numerical slip.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These devices and plans are not just "box-ticking" items. They are:
> - **Life-critical** — the difference between a 100 % rescue and a catastrophe.
> - **Insurance-critical** — an expired fire-pump test can void the hull & machinery P&I cover.
> - **Legal-critical** — non-conformity with the SEC is a **detainable deficiency** under the Paris MoU and Tokyo MoU port state control regimes.
> - **Design-critical** — naval architects must reserve the *most structurally reinforced* deck space for lifeboat davits (up to **8 × ship-length in gravity loads** during a 5° trim).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fire Pump Capacity for a Cargo Ship

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem statement:** A bulk carrier has two main fire pumps (each 80 m³/h) and one emergency fire pump (40 m³/h). Verify compliance with FSS Code Clause 12.2.2.

**Step 1 — State the SOLAS rule.**

Per FSS 12.2.2:
$$Q_{total} = (0.7 \times Q_{second\ main}) + Q_{emergency}$$

The required total capacity must equal the capacity of the **largest** main pump (or larger).

**Step 2 — Plug in the values.**

$$Q_{second\ main} = 80 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$
$$Q_{emergency} = 40 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$
$$Q_{largest\ main} = 80 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required minimum.**

$$Q_{total,\ min} = (0.7 \times 80) + 40 = 56 + 40 = 96 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the largest pump capacity.**

$$Q_{largest} = 80 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \;\;<\;\; 96 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

> [!WARNING]
> The configuration **fails** FSS Clause 12.2.2 because the *total available* is 80 m³/h (largest main) which is **less** than the *required* 96 m³/h.
> **Remediation:** Increase the second main pump to $\ge 71.4$ m³/h, OR add a second emergency pump, OR install a power-driven pump dedicated to the sprinkler system.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Life-Saving Appliance Quantities for a Specific Ship

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem statement:** A product tanker of 8,000 GT has a **complement of 22 persons** (20 crew + 2 riders). Determine the number of lifejackets (LJs), children's LJs, life buoys, and liferafts required.

**Step 1 — Lifejackets (LJs).**

Per SOLAS III/7.2: one LJ per person + 5 % spares.

$$N_{LJ} = 22 \times 1.05 = 23.1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 24 \text{ LJs (rounded up)}$$

**Step 2 — Children's LJs.**

$$10\% \text{ of complement} = 0.10 \times 22 = 2.2 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 3 \text{ Children's LJs}$$

(We round up because a partial child LJ is meaningless. A new SOLAS amendment also requires 1 LJ for each infant onboard.)

**Step 3 — Life buoys (LBs).**

Per SOLAS III/7.1.1 (cargo ship, ≥ 100 m length):

$$N_{LB} = 8$$

Of these:
- **Self-igniting lights:** $\ge 1/2 \text{ of total} = 4$ (and **2 must be** smoke-marker LBs, **1 with** a buoyant lifeline).
- **1 LB with lifeline on each bridge wing.**
- **2 LBs with self-activating smoke signals** stowed on the bridge.

**Step 4 — Liferafts.**

Per SOLAS III/31.1.4: 100 % complement in liferafts.

$$N_{raft,\ min} = 22 \text{ persons (total)}$$

Use 2 × 12-person rafts (one each side) + 1 × 6-person reserve raft (optional) **OR** 3 × 10-person rafts (two one side, one other side — *but each side must independently float*).

**Lateral distribution rule:**
$$\ge 50\% \text{ of complement on each side} = 0.5 \times 22 = 11 \text{ persons}$$

So **1 × 12-person raft on each side** = 24 persons total (excess of 2 persons — permitted).

**Step 5 — Lifeboats.**

For an 8,000 GT product tanker: at least **one TELB on each side** (each 22 persons) OR one **free-fall lifeboat at the bow** (50–60 % complement).

**Step 6 — Final tally matrix.**

| Equipment | Quantity | SOLAS Reg. |
|---|---|---|
| Adult LJs | 24 | III/7.2 |
| Children LJs | 3 | III/7.2 |
| Life Buoys (total) | 8 | III/7.1 |
| Life Buoys with light | 4 | III/7.1.2 |
| Life Buoys with smoke | 2 | III/7.1.3 |
| Life Buoys with lifeline | 1 (≥ 30 m) | III/7.1.4 |
| Liferafts | 2 × 12-person | III/31.1.4 |
| Lifeboats (TELB) | 2 × 22-person (davit-launched) | III/31.1.2 |
| EPIRB | 1 | IV/14.1 |
| SART | 2 | IV/13.1 |
| Immersion Suits | 22 (cold-water trade) | III/7.3 |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CO₂ System Quantity for an Engine Room

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem statement:** The engine room of a bulk carrier measures **L = 25 m, B = 15 m, H = 12 m**. Determine the CO₂ quantity required by FSS Code 5.2.1.1.

**Step 1 — Gross volume.**

$$V_{gross} = L \times B \times H = 25 \times 15 \times 12 = 4500 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply FSS 5.2.1.1 rule.**

For machinery spaces, the **FSS rule is 40 % of the gross volume** (the standard engine room CO₂ flooding factor).

$$V_{flood} = 0.40 \times V_{gross} = 0.40 \times 4500 = 1800 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Convert to CO₂ mass.**

At standard NTP, **1 kg of CO₂ = 0.56 m³ of gas**. Therefore:

$$m_{CO_2} = \frac{V_{flood}}{0.56} = \frac{1800}{0.56} = 3214.3 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{CO_2} \approx 3215 \text{ kg} \;\; \text{(rounded to nearest } 25 \text{ kg cylinder size)}}$$

**Step 4 — Cylinder count.**

Each standard CO₂ cylinder holds **45 kg**. So:
$$N_{cyl} = \frac{3215}{45} \approx 72 \text{ cylinders}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 5 % over-supply is mandatory because of leakage, so the **final order is 76 cylinders** of 45 kg CO₂.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fire-Drill Crew Distribution (Muster List Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem statement:** A 22-person ship needs a Muster List. Distribute the crew into Boat, Fire, and Damage-Control parties.

**Step 1 — Boat Party (for each lifeboat).**
- **Boatman-in-Charge:** 1 (Senior AB)
- **Boat No. 2 / Stern / Hook:** 2
- **Davit operator / Winchman:** 1
- **Crowd control at embarkation deck:** 2
- **Search of accommodation:** 2

Total per boat: **8 persons × 2 boats = 16 persons**.

**Step 2 — Fire Party (BA Teams).**
- **2 BA teams × 3 persons = 6** (each team: 2 in BA, 1 outside as BA-Control).
- **PPE/back-up team:** 2.
- **Party leader:** 1 (Chief Officer).

**Step 3 — Damage Control / Engine.**
- **Chief Engineer + 2nd Eng + Motorman:** 3 persons.
- **Auxiliary:** 1.

**Step 4 — Master / Bridge / Communication.**
- **Master** at Muster Station.
- **Bridge: OOW + AB lookout + Radio Officer** = 3.

**Step 5 — Substitutes.**
- **Each key post has a designated substitute** named in the Muster List.

This Muster List is posted on the bridge, in mess rooms, in each cabin, and at every muster station.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — "What the Surveyor Checks"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this matrix as a **mental checklist** when answering KTU survey-type questions. The "Item" column is what you list, the "Reg." column is the cite, and "Survey Verifies" is the value-add.

| # | Item | Reg. | Survey Verifies |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Liferaft hydrostatic release | LSA 4.2.6 | Wedge in place, HRU date not expired (≤ 24 months since manufacture) |
| 2 | Lifeboat release mechanism | MSC.1/Circ.1392 | On-load release with **≥ 1.1 × SWL** load test |
| 3 | EPIRB battery + hydrostatic | SOLAS IV/14 | Battery expiry date; HRU weight 1.5–4 m depth |
| 4 | SART battery | LSA 4.2.13 | 96+8 h endurance label |
| 5 | BA sets | FSS 3.1.1 | Air cylinder ≥ 200 bar; 30 min endurance |
| 6 | CO₂ release system | FSS 5.2.1 | Discharge time ≤ 120 s; pre-discharge alarm 30 s |
| 7 | Fire pump flow test | FSS 12.2.2 | Q ≥ 0.35 N/mm² at most remote hydrant |
| 8 | Muster List update | SOLAS III/8.2 | List reflects current crew; posted in 5+ locations |
| 9 | Fire & Safety Plan | SOLAS II-2/15.2.4 | A3 laminated, red/green colour code, latest revision |
| 10 | Damage Control Plan | SOLAS II-1/19 | Watertight doors, vents, scuttles labelled |
| 11 | Pyrotechnics | SOLAS III/6.3 | Para-flares & line-throwers not past expiry |
| 12 | EPIRB ID | COSPAS-SARSAT | 15-character hex code registered to the ship |

## 3.6 Worked Example — Total Number of Life Buoys for a 250 m Bulk Carrier

$$N_{LB} = 8 + \left\lfloor \frac{L - 100}{50} \right\rfloor \text{ (for } L > 100 \text{ m, cargo ships)}$$

$$N_{LB} = 8 + \left\lfloor \frac{250 - 100}{50} \right\rfloor = 8 + 3 = 11 \text{ life buoys}$$

Of these:
- **6 with self-igniting lights** (½ of 11 rounded up).
- **2 with smoke marker** (1 on each bridge wing with a light).
- **1 with a buoyant lifeline (≥ 30 m).**
- **1 with self-activating smoke signal** stowed on the bridge (in addition to the 2 above).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Emergency Response Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The block diagram below is a **functional architecture** describing the sequential interactions of safety devices during a *real* emergency at sea, from detection → alarm → response → recovery.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Fire or Flood Detected] --> B1[Manual Call Point or Sensor]
    A2[Man Overboard] --> B2[Bridge Crew Witnessed]
    A3[Abandon Ship] --> B3[General Alarm 7 Short 1 Long]

    B1 --> C1[Fire Detection Panel FFP]
    B2 --> C2[3 Long Blasts Mayday on VHF]
    B3 --> C3[Public Address and Muster List Read]

    C1 --> D1[General Alarm Triggered]
    C2 --> D2[Mayday Procedure SOLAS IV]
    C3 --> D3[Crew to Muster Stations]

    D1 --> E1[Fire Party with BA]
    D2 --> E2[Distress Alert EPIRB and DSC]
    D3 --> E3[Lifejackets Donned Lifeboat Embarkation]

    E1 --> F1[CO2 Released or Hose Reel Used]
    E2 --> F2[Rescue Coordination Centre RCC Notified]
    E3 --> F3[Lifeboat Launched or Liferaft Inflated]

    F1 --> G1[Fire Extinguished and Patrol]
    F2 --> G2[RCC Dispatches MRCC Aircraft or Vessel]
    F3 --> G3[Survival at Sea with SART and Flares]
```

graph LR

| **Red** | Fire-fighting equipment, hydrants, extinguishers, pumps |
