# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the LSA Code and list the SOLAS Chapter III requirements for lifejackets and life buoys on a 200 m cargo ship.**

**Model Answer (Model Key for 3 Marks):**

> **LSA Code (Resolution MSC.48(66), amended)**: The International Life-Saving Appliance Code, adopted under SOLAS Chapter III, that prescribes the design, construction, performance, and testing standards for all life-saving appliances installed on ships.

**Lifejackets (SOLAS III/7.2):**
- One LJ for every person onboard + **5 % spares** (rounded up to next whole number).
- **6 LJs** additional on the bridge.
- Children's LJs: at least **10 % of complement** or enough for all children, whichever is greater.

**Life Buoys (SOLAS III/7.1.1, for 200 m cargo ship):**
- **Total: 8 + ⌊(200 − 100)/50⌋ = 8 + 2 = 10** life buoys.
- **6 with self-igniting lights** (≥ ½ of total).
- **2 with self-activating smoke signals** (1 stowed on bridge, 1 on each bridge wing with light).
- **1 with a buoyant lifeline of ≥ 30 m**.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the FSS Code requirement for fire pump capacity on a cargo ship, with the relevant formula.**

**Model Answer (Model Key for 3 Marks):**

> Per FSS Code Clause 12.2.2, a cargo ship must have at least **two main fire pumps** and **one emergency fire pump**. The **total capacity** of all pumps (with the largest pump at zero) must be at least:
>
> $$Q_{total} = (0.7 \times Q_{second\ main}) + Q_{emergency} \;\;\ge\;\; Q_{largest\ main}$$
>
> Each main pump must independently deliver water at **≥ 0.35 N/mm² (3.5 bar)** to the most remote hydrant. The emergency pump must be independently powered (e.g., diesel-driven) and have a separate sea inlet. *(End — 3 marks distributed as: 1 for citing FSS 12.2.2, 1 for the formula, 1 for the 3.5 bar hydrant rule.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Re-Attempted for 2024 Scheme]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the **Muster List** structure for a 22-person dry-cargo ship and explain each duty assignment in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to **SOLAS Chapter II-2/15.2.4**, describe the **Fire and Safety Plan (FSP)** — its location, colour code, scale, and minimum information to be shown.

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

> The Muster List, required by **SOLAS Regulation III/8**, is a permanent document exhibited on the bridge, in mess rooms, alleyways, and each crew cabin. It must specify:
>
> 1. **General alarm signal:** Seven short + one long blast (≥ 10 s continuous) per SOLAS III/50.
> 2. **Crew assembly action:** Crew proceed to assigned muster stations with their assigned lifejacket.
> 3. **Boat station duties:** 8 persons per lifeboat (Boatman-in-Charge, hookman, winchman, embarkation assistants, accommodation searcher, stretcher-bearer).
> 4. **Fire station duties:** 6 persons (2 BA teams × 3, plus 2 PPE assistants), Chief Officer as Party Leader.
> 5. **Damage control / engine:** Chief Engineer, 2nd Engineer, Motorman — 3 persons.
> 6. **Substitutes for key persons** (OOW, Master, Chief Engineer, Chief Officer).
> 7. **Duties of crew for operating the survival craft** — release mechanism, painter line, embarkation, lowering.
> 8. **Lifejacket-donning drill order.**
> 9. **Signalling apparatus** (pyrotechnics, EPIRB, SART locations).

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

> The Fire and Safety Plan (sometimes called the Fire Control Plan) is required by **SOLAS Regulation II-2/15.2.4** and applies to **all ships**.
>
> 1. **Format:** Permanently exhibited plans, **A3 minimum**, plastic-coated, **waterproof**, in **distinctive colours**.
> 2. **Locations (a copy in each):** Bridge, engine room, CO₂ room, each accommodation alley, bridge wing (in a weathertight enclosure, painted red).
> 3. **Scale:** Symbolic (not-to-scale) is acceptable; **the most common ratio is 1:200** for general arrangement.
> 4. **Colour code:** Red (FFA), Yellow (ventilation/CO₂), Green (escape routes), Blue (mandatory PPE signs), Black/White (structural fire divisions).
> 5. **Information shown:** Each deck, fire stations, hydrants, extinguishers (with media), CO₂ release stations, fire-detection panel, fire dampers, watertight doors, helicopter winching area, muster stations, escape arrows, structural A/B/H class divisions.
> 6. **Updates:** The plan must reflect the current ship; revisions approved by the flag administration and updated during Dry-Dock Survey.
> 7. **Survey verification:** Surveyor checks a copy on the bridge wing, in the CO₂ room, in the engine room, and in at least one accommodation alley.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Re-Attempted for 2024 Scheme]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** State **SOLAS Chapter III** requirements for **lifeboats, liferafts, and lifejackets** on a 6,000 GT product tanker (complement 24).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Damage Control Plan (DCP)** of a passenger ship under SOLAS II-1/19. List the contents and explain how the surveyor verifies it.

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

> **Lifeboats (SOLAS III/31, III/32):**
> - At least **one TELB on each side** OR one **free-fall lifeboat** at the bow (≥ 30 % complement).
> - Capacity: must accommodate the entire complement.
> - For 24 pax: **2 × 25-person TELB** (or 1 × 50-person free-fall if bow is fitted).
> - Engine: diesel inboard, 24-h endurance, ≥ 6 knots.
> - FFD pump: diesel-driven, 5000 L/h at 0.7 bar.
> - On-load release mechanism tested annually.
>
> **Liferafts (SOLAS III/31.1.4):**
> - 100 % complement in liferafts, e.g., **3 × 10-person** or **2 × 12-person + 1 × 6-person**.
> - At least **one raft on each side** (≥ 50 % of complement per side).
> - Davit-launched or throw-overboard with **HRU** at 1.5–4 m.
>
> **Lifejackets (SOLAS III/7.2):**
> - 24 + 5 % = **26 lifejackets** (rounded up).
> - **6 on the bridge**.
> - **3 children's LJs** (10 % of complement).
> - **22 immersion suits** if the ship operates in cold-water zones (sea temp ≤ 10 °C).
>
> **Survey checks:** HRU date, paint condition, embarkation ladder, painter line, hydrostatic release test, load (1.1 × SWL) test.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

> The Damage Control Plan (DCP) is required for **all passenger ships** under **SOLAS II-1/19**, and the IMO Resolution A.601(15) provides the recommended contents.
>
> **Contents of the DCP:**
> 1. **General arrangement of watertight compartments** with flooding boundaries.
> 2. **Locations of watertight doors (WTDs), scuttles, and their indicators.**
> 3. **Ventilation closures, fire dampers, sounding pipes.**
> 4. **Pump room and bilge line layout.**
> 5. **Cross-flooding arrangements** to correct list.
> 6. **Stability information** (GM, permissible list, free-surface effect) for **flooded condition**.
> 7. **Crew damage-control teams** with duty assignments.
> 8. **Emergency lighting and communications** for damage-control teams.
>
> **Surveyor verifies:**
> - A copy is posted on the bridge, in the engine room, and at each damage-control station.
> - The plan is the **latest revision** approved by the flag state.
> - **WTDs** are tested weekly (sliding doors) and **drills** are recorded.
> - **Cross-flooding valves** are accessible, labelled, and the master has the operating key.
> - The **stability booklet** is on board and reflects the actual loading condition.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Traps (verified against board papers 2019–2024):**
> 1. **Forgetting the "per side" rule** for liferafts and lifeboats. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Writing SOLAS III/7 instead of III/7.1 / III/7.2** — they are sub-clauses. The examiner expects the precise sub-clause.
> 3. **Confusing ½ of life buoys with lights** (the rule is "at least ½"; many students write "exactly ½" and lose 1 mark).
> 4. **Failing to specify the SOLAS ch. II-2/15.2.4 reference** for the Fire & Safety Plan. The examiner wants the *exact regulation cite*, not "SOLAS Chapter II".
> 5. **Writing "fire pump = 2 pumps"** without naming the *third (emergency)* pump. Marks lost: 2.
> 6. **Confusing TELB and PELB.** PELBs were banned on tankers **post-1986 SOLAS**; the correct answer is always TELB for a product tanker.
> 7. **Using "EPIRB battery is 24 h"** — the correct number is **48 h** endurance at −20 °C.
> 8. **Drawing the FSP without a colour code** — even a quick legend (Red = FFA, Green = escape) fetches 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Safety Devices and Plans: The 30-Point Cheat-Sheet for Last-Minute Revision**
>
> 1. **LSA Code = SOLAS III**; **FSS Code = SOLAS II-2**; **GMDSS = SOLAS IV**.
> 2. **Lifejackets:** 1 per pax + 5 % spares + 6 on bridge + 10 % children.
> 3. **Life buoys (cargo ship):** 8 + ⌊(L − 100)/50⌋ for L > 100 m.
> 4. **Half of life buoys** must have self-igniting lights.
> 5. **Liferafts:** 100 % complement; ≥ 50 % per side.
> 6. **Lifeboats:** ≥ 1 per side OR 1 free-fall ≥ 30 % complement.
> 7. **Lifeboat engine endurance:** 24 hours continuous.
> 8. **Lifeboat FFD pump:** 5000 L/h at 0.7 bar.
> 9. **EPIRB:** 406 MHz, 48-h battery, hydrostatic at 1.5–4 m.
> 10. **SART:** 9 GHz, 96 h standby + 8 h active.
> 11. **Pyrotechnics expiry:** Para-flare ≥ 300 m altitude, ≥ 40 s burn.
> 12. **Fire pumps (cargo):** 2 main + 1 emergency; Q_total = 0.7 Q₂ + Q_emerg.
> 13. **Hydrant pressure:** ≥ 3.5 bar at any hydrant.
> 14. **CO₂ discharge time:** ≤ 120 s with 30 s pre-alarm.
> 15. **CO₂ quantity:** 40 % of engine room gross volume.
> 16. **BA set:** ≥ 30 min endurance at 200 bar.
> 17. **Fire drill:** every 3 months; **Boat drill:** every 3 months.
> 18. **Muster List:** SOLAS III/8 — bridge, mess, cabins, alleyways.
> 19. **FSP:** SOLAS II-2/15.2.4 — A3, coloured, weathertight container on bridge wing.
> 20. **DCP:** SOLAS II-1/19 (passenger ships) — A.601(15) contents.
> 21. **Colour code:** Red FFA, Yellow vent/CO₂, Green escape, Blue mandatory.
> 22. **General alarm:** 7 short + 1 long blasts (SOLAS III/50).
> 23. **Mayday alarm:** 3 long blasts (fire/emergency) per SOLAS V.
> 24. **Radar:** X-band AND S-band for ships ≥ 3,000 GT.
> 25. **ECDIS mandatory** for new builds from 2012; retrofit programme.
> 26. **VDR:** 12-hour recording, float-free capsule.
> 27. **BNWAS:** mandatory for ships ≥ 150 GT.
> 28. **SOPEP:** MARPOL Annex I Reg. 26 — for tankers and ships ≥ 150 GT.
> 29. **Garbage Management Plan:** MARPOL Annex V — ships ≥ 100 GT or 15 crew.
> 30. **ISM Code** demands that *all* above devices be tested, logged, and audited.

> **Final KTU Mnemonic — "Big Four Regulations":** **S - F - G - M**
> **S**OLAS (III LSA, II-2 FSS, IV Radio, V Nav) — **F**SS Code — **G**MDSS — **M**ARPOL.
> Any safety device you are asked about sits under one of these four letters. Identifying the letter first gives you the regulation cite — and the regulation cite is **half the marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dry-docking Works - Docking and Undocking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dry-docking Works - Docking and Undocking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Dry-Docking Works

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Dry-docking** is the controlled engineering operation in which a floating vessel is brought into a specialized marine structure, gradually removed from the water, and supported on a system of pre-arranged keel and bilge blocks so that the underwater hull, propellers, rudders, sea valves, and appendages become fully accessible for statutory survey, classification society inspection, cleaning, painting, structural repair, or major conversion work.

Within the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST861)** framework, *docking* specifically refers to the *inward* sequence of taking the vessel from her free-floating condition to her fully landed, blocked, and secured position ashore. *Undocking* is the reverse operation, returning the vessel to a free-floating condition with positive under-keel clearance, after all repairs, sea trials (if any), and pre-departure checks are completed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PENST861 – Module 4):**
> The student is expected to demonstrate competence in (a) the *operational sequence* of docking and undocking, (b) the *engineering calculations* associated with blocking, stability, and draft corrections, and (c) the *safety and regulatory* obligations imposed by IACS Classification Societies (ABS, BV, DNV, LR, IRS) and the IMO.

The three principal categories of dry-docking installations are:

| Type of Dry Dock | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Graving Dock** | A land-locked, U-shaped basin closed by a caisson or flap gate; water is pumped out. | New-building, major repairs, very large vessels. |
| **Floating Dock** | A U-shaped, self-floating pontoon dock that submerges, takes the ship, then lifts. | Routine docking, repair afloat, mobile. |
| **Slipway / Marine Railway** | An inclined plane with rails; the ship is hauled up on a cradle. | Small craft, fishing vessels, tugs. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Ship Hospital"**
> Imagine your car needs work on its underbody. You drive it onto a hydraulic lift, the lift rises, the wheels leave the ground, and the mechanic now has complete access to the exhaust, gearbox, and chassis. The shipyard does exactly the same — but the "car" weighs anything from **5,000 tonnes to 300,000 tonnes**, and the "lift" can be a giant concrete pit with a sliding door at one end, or a self-floating platform that dives under the ship and then rises.
>
> The act of getting the car onto the lift = **Docking**.
> The act of lowering the car back to the road = **Undocking**.

The "surgery" that follows is the repair work — but before the surgeon (fitter/welder) can start, the **operating table (the keel and bilge blocks)** must be in position, the **patient (the ship)** must be perfectly level and stable, and the **anesthesia (ballast)** must be managed to keep her still.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Values (KTU Board Favourites)

- **Standard seawater density (ρ_sw):** **1.025 t/m³**
- **Standard fresh water density (ρ_fw):** **1.000 t/m³**
- **Typical block bearing pressure:** **0.2 MPa to 0.4 MPa (20 to 40 tonnes/m²)** for timber blocks.
- **Minimum under-keel clearance at entry:** **0.5 m to 1.0 m**.
- **Number of keel blocks for a 150 m vessel:** typically **35 to 45 blocks** at **2.5 m to 3.5 m spacing**.
- **Deflection limit on the keel line** (LR/ABS): **L/1000 mm**, where L is the supported keel length in metres.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently award a half-mark or full-mark for quoting the correct reference standard (e.g., *"Block pressure shall not exceed 0.4 N/mm² for greenheart blocks per Lloyd's Register Pt. 1, Ch. 2"*).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Docking Sequence (Why-How Breakdown)

The dry-docking operation is a tightly choreographed, multi-stage engineering procedure. Each stage has a specific engineering "Why" and a procedural "How".

### **Stage 1 — Pre-Docking Preparations (≈ 7–10 days before docking)**

- **Why?** Eliminate uncertainty, optimise dock time, ensure safety of life and asset.
- **How?**
  1. Issue the **Docking Plan** to all stakeholders (ship's staff, shipyard, classification surveyor).
  2. Conduct **Pre-Docking Survey**: hull plating thickness measurements, cathodic-protection (ICCP/Zinc) status, sea-valve condition.
  3. Pre-stage **keel blocks, bilge blocks, side blocks, shores, and shores-pre-fabricated** for the dock floor.
  4. Calculate **Light Ship Weight** (LSW) and **Centre of Gravity (KG, LCG, TCG)** from the inclining experiment data.
  5. **Empty the fresh-water tanks** to reduce displacement variation; double bottom (D.B.) tanks may be used for trimming correction.
  6. Disconnect shore power lines, remove radar mast, ventilators that foul the air draught, or stow cranes/lifeboats as per the **Air Draught Diagram**.

### **Stage 2 — Entry into the Dock**

- **Why?** Position the vessel precisely over the keel block centre-line, with correct trim and heel, before blocking the keel.
  - **How?**
    1. **Dock flooding** opens the caisson (graving) or the dock submerges (floating).
    2. Vessel enters under her own propulsion or with the assistance of **tug escorts** (typically 2 to 4 tugs).
    3. **Moorings** are set: headline forward, spring lines, and stern lines.
    4. **Centring** is done using mooring winches and visual references (centre marks on the dock side).
    5. The **Transit Draft** is carefully monitored; ballast is adjusted to achieve the planned **Docking Trim (typically 0.5 m by the stern)**.

### **Stage 3 — Initial Support (Keel Touch-Down)**

- **Why?** Transfer the entire weight of the vessel from water buoyancy to the block system progressively.
- **How?**
  1. The water level is slowly lowered (deballasting for floating dock, pumping out for graving dock).
  2. The vessel *touches* the central keel block at a single point — this is the **Keel-Land Point**.
  3. A second set of "softwood" or "popping" blocks is positioned on either side.
  4. Continue dewatering — the vessel now rests on the **Main Keel Blocks**.
  5. **Bilge blocks** (port and starboard) are pre-positioned but kept low; as the dock empties, they engage the bilge to provide transverse stability.

### **Stage 4 — Securing on the Hard**

- **Why?** Convert the vessel from a "docked" state to a "stable workshop" state — full blocking, shoring, and shore-power.
- **How?**
  1. **Side shores / shores** (timber or hydraulic) are wedged between the hull side and the dock wall.
  2. **Dolphins, breast shores, and headings** are set.
  3. **Shore power** (typically 440 V / 60 Hz or 380 V / 50 Hz) is connected.
  4. **Gas-free, clean, and paint** permits are issued.
  5. **Class Survey** commences — underwater hull, tail-shaft, sea-valves, propellers.

### **Stage 5 — Undocking (Reverse Procedure)**

1. **Re-flood** the dock (or submerge the floating dock).
2. Remove all side shores and shore power.
3. Pump out all ballast to **Undocking Draft** (typically deeper than transit).
4. **Push-off** using capstans / winches, or tug assistance, until vessel clears the dock entrance.
5. Mooring afloat, post-docking trial (sea trial if scheduled).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat-Sheet — Memorise these equations. KTU boards ask direct numerical problems on at least one of these per year.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units & Symbols |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Displacement (W)** | $$W = \nabla \times \rho$$ | ∇ = submerged volume (m³); ρ = water density (t/m³) |
| 2 | **Mean Draft** | $$T_m = \dfrac{T_f + T_a}{2}$$ | T_f = forward draft, T_a = aft draft (m) |
| 3 | **Trim** | $$Trim = T_a - T_f \quad \text{(trim by stern positive)}$$ | metres |
| 4 | **Density Correction to Draft** | $$T_c = T_{act} \times \dfrac{\rho_{act}}{\rho_{ref}}$$ | All drafts in m; ρ in t/m³ |
| 5 | **Block Bearing Pressure (avg)** | $$P_{avg} = \dfrac{W}{A_b}$$ | W in tonnes, A_b = total block contact area (m²) |
| 6 | **Heel Correction (off-centre GZ)** | $$P_{max} = P_{avg}\left(1 + \dfrac{6e}{b}\right)$$ | e = eccentricity of G off centreline (m); b = bearing width (m) |
| 7 | **Longitudinal Block Load Distribution** | $$q(x) = \dfrac{W}{L_k} + \dfrac{M \cdot x}{I_b}$$ | q = load per unit length; M = trim moment; I_b = block system second moment |
| 8 | **Deflection of Keel (simply-supported analogy)** | $$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5\, q\, L_k^4}{384\, E\, I}$$ | q = distributed load; E = block modulus; I = block moment of inertia |
| 9 | **Draft Survey — Loading Condition** | $$\nabla = \dfrac{\text{TPC}}{100} \times 100 \times T_m$$ | TPC = Tonnes Per Centimetre Immersion (t/cm) |
| 10 | **Bottom Block Count** | $$N_b = \dfrac{L_{pp}}{S_b} + 1$$ | L_pp = length between perpendiculars; S_b = block spacing (m) |
| 11 | **Pillaring / Blocking Height Correction** | $$h = T_{max} - D_{dock} - c$$ | h = block height; c = minimum keel clearance (≥ 0.5 m) |
| 12 | **Squat (shallow-water draft increase)** | $$T_{sq} = \dfrac{C_b \cdot V^2}{20}$$ | C_b = block coefficient; V = speed (knots) |
| 13 | **Required Block Spacing for Stress** | $$S_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \sigma_{allow} \cdot Z}{q}}$$ | σ_allow = allowable stress; Z = section modulus |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU table-syntax rule: vertical bars `|x|` will break the table. Use **`\vert x \vert`** in any LaTeX within a table cell.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The calculations above are not academic exercises — they are **daily tools** in:

- **Ship repair yard planning** (estimating dock occupancy days).
- **Classification society approval** (LR, ABS, DNV, BV rule compliance — e.g., LR Pt. 1, Ch. 2, Sec. 2 "Docking").
- **Stability booklet** validation after hull modifications.
- **Insurance and P&I club** surveys for hull-condition warranties.
- **Naval architecture firms** designing floating-dock and graving-dock infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heel Correction to Block Load (Why the dock floor must be designed for the *worst* case)

When the vessel is suspended on the keel and bilge blocks, the *centroid* of the ship (G) may not lie exactly on the centreline of the keel blocks. This happens because:

- Asymmetric fuel oil (F.O.) or fresh water (F.W.) in port/starboard tanks.
- Off-centre workshop materials (welding machines, scaffolding).
- Wind force during the critical "land" moment.

Let the **block system** be treated as a continuous rectangular bearing of width $b$ (transverse). The **average pressure** under the blocks is:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{W}{A_b} = \frac{W}{b \cdot L_{eff}}$$

where $L_{eff}$ is the effective length of the block system.

**Step 1 — Set up the moment about the centreline.** If the centre of gravity G is offset by an eccentricity $e$ from the dock centreline, the moment about the centreline equals $W \cdot e$.

**Step 2 — The pressure distribution becomes linear.** For a rectangular bearing strip, the linear pressure distribution is given by:

$$P(x) = P_{avg} + P_{var} \cdot \left(\frac{2x}{b}\right)$$

where $P_{var}$ is the variation term and $x$ is measured from the centreline.

**Step 3 — Equate the moment of the pressure distribution to the applied moment.**

The first moment of area of the linear pressure about the centreline:

$$M_{press} = \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} P(x) \cdot x \cdot L_{eff} \, dx$$

Substituting $P(x)$:

$$M_{press} = \int_{-b/2}^{b/2} \left[ P_{avg} + P_{var} \cdot \left(\frac{2x}{b}\right) \right] \cdot x \cdot L_{eff} \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral term by term.**

$$\int_{-b/2}^{b/2} P_{avg} \cdot x \cdot L_{eff} \, dx = 0 \quad \text{(odd function)}$$

$$\int_{-b/2}^{b/2} \frac{2 P_{var}}{b} \cdot x^2 \cdot L_{eff} \, dx = \frac{2 P_{var} L_{eff}}{b} \cdot \frac{b^3}{12} = \frac{P_{var} \cdot L_{eff} \cdot b^2}{6}$$

**Step 5 — Equate to applied moment and solve for $P_{var}$.**

$$W \cdot e = \frac{P_{var} \cdot L_{eff} \cdot b^2}{6}$$

$$P_{var} = \frac{6 W e}{L_{eff} \cdot b^2}$$

**Step 6 — Write the maximum pressure on the heavily loaded side.**

$$P_{max} = P_{avg} + P_{var} = \frac{W}{L_{eff} \cdot b} + \frac{6 W e}{L_{eff} \cdot b^2} = \frac{W}{L_{eff} \cdot b}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{b}\right)$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} = P_{avg} \left( 1 + \frac{6e}{b} \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical **"6e/b" rule** that KTU examiners love to set as a 7-mark numerical. If $e = b/6$, the maximum pressure is **double** the average — the dock floor can be overloaded just by an off-centre load!

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Keel Blocks and Spacing for a 150 m Bulker

**Given data:**

- Length between perpendiculars, $L_{pp} = 150$ m
- Allowable block bearing pressure, $P_{allow} = 0.30$ MPa $= 30$ t/m²
- Displacement at docking draft, $W = 12{,}000$ tonnes
- Block width (each), $w_b = 0.30$ m
- Block length (each), $l_b = 1.20$ m
- Block contact length (with hull), $L_c = l_b \approx 1.20$ m (each block)

**Step 1 — Required total block area.**

$$A_{req} = \frac{W}{P_{allow}} = \frac{12{,}000}{30} = 400 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Area contributed by one block (effective).**

$$A_{one} = L_c \times w_b = 1.20 \times 0.30 = 0.36 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Number of blocks.**

$$N_b = \frac{A_{req}}{A_{one}} = \frac{400}{0.36} = 1111.1 \rightarrow 1112 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to spacing along the keel.**

Total effective length covered:

$$L_{eff} = N_b \times l_b = 1112 \times 1.20 = 1334.4 \text{ m (unrealistic → distribute fore-aft)}$$

A more realistic *keel-block* approach uses a continuous centreline block. We instead use:

$$N_{keel} = \frac{L_{pp}}{S_b} + 1 = \frac{150}{3.0} + 1 = 51 \text{ keel blocks}$$

**Step 5 — Verify bearing pressure on a single keel block line.**

Effective contact length $L_c \times N_{keel} = 1.20 \times 51 = 61.2$ m

$$P_{avg} = \frac{12{,}000}{61.2 \times 0.30} = \frac{12{,}000}{18.36} = 653.6 \text{ t/m}^2 = 6.54 \text{ MPa} \quad (\text{TOO HIGH!})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The keel blocks alone cannot carry the vessel.** This is why *bilge blocks* and *side shores* are essential. In a real dock, only **40–60%** of the displacement is carried by the keel blocks; the rest is shared with the bilge blocks and shores.

**Step 6 — Re-distribute the load realistically.**

Assume keel blocks carry 50%: $W_{keel} = 6000$ t

$$P_{keel} = \frac{6000}{18.36} = 326.8 \text{ t/m}^2 = 3.27 \text{ MPa} \quad (\text{still high for timber})$$

Conclusion: For a 12,000 t vessel, *fibreglass-reinforced plastic (FRP) blocks* or *concrete cradle* is required, not just timber.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Trim Correction for Docking

The vessel must be docked with a small trim (typically 0.3–0.5 m by the stern) so that the stern clears the after-blocks of the dock floor.

**Step 1 — Compute present drafts.**

$T_f = 6.20$ m (forward), $T_a = 6.50$ m (aft)

**Step 2 — Mean draft and trim.**

$$T_m = \frac{6.20 + 6.50}{2} = 6.35 \text{ m}$$

$$Trim = 6.50 - 6.20 = +0.30 \text{ m (by stern)} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Density correction** (water density in dock = 1.020 t/m³ vs. reference 1.025 t/m³).

$$T_c = 6.35 \times \frac{1.020}{1.025} = 6.32 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Verify under-keel clearance in dock.**

Dock floor depth at entry: $D_{dock} = 8.50$ m
Required block height (lowest block) = 0.50 m
Squat allowance = 0.10 m

$$T_{max,allow} = 8.50 - 0.50 - 0.10 = 7.90 \text{ m}$$

Since $T_c = 6.32$ m $< 7.90$ m, the vessel can safely enter. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Dock Selection

| Criterion | Graving Dock | Floating Dock | Slipway |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vessel Size** | Small to VLCC | Small to Capesize | Small craft only |
| **Docking Time** | 4–8 h | 2–4 h | 4–6 h |
| **Capital Cost** | Very high | High | Low |
| **Mobility** | Fixed | Mobile | Fixed |
| **Weather Sensitivity** | Low (indoor type) | High (sea-state) | Medium |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | High | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Complete Docking Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pre-Docking Survey and Plan] --> B[Issue Docking Plan and Permits]
    B --> C[Flood Dock / Submerge Floating Dock]
    C --> D[Vessel Approaches Under Tugs]
    D --> E[Set Mooring Lines - Head, Spring, Stern]
    E --> F[Adjust Ballast to Docking Trim]
    F --> G[Centring on Dock Centreline]
    G --> H[Start Dewatering - Keel Touch Down]
    H --> I[Engage Softwood Popping Blocks]
    I --> J[Continue Dewatering to Working Level]
    J --> K[Engage Bilge Blocks]
    K --> L[Set Side Shores and Headings]
    L --> M[Connect Shore Power and Services]
    M --> N[Hand Over to Class Surveyor]
    N --> O[Docking Complete - Repairs Begin]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Bottom Support System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VesselHull[Vessel Hull Cross Section]
        H1[Port Side Shell]
        H2[Bottom Shell Plating]
        H3[Starboard Side Shell]
        H4[Keel Plate]
    end

    subgraph BlockSystem[Dock Block System]
        K1[Main Keel Block - Centreline]
        B1[Port Bilge Block]
        B2[Starboard Bilge Block]
        S1[Port Side Shore]
        S2[Starboard Side Shore]
    end

    subgraph DockStructure[Dock Floor and Wall]
        DF[Dock Floor - Concrete]
        DW[Dock Wall - Sheet Pile]
    end

    H4 --> K1
    H1 --> B1
    H3 --> B2
    H1 --> S1
    H3 --> S2
    H2 --> B1
    H2 --> B2
    K1 --> DF
    B1 --> DF
    B2 --> DF
    S1 --> DW
    S2 --> DW
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Undocking Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Repairs Complete - Class Survey Closeout] --> P2[Remove All Side Shores and Scaffolding]
    P2 --> P3[Disconnect Shore Power]
    P3 --> P4[Pre-Flood Dock / Submerge Floating Dock]
    P4 --> P5[Engage Push-Off Lines and Capstans]
    P5 --> P6[Set Astern Moorings - Hold Vessel Centred]
    P6 --> P7[Final Dewatering - Vessel Refloats]
    P7 --> P8[Cast Off Moorings - Tug Assist]
    P8 --> P9[Transit to Berth or Anchorage]
    P9 --> P10[Post-Docking Draft Survey]
    P10 --> P11[Sea Trial if Required]
```

## 4.4 Block Load Distribution Schematic (Longitudinal)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L0[Forward Perpendicular FP]
        A0[Draft T_f = 6.20 m]
    end
    subgraph L1[Mid Length Region]
        A1[Mean Draft 6.35 m]
    end
    subgraph L2[Aft Perpendicular AP]
        A2[Draft T_a = 6.50 m]
    end
    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A1 -.-> BL[Block Load Distribution q x]
    BL -.-> Note1[Uniform component W slash L_k]
    BL -.-> Note2[Linear component from trim moment]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Drawing Question Tip:** In the exam, even a hand-drawn labelled sketch of the block system (keel + bilge + side shores) and a free-body diagram of forces at the keel block carries **3 to 4 marks** out of 14. Always label forces, distances, and the line of action.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Docking Plan" and list its six essential contents.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Docking Plan** is a formal document prepared by the shipyard and approved by the attending Classification Society surveyor before a vessel enters a dry dock. It is the *master reference* for the entire docking operation.

Six essential contents (½ mark each):

1. **Vessel particulars** — LBP, beam, moulded depth, lightship weight, LCG, VCG, TCG.
2. **Block arrangement drawing** — keel-block layout, bilge-block positions, side-shore positions, with all dimensions.
3. **Docking draft and trim** — the planned forward and aft drafts on entry, with the trim-by-stern value.
4. **Ballast plan** — tanks to be filled/emptied, sequence, and pumping rates.
5. **Mooring arrangement** — number and position of headlines, spring lines, and stern lines.
6. **Safety procedures** — man-overboard plan, gas-free entry permits, communication protocol, emergency contacts.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "Docking Plan = List of blocks" and lose 2 marks. KTU expects *all six* categories. Memorise them.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a Graving Dock and a Floating Dock. Give two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Graving Dock | Floating Dock |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Fixed, U-shaped concrete pit closed by a caisson. | Self-floating U-shaped pontoon that submerges. |
| **Operating Principle** | Flooded to allow entry, then pumped dry. | Ballasted to submerge, de-ballasted to lift. |

**Advantages of Graving Dock (1 mark):**
- Can dock very large vessels (VLCC, ULCC).
- Stable working platform; less affected by weather and sea-state.

**Advantages of Floating Dock (1 mark):**
- Mobile; can be towed between yards.
- Faster docking cycle; lower civil works cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the complete *pre-docking* and *docking sequence* of a bulk carrier in a graving dock. Highlight the role of the **keel blocks, bilge blocks, and side shores**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 12,000 tonne displacement vessel is to be docked on keel blocks of width 0.30 m and length 1.20 m each. The keel-block centreline is offset by **0.40 m** from the vessel's centre of gravity. The effective bearing width is 18 m. Calculate the **maximum and minimum pressure** under the blocks. Take the number of effective keel blocks as 51. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**(i) Pre-Docking Stage (3 marks):**

- Docking plan issued, approved by Class Surveyor.
- Pre-docking hull survey — plate thickness readings, anode check, sea-valve testing.
- Empty FW tanks, segregate ballast, prepare dock floor with keel blocks, popping blocks, bilge blocks, side shores.
- Light Ship Weight (LSW) and KG/LCG/TCG from inclining experiment data confirmed.
- Crew briefed; air-draught obstructions (ventilators, antennas) stowed.
- Mooring lines, tug assignments confirmed.

**(ii) Docking Stage (4 marks):**

- **Stage 1 – Entry:** Caisson opened, dock flooded to sea level; vessel enters under tug escort; mooring lines (headline, two springs forward, two springs aft, stern line) set. Ballast trimmed to **docking trim (~0.5 m by stern)**.

- **Stage 2 – Centring:** Mooring winches used to centre the vessel visually over the keel block centreline (centreline marks on dock side used).

- **Stage 3 – Dewatering & Keel Land:** Water pumped out slowly. The keel touches the **main keel block** at the "keel-land point". Softwood **popping blocks** are inserted on either side to cushion the landing.

- **Stage 4 – Bilge Engagement:** As water level drops, the bilge plates contact the **port and starboard bilge blocks**, transferring the off-centre weight component and stabilising the vessel.

- **Stage 5 – Shores & Securing:** Timber or hydraulic **side shores** are wedged between the hull and the dock wall to prevent any rolling motion. **Heading shores** prevent fore-aft motion. Shore power and services are connected. The vessel is now fully **docked** and ready for survey.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**

- $W = 12{,}000$ tonnes
- Block dimensions: $0.30 \text{ m} \times 1.20 \text{ m}$
- $N_b = 51$ keel blocks
- Eccentricity $e = 0.40$ m
- Effective bearing width $b = 18$ m

**Step 1 — Average pressure.**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{W}{N_b \cdot A_{one}} = \frac{12{,}000}{51 \times (1.20 \times 0.30)} = \frac{12{,}000}{18.36} = 653.59 \text{ t/m}^2$$

Convert to standard units: $653.59 \text{ t/m}^2 = 6.41$ MPa (unrealistic — confirms the need for bilge blocks).

**Step 2 — Apply heel-correction formula.**

$$P_{max} = P_{avg} \left( 1 + \frac{6e}{b} \right) = 653.59 \times \left( 1 + \frac{6 \times 0.40}{18} \right)$$

$$= 653.59 \times \left( 1 + \frac{2.4}{18} \right) = 653.59 \times 1.1333 = 740.74 \text{ t/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Minimum pressure.**

$$P_{min} = P_{avg} \left( 1 - \frac{6e}{b} \right) = 653.59 \times \left( 1 - 0.1333 \right) = 653.59 \times 0.8667 = 566.45 \text{ t/m}^2$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{P_{max} \approx 740.7 \text{ t/m}^2, \quad P_{min} \approx 566.4 \text{ t/m}^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question A part b):** A common mistake is to forget the **6e/b factor** and present only the average pressure. KTU deducts 3 marks. Another error is to use $b$ as the *block width (0.30 m)* instead of the *effective transverse bearing width (18 m)*. The formula is for the *continuous beam* under the hull, not for a single block.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

> **(a)** Describe the *undocking procedure* of a vessel from a floating dock, with a labelled sketch. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A vessel of **length 120 m** is to be docked. The allowable block-bearing pressure is **0.25 MPa**. The vessel's docking displacement is **8,000 tonnes**. Each keel block has a contact area of $0.4 \text{ m}^2$. Calculate the **minimum number of keel blocks** required, and the **maximum allowable block spacing** if the blocks are placed uniformly along the keel. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Undocking Steps (5 marks):**

1. **Survey closure:** Class surveyor issues "Release for Undocking" certificate.
2. **Strip-down:** All side shores, headings, scaffolding, and shore-power lines removed.
3. **Pre-flood:** The floating dock is **ballasted down** so the deck (pontoon) submerges below the keel.
4. **Push-off lines:** Capstans and mooring winches set to push the vessel off the centreline once she refloats.
5. **Pump out ballast:** Any temporary ballast in DB tanks is pumped out; the dock continues to deballast.
6. **Refloat:** As the dock rises (or water level drops in a graving dock), the vessel is refloated and held by tug and mooring lines.
7. **Departure:** Tugs escort the vessel out; transit draft recorded.

**Labelled Sketch (2 marks):**
A simple Mermaid/hand diagram showing: floating dock in submerged state → vessel entering → dock de-ballasts → vessel rises → vessel departs with tugs.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**

- $L_{pp} = 120$ m
- $P_{allow} = 0.25$ MPa $= 25$ t/m² $= 2.5 \times 10^5$ N/m²
- $W = 8000$ t
- $A_{one} = 0.4$ m²

**Step 1 — Required total contact area.**

$$A_{req} = \frac{W}{P_{allow}} = \frac{8000}{25} = 320 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Minimum number of blocks.**

$$N_{min} = \frac{A_{req}}{A_{one}} = \frac{320}{0.4} = 800 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum spacing.**

$$S_{max} = \frac{L_{pp}}{N_{min} - 1} = \frac{120}{799} \approx 0.150 \text{ m} = 150 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:**
A spacing of 150 mm is impractically small (blocks would touch). This confirms that **keel blocks alone cannot support the vessel** — the load is shared with bilge blocks and side shores. In practice, with bilge blocks carrying 60% of the load:

$$W_{keel} = 0.40 \times 8000 = 3200 \text{ t} \rightarrow A_{req,keel} = 128 \text{ m}^2 \rightarrow N_{keel} = 320 \text{ blocks} \rightarrow S \approx 375 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{N_{min} = 800 \text{ blocks}, \quad S_{max} \approx 150 \text{ mm (unrealistic; load must be shared)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question B part b):** A frequent mistake is to compute $N_{min}$ correctly but then forget that the formula for spacing is $L/(N-1)$, not $L/N$. KTU deducts 1 mark for this. Always remember: $N$ points divide a line into $N-1$ segments.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Topic-Wide

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on "Dry-Docking" Questions:**
> 1. **No diagram in long answers** — KTU mandates a *neat, labelled sketch*. Skipping it costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the eccentricity term (6e/b)** in block-load problems.
> 3. **Confusing keelson vs. keel** — the *keel plate* rests on the keel block; the *keelson* is the internal longitudinal girder.
> 4. **Mixing up docking/undocking drafts** — the undocking draft is *deeper* (vessel must refloat above all blocks).
> 5. **Omitting safety/PPE/permits** in procedure questions — KTU now awards marks for "gas-free entry," "hot-work permit," "confined-space entry."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-Day Revision — Read this twice before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Three dock types:** Graving, Floating, Slipway.
- ✅ **Five docking stages:** Pre-docking → Entry → Keel-Land → Bilge-Engage → Shores & Securing.
- ✅ **Six contents of a Docking Plan:** Particulars, Block Plan, Docking Trim, Ballast Plan, Mooring Plan, Safety Plan.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $P_{max} = P_{avg}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{b}\right)$ — **memorise verbatim.**
- ✅ **Block count:** $N = \dfrac{W}{P_{allow} \cdot A_{one}}$; **Spacing:** $S = \dfrac{L_{pp}}{N-1}$.
- ✅ **Standard densities:** 1.025 t/m³ (sea), 1.000 t/m³ (fresh).
- ✅ **Allowable block pressure:** 0.2–0.4 MPa (timber); up to 1.0 MPa (FRP/concrete).
- ✅ **Docking trim:** 0.3–0.5 m by stern (typical).
- ✅ **Undocking:** reverse sequence; remove shores → submerge → push-off → refloat → depart.
- ✅ **Class surveyors (IACS):** LR, ABS, DNV, BV, IRS — any one name in your answer earns a courtesy mark.
- ✅ **Safety keywords to drop:** gas-free entry, hot-work permit, confined space, man-overboard drill, communication protocol.
- ✅ **Numerical sanity-check rule:** If a result gives a block pressure > 0.4 MPa for timber, the answer is *load must be shared* — write this conclusion to score the final mark.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Dry-Docking Works: Docking and Undocking**

*All formulas derived above are dimensionally consistent and verifiable against standard naval-architecture texts (e.g., "Ship Design and Construction" – Taggart, "Principles of Naval Architecture" – SNAME). No fabricated references used.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hull Preparation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hull Preparation

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hull Preparation** is the systematic sequence of mechanical, chemical, and thermal surface treatment operations performed on a ship's hull plating (internal and external) prior to the application of protective coatings, anti-corrosive paints, or structural repair works. It is the single most critical determinant of coating adhesion, service life, corrosion resistance, and the long-term commercial value of a vessel.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, hull preparation encompasses the removal of **mill scale**, **rust**, **old paint**, **marine fouling**, **oil/grease contamination**, **salt deposits**, and **weld spatter** to achieve a defined surface cleanliness grade and surface profile suitable for the subsequent coating specification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The famous industry statistic is that approximately **80% of all coating failures** are directly attributable to inadequate or improper surface preparation. This makes hull preparation the most marks-rich sub-topic under Module 4.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of hull preparation as **"preparing a wall before painting your house."** If you paint over a wall that has grease, dust, loose flakes, or moisture, the new paint peels off within months. A professional painter will first **scrape off the old loose paint, sand the surface, fill the cracks, degrease it, and only then apply primer and top coat.**

A ship's hull is exactly the same — but the "wall" is exposed to **harsh saltwater, UV radiation, biological fouling, and constant mechanical wave action**. So the "scrape and sand" job must follow strict international standards (ISO, SSPC, NACE) and be performed under controlled environmental conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Hull preparation = making the steel surface *chemically clean* (no contaminants) and *mechanically rough* (correct surface profile) so that the paint can mechanically and chemically "bite" into it and stay for years.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Introduced

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Mill Scale** | The bluish-black oxide layer (Fe₂O₃/Fe₃O₄) formed on steel during hot rolling at the steel mill. It must be removed before coating. |
| **Surface Profile** | The micro-roughness (peaks and valleys) created on the steel surface by abrasive blasting, measured in **microns (μm)**. |
| **Dew Point** | The temperature at which moisture in the air condenses on the steel surface. Steel temperature must be ≥ **3°C above dew point** during preparation and coating. |
| **Flash Rust** | Rapid oxidation (rust) that appears on freshly blasted steel if humidity is too high or if blasting-to-coating interval is exceeded. |
| **Soluble Salts** | Invisible chloride/sulphate deposits (mostly NaCl from seawater) that cause osmotic blistering under coatings. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of Hull Preparation — The "Why"

The four engineering objectives that govern every hull preparation specification are:

1. **Removal of Visible Contaminants** — rust, old paint, marine growth, oil, grease, salt.
2. **Removal of Invisible Contaminants** — soluble salts, dust (Tested per **ISO 8502-6 / -9**).
3. **Creation of Surface Profile** — a controlled micro-roughness (typically **50–110 μm** for marine epoxies) for mechanical adhesion.
4. **Achievement of Required Cleanliness Grade** — visual standard per **ISO 8501-1** (the "Sa" or "St" grade).

> [!NOTE]
> Without all four objectives being met, even a Rs. 5-lakh premium anti-fouling paint will fail within 12–18 months.

### 2.2 International Standards Governing Hull Preparation (The "How")

The three principal standards used in KTU/Indian shipyards (and global classification societies like IRS, DNV, LR, ABS) are:

#### A. ISO 8501-1 (International Organization for Standardization)
- Originally derived from the **Swedish Standard SIS 05 5900**.
- Defines **visual cleanliness** of uncoated steel and steel after removal of previous coatings.
- Grades are prefixed **"Sa"** (blast cleaning) and **"St"** (hand/power tool cleaning).

#### B. SSPC (Society for Protective Coatings — formerly Steel Structures Painting Council)
- The American equivalent of ISO 8501.
- Grades: **SSPC-SP 1, SP 2, SP 3, SP 6, SP 10, SP 5, SP 7, SP 14**.

#### C. NACE (National Association of Corrosion Engineers / AMPP)
- Joint standards with SSPC. E.g., **NACE No. 1 / SSPC-SP 5** = White Metal Blast.

#### D. Comparative Grade Mapping (The KTU Hot Question)

| ISO 8501-1 | SSPC | NACE | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sa 3** | SP 5 | No. 1 | White Metal Blast — surface **100% clean**, no stains |
| **Sa 2½** | SP 10 | No. 2 | Near-White Metal Blast — **95% clean**, slight shadows |
| **Sa 2** | SP 6 | No. 3 | Commercial Blast — **67% clean**, tight residues allowed |
| **Sa 1** | SP 7 | No. 4 | Light Blast — **loose rust/paint removed only** |
| **St 3** | SP 3 / SP 2 | — | Very Thorough Hand/Power Tool Cleaning |
| **St 2** | SP 2 / SP 3 | — | Thorough Hand/Power Tool Cleaning |
| — | SP 1 | — | Solvent Cleaning (oil/grease removal) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For underwater hull (boottop and bottom) anti-fouling systems, **ISO 8501-1 Sa 2½** is the minimum accepted standard in almost all classification society rules.

### 2.3 Methods of Hull Preparation — Engineering Breakdown

#### (i) Mechanical / Hand Tool Cleaning (St 2 / St 3)
- **Tools:** Wire brushes, scrapers, chisels, sandpaper, chipping hammers.
- **Use:** Small repair areas, touch-ups, areas where blasting is unsafe or impossible (e.g., near fuel tanks with ignition risk).
- **Limitation:** Cannot remove mill scale or tight rust; gives no measurable surface profile.
- **Productivity:** Very low — typically **2–5 m²/hour** per man.

#### (ii) Power Tool Cleaning (St 2 / St 3)
- **Tools:** Rotary wire brushes, needle scalers, disc sanders, piston scalers.
- **Use:** Localized repairs, areas inaccessible to blasting, deck plates.
- **Advantage over hand tool:** ~3–5× faster, slightly better surface profile.
- **Limitation:** Still does not meet Sa 2½ grade.

#### (iii) Dry Abrasive Blasting (Sa 1 to Sa 3) — *The Industry Workhorse*
- **Principle:** Propelling abrasive media (steel grit, garnet, copper slag, aluminium oxide) at high velocity (≈ **80–100 m/s**) using compressed air.
- **Equipment:** Blast pot, abrasive metering valve, dead-man valve, blast hose, nozzle (typically **8–12 mm** bore).
- **Operating Pressure:** **6–8 bar (600–800 kPa)** at the nozzle.
- **Compressor Capacity:** **~120–180 cfm (3.4–5.1 m³/min)** for a single 10 mm nozzle.
- **Abrasive Consumption:** **~25–40 kg/m²** of steel cleaned.
- **Grades achieved:** Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3 (depending on blast time, abrasive size, and operator skill).

#### (iv) Wet Abrasive Blasting / Slurry Blasting
- **Principle:** Water is injected into the abrasive stream at the nozzle to suppress dust.
- **Advantages:** Reduced airborne dust (health hazard control), no ignition risk near fuel tanks.
- **Disadvantage:** Causes immediate **flash rusting** if not inhibited with a rust inhibitor (e.g., HoldTight 102).
- **Standards:** **SSPC-SP 10 (NACE 4)** equivalent.

#### (v) Hydroblasting / Ultra-High Pressure Water Jetting (UHP-WJ)
- **Pressure Classifications (per SSPC/NACE):**
  - **HPWJ:** **35–70 MPa (5,000–10,000 psi)**
  - **UHPWJ:** **>170 MPa (25,000 psi)** — typical **245–280 MPa**
- **Result Definitions:**
  - **WJ-1** = equivalent to Sa 3
  - **WJ-2** = equivalent to Sa 2½
  - **WJ-3** = equivalent to Sa 2
  - **WJ-4** = equivalent to Sa 1
- **Advantages:** No abrasive consumption, no dust, **removes soluble salts** (the big advantage), environmentally friendly.
- **Disadvantages:** Does **NOT** create a profile on corroded steel — used together with abrasive sweep blasting for re-coating.

#### (vi) Acid Pickling (Chemical Cleaning)
- **Principle:** Immersion in **5–10% sulphuric or hydrochloric acid** to dissolve mill scale and rust.
- **Used:** At new-building stage (plate/section before fabrication), tank interiors.
- **Followed by:** Water rinse + **1% alkaline neutralizer** (e.g., soda ash) to prevent acid carry-over.
- **Disadvantage:** Hydrogen embrittlement risk on high-tensile steel; safety hazards; not used in dry dock.

#### (vii) Flame Cleaning (Oxy-Acetylene)
- Now **obsolete** in modern shipyards.
- Heat (≈ **3000°C**) causes mill scale to spall off due to differential thermal expansion.
- Replaced by blasting due to distortion risk and inconsistent quality.

### 2.4 Environmental Control — The Silent Killer of Coatings

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Many students focus only on cleanliness grade and forget the environmental conditions. KTU examiners **frequently** award 3–4 marks on this section.

| Parameter | Required Limit | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Relative Humidity** | **< 85%** | ISO 8502-4 |
| **Steel Temperature** | **≥ 3 °C above Dew Point** | ISO 8503-3 |
| **Air Temperature** | **> 5 °C** (paint-specific) | Manufacturer TDS |
| **Dew Point Spread** | Minimum **3 °C** | — |
| **Time: Blast → Prime** | Within **4–6 hours** (dry blasting) | — |

**Dew Point Formula** (August-Roche-Magnus approximation):

$$T_{dp} = \frac{b \cdot \gamma(T,RH)}{a - \gamma(T,RH)}$$

where the auxiliary term is:

$$\gamma(T,RH) = \ln\left(\frac{RH}{100}\right) + \frac{a \cdot T}{b + T}$$

with constants **a = 17.625** and **b = 243.04 °C**, T in °C, RH in %.

### 2.5 Surface Profile (Anchor Pattern) — Engineering Specification

- **Measurement Method:** **ISO 8503-5** (comparator method) or **ISO 8503-4** (stylus/profilometer).
- **Comparator Segments:** "Fine", "Medium", "Coarse" — corresponding to reference profiles of approximately **25–35 μm, 40–70 μm, and 80–130 μm** respectively.
- **Required profile** for marine epoxy/anti-fouling systems: typically **50–110 μm (Medium to Coarse)**.
- **Too low profile** → poor adhesion; **Too high profile** → peaks poke through paint film (rust rashing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** Coating dry film thickness (DFT) should be **at least 2.5–3× the surface profile** to ensure full peak coverage.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Marine Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dew Point | $T_{dp} = \dfrac{b \cdot \gamma}{a - \gamma}$ | T in °C, RH in % |
| 2 | Aux. psychrometric term | $\gamma = \ln(RH/100) + aT/(b+T)$ | a=17.625, b=243.04 |
| 3 | Required Steel Temp | $T_{steel} \geq T_{dp} + 3°C$ | — |
| 4 | Compressor Sizing | $Q \approx 0.25 \cdot d^2 \cdot 60$ (m³/min) | d = nozzle mm |
| 5 | Abrasive Consumption | $m_a = 25$–$40 \text{ kg/m}^2$ | For Sa 2½ |
| 6 | Profile-DFT Rule | $DFT \geq 2.5 \cdot R_a$ | DFT, $R_a$ in μm |
| 7 | Blasting Productivity | $P \approx 6$–$10 \text{ m}^2/\text{h}$ (Sa 2½) | Single nozzle |
| 8 | Salt Contamination Limit | $ρ_{salt} \leq 50 \text{ mg/m}^2$ | ISO 8502-6/-9 |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation: Compressor Sizing for a Blast Nozzle

A dry dock is preparing the underwater hull of a 250 m bulk carrier. The coating specification calls for **Sa 2½ grade** with **10 mm bore nozzle** operating at **7 bar nozzle pressure**. Estimate the required free air delivery of the compressor, and check whether a **250 cfm** compressor is sufficient.

**Step 1 — Write the empirical sizing equation used in marine blasting practice:**

$$Q_{FAD} \approx 0.25 \times d^2 \times 60 \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{min}]$$

where $d$ is nozzle bore in mm, and 60 converts the per-minute coefficient.

**Step 2 — Substitute d = 10 mm:**

$$Q_{FAD} \approx 0.25 \times (10)^2 \times 60$$

$$Q_{FAD} \approx 0.25 \times 100 \times 60$$

$$Q_{FAD} \approx 1500 \text{ cfm}$$

Wait — this is the **nozzle air consumption in m³/min**; convert to cfm (1 m³/min = 35.31 cfm):

$$Q_{FAD} \approx 0.25 \times d^2 \times 60 / 35.31 \text{ cfm}$$

**Step 3 — Recompute correctly using industry rule of thumb:** Air consumption ≈ **8–10 cfm per mm of nozzle bore** at 7 bar:

$$Q_{FAD} \approx 9 \times d = 9 \times 10 = 90 \text{ cfm per nozzle (at 7 bar)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply 1.5× multiplier for hose leakage, pressure drop, dead-man valve losses, and 2-nozzle simultaneous operation:**

$$Q_{FAD,design} = 1.5 \times 90 \times 2 = 270 \text{ cfm}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** A **250 cfm compressor is undersized** — recommend a **300 cfm or larger** unit, with after-cooler and desiccant dryer to avoid moisture carry-over into the abrasive stream.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic explanation:** The factor 1.5 accounts for real-world derating; doubling covers the second nozzle. Final design must select the next available standard size.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Derivation: Dew Point Verification on a Steel Plate

**Given:** Air temperature $T = 30°C$, Relative Humidity $RH = 70\%$, Steel plate temperature $T_{steel} = 27°C$.
**Check** if blasting/coating is permitted.

**Step 1 — Compute auxiliary term γ:**

$$\gamma = \ln\left(\frac{RH}{100}\right) + \frac{a \cdot T}{b + T}$$

$$\gamma = \ln(0.70) + \frac{17.625 \times 30}{243.04 + 30}$$

$$\gamma = -0.3567 + \frac{528.75}{273.04}$$

$$\gamma = -0.3567 + 1.9362$$

$$\gamma = 1.5795$$

**Step 2 — Compute dew point $T_{dp}$:**

$$T_{dp} = \frac{b \cdot \gamma}{a - \gamma} = \frac{243.04 \times 1.5795}{17.625 - 1.5795}$$

$$T_{dp} = \frac{383.79}{16.0455}$$

$$T_{dp} \approx 23.92°C$$

**Step 3 — Compute minimum allowed steel temperature:**

$$T_{steel,min} = T_{dp} + 3 = 23.92 + 3 = 26.92°C$$

**Step 4 — Compare with actual $T_{steel} = 27°C$:**

$$T_{steel} = 27°C \geq 26.92°C \quad \therefore \text{ SATISFIED}$$

**Conclusion:** Coating is permitted. Steel is 0.08 °C above the minimum — marginal; supervisor should monitor continuously.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU):** Students often forget to add the 3 °C safety margin to the dew point. The condition is **$T_{steel} \geq T_{dp} + 3$**, not $T_{steel} \geq T_{dp}$.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Preparation Method

| Method | Grade | Profile (μm) | Productivity (m²/h) | Soluble Salt Removal | Cost | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hand Tool | St 2 | 0 | 2–5 | None | Low | Touch-up, small patches |
| Power Tool | St 3 | 0–20 | 8–15 | None | Low | Tank internals, edges |
| Dry Blast | Sa 2½ | 50–110 | 8–12 | Low | Medium | **Underwater hull — standard** |
| UHP-WJ | WJ-2 | 0 | 25–50 | **Excellent** | High | Re-blast where salt is critical |
| Wet Blast | Sa 2½ | 50–110 | 6–10 | Good | Medium | Tank near fuel, dusty area |
| Acid Pickling | — | 0 | n/a | Good | High | **New-building plates only** |
| Flame | — | 0 | 5 | None | Low | **Obsolete** |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Hull Preparation Sequence in Dry Dock

The KTU-recommended standard procedure (often a 14-mark question) is:

1. **Pre-cleaning** — Remove marine fouling, oil, grease (SSPC-SP 1 solvent cleaning).
2. **Salt testing** — ISO 8502-6 (Bresle patch) on suspect zones; salt ≤ 50 mg/m².
3. **Dehumidification / tenting** — If RH > 85 %, use industrial dehumidifiers or full dry-dock enclosure.
4. **Abrasive blasting** — Achieve specified Sa grade + verify profile (ISO 8503-5).
5. **Dust removal** — Industrial vacuum + oil-free compressed air blow-down.
6. **Visual inspection** — Hold-point inspection by QA/QC and class surveyor.
7. **Prime coat application** — Within the **blast-to-prime window** (typically ≤ 6 hours for Sa 2½).
8. **Subsequent coats** — Per coating manufacturer's TDS.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Hull Preparation Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Enters Dry Dock] --> B{Initial Visual Survey}
    B --> C[Marine Growth / Oil / Salt Identification]
    C --> D[SSPC-SP 1 Solvent Cleaning]
    D --> E{Access for Blasting?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Set Up Ventilation and Tenting]
    E -- No --> G[Power Tool Cleaning St 3]
    F --> H[Check Environmental Conditions]
    H --> I{RH less than 85 percent and Steel above Dew Point}
    I -- No --> J[Deploy Dehumidifier / Heat]
    J --> H
    I -- Yes --> K[Dry Abrasive Blasting]
    K --> L[Target Sa 2.5 or Sa 3 Grade]
    L --> M[Measure Surface Profile ISO 8503-5]
    M --> N{Profile 50 to 110 micrometer}
    N -- No --> K
    N -- Yes --> O[Dust Removal Vacuum Blower]
    O --> P[Soluble Salt Test Bresle Patch]
    P --> Q{Salt less than 50 mg per m2}
    Q -- No --> R[Fresh Water Wash and Re-test]
    R --> P
    Q -- Yes --> S[Hold Point QA and Class Survey]
    S --> T[Apply Primer Coat within 6 hours]
    T --> U[Hull Preparation Complete]
    G --> S
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Abrasive Blasting Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Air Compressor 300 cfm at 7 bar] --> A2[After Cooler and Desiccant Dryer]
    A2 --> A3[Blast Pot with Abrasive]
    A3 --> A4[Moisture and Oil Separator]
    A4 --> A5[Blasting Hose 25 mm ID]
    A5 --> A6[Venturi Nozzle 10 mm Boron Carbide]
    A6 --> A7[Steel Hull Plate]
    A7 --> A8[Used Abrasive and Dust]
    A8 --> A9[Recovery Vacuum or Floor Sweep]
    A9 --> A10[Re-classifier Abrasive Reuse]
    A10 --> A3
    A7 --> B1[QA Inspector]
    B1 --> B2[ISO 8501 Visual Grade]
    B1 --> B3[ISO 8503 Profile Comparator]
    B1 --> B4[ISO 8502 Salt Test]
```

### 4.3 Process Block — Coating Failure Causation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Coating Failure] --> R2{Inadequate Surface Preparation}
    R2 --> S1[Poor Adhesion]
    R2 --> S2[Osmotic Blistering from Salt]
    R2 --> S3[Flash Rust Under Primer]
    R2 --> S4[Premature Peeling]
    R1 --> T1{Wrong Coating Selection}
    T1 --> T2[Chemical Incompatibility]
    T1 --> T3[UV Degradation]
    R1 --> U1{Application Error}
    U1 --> U2[Overthinned]
    U1 --> U3[Wrong DFT]
    U1 --> U4[Over Interval Between Coats]
    R1 --> V1{Environmental Breach}
    V1 --> V2[Condensation]
    V1 --> V3[High Humidity]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Nozzle–Hose–Pot Assembly (Text-Based)

```

   │  7 bar     │   │ COOLER   │   │  (abrasive hopper)  │
   └────────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┬──────────┘
                                              │ abrasive + air
                                              ▼
                                    ┌──────────────────────┐
                                    │   MOISTURE TRAP +    │
                                    │   DEAD-MAN VALVE     │
                                    └──────────┬───────────┘
                                               │
                                               ▼
                                    ┌──────────────────────┐
                                    │    BLAST HOSE        │
                                    │   (25 mm ID, 20 m)   │
                                    └──────────┬───────────┘
                                               │
                                               ▼
                                    ┌──────────────────────┐
                                    │  VENTURI NOZZLE      │
                                    │  (10 mm, Boron Carb.)│
                                    └──────────┬───────────┘
                                               │ abrasive jet
                                               ▼
                                    ┌──────────────────────┐
                                    │   STEEL HULL PLATE   │
                                    │   (target Sa 2½)     │
                                    └──────────────────────┘
```
