<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the four major surface preparation grades specified by ISO 8501-1 for blast cleaning and write a one-line description of each.*

**Model Answer:**

ISO 8501-1 (originally Swedish Standard SIS 05 5900) specifies four blast cleaning grades:

- **Sa 1 — Light Blast Cleaning:** Loose mill scale, rust, loose paint and foreign matter removed; tight residues remain.
- **Sa 2 — Commercial Blast Cleaning:** At least **67%** of every square metre free from all visible residues.
- **Sa 2½ — Near-White Metal Blast Cleaning:** At least **95%** free; only slight shadows/streaks permitted. *Most common for underwater hull.*
- **Sa 3 — White Metal Blast Cleaning:** **100%** free; uniform metallic finish. *Used for cargo oil tanks, chemical carriers.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "Dew Point" in the context of hull preparation. State the rule relating steel temperature to dew point before blasting.*

**Model Answer:**

**Dew Point** is the temperature at which air becomes saturated with moisture and condensation begins to form on a surface. In hull preparation, the **steel temperature must always be at least 3 °C above the measured dew point** during blasting, inspection, and coating application. This 3 °C safety margin prevents invisible moisture films from forming on freshly cleaned steel, which would cause **poor adhesion, flash rust, and osmotic blistering** of the coating.

---

### Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering Part B questions on hull preparation, students typically lose marks in three places: (1) forgetting to **state the ISO/SSPC grade by name** (e.g., Sa 2½), (2) omitting the **environmental condition check** (dew point, humidity), and (3) skipping the **blast-to-prime window** (4–6 hours). Always include all three.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **International Standards governing hull surface preparation** — ISO 8501, SSPC, and NACE — with a comparative grade mapping table. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Describe the **Dry Abrasive Blasting** procedure for ship hull, including equipment, abrasive types, operating parameters, productivity, and the Sa grades achievable. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) International Standards:**

The three governing standards are:

1. **ISO 8501-1:2007** — International standard for visual assessment of surface cleanliness of uncoated steel and steel after coating removal. Derived from Swedish Standard SIS 05 5900. Defines grades **Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3** (blast) and **St 2, St 3** (tool).
2. **SSPC (Society for Protective Coatings)** — American standard; grades **SP 1 to SP 14**. SP 1 is solvent cleaning; SP 5 = White Metal; SP 10 = Near-White.
3. **NACE (now AMPP)** — Jointly publishes with SSPC. E.g., **NACE No. 2 / SSPC-SP 10** = Sa 2½.

**Comparative Mapping Table:**

| ISO 8501-1 | SSPC | NACE | Visual Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sa 3 | SP 5 | No. 1 | White metal — 100% clean |
| Sa 2½ | SP 10 | No. 2 | Near-white — 95% clean |
| Sa 2 | SP 6 | No. 3 | Commercial — 67% clean |
| Sa 1 | SP 7 | No. 4 | Light blast — loose only |
| St 3 | SP 3 | — | Very thorough power tool |
| St 2 | SP 2 | — | Thorough hand tool |
| — | SP 1 | — | Solvent cleaning |

**(b) Dry Abrasive Blasting Procedure:**

**Principle:** Abrasive particles are accelerated through a Venturi nozzle using compressed air (≈ 80–100 m/s) and impact the steel surface, removing rust/scale/paint by impact and abrasion.

**Equipment Train:**
- Air compressor (≈ 6–8 bar, 90 cfm per nozzle)
- After-cooler and desiccant dryer
- Blast pot (200–300 L capacity) with abrasive metering valve
- Moisture-oil separator
- Blast hose (25 mm ID, antistatic)
- Venturi nozzle (8–12 mm bore, boron carbide for longevity)

**Abrasives (Marine Yard Use):**
- **Steel grit / steel shot** — recyclable, 2–3% consumption
- **Copper slag** — cheapest, single-use, dark dust
- **Garnet** — low dust, good profile
- **Aluminium oxide** — for stainless, high purity

**Operating Parameters:**
- Nozzle pressure: **6–8 bar**
- Nozzle-to-surface distance: **200–400 mm**
- Nozzle angle: **60°–90°** to surface
- Abrasive consumption: **25–40 kg/m²** for Sa 2½
- Productivity: **8–12 m²/hour per nozzle** for Sa 2½

**Grades Achievable:** All four — Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3 — depending on operator skill, abrasive, and time invested.

**Inspection:** Visual per ISO 8501-1; profile per ISO 8503-5 (comparator or profilometer, 50–110 μm typical).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Discuss the **environmental conditions** that must be controlled during hull preparation. Derive the relation between steel temperature, dew point, and humidity using the August-Roche-Magnus formula. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** What is **Ultra-High Pressure Water Jetting (UHP-WJ)**? Compare it with dry abrasive blasting in terms of surface profile, soluble salt removal, productivity, and environmental impact. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Environmental Conditions:**

The four critical parameters are:

1. **Relative Humidity (RH):** Must be < **85%** (per ISO 8502-4). Above this, moisture condenses on cold steel.
2. **Steel Temperature:** Must be ≥ **3 °C** above dew point — this 3 °C safety margin is a **classification society requirement**.
3. **Air Temperature:** Typically > **5 °C** (paint-specific; check TDS).
4. **Surface Condition:** Free from oil, grease, soluble salts, dust.

**Dew Point Derivation (August-Roche-Magnus):**

The saturation vapour pressure at temperature T (°C) is:

$$E(T) = 6.1094 \cdot e^{\frac{17.625 \cdot T}{243.04 + T}} \quad [\text{hPa}]$$

The actual vapour pressure is:

$$E_{act} = \frac{RH}{100} \cdot E(T)$$

The dew point $T_{dp}$ is the temperature at which $E(T_{dp}) = E_{act}$:

$$\frac{6.1094 \cdot RH}{100} \cdot e^{\frac{17.625 T}{243.04 + T}} = 6.1094 \cdot e^{\frac{17.625 T_{dp}}{243.04 + T_{dp}}}$$

Solving yields the working formula:

$$T_{dp} = \frac{243.04 \cdot \left[\ln(RH/100) + \dfrac{17.625 T}{243.04 + T}\right]}{17.625 - \left[\ln(RH/100) + \dfrac{17.625 T}{243.04 + T}\right]}$$

**Acceptance Criterion:**

$$T_{steel} \geq T_{dp} + 3°C \quad \text{(MANDATORY)}$$

**Numerical Illustration:** T = 30 °C, RH = 70% → T_dp ≈ 23.9 °C → T_steel must be ≥ **26.9 °C**.

**(b) UHP-WJ vs Dry Abrasive Blasting:**

| Parameter | Dry Abrasive Blast | UHP-WJ |
|---|---|---|
| Working Pressure | 6–8 bar (air) | 245–280 MPa (water) |
| Surface Profile Created | **50–110 μm** | **0 μm (none)** on corroded steel |
| Soluble Salt Removal | Poor (residual salts in pits) | **Excellent (key advantage)** |
| Productivity (Sa 2½ / WJ-2) | 8–12 m²/h | 25–50 m²/h |
| Dust Generation | High (silicosis risk) | **None** |
| Abrasive Cost | 25–40 kg/m² | **Zero** |
| Waste Disposal | Spent abrasive + paint chips | Water + paint sludge (filtered) |
| Environmental | Dust, abrasive disposal issues | **Eco-friendly** |
| Flash Rust Risk | Low (if primer applied in time) | **High — needs inhibitor** |
| Typical Use | New steel, full re-blast | **Re-coating maintenance** |

**Conclusion:** UHP-WJ is preferred for **in-service re-blasting** where salt contamination is critical (e.g., chemical/product tankers). For **new steel** or **full re-blasting**, dry abrasive blasting is mandatory to create the required anchor profile.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Four objectives** of hull prep: remove visible contaminants, remove invisible contaminants, create surface profile, achieve cleanliness grade.
- **80% of coating failures** are due to poor surface preparation — hence Module 4's industrial weight.
- **ISO 8501-1** is the primary KTU-referenced standard; grades are **Sa 1 / Sa 2 / Sa 2½ / Sa 3** (blast) and **St 2 / St 3** (tool).
- **Sa 2½ is the marine underwater hull industry standard.**
- **SSPC-SP 5 = Sa 3; SSPC-SP 10 = Sa 2½; SSPC-SP 6 = Sa 2; SSPC-SP 1 = Solvent.**
- **Dew point rule:** $T_{steel} \geq T_{dp} + 3°C$ — never forget the 3 °C margin.
- **Surface profile** for marine epoxy systems: **50–110 μm**, measured per **ISO 8503-5**.
- **DFT ≥ 2.5 × Profile** is the engineering thumb rule.
- **Blast-to-prime window:** typically **≤ 6 hours** for Sa 2½ dry blasting.
- **Soluble salt limit:** ≤ **50 mg/m²** of chloride, tested by **ISO 8502-6 (Bresle patch)**.
- **RH < 85%** is mandatory; use dehumidifiers or full dock tenting if exceeded.
- **UHP-WJ** removes salts excellently but **creates no profile** — must be combined with sweep blasting for re-coating.
- **Acid pickling** is restricted to **new-building plates**; never in dry-dock.
- **Flame cleaning is obsolete.**
- **Compressor sizing** rule: **~9 cfm per mm of nozzle bore** at 7 bar; multiply by 1.5× for derating.
- **Abrasive consumption** for Sa 2½: **25–40 kg/m²**.
- **Inspection hold-point** must be witnessed by **class surveyor** (IRS/DNV/LR/ABS) before primer application.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hull Painting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hull Painting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hull Painting** is the systematic application of a multi-layered coating system on the external and internal (ballast tanks, void spaces) surfaces of a ship's hull to provide protection against **corrosion**, **marine fouling**, and **mechanical abrasion**, while simultaneously optimizing hydrodynamic performance and extending the operational service life of the vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Hull painting constitutes the primary *passive corrosion protection* methodology onboard a vessel. The coating system forms a dielectric barrier between the steel substrate and the aggressive marine environment (seawater, atmospheric salts, UV radiation).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the steel hull of a ship as the **human skin**. Just as skin protects the body from infection, sunburn, and dehydration, the paint system protects the steel from rust, barnacles, and the salty ocean. If the skin gets damaged (scratches, cracks), the underlying tissue (steel) becomes vulnerable to infection (corrosion). Therefore, regular maintenance painting is the *vaccination* that keeps the ship's body healthy and seaworthy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Decoded for KTU Students:**
> - **Substrate:** The base steel surface to be painted.
> - **Primer:** The first coat that bonds directly to the steel.
> - **Anti-Corrosive (AC) Paint:** Mid-coat that resists corrosion.
> - **Anti-Fouling (AF) Paint:** Outermost underwater coat that resists marine growth.
> - **Topcoat:** Final decorative and protective layer above the waterline.
> - **DFT (Dry Film Thickness):** Thickness of paint after solvent evaporation (typically measured in **micrometres (μm)** or **mils**).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Classification

The hull is broadly divided into **three distinct zones**, each requiring a different coating system:

| Hull Zone | Location | Primary Paint Function |
|-----------|----------|------------------------|
| **Underwater Hull** | Below the waterline | Anti-fouling + Anti-corrosion |
| **Boot Top** | At the load waterline | Anti-fouling + UV/abrasion resistance |
| **Topside** | Above the waterline | Cosmetic + Corrosion resistance |
| **Superstructure** | Above main deck | Cosmetic + Weatherproofing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Fact:** Approximately **60% to 70%** of all dry-docking expenditure is attributed to surface preparation and hull painting works, making it a critical survey topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Composition of Marine Paint

Marine paints are formulated complex mixtures. Each component has a specific engineering role:

- **Binder (Resin):** The film-forming polymer (e.g., epoxy, vinyl, polyurethane). It provides cohesion and adhesion.
- **Pigments:** Insoluble particles providing colour, opacity, and corrosion inhibition (e.g., zinc chromate, red oxide, cuprous oxide).
- **Solvents/Thinners:** Volatile liquids that reduce viscosity for application (e.g., xylene, toluene).
- **Additives:** Driers, anti-settling agents, biocides.

### 2.2 Hull Coating System Architecture (Layer-by-Layer)

The protective coating is built up as a stratified composite. KTU students must memorize this hierarchical sequence:

**Layer 1 – Surface Preparation (SSPC-SP 10 / Sa 2.5 Near-White Metal Blast)**
Removal of mill scale, rust, and old paint to achieve a defined surface profile (anchor pattern) of **75 μm to 100 μm**.

**Layer 2 – Primer Coat (Shop Primer / Holding Primer)**
Applied immediately after blasting. Typical DFT = **25 μm to 40 μm**. Common type: Zinc-rich epoxy or zinc silicate.

**Layer 3 – Anti-Corrosive (AC) Coats**
Typically **2 full coats** of epoxy-based AC paint. Each coat DFT = **125 μm to 150 μm**. Total AC system DFT ≈ **250 μm to 300 μm**.

**Layer 4 – Anti-Fouling (AF) Coat (Underwater only)**
Conventional or self-polishing copolymer (SPC) anti-fouling. Total DFT ≈ **150 μm to 300 μm** depending on trade route.

**Layer 5 – Topcoat (Above waterline)**
Polyurethane or acrylic finish for gloss, colour retention, and UV resistance. DFT ≈ **50 μm**.

### 2.3 Theoretical Framework – The Paint Engineering Equations

The following equations are **board-exam favourites**. Mastering them is mandatory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Paint Quantity Calculation (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet)**

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Hull Painting)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Wet Film Thickness (WFT) | $\text{WFT} = \dfrac{\text{DFT} \times 100}{\text{VS}\%}$ | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 2 | Dry Film Thickness (DFT) | $\text{DFT} = \dfrac{\text{WFT} \times \text{VS}\%}{100}$ | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 3 | Theoretical Spreading Rate | $\text{TSR} = \dfrac{10 \times \text{VS}\%}{\text{DFT}}$ | $\text{m}^2 / \text{L}$ |
| 4 | Practical Spreading Rate | $\text{PSR} = \text{TSR} \times \eta_{\text{transfer}}$ | $\text{m}^2 / \text{L}$ |
| 5 | Paint Volume Required | $V_{\text{paint}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{surface}}}{\text{PSR}}$ | $\text{Litres}$ |
| 6 | Total Paint for N Coats | $V_{\text{total}} = N \times V_{\text{paint}}$ | $\text{Litres}$ |
| 7 | Weight of Paint | $W = V_{\text{paint}} \times \rho_{\text{paint}}$ | $\text{kg}$ |
| 8 | Paint Loss Factor (overcoating) | $\text{Loss}\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{PSR}}{\text{TSR}}\right) \times 100$ | $\%$ |

> **Where:**
> - $\text{VS}\%$ = Volume Solids percentage of the paint (given on data sheet, typically 50–65% for epoxy).
> - $\eta_{\text{transfer}}$ = Transfer efficiency (Brush ≈ 0.90, Roller ≈ 0.85, Airless Spray ≈ 0.65 to 0.75).
> - $A_{\text{surface}}$ = Total area to be painted in $\text{m}^2$.

### 2.4 Practical Application Methodology

KTU recognizes **three primary application methods**. Each has distinct engineering trade-offs:

1. **Brush Application**
   - Lowest transfer efficiency but excellent for touch-ups and edges.
   - DFT control: visual + WFT comb.

2. **Roller Application**
   - Medium efficiency. Suitable for flat topside areas.
   - Cannot achieve high DFT in single pass.

3. **Airless Spray Application**
   - Industry standard for shipyards. Highest productivity.
   - Requires careful humidity control (Relative Humidity < **85%**, surface temp > **3°C** above dew point).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Engineering Utility:** Shipyard estimators use these formulas daily to prepare the **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** during dry-dock repair tenders. A 10% error in DFT estimation can lead to thousands of litres of paint waste per vessel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Theoretical Spreading Rate (TSR)

The relationship between paint volume and covered area is the foundation of paint estimation. Let us derive it rigorously.

**Given:**
A paint with Volume Solids = $\text{VS}\%$.
Required Dry Film Thickness = $\text{DFT}$ (in $\mu$m).

**Step 1: Define Volume Solids Concept.**
Volume Solids is the fraction of wet paint that remains as solid film after solvent evaporation.

$$
V_{\text{solid film}} = V_{\text{wet paint}} \times \dfrac{\text{VS}\%}{100}
$$

**Step 2: Express Volume of Paint Required for 1 m².**
The volume of solid film covering 1 $\text{m}^2$ at DFT thickness is:

$$
V_{\text{solid film}} = A \times \text{DFT} = 1\ \text{m}^2 \times \text{DFT}(\mu\text{m})
$$

**Step 3: Convert μm to metres for unit consistency.**
Since $1\ \text{m} = 10^6\ \mu\text{m}$:

$$
1\ \mu\text{m} = 10^{-6}\ \text{m}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{\text{solid film}} = 1 \times \text{DFT} \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Convert m³ to Litres.**
Since $1\ \text{m}^3 = 1000\ \text{L}$:

$$
V_{\text{solid film}} = \text{DFT} \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 = \text{DFT} \times 10^{-3}\ \text{L}
$$

**Step 5: Equate and Solve for Wet Paint Volume (W).**

$$
W \times \dfrac{\text{VS}\%}{100} = \text{DFT} \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
W = \dfrac{\text{DFT} \times 10^{-3} \times 100}{\text{VS}\%}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate Spreading Rate (Inverse of W).**
Spreading rate is area covered per litre. Since W is volume for 1 $\text{m}^2$:

$$
\text{TSR} = \dfrac{1}{W} = \dfrac{\text{VS}\%}{100 \times \text{DFT} \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\text{TSR} = \dfrac{\text{VS}\% \times 10^3}{100 \times \text{DFT}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TSR} = \dfrac{10 \times \text{VS}\%}{\text{DFT}}} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{L}}\right]
$$

This is the canonical formula used in KTU board examinations.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Pattern)

**Question:** A ship's underwater hull plate area to be painted with anti-fouling paint is **4500 m²**. The paint has a Volume Solids of **60%** and required DFT of **150 μm**. The transfer efficiency for airless spray is **70%**. Calculate:
(a) Theoretical Spreading Rate
(b) Practical Spreading Rate
(c) Total paint volume required for **2 full coats**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**(a) Theoretical Spreading Rate (TSR):**

$$
\text{TSR} = \dfrac{10 \times \text{VS}\%}{\text{DFT}}
$$

$$
\text{TSR} = \dfrac{10 \times 60}{150}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TSR} = 4.0\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}}
$$

**(b) Practical Spreading Rate (PSR):**

$$
\text{PSR} = \text{TSR} \times \eta_{\text{transfer}}
$$

$$
\text{PSR} = 4.0 \times 0.70
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PSR} = 2.8\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}}
$$

**(c) Total Paint Volume for 2 Coats:**

Volume per coat:

$$
V_{\text{coat}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{surface}}}{\text{PSR}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{coat}} = \dfrac{4500}{2.8}
$$

$$
V_{\text{coat}} = 1607.14\ \text{L}
$$

Total for 2 coats:

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 1607.14
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{total}} = 3214.29\ \text{L} \approx 3215\ \text{L}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Paint Application Methods

| Parameter | Brush | Roller | Airless Spray |
|-----------|-------|--------|---------------|
| Transfer Efficiency ($\eta$) | 0.90 | 0.85 | 0.65 to 0.75 |
| Productivity ($\text{m}^2$/hr) | 30 to 50 | 80 to 120 | 400 to 800 |
| Achievable DFT per coat | Low to Medium | Medium | High (up to 300 μm) |
| Surface Finish Quality | Excellent on edges | Good on flats | Excellent uniformity |
| Operator Skill Required | Basic | Basic | Advanced |
| Typical Use in Shipyard | Touch-up, edges, corners | Topsides, superstructure | Bulk hull plating |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Concept:** The **overlapping factor** (5–10%) must be added to total paint volume to account for stripe coats on edges, weld seams, and complex geometrical details.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hull Coating System Architecture (Layered Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the hierarchical build-up of the hull coating system from the substrate upward:

```mermaid
flowchart BT
    base1[Substrate Steel Plate Sa 2.5] --> base2[Anchor Profile 75 to 100 micrometres]
    base2 --> layer1[Primer Coat DFT 25 to 40 micrometres]
    layer1 --> layer2[Anti Corrosive Coat 1 DFT 125 to 150 micrometres]
    layer2 --> layer3[Anti Corrosive Coat 2 DFT 125 to 150 micrometres]
    layer3 --> branch{Service Zone}
    branch -->|Underwater| layer4a[Anti Fouling Coat DFT 150 to 300 micrometres]
    branch -->|Above Water| layer4b[Polyurethane Topcoat DFT 50 micrometres]
```

### 4.2 Hull Painting Process Flow (Survey Yard Operations)

This flowchart maps the end-to-end operational workflow followed during a typical dry-dock hull painting campaign:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Vessel Enters Dry Dock]) --> inspect1[Initial Hull Survey and Condition Assessment]
    inspect1 --> prep[Surface Preparation SSPC SP 10 Blast Cleaning]
    prep --> dust[Dust Removal and Dew Point Check]
    dust --> prime[Primer Application Stripe Coat First]
    prime --> ac1[Anti Corrosive Coat 1 Full Spray]
    ac1 --> ac2[Anti Corrosive Coat 2 Full Spray]
    ac2 --> zonecheck{Hull Zone}
    zonecheck -->|Underwater| af1[Anti Fouling Coat Application]
    zonecheck -->|Topside| tc1[Polyurethane Topcoat Application]
    af1 --> inspect2[DFT Measurement and Holiday Detection]
    tc1 --> inspect2
    inspect2 --> def{Coating Defects Found}
    def -->|Yes| repair[Localized Repair and Recoating]
    def -->|No| finish([Vessel Ready for Re Launch])
    repair --> inspect2
```

### 4.3 Hull Zone Paint System Selector (Decision Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    zonein([Select Hull Zone]) --> z1[Underwater Hull]
    zonein --> z2[Boot Top Zone]
    zonein --> z3[Topside and Superstructure]
    z1 --> sys1[AC Epoxy plus Anti Fouling SPC System]
    z2 --> sys2[AC Epoxy plus Strong AF plus PU Topcoat]
    z3 --> sys3[AC Epoxy plus Polyurethane Finish]
    sys1 --> out1[Red Brown Colour Biocide Loaded]
    sys2 --> out2[Black and Red Boundary Marking]
    sys3 --> out3[Cosmetic Gloss Colours per Class Requirement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The diagrams above reflect the **ISO 12944** standard for corrosion protection of steel structures by protective paint systems, which is the recognized international reference adopted by IACS (International Association of Classification Societies) and major classification societies like IRS, DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, and BV.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Volume Solids" of a marine paint. Why is it critical for hull paint estimation?*

**Model Answer:**
Volume Solids (VS%) of a paint is the percentage of the wet paint volume that remains as a solid dry film after all volatile solvents have evaporated. For example, an epoxy paint with 60% VS means 100 L of wet paint leaves 60 L of dry film.

It is critical because **DFT (target dry film thickness)** can only be achieved if the estimator knows how much wet paint to apply. The relationship $\text{WFT} = \dfrac{\text{DFT} \times 100}{\text{VS}\%}$ governs the entire paint quantity calculation in shipyard BOQ preparation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Conventional Anti-Fouling (CAF) and Self-Polishing Copolymer (SPC) Anti-Fouling paints.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Conventional AF | Self-Polishing Copolymer AF |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Binder | Rosin or gum base | Hydrolysable acrylic copolymer |
| Biocide Release | Dissolution-controlled (uneven) | Hydrolysis-controlled (linear) |
| Polishing Rate | Irregular | Constant and predictable |
| Effective Life | 24 to 36 months | Up to 60 months |
| Hull Roughness Over Time | Increases | Maintained smooth |
| Fuel Penalty | Higher | Lower (saves ~3 to 6% fuel) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. You must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

**Q.A (a)** Explain in detail the **surface preparation standards (SSPC / ISO 8501)** used prior to hull painting. Discuss the importance of surface profile (anchor pattern) and dew point control. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Surface preparation is the single most critical factor determining coating longevity. The widely adopted standards are:

1. **SSPC-SP 10 / ISO 8501-1 Sa 2.5 (Near-White Metal Blast)**
   - Removes at least 95% of all mill scale, rust, paint, and contaminants.
   - Remaining staining limited to 5% as light shadows or patches.
   - Standard for underwater hull AC systems.

2. **SSPC-SP 6 / Sa 2 (Commercial Blast)**
   - Removes at least 67% of contaminants. Used for topsides and superstructures.

3. **SSPC-SP 3 (Power Tool Cleaning)**
   - Used for spot repairs and maintenance. Acceptable only for small areas.

**Surface Profile (Anchor Pattern):**
The blasted surface must have a roughness of **75 μm to 100 μm**, measured using a **Testex Press-O-Film** replica tape. This profile provides mechanical interlocking for the primer. Too smooth → poor adhesion; too rough → peaks remain uncoated → rust creep.

**Dew Point Control:**
The substrate temperature must be at least **3°C above the dew point** to prevent condensation forming between substrate and wet paint, which causes adhesion failure (blistering).

**Q.A (b)** A ship's topside area of **3200 m²** is to be painted with a polyurethane finish having **VS = 55%**, required **DFT = 50 μm**, applied by **roller (η = 0.85)**. Calculate the **total paint volume (Litres)** required for **3 coats**, including a **10% wastage allowance**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Theoretical Spreading Rate (TSR):**

$$
\text{TSR} = \dfrac{10 \times 55}{50} = \dfrac{550}{50} = 11\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Practical Spreading Rate (PSR):**

$$
\text{PSR} = 11 \times 0.85 = 9.35\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume per Coat:**

$$
V_{\text{coat}} = \dfrac{3200}{9.35} = 342.25\ \text{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Total for 3 Coats (without wastage):**

$$
V_{\text{base}} = 3 \times 342.25 = 1026.75\ \text{L}
$$

**Step 5 — Adding 10% Wastage:**

$$
V_{\text{final}} = 1026.75 \times 1.10 = 1129.43\ \text{L}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{final}} \approx 1130\ \text{L}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]*

**Q.B (a)** Describe the **complete multi-layer hull paint system** for an underwater hull. State the function and typical DFT of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The underwater hull paint system (applied during new-building or full re-painting in dry-dock) consists of **four functional layers**:

| Layer | Paint Type | Function | Typical DFT |
|-------|-----------|----------|-------------|
| 1. Primer | Zinc-rich epoxy | Galvanic protection, adhesion to steel | 25 to 40 μm |
| 2. AC Coat 1 | Epoxy anti-corrosive | Moisture barrier, corrosion resistance | 125 to 150 μm |
| 3. AC Coat 2 | Epoxy anti-corrosive | Builds total DFT, sealing pinholes | 125 to 150 μm |
| 4. AF Coat | Self-Polishing Copolymer (SPC) | Biocide release, prevents fouling | 150 to 300 μm |
| | | **Total System DFT** | **425 to 640 μm** |

**Additional Engineering Considerations:**
- **Stripe coats** are applied by brush on all edges, welds, and corners before each spray coat to ensure full coverage on geometrical high-risk areas.
- **Inter-coat overcoating window** must be respected; exceeding the re-coat interval requires re-roughening.
- **Holiday detection** (low-voltage 67.5V DC sponge test) is mandatory after AF application to find pinholes.

**Q.B (b)** Explain the **common causes of coating failure** on ship hulls and the **survey methods** used to assess paint condition during Class survey. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Common Failure Modes:**

1. **Blistering:** Caused by osmotic pressure from soluble salts (chlorides/sulphates) trapped under the coating. Detected as dome-shaped bubbles.
2. **Delamination / Peeling:** Loss of adhesion due to inadequate surface preparation or dew point violation.
3. **Rust Creep (Edge Creep):** Under-film corrosion spreading from a defect. Measured as creep distance from scribe (per **ASTM D7087**).
4. **Cracking and Mud-Cracking:** Solvent entrapment, excessive WFT, or wrong paint system.
5. **Fouling Accumulation:** Ineffective AF due to biocide depletion or polishing failure.
6. **Cathodic Disbondment:** In ballast tanks, caused by inadequate CP coordination.

**Survey Methods for Paint Condition Assessment:**

- **Visual-Tactile Inspection:** Classification surveyor walks the hull with a knife and magnifying glass.
- **DFT Measurement:** Using **magnetic induction** (for steel) or **eddy current** gauges; calibration per **ISO 19840**.
- **Adhesion Testing:** **Pull-Off Test** (Elcometer 106) per **ASTM D4541**; minimum acceptable = **5 MPa**.
- **Holiday Detection:** Wet sponge (67.5V DC) for films < 500 μm; high-voltage DC for thicker films.
- **Gloss and Colour Measurement:** For cosmetic topsides using gloss meters (per **ISO 2813**).
- **Sampling and Lab Analysis:** FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared) spectroscopy to identify paint composition and remaining DFT.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** from $\mu$m to m in TSR derivation — losing **2 marks** consistently.
> 2. **Confusing DFT with WFT** in numerical problems — re-read the question carefully.
> 3. **Ignoring transfer efficiency** in PSR calculation — KTU specifically tests this.
> 4. **Not stating units** (m²/L, μm, L) in final answers — deducts **0.5 mark** per sub-question.
> 5. **Vague paint names** — always specify "epoxy AC", "SPC AF", "PU topcoat", not just "paint".
> 6. **Missing the 3°C dew point rule** in surface preparation theory questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

- **Hull Painting = Protective + Cosmetic** dual-purpose engineering activity.
- **Three Hull Zones:** Underwater, Boot Top, Topside/Superstructure.
- **Coating Build-up Order:** Blast → Primer → AC (×2) → AF (underwater) or PU Topcoat (above).
- **Total underwater DFT** = **425 to 640 μm** (memorize the range).
- **Key Formula (CANONICAL):**
$$
\text{TSR}\ (\text{m}^2/\text{L}) = \dfrac{10 \times \text{VS}\%}{\text{DFT}\ (\mu\text{m})}
$$
- **PSR = TSR × η**; Brush η = 0.90, Roller η = 0.85, Airless Spray η ≈ 0.65–0.75.
- **Surface Preparation Standard** for underwater hull = **SSPC-SP 10 / Sa 2.5**.
- **Anchor Profile** requirement = **75 to 100 μm**.
- **Dew Point Rule** = Substrate temperature ≥ **Surface temperature + 3°C**.
- **Holiday Detection Voltage** for films < 500 μm = **67.5V DC**.
- **Minimum Pull-Off Adhesion** = **5 MPa** (per ASTM D4541).
- **Volume Solids (VS%)** is the percentage of wet paint that becomes solid film — drives all estimation.
- **Always add 5–10%** wastage allowance for stripe coats, overlap, and spillage.
- **Paint quantities are in Litres (V)** or Kilograms (W = V × ρ); density ρ of epoxy ≈ **1.4 to 1.5 kg/L**.
- **Classification societies** reference: **ISO 12944, SSPC, IMO PSPC** (for ballast tanks).
- **IMO PSPC** (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings) applies to **ballast tanks** of new ships — mandatory since 2008.
- **SPC AF** saves **3 to 6%** fuel compared to conventional AF due to maintained hull smoothness.
- **Inspection tools** to remember: DFT gauge, holiday detector, pull-off tester, gloss meter, Testex tape.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propeller Works
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Propeller Works

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Propeller Works** in the context of ship repair and survey refers to the comprehensive set of engineering operations performed on the ship's propulsion propeller, encompassing inspection, defect assessment, repair, reconditioning, removal, reinstallation, and post-repair verification, all conducted in compliance with Classification Society rules (IACS members such as Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV, IRS), SOLAS/IMO requirements, and the ship's planned maintenance system (PMS).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST861)** context, propeller works forms a critical sub-module of dry-dock and afloat repair procedures, addressing the integrity of the propeller as a Class-approved component essential for safe navigation, fuel efficiency, and emission compliance (EEDI/EEXI/CII regimes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Propeller works under Module 4 typically covers (a) types of marine propellers, (b) classification society survey requirements for propellers, (c) propeller inspection (dry-dock, afloat, and underwater), (d) common defects, (e) repair techniques (welding, machining, fairing, polishing), (f) propeller removal/reinstallation, and (g) estimation and man-hour planning for propeller works.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **propeller as the "shoe" of a marathon runner**.

Just as a runner's shoes suffer wear, tear, cracks, and uneven soles after thousands of kilometers, the ship's propeller—pumping millions of tons of seawater—suffers from **cavitation erosion, leading-edge damage, bent blades, fouling, and cracks**.

- **Propeller inspection** is like checking the shoe soles after a race.
- **Propeller repair (welding, fairing)** is like re-soling and reshaping the shoe.
- **Dynamic balancing** is like making sure both shoes have equal weight so the runner doesn't limp.
- **Reclassification survey** is like the coach confirming the shoes are race-legal before the next marathon.

When the runner (ship) cannot afford to limp, the propeller (shoe) must be brought back to OEM and Class standards.

## 1.3 Classification of Marine Propellers (KTU Board Frequently Tested)

| Propeller Type | Description | Typical Application |
|----------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Fixed Pitch Propeller (FPP)** | Blades permanently fixed to hub; pitch non-adjustable | Most merchant ships, tankers, bulk carriers |
| **Controllable Pitch Propeller (CPP)** | Blades rotate on hub axis; pitch adjustable from bridge | Ferries, naval vessels, tugs, offshore |
| **Kaplan/Kort Nozzle Propeller** | Propeller inside a fixed nozzle (shroud) for higher bollard pull | Tugs, trawlers, harbour vessels |
| **Voith Schneider Propeller (VSP)** | Cycloidal vertical-axis rotor | Tractors, special vessels |
| **Water-jet Propeller** | Pump-based thrust system | Fast ferries, naval craft |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Box — Controllable Pitch Propeller (CPP):** A propeller whose blade pitch can be adjusted while the propeller is rotating, via hydraulic or electro-mechanical servo mechanisms housed within the hub, allowing the ship to change thrust direction without reversing engine rotation.

---

## 1.4 Propeller Materials of Construction (Critical for Repair)

The propeller material dictates **repair procedure qualification** under Class rules. A Class-approved *Procedure Qualification Test (PQT)* on test coupons must precede production welding.

| Material | Designation | UNS | Notes |
|----------|-------------|-----|-------|
| Manganese Bronze | **High Tensile Brass** (HTB) | C86500 / C95800 | Traditional; max 60% Sn for marine use |
| Nickel-Aluminium Bronze (NAB) | **Cu-Al-Ni-Fe alloy** | C95800 | Modern standard; superior corrosion & cavitation resistance |
| Manganese-Nickel-Aluminium Bronze | **MAB / Super-NAB** | C95700 | High-strength applications |
| Stainless Steel | 17-4 PH, 254 SMO | — | Submarines, ice-class, special vessels |
| Titanium Alloys | Ti-6Al-4V | — | Submarine propellers, mine countermeasure vessels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners' Favourite:** Difference between **HTB (Manganese Bronze)** and **NAB**. NAB contains ~9–12% Al and ~4–6% Fe+Ni; HTB contains ~38–45% Zn and up to 1% Sn. NAB welds require **inert gas shielding (GTAW/GMAW with Argon)** and **preheat ≤ 150 °C**; HTB welds allow higher preheat but suffer dezincification.

---

## 1.5 Standards Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Maximum permissible blade thickness reduction** = **10% of design thickness** (Class rule baseline).
- **Maximum permissible reduction in propeller diameter** = **3%** (some Class rules allow up to 5% with re-rating).
- **Underwater cleaning limit (in-water surveys)** = blade surface roughness **≤ 150 µm Ra** before Class consideration.
- **Welding pre-heat temperature for NAB** = **100–150 °C** (interpass ≤ 150 °C).
- **Dynamic balance tolerance** (ISO 1940) = **G 6.3** for marine propellers.

---

## 1.6 Why Propeller Works Matter in Ship Repair Economy

- Propeller accounts for ~**15–25%** of total ship resistance at service speed.
- A **5% loss in propeller efficiency** can increase fuel consumption by **~2.5%**, translating to **~USD 50,000–150,000/year** for a mid-size bulker.
- Roughness increase from **10 µm to 50 µm Ra** can cause efficiency drop of **~3–5%**.
- Underwater propeller polishing has become a major in-service maintenance industry (estimated global market > USD 200 million annually).
- **EEXI/CII regulations (IMO MEPC 328(76))** post-2023 force operators to maintain propeller surface finish rigorously.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Propeller Works

Propeller works, as a sub-discipline of marine engineering, integrates knowledge from:

- **Marine Hydrodynamics** (cavitation, thrust, torque, efficiency)
- **Welding Metallurgy** (NAB/HTB weldability, PQR/WPS)
- **Nondestructive Testing (NDT)** (UT, MPI/DPI, eddy current)
- **Tribology & Surface Engineering** (Ra roughness, fouling)
- **Classification Society Rules** (IACS UR M3, M49, M58, M71)
- **Project Management & Cost Estimation** (man-hours, dry-dock days, materials)

### 2.1.1 The Five Pillars of Propeller Works (Logical Steps)

1. **Trigger Event** → scheduled docking, Class survey due, performance drop, vibration report, or accidental contact (e.g., rope/net strike).
2. **Inspection & Survey** → visual, dimensional, NDT, in-water or in dry-dock.
3. **Defect Mapping & Repair Scope** → defects are classified as Class-*condition* (CTL/SEE) and quantified against Class tolerances.
4. **Repair Execution** → welding, machining, fairing, polishing, balancing, blade cropping, or full replacement.
5. **Verification & Reclassification** → NDT, dimensional report, dynamic balance, sea trials, Class endorsement.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Many students skip step 3 (defect mapping). Class surveyors grade your repair *scope definition* heavily because it drives the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) and ultimately the **repair cost estimate**.

---

## 2.2 Propeller Defect Catalog (Theory-Mapping Table)

| Defect | Cause | Inspection Method | Class Limit |
|--------|-------|-------------------|-------------|
| Cavitation erosion | Local low-pressure vapor collapse | Visual + UT | ≤ 10% thickness loss |
| Leading-edge cracks | High cycle fatigue, impact | MPI / DPI | No cracks > 25 mm |
| Bent / distorted blade | Grounding, rope strike | Pitch measurement | Pitch deviation ≤ ±2% |
| Marine fouling | Biofouling (barnacles, slime) | Visual, Ra gauge | Class may reject > 5% efficiency drop |
| Edge erosion | Cavitation at tip vortex | Visual | Blade tip thinning ≤ 5% |
| Hub taper wear | Stern bearing wear → vibration | Feeler gauge | Taper within OEM spec |
| Keyway fretting | Loose fit, vibration | Visual + UT | Replace key if fretting > 5% |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Master Table — Propeller Works & Repair Estimation**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T = \rho \, n^2 \, D^4 \, K_T$ | Thrust coefficient relation (Kt) | T [N], ρ [kg/m³], n [rps], D [m] |
| 2 | $Q = \rho \, n^2 \, D^5 \, K_Q$ | Torque coefficient relation (Kq) | Q [N·m] |
| 3 | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{J \, K_T}{2\pi \, K_Q}$ | Open-water propulsive efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $J = \dfrac{V_a}{n D}$ | Advance coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta \eta \,(\%) = C \, \Delta \overline{Ra}^{0.3}$ | Efficiency loss vs roughness (Schmiedl/Granville) | % |
| 6 | $f_{bal} = \dfrac{e \, \omega^2}{g}$ | Specific unbalance force | mm/s² |
| 7 | $w_{steel} = \dfrac{\pi \, D^3}{4} \, \rho_{steel} \, t_{avg}$ | Approx. propeller mass | kg |
| 8 | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{0.6 \, \sigma_{UTS}}{FS}$ | Allowable stress (Class) | MPa |
| 9 | $\% \, D_{red} = \dfrac{D_{nom} - D_{actual}}{D_{nom}} \times 100$ | Diameter reduction percentage | % |
| 10 | $T_{preheat} \, [{}^\circ C] = 0.7 \, T_{sol}$ | Preheat rule for NAB | °C |
| 11 | $W_{prop} = \rho_w \, n^3 \, D^5 \, K_W$ | Power delivered to water | kW |
| 12 | $N_{days} = \dfrac{MH_{total}}{MH_{day} \times N_{crew}}$ | Dry-dock day estimation | days |

> **Definitions used:** $V_a$ = advance speed [m/s], $n$ = rotational speed [rps], $D$ = propeller diameter [m], $\overline{Ra}$ = mean surface roughness [µm], $C$ ≈ 1.1 (typical for clean vs. fouled), $K_T$, $K_Q$, $K_W$ are open-water coefficients (read from Wageningen B-series or $K_{p}$–$K_{q}$ charts), $T_{sol}$ = solidus temperature, $MH$ = man-hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Avoidance Rule:** All absolute-value expressions and ratios use the LaTeX vertical-bar command `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert \Delta \eta \vert$) to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel-saving retrofit:** Hull cleaning + propeller polishing routinely deliver 5–10% fuel savings.
- **EEXI compliance:** Many older ships must use *Engine Power Limitation (EPL)* + propeller polishing to meet Phase 3 EEXI.
- **Cold ironing vs. polished propeller:** A clean propeller is the cheapest "alternative fuel" available.
- **Repair-yard business model:** Approximately **40–60%** of propeller work in dry-dock is *precautionary* (Class-driven), not corrective.
- **NAB vs. HTB decision tree:** New builds (>2000 DWT) almost universally use NAB; legacy HTB propellers may be weld-repaired with HTB-compatible consumables only.

---

## 2.5 The Propeller Repair Decision Tree (Logic Flow)

When a defect is found, the surveyor applies a Class-driven decision tree:

```
Condition Found (Cavitation / Crack / Bend / Erosion / Wear)
        |
        v
Is defect within Class limits?
        |
   [ YES ] ----- > Routine polishing/fairing -> Annual survey sign-off
        |
   [ NO ] 
        |
        v
Is weld repair feasible? (material thickness, geometry, Class PQR)
        |
   [ YES ] ----- > Welding + NDT + Fairing -> Class weld repair approval
        |
   [ NO ]
        |
        v
Is blade cropping acceptable? (≤ 5% diameter reduction)
        |
   [ YES ] ----- > Machine cropping + Re-pitch check -> Class re-rating
        |
   [ NO ]
        |
        v
Propeller replacement (full set) -> Shaft withdrawal -> Class new fit-out
```

> This tree maps to **IACS UR M3 (Propellers)** and **UR M58 (In-Water Survey)** — both are KTU-recommended reading.

---

## 2.6 Classification Society Survey Schedule (Propeller-Specific)

| Survey Type | Propeller Scope | Interval |
|-------------|-----------------|----------|
| **Annual Survey** | Visual + general check | Every year |
| **Intermediate Survey** | Drawn for internal exam (CPP), hub oil sample | 2nd/3rd year of Class cycle |
| **Special Survey (Class Renewal)** | Full withdrawal, UT, MPI of blades, hub, keyway | Every 5 years |
| **Continuous Survey** | Rotational partial scope | Continuous (every year) |
| **Propeller Shaft & CPP** | Withdrawal, bearing check | Every 5 years typically |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Survey intervals of Class renewals can be extended up to 5 years; IACS UR Z17/Z23 govern *short-term surveys* and *CE/SE* deliverables.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Propeller Efficiency Loss Due to Surface Roughness

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A bulk carrier propeller of diameter **D = 6.0 m**, rotational speed **n = 1.6 rps**, advance speed **V_a = 6.5 m/s**, open-water coefficients $K_T = 0.42$ and $K_Q = 0.055$ is delivered with mean roughness $\overline{Ra}_1 = 10 \,\mu m$. After 18 months of service, roughness has increased to $\overline{Ra}_2 = 80 \,\mu m$. Using the Granville/Schmiedl correlation, estimate the **percentage drop in open-water efficiency**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute advance coefficient (initial condition)**

$$
J_1 = \frac{V_a}{n \, D} = \frac{6.5}{1.6 \times 6.0}
$$

$$
J_1 = \frac{6.5}{9.6} = 0.6771
$$

**Step 2 — Compute open-water efficiency (clean)**

$$
\eta_{0,1} = \frac{J_1 \, K_T}{2\pi \, K_Q} = \frac{0.6771 \times 0.42}{2\pi \times 0.055}
$$

$$
\eta_{0,1} = \frac{0.2844}{0.3456} = 0.8230
$$

So $\eta_{0,1} = 82.30\%$.

**Step 3 — Apply Granville roughness penalty**

The Granville correction (1958) for a flat plate is:

$$
\Delta C_F \,(\%) = 0.044 \left[ \left( \frac{\overline{Ra}_2}{\overline{Ra}_1} \right)^{0.6} - 1 \right] \times 1000
$$

For propellers, we may approximate (per Townsin, 1986) the efficiency degradation as:

$$
\Delta \eta_0 \,(\%) \approx 0.6 \, \Delta C_F \,(\%)
$$

Compute the roughness ratio:

$$
\frac{\overline{Ra}_2}{\overline{Ra}_1} = \frac{80}{10} = 8.0
$$

Raise to the 0.6 power:

$$
8.0^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 8} = e^{0.6 \times 2.0794} = e^{1.2477} = 3.482
$$

Then:

$$
\Delta C_F \,(\%) = 0.044 \times (3.482 - 1) \times 1000 = 0.044 \times 2.482 \times 1000
$$

$$
\Delta C_F = 109.21 \,\%
$$

> Note: Granville gives drag *increase*; we then map to efficiency *loss* via the approximate multiplier.

**Step 4 — Convert to efficiency loss**

$$
\Delta \eta_0 \,(\%) \approx 0.6 \times 109.21 \approx 65.5 \,\%
$$

This is unrealistically high for a propeller (Granville is for flat plates, propellers only absorb ~1/10 of this). The corrected propeller-specific form (Schmiedl/Schultz) is:

$$
\Delta \eta_0 \,(\%) \approx C \left( \overline{Ra}_2^{0.3} - \overline{Ra}_1^{0.3} \right)
$$

with $C \approx 0.85$ for medium-sized merchant propellers.

**Step 5 — Recompute using Schmiedl form**

$$
\overline{Ra}_1^{0.3} = 10^{0.3} = e^{0.3 \ln 10} = e^{0.6908} = 1.995
$$

$$
\overline{Ra}_2^{0.3} = 80^{0.3} = e^{0.3 \ln 80} = e^{0.3 \times 4.3820} = e^{1.3146} = 3.723
$$

$$
\Delta \eta_0 = 0.85 \times (3.723 - 1.995) = 0.85 \times 1.728
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta \eta_0 \approx 1.47 \,\%}
$$

**Step 6 — New open-water efficiency**

$$
\eta_{0,2} = 82.30\% - 1.47\% = 80.83\%
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation**

A **1.47% drop** in $\eta_0$ for a 25,000 DWT bulker consuming ~25 t/day fuel at $V_a = 14$ knots translates to:

- Daily fuel penalty ≈ **0.0147 × 25 = 0.37 t/day**
- Annual penalty (~250 sailing days) ≈ **92 t/year**
- At $VLSFO = $600/MT → **USD 55,200/year lost**

> **Conclusion:** Polishing the propeller (reducing $\overline{Ra}_2$ back to 15 µm) recovers nearly **all** of this loss — a typical ROI of < 6 months for an underwater polishing campaign.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Diameter Reduction & Re-rating

**Problem Statement:**
A FPP with nominal diameter $D_{nom} = 5.5 \, m$ has had tip erosion reducing the actual diameter to $D_{act} = 5.36 \, m$. Determine:
- (i) Diameter reduction percentage.
- (ii) Whether the propeller is still within IACS UR M3 acceptance limits.
- (iii) Effect on thrust if $n$ and $V_a$ are unchanged.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Diameter reduction percentage**

$$
\% D_{red} = \frac{D_{nom} - D_{act}}{D_{nom}} \times 100 = \frac{5.5 - 5.36}{5.5} \times 100
$$

$$
\% D_{red} = \frac{0.14}{5.5} \times 100 = 2.545 \,\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compare against Class limit**

Class rule baseline: **≤ 3%** diameter reduction without re-rating; up to **5%** with performance re-evaluation. Our value $2.545\%$ is within the **3% band**, so it **passes** IACS UR M3 without mandatory re-rating.

**Step 3 — Effect on thrust**

Thrust $T \propto K_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4$. For a small change $\Delta D$:

$$
\frac{\Delta T}{T} \approx 4 \cdot \frac{\Delta D}{D} = 4 \times (-0.02545)
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{\Delta T}{T} \approx -10.18 \,\%}
$$

The ship loses ~10% of available thrust at constant RPM, equivalent to a **service speed loss of approximately 0.5 knots** at the same engine power.

> **Practical Action:** If diameter reduction > 3%, the surveyor may require **blade cropping & re-pitching** to restore nominal thrust at reduced diameter. IACS UR M3 §3.5 governs.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Welding Preheat Temperature for NAB

**Problem Statement:**
Determine the minimum preheat temperature for welding a Nickel-Aluminium Bronze (NAB) propeller blade of nominal thickness 60 mm. Solidus temperature $T_{sol} = 1035 \, {}^\circ C$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply preheat rule**

$$
T_{preheat} = 0.7 \, T_{sol} \quad \text{(upper bound, but practical preheat is capped at 150 °C for NAB)}
$$

$$
T_{preheat} = 0.7 \times 1035 = 724.5 \, {}^\circ C \quad \text{(theoretical)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply practical Class limit**

IACS UR M3 / AWS D3.6M caps NAB preheat at:

$$
T_{preheat,\,max,\,Class} = 150 \, {}^\circ C
$$

$$
T_{preheat,\,min,\,Class} = 100 \, {}^\circ C
$$

**Step 3 — Final answer**

$$
\boxed{T_{preheat} = 100 \, {}^\circ C \le T \le 150 \, {}^\circ C}
$$

Interpass temperature must also stay ≤ 150 °C. Use **GTAW (TIG)** with Argon shielding, ER CuAl-A2 / ER CuAl-A3 filler (AWS A5.7 / A5.8).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often write the *theoretical* 724 °C. Always cite the **Class-capped practical** value of 150 °C in your answers.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4 — Dynamic Balance Tolerance (ISO 1940)

**Problem Statement:**
A 4-blade NAB propeller weighs $m = 12{,}500 \, kg$, design service speed $n = 120 \, rpm = 2.0 \, rps$. Calculate the **residual specific unbalance** allowed under ISO 1940 Grade G 6.3 for marine propellers.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Angular velocity**

$$
\omega = 2\pi n = 2\pi \times 2.0 = 12.566 \, rad/s
$$

**Step 2 — ISO 1940 G 6.3 permissible specific unbalance**

Grade G 6.3 means the residual eccentricity × angular velocity equals $6.3 \, mm/s$:

$$
e_{per} \cdot \omega = 6.3 \, mm/s
$$

$$
e_{per} = \frac{6.3 \, mm/s}{12.566 \, rad/s} = 0.501 \, mm = 501 \, \mu m
$$

**Step 3 — Permissible unbalance mass (for reference radius)**

If we assume a correction radius $r_c = 1.5 \, m$ (typical at blade tip):

$$
m_{unb,\,per} = \frac{e_{per} \times m}{r_c} = \frac{0.501 \times 10^{-3} \, m \times 12{,}500 \, kg}{1.5 \, m}
$$

$$
m_{unb,\,per} = \frac{6.263}{1.5} = 4.175 \, kg
$$

> **Interpretation:** Up to **~4 kg** of material may be removed from any one blade (at 1.5 m radius) before dynamic balance is breached. Any weld deposit must be distributed to keep the final assembly within this window.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Problem 5 — Man-Hour & Dry-Dock Day Estimation

**Problem Statement:**
A dry-dock scope for a 5-blade FPP includes: blade fairing (3 blades), 200 mm crack repair on one blade, hub polishing, and propeller dynamic balancing. Use the standard man-hour tables given below.

| Activity | Man-hours per unit |
|----------|--------------------|
| Blade fairing & polishing | 24 MH/blade |
| Crack repair (GTAW + NDT) | 12 MH per 100 mm crack length |
| Hub polishing | 16 MH |
| Dynamic balancing | 32 MH |

Assume 2 shifts/day × 8 h/shift × 4 men/shift = **64 MH/day**. Compute total dock days.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute MH per activity**

- Fairing: $3 \times 24 = 72 \, MH$
- Crack repair: $(200 / 100) \times 12 = 24 \, MH$
- Hub polishing: $16 \, MH$
- Dynamic balancing: $32 \, MH$

**Step 2 — Sum**

$$
MH_{total} = 72 + 24 + 16 + 32 = 144 \, MH
$$

**Step 3 — Days required**

$$
N_{days} = \frac{MH_{total}}{MH_{day}} = \frac{144}{64} = 2.25 \, days
$$

**Step 4 — Add contingency (20%) and curing/inspection buffers**

$$
N_{days,\,plan} = 2.25 \times 1.2 = 2.7 \approx 3 \, days
$$

> **Rule of thumb used in yards:** Add 1 buffer day per welding activity for **NDT re-inspection** post-cool.

$$
\boxed{N_{days,\,final} \approx 4 \, \text{days}}
$$

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Controllable Pitch Propeller (CPP). List any two advantages over Fixed Pitch Propellers (FPP).

`[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Controllable Pitch Propeller (CPP)** is a propeller whose blades can be rotated about their own longitudinal axis while the propeller is rotating, thereby allowing the **pitch** (and hence thrust magnitude and direction) to be varied without changing engine rotational direction. Pitch control is effected through hydraulic or electro-mechanical servo systems located within the hub, commanded from the bridge or engine control room.

**Two advantages over FPP:**

1. **Superior maneuverability** — Full thrust can be produced in either direction instantly without reversing the engine; emergency stopping distance is reduced by up to 40% compared with FPP of equivalent power.
2. **Better fuel efficiency at part load** — Pitch is optimized for every speed/power combination, often saving 8–15% fuel over an FPP operating on a fixed-pitch curve.

> *Examiner cue: A 3-mark answer expects definition (1 mark) + two advantages (1 mark each) with quantification.*

---

### Q2. What is cavitation erosion in a marine propeller? State any two methods to mitigate it.

`[KTU University Exam — December 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Cavitation erosion** is the progressive material loss on the propeller blade surface caused by the **collapse of vapour bubbles** that form in regions of locally low pressure (typically near the leading edge, tip, or in the sheet-cavitation zone). The collapse of each bubble generates a **microjet** that strikes the blade surface at velocities of 100–200 m/s, producing fatigue and pitting damage.

**Two mitigation methods:**

1. **Material upgrade** — Use Nickel-Aluminium Bronze (NAB) instead of Manganese Bronze; NAB's aluminium-oxide surface film resists erosion far better.
2. **Improved blade design** — Increase leading-edge radius, reduce blade thickness, and select a more favorable $K_T$–$K_Q$ combination (e.g., higher skew, wider blade tip) to delay cavitation inception.

> *Bonus point: Anti-cavitation paint coatings (e.g., epoxy-ceramic composites) can be applied for marginal service-life extension.*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Propeller Survey, Defect Analysis, and Welding Repair

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper for Module 4]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Describe, with a clear flowchart, the **procedure for propeller survey during the Special Survey (Class Renewal)** of a merchant ship. Include at least **four distinct inspection activities** and reference applicable IACS UR / Classification Society rules.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

A 4-blade NAB propeller shows a **150 mm long leading-edge crack** at the 0.7 R station on one blade, with adjacent cavitation erosion pits of up to **3 mm depth**. The blade design thickness is 70 mm. Outline the **step-by-step repair procedure** you would recommend, including welding process, preheat, filler metal, NDT, and acceptance criteria.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Preparation**
- Dry-dock the vessel; support the propeller shaft with the **cradle**; clean the propeller with high-pressure freshwater wash.

**Step 2 — Visual Inspection (100%)**
- Surveyor and shipyard engineer walk around the propeller, photographing each blade.
- Document: cracks, dents, erosion, fouling, anode condition.

**Step 3 — Dimensional Checks**
- Measure **diameter** with steel tape or laser scanner; verify reduction ≤ 3%.
- Measure **pitch** with pitchometer or template at 0.3 R, 0.7 R, and 0.9 R; deviation must be ≤ ±2% of design.

**Step 4 — Nondestructive Testing (NDT)**
- **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)** or **Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI)** on all accessible surfaces for cracks.
- **Ultrasonic Thickness (UT)** at erosion-prone zones to confirm remaining thickness ≥ 90% of nominal.

**Step 5 — Hub and Keyway Examination**
- Check **keyway fit**, measure **taper** of the hub bore, examine **locking nut** torque marks.

**Step 6 — Dynamic Balance**
- Mount on balance machine; residual unbalance must satisfy **ISO 1940 G 6.3**.

**Step 7 — Reporting and Class Sign-off**
- Issue *Survey Report* with defect map; Class endorses *Condition of Class* (CoC) or *Recommendation* (Rec).

**Reference:** **IACS UR M3** (Propellers), **UR M58** (In-Water Survey), and the relevant Class rules (e.g., Lloyd's *ShipRight* procedure).

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Damage Assessment**
- Crack length = 150 mm; pit depth = 3 mm in 70 mm blade thickness.
- Remaining thickness after crack excavation = 70 − 3 − 5 (groove allowance) = 62 mm — adequate for welding.

**Step 2 — Defect Excavation**
- Grind out the crack to a U-groove using tungsten-carbide burr or pneumatic grinder. Depth of groove ≥ 5 mm; root radius ≥ 3 mm; sidewall angle ≈ 60–70°.

**Step 3 — Preheat**
- Apply **preheat of 100–150 °C** using oxy-acetylene or electric resistance heating mats. Verify with calibrated contact pyrometers on both faces of the blade.

**Step 4 — Welding Process**
- Use **GTAW (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding)** with **DCEN** polarity and **Argon shielding**.
- Filler metal: **AWS A5.7 ER CuAl-A2** (or A3 for thicker sections). Filler diameter 2.4 mm root, 3.2 mm fill.

**Step 5 — Welding Sequence**
- Multi-pass: root → fill → cap, with **interpass ≤ 150 °C**.
- **Peening** between passes to relieve residual stress.
- **Back-purge** with Argon if accessing the back side.

**Step 6 — Post-weld Cooling**
- Slow cool under insulating blanket for ≥ 4 hours; do not quench.

**Step 7 — NDT**
- **MPI / DPI** on completed weld and adjacent HAZ 100% (Class requirement).
- **UT** for volumetric check if blade thickness > 30 mm.

**Step 8 — Fairing and Polishing**
- Restore aerodynamic profile to within ±0.5 mm of design template.
- Final surface roughness ≤ **5 µm Ra** (or as per Class guidance).

**Step 9 — Acceptance Criteria**
- No cracks in weld or HAZ.
- Hardness within ±10% of base metal (typically 140–180 HB for NAB).
- Pitch within ±2% of design.
- Final visual + Class sign-off.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Propeller Efficiency, Estimation & Cost Trade-off

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Explain the **effect of surface roughness and fouling on open-water propeller efficiency**. Use the Granville / Schmiedl correlations qualitatively and estimate the **fuel-cost penalty** for a ship burning 30 t/day at a fuel price of USD 620/MT for an efficiency drop of 2.0%.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Prepare an **estimated work scope, man-hour schedule, and approximate cost** for the following propeller works during a 10-day scheduled dry-docking:

| Task | Quantity |
|------|----------|
| Blade fairing & polishing (per blade) | All 5 blades |
| Weld repair of eroded leading edge (per blade) | 2 blades |
| Hub face machining | 1 |
| Dynamic balancing | 1 |
| Sea trial | 1 |

Assume the man-hour rates (per Section 3.5) and labour cost USD 35/man-hour. Material & consumable cost: USD 8,500. Subcontract (balancing) cost: USD 4,200. Class survey fee: USD 2,800. Add 15% yard overhead.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Theory**

Propeller efficiency depends on the **frictional resistance of the blade surface**. As roughness (or fouling) increases, the local skin-friction coefficient $C_F$ rises sharply. Granville (1958) showed:

$$
\Delta C_F \propto \left( \overline{Ra}^{0.6} - \overline{Ra}_{ref}^{0.6} \right)
$$

For a propeller, Schmiedl/Schultz showed efficiency loss is approximately:

$$
\Delta \eta_0 \,(\%) \approx 0.85 \left( \overline{Ra}_2^{0.3} - \overline{Ra}_1^{0.3} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Estimate fuel penalty**

Fuel penalty per day:

$$
F_{loss} = \eta_{loss} \times F_{day} = 0.020 \times 30 = 0.60 \, t/day
$$

Cost penalty per day:

$$
C_{loss} = 0.60 \times 620 = USD \, 372 / day
$$

Annual penalty (assuming 280 sailing days/year):

$$
C_{annual} = 372 \times 280 = USD \, 104{,}160 / year
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion**

A 2% efficiency drop costs the operator over **USD 100k per year**. An underwater propeller cleaning/polishing campaign (typically USD 15–30k per occurrence, every 6–12 months) pays back in **less than 4 months**.

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Man-hour calculation**

Using Section 3.5 rates:

| Task | Calculation | MH |
|------|------------|-----|
| Fairing & polishing (5 blades) | $5 \times 24$ | 120 |
| Weld repair (2 blades) | $2 \times 36$ (assumed) | 72 |
| Hub face machining | — | 40 |
| Dynamic balancing | — | 32 |
| Sea trial | — | 48 |

$$
MH_{total} = 120 + 72 + 40 + 32 + 48 = 312 \, MH
$$

**Step 2 — Labour cost**

$$
C_{lab} = 312 \times 35 = USD \, 10{,}920
$$

**Step 3 — Add direct costs**

| Item | Cost (USD) |
|------|-----------|
| Materials & consumables | 8,500 |
| Subcontract (balancing) | 4,200 |
| Class survey fee | 2,800 |
| Labour | 10,920 |
| **Subtotal** | **26,420** |

**Step 4 — Add yard overhead (15%)**

$$
C_{overhead} = 0.15 \times 26{,}420 = 3{,}963
$$

**Step 5 — Final estimated cost**

$$
\boxed{C_{total} \approx USD \, 30{,}383}
$$

**Step 6 — Schedule check**

- MH/day capacity at 4 men × 2 shifts × 8 h = 64 MH.
- Net working days: $312 / 64 = 4.875$ days → **5 net days**.
- With 20% contingency: **6 days**, well within the 10-day docking window.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Propeller Works Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up CPP vs FPP:** CPP pitch is adjusted *without* engine reversal; FPP requires engine reversal for astern thrust.
> 2. **Confusing cavitation erosion with corrosion:** Cavitation is mechanical (bubble-collapse); corrosion is chemical/electrochemical. NAB resists *both*; HTB (Manganese Bronze) is prone to **dezincification**, a form of corrosion.
> 3. **Wrong preheat:** Always cite **150 °C maximum** for NAB. Don't write theoretical 0.7·T_sol values.
> 4. **Forgetting the +20% contingency buffer** in man-hour / cost estimation — marks awarded separately for *contingency methodology*.
> 5. **Skipping reference to Class rules (IACS UR M3, M58, M71).** At least one rule citation is *required* in any 14-mark survey/inspection question.
> 6. **Wrong formula for thrust scaling:** Thrust scales with $D^4$, *not* $D^2$. A 5% diameter reduction → ~20% thrust loss at constant RPM.
> 7. **Misidentifying blade stations:** $0.3R$, $0.7R$, $0.9R$ are *radius fractions*, not chord fractions.

---

## Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Propeller types:** FPP (most common in merchant), CPP (manoeuvring vessels), Kaplan/nozzle (tugs), VSP (special), water-jet (fast).
- ✅ **Materials:** HTB (legacy, dezincification risk) vs NAB (modern, Class-preferred) vs Stainless Steel / Titanium (special).
- ✅ **Key Class rules:** **IACS UR M3** (Propellers), **UR M58** (In-Water Survey), **UR M71** (Shafting alignment).
- ✅ **Survey triggers:** Annual → Intermediate → Special (5-year Class Renewal); also unscheduled (accident, performance drop).
- ✅ **Inspection methods:** Visual, dimensional (diameter, pitch), NDT (UT, MPI, DPI), dynamic balance (ISO 1940 G 6.3).
- ✅ **Defect catalog:** Cavitation erosion, leading-edge cracks, bent blades, fouling, hub/keyway fretting.
- ✅ **Repair methods:** Fairing & polishing, weld build-up (GTAW with ER CuAl), blade cropping & re-pitch, full replacement.
- ✅ **Critical tolerances:** Diameter reduction ≤ 3% (UR M3), thickness loss ≤ 10%, pitch deviation ≤ ±2%, preheat 100–150 °C for NAB.
- ✅ **Efficiency loss formula:** $\Delta \eta_0 \,(\%) \approx 0.85(\overline{Ra}_2^{0.3} - \overline{Ra}_1^{0.3})$ (Schmiedl).
- ✅ **Thrust scaling:** $T \propto n^2 D^4$; torque $Q \propto n^2 D^5$.
- ✅ **Dynamic balance grade:** ISO 1940 **G 6.3** for marine propellers → e·ω = 6.3 mm/s.
- ✅ **Estimation:** Always add **15–20% contingency** to man-hours; plan 1 buffer day per major welding activity for NDT re-inspection.
- ✅ **Underwater surveys** allow inspection without dry-docking — but Class still requires withdrawal at Special Survey intervals.
- ✅ **EEXI/CII compliance** makes propeller polishing a regulatory as well as economic necessity post-2023.
- ✅ **Practical takeaway:** A 2% efficiency drop ≈ **USD 100k/year** in wasted fuel for a mid-size bulker — propeller works is *high-ROI* maintenance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chain Lockers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chain Lockers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Chain Locker** is a dedicated, watertight compartment located in the forward region of a ship, immediately abaft (behind) the hawse pipes and below the forecastle deck, used exclusively for the stowage of the anchor chain cables when the anchors are fully housed. It forms an integral part of the ship's hull structure forward and is treated as a **special-purpose space** under SOLAS and Classification Society rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST861 – Module 4):**
> Chain lockers are studied under the "Hull Inspection & Survey" cluster. Students must know (a) structural design, (b) lining/coating requirements, (c) survey and defect identification, and (d) repair methodology with **Bill of Materials (BOM) and cost estimation**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine a **garage built specifically to park a heavy, greasy, rust-prone roller-chain** that can be thrown out (dropped) at any moment. The garage must be:

1. **Tough and reinforced** – because the chain is enormously heavy (often **100 to 200 tonnes per side**) and slams down with kinetic energy.
2. **Slip-resistant and soft on the inside** – so the chain doesn't damage the steel floor and the noise is damped.
3. **Self-draining** – because sea water constantly drips in through the hawse pipes.
4. **Vented** – to release hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) gas that forms from chain grease and seawater reaction.
5. **Easily inspectable** – because surveyors and crew must be able to crawl in and check every link for cracks and wear.

That, in essence, is a chain locker.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standards

| Parameter | Standard Metric |
|---|---|
| Location | Forward, below forecastle deck, abaft collision bulkhead |
| Capacity | ~30 m³ per side (typical for bulk carriers) |
| Chain diameter range | **50 mm – 130 mm** (depending on ship size) |
| Chain mass per metre | **~10 to 110 kg/m** |
| Coating thickness (chock hold) | **2.5 mm – 5 mm** (epoxy-based) |
| Slope of bottom | **1:30 to 1:40** toward drain plug |
| Drain plug diameter | **50 mm – 75 mm** (sea-water suction side) |
| Design rule | **IACS UR S21, SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 24, Class rules (DNV, LR, ABS, BV)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why chain lockers matter in repair estimation:**
> Chain lockers corrode faster than any other space on a ship. Sea water, dissolved oxygen, chlorides, and H₂S combine to create an extremely aggressive corrosion cell. **In ship repair yards, chain locker renewal is one of the top-five highest-cost steel-replacement jobs** alongside side-shell plates, hatch coamings, and bottom plating.

## 1.4 Functional Roles (Bullet List)

- **Stowage of anchor chain cable** in a controlled environment.
- **Prevention of chain self-rotation** (using stopper knobs, bitter ends, and devil's claw).
- **Containment of water ingress** from hawse pipe seals.
- **Gas venting** to expel H₂S and methane from biological degradation.
- **Accessibility for survey** of the cable and structural members.
- **Safety barrier** to prevent anchor cable from flailing into forward holds (cargo protection).
- **Structural reinforcement** of the forward hull girder (acts as a stiffened void compartment).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Design Philosophy

The chain locker must satisfy **four conflicting demands simultaneously**:

1. **Strength** – resist the dynamic braking force of a free-falling chain (during emergency letting-go or while paying out in rough weather).
2. **Tightness** – prevent water seepage into forward cargo holds via the collision bulkhead.
3. **Corrosion resistance** – survive decades in a permanently damp, oxygenated, chloride-rich micro-environment.
4. **Surveyability** – every internal surface must be reachable by a human inspector.

### 2.1.1 Design Load Analysis (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 – Determine the maximum breaking load of the chain:**
The classification society rules prescribe the chain cable's **proof load (PL)** and **breaking load (BL)** based on the **equipment number (EN)** of the ship.

**Step 2 – Compute the stowed chain weight per locker:**
The chain hangs under gravity, supported at the bitter end. The dynamic shock load during a free fall is amplified by a **dynamic amplification factor (DAF)**.

**Step 3 – Apply shock load to internal structures:**
The bottom shell plate of the locker must be doubled or tripled in thickness, with closely-spaced floors and longitudinals.

**Step 4 – Sizing of the chain cable compressor (stopper):**
A typical hydraulic compressor generates **30–60 tonnes** of braking force. The compressor's foundation must be welded to extra-thick doubling plates.

## 2.2 Lining & Coating System (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Chain lockers are **class-mandated** to be coated with a special high-build, abrasion-resistant system. The most common specifications are:

| Layer | Coating Type | DFT (Dry Film Thickness) |
|---|---|---|
| Primer | Zinc-rich epoxy | 50 – 75 µm |
| Tie coat | Epoxy mastic | 100 – 150 µm |
| Body coat | Glass-flake filled epoxy | 500 – 1000 µm |
| Top coat (optional) | Polysiloxane / polyurethane | 50 – 75 µm |
| **Total System DFT** | | **700 – 1300 µm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why glass-flake epoxy?**
> Microscopic glass flakes align parallel to the substrate, creating a **"tortuous path"** for water and chloride ions. This is the same technology used in ballast tanks and is the **industry standard for chain lockers** under IACS UR S21 and PSPC (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are essential for **estimation and repair** of chain lockers:

### 2.3.1 Chain Mass per Locker

$$
W_c = L \times m
$$

where:
- $W_c$ = total chain mass stowed (kg)
- $L$ = total length of cable stowed (m)
- $m$ = chain mass per unit length (kg/m)

### 2.3.2 Total Chain Stowage Volume (Capacity Check)

$$
V_{req} = \frac{\pi d_c^2}{4} \times L \times k
$$

where:
- $d_c$ = chain link diameter (m)
- $k$ = stowage factor (typically **1.4 – 1.8** for studded chain in compact stowage)
- $V_{req}$ = required stowage volume (m³)

### 2.3.3 Coating Material Estimation

$$
A_{coat} = (2 \times L_w \times H_w) + (L_w \times B_w) + (2 \times H_w \times B_w)
$$

where $L_w$, $H_w$, $B_w$ are the locker's effective length, height, and breadth respectively.

Theoretical paint quantity (litres) is then:

$$
Q_{paint} = \frac{A_{coat} \times DFT_{total}}{10 \times VS\% \times \rho_{paint}}
$$

where:
- $DFT_{total}$ = total dry film thickness (µm)
- $VS\%$ = volume solids of paint (%)
- $\rho_{paint}$ = paint density (kg/L)

### 2.3.4 Repair Cost Estimation (BOM)

$$
C_{total} = C_{steel} + C_{prep} + C_{coat} + C_{labour} + C_{overhead}
$$

| Term | Description | Typical Value (USD, 2024) |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{steel}$ | Replacement plate + stiffeners | 8,000 – 25,000 |
| $C_{prep}$ | Blast to Sa 2.5 + primer | 4,000 – 10,000 |
| $C_{coat}$ | Full glass-flake epoxy system | 6,000 – 15,000 |
| $C_{labour}$ | Man-hours × rate | 10,000 – 30,000 |
| $C_{overhead}$ | Yard margin + consumables | 15 – 25% of subtotal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> These formulas are used directly by **ship repair estimators and class surveyors** in tenders for **special survey (SS) and dry-dock repair (DDR)** projects. Errors in $V_{req}$ or $A_{coat}$ can cause **15–30% cost overruns**, which is a common KTU 14-mark question scenario.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Chain Stowage Volume for a Capesize Bulk Carrier

### Given Data
- Chain diameter, $d_c = 0.097$ m (97 mm studded)
- Length of cable per side, $L = 385$ m (typical for Capesize)
- Stowage factor, $k = 1.6$
- Locker internal length, $L_w = 5.5$ m
- Locker internal breadth, $B_w = 3.8$ m
- Locker internal height, $H_w = 4.2$ m

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional area of one chain link bundle:**

$$
A_{link} = \frac{\pi \times d_c^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_{link} = \frac{\pi \times (0.097)^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_{link} = \frac{\pi \times 0.009409}{4} = 0.007390 \; m^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the geometric stowage volume (no voids):**

$$
V_{geom} = A_{link} \times L
$$

$$
V_{geom} = 0.007390 \times 385 = 2.8452 \; m^3
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the stowage factor to account for voids between links:**

$$
V_{req} = V_{geom} \times k
$$

$$
V_{req} = 2.8452 \times 1.6 = 4.5523 \; m^3
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the available volume of the locker:**

$$
V_{avail} = L_w \times B_w \times H_w
$$

$$
V_{avail} = 5.5 \times 3.8 \times 4.2 = 87.78 \; m^3
$$

**Step 5 — Check utilisation ratio (KTU Board-Examiner favourite):**

$$
\eta = \frac{V_{req}}{V_{avail}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{4.5523}{87.78} \times 100 = 5.19\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Only ~5% of the locker volume is occupied by chain. The remaining **~95%** is empty space that **collects water, sludge, and H₂S gas** — this is the principal reason chain lockers are so problematic for survey and repair.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Coating Quantity for a Chain Locker

### Given Data
- $L_w = 5.5$ m, $B_w = 3.8$ m, $H_w = 4.2$ m
- Total DFT = 900 µm
- Volume solids, $VS\% = 80\%$
- Paint density, $\rho_{paint} = 1.4$ kg/L
- Number of coats = 3

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the total surface area to be coated (all six internal faces):**

$$
A_{coat} = 2(L_w H_w + H_w B_w + L_w B_w)
$$

$$
A_{coat} = 2(5.5 \times 4.2 + 4.2 \times 3.8 + 5.5 \times 3.8)
$$

$$
A_{coat} = 2(23.10 + 15.96 + 20.90)
$$

$$
A_{coat} = 2 \times 59.96 = 119.92 \; m^2
$$

**Step 2 — Add 15% for wastage, overlaps, and difficult access:**

$$
A_{eff} = A_{coat} \times 1.15
$$

$$
A_{eff} = 119.92 \times 1.15 = 137.91 \; m^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute paint quantity (litres) using the standard formula:**

$$
Q_{paint} = \frac{A_{eff} \times DFT_{total}}{10 \times VS\% \times \rho_{paint}}
$$

$$
Q_{paint} = \frac{137.91 \times 900}{10 \times 80 \times 1.4}
$$

$$
Q_{paint} = \frac{124119}{1120} = 110.82 \; L
$$

**Step 4 — Round up + 10% contingency for KTU practical mark:**

$$
Q_{final} = 110.82 \times 1.10 = 121.90 \; L
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Approximately **122 litres of glass-flake epoxy** are required to coat one chain locker. For a ship with two lockers, double this value. In repair yards, the cost of paint alone can be **USD 6,000 – 15,000 per locker** (2024 prices).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Defect – Cause – Repair Method (KTU Board Favourite)

| Defect Observed | Probable Cause | Survey Method | Repair Method | Estimation Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heavy pitting on bottom plates | Galvanic + chloride attack | UT thickness gauging | Crop & renew plate | Cost based on plate kg × USD/kg |
| Perforation near drain plug | Crevice corrosion | Visual + hose test | Insert plate + doubler | Includes welding + NDT |
| Coating blistering | Osmotic pressure | Adhesion test | Blast to Sa 2.5, recoat | Coat reapplication BOM |
| Cracks at chain compressor foundation | Fatigue from cyclic load | MPI / DP test | Weld repair + doubler | Add 20% reinforcement |
| Loose / corroded drain plug | Thread wear | Function test | Renew plug + gasket | Material + 1 hr labour |
| H₂S smell / black water | Anaerobic bacterial activity | Gas detector | Flush, ventilate, recoat | Safety PPE cost +20% |
| Hawse pipe seal failure | Rubber packing aged | Visual + water test | Renew packing, caulk | Minor cost, critical defect |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Chain Locker Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Forecastle Deck Above] --> node2[Chain Locker Compartment]
    node3[Hawse Pipe Entry] --> node2
    node2 --> node4[Spurling Pipe Internal Guide]
    node4 --> node5[Chain Pile Stowed]
    node5 --> node6[Bitter End + Securing Device]
    node2 --> node7[Drain Plug to Sea]
    node2 --> node8[Vent Pipe to Above Deck]
    node2 --> node9[Access Manhole from Forecastle]
    node10[Chain Compressor / Devil Claw] --> node2
    node2 --> node11[Internal Lining Glass Flake Epoxy]
    node2 --> node12[Collision Bulkhead Aft Face]
    node2 --> node13[Bottom Plate Doubler Doubled Thickness]
    node2 --> node14[Survey Access for Thickness Gauging]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This flowchart traces the **physical path of the chain** (from hawse pipe → spurling pipe → stowage → bitter end) and overlays the **engineering systems** that interact with the locker — drainage, ventilation, coating, structural reinforcement, and survey access. Master this in 60 seconds before the KTU exam.

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Chain Locker Survey Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Initial Risk Assessment and Permit to Work] --> nodeB[Gas Test for H2S and O2 Deficiency]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Ventilation Forced for 30 Minutes]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Visual Inspection All Surfaces]
    nodeD --> nodeE[UT Thickness Measurement on Critical Plates]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Coating Adhesion Pull Off Test]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Photographic Documentation]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Defect Mapping on Plan]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Repair Specification and Cost Estimate]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Class Surveyor Approval]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Execution of Repairs]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Final Inspection and NDT]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Repair Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[Defect Identified] --> nodeS2{Is Corrosion Less Than 25 percent of Original Plate?}
```
