```mermaid
    nodeS2 -->|Yes| nodeS3[Local Surface Prep and Recoat Only]
    nodeS2 -->|No| nodeS4{Is Pitting Perforated?}
    nodeS4 -->|Yes| nodeS5[Insert New Plate Crop and Renew]
    nodeS4 -->|No| nodeS6[Renew Doubler Plate to 2 x Original Thickness]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS7[Apply Full Glass Flake Epoxy System]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS7
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8[Class Survey and Sign Off]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs use the **letter-then-number convention** (`node1`, `nodeS5`, etc.) to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 diagram rules. No reserved keywords are used as node names.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a chain locker. State any two mandatory design requirements under SOLAS / IACS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A chain locker is a **watertight compartment located in the forward region of a ship, abaft the hawse pipes and below the forecastle deck**, used for stowage of the anchor chain cable.

**Two mandatory design requirements:**
1. The locker must be **lined with an abrasion- and corrosion-resistant coating system** (typically glass-flake filled epoxy) to a minimum DFT of **900 µm** under IACS UR S21.
2. The locker must be provided with a **drain plug of at least 50 mm diameter** leading to the sea or a drain tank, and **independent venting** to release H₂S gas.

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition + 2 marks design requirements.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is H₂S gas accumulation a critical safety concern during chain locker survey?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
H₂S is produced by **anaerobic bacterial reduction of sulfates** in stagnant seawater mixed with chain grease and organic debris. It is:
- **Toxic at 100 ppm** (causes olfactory nerve paralysis in minutes).
- **Explosive between 4% and 44%** by volume in air.
- **Heavier than air**, so it accumulates at the bottom of the locker.

**Survey protocol:** Forced ventilation for at least **30 minutes**, continuous gas monitoring, and use of **self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA)** by inspectors before entry.

*(Mark split: 1 mark mechanism + 1 mark hazard levels + 1 mark safety protocol.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **constructional features of a chain locker** in a modern bulk carrier. Highlight the structural reinforcement, drainage, ventilation, and lining system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chain locker of internal dimensions **5.5 m × 3.8 m × 4.2 m** is to be re-coated with a glass-flake epoxy system of total **DFT 900 µm**. The paint has **80% volume solids** and density **1.4 kg/L**. Allowing **15% wastage** and **10% contingency**, calculate the total paint quantity required in litres. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

| Element | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Forward, between collision bulkhead and hawse pipe; below forecastle deck | 1 |
| Structural reinforcement | Doubled bottom plate, additional floors, stringers to resist chain shock load | 2 |
| Drainage | Self-draining bottom (slope 1:30) → 50–75 mm drain plug to sea chest or drain tank | 1 |
| Ventilation | Independent vent pipe extended to a safe height above deck | 1 |
| Lining | Glass-flake filled epoxy system, total DFT ≥ 900 µm (IACS UR S21) | 1 |
| Access | Watertight manhole from forecastle deck; internal ladder / rungs | 1 |

**Sketch** (block diagram in lieu of physical drawing):
```
FORECASTLE DECK ─────────────────────────
   │            │                    │
HAWSE PIPE    MANHOLE              VENT PIPE
   │            │                    │
   ▼            ▼                    ▲
┌──────── CHAIN LOCKER ───────────────┐
│ [Spurling Pipe]  [Chain Pile]       │
│ [Compressor]     [Bitter End]       │
│ [Lined with glass-flake epoxy]      │
│ Bottom sloped 1:30 to drain plug ───┘
└──────────────────────────────────────┘
       ▲ COLLISION BULKHEAD AFT
```

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Surface area calculation** (2 marks):

$$
A_{coat} = 2(L_w H_w + H_w B_w + L_w B_w)
$$

$$
A_{coat} = 2(5.5 \times 4.2 + 4.2 \times 3.8 + 5.5 \times 3.8) = 119.92 \; m^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply 15% wastage** (1 mark):

$$
A_{eff} = 119.92 \times 1.15 = 137.91 \; m^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply paint quantity formula** (3 marks):

$$
Q_{paint} = \frac{A_{eff} \times DFT}{10 \times VS\% \times \rho}
$$

$$
Q_{paint} = \frac{137.91 \times 900}{10 \times 80 \times 1.4} = 110.82 \; L
$$

**Step 4 — Add 10% contingency and present final answer** (1 mark):

$$
Q_{final} = 110.82 \times 1.10 \approx 121.9 \; L \approx \mathbf{122 \; litres}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **survey procedure for a chain locker during special survey (SS)**. List the critical checkpoints, NDT requirements, and class-society acceptance criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a special survey, the chain locker bottom plate of a Panamax bulk carrier shows **30% wastage** with isolated pitting. Original plate thickness is **18 mm**. The locker internal dimensions are **4.8 m × 3.2 m × 3.9 m**. Prepare a **Bill of Materials (BOM) and cost estimate** in tabular form for:
- Steel renewal (using 20 mm plate, cost USD 3.50/kg)
- Surface preparation (USD 25/m²)
- Glass-flake epoxy coating (USD 80/m²)
- Labour (USD 45/man-hour, 200 man-hours)

Use a **20% yard overhead** on material cost. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

| Step | Action | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk assessment, gas test for H₂S and O₂, ventilation, permit to work | 1 |
| 2 | Visual inspection of all accessible internal surfaces (photograph defects) | 1 |
| 3 | UT thickness measurement on bottom plate, side shell, and deck plate at 5-point grid | 2 |
| 4 | Coating adhesion test (pull-off, minimum 5 MPa) per IACS UR S21 | 1 |
| 5 | NDT on chain compressor foundation and bilge wells (MPI / DP) | 1 |
| 6 | Verify drain plug function, vent pipe integrity, manhole gasket condition | 1 |

**Acceptance criteria:** Minimum thickness ≥ **75% of original** (as-built) per IACS. Wastage in excess of 25% triggers **crop-and-renew** recommendation.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute bottom plate area (full renewal assumed):** (1 mark)

$$
A_{bottom} = L_w \times B_w = 4.8 \times 3.2 = 15.36 \; m^2
$$

With **20 mm plate**, density of steel = **7850 kg/m³**:

$$
M_{steel} = A_{bottom} \times t \times \rho = 15.36 \times 0.020 \times 7850 = 2411.52 \; kg
$$

**Step 2 — Internal surface area (all six faces) for prep + coat:** (1 mark)

$$
A_{int} = 2(L_w H_w + H_w B_w + L_w B_w) = 2(18.72 + 12.48 + 15.36) = 93.12 \; m^2
$$

**Step 3 — Build the BOM table:** (4 marks)

| Item | Quantity | Rate (USD) | Cost (USD) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steel plate 20 mm | 2,412 kg | 3.50/kg | 8,442.00 |
| Surface preparation (blast to Sa 2.5) | 93.12 m² | 25.00/m² | 2,328.00 |
| Glass-flake epoxy coating | 93.12 m² | 80.00/m² | 7,449.60 |
| Labour | 200 man-hours | 45.00/hr | 9,000.00 |
| **Subtotal (Material + Labour)** | | | **27,219.60** |
| Yard overhead @ 20% of material cost (8,442 + 2,328 + 7,449.60) = 18,219.60 | | | 3,643.92 |
| **Grand Total** | | | **USD 30,863.52** |

**Step 4 — Round off and present final answer:** (1 mark)

$$
\boxed{C_{total} \approx \mathbf{USD \; 30,864}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-losing mistakes in chain locker questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert thickness from mm to m** in the steel mass formula → loses 2 marks silently.
> 2. **Mixing up volume solids and weight solids** when applying the paint quantity formula → loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the wastage/contingency factors** in coating estimation → examiner deducts 1 mark for "lack of practical awareness".
> 4. **Drawing the chain locker sketch without slope indication** for drainage → lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Using the wrong rule reference** (e.g., citing SOLAS for coating) — coating is IACS UR S21; watertight integrity is SOLAS.
> 6. **Omitting H₂S safety protocol** in survey answers — examiner will deduct 1 mark for incomplete safety consideration.
> 7. **Not mentioning pull-off adhesion test value (≥ 5 MPa)** — a class-specific number that examiners love to test.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A chain locker is a forward, below-forecastle, watertight compartment for anchor chain stowage.
- **Location:** Between hawse pipes and collision bulkhead, below forecastle deck.
- **Chain mass per locker:** Often **100–200 tonnes per side**; dynamic shock loads are extreme.
- **Stowage factor** $k$: **1.4–1.8** (account for voids between studded links).
- **Locker volume utilisation:** Typically only **5–10%** occupied by chain; the rest is water/sludge/gas.
- **Drainage:** Self-draining slope **1:30 to 1:40**; drain plug ≥ **50 mm** diameter.
- **Ventilation:** Independent vent pipe to release H₂S and methane.
- **Coating system:** Glass-flake filled epoxy; total DFT **≥ 900 µm**; **3-coat minimum**.
- **Pull-off adhesion:** ≥ **5 MPa** acceptance (IACS UR S21).
- **Minimum acceptable thickness:** **75% of original** as-built; below that → crop & renew.
- **Critical defects:** Perforation, compressor foundation cracks, drain plug failure, coating blistering.
- **Common NDT:** UT thickness, MPI/DP for cracks, pull-off adhesion for coating.
- **Safety:** **H₂S + O₂ test + forced ventilation + SCBA** mandatory before entry.
- **Repair philosophy:** Crop & renew if wastage > 25%; insert plate if perforated; recoat always.
- **Cost drivers (2024):** Steel (USD 3–4/kg), surface prep (USD 20–30/m²), coating (USD 60–100/m²), labour (USD 40–50/hr).
- **Rules to cite:** **SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 24**, **IACS UR S21**, **Class rules (DNV / LR / ABS / BV)**.
- **BOM formula:** $C_{total} = C_{steel} + C_{prep} + C_{coat} + C_{labour} + C_{overhead}$.
- **Paint quantity formula:** $Q_{paint} = \dfrac{A_{eff} \times DFT}{10 \times VS\% \times \rho_{paint}}$.
- **Always add 15% wastage + 10% contingency** in paint calculation (practical yard norm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second KTU Revision Mantra:**
> "**LOCATION = Forward, Below Forecastle, Aft of Hawse. LINING = Glass-Flake Epoxy ≥ 900 µm. DRAINAGE = 1:30 slope + 50 mm plug. VENTING = H₂S release. SURVEY = UT + MPI + Pull-Off. REPAIR = > 25% wastage → renew. SAFETY = gas test + ventilation + SCBA.**"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steel Works
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steel Works

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Steel Works** in the context of ship survey, estimation, and repair refer to the complete set of engineering operations involving the **inspection, measurement, renewal, fabrication, and fitting of structural steel components** of a vessel's hull, superstructure, and associated parts. These works are governed by the rules of Classification Societies (IACS members: ABS, DNV, LR, BV, IRS, etc.), SOLAS, MARPOL, and flag-state regulations.

Steel works include:
- **Renewal steel** (replacement of corroded/damaged plates, sections, stiffeners)
- **Renewal criteria** (minimum acceptable thickness, wastage allowance)
- **Steel grades** (ordinary strength and high tensile steel)
- **Cutting, fabrication, fitting, and welding** operations
- **Nesting, rolling, and forming** of plates
- **Weight estimation** of steel required for repairs
- **Cost estimation** (material, labor, consumables, testing)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** *Steel Works* is the comprehensive term used in ship-repair yards to describe all activities that involve the **removal of worn-out or defective steel** and its **replacement with new steel** conforming to the original or upgraded specifications, including cutting, prefabrication, welding, and survey verification.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **doctor examining a patient** with bone damage (the ship surveyor examining steel). The doctor orders **X-rays** (ultrasonic thickness measurement, UTM), **identifies damaged bones** (corroded plates), and prescribes **surgery and replacement** with prosthetics (renewal steel inserted by welding). Just as a surgeon must know:
- How much bone to remove
- The grade of prosthetic material
- The cost of the procedure
- Post-operative checks

…the ship surveyor must know:
- How much steel to renew
- The grade of replacement steel
- The cost of renewal (material + labor + consumables)
- Post-renewal survey (NDE, pressure test, sea trial)

**Steel works = the "surgery" stage of ship repair** — it follows diagnosis (survey) and precedes recovery (sea trial).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Steel works are usually the **single largest cost component** in a dry-dock repair, often accounting for **40–60% of the total repair bill** in conventional merchant vessels. Hence, accurate estimation is critical for shipowners and shipyards alike.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| Density of mild steel | **7.85 kg/dm³** (i.e., **7850 kg/m³**) |
| Density of high-tensile steel | **7.85 kg/dm³** (essentially same) |
| 1 tonne (metric) | **1000 kg** |
| 1 long ton (UK) | **1016 kg** |
| 1 short ton (USA) | **907.2 kg** |
| Yield strength – Grade A | **235 N/mm²** |
| Yield strength – Grade AH32/DH32/EH32 | **315 N/mm²** |
| Yield strength – Grade AH36/DH36/EH36 | **355 N/mm²** |
| Corrosion addition (typical) | **0.5 mm to 2.0 mm** (depends on compartment) |
| Wastage allowance (typical) | **1.0 mm to 2.0 mm** per side |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Sequence of Steel Works Operations (Logical Flow)

1. **Initial Survey (Pre-Docking)**
   - Visual inspection, UTM readings, condition assessment.
   - Identify the scope of renewal (which plates, sections, stiffeners).

2. **Docking and Cleaning**
   - High-pressure fresh-water washing, sandblasting, chipping, scaling.
   - Reveals true condition of steel (corrosion, cracks, pitting).

3. **Final Survey and Marking**
   - Surveyor (Class / Owner) marks **renewal zones** with chalk/paint.
   - Limits of renewal are clearly drawn on the hull.

4. **Cutting and Removal of Old Steel**
   - Oxy-fuel cutting, plasma cutting, or cold cutting (for critical areas).
   - Old steel is removed piece by piece and weighed (wastage steel).

5. **Preparation of New Steel**
   - **Nesting** of plates on standard stock plates (minimise wastage).
   - Cutting to size, edge preparation (V, X, U, J grooves for welding).
   - Rolling/forming to required curvature (shell plates, deck plates).

6. **Pre-Fabrication**
   - Sub-assemblies built outside the dock (panels, brackets, inserts).
   - Improves productivity and quality.

7. **Fitting and Welding**
   - Tack welding, alignment, full-penetration welding.
   - Sequence welding to minimise distortion.

8. **Inspection and Testing (NDE)**
   - Visual, MPI/DPI, UT, RT as required.
   - Class surveyor attendance.

9. **Final Documentation**
   - Steel weight statement, weld maps, test reports, Class certificates.

---

## 2.2 Steel Grades Used in Shipbuilding (IACS UR Z23)

| Grade | Yield Strength (N/mm²) | Tensile Strength (N/mm²) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 235 | 400–520 | Mild steel – shell, decks (temperate) |
| **B** | 235 | 400–520 | Mild steel – shell (slightly lower temp) |
| **D** | 235 | 400–520 | Mild steel – shell (cold service) |
| **E** | 235 | 400–520 | Mild steel – shell (Arctic service) |
| **AH32** | 315 | 440–590 | High tensile – side, bottom, deck |
| **DH32** | 315 | 440–590 | High tensile – side, bottom, deck (cold) |
| **EH32** | 315 | 440–590 | High tensile – Arctic service |
| **AH36** | 355 | 490–620 | High tensile – highly stressed areas |
| **DH36** | 355 | 490–620 | High tensile – highly stressed (cold) |
| **EH36** | 355 | 490–620 | High tensile – Arctic service |
| **FH36** | 355 | 490–620 | High tensile – Polar service |

> [!NOTE]
> **A** = As-rolled, **D** = Normalised, **E** = Normalised fine-grain, **F** = Quenched & Tempered.
> The suffix **32, 36, 40** denotes the **minimum yield strength in ksi × 10** (legacy imperial nomenclature).

---

## 2.3 Renewal Thickness Criteria (Corrosion Addition & Wastage)

The **renewal thickness** (minimum acceptable thickness before Class/Owner requires renewal) is calculated as:

$$t_{\text{renewal}} \;=\; t_{\text{as-built}} \;-\; (\text{corrosion addition} + \text{wastage allowance})$$

**Plain English:** Subtract the **expected lifetime corrosion** and an **extra margin (wastage)** from the original (as-built) thickness. Anything thinner than this must be renewed.

| Component | Corrosion Addition (typical) |
|---|---|
| Bottom plating (in ballast tanks) | **1.0 – 1.5 mm** |
| Side shell (in ballast tanks) | **0.8 – 1.2 mm** |
| Deck plating (exposed) | **0.5 – 1.0 mm** |
| Tank internal structure | **0.5 – 1.0 mm** |
| Superstructure | **0.3 – 0.5 mm** |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = L \times B \times t \times \rho$ | Weight of a rectangular plate | kg |
| 2 | $W = \pi \times d \times t \times L \times \rho$ | Weight of a pipe / cylinder wall | kg |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{A_{\text{renewal}}}{A_{\text{plate stock}}} \times 100\%$ | Nesting efficiency | % |
| 4 | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{steel}} + C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{weld}} + C_{\text{NDE}}$ | Total steel-work cost | currency |
| 5 | $t_{\text{new}} = t_{\text{meas}} + t_{\text{add}}$ | New plate thickness (renewal) | mm |
| 6 | $t_{\text{add}} = t_{\text{corr}} + t_{\text{wastage}}$ | Total thickness addition | mm |
| 7 | $V_{\text{old steel}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{wastage}}}{\rho}$ | Volume of old steel removed | m³ |
| 8 | $\text{Weight (t)}} = \dfrac{L\,(\text{m}) \times B\,(\text{m}) \times t\,(\text{mm})}{1000} \times 7.85$ | Quick plate weight (in tonnes) | t |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion trick for plate weight:** Multiply Length (m) × Width (m) × Thickness (mm) × 7.85, then divide by 1000 → answer in **tonnes**. This is the most-used formula in any KTU ship-repair estimation paper.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Steel works form the **backbone of ship-repair economics**. For a shipowner:
- Steel renewal cost determines **off-hire duration** and **insurance claims**.
- Accurate estimation prevents **budget overruns**.
- Class-approved renewal ensures **continued trading certification**.

For a shipyard:
- Steel procurement, nesting, and fabrication efficiency determine **profit margin**.
- Welder productivity (kg/h of weld deposit) is a key KPI.
- Wastage steel (old steel sold as scrap) is a small but real **revenue stream**.

> [!TIP]
> In a typical **Panamax bulk carrier dry-docking**, the steel renewal weight often lies between **50 – 300 tonnes**, with a unit cost of **USD 1,200 – 2,500 per tonne** (all-in, including labor, consumables, NDE).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the "Plate Weight in Tonnes" Formula

**Starting point:** Physical principle of mass = volume × density.

For a rectangular steel plate of:
- Length = $L$ (in metres)
- Breadth = $B$ (in metres)
- Thickness = $t$ (in millimetres)
- Density of steel = $\rho = 7.85 \,\text{kg/dm}^3 = 7850 \,\text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 – Express dimensions in SI metres:**
- Length = $L$ m
- Breadth = $B$ m
- Thickness = $\dfrac{t}{1000}$ m (convert mm → m)

**Step 2 – Compute volume in cubic metres:**

$$
V \;=\; L \times B \times \frac{t}{1000} \;\; (\text{m}^3)
$$

**Step 3 – Apply mass = volume × density:**

$$
m \;=\; V \times \rho \;=\; L \times B \times \frac{t}{1000} \times 7850 \;\; (\text{kg})
$$

**Step 4 – Convert kilograms to tonnes (divide by 1000):**

$$
m \;=\; L \times B \times t \times \frac{7850}{1\,000\,000} \;\; (\text{tonnes})
$$

**Step 5 – Simplify the constant 7850 / 1 000 000:**

$$
m \;=\; L \times B \times t \times 0.00785
$$

Or equivalently (and more convenient for back-of-envelope work):

$$
\boxed{\,m \;\; (\text{tonnes}) \;=\; \frac{L \times B \times t \times 7.85}{1000}\,}
$$

with $L$ and $B$ in **metres**, $t$ in **mm**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A corroded bottom plate of dimensions **8.0 m × 2.0 m**, original as-built thickness **12.0 mm**, is to be renewed. The measured average thickness is **7.5 mm**. Calculate:
1. The wastage of steel (in kg) removed.
2. The renewal thickness required.
3. The new plate weight (in tonnes) if a 14 mm thick plate is fitted.

### Part (i) — Wastage Steel Weight

**Step 1:** Thickness removed = original − measured
$$
\Delta t \;=\; 12.0 \;-\; 7.5 \;=\; 4.5 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Volume removed
$$
V \;=\; 8.0 \times 2.0 \times \frac{4.5}{1000} \;=\; 0.072 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Mass of wastage steel
$$
m_{\text{waste}} \;=\; 0.072 \times 7850 \;=\; 565.2 \;\text{kg}
$$

> The yard can sell this as scrap (typically at 60–70 % of new-steel price).

### Part (ii) — Renewal Thickness

**Step 1:** Assume corrosion addition = **1.0 mm** (bottom plate, ballast tank), wastage allowance = **1.0 mm**.

**Step 2:** Total addition
$$
t_{\text{add}} \;=\; 1.0 + 1.0 \;=\; 2.0 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Renewal thickness
$$
t_{\text{renewal}} \;=\; t_{\text{as-built}} - t_{\text{add}} \;=\; 12.0 - 2.0 \;=\; 10.0 \;\text{mm}
$$

Since measured 7.5 mm < 10.0 mm → **renewal is mandatory**.

### Part (iii) — New Plate Weight (14 mm fitted)

$$
m_{\text{new}} \;=\; \frac{8.0 \times 2.0 \times 14 \times 7.85}{1000} \;=\; \frac{1758.4}{1000} \;=\; 1.758 \;\text{tonnes}
$$

---

## 3.3 Cost Estimation Derivation (All-In Cost per Tonne)

**Step 1 – Material cost (Cₘ):**
$$
C_m \;=\; m_{\text{new}} \times P_{\text{steel}}
$$
where $P_{\text{steel}}$ = price of steel plate per tonne (market rate).

**Step 2 – Labor cost (Cₗ):**
$$
C_l \;=\; T_{\text{labour}} \times R_{\text{labour}}
$$
where $T$ = man-hours, $R$ = labour rate per hour.

**Step 3 – Welding consumable cost (C_w):**
$$
C_w \;=\; W_{\text{deposit}} \times P_{\text{electrode}}
$$
where $W$ = kg of weld metal deposited.

**Step 4 – NDE and testing cost (C_n):**
$$
C_n \;=\; N_{\text{tests}} \times P_{\text{test}}
$$

**Step 5 – Total cost:**
$$
\boxed{\,C_{\text{total}} \;=\; C_m + C_l + C_w + C_n\,}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Marks Distribution

| Sub-topic | Marks Weightage (approx.) | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|
| Steel grade classification & properties | 2–3 | Remember / Understand |
| Plate weight calculation | 3–4 | Apply |
| Renewal thickness criteria | 2–3 | Understand / Apply |
| Cost estimation (component-wise) | 3–4 | Apply / Analyse |
| IACS / Class rule interpretation | 2 | Remember / Understand |
| Welding & NDE awareness | 1–2 | Understand |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Steel Works

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Pre-Docking Survey] --> A2[UTM Readings and Visual]
    A2 --> A3{Condition OK?}
    A3 -->|No| A4[Mark Renewal Zones]
    A3 -->|Yes| A5[Continue in Service]
    A4 --> B1[Move to Dry Dock]
    B1 --> B2[Cleaning Sandblasting]
    B2 --> B3[Final Survey Confirmation]
    B3 --> C1[Cut Old Steel Oxy Plasma]
    C1 --> C2[Remove and Weigh Wastage]
    C2 --> D1[New Steel Nesting]
    D1 --> D2[Cutting Edge Prep]
    D2 --> D3[Rolling Forming]
    D3 --> D4[Pre-Fabrication Panels]
    D4 --> E1[Fitting and Tack Welding]
    E1 --> E2[Full Welding Sequence]
    E2 --> F1[NDE Visual MPI UT]
    F1 --> F2{Class Accepts?}
    F2 -->|No| E2
    F2 -->|Yes| G1[Documentation and Sign-off]
    G1 --> H1[Dock-out Sea Trial]
```

---

## 4.2 Steel Renewal Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Measured Thickness] --> S2{Compare with t_renewal}
    S2 -->|t_meas greater than t_renewal| S3[Accept Continue Survey]
    S2 -->|t_meas equals t_renewal| S4[Flag for Close Watch]
    S2 -->|t_meas less than t_renewal| S5[Renewal Required]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Class Surveyor Approval]
    S6 --> S7[Estimate Steel Required]
    S7 --> S8[Procure Cut Fit Weld]
```

---

## 4.3 Steel Grade Selection Logic (IACS UR Z23 / Z25)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G0[Choose Steel Grade] --> G1{Strength Needed?}
    G1 -->|Normal| G2[Mild Steel Grade A B D E]
    G1 -->|Higher| G3[High Tensile 32 36 40]
    G2 --> G4{Service Temp?}
    G4 -->|Greater than 0C| G5[Grade A]
    G4 -->|0 to minus 20C| G6[Grade B]
    G4 -->|minus 20 to minus 40C| G7[Grade D]
    G4 -->|Below minus 40C| G8[Grade E]
    G3 --> G9{Yield Reqd?}
    G9 -->|315 MPa| G10[AH DH EH 32]
    G9 -->|355 MPa| G11[AH DH EH 36]
    G9 -->|390 MPa| G12[AH DH EH 40]
```

---

## 4.4 Cost Build-Up Pyramid for Steel Works

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CT[Total Steel Work Cost] --> M[Material Cost 40 to 60 percent]
    CT --> L[Labour Cost 25 to 35 percent]
    CT --> W[Welding Consumables 8 to 12 percent]
    CT --> N[NDE and Testing 3 to 5 percent]
    CT --> O[Overheads and Profit 5 to 10 percent]
    M --> M1[Steel Plate Price]
    M --> M2[Profile Sections]
    L --> L1[Cutter Hours]
    L --> L2[Fitter Hours]
    L --> L3[Welder Hours]
    W --> W1[Electrodes Fluxes]
    W --> W2[Shielding Gas]
    N --> N1[Visual MPI UT]
    N --> N2[Class Fees]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three properties of Grade A shipbuilding steel and state its yield strength.**

**Model Answer:**
- Grade A is a **mild-steel** grade specified under **IACS UR Z23**.
- Minimum **yield strength = 235 N/mm²** (MPa).
- Tensile strength range = **400 – 520 N/mm²**.
- Elongation ≥ 22 % on a standard gauge length.
- Used for shell plating, deck plating, and internal structures in **non-arctic service** (design temperature above 0 °C).
- Supplied in **as-rolled** condition; suitable for normal thickness range **5 mm – 100 mm**.

*(3 marks – KTU typically awards 1 mark per correct point up to 3.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define "renewal thickness" in the context of ship steel works.**

**Model Answer:**
Renewal thickness is the **minimum acceptable plate thickness**, below which the plate must be **renewed (replaced)** to maintain the structural integrity and classification of the vessel. It is given by:

$$
t_{\text{renewal}} \;=\; t_{\text{as-built}} \;-\; (t_{\text{corrosion addition}} + t_{\text{wastage allowance}})
$$

It is determined by the **Classification Society** rules and the **owner's corrosion / wastage policy**, ensuring that the plate retains adequate strength to resist the design loads for the intended remaining service life of the vessel.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the explanation, 1 mark for the rule reference.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> KTU ESE rule: Two questions of 14 marks each, with internal choice (OR). Below, both alternatives are answered.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of shipbuilding steel** under IACS Unified Requirements, giving at least three grades of ordinary-strength and three grades of high-tensile steel. State the yield strength and typical application of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A side-shell plate of a bulk carrier is **6.0 m long × 1.8 m wide**, with original as-built thickness **14 mm**. The average measured thickness is **9.0 mm**. If the corrosion addition is **1.0 mm** and wastage allowance is **1.0 mm**, determine:
1. Whether renewal is required.
2. The weight of wastage steel (in kg).
3. The weight of new plate (in tonnes) if a **16 mm** plate is fitted. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a) — Steel Classification

**Ordinary-strength hull steels (Yield = 235 N/mm²):**

| Grade | Condition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | As-rolled | Shell, deck in temperate service (> 0 °C) |
| **B** | As-rolled | Shell in moderate service (0 to −20 °C) |
| **D** | Normalised | Shell, bottom in colder service (−20 °C) |
| **E** | Normalised fine-grain | Shell in arctic service (< −40 °C) |

**High-tensile hull steels:**

| Grade | Yield (N/mm²) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **AH32 / DH32 / EH32** | 315 | Highly stressed side, bottom, hatch coamings |
| **AH36 / DH36 / EH36** | 355 | Bow, stern, sheer strake, superstructure brackets |
| **AH40 / DH40 / EH40** | 390 | Ultra-large container ships, very high stress zones |

**Key points for full marks:**
- The suffix **A / D / E / F** denotes **impact-test temperature category** (A = +20 °C, D = −20 °C, E = −40 °C, F = −60 °C).
- The number **32 / 36 / 40** denotes **yield strength** (legacy: ksi × 10; metric = 315 / 355 / 390 N/mm²).
- Governed by **IACS UR Z23 (Hull Steel)** and **UR Z25 (Rolled Sections)**.

---

#### Solution to A(b) — Numerical

**1. Renewal check:**

$$
t_{\text{renewal}} \;=\; 14.0 \;-\; (1.0 + 1.0) \;=\; 12.0 \;\text{mm}
$$

Measured $9.0$ mm $< 12.0$ mm → **Renewal is required**. ✅

**2. Wastage steel weight:**

Thickness removed:
$$
\Delta t \;=\; 14.0 - 9.0 \;=\; 5.0 \;\text{mm}
$$

Volume:
$$
V \;=\; 6.0 \times 1.8 \times \frac{5.0}{1000} \;=\; 0.054 \;\text{m}^3
$$

Mass:
$$
m_{\text{waste}} \;=\; 0.054 \times 7850 \;=\; \mathbf{423.9 \;\text{kg}}
$$

**3. New plate weight (16 mm):**

$$
m_{\text{new}} \;=\; \frac{6.0 \times 1.8 \times 16 \times 7.85}{1000} \;=\; \frac{1356.48}{1000} \;=\; \mathbf{1.356 \;\text{tonnes}}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step sequence of steel works** carried out in a dry-dock, from initial survey to sea trial. Mention the role of the Classification Society surveyor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the **total cost of renewing 12 bottom plates**, each of size **4.0 m × 2.5 m**, with original thickness **12 mm** and measured thickness **6 mm**. New plate thickness is **14 mm**. Given data:
- Steel price = **USD 950 per tonne**
- Labour = **USD 35 per hour × 8 hours × 6 men × 2 days = USD 3360**
- Welding consumables = **USD 250 per tonne of new steel**
- NDE and testing = **USD 800 lump sum**
- Scrap recovery = **USD 200 per tonne of wastage steel**
- Density = **7.85 kg/dm³** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a) — Sequence of Steel Works

1. **Pre-docking survey** — visual and UTM to identify suspect areas.
2. **Docking and cleaning** — high-pressure wash, sandblasting, scaling.
3. **Final survey and marking** — Class surveyor and owner representative jointly mark renewal zones.
4. **Cutting and removal** — oxy-fuel or plasma cutting; old steel weighed and removed.
5. **New-steel preparation** — nesting, cutting, edge bevel preparation.
6. **Rolling/forming** — shell plates are rolled to the required curvature.
7. **Pre-fabrication** — sub-assemblies built outside the dock for efficiency.
8. **Fitting and tack welding** — alignment, dimensional checks.
9. **Full welding** — qualified procedure, qualified welders, sequence welding.
10. **NDE** — visual, MPI, UT, RT as per class rules.
11. **Final survey by Class** — issuing of **Class Repair Certificate**.
12. **Dock-out, sea trial, and sign-off** by Master and Class.

**Role of Classification Surveyor:** Approves renewal limits, supervises welding, attends NDE, and issues the **Class Repair Recommendation / Certificate** which is mandatory for the vessel to resume trading.

---

#### Solution to B(b) — Cost Estimation

**Step 1: Total renewal area**

$$
A \;=\; 12 \times (4.0 \times 2.5) \;=\; 12 \times 10 \;=\; 120 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Wastage thickness**

$$
\Delta t \;=\; 12 - 6 \;=\; 6 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3: Wastage weight**

$$
m_{\text{waste}} \;=\; \frac{120 \times 6 \times 7.85}{1000} \;=\; \frac{5652}{1000} \;=\; 5.652 \;\text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 4: New plate weight (14 mm)**

$$
m_{\text{new}} \;=\; \frac{120 \times 14 \times 7.85}{1000} \;=\; \frac{13188}{1000} \;=\; 13.188 \;\text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 5: Cost build-up**

| Item | Calculation | Cost (USD) |
|---|---|---|
| Material (steel) | $13.188 \times 950$ | **12 528.60** |
| Labour | given | **3 360.00** |
| Welding consumables | $13.188 \times 250$ | **3 297.00** |
| NDE and testing | lump sum | **800.00** |
| **Sub-total** | | **19 985.60** |
| Less: scrap recovery | $5.652 \times 200$ | **(1 130.40)** |
| **Net Total Cost** | | **≈ USD 18 855.20** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in plate weight calculations (results in answers 1000× too high or low).
> 2. **Confusing tonne vs ton**: use **1000 kg = 1 tonne (metric)** unless the question specifies long/short ton.
> 3. **Omitting scrap recovery credit** — examiners often award 1 mark for this in cost-estimation problems.
> 4. **Not stating the IACS rule reference** (e.g., UR Z23) — full-marks answers always cite the governing standard.
> 5. **Mixing up steel grade suffixes** (A vs AH, D vs DH) — a single-letter difference changes the application entirely.
> 6. **Forgetting to verify renewal necessity** before computing new plate cost — examiners want to see the decision step.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 📌 **Density of steel = 7.85 kg/dm³** (use this in **every** weight calculation).
- 📌 **Plate weight in tonnes = (L × B × t × 7.85) / 1000** with L, B in metres and t in mm.
- 📌 **Renewal thickness = As-built − (Corrosion addition + Wastage allowance)**.
- 📌 **Grade A, B, D, E** → ordinary strength (**235 N/mm²**); **A32, D32, E32** → **315 N/mm²**; **A36, D36, E36** → **355 N/mm²**.
- 📌 **A** = as-rolled, **D** = normalised, **E** = normalised fine-grain, **F** = quenched & tempered.
- 📌 Suffix **letter** = impact-test temperature category; **number** = yield strength (ksi×10 legacy).
- 📌 **IACS UR Z23** governs hull plates; **UR Z25** governs rolled sections; **UR S21** governs hull survey.
- 📌 Total steel-work cost = **Material + Labour + Welding Consumables + NDE − Scrap recovery**.
- 📌 Typical steel-work share in a dry-dock repair = **40 – 60 %** of total cost.
- 📌 **Always quote units** (mm, m, kg, tonnes) — KTU examiners are strict about this.
- 📌 **Show all intermediate steps** — even a simple multiplication; partial marks depend on the working shown.
- 📌 **Class surveyor must attend** cutting, fitting, welding, and NDE — mention this in any procedural answer.
- 📌 **Wastage steel is sold as scrap** — don't forget to deduct this credit in cost problems.
- 📌 **Nesting efficiency** ≥ 70 % is a benchmark for a well-planned repair yard.
- 📌 **Welding sequence matters** — always mention "qualified procedure, qualified welders" in fabrication answers.
- 📌 **High-tensile steel costs 10 – 20 % more** than mild steel — reflect this in cost estimates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planning Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Planning Chart** in the context of ship repair and maintenance engineering is a **graphical, time-scaled schematic representation** of all repair activities, tasks, milestones, resources, and their inter-dependencies, arranged chronologically to facilitate the systematic scheduling, monitoring, and control of dry-dock and afloat repair operations of a vessel.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with industrial standards, planning charts include:
- **Gantt Charts (Bar Charts)** – time-scaled task bars plotted against a calendar
- **CPM/PERT Network Diagrams** – logic-based precedence diagrams
- **Progress Curves ("S-Curves")** – cumulative output vs. time graphs
- **Man-hour Histograms** – resource loading diagrams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"A planning chart is a managerial tool used by ship repair planners to visualise the sequence, duration, and inter-relationship of repair jobs, thereby determining the critical path, total project duration, and resource requirements necessary to deliver the vessel back to the owner within the contracted repair period."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **wedding function that has 30 different tasks** — booking the hall, catering, decorations, music, priest, dresses, invitations, photography, etc. Some tasks *must* happen *before* others (you can't decorate before the hall is booked), some can run *in parallel* (catering and photography), and some have *float* (idle time) without delaying the function.

A **planning chart is the master whiteboard** where:
- Each task is written on a sticky note
- The note's length represents how long the task takes
- Arrows show which task must finish before another starts
- The longest chain of dependent tasks (the "critical path") determines the **earliest date the wedding can happen**

In a **shipyard**, the "wedding" is the **vessel delivery date** — the day the ship must leave the yard. Every steel renewal, engine overhaul, blasting job, and paint task is a sticky note on the planner's board. The planning chart is what ensures the ship leaves **on time, on budget, and on quality**.

## 1.3 Purpose of Planning Charts in Ship Repair

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Shipyards Cannot Operate Without Planning Charts:**
>
> 1. **Contractual Compliance** — Owners impose fixed delivery dates with **Liquidated Damages (LD)** for delay (often **$15,000 – $50,000/day** for commercial vessels)
> 2. **Resource Optimisation** — Yards have limited berths, dry-docks, cranes, and skilled labour
> 3. **Coordination of Multiple Trades** — A single repair may involve **15–25 specialised trades** (fitters, welders, electricians, painters, riggers)
> 4. **Progress Tracking** — Enables % completion reporting against a baseline
> 5. **Risk Identification** — Highlights bottlenecks before they cause delays

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted Constants)

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
|-----------|------------------------|
| Average repair period (dry-dock) | **14 – 45 days** |
| Number of tasks per vessel | **300 – 1,500 work packages** |
| Critical path length | **70 – 80% of total project duration** |
| Standard planning software | **Primavera P6, MS Project** |
| Planning horizon for ship repair | **T-60 to T+0 days** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Planning Charts Used in Ship Repair

### A. Gantt Chart (Bar Chart)
- **Developed by**: Henry Gantt (1917)
- **Format**: Horizontal bars plotted on a **time axis (X)** against a **task axis (Y)**
- **Bar length** = task duration
- **Bar position** = start and finish dates
- **Colour codes** indicate discipline, status, or progress
- **Most widely used chart in shipyards** for daily planning meetings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** When asked to "draw a planning chart", a Gantt chart is the **default expected answer** unless specifically asked for a network diagram.

### B. CPM (Critical Path Method) Network Diagram
- **Logic-based** chart showing **nodes (events)** connected by **arrows (activities)**
- Identifies the **critical path** — the longest sequence of dependent activities
- Used when **delay analysis** or **extension-of-time claims** are involved

### C. PERT (Programme Evaluation and Review Technique)
- Similar to CPM but uses **three time estimates** (optimistic, most likely, pessimistic)
- Used when task durations are **uncertain** (e.g., first-of-its-kind repair)

### D. Progress Curve (S-Curve)
- Plots **cumulative % completion (Y)** vs. **time (X)**
- The ideal curve is a smooth **"S" shape** — slow start, fast middle, tapering end
- Used to **forecast final completion** and detect early delays

### E. Man-hour / Resource Histogram
- Bar chart showing **man-hours per day (Y)** vs. **calendar date (X)**
- Used to **level workforce** and detect over- or under-allocation

## 2.2 Components of a Planning Chart

Every professional planning chart contains the following standard components:

1. **Header Block** — Vessel name, IMO number, repair scope, yard, contract number
2. **Task List** — Unique Job Number (JN), description, discipline
3. **Time Scale** — Calendar axis (days, weeks, or hours from vessel arrival)
4. **Activity Bars / Nodes** — Visual representation of work
5. **Milestones** — Diamond markers (◇) for key events (e.g., "Keel laid", "Sea trials")
6. **Dependencies** — Solid arrows showing predecessor-successor links
7. **Float / Slack** — Shaded or transparent bars showing available delay
8. **Critical Path** — Highlighted (usually **red**) bars/arrows
9. **Progress Overlay** — A second bar (often hatched) showing actual % complete
10. **Legend** — Colour codes, symbols, abbreviations

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **must-know** for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) and are tested regularly in Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Earliest Start Time | $ES_i = \max \{ EF_j \text{ for all predecessors } j \}$ | A task cannot start before its predecessors finish |
| 2 | Earliest Finish Time | $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$ | Earliest finish = start + duration |
| 3 | Latest Finish Time | $LF_i = \min \{ LS_k \text{ for all successors } k \}$ | Latest finish without delaying project |
| 4 | Latest Start Time | $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$ | Latest start = finish − duration |
| 5 | Total Float (Slack) | $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i = LF_i - EF_i$ | Time a task can be delayed without delaying project end |
| 6 | Free Float | $FF_i = ES_{\text{successor}} - EF_i$ | Delay allowed without delaying successor |
| 7 | Critical Path Duration | $T_{cp} = \sum_{i \in \text{critical}} D_i$ | Sum of durations of longest chain |
| 8 | PERT Expected Time | $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | Weighted average of three estimates |
| 9 | PERT Variance | $\sigma^2 = \left( \dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6} \right)^2$ | Uncertainty in activity duration |
| 10 | Project Variance | $\sigma^2_{\text{proj}} = \sum \sigma^2_i \text{ on critical path}$ | Total project uncertainty |
| 11 | Probability of on-time finish | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{proj}}}$ | Standard normal deviate |
| 12 | % Progress (S-Curve) | $\%P = \dfrac{\text{Man-hours earned}}{\text{Man-hours planned}} \times 100$ | Earned value indicator |
| 13 | Productivity Index | $PI = \dfrac{\text{Actual Progress \%}}{\text{Planned Progress \%}}$ | PI > 1 → ahead, PI < 1 → behind |
| 14 | Man-hour Loading | $MH_{\text{day}} = \sum_{i} n_i \times h_i$ | Sum of (workers × hours) for a day |

## 2.4 Why This System Matters in Engineering

In real shipyards:
- A **planner's daily life** revolves around the Gantt chart on the planning room wall
- A **1-day delay** in a critical activity = a 1-day delay in vessel delivery = **LD of $25,000+**
- The **critical path** is re-computed **every 24 hours** to maintain control
- **Earned Value Management (EVM)** uses planning charts to compute Cost Performance Index (CPI) and Schedule Performance Index (SPI)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Forward & Backward Pass on a CPM Network

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most common 14-mark KTU question pattern**. The student is given an activity table and asked to draw the network, compute ES/EF/LS/LF/TF, and identify the critical path.

### Given Activity Table

| Activity | Predecessor(s) | Duration (days) |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|
| A | — | 4 |
| B | — | 6 |
| C | A | 5 |
| D | A | 3 |
| E | B, C | 7 |
| F | D | 4 |
| G | E, F | 5 |

### Step 1 — List the Network Nodes

The terminal nodes are determined by **topological ordering**:
- A and B have no predecessors → start
- G has no successors → end

### Step 2 — Forward Pass (Compute ES and EF)

Starting from the start node (Day 0) and moving rightward:

$$
ES_A = 0, \quad EF_A = ES_A + D_A = 0 + 4 = 4
$$

$$
ES_B = 0, \quad EF_B = 0 + 6 = 6
$$

$$
ES_C = EF_A = 4, \quad EF_C = 4 + 5 = 9
$$

$$
ES_D = EF_A = 4, \quad EF_D = 4 + 3 = 7
$$

$$
ES_E = \max(EF_B, EF_C) = \max(6, 9) = 9, \quad EF_E = 9 + 7 = 16
$$

$$
ES_F = EF_D = 7, \quad EF_F = 7 + 4 = 11
$$

$$
ES_G = \max(EF_E, EF_F) = \max(16, 11) = 16, \quad EF_G = 16 + 5 = 21
$$

### Step 3 — Project Duration

$$
T_{\text{proj}} = EF_G = 21 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 4 — Backward Pass (Compute LS and LF)

Starting from the end node, project finish = 21 days, and moving leftward:

$$
LF_G = 21, \quad LS_G = 21 - 5 = 16
$$

$$
LF_F = LS_G = 16, \quad LS_F = 16 - 4 = 12
$$

$$
LF_E = LS_G = 16, \quad LS_E = 16 - 7 = 9
$$

$$
LF_D = LS_F = 12, \quad LS_D = 12 - 3 = 9
$$

$$
LF_C = LS_E = 9, \quad LS_C = 9 - 5 = 4
$$

$$
LF_B = LS_E = 9, \quad LS_B = 9 - 6 = 3
$$

$$
LF_A = \min(LS_C, LS_D) = \min(4, 9) = 4, \quad LS_A = 4 - 4 = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Total Float (TF)

$$
TF_i = LS_i - ES_i
$$

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? |
|----------|----------|----|----|----|----|----|-----------|
| A | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** | ✓ |
| B | 6 | 0 | 6 | 3 | 9 | 3 | ✗ |
| C | 5 | 4 | 9 | 4 | 9 | **0** | ✓ |
| D | 3 | 4 | 7 | 9 | 12 | 5 | ✗ |
| E | 7 | 9 | 16 | 9 | 16 | **0** | ✓ |
| F | 4 | 7 | 11 | 12 | 16 | 5 | ✗ |
| G | 5 | 16 | 21 | 16 | 21 | **0** | ✓ |

### Step 6 — Identify the Critical Path

$$
\boxed{\text{Critical Path: } A \to C \to E \to G, \quad T_{cp} = 4 + 5 + 7 + 5 = 21 \text{ days}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — PERT Three-Time Estimate

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU 2024 Part-A (3-mark) frequent question type.

### Given

| Activity | Optimistic $t_o$ | Most Likely $t_m$ | Pessimistic $t_p$ |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| X | 2 | 4 | 12 |
| Y | 1 | 3 | 5 |

### Step 1 — Expected Time for Activity X

$$
t_{e,X} = \frac{t_o + 4 t_m + t_p}{6} = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 12}{6} = \frac{2 + 16 + 12}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 2 — Variance for Activity X

$$
\sigma^2_X = \left( \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{12 - 2}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{10}{6} \right)^2 = 2.78
$$

### Step 3 — Expected Time for Activity Y

$$
t_{e,Y} = \frac{1 + 4(3) + 5}{6} = \frac{1 + 12 + 5}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 4 — Variance for Activity Y

$$
\sigma^2_Y = \left( \frac{5 - 1}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{4}{6} \right)^2 = 0.44
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — S-Curve Progress Analysis

### Given

A vessel was scheduled for **40 days**. The total man-hours budgeted = **8,000 MH**. At **Day 20**, the yard reports **3,600 MH earned**.

### Step 1 — Planned Progress at Day 20 (assuming linear)

$$
\%P_{\text{planned}} = \frac{20}{40} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

### Step 2 — Actual Progress

$$
\%P_{\text{actual}} = \frac{3,600}{8,000} \times 100 = 45\%
$$

### Step 3 — Productivity Index (PI)

$$
PI = \frac{\%P_{\text{actual}}}{\%P_{\text{planned}}} = \frac{45}{50} = 0.90
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PI = 0.90 < 1** → The vessel is **10% behind schedule**. The planner must immediately investigate critical-path activities and consider crashing, overtime, or parallel execution to recover the slippage.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the PERT Expected Time Formula

The PERT expected time $t_e$ is the **mean of a Beta distribution**. For a Beta distribution with mode at $t_m$ and range $[t_o, t_p]$, the mean is approximated by:

$$
t_e = \frac{t_o + 4 t_m + t_p}{6}
$$

This weights the most-likely time **four times** because in ship repair, the "most likely" duration is the planner's best engineering judgment based on similar past jobs. The factor of 6 normalises the sum of weights $(1 + 4 + 1 = 6)$.

The **standard deviation** of a Beta distribution (for PERT) is:

$$
\sigma = \frac{t_p - t_o}{6}
$$

This is because in a Beta distribution, the **range $[t_o, t_p]$** spans approximately **6 standard deviations** (i.e., $\pm 3\sigma$ on each side of the mean).

The **variance** is therefore:

$$
\sigma^2 = \left( \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} \right)^2
$$

For a project (sum of critical-path activities), variances **add** because activity durations are assumed independent:

$$
\sigma^2_{\text{proj}} = \sum_{i \in \text{critical}} \sigma^2_i
$$

The standard normal deviate for the probability of finishing by scheduled time $T_s$ is:

$$
Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{proj}}}
$$

where $T_e$ is the sum of expected times on the critical path. The probability of on-time completion is read from standard normal tables $P(Z)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Planning Chart Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following block diagram maps the **hierarchical structure** of a typical ship-repair planning chart and the data flow between its components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Header Block] --> B[Task Database]
    A1[Vessel Name] --> A
    A2[IMO Number] --> A
    A3[Repair Scope] --> A
    A4[Contract Period] --> A
    B --> C[Time Axis Calendar]
    B --> D[Activity Bars / Nodes]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Milestones Diamonds]
    D --> F[Dependency Arrows]
    D --> G[Float Bars Shaded]
    F --> H[Critical Path Highlight Red]
    G --> H
    D --> I[Progress Overlay Hatched]
    D --> J[Resource Histogram]
    D --> K[Man-hour Loading]
    L[Legend Colour Code] --> D
    L --> E
    L --> F
    L --> G
    L --> H
    L --> I
    M[Daily Progress Update] --> I
    M --> N[Productivity Index PI]
    M --> O[Earned Value EV]
    N --> P[Planner Decision]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Crash or Re-sequence]
    P --> R[Resource Re-allocate]
    P --> S[Owner Progress Report]
```

## 4.2 CPM Network Schematic

The following diagram shows a **CPM network for a hull-repair scope** with the critical path highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S((Start)) --> A1[Hull Blast Day 0 to 2]
    S --> B1[Engine Overhaul Day 0 to 5]
    A1 --> A2[Steel Renewal Day 2 to 7]
    A1 --> A3[Pipe Renewal Day 2 to 6]
    B1 --> B2[Align Shaft Day 5 to 7]
    A2 --> A4[Paint Hull Day 7 to 10]
    A3 --> A4
    B2 --> A4
    A4 --> F1((Vessel Delivery Day 10))
    A2 -. Float 3 days .-> A3
    B1 -. Float 0 days critical .-> B2
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, the **critical path** is S → A1 → A2 → A4 → F1, total = 10 days. Engine overhaul (B1) is *parallel* and also critical because it ties into paint completion.

## 4.3 S-Curve (Progress Curve) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCurve[Ideal S Curve]
        direction LR
        P1[0 percent Day 0] --> P2[10 percent Day 5 Slow Start]
        P2 --> P3[50 percent Day 20 Linear Mid]
        P3 --> P4[90 percent Day 35 Fast End]
        P4 --> P5[100 percent Day 40 Delivery]
    end
```

The **actual curve** typically **dips below** the ideal curve in the middle, then **catches up** at the end via overtime — a common pattern in real shipyards.

## 4.4 Gantt Chart Layout (Textual Schematic)

```
Job No. | Discipline | Task Description          | D-40 ... D-30 ... D-20 ... D-10 ... D0
--------|------------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------------------
JN-001  | Hull       | Steel Renewal P/S Fr 5-9  |          ████████
JN-002  | Hull       | Blast & Paint Topsides    |                ████████████
JN-003  | Machinery  | Main Engine Overhaul      |    ████████████
JN-004  | Machinery  | Generator Rewind          |          ██████
JN-005  | Electrical | Bow Thruster Service      |                ████
JN-006  | Painting   | Boot Top & Antifouling    |                      ████████████
JN-007  | Outfitting | Lifeboat Annual Service   |                            ████
        |            |                           |
        | MILESTONES |                           |◇ Keel Blocks  ◇ Dry-dock Flood ◇ Sea Trial ◇ Delivery
```

Legend: **█** = Planned, **▓** = Progress overlay, **◇** = Milestone

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a planning chart. List any four types of planning charts used in ship repair.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A planning chart is a graphical, time-scaled representation of repair activities, their durations, and inter-dependencies, used to schedule, monitor, and control the execution of ship repair work.

Four types:
1. **Gantt Chart (Bar Chart)** – Horizontal bars on a time axis
2. **CPM Network Diagram** – Logic-based activity-on-arrow diagram
3. **PERT Chart** – Uses three-time estimates
4. **S-Curve (Progress Curve)** – Cumulative % completion vs. time

*(1 mark definition + ½ mark × 4 types = 3 marks)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is meant by "critical path" in a planning chart? Why is it important in ship repair planning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **critical path** is the longest chain of dependent activities in a network that determines the **minimum project duration**. It is the sequence of tasks with **zero total float** — any delay in these tasks directly delays the vessel delivery.

**Importance in ship repair (any 3 points):**
1. Identifies tasks that need strict monitoring (1 mark)
2. Determines the minimum repair period committed to the owner (1 mark)
3. Basis for extension-of-time claims under contractual disputes (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**a)** Define a planning chart. With a neat sketch, explain the components of a **Gantt chart** as applied to ship repair planning. **(7 marks)**

**b)** The activities of a ship repair job are given below. Draw the **CPM network**, compute **ES, EF, LS, LF, and Total Float** for each activity, and identify the **critical path**. **(7 marks)**

| Activity | Predecessor(s) | Duration (days) |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|
| A | — | 5 |
| B | A | 4 |
| C | A | 6 |
| D | B | 3 |
| E | B, C | 5 |
| F | C | 4 |
| G | D, E | 6 |
| H | F, G | 5 |

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Forward Pass**

$$
ES_A = 0, \quad EF_A = 0 + 5 = 5
$$

$$
ES_B = EF_A = 5, \quad EF_B = 5 + 4 = 9
$$

$$
ES_C = EF_A = 5, \quad EF_C = 5 + 6 = 11
$$

$$
ES_D = EF_B = 9, \quad EF_D = 9 + 3 = 12
$$

$$
ES_E = \max(EF_B, EF_C) = \max(9, 11) = 11, \quad EF_E = 11 + 5 = 16
$$

$$
ES_F = EF_C = 11, \quad EF_F = 11 + 4 = 15
$$

$$
ES_G = \max(EF_D, EF_E) = \max(12, 16) = 16, \quad EF_G = 16 + 6 = 22
$$

$$
ES_H = \max(EF_F, EF_G) = \max(15, 22) = 22, \quad EF_H = 22 + 5 = 27
$$

**Project Duration = 27 days**

**Step 2 — Backward Pass**

$$
LF_H = 27, \quad LS_H = 27 - 5 = 22
$$

$$
LF_G = LS_H = 22, \quad LS_G = 22 - 6 = 16
$$

$$
LF_F = LS_H = 22, \quad LS_F = 22 - 4 = 18
$$

$$
LF_E = LS_G = 16, \quad LS_E = 16 - 5 = 11
$$

$$
LF_D = LS_G = 16, \quad LS_D = 16 - 3 = 13
$$

$$
LF_C = \min(LS_E, LS_F) = \min(11, 18) = 11, \quad LS_C = 11 - 6 = 5
$$

$$
LF_B = \min(LS_D, LS_E) = \min(13, 11) = 11, \quad LS_B = 11 - 4 = 7
$$

$$
LF_A = \min(LS_B, LS_C) = \min(7, 5) = 5, \quad LS_A = 5 - 5 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Total Float and Critical Path**

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? |
|----------|----------|----|----|----|----|----|-----------|
| A | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | **0** | ✓ |
| B | 4 | 5 | 9 | 7 | 11 | 2 | ✗ |
| C | 6 | 5 | 11 | 5 | 11 | **0** | ✓ |
| D | 3 | 9 | 12 | 13 | 16 | 4 | ✗ |
| E | 5 | 11 | 16 | 11 | 16 | **0** | ✓ |
| F | 4 | 11 | 15 | 18 | 22 | 7 | ✗ |
| G | 6 | 16 | 22 | 16 | 22 | **0** | ✓ |
| H | 5 | 22 | 27 | 22 | 27 | **0** | ✓ |

$$
\boxed{\text{Critical Path: } A \to C \to E \to G \to H, \quad T_{cp} = 5 + 6 + 5 + 6 + 5 = 27 \text{ days}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**

**a)** Explain the **Critical Path Method (CPM)** and **PERT** with their merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A ship repair project has the following activities with **three-time estimates** (in days). Compute the **expected time** and **variance** for each activity. If the critical path is A → C → E, find the **probability of completing the project in 25 days**. **(7 marks)**

| Activity | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ |
|----------|-------|-------|-------|
| A | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| C | 3 | 5 | 13 |
| E | 4 | 8 | 18 |

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Expected Time and Variance**

For Activity A:
$$
t_{e,A} = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}
$$
$$
\sigma^2_A = \left( \frac{6 - 2}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{4}{6} \right)^2 = 0.444
$$

For Activity C:
$$
t_{e,C} = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 13}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days}
$$
$$
\sigma^2_C = \left( \frac{13 - 3}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{10}{6} \right)^2 = 2.778
$$

For Activity E:
$$
t_{e,E} = \frac{4 + 4(8) + 18}{6} = \frac{54}{6} = 9 \text{ days}
$$
$$
\sigma^2_E = \left( \frac{18 - 4}{6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{14}{6} \right)^2 = 5.444
$$

**Step 2 — Project Expected Duration and Variance**

$$
T_e = t_{e,A} + t_{e,C} + t_{e,E} = 4 + 6 + 9 = 19 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\sigma^2_{\text{proj}} = 0.444 + 2.778 + 5.444 = 8.666
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{proj}} = \sqrt{8.666} = 2.944 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Normal Deviate for 25 days**

$$
Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{proj}}} = \frac{25 - 19}{2.944} = \frac{6}{2.944} = 2.04
$$

**Step 4 — Probability from Standard Normal Table**

For $Z = 2.04$, the cumulative probability $P(Z \leq 2.04) = 0.9793$

$$
\boxed{P(\text{finish in 25 days}) \approx 97.93\%}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong critical path** — Students often pick the path with the *most activities* instead of the *longest duration*. The critical path is **duration-based**, not count-based.
> 2. **Forgetting max/min in ES/LF** — Forward pass uses **max** of predecessor EFs; backward pass uses **min** of successor LSs. Marks deducted for incorrect use.
> 3. **Sign error in PERT Z-formula** — Must be $(T_s - T_e)$, not $(T_e - T_s)$.
> 4. **No units in float** — Always write "**days**" beside the TF value.
> 5. **Skipping the network diagram** — In 14-mark questions, the **network diagram is mandatory** (2–3 marks) even if the table computation is correct.
> 6. **Mixing CPM and PERT** — CPM uses **single deterministic duration**; PERT uses **three estimates**. Don't interchange.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Planning Charts (Module 4)**

- [ ] **Planning Chart** = time-scaled graphical tool for scheduling ship repair
- [ ] **Types**: Gantt, CPM, PERT, S-Curve, Man-hour Histogram
- [ ] **Gantt** = most common in yards; bars on time axis
- [ ] **CPM** = deterministic single-time; **PERT** = three-time estimate $(t_o, t_m, t_p)$
- [ ] **ES, EF, LS, LF, TF** = the five key schedule parameters
- [ ] **Total Float = LS − ES = LF − EF**
- [ ] **Critical Path** = sequence with **TF = 0**; longest duration chain
- [ ] **PERT expected time** $t_e = (t_o + 4t_m + t_p)/6$
- [ ] **PERT variance** $\sigma^2 = [(t_p - t_o)/6]^2$
- [ ] **Probability formula** $Z = (T_s - T_e)/\sigma_{\text{proj}}$
- [ ] **Productivity Index PI** = Actual % / Planned %; **PI < 1 = behind schedule**
- [ ] **Milestones** shown as **diamonds (◇)** in Gantt charts
- [ ] **Float** = allowable delay; **critical activities have zero float**
- [ ] **Standard software**: MS Project, Primavera P6
- [ ] **Liquidated Damages (LD)** of **$15k–$50k/day** drive the importance of planning
- [ ] **Draw the network diagram first** in 14-mark numerical questions
- [ ] **Always carry units** (days) throughout the calculation
- [ ] **Critical path is duration-driven, not count-driven**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shipbuilding Contracts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Shipbuilding Contracts # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Shipbuilding Contract A **Shipbuilding Contract** is a legally binding agreement between a **Shipowner** (the Buyer) and a **Shipbuilder** (the Builder/Yard) that defines the obligations, deliverables, technical specifications, commercial terms, payment schedule, delivery timeline, warranties, and risk allocation for the design, construction, and delivery of a vessel. Under international maritime law, it is treated as a *nominate contract* — a specialized sub-class of contract governed by both general contract law principles and industry-specific maritime conventions such as the **SAJ Form (1997)** and the **NEWBUILDCON (2007)** standard form adopted by BIMCO. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Shipbuilding contracts sit at the intersection of **commercial law**, **marine engineering**, and **naval architecture**. Unlike generic engineering contracts, shipbuilding agreements must address vessel classification, sea trials, flag-state compliance, and the unique transfer of ownership risk before, during, and after launch. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Custom House Builder" Intuition Imagine you (the Shipowner) walk into a custom home builder's office (the Shipyard) and say: *"Build me a 4-bedroom house (a 60,000 DWT bulker), make sure it passes the city inspector's code (Class Society rules), hand it over in 24 months (delivery date), and I'll pay you in 5 installments (milestone payments). If the roof leaks within the first year (warranty period), you fix it for free."* A shipbuilding contract works exactly this way — except: - The "house" is a **50,000–300,000-tonne floating steel structure** - The "city inspector" is a **Classification Society (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS, BV)** - The "occupancy permit" is the **Class Certificate + Flag State Delivery Certificate** - The "first-year warranty" is the **Guarantee Period (typically 12 months for machinery, varying for hull)** The contract formalizes this relationship with ironclad clauses because **a single delivery-day dispute can involve tens of millions of dollars** in liquidated damages. ## 1.3 Key Parties to a Shipbuilding Contract | Party | Role | Typical Responsibilities | |---|---|---| | **Buyer / Shipowner** | Commissioning party | Pays the contract price, provides approvals, accepts the vessel | | **Builder / Shipyard** | Constructing party | Designs, fabricates, launches, fits out, and trials the vessel | | **Classification Society** | Independent technical supervisor | Verifies compliance with rules, issues Class certificates | | **Flag State Administration** | Sovereign regulator | Issues statutory certificates (SOLAS, MARPOL compliance) | | **Mortgagee / Bank** | Financing party (if any) | Holds title security until loan repayment | | **P&I Club / Insurer** | Risk underwriter | Covers builder's risks and post-delivery liabilities | | **Supervisor / Owner's Representative** | Optional on-site monitor | Tracks progress, signs off milestones | > [!NOTE] > The **Buyer** does **not** automatically take ownership at contract signing. Legal title transfer typically occurs at **delivery**, although **builder's risk** and **buyer's risk** transition points are explicitly negotiated. ## 1.4 Standard Form Contracts Used in Shipbuilding > [!IMPORTANT] > Three standard forms dominate global shipbuilding contracts. KTU students must know their distinguishing features. - **SAJ Form (1997)** — *"Standard New Building Contract for Ships and Offshore Units."* Used primarily between **Japanese and Scandinavian** yards/owners. Known for its pro-builder clauses. - **NEWBUILDCON (2007)** — *BIMCO Standard Newbuilding Contract.* Considered the most **balanced** modern form. Widely adopted in **South Korean, Chinese, and European** yards. - **SHIPBUILD 2000 / AWES Form** — Earlier or yard-specific proprietary forms. The contract may also be **bespoke (ad hoc)**, drafted entirely from scratch for unique vessels (Navy ships, specialized offshore units). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Essential Clauses of a Shipbuilding Contract — The "Skeleton" A robust shipbuilding contract contains the following **10 critical clauses**: 1. **Subject of the Contract** — Vessel description, type, deadweight, principal dimensions. 2. **Contract Price** — Lump-sum figure, currency (USD/EUR), and revisions if any. 3. **Specifications & Plans** — Reference to General Specs, Particular Specs, and Plan Approval. 4. **Payment Terms** — Instalment schedule tied to milestones. 5. **Delivery Date & Place** — When and where ownership transfers. 6. **Trials** — Sea trial protocol, speed/power/fuel-consumption guarantees. 7. **Delays & Liquidated Damages (LD)** — Penalties for late delivery. 8. **Guarantees & Defects Liability** — Quality assurance post-delivery. 9. **Force Majeure & Cancellation** — Excused delays, termination rights. 10. **Risk, Title & Insurance** — When builder's risk ends and buyer's risk begins. ## 2.2 Classification of Shipbuilding Contracts ### A. Based on Pricing Structure | Contract Type | Description | Risk Bearer | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Lump Sum (Turnkey)** | Fixed price; Builder absorbs cost overruns | **Builder** | Commercial vessels, standardized designs | | **Cost-Plus / Cost Reimbursable** | Owner reimburses actual costs + fee/profit | **Owner** | First-of-class vessels, R&D ships, naval | | **Unit Price (Admeasurement)** | Price per tonne or per unit of work | **Mixed** | Conversion/repair work, modular builds | > [!NOTE] > The **Lump Sum Contract** is the **most common** in commercial shipbuilding. It transfers cost-overrun risk to the yard but typically includes a **price adjustment clause (escalation)** tied to steel/labour indices for very long contracts (>24 months). ### B. Based on Design Responsibility - **Design-Build (Package Deal)** — Yard designs *and* builds. Common for standard tanker/bulker orders. - **Design-Bid-Build (Owner-Designed)** — Owner supplies plans; Yard only constructs. Used for Navy or specialized vessels. - **Conversion Contracts** — For transforming an existing hull into a different vessel type. ## 2.3 The Standard 5-Stage Payment Schedule (Milestones) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most exam-relevant** element of shipbuilding contracts under KTU Module 4. | Instalment | Trigger Event | Typical % | Risk Allocation | |---|---|---|---| | **1st Instalment** | Contract signing | **10%** | Refundable if cancelled by Builder | | **2nd Instalment** | Steel cutting (keel-laid for some forms) | **10%** | Becomes non-refundable | | **3rd Instalment** | Keel laying | **20%** | Linked to structural progress | | **4th Instalment** | Launching | **20%** | Major progress milestone | | **5th Instalment** | Delivery (after sea trials) | **40%** | Final & largest payment | **Total = 100%** > [!WARNING] > The Buyer must pay the **5th instalment (40%) at delivery**, even if minor defects exist — these are addressed under the **Guarantee Period** rather than withheld. ## 2.4 Delivery, Trials & Performance Guarantees ### Sea Trial Performance Guarantees The Builder guarantees certain measurable performance figures during the **Official Sea Trial**. If the vessel fails to meet them, the contract imposes penalties or rectification obligations: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Speed Deficit Penalty} &= P_s \times (V_c - V_a) \times C \\ \text{Fuel Consumption Penalty} &= P_f \times (F_a - F_c) \times C \\ \text{Deadweight Shortfall} &= P_d \times (D_c - D_a) \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $V_c$ = Contracted service speed (knots) - $V_a$ = Attained speed (knots) - $F_c$ = Contracted daily fuel consumption (tonnes/day) - $F_a$ = Attained fuel consumption (tonnes/day) - $D_c$ = Contracted deadweight (tonnes) - $D_a$ = Attained deadweight (tonnes) - $C$ = A multiplier constant defined in contract - $P_s, P_f, P_d$ = Penalty rates per unit shortfall (USD) > [!IMPORTANT] > Under the **NEWBUILDCON** form, if the speed deficit exceeds **0.5 knots**, the Buyer has the right to **reject the vessel** — an extreme remedy rarely exercised but legally powerful. ## 2.5 Liquidated Damages (LD) for Late Delivery Late delivery is a **Builder's breach**. The contract pre-sets a daily LD rate: $$ \text{LD}_{\text{total}} = L_d \times N_d $$ Where: - $L_d$ = Daily liquidated damages (e.g., USD 25,000/day for a 50,000 DWT bulker) - $N_d$ = Number of days of delay (excluding permitted extensions) - **Cap:** Typically **5%–10% of contract price** > [!NOTE] > LD clauses must be a *genuine pre-estimate* of loss, not a penalty — otherwise they may be unenforceable under common law. ## 2.6 Force Majeure and Permitted Extensions A Force Majeure clause excuses delays caused by events beyond either party's control: - **Acts of God** (typhoons, earthquakes, floods) - **War, insurrection, sanctions** - **Pandemics / government-mandated lockdowns** - **Strikes not confined to the yard** - **Major raw-material shortages** The contract usually specifies: - A **notification window** (e.g., 7–14 days) - A **maximum extension period** (e.g., cumulative 90 days) - The Buyer's **right of cancellation** if force majeure persists beyond the cap. ## 2.7 Guarantee Period & Defects Liability After delivery, the vessel enters a **Guarantee Period** (typically **12 months for machinery** and **12–24 months for hull coating and outfitting**). During this period: - The Builder must **rectify defects** free of charge, provided they arise from faulty design, materials, or workmanship. - The Owner must give **written notice** within a stipulated window after discovery. - Defects arising from **normal wear and tear, owner's negligence, or operation outside design limits** are excluded. - The Builder's liability is **capped** at the contract price (sometimes at the LD cap). ## 2.8 Risk and Title Transfer | Stage | Risk Bearer | Title Bearer | |---|---|---| | Pre-launch | **Builder** | Builder | | At Launch | **Builder** | Builder | | During Trials | **Builder** | Builder | | At Delivery (signing Protocol of Delivery and Acceptance — PDA) | **Buyer** | **Buyer** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Title** (legal ownership) and **Risk** (liability for loss/damage) usually transfer **together at delivery**, but the contract can split them — for example, risk may pass at launch while title remains with Builder until PDA. ## 2.9 Insurance Obligations During Construction The contract mandates **Builder's Risk Insurance** covering: - Hull under construction - Launching risks - Trials risks (including port liability) - Equipment and materials at the yard The premium is usually **paid by the Builder** but may be **recharged to the Buyer** as part of the contract price. ## 2.10 Cancellation & Rescission Rights | Cancellation Trigger | Effect | |---|---| | Buyer fails to pay an instalment on time (after notice) | Builder may cancel and resell | | Builder fails to deliver within permitted LD cap | Buyer may cancel and recover payments + damages | | Force majeure extends beyond cap | Either party may cancel | | Material breach not cured within 30 days | Non-breaching party may cancel | > [!WARNING] > If the Buyer cancels without justification, the **Builder retains the first instalments** (10%–40%) as **liquidated damages**, and resells the vessel. The Buyer's secondary loss (price differential) is recoverable but capped. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Speed & Fuel Penalty Computation > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU-style, 14 marks):** A 60,000 DWT bulk carrier is contracted at **USD 32 million**. The contract specifies: > - Contracted service speed: $V_c = 14.5$ knots > - Contracted daily fuel consumption: $F_c = 55.0$ tonnes/day > - Penalty multiplier for speed: $P_s = $ USD 750,000 per knot of deficit > - Penalty multiplier for fuel: $P_f = $ USD 250,000 per tonne/day excess > - Multiplier constant: $C = 1.0$ > > During the official sea trial, the vessel achieves: $V_a = 13.9$ knots, $F_a = 58.5$ tonnes/day. > > **(a)** Calculate the total penalty owed by the Builder. > **(b)** Determine whether the Buyer can reject the vessel under the NEWBUILDCON threshold. ### Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Compute Speed Deficit** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta V &= V_c - V_a \\ &= 14.5 - 13.9 \\ &= 0.6 \text{ knots} \end{aligned} $$ The vessel is **0.6 knots slower** than the contracted speed. **Step 2 — Compute Fuel Excess Consumption** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta F &= F_a - F_c \\ &= 58.5 - 55.0 \\ &= 3.5 \text{ tonnes/day} \end{aligned} $$ The vessel consumes **3.5 tonnes/day more fuel** than contracted. **Step 3 — Apply the Speed Penalty Formula** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Penalty}_{\text{speed}} &= P_s \times \Delta V \times C \\ &= 750{,}000 \times 0.6 \times 1.0 \\ &= \text{USD } 450{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the Fuel Penalty Formula** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Penalty}_{\text{fuel}} &= P_f \times \Delta F \times C \\ &= 250{,}000 \times 3.5 \times 1.0 \\ &= \text{USD } 875{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Compute Total Penalty** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Penalty} &= \text{Penalty}_{\text{speed}} + \text{Penalty}_{\text{fuel}} \\ &= 450{,}000 + 875{,}000 \\ &= \text{USD } 1{,}325{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** The Builder owes **USD 1,325,000** in performance penalties. ### Solution — Part (b) **Step 6 — Compare Against the NEWBUILDCON Rejection Threshold** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta V &= 0.6 \text{ knots} \\ \text{Threshold} &= 0.5 \text{ knots} \\ \text{Check: } \Delta V &> \text{Threshold?} \\ 0.6 &> 0.5 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{YES} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Since the speed deficit (0.6 knots) **exceeds** the NEWBUILDCON threshold of 0.5 knots, the Buyer is **legally entitled to reject the vessel** and recover all instalments paid, plus proven damages. > [!IMPORTANT] > In practice, owners rarely exercise the rejection right because rejection triggers a lengthy dispute. They usually accept the vessel with the penalty amount **deducted from the 5th instalment (delivery payment)**. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Liquidated Damages Calculation > **Problem:** A ship is contracted at **USD 28 million**, with delivery due **30 June 2024**. The Builder delivers on **15 September 2024** — a delay of **77 days**. > The contract specifies: > - Daily LD rate: $L_d = $ USD 20,000/day > - Maximum LD cap: **8% of contract price** > - Permitted extensions for force majeure: **15 days already availed** > > Calculate the LD payable. ### Solution **Step 1 — Determine the Chargeable Delay** $$ \begin{aligned} N_d &= \text{Total delay} - \text{Permitted extension} \\ &= 77 - 15 \\ &= 62 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the LD Cap** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{LD}_{\text{cap}} &= 0.08 \times 28{,}000{,}000 \\ &= \text{USD } 2{,}240{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Uncapped LD** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{LD}_{\text{uncapped}} &= L_d \times N_d \\ &= 20{,}000 \times 62 \\ &= \text{USD } 1{,}240{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the Cap** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{LD}_{\text{uncapped}} &= 1{,}240{,}000 \\ \text{LD}_{\text{cap}} &= 2{,}240{,}000 \\ \text{Check: } 1{,}240{,}000 &< 2{,}240{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ The uncapped LD is **below** the cap. **Step 5 — Final LD Payable** $$ \boxed{\text{LD payable} = \text{USD } 1{,}240{,}000} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Standard Forms | Feature | **SAJ (1997)** | **NEWBUILDCON (2007)** | **Proprietary / Ad Hoc** | |---|---|---|---| | Origin | Japanese / Scandinavian | BIMCO (international) | Yard-drafted | | Bias | Pro-Builder | Balanced | Variable | | Payment Schedule | 5-stage (20/20/20/20/20) | 5-stage (10/10/20/20/40) | Negotiable | | LD Cap | 10% | 5–10% | Negotiable | | Speed Rejection | 1.0 knot deficit | 0.5 knot deficit | Custom | | Arbitration | Tokyo / London / Oslo | London (default) | Custom | | Guarantee Period | 12 months | 12–24 months | Negotiable | | Force Majeure Cap | 180 days | 90 days | Negotiable | | Use Today | Rare (legacy) | **Dominant** | Common in China/Navy | ## 3.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Contract Type ``` Start │ ├─ Vessel Standard Design? ──YES──► Lump-Sum / Turnkey │ │ │ ├─ Commercial? ──YES──► NEWBUILDCON │ └─ Specialized? ──► SAJ or Proprietary │ ├─ First-of-Class / R&D Vessel? ──► Cost-Plus │ └─ Government / Naval? ──► Cost-Plus + Bespoke Clauses (security, classification rules, sovereign immunity, dispute resolution) ``` > [!NOTE] > For KTU exams, remember this hierarchy: **Commercial bulkers/tankers = Lump Sum + NEWBUILDCON; Warships/research vessels = Cost Plus + Proprietary.** # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Shipbuilding Contract Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Negotiation and Tendering] --> B[Contract Signing
First Instalment 10 percent] B --> C[Design Approval
Class Society Review] C --> D[Steel Cutting
Second Instalment 10 percent] D --> E[Keel Laying
Third Instalment 20 percent] E --> F[Block Assembly and Hull Erection] F --> G[Launching
Fourth Instalment 20 percent] G --> H[Outfitting and Machinery Installation] H --> I[Mooring Trials and Dock Trials] I --> J[Official Sea Trial
Performance Verification] J --> K{Performance
Acceptable?} K -- Yes --> L[Protocol of Delivery and Acceptance
Fifth Instalment 40 percent] K -- No --> M[Rectification or Penalty Deduction] M --> J L --> N[Guarantee Period 12 to 24 months] N --> O[Final Settlement and Release of Guarantees] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Risk and Title Transfer ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Phase1[Pre-Launch Phase] R1[Builder Risk] T1[Builder Title] end subgraph Phase2[Launch Phase] R2[Builder Risk] T2[Builder Title] end subgraph Phase3[Trial Phase] R3[Builder Risk] T3[Builder Title] end subgraph Phase4[Delivery Phase] R4[Buyer Risk] T4[Buyer Title] end Phase1 --> Phase2 --> Phase3 --> Phase4 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Payment Milestones ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant O as Owner participant Y as Yard participant C as Class Society O->>Y: Sign Contract and Pay 10 percent Y->>C: Submit Drawings for Approval C-->>Y: Approved Plans Y->>Y: Cut Steel O->>Y: Pay 10 percent on Steel Cutting Y->>Y: Lay Keel O->>Y: Pay 20 percent on Keel Laying Y->>Y: Launch Hull O->>Y: Pay 20 percent on Launch Y->>Y: Complete Outfitting Y->>O: Conduct Sea Trial O->>Y: Sign PDA and Pay 40 percent Y->>O: Hand Over Vessel ``` ##
