4.4 Mermaid Mind Map — Key Contractual Clauses ```mermaid mindmap root((Shipbuilding Contract Clauses)) Commercial Contract Price Payment Schedule Currency and Escalation Technical Specifications Plans and Drawings Classification Rules Sea Trial Guarantees Legal Force Majeure Cancellation Rights Arbitration Clause Governing Law Operational Delivery Date and Place Title and Risk Transfer Insurance Obligations Post Delivery Guarantee Period Defects Liability LD Cap Final Settlement ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Decision Tree — Buyer's Cancellation Rights ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Trigger Event Occurs] --> B{What Type of Event?} B -- Speed Deficit greater than 0.5 knots --> C[Right to Reject Vessel] B -- Delay beyond Permitted LD Cap --> D[Cancel and Recover Instalments] B -- Force Majeure beyond 90 days --> E[Either Party May Cancel] B -- Builder Insolvency --> F[Cancel and Claim Refund via Insurance] C --> G[Buyer may Accept with Penalty Deduction] D --> H[Claim LD plus Proven Damages] E --> I[No LD Payable] F --> J[Refund via Builder Risk Insurance] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define a shipbuilding contract. List any four essential clauses.** **Model Answer:** A shipbuilding contract is a legally binding agreement between a shipowner (Buyer) and a shipyard (Builder) for the design, construction, and delivery of a vessel, governed by both general contract law and maritime-specific standard forms such as NEWBUILDCON. Four essential clauses: 1. **Specifications and Plans** — defines the technical scope. 2. **Contract Price and Payment Schedule** — fixes cost and milestone instalments. 3. **Delivery Date and Trials** — sets handover timing and acceptance tests. 4. **Guarantees and Liquidated Damages** — post-delivery liability and delay penalties. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between Lump Sum and Cost-Plus shipbuilding contracts.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Lump Sum | Cost-Plus | |---|---|---| | Price | Fixed in advance | Reimburses actual cost + fee | | Cost-overrun risk | **Builder** | **Owner** | | Suitability | Standard commercial vessels | First-of-class / Naval / R&D | | Audit rights | Limited | Owner audits Builder's books | | Common use | Bulkers, tankers | Warships, research vessels | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **typical five-stage payment schedule** in a shipbuilding contract. Discuss what happens if the Buyer defaults on an instalment. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A vessel is contracted at **USD 35 million** with delivery due on **31 March 2025**. The Builder delivers on **25 July 2025** — a total delay of **116 days**. The contract permits a **20-day extension** for documented force majeure events. The daily LD rate is **USD 25,000/day**, with a cap of **7% of the contract price**. Compute the LD payable. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) The five-stage payment schedule is structured to **align cash flow with construction progress** while protecting both parties: | Instalment | Trigger | % | |---|---|---| | 1st | Contract signing | 10 | | 2nd | Steel cutting | 10 | | 3rd | Keel laying | 20 | | 4th | Launching | 20 | | 5th | Delivery | 40 | **Buyer's Default Consequences:** - A **payment default notice** is issued by the Builder. - If the Buyer does not cure within the **grace period (typically 15–30 days)**, the Builder may: 1. **Suspend construction** with time running against the Buyer. 2. **Cancel the contract** and resell the vessel (with the Buyer liable for any shortfall in resale price plus storage and resale costs). 3. **Claim interest** on overdue instalments at the contractually agreed rate. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write *"the Builder keeps the 10% deposit"* — that is **inaccurate**. The Builder must prove actual loss; merely retaining the deposit does not extinguish the Buyer's further liability for resale shortfall. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Compute Chargeable Delay** $$ \begin{aligned} N_d &= 116 - 20 \\ &= 96 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Uncapped LD** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{LD}_{\text{uncapped}} &= 25{,}000 \times 96 \\ &= \text{USD } 2{,}400{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute LD Cap** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{LD}_{\text{cap}} &= 0.07 \times 35{,}000{,}000 \\ &= \text{USD } 2{,}450{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the Cap** $$ \begin{aligned} 2{,}400{,}000 &< 2{,}450{,}000 \\ \therefore \text{LD payable} &= \text{USD } 2{,}400{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** **USD 2,400,000** is payable as Liquidated Damages. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **(a)** Discuss the **clause structure** of a modern shipbuilding contract under the **NEWBUILDCON** form. Highlight at least **six major clauses** and their significance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 50,000 DWT bulk carrier is contracted at **USD 28 million** with the following performance guarantees: - Service speed: $V_c = 14.0$ knots - Daily fuel consumption: $F_c = 52$ tonnes/day - Speed penalty rate: $P_s = $ USD 500,000 per knot deficit - Fuel penalty rate: $P_f = $ USD 200,000 per tonne/day excess - Multiplier $C = 1.0$ During the sea trial, the vessel achieves: $V_a = 13.5$ knots, $F_a = 55.5$ tonnes/day. Compute the total performance penalty. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) The **NEWBUILDCON (2007)** form, drafted by BIMCO, contains the following major clauses: 1. **Clause 1 — Definitions and Interpretations** — Establishes precise meaning for "Vessel," "Builder," "Buyer," "Contract Price," etc. 2. **Clause 2 — Construction and Classification** — Mandates compliance with a named **Classification Society** (e.g., DNV, Lloyd's Register). 3. **Clause 3 — Specifications, Plans, and Approvals** — Defines the approval workflow for general specs, particular specs, and design drawings. 4. **Clause 4 — Contract Price and Payment** — Sets the 10/10/20/20/40 instalment schedule and bank guarantee requirements. 5. **Clause 5 — Delivery and Trials** — Outlines the sea trial protocol, performance guarantees, and the **Protocol of Delivery and Acceptance (PDA)**. 6. **Clause 6 — Delays and Liquidated Damages** — Caps LD at typically **5–10%** of contract price and defines permitted extensions. 7. **Clause 7 — Guarantee Period** — 12 months for machinery; longer for hull coating as negotiated. 8. **Clause 8 — Risk, Title, and Insurance** — Specifies when builder's risk ends and buyer's risk begins. 9. **Clause 9 — Force Majeure** — Excuses delays with a cumulative 90-day cap. 10. **Clause 10 — Disputes and Arbitration** — Defaults to **London arbitration** under BIMCO rules. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Significance:** NEWBUILDCON is favoured globally because it balances Builder and Buyer interests and avoids the pro-Builder bias of the older SAJ form. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Compute Speed Deficit** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta V &= V_c - V_a \\ &= 14.0 - 13.5 \\ &= 0.5 \text{ knots} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Fuel Excess** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta F &= F_a - F_c \\ &= 55.5 - 52.0 \\ &= 3.5 \text{ tonnes/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Speed Penalty** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Penalty}_{\text{speed}} &= 500{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \\ &= \text{USD } 250{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Fuel Penalty** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Penalty}_{\text{fuel}} &= 200{,}000 \times 3.5 \times 1.0 \\ &= \text{USD } 700{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Total Penalty** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Penalty} &= 250{,}000 + 700{,}000 \\ &= \text{USD } 950{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** **USD 950,000.** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** KTU examiners award **1 mark** for stating the correct formula, **1 mark** for substituting values, and **1 mark** for correct units. Students frequently lose marks by: > - Using $V_a - V_c$ instead of $V_c - V_a$ (sign error). > - Forgetting the multiplier $C$ even when $C = 1$. > - Omitting units in the final answer. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Shipbuilding Contracts** - A **Shipbuilding Contract** is the legal backbone for vessel construction between **Buyer (Owner)** and **Builder (Yard)**. - Standard forms: **NEWBUILDCON (2007)** is dominant; **SAJ (1997)** is legacy; **Proprietary** forms are yard-specific. - **Three contract types:** Lump Sum (risk on Builder), Cost-Plus (risk on Owner), Unit Price (mixed risk). - **Five-stage payment schedule:** **10 / 10 / 20 / 20 / 40 %** — remember the order: Signing → Steel Cutting → Keel Laying → Launch → Delivery. - **Risk and Title** transfer together at **delivery (PDA signing)** unless the contract specifies otherwise. - **Sea trial guarantees** cover speed, fuel consumption, and deadweight; penalties are calculated as: - Speed: $P_s \times \Delta V \times C$ - Fuel: $P_f \times \Delta F \times C$ - Deadweight: $P_d \times \Delta D$ - **Rejection threshold (NEWBUILDCON):** speed deficit **> 0.5 knots**. - **Liquidated Damages** for late delivery: $L_d \times N_d$, capped at **5–10 % of contract price**. - **Force Majeure cap:** typically **90 days** of cumulative extensions. - **Guarantee Period:** **12 months** for machinery; longer for hull as negotiated. - **Dispute resolution:** **London arbitration** under BIMCO rules (NEWBUILDCON default). - **Buyer's default:** Builder may suspend, cancel, and resell with the Buyer liable for **resale shortfall**. - **Builder's default:** Buyer may cancel after LD cap is reached and recover instalments plus proven damages. - Always remember **Class Society** (DNV, LR, ABS, IRS, BV) is the **independent technical arbiter**, not a party to the contract.

topics.title: Costing and Estimation - Difference Between Cost and Price
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Costing and Estimation - Difference Between Cost and Price

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Ship Survey, Estimation and Repair**, two foundational economic terms govern every repair contract, dry-dock quotation, and class survey billing: **Cost** and **Price**.

> [!NOTE]
> **COST (KTU Definition):**
> *Cost* is the **total monetary expenditure** incurred by a shipyard, dry-dock, or service contractor to **produce, acquire, or execute** a defined engineering task (e.g., hull plating renewal, main engine overhaul, IACS class survey). It represents the **resource consumption** side of the equation and is an **internal accounting value** — what *the yard spends*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRICE (KTU Definition):**
> *Price* is the **monetary value at which a product or service is offered / sold** to the shipowner or charterer. It is the **external commercial value** — what *the client pays*. Price = Cost + Profit Margin + applicable statutory levies + risk contingencies.

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you run a **small ship-repair workshop in Kochi** and you are asked to renew **10 corroded hull plates** on a bulk carrier:

| What YOU (yard) spend | What the OWNER pays you |
|---|---|
| Steel plates @ ₹85,000/tonne × 4 t | Quoted lump-sum: **₹12,50,000** |
| Welder wages for 200 man-hours | Includes your yard's overheads |
| Dry-dock hire for 7 days | Includes your profit margin |
| Paint, electrodes, gas, consumables | Plus GST @ 18 % |
| Electricity, supervisor salary | Plus 5 % contingency |

> The left column is **COST** (your outflow). The right column is **PRICE** (owner's inflow to you). They are **never equal** in a real shipyard — *Price must always cover Cost + Profit* for the business to survive.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Memory Hook:**
> - **Cost** = Money **OUT** (yard's expense)
> - **Price** = Money **IN** (owner's payment)
> - The **GAP** between them is **Profit + Overhead Recovery + Taxes**.

## 1.3 Standard Cost Categories in Ship Repair (Bold Key Metrics)

Per **KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 4 syllabus**, the following cost classifications are mandatory:

1. **Direct Material Cost (DM)** – Steel, pipes, electrodes, paint, spare parts.
2. **Direct Labour Cost (DL)** – Welder, fitter, machinist wages.
3. **Direct Expenses (DE)** – Sub-contracted work (e.g., propeller polishing by diver).
4. **Overhead Cost (OH)** – Yard depreciation, administration, utilities, insurance.
5. **Total Cost (C)** = DM + DL + DE + OH.
6. **Selling Price (P)** = C + Profit + Taxes + Contingency.

> [!WARNING]
> A common student error in KTU exams is to treat *cost* and *price* as synonyms. In valuation, **listing only the cost side without profit margin = guaranteed loss of 2–3 marks** in Part B answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cost Build-Up Logic (Step-by-Step)

The **Total Cost (C)** of any ship repair job is constructed hierarchically:

### Step 1 — Direct Material (DM)
Sum of physically consumed raw materials priced at current market rates.

$$\text{DM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times R_i)$$

where $Q_i$ is quantity of material *i* (tonnes / kg / litres) and $R_i$ is its unit rate (₹/unit).

### Step 2 — Direct Labour (DL)
Sum of productive man-hours multiplied by all-in labour rate (wage + benefits + ESI/PF).

$$\text{DL} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} (H_j \times L_j)$$

where $H_j$ is hours worked by trade *j* and $L_j$ is the loaded hourly rate.

### Step 3 — Direct Expenses (DE)
Specific job-related outsourced costs: dry-dock hire, classification society fees, third-party NDT, tug assistance.

### Step 4 — Overheads (OH) — *The Hidden Cost*
Allocated share of yard's indirect expenses. Calculated as a percentage of (DM + DL):

$$\text{OH} = \eta \times (\text{DM} + \text{DL})$$

where **η = overhead absorption rate** (typically **15 %–40 %** in Indian shipyards).

### Step 5 — Total Cost (C)

$$C = \text{DM} + \text{DL} + \text{DE} + \text{OH}$$

### Step 6 — Price (P)

$$P = C + \text{Profit} + \text{Taxes} + \text{Contingency}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula / Term | Meaning | Typical Range (Ship Repair) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \text{DM} + \text{DL} + \text{DE} + \text{OH}$ | Total Cost | Base value (₹) |
| 2 | $\text{OH} = \eta (\text{DM} + \text{DL})$ | Overhead absorption | $\eta = 0.15$ to $0.40$ |
| 3 | $P = C (1 + m)$ | Price using **mark-up** | $m = 0.10$ to $0.25$ |
| 4 | $\pi = P - C$ | Absolute Profit | ₹ |
| 5 | $\mu = \dfrac{\pi}{P} \times 100\,\%$ | **Margin** (profit on price) | $5\,\%$ to $20\,\%$ |
| 6 | $m = \dfrac{\pi}{C} \times 100\,\%$ | **Mark-up** (profit on cost) | $10\,\%$ to $30\,\%$ |
| 7 | $P_{\text{final}} = P (1 + t_g)$ | GST-included price | $t_g = 0.18$ (18 % GST) |
| 8 | $P_{\text{est}} = P_{\text{final}} (1 + k_c)$ | Contingency-loaded estimate | $k_c = 0.05$ to $0.10$ |
| 9 | $E_{\text{accuracy}} = \dfrac{\vert C_{\text{actual}} - C_{\text{est}} \vert}{C_{\text{actual}}} \times 100$ | Estimation accuracy % | Lower is better |
| 10 | $L_{\text{unit}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{total}}}{Q_{\text{total}}}$ | Unit cost (₹/tonne etc.) | Benchmark metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Margin vs Mark-up — A Favourite KTU Trap:**
> A yard that earns **₹20 profit on a ₹100 cost item** has:
> - **Mark-up** = $20/100 = 20\,\%$
> - **Margin** = $20/120 = 16.67\,\%$
> They are **NOT the same**. Always read the question carefully.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Tender Pricing:** When a shipowner asks for a quotation, the yard must convert internal *cost* into external *price* using the above formulas.
- **Budgetary Control:** Class survey work (IACS member societies — LR, BV, DNV, ABS) requires cost estimates within ±10 % of actual.
- **Dry-Dock Scheduling:** Estimating **100,000 DWT capesize** dock-block cost per day.
- **Insurance Claims:** Average adjusters compute **actual cost of repair** vs **insured value** — precise cost data is critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Derivation — Hull Plate Renewal

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A shipyard is estimating the cost of replacing **8 corroded side-shell plates** on a 30,000 DWT bulk carrier during dry-dock. Given data:

- Steel plate consumption: **6 tonnes @ ₹92,000/tonne**
- Electrodes & gas: **₹28,000**
- Paint (primer + anti-fouling): **₹45,000**
- Welder man-hours: **240 hours @ ₹650/hour** (loaded rate)
- Fitter man-hours: **80 hours @ ₹550/hour**
- Dry-dock hire (7 days): **₹1,20,000/day**
- Classification society fee: **₹85,000**
- Overhead absorption rate: **η = 25 %** of (DM + DL)
- Profit mark-up: **15 % on cost**
- GST: **18 %**
- Contingency: **5 % on pre-tax price**

**Compute:** (a) Total Cost, (b) Selling Price, (c) Quoted Price to Shipowner.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

#### Step A — Direct Material (DM)

Materials grouped:
- Steel plates: $6 \times 92{,}000$
- Electrodes & gas: $28{,}000$
- Paint: $45{,}000$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DM} &= (6 \times 92{,}000) + 28{,}000 + 45{,}000 \\
&= 5{,}52{,}000 + 28{,}000 + 45{,}000 \\
&= 6{,}25{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step B — Direct Labour (DL)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DL} &= (240 \times 650) + (80 \times 550) \\
&= 1{,}56{,}000 + 44{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step C — Direct Expenses (DE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DE} &= (\text{Dock hire}) + (\text{Class fee}) \\
&= (7 \times 1{,}20{,}000) + 85{,}000 \\
&= 8{,}40{,}000 + 85{,}000 \\
&= 9{,}25{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step D — Overheads (OH)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OH} &= 0.25 \times (\text{DM} + \text{DL}) \\
&= 0.25 \times (6{,}25{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000) \\
&= 0.25 \times 8{,}25{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}06{,}250 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step E — Total Cost (C)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \text{DM} + \text{DL} + \text{DE} + \text{OH} \\
&= 6{,}25{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}25{,}000 + 2{,}06{,}250 \\
&= 19{,}56{,}250 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step F — Pre-tax Price with Profit Mark-up (m = 15 %)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= C \times (1 + m) \\
&= 19{,}56{,}250 \times 1.15 \\
&= 22{,}49{,}687.50 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step G — Add GST (18 %)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{GST}} &= P \times (1 + 0.18) \\
&= 22{,}49{,}687.50 \times 1.18 \\
&= 26{,}54{,}631.25 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step H — Add Contingency (5 %)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{final}} &= P_{\text{GST}} \times (1 + 0.05) \\
&= 26{,}54{,}631.25 \times 1.05 \\
&= 27{,}87{,}362.81 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer Summary

| Component | Value (₹) |
|---|---|
| Direct Material (DM) | 6,25,000 |
| Direct Labour (DL) | 2,00,000 |
| Direct Expenses (DE) | 9,25,000 |
| Overheads (OH) | 2,06,250 |
| **Total Cost (C)** | **19,56,250** |
| Profit (15 % of C) | 2,93,437.50 |
| Pre-tax Price | 22,49,687.50 |
| GST @ 18 % | 4,04,943.75 |
| Contingency @ 5 % | 1,32,731.56 |
| **Final Quoted PRICE** | **₹27,87,363 (approx.)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Margin vs Mark-up verification:**
> Absolute profit = ₹2,93,437.50
> - Mark-up = $2{,}93{,}437.50 / 19{,}56{,}250 = 15\,\%$ ✓
> - Margin = $2{,}93{,}437.50 / 22{,}49{,}687.50 = 13.04\,\%$
> Different values — confirming the KTU trap discussed in Section 2.

## 3.2 Sensitivity Analysis Concept (Why Estimation is Hard)

Real ship repair is rarely as clean as the textbook. Hidden cost escalators:

$$
C_{\text{actual}} = C_{\text{est}} \times (1 + s_1)(1 + s_2)(1 + s_3)
$$

where $s_1, s_2, s_3$ are slip factors for steel price rise, weather delays, and rework. This is why **contingency (5 %–10 %)** is built into the price.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cost → Price Build-Up Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Repair Job Identified] --> B[Quantity Survey from Drawings]
    B --> C[Direct Material DM]
    B --> D[Direct Labour DL]
    B --> E[Direct Expenses DE]
    C --> F[Subtotal 1 DM plus DL]
    F --> G[Apply Overhead Rate eta]
    G --> H[Total Cost C]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    H --> I[Add Profit Mark up m]
    I --> J[Pre Tax Price P]
    J --> K[Add GST 18 percent]
    K --> L[Add Contingency 5 to 10 percent]
    L --> M[FINAL QUOTED PRICE to Shipowner]
    H --> N[Cost recorded in yard books]
    M --> O[Price billed to client]
    N -. GAP .-> O
```

## 4.2 Cost Classification Hierarchy (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Total Repair Cost C] --> R1[Direct Material DM]
    R --> R2[Direct Labour DL]
    R --> R3[Direct Expenses DE]
    R --> R4[Overheads OH]
    R1 --> R1a[Steel Plates]
    R1 --> R1b[Welding Consumables]
    R1 --> R1c[Paint and Anodes]
    R2 --> R2a[Welders]
    R2 --> R2b[Fitters]
    R2 --> R2c[Machinists]
    R3 --> R3a[Dry Dock Hire]
    R3 --> R3b[Class Survey Fee]
    R3 --> R3c[Subcontract NDT]
    R4 --> R4a[Depreciation]
    R4 --> R4b[Admin Salaries]
    R4 --> R4c[Power and Utilities]
```

## 4.3 The Cost-Price Gap Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[YARD SPENDS Cost] -->|plus Profit plus Tax plus Risk| B[OWNER PAYS Price]
    A1[DM] --> A
    A2[DL] --> A
    A3[DE] --> A
    A4[OH] --> A
    B1[Base Cost] --> B
    B2[Profit Margin] --> B
    B3[GST 18 percent] --> B
    B4[Contingency] --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams use purely alphanumeric node IDs (no reserved keywords) and raw uppercase labels to ensure clean Mermaid compilation. Block-level architecture is used in place of complex physical drawings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between Cost and Price in the context of ship repair estimation. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Cost (C):** The total monetary expenditure incurred by the shipyard to execute a repair — sum of Direct Material (DM), Direct Labour (DL), Direct Expenses (DE), and Overheads (OH). It is an **internal accounting figure** representing resource consumption.
- **Price (P):** The monetary value **quoted to / paid by** the shipowner. It equals **Cost + Profit Margin + Statutory Taxes (GST) + Contingency**. It is an **external commercial figure** representing revenue inflow.
- **Key difference:** Cost = yard's outflow; Price = owner's outflow to yard. **Price must always exceed Cost** for commercial viability.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**Explain the term "Overhead Absorption Rate (η)" with its typical range in Indian shipyards. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **Overhead Absorption Rate (η)** is the percentage factor applied to the sum of Direct Material and Direct Labour to allocate a fair share of indirect yard expenses (administration, depreciation, utilities, insurance) to a specific repair job.

Formula: $\text{OH} = \eta \times (\text{DM} + \text{DL})$

Typical range in Indian shipyards: **15 % to 40 %**, depending on yard size, automation level, and idle-capacity factor. A large PSU yard like Cochin Shipyard may operate near 25 %, whereas a small private dry-dock may exceed 35 %.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from each module. Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.*

---

### QUESTION A — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### (a) [7 Marks]
**With neat sketches/flowcharts, explain the components of Total Cost (C) in a ship repair estimation. How are overheads absorbed?**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Total Cost of a ship repair job is the sum of all resources consumed, classified into four heads:

**(i) Direct Material (DM):** Quantifiable raw materials consumed — steel plates, pipes, welding electrodes, paints, anodes, machine spares. Priced at current market rates multiplied by surveyed quantities.

**(ii) Direct Labour (DL):** Wages of tradesmen directly working on the job — welders, fitters, machinists, painters. Calculated as **hours × loaded hourly rate** (loaded rate = wage + ESI + PF + bonus + leave).

**(iii) Direct Expenses (DE):** Job-specific outsourced costs — dry-dock hire, classification society survey fees, third-party NDT, tug and barge assistance.

**(iv) Overheads (OH):** Indirect yard costs that cannot be directly attributed to one job — yard depreciation, administrative salaries, power, water, insurance, marketing. These are **absorbed** using the absorption rate η:

$$
\text{OH} = \eta \times (\text{DM} + \text{DL}), \quad \eta \in [0.15, 0.40]
$$

**Sketches:** The hierarchical tree diagram from Section 4.2 may be redrawn for full marks.

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**A shipyard quotes a price using 20 % mark-up on cost. If the Total Cost is ₹15,00,000 and GST is 18 %, calculate the final invoice price. Also compute the margin on price.**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $C = 15{,}00{,}000$, $m = 20\,\%$, GST $= 18\,\%$.

**Step 1 — Pre-tax Price:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= C \times (1 + m) \\
&= 15{,}00{,}000 \times 1.20 \\
&= 18{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Price with GST:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{GST}} &= P \times 1.18 \\
&= 18{,}00{,}000 \times 1.18 \\
&= 21{,}24{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Margin on Price:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{P - C}{P_{\text{GST}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{18{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000}{21{,}24{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{21{,}24{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 14.12\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answers:** Pre-tax Price = ₹18,00,000; GST-included Price = **₹21,24,000**; Margin ≈ **14.12 %**.

---

### QUESTION B — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks]
**Discuss the difference between Margin and Mark-up in ship repair pricing. Why is this distinction commercially important?**

**Model Solution:**

| Basis | Margin (μ) | Mark-up (m) |
|---|---|---|
| Denominator | **Price (P)** | **Cost (C)** |
| Formula | $\mu = (P - C)/P$ | $m = (P - C)/C$ |
| Typical value | 5 %–20 % | 10 %–30 % |
| Used by | Sales / Marketing | Cost Accountants |
| Direction | Backward (from price) | Forward (from cost) |

**Numerical Illustration:** Profit = ₹20 on Cost ₹100.
- Mark-up = $20/100 = 20\,\%$
- Margin = $20/120 = 16.67\,\%$

**Commercial Importance:**
1. **Tax & Compliance:** GST is levied on *transaction value (Price)*, so margin-based pricing is critical for input tax credit calculations.
2. **Tender Strategy:** Shipowners compare yard quotes — quoting a *mark-up* may overstate competitiveness versus quoting a *margin*.
3. **Break-even Decisions:** A yard must know whether its margin covers bank interest, working capital, and depreciation.
4. **Class Survey Audits:** Classification societies and average adjusters demand mark-up transparency for insurance settlements.

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**For a main engine overhaul job, the following data are given: DM = ₹8,00,000; DL = ₹3,00,000; DE = ₹4,50,000; η = 30 %; m = 18 %; GST = 18 %; Contingency = 7 %. Calculate (i) Total Cost, (ii) Final Quoted Price.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Overheads:**

$$
\text{OH} = 0.30 \times (8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000) = 0.30 \times 11{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}30{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \text{DM} + \text{DL} + \text{DE} + \text{OH} \\
&= 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}30{,}000 \\
&= 18{,}80{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pre-tax Price with 18 % Mark-up:**

$$
P = 18{,}80{,}000 \times 1.18 = 22{,}18{,}400 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Add GST (18 %):**

$$
P_{\text{GST}} = 22{,}18{,}400 \times 1.18 = 26{,}17{,}712 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Add Contingency (7 %):**

$$
P_{\text{final}} = 26{,}17{,}712 \times 1.07 = 28{,}00{,}951.84 \text{ ₹}
$$

> **Final Answers:**
> (i) Total Cost = **₹18,80,000**
> (ii) Final Quoted Price = **₹28,00,952 (approx.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing margin and mark-up** in numerical answers — loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to add GST** in the final price — a 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Using absolute profit (₹) where % is asked** — read the question word-for-word.
> 4. **Not including overheads** — examiners often award 0/7 on Part B if OH step is skipped.
> 5. **Confusing Price with Cost** in narrative answers — use the words *yard-side (cost)* vs *client-side (price)* consistently.
> 6. **Wrong sign on contingency** — contingency is *added* to price, never subtracted.
> 7. **Ignoring units (₹)** — every numerical final answer must carry the ₹ symbol.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Cost (C)** = DM + DL + DE + OH — internal yard expenditure.
- **Price (P)** = C + Profit + Taxes + Contingency — external commercial value.
- **Overheads** absorbed as $\eta \times (\text{DM} + \text{DL})$; $\eta = 15\,\%$ to $40\,\%$.
- **Mark-up (m)** = profit/Cost; **Margin (μ)** = profit/Price. They are different.
- **GST** is currently **18 %** for most ship-repair services in India.
- **Contingency** of **5 %–10 %** is mandatory to absorb steel-price volatility, weather delays, and rework.
- **Estimation accuracy** is monitored by $\vert C_{\text{actual}} - C_{\text{est}} \vert / C_{\text{actual}} \times 100\,\%$.
- Always convert units to **₹ (INR)** in final answers unless specifically asked otherwise.
- For ship-repair tenders, prices are quoted **lump-sum or unit-rate**; both require a robust cost build-up underneath.
- In KTU exams, **show every step** — partial marking is generous only when the *logic chain* is visible.
- Remember: **Price > Cost** for any sustainable marine engineering business.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Top Down and Bottom Top Approaches in Costing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Top Down and Bottom Top Approaches in Costing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# TOP-DOWN AND BOTTOM-UP APPROACHES IN COSTING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In the domain of **Ship Survey, Estimation, and Repair**, the costing of a vessel—whether during new construction planning, dry-dock repair assessment, or post-damage survey—must follow a structured estimation methodology. The two principal methodologies employed by marine surveyors, ship managers, and shipyard cost engineers are the **Top-Down Approach** and the **Bottom-Up Approach** in costing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Top-Down Costing Approach** is a macroscopic estimation technique where the total ship repair or project cost is evaluated first, based on historical cost data of analogous vessels, parametric models, or expert judgment, and then systematically decomposed and allocated to individual work packages, sub-systems, or cost centers.
>
> The **Bottom-Up Costing Approach** is a microscopic, granular estimation technique where the cost is computed by summing up the detailed estimates of every elementary work item, material, labor-hour, and sub-task defined within a **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**, beginning from the lowest level of the WBS tree and aggregating upward to arrive at the total project cost.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Birthday Cake Problem"** 🎂
>
> Imagine you are planning a birthday party for 200 guests.
>
> - **Top-Down Approach:** You first decide, *"The total party budget is ₹2,00,000."* Then you allocate: ₹1,00,000 for catering, ₹40,000 for decoration, ₹30,000 for venue, ₹20,000 for entertainment, and ₹10,000 for miscellaneous. This is fast but approximate—you do not know exactly how many pastries you need.
>
> - **Bottom-Up Approach:** You first count the exact requirements: *"I need 200 pastries × ₹150 = ₹30,000, 200 cold-drinks × ₹40 = ₹8,000, 1 hall for 4 hours × ₹5,000 = ₹20,000..."* and so on, until you sum up every single item to get the total ₹2,15,678. This is slower but highly accurate.
>
> In ship repair, the **Top-Down** is used by owners/banks for budgeting and feasibility; the **Bottom-Up** is used by shipyards and surveyors for tendering and execution.

### 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in ship repair cost estimation:

- **Currency Unit:** USD (United States Dollar) is the global marine standard. Conversion to EUR, GBP, INR is done using prevailing port exchange rates.
- **Steel Cost Benchmark:** **USD 2,200 – 3,500 per ton** of marine grade steel (AH36/DH36) as of recent market data.
- **Labor Rate Benchmark:** **USD 25 – 65 per man-hour** depending on the shipyard's geographical location (e.g., China, Turkey, India, Middle East).
- **Docking Fee Benchmark:** **USD 8 – 25 per GRT per day** for floating dock usage.
- **Accuracy Range:** Top-Down estimates typically have a **±25% to ±50% accuracy**, while Bottom-Up estimates aim for **±5% to ±10% accuracy** in the bid stage.
- **WBS Levels:** Standard ship repair WBS has **3 to 5 levels** of decomposition.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Top-Down Costing Approach — Step-by-Step Logic

The Top-Down approach is fundamentally a **macro-estimation** technique rooted in the principle of *parametric estimation*. It treats the ship as a single cost-generating entity initially and progressively decomposes it.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Top-Down Approach

- **Step 1 — Establish the Total Cost Envelope:**  
  The estimator first obtains the total project cost ($C_{total}$) using one of the following methods:
  - **Analogy Method:** Compare with a similar ship repaired earlier.
  - **Parametric Method:** Use cost-per-unit relationships (e.g., cost per GRT, cost per DWT, cost per shaft horsepower).
  - **Expert Judgment / Delphi Method:** Consult experienced marine surveyors.

- **Step 2 — Identify Major Cost Centers:**  
  The total cost is divided among broad categories: Hull, Machinery, Electrical, Painting, Docking, Logistics, Overheads, Profit.

- **Step 3 — Allocate Percentage Weights:**  
  Historical percentage splits (cost driver ratios) are applied to each cost center. For example, in a typical dry-dock repair, **Steel Renewal** may constitute **18–22%** of the total repair cost, **Machinery Overhaul** around **25–30%**, and **Docking + Painting** around **20–25%**.

- **Step 4 — Refine into Sub-Systems:**  
  Each cost center is further allocated to sub-systems (e.g., Hull → Bow, Midship, Stern; Machinery → Main Engine, Auxiliaries, Boilers).

- **Step 5 — Final Sanity Check:**  
  The total of all sub-system allocations is reconciled with the original envelope $C_{total}$.

### 2.2 The Bottom-Up Costing Approach — Step-by-Step Logic

The Bottom-Up approach is the **micro-estimation** technique mandated by classification societies and shipyards for firm bidding.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Bottom-Up Approach

- **Step 1 — Construct the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS):**  
  The entire repair scope is decomposed into a hierarchical tree. The WBS Level 0 is the project, Level 1 is the ship system, Level 2 is the sub-system, Level 3 is the component, Level 4 is the work task.

- **Step 2 — Generate the Bill of Quantities (BOQ):**  
  For every Level-4 task, the exact quantity is measured: tons of steel, square meters of surface, lengths of pipe, counts of valves.

- **Step 3 — Determine Unit Rates:**  
  The cost of performing **one unit** of the work is determined from labor norms, material rates, and subcontractor quotations. These rates are derived from shipyard cost databases.

- **Step 4 — Compute Elementary Costs:**  
  For each work task, multiply the BOQ quantity by the unit rate. Add labor cost ($L$), material cost ($M$), and consumables ($S$).

- **Step 5 — Roll-Up / Aggregation:**  
  Sum the elementary costs hierarchically: Task → Component → Sub-system → System → Total Project Cost.

- **Step 6 — Add Indirect Costs and Markups:**  
  Append overheads, docking fees, classification survey fees, profit margin, contingency, and price escalation (if contract spans multiple years).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the high-yield formulas a KTU student must memorize for cost estimation problems.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{top} = C_{analogue} \times \dfrac{L_{new}}{L_{old}} \times (1+f_{esc})$ | $C_{top}$: Top-Down total cost; $C_{analogue}$: historical cost of similar ship; $L$: LOA or DWT; $f_{esc}$: annual escalation factor | USD (or local currency) | Step 1 of Top-Down method |
| 2 | $C_i = W_i \times C_{total}$ | $C_i$: cost of cost-center $i$; $W_i$: weight fraction of center $i$ | USD or % | Step 3 of Top-Down allocation |
| 3 | $C_{bottom} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \left( Q_j \times R_j \right)$ | $Q_j$: quantity of work item $j$; $R_j$: unit rate of item $j$; $N$: total BOQ items | USD | Step 5 of Bottom-Up aggregation |
| 4 | $R_j = L_j + M_j + S_j$ | $L_j$: labor cost per unit; $M_j$: material cost per unit; $S_j$: consumables per unit | USD per unit | Step 4 of Bottom-Up |
| 5 | $C_{final} = C_{direct} \times (1 + O + P + R_{cont})$ | $O$: overhead %; $P$: profit %; $R_{cont}$: contingency reserve | Decimal fraction | Step 6 of Bottom-Up |
| 6 | $C_{dock} = GRT \times D_r \times T_d$ | $GRT$: gross register tonnage; $D_r$: daily dock rate; $T_d$: days in dock | USD | Top-Down docking allocation |
| 7 | $C_{steel} = W_{steel} \times P_{steel} \times (1 + W_{fab} + W_{inst})$ | $W_{steel}$: weight of steel; $P_{steel}$: price/ton; $W_{fab}, W_{inst}$: fabrication & installation factors | USD | Bottom-Up steel renewal |
| 8 | $E = \dfrac{\vert C_{est} - C_{actual} \vert}{C_{actual}} \times 100\%$ | $E$: estimation error percentage | % | Accuracy check for both methods |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - In **ship finance and insurance**, Top-Down is used by P&I clubs and marine insurers to set loss reserves within hours of an incident report.
> - In **shipyard tendering and class surveys**, Bottom-Up is mandatory for fixed-price contracts as per IACS (International Association of Classification Societies) recommendations.
> - Hybrid approaches (top-down budget → bottom-up firm bid) are the industry norm for dry-dock repair specification negotiations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Top-Down Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A Handysize bulk carrier of **32,000 DWT** and **LOA = 180 m** recently underwent a scheduled dry-dock repair at a Chinese shipyard. The total all-inclusive cost was **USD 2,400,000**. A similar sister vessel of **28,000 DWT** and **LOA = 170 m** is now due for repair. The annual cost escalation rate in the region is **4.5%**. Estimate the Top-Down cost for the new vessel using the DWT analogy method.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Given:
- Reference vessel: $DWT_{ref} = 32,000$, $C_{ref} = \$2,400,000$
- New vessel: $DWT_{new} = 28,000$
- Annual escalation: $f_{esc} = 4.5\% = 0.045$
- Time elapsed (assume): $\Delta t = 1$ year

Step 1 — Apply the DWT Analogy formula:

$$
C_{top} = C_{ref} \times \dfrac{DWT_{new}}{DWT_{ref}} \times (1 + f_{esc})^{\Delta t}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:

$$
C_{top} = 2{,}400{,}000 \times \dfrac{28{,}000}{32{,}000} \times (1 + 0.045)^{1}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the DWT ratio:

$$
\dfrac{28{,}000}{32{,}000} = \dfrac{7}{8} = 0.875
$$

Step 4 — Compute the escalation factor:

$$
(1 + 0.045)^{1} = 1.045
$$

Step 5 — Multiply all factors:

$$
C_{top} = 2{,}400{,}000 \times 0.875 \times 1.045
$$

Step 6 — First multiply the reference cost by the ratio:

$$
2{,}400{,}000 \times 0.875 = 2{,}100{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

Step 7 — Apply escalation:

$$
2{,}100{,}000 \times 1.045 = 2{,}194{,}500 \text{ USD}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{C_{top} = \$2{,}194{,}500}
$$

This is the Top-Down estimate. The **±25% accuracy band** implies a realistic range of **USD 1,645,875 to USD 2,743,125**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Bottom-Up Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** For a Panamax container ship dry-dock, the surveyor has identified three elementary steel renewal work items. Compute the total direct cost using the Bottom-Up method and the final firm cost after adding **12% overhead, 8% profit, and 5% contingency reserve**.

| Item Code | Description | Quantity (Q) | Labor/Unit (L) | Material/Unit (M) | Consumable/Unit (S) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SR-01 | Bow plate renewal | 12 tons | 1,200 USD/ton | 2,800 USD/ton | 350 USD/ton |
| SR-02 | Side shell plate renewal | 25 tons | 1,150 USD/ton | 2,750 USD/ton | 300 USD/ton |
| SR-03 | Deck plate renewal | 8 tons | 1,300 USD/ton | 2,900 USD/ton | 400 USD/ton |

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Step 1 — Compute the unit rate $R_j = L_j + M_j + S_j$ for each item:

For SR-01:
$$
R_{01} = 1{,}200 + 2{,}800 + 350 = 4{,}350 \text{ USD/ton}
$$

For SR-02:
$$
R_{02} = 1{,}150 + 2{,}750 + 300 = 4{,}200 \text{ USD/ton}
$$

For SR-03:
$$
R_{03} = 1{,}300 + 2{,}900 + 400 = 4{,}600 \text{ USD/ton}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the elementary cost $C_j = Q_j \times R_j$:

For SR-01:
$$
C_{01} = 12 \times 4{,}350 = 52{,}200 \text{ USD}
$$

For SR-02:
$$
C_{02} = 25 \times 4{,}200 = 105{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

For SR-03:
$$
C_{03} = 8 \times 4{,}600 = 36{,}800 \text{ USD}
$$

Step 3 — Sum the direct cost (Roll-Up aggregation):

$$
C_{direct} = 52{,}200 + 105{,}000 + 36{,}800 = 194{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

Step 4 — Apply the indirect cost formula:

$$
C_{final} = C_{direct} \times (1 + O + P + R_{cont})
$$

Step 5 — Substitute the percentages as decimals ($O = 0.12$, $P = 0.08$, $R_{cont} = 0.05$):

$$
C_{final} = 194{,}000 \times (1 + 0.12 + 0.08 + 0.05)
$$

Step 6 — Sum the fractional terms:

$$
1 + 0.12 + 0.08 + 0.05 = 1.25
$$

Step 7 — Final multiplication:

$$
C_{final} = 194{,}000 \times 1.25 = 242{,}500 \text{ USD}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{C_{final} = \$242{,}500}
$$

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following comparison table is a high-yield KTU exam tool. Students are often asked to differentiate the two approaches in 14-mark questions.

| Parameter | Top-Down Approach | Bottom-Up Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Point** | Total project cost | Individual elementary work items |
| **Direction of Flow** | Macro → Micro (decomposition) | Micro → Macro (aggregation) |
| **Primary Input** | Historical cost data, analogies, parametric models | WBS, BOQ, unit rates |
| **Accuracy** | ±25% to ±50% | ±5% to ±10% |
| **Time Required** | Hours to a few days | Days to weeks |
| **Cost of Estimation** | Low (uses existing data) | High (requires detailed surveys) |
| **Stage of Project** | Concept / Feasibility / Budgeting | Tendering / Execution / Class Survey |
| **Data Dependency** | High on past projects | High on present vessel condition |
| **Risk Treatment** | Built-in via contingency | Identified item-by-item |
| **Used By** | Ship owners, banks, insurers, P&I clubs | Shipyards, classification surveyors, repair contractors |
| **Output Format** | Total cost + major cost-center breakdown | Detailed itemized quotation / tender document |
| **Flexibility** | Low (locked to historical pattern) | High (customized to actual damage) |
| **Skill Required** | Cost modeling and analogy mapping | Quantity surveying and rate analysis |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Down Costing — Decomposition Flow

```mermaid

    A --> F[OVERHEADS AND PROFIT]

    style E2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E3 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style F2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F3 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Bottom-Up Costing — Aggregation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Work Task Level 4: Cut and Renew Plate 1 sqm] --> K1[Component Cost: Plate 1]
    L2[Work Task Level 4: Cut and Renew Plate 2 sqm] --> K1
    L3[Work Task Level 4: Welding Inspection] --> K2[Component Cost: Welding]

    K1 --> J1[Sub-System Cost: Bow Section]
    K2 --> J1
    K3[Component Cost: Pipes] --> J2[Sub-System Cost: Side Shell]
    K4[Component Cost: Frames] --> J2

    J1 --> M1[System Cost: Hull]
    J2 --> M1
    J3[Sub-System Cost: Engine Mounts] --> M2[System Cost: Machinery]
    J4[Sub-System Cost: Auxiliaries] --> M2

    M1 --> N1[TOTAL DIRECT COST]
    M2 --> N1
    M3[System Cost: Electrical] --> N1
    M4[System Cost: Painting] --> N1

    N1 --> O1[Add Overheads]
    O1 --> P1[Add Profit]
    P1 --> Q1[Add Contingency]
    Q1 --> R1[FINAL FIRM CONTRACT PRICE]

    style R1 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J1 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J2 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J3 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J4 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M4 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style O1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Hybrid Costing Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PHASE1[Phase 1: Concept] --> TD[Top-Down Budget Estimate]
    TD --> DECISION{Budget Approved?}
    DECISION -->|Yes| PHASE2[Phase 2: Survey and Specification]
    DECISION -->|No| PHASE1
    PHASE2 --> BU[Bottom-Up Firm Estimate]
    BU --> TENDER[Tender Submission to Shipyard]
    TENDER --> NEG[Contract Negotiation]
    NEG --> AWARD[Contract Award]
    AWARD --> EXEC[Execution and Variation Control]
    EXEC --> FINAL[Final Actual Cost and Reconciliation]

    style PHASE1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style TD fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style BU fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style FINAL fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style DECISION fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Top-Down approach in ship repair cost estimation. State two situations where it is preferred over the Bottom-Up method.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Top-Down approach** in ship repair cost estimation is a parametric and analogy-based methodology where the total project cost is first determined from historical cost data, parametric models, or expert judgment, and then allocated downward to the major cost centers such as hull, machinery, electrical, and docking.

It is preferred in the following two situations:
- **Pre-feasibility and budgeting stage** — when the shipowner needs a quick order-of-magnitude estimate within hours of receiving a damage report, before the vessel has even reached the shipyard.
- **Insurance and P&I claim reserving** — when the marine insurer or P&I club must set aside a loss reserve immediately after a maritime casualty, well before any detailed survey is conducted.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) in the context of Bottom-Up ship repair costing? Mention its typical levels.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** in ship repair costing is a hierarchical decomposition of the entire repair project into progressively smaller and more manageable work packages. It forms the structural backbone of the Bottom-Up approach, since each WBS leaf node corresponds to an elementary cost item that can be quantified, rate-applied, and aggregated.

The typical levels are:
- **Level 0:** The complete ship repair project.
- **Level 1:** Major systems (Hull, Machinery, Electrical, Outfit, Painting).
- **Level 2:** Sub-systems (e.g., Bow, Midship, Stern; Main Engine, Auxiliaries).
- **Level 3:** Components (e.g., Side shell plate, Main engine turbocharger).
- **Level 4:** Elementary work tasks (e.g., Cut-out 1 m² of plate, supply 1 ton of AH36 steel, perform 1 meter of butt weld).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Top-Down approach** to ship repair cost estimation. Describe the analogy method and parametric method with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Handymax bulk carrier of **45,000 DWT** is to undergo a special survey. A similar vessel of **50,000 DWT** was repaired last year for **USD 3,200,000**. The cost escalation index has risen by **6%** per annum, and **18 months** have elapsed. Compute the Top-Down cost estimate using the DWT analogy. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Theoretical Explanation (7 Marks)

The **Top-Down approach** is a macro-level estimation technique. The estimator begins with a global figure for the entire project cost and decomposes it into smaller cost centers. The two most common Top-Down sub-methods are:

- **Analogy Method:** Cost of a new vessel repair is estimated by scaling the cost of a previous similar vessel using a key parameter (DWT, GRT, LOA, or shaft horsepower). The formula is:
$$
C_{new} = C_{ref} \times \dfrac{P_{new}}{P_{ref}} \times (1+f_{esc})^{\Delta t}
$$
where $P$ is the chosen scaling parameter.

- **Parametric Method:** Uses industry-standard cost-estimating relationships (CERs). For example, hull blasting and painting is often estimated as **USD 18–30 per square meter**, and main engine overhaul is estimated as a percentage of the engine's new-build price (typically **8–14%**).

**Example of Analogy Method:** If a sister vessel of 50,000 DWT cost USD 3.2M for special survey, a 45,000 DWT vessel would be estimated as $3.2M \times (45,000/50,000) = \$2.88M$ (before escalation).

**Example of Parametric Method:** A 15,000 m² hull blast and paint job at USD 22/m² equals USD 330,000. This figure is plugged into the top-level hull cost center.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $C_{ref} = \$3,200,000$
- $DWT_{ref} = 50,000$
- $DWT_{new} = 45,000$
- $f_{esc} = 6\% = 0.06$ per annum
- $\Delta t = 18 \text{ months} = 1.5 \text{ years}$

**Step 1 — Apply the Top-Down analogy formula:**

$$
C_{top} = C_{ref} \times \dfrac{DWT_{new}}{DWT_{ref}} \times (1+f_{esc})^{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
C_{top} = 3{,}200{,}000 \times \dfrac{45{,}000}{50{,}000} \times (1+0.06)^{1.5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the DWT ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{45{,}000}{50{,}000} = 0.9
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the escalation factor:**

$$
(1.06)^{1.5} = e^{1.5 \times \ln(1.06)} = e^{1.5 \times 0.0583} = e^{0.0874} = 1.0913
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply all factors:**

$$
C_{top} = 3{,}200{,}000 \times 0.9 \times 1.0913
$$

**Step 6 — First multiplication:**

$$
3{,}200{,}000 \times 0.9 = 2{,}880{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 7 — Final multiplication:**

$$
2{,}880{,}000 \times 1.0913 = 3{,}142{,}944 \text{ USD}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{C_{top} \approx \$3{,}142{,}944}
$$

**Accuracy Band (±25%):** USD 2,357,208 to USD 3,928,680.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **Bottom-Up approach** to ship repair cost estimation with the help of a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) diagram. Explain the role of BOQ and unit rate analysis. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following elementary work items are identified during the dry-dock survey of a product tanker. Compute the total direct cost using the Bottom-Up method. Apply overheads of **15%**, profit of **10%**, and a contingency of **5%** to arrive at the final firm price. **(7 Marks)**

| Item | Description | Quantity | Labor/Unit | Material/Unit | Sundry/Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| W-01 | Pipe renewal (m) | 40 | 80 | 120 | 20 |
| W-02 | Valve replacement (nos) | 12 | 250 | 480 | 60 |
| W-03 | Steel renewal (ton) | 6 | 1,400 | 2,600 | 350 |

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Theoretical Explanation (7 Marks)

The **Bottom-Up approach** is a micro-level estimation technique. The estimator begins with a WBS, which decomposes the repair into a hierarchical tree of manageable work packages. The WBS structure for a typical dry-dock repair is:

- **Level 0:** Project (Special Survey No. 3)
- **Level 1:** Hull, Machinery, Electrical, Outfit, Painting
- **Level 2:** Bow, Side Shell, Engine Room, Bridge
- **Level 3:** Plate, Frame, Pipe, Pump, Motor
- **Level 4:** Cut-out 1 m² of plate, renew 1 m of pipe, overhaul 1 pump

The **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** is the numerical measurement of each Level-4 work item (e.g., 12 tons of steel, 40 m of pipe, 12 valves).

**Unit rate analysis** is the determination of the all-inclusive cost of performing one unit of the work, which includes:
- **Direct Labor (L):** Wage × man-hours per unit
- **Direct Material (M):** Cost of steel, pipes, valves, consumables per unit
- **Sundry (S):** Welding gas, electrodes, grinding discs, scaffolding per unit

The total direct cost is:
$$
C_{direct} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} Q_j \times (L_j + M_j + S_j)
$$

The final firm price is obtained by rolling up the direct cost and adding percentage markups for overheads, profit, and contingency.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the unit rate $R_j = L_j + M_j + S_j$ for each item:**

For W-01 (pipe renewal per meter):
$$
R_{01} = 80 + 120 + 20 = 220 \text{ USD/m}
$$

For W-02 (valve replacement per number):
$$
R_{02} = 250 + 480 + 60 = 790 \text{ USD/no}
$$

For W-03 (steel renewal per ton):
$$
R_{03} = 1{,}400 + 2{,}600 + 350 = 4{,}350 \text{ USD/ton}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the elementary cost $C_j = Q_j \times R_j$:**

For W-01:
$$
C_{01} = 40 \times 220 = 8{,}800 \text{ USD}
$$

For W-02:
$$
C_{02} = 12 \times 790 = 9{,}480 \text{ USD}
$$

For W-03:
$$
C_{03} = 6 \times 4{,}350 = 26{,}100 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the direct cost:**

$$
C_{direct} = 8{,}800 + 9{,}480 + 26{,}100 = 44{,}380 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply indirect cost formula:**

$$
C_{final} = C_{direct} \times (1 + O + P + R_{cont})
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the percentages ($O = 0.15$, $P = 0.10$, $R_{cont} = 0.05$):**

$$
C_{final} = 44{,}380 \times (1 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.05)
$$

**Step 6 — Sum the fractional terms:**

$$
1 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.05 = 1.30
$$

**Step 7 — Final multiplication:**

$$
C_{final} = 44{,}380 \times 1.30 = 57{,}694 \text{ USD}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{C_{final} = \$57{,}694}
$$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Exam!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the time unit in escalation:** Students often write $(1+f_{esc})^{\Delta t}$ but plug in $\Delta t$ in months instead of years. Escalation rates are always quoted **per annum**. 18 months = **1.5 years**, not 18.
>
> 2. **Wrong choice of analogy parameter:** Using LOA when the hull form has changed (e.g., bulbous bow retrofit) will give a misleading estimate. Always select the parameter most closely correlated with the cost driver (DWT for steel, GRT for docking, SHP for propulsion).
>
> 3. **Omitting unit consistency:** If labor is in **USD/ton** and material in **USD/kg**, the answer will be off by a factor of 1000. Always normalize units first.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to add the indirect markups in Bottom-Up:** Many students compute only the direct cost and stop, losing 2–3 marks for the final firm price.
>
> 5. **Mixing up the direction of Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up:** Top-Down is **macro → micro** (decomposition). Bottom-Up is **micro → macro** (aggregation). Drawing the wrong arrow direction in a 7-mark question loses at least 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the contingency reserve:** Insurance and IACS guidelines mandate a minimum of **3–7%** contingency in any firm ship repair estimate. Omitting it shows a lack of professional awareness.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Top-Down = Macro → Micro** (decomposition); **Bottom-Up = Micro → Macro** (aggregation).
> - Top-Down is **fast, cheap, but rough** (±25–50% accuracy); Bottom-Up is **slow, costly, but precise** (±5–10%).
> - Top-Down is used at the **concept, feasibility, and budgeting** stage; Bottom-Up is used at the **tendering, class survey, and execution** stage.
> - The **analogy formula** is the heart of Top-Down: $C_{new} = C_{ref} \times (P_{new}/P_{ref}) \times (1+f_{esc})^{\Delta t}$.
> - The **Bottom-Up formula** is: $C_{bottom} = \sum Q_j \times R_j$, where $R_j = L_j + M_j + S_j$.
> - The **final firm price** formula adds overheads, profit, and contingency: $C_{final} = C_{direct} \times (1 + O + P + R_{cont})$.
> - The **WBS** is the structural backbone of Bottom-Up, with 3 to 5 levels of decomposition.
> - The **BOQ** is the numerical measurement of WBS leaf nodes.
> - **Unit rate analysis** decomposes each rate into Labor + Material + Sundry.
> - **Docking fees** are calculated as $GRT \times DailyRate \times Days$.
> - **Steel renewal** is the single largest cost element in hull repair (~20% of dry-dock cost).
> - **Industry best practice** uses a **hybrid approach**: Top-Down budget first → Bottom-Up firm bid later.
> - **Escalation rates** are always **per annum**; convert months to years before applying.
> - **Contingency reserve** of 3–7% is a professional must-have, not optional.
> - **Accuracy check** formula: $E = \vert C_{est} - C_{actual} \vert / C_{actual} \times 100\%$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demarcations and Subdivisions of Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Demarcations and Subdivisions of Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Ship Survey, Estimation and Repair**, *Demarcations and Subdivisions of Costs* refers to the systematic classification, segregation, and allocation of the total monetary expenditure associated with a shipbuilding or ship-repair project into clearly defined logical groupings. The primary objective is to enable accurate **cost control**, transparent **tendering**, defensible **variation accounting**, and auditable **claim settlement** between the Shipowner, the Shipyard (Builder/Repairer), and the Classification Society / Marine Warranty Surveyor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> A Naval Architect / Marine Engineer must clearly *demarcate* (separate/isolate) costs and then *subdivide* (further break down) them so that the final **Ship Repair Estimate (SRE)** is unambiguous. Any ambiguity in cost demarcation leads to contractual disputes, especially under **Lump Sum (LS)**, **Unit Price (UP)**, or **Cost Plus (CP)** contract forms recognized by BIMCO and Lloyd's Standard Ship Repair Contract (SAJ).

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Cost Demarcation

A repair-estimate master file typically has three vertical demarcation lines:

1. **Demarcation by Ownership Boundary** – separating costs borne by the *Owner* (e.g., classification fees, spares, flag-state dues) versus costs borne by the *Yard* (e.g., labour, consumables, utilities).
2. **Demarcation by Contract Boundary** – separating *Firm* work, *Tendered* work, and *Budgetary* (provisional / PC-sum) work.
3. **Demarcation by Time Boundary** – separating costs incurred *before* docking, *during* docking, and *after* undocking (post-docking sea trials, gas-freeing, etc.).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Ship Repair Estimate like a Family Vacation Budget:**
>
> Imagine you are planning a 10-day family trip. Before booking, you split expenses into **fixed** (flight tickets – you pay no matter what) and **variable** (food – depends on appetite). You then sub-divide them by **who pays** (dad, mom, kids), by **category** (travel, stay, food, sightseeing), and by **day** (Day 1, Day 2 …).
>
> A shipyard does the exact same thing with a vessel:
> - **Fixed cost** ↔ Dry-dock hire (paid even if no work is done).
> - **Variable cost** ↔ Steel consumed (kg × rate).
> - **Demarcation by boundary** ↔ Who pays – Yard or Owner.
> - **Subdivision** ↔ Hull, Machinery, Electrical, Outfit, Paint, Joiner.
>
> Without this segregation, the yard would not know whether to invoice the owner for a propeller crack or absorb it as overhead.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Cost Subdivision

The following standard units and metrics are universally used in ship-cost literature:

- **Currency:** **USD (United States Dollar)**, **EUR (Euro)**, or **INR (₹ – Indian Rupee)** as per contract.
- **Weight unit for steel:** **Tonne (t) = 1000 kg** or **Long Ton (LTON) = 1016 kg** (Lloyd's Register convention).
- **Length unit:** **Metre (m)** for plate area calculations.
- **Man-hour (MH):** **1 MH = 1 skilled worker × 1 hour**; the most fundamental labour metric.
- **Standard Man-Hour (SMH):** The benchmark time derived from work-study data.
- **Dry-dock day:** **24-hour billing cycle** for a dock position.
- **Currency-of-Account (COA):** The base currency of the estimate (often USD under BIMCO/SAJ forms).
- **Indexation factor:** Uses **escalation indices** such as the **OECD Mechanical Engineering Price Index** or **Indian Wholesale Price Index (WPI)**.

> [!TIP]
> Always write *Demarcation* (noun: the act of marking a boundary) and *Subdivision* (noun: the act of breaking a whole into parts) – these two words are NOT interchangeable. A *demarcation* sets a *boundary*; a *subdivision* creates *parts inside* that boundary.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Demarcate and Subdivide Costs?

The "Why" behind the system rests on four engineering-economic principles:

- **Traceability (Cost Object Identification):** Every ₹ must be linked to a *Cost Object* – a single plate, a single pipe, a single repair item. Without this, the yard cannot answer the owner's query, *"Why is the bill so high?"*
- **Cost Control:** By comparing *Actual* vs *Standard* (estimated) cost per subdivision, the yard triggers **Variance Analysis** and corrective action.
- **Contractual Fairness:** Many ship-repair contracts are *Unit-Price* (e.g., ₹/tonne of steel renewed) – without subdivision, the rate × quantity formula is meaningless.
- **Performance Benchmarking:** Subdivisions enable *Productivity Indices* (e.g., MH/tonne of steel) to be compared yard-to-yard.

### 2.2 The "How" – Six Classical Methods of Cost Demarcation

A senior estimator applies **six orthogonal (independent) ways** of slicing the cost-pie. They are *not* alternatives – they are *simultaneous* views of the SAME money.

| # | Demarcation Axis | Two (or more) Categories Produced | KTU Exam Trigger Words |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **By Element (Nature)** | Material, Labour, Expenses | *"Classify the elements of cost."* |
| 2 | **By Function** | Production, Administration, Selling, Distribution, R&D | *"Give functional classification."* |
| 3 | **By Behaviour** | Fixed, Variable, Semi-variable (Mixed) | *"Distinguish fixed from variable cost."* |
| 4 | **By Controllability** | Controllable, Non-controllable | *"What can the foreman control?"* |
| 5 | **By Traceability (Direct/Indirect)** | Direct, Indirect | *"Define direct and indirect material."* |
| 6 | **By Cost Object / Activity** | Hull, Machinery, Electrical, Outfit, Painting, Joiner, etc. | *"Subdivide into ship systems."* |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation convention used:** Roman capitals denote totals, lower-case italics denote per-unit values. All cost values are in the **Currency-of-Account (COA)** unless otherwise stated. Vertical bars use `\vert` inside LaTeX to avoid Markdown table breakage.

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{T}$ | $C_{T} = C_{M} + C_{L} + C_{E}$ | Total Cost = Material + Labour + Expenses (Element-wise summation) |
| $C_{P}$ | $C_{P} = C_{M} + C_{L} + C_{E,\text{direct}}$ | Prime Cost (a.k.a. Flat Cost / First Cost) |
| $C_{W}$ | $C_{W} = C_{P} + O_{H,\text{works}}$ | Works Cost = Prime + Works Overhead |
| $C_{Prod}$ | $C_{Prod} = C_{W} + O_{H,\text{admin}}$ | Cost of Production = Works + Admin Overhead |
| $C_{S}$ | $C_{S} = C_{Prod} + O_{S,D}$ | Cost of Sales = Cost of Production + Selling & Distribution |
| $C_{Total}$ | $C_{Total} = C_{S} + \Pi$ | Total Cost (with Profit $\Pi$) |
| $C_{F}$ | $C_{F} = \text{constant}$ | Fixed Cost (independent of output $Q$) |
| $C_{V}$ | $C_{V} = v \cdot Q$ | Variable Cost (linear in output $Q$) |
| $C_{SV}$ | $C_{SV} = C_{F} + v \cdot Q$ | Semi-Variable (Mixed) Cost |
| $BEP$ | $BEP = \dfrac{C_{F}}{p - v}$ | Break-Even Point (units) – $p$ = price per unit |
| $\eta_{L}$ | $\eta_{L} = \dfrac{SMH}{AMH}$ | Labour Efficiency = Standard MH ÷ Actual MH |
| $C_{u}$ | $C_{u} = \dfrac{C_{T}}{Q}$ | Unit Cost |
| $V_{M}$ | $V_{M} = (AQ \cdot AP) - (SQ \cdot SP)$ | Material Price Variance |
| $V_{U}$ | $V_{U} = AQ \cdot (AP - SP)$ | Material Usage Variance |
| $K_{esc}$ | $K_{esc} = \dfrac{I_{n}}{I_{0}}$ | Escalation factor from base year $0$ to estimate year $n$ |
| $C_{adj}$ | $C_{adj} = C_{0} \cdot K_{esc}$ | Inflation-adjusted cost |

### 2.4 Boundary Conditions & Engineering Wisdom

- The **Boundary Condition** for the *direct* label is *physical traceability* to a single cost object. The instant a cost loses traceability (e.g., a yard electrician's salary covering 5 jobs), it becomes *indirect* and is recovered through an **Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)**.
- **Recoverable cost** is reimbursable by the owner (e.g., classification fees, owner-supplied spares handling).
- **Non-recoverable cost** is yard-borne (e.g., warranty rework, liquidated damages for yard delay).
- **Escalation boundary:** A quote older than **180 days** is generally re-opened for price escalation under BIMCO REPAIR 2020 standard terms.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Yard MIS (Management Information System):** The Six-Demarcation Matrix feeds a Pivot Table in SAP/ERP, enabling the *Cost Accountant* and *Production Planner* to query *"Show me variable, direct, hull costs of Ship-A in Q3."*
- **Insurance Claim (Average Adjuster):** In a marine casualty, the *Average Adjuster* (often a chartered loss-adjuster) uses the **Demarcation by Time** to separate *general average* (GA) sacrifices from *particular charges* (PC).
- **Tender Benchmarking:** A naval architect comparing bids uses the **unit-cost** $\left(C_{u}\right)$ subdivision to detect *front-loaded* or *back-loaded* pricing.
- **Classification Society Audit:** Surveyors cross-check the *controllability* and *traceability* demarcations during ISO 9001 / 14001 audits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cost-Build-Up Pyramid (Bottom-Up to Total Cost)

This derivation proves that the **Total Contract Price (TCP)** of a ship-repair job is the *hierarchical summation* of six discrete layers, each separated by a *demarcation line*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> We are forbidden from skipping algebraic transitions. Every line of the derivation is shown in full.

**Step 1 – Direct Material Cost (DMC).** Direct material is any physical substance that becomes a traceable part of the finished repair. Example: replacement steel plate.

$$
DMC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i \cdot p_i
$$

where $q_i$ is the *quantity* of item $i$ (e.g., kg, m, pieces) and $p_i$ is the *unit price* of item $i$. The summation index $i$ runs from $1$ to $n$, the total number of distinct direct-material line-items.

**Step 2 – Direct Labour Cost (DLC).** Direct labour is the productive man-hours of workers physically executing the traceable task.

$$
DLC = \sum_{j=1}^{m} h_j \cdot r_j
$$

where $h_j$ is the *man-hours* spent by worker/category $j$ and $r_j$ is the *hourly wage rate* (including fringe benefits) of that category. $m$ is the number of labour categories (e.g., welder, fitter, painter).

**Step 3 – Direct Expenses (DEX).** These are *traceable third-party* or *special-process* charges (e.g., NDT agency fee, sub-contracted propeller balancing).

$$
DEX = \sum_{k=1}^{s} e_k
$$

**Step 4 – Prime Cost (PC).** The *first demarcation line* in classical cost accounting is drawn here, isolating all *direct* elements from *indirect*.

$$
PC = DMC + DLC + DEX
$$

**Step 5 – Works Overhead (WHO).** Indirect costs incurred *inside* the works boundary (depreciation of dock, yard electrician, storekeeper, consumables, supervisory staff).

$$
WHO = \alpha \cdot PC
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **Works Overhead Absorption Rate** (typically **0.35 to 0.55** in Indian shipyards and **0.45 to 0.65** in European yards). The exact value is determined from historical cost ledgers.

**Step 6 – Works Cost (WC).** The *second demarcation line* sits here, separating *production-side* cost from *administration-side* cost.

$$
WC = PC + WHO = PC \cdot (1 + \alpha)
$$

**Step 7 – Administration Overhead (AOH).** Costs of head-office, HR, finance, marketing – not tied to any specific repair.

$$
AOH = \beta \cdot WC
$$

$\beta$ is the **Administration Overhead Absorption Rate** (typically **0.05 to 0.10**).

**Step 8 – Cost of Production (COP).** The *third demarcation line* isolates *production* from *selling/distribution*.

$$
COP = WC + AOH = WC \cdot (1 + \beta)
$$

**Step 9 – Selling & Distribution Overhead (SDH).** Quotation, follow-up, transport of finished vessel to the delivery berth, warranty.

$$
SDH = \gamma \cdot COP
$$

**Step 10 – Cost of Sales (CS).** Pre-profit, pre-tax total cost.

$$
CS = COP + SDH = COP \cdot (1 + \gamma)
$$

**Step 11 – Profit Margin (Π).** Yard's reward.

$$
\Pi = \mu \cdot CS
$$

**Step 12 – Total Contract Price (TCP).** The *final* total, with the *fourth demarcation line* drawn between *cost* and *price*.

$$
TCP = CS + \Pi = CS \cdot (1 + \mu)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Substituting everything back** in a single closed-form expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCP &= PC \cdot (1 + \alpha) \cdot (1 + \beta) \cdot (1 + \gamma) \cdot (1 + \mu) \\
&= \left( DMC + DLC + DEX \right) \cdot (1 + \alpha) \cdot (1 + \beta) \cdot (1 + \gamma) \cdot (1 + \mu)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Master Formula** for any ship-repair tender.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Calculation Style)

**Problem.** A bulk carrier undergoes bow-repair. The estimate yields:

- Direct material: **DMC = ₹ 18,00,000**
- Direct labour: **DLC = ₹ 12,00,000**
- Direct expenses: **DEX = ₹ 2,00,000**
- $\alpha = 0.50$, $\beta = 0.08$, $\gamma = 0.04$, $\mu = 0.10$

**Solution – Stepwise:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &= 18{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 32{,}00{,}000 \\
WC &= PC \cdot (1 + \alpha) = 32{,}00{,}000 \cdot 1.50 = \text{₹ } 48{,}00{,}000 \\
COP &= WC \cdot (1 + \beta) = 48{,}00{,}000 \cdot 1.08 = \text{₹ } 51{,}84{,}000 \\
CS &= COP \cdot (1 + \gamma) = 51{,}84{,}000 \cdot 1.04 = \text{₹ } 53{,}91{,}360 \\
TCP &= CS \cdot (1 + \mu) = 53{,}91{,}360 \cdot 1.10 = \text{₹ } 59{,}30{,}496
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Demarcation × Subdivision Cross-Table

The table below shows the **same money seen through two demarcation axes simultaneously** – a frequent KTU "explain with a matrix" question.

| Subdivision (by Ship-System) ↓ \ Demarcation (by Element) → | Direct Material (₹) | Direct Labour (₹) | Direct Expenses (₹) | Works OH (₹) | **Sub-Total (₹)** |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Hull Steel Renewal | 8,00,000 | 4,50,000 | 80,000 | 5,55,000 | 18,85,000 |
| Machinery Overhaul | 3,20,000 | 2,80,000 | 60,000 | 2,77,200 | 9,37,200 |
| Electrical Rewiring | 2,10,000 | 1,80,000 | 30,000 | 1,76,400 | 5,96,400 |
| Outfit & Piping | 3,70,000 | 2,40,000 | 50,000 | 2,77,200 | 9,37,200 |
| Painting & Corrosion Control | 1,00,000 | 50,000 | 20,000 | 70,800 | 2,40,800 |
| **Column Total (₹)** | **18,00,000** | **12,00,000** | **2,40,000** | **13,56,000** | **45,96,000** |

> [!NOTE]
> The column totals reconcile to the elements of cost. The grand total becomes the **Cost of Production (COP)** in our build-up, before profit and selling-overhead are added.

### 3.4 Fixed / Variable Cost Separation (High-Leverage Topic)

For a *Floating Dock* (a common cost object in a shipyard), the following demarcation is applied:

- **Fixed Cost (FC):** Dock depreciation, dock crew salaries, insurance, statutory survey – incurred *whether* a ship is in dock or not.
- **Variable Cost (VC):** Block-shore labour, electricity consumed by dock pumps, fresh-water flushing, dock-master's per-job allowance – incurred *only when* a ship occupies the dock.
- **Semi-Variable:** A small part is partly fixed (e.g., minimum 1 watch-keeper) + partly variable (e.g., extra watch-keepers per 50 m of vessel length).

$$
C_{dock}(D) = FC_{dock} + v_{dock} \cdot D
$$

where $D$ is the *number of docking days* and $v_{dock}$ is the *per-day variable dock cost*. The **Break-Even Docking Days** for the dock to recover its fixed cost is:

$$
D_{BE} = \frac{FC_{dock}}{r_{dock} - v_{dock}}
$$

where $r_{dock}$ is the daily hire rate charged to the vessel.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Cost Build-Up Pyramid

The following Mermaid block-flow schematic depicts the *six-layer* build-up from a *single traceable item* (bottom) to the *Total Contract Price* (top). Each layer is a *demarcation boundary*, and each layer-internal block is a *subdivision*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[itemA["Direct Material q1 x p1"]] --> L1[sumA["Direct Material Total DMC"]]
    itemB["Direct Labour h1 x r1"] --> L2[sumB["Direct Labour Total DLC"]]
    itemC["Direct Expenses e1"] --> L3[sumC["Direct Expenses DEX"]]
    sumA --> L4[primeNode["Prime Cost PC = DMC + DLC + DEX"]]
    sumB --> L4
    sumC --> L4
    L4 -->|x 1 plus alpha| L5[worksNode["Works Cost WC"]]
    L5 -->|x 1 plus beta| L6[copNode["Cost of Production COP"]]
    L6 -->|x 1 plus gamma| L7[csNode["Cost of Sales CS"]]
    L7 -->|x 1 plus mu| L8[tcpNode["TOTAL CONTRACT PRICE TCP"]]
    L5 --> who1["Works Overhead alpha x PC"]
    L6 --> aoh1["Admin Overhead beta x WC"]
    L7 --> sdh1["Selling and Distribution gamma x COP"]
    L8 --> prof1["Profit mu x CS"]
```

### 4.2 Six-Axis Demarcation Wheel (Radial View)

The *radial* Mermaid graph below shows that the **same total cost** has *six simultaneous* demarcation views. The centre is the *Total Cost*; the six outer petals are the *six axes* (Element, Function, Behaviour, Controllability, Traceability, Cost Object).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    coreN["TOTAL COST CT"] --> ax1["AXIS 1 BY ELEMENT Material Labour Expenses"]
    coreN --> ax2["AXIS 2 BY FUNCTION Production Admin Selling RD"]
    coreN --> ax3["AXIS 3 BY BEHAVIOUR Fixed Variable Semivariable"]
    coreN --> ax4["AXIS 4 BY CONTROLLABILITY Controllable Noncontrollable"]
    coreN --> ax5["AXIS 5 BY TRACEABILITY Direct Indirect"]
    coreN --> ax6["AXIS 6 BY COST OBJECT Hull Machinery Electrical Outfit Paint"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Flow: Time-Boundary Demarcation of a Dry-Dock Repair

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tA["PHASE A PRE DOCKING Demobilisation Survey Quotation Owner Approvals"] --> tB["PHASE B DURING DOCKING Hull Work Machinery Work Electrical Work Painting Outfitting Sea Trial"]
    tB --> tC["PHASE C POST DOCKING Undocking Mooring Gas Freeing Handing Over Documentation"]
    tA --> capA["Cost Bucket Pre Docking" ]
    tB --> capB["Cost Bucket Docking" ]
    tC --> capC["Cost Bucket Post Docking" ]
```

### 4.4 Cost-Object Subdivision Map (Ship-System View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    shipA["SHIP REPAIR JOB"] --> hullN["HULL Steel Renewal Blasting Painting Corrosion Protection"]
    shipA --> machN["MACHINERY Main Engine Auxiliaries Boilers Propulsor Shafting"]
    shipA --> elecN["ELECTRICAL Generators Cabling Switchboards Automation"]
    shipA --> outN["OUTFIT AND PIPING Valves Pumps Sanitary Deck Fittings"]
    shipA --> joinN["JOINERY Cabins Galley Insulation"]
    shipA --> navN["NAVIGATION AND COMMS Radar GMDSS Radio"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node identifiers above are *alphanumeric* (e.g., `ax1`, `capA`) and contain *no* reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) as standalone node names. All labels are *raw uppercase alphanumeric text* without markdown formatting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] *Define the term "Demarcation of Cost" in the context of ship-repair estimation. List the three principal boundaries used for cost demarcation.*

**Model Answer:**
Demarcation of cost is the act of *isolating* a specific monetary value from the total estimate using a clear boundary line, so that the cost of a defined scope, owner, or time-window is unambiguous. The three principal boundaries are: (i) **Ownership boundary** (yard-borne vs owner-borne), (ii) **Contract boundary** (firm, tendered, provisional), and (iii) **Time boundary** (pre-docking, in-docking, post-docking). Each demarcation prevents double-counting and contractual dispute.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *Distinguish between "Direct Cost" and "Indirect Cost" with two ship-repair examples each.*

**Model Answer:**
A **direct cost** is traceable to a single cost object (job, item, system) without allocation. Examples: (i) cost of a replacement marine-grade steel plate for the bow (Direct Material), (ii) man-hours of a certified welder executing that specific plate renewal (Direct Labour).
An **indirect cost** cannot be traced to a single cost object and is recovered via an overhead absorption rate. Examples: (i) salary of the storekeeper serving all jobs, (ii) depreciation of the floating dock.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** A Part-B question carries 14 marks, sub-divided as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. The student is offered an *internal choice* (either A or B). Both alternatives are provided below for practice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[A-(a)]** *(7 Marks)* [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

*Classify the cost of a typical ship-repair project using the SIX classical methods of demarcation. Give one example for each category in every method.*

**Model Solution – Stepwise (7 marks):**
