The six methods are independent *views* of the same money. For a *propeller-shaft withdrawal* job:

1. **By Element:** Material (e.g., new manganese-bronze bush), Labour (e.g., fitter's hours), Expenses (e.g., third-party NDT fee).
2. **By Function:** Production (machining the shaft), Administration (yard HR cost), Selling (quotation cost), Distribution (transport of finished propeller), R&D (rare for repair, but could apply to a novel weld-procedure qualification).
3. **By Behaviour:** Fixed (dock hire irrespective of shaft work), Variable (steel consumed = kg × rate), Semi-variable (yard electrician – base + overtime).
4. **By Controllability:** Controllable (foreman's choice of welding sequence), Non-controllable (statutory minimum wage hike).
5. **By Traceability:** Direct (bolts used on this specific shaft), Indirect (works electrician's salary).
6. **By Cost Object:** Hull (none for shaft), Machinery (shaft, bearings, coupling), Electrical (shaft-alarm sensor), Outfit (none), Painting (shaft-tunnel repaint), Joinery (none).

> Full marks require *both* the list and *one example per sub-category*.

**[A-(b)]** *(7 Marks)* [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

*For a ship-repair estimate, the following data is given:*
*DMC = ₹ 9,00,000; DLC = ₹ 5,00,000; DEX = ₹ 1,00,000.*
*Works overhead absorption rate α = 0.40; Administration overhead rate β = 0.10; Selling rate γ = 0.05; Profit margin μ = 0.12.*
*Compute the Total Contract Price using the cost build-up formula.*

**Model Solution – Stepwise (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &= DMC + DLC + DEX = 9{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 15{,}00{,}000 \\
WC &= PC \cdot (1 + \alpha) = 15{,}00{,}000 \cdot 1.40 = \text{₹ } 21{,}00{,}000 \\
COP &= WC \cdot (1 + \beta) = 21{,}00{,}000 \cdot 1.10 = \text{₹ } 23{,}10{,}000 \\
CS &= COP \cdot (1 + \gamma) = 23{,}10{,}000 \cdot 1.05 = \text{₹ } 24{,}25{,}500 \\
TCP &= CS \cdot (1 + \mu) = 24{,}25{,}500 \cdot 1.12 = \text{₹ } 27{,}16{,}560
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Contract Price = ₹ 27,16,560.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[B-(a)]** *(7 Marks)* [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

*Explain, with a neat sketch, the Time-Boundary Demarcation of ship-repair costs. State one cost item belonging to each phase.*

**Model Solution – Stepwise (7 marks):**

The Time Boundary divides the repair into three consecutive, non-overlapping windows:

- **Phase A – Pre-Docking:** From vessel arrival to keel-block setting. Cost items: gas-freeing, in-water survey by divers, scaffolding dismantling for tow-in, owner's class survey.
- **Phase B – In-Docking (the core):** From first keel-block contact to undocking flood. Cost items: hull blasting/painting, steel renewal, propeller withdrawal, machinery overhaul, electrical rewire, sea trial.
- **Phase C – Post-Docking:** From undocking to vessel departure. Cost items: berth hire, final documentation handover, classification issuance, owner's flag-state clearance.

A neat Mermaid-style or hand-drawn timeline (Gantt-like) showing the three coloured bands with one labelled cost each earns the full 7 marks. Students should *not* confuse "post-docking" with "off-hire period" – the latter is an *Owner-side* concept, not a yard cost phase.

**[B-(b)]** *(7 Marks)* [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

*A shipyard is evaluating whether to accept a 3-day docking job at a daily hire rate of ₹ 6,00,000. The fixed cost of the dock per day is ₹ 4,50,000 and the variable cost per docking-day is ₹ 1,00,000. Find the Break-Even Number of Docking Days and advise the yard.*

**Model Solution – Stepwise (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{dock} &= \text{₹ } 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ per day} \\
FC_{dock,\text{per day}} &= \text{₹ } 4{,}50{,}000 \\
v_{dock} &= \text{₹ } 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ per day} \\
D_{BE} &= \frac{FC_{dock,\text{per day}}}{r_{dock} - v_{dock}} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 0.90 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Break-Even = 0.9 days.**
**Advice:** The yard recovers its daily fixed cost in just 0.9 days of a 1-day hire, so the 3-day job yields a substantial contribution margin of **(r − v) × (3 − 0.9) = 5,00,000 × 2.1 = ₹ 10,50,000** over the break-even point. The yard should *accept* the job.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Triggers** (compiled from KTU valuation reports 2022-2024):
> 1. **Confusing Demarcation with Subdivision:** Demarcation sets a *boundary*; Subdivision breaks the cost into *parts*. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks if the answer treats them as synonyms.
> 2. **Skipping the Prime-Cost step:** Many students jump from *Direct Material* straight to *Total Cost*. The classical chain is DMC → DLC → DEX → PC → WC → COP → CS → TCP. Each layer is a demarcation line.
> 3. **Forgetting the "Plus One" bracket:** When multiplying by $(1 + \alpha)$, students sometimes write $PC \cdot \alpha$ (the *overhead* only) instead of $PC \cdot (1 + \alpha)$ (the *cost-including overhead*). The latter is the Works Cost.
> 4. **Wrong overhead base:** Administration Overhead is applied on **Works Cost**, NOT on **Prime Cost**. Selling & Distribution is applied on **Cost of Production**, NOT on **Works Cost**.
> 5. **Unit-mismatch in Break-Even:** Mixing daily rate, per-day fixed cost, and per-job fixed cost in a single equation.
> 6. **Ignoring escalation in old estimates:** If the quote date is older than 6 months, an escalation factor $K_{esc}$ is mandatory. Examiners reward this nuance.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert units:** A *tonne* in shipping is 1000 kg, but in some UK contracts a *ton* is 1016 kg (long ton). Always state the unit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read before entering the exam hall):**

- **Demarcation** is the act of drawing a *boundary*; **Subdivision** is the act of creating *parts* inside that boundary. They are sequential steps in cost preparation.
- The **six orthogonal demarcation axes** are: Element, Function, Behaviour, Controllability, Traceability, and Cost Object.
- The **three principal boundaries** are: Ownership, Contract, and Time.
- The **classical cost build-up** is: **DMC + DLC + DEX = PC → WC (×1+α) → COP (×1+β) → CS (×1+γ) → TCP (×1+μ)**.
- **Prime Cost (PC)** is also called *Flat Cost* or *First Cost*; it stops at direct elements only.
- **Works Cost (WC)** = PC + Works Overhead – the first *fully-loaded* production cost.
- **Fixed Cost** ≠ *Direct* Cost. A salary can be *fixed* and *indirect* simultaneously; a piece-rate labour is *variable* and *direct* simultaneously.
- **Break-Even Point** = Fixed Cost ÷ (Price − Variable Cost per unit).
- **Escalation factor** $K_{esc} = I_{n} / I_{0}$ must be applied to quotes older than ~180 days.
- **Owner-borne vs Yard-borne** is the single most important demarcation in BIMCO/SAJ contract law.
- **Demolition/scrap credit** must always be netted off the *Material* subdivision; it is *negative* DMC.
- A **Cost Centre** in a shipyard is typically organised by *trade* (Hull, Machinery, Electrical, Outfit, Paint, Joiner, Foundry, Forging).
- A **Cost Object** is what you are *costing for* – a specific repair item, a specific vessel, a specific job.
- Always mention **units** and **currency** in the answer; examiners reward discipline.
- For dry-dock jobs, remember the **Time-Boundary Demarcation**: Pre-Docking, In-Docking, Post-Docking.
- For long-answer derivations, write the *complete* build-up chain – skipping a layer is the #1 mark-loss trigger in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structural Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Structural Costs** in ship survey and estimation refer to the complete monetary valuation of all expenses incurred in the inspection, assessment, renewal, fabrication, and reinstatement of a vessel's load-bearing and watertight structural elements, expressed as the sum of **material cost**, **direct labor cost**, and **applied overheads**, normalized against standard units (per tonne of steel, per square metre of plating, or per running metre of stiffener).

In the context of the **2024 Scheme** and NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework, structural cost estimation is treated as the quantitative bridge between a **surveyor's qualitative condition assessment** (e.g., "severe corrosion, 25% scantling reduction") and a **shipyard's commercial quotation** to the owner. The estimator must translate Condition class notations (Class A, B, C by IACS UR Z17) into a bill of quantities (BOQ).

### 1.2 Constituent Categories of Structural Costs

Structural costs are never a single line item. They are decomposed as follows:

| S. No. | Category | Examples |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Primary Hull Structure** | Shell plating, deck plating, bottom plating, bulkheads |
| 2 | **Secondary Structure** | Frames, stiffeners, girders, stringers, webs |
| 3 | **Superstructure** | Bulwarks, deckhouses, bridge wings, forecastle plating |
| 4 | **Appendages & Fittings** | Rudder, sternframe, shaft brackets, stabiliser fins, hawse pipes |
| 5 | **Auxiliary Steelwork** | Foundations for engines, boilers, masts, cranes |
| 6 | **Renewal Accessories** | Doublers, insert plates, collars, sleeve pieces |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Bill"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of a ship's hull as a human body admitted to a hospital after an accident. The **surveyor** is the doctor who diagnoses what is broken. The **estimator** is the billing department that turns the diagnosis ("left tibia fractured, two ribs cracked") into a financial figure (X-ray fee + orthopaedic surgeon fee + titanium plate + 3-day ward charge). **Structural costs are the "orthopaedic surgery bill" of the ship** — only the bones, not the organs (machinery) or the skin (paint, outfit).

### 1.4 Key Metrics & Standard Reference Constants

- **Density of mild steel for shipbuilding:** **ρ = 7.85 t/m³ (tonnes per cubic metre)**
- **Standard man-hour rate for structural renewal (IACS benchmark):** **25–50 man-hours per tonne of inserted steel** depending on accessibility and curvature.
- **Typical plate-to-scrap recovery ratio:** **70%–80%** of original plate weight is recoverable scrap value; net material cost therefore = (1 − 0.75) × new plate rate as a first-order approximation.
- **KTU-recommended unit cost basis:** **INR/tonne (₹/t)** for renewals, **INR/m² (₹/m²)** for surface preparation and coating.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Layer Cost Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every structural cost line item, when fully decomposed, must resolve into three independent cost layers. Skipping any layer will cause you to lose marks in the **14-mark estimation problem** of the KTU ESE.

**Layer 1 — Material Cost (MC)**
This is the procurement cost of the new steel entering the vessel. It includes:
- Base steel rate (HR plates per IS 2062 / ASTM A36 / AH36 grade as per class requirement)
- Premium for higher-tensile grades (AH36, DH36, EH36 attract a **15%–25% surcharge** over mild steel)
- Section profile cost (rolled sections vs fabricated T-bars)
- Welding consumables (electrodes, wire, flux) — typically **3%–5%** of base steel cost
- Temporary staging timber / support steel — typically **2%** of base steel cost

**Layer 2 — Direct Labor Cost (DLC)**
This is the wages paid to the tradesmen physically cutting, fitting, and welding.
- Cutter hours, fitter hours, welder hours, grinder hours
- Productivity factor (kg of weld deposited per man-hour)
- Standard Indian yard productivity benchmark: **1.0–1.5 kg weld metal deposited per welder-hour** (SMAW in overhead position)
- Overtime premiums for dry-dock urgency

**Layer 3 — Applied Overheads (OH)**
These are the indirect costs the yard loads onto the job:
- Dry-dock or berth hire (per day)
- Crane and equipment hire
- Power, gas, water, safety
- Insurance, supervision, profit margin
- Class survey fees and tonnage dues
- Standard yard loading: **40%–80% of (MC + DLC)** depending on yard size and locality

### 2.2 Operational Flow — From Survey Finding to Costed Item

1. **Surveyor tags** the defective member in the survey report (e.g., "Plate 4S, port side, between frames 38–46, corroded to 30% of original").
2. **Estimator measures** the plate from the ship's general arrangement drawing: length × breadth × thickness → volume × 7.85 t/m³ → tonnage.
3. **Estimator consults standard rate book** (e.g., yard internal Schedule of Rates, or industry-published Clarkson/Drewry benchmarks).
4. **Estimator applies adjustment factors** for grade, position (overhead = +20%, vertical = +10%), curvature, and accessibility.
5. **Estimator multiplies** the adjusted unit rate by the calculated tonnage.
6. **Estimator adds** the three cost layers and sums to a line-item total.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **high-yield** — these are the equations the KTU Board Examiner expects you to reproduce in your answer script for any estimation problem.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $W = L \times B \times t \times \rho$ | Weight of a steel plate (L, B, t in metres, ρ = 7.85) | tonnes (t) |
| 2 | $W = L \times \rho_s$ | Weight per running metre of a rolled section (ρ_s = mass/m, from steel table) | t/m |
| 3 | $C_{total} = C_{MC} + C_{DLC} + C_{OH}$ | Three-layer cost summation | ₹ |
| 4 | $C_{OH} = (f_{OH}) \times (C_{MC} + C_{DLC})$ | Overhead loading (f_OH typically 0.4 to 0.8) | ₹ |
| 5 | $C_{DLC} = N_{mh} \times R_{labour}$ | Labor cost = man-hours × hourly rate | ₹ |
| 6 | $N_{mh} = k \times W$ | Total man-hours (k = 25–50 hr/t) | man-hours |
| 7 | $C_{eff} = C_{MC,new} \times (1 - R_{scrap}) + C_{labour,scrap}$ | Net material cost after scrap credit | ₹ |
| 8 | $f_{pos} \in \{1.00, 1.10, 1.20\}$ | Position factor: flat / vertical / overhead | dimensionless |
| 9 | $f_{grade} \in \{1.00, 1.20\}$ | Grade factor: mild steel / AH36 | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_{line} = C_{unit} \times W \times f_{pos} \times f_{grade} \times f_{curv}$ | Adjusted line-item cost | ₹ |

Where the standard variables are:
- $W$ = weight of the structural member in tonnes
- $C_{MC}$, $C_{DLC}$, $C_{OH}$ = material, direct labor, overhead costs
- $R_{scrap}$ = scrap recovery factor (0.70–0.80)
- $k$ = labour productivity constant (man-hours per tonne of inserted steel)
- $f_{pos}$, $f_{grade}$, $f_{curv}$ = adjustment multipliers for position, grade, and curvature

### 2.4 Why This System Exists in Real Engineering Practice

- **For Owners:** It allows a Class-approved budget forecast before the dry-dock Special Survey (SS).
- **For Yards:** It standardises quotations across heterogeneous ship types (tankers, bulk carriers, container ships).
- **For Surveyors:** It justifies the scope of work recommended in the survey report with a financial envelope.
- **For Insurers (H&M, P&I clubs):** It provides the basis for partial loss settlements and "constructive total loss" assessments.
- **For KTU Graduates:** It is the **single most frequently tested estimation skill** in ship repair management, often carrying 7–10 marks in Module 4 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Plate Weight Formula

We start from the volumetric definition of density and apply it to a rectangular plate.

**Step 1 — Definition of mass density**

Mass density $\rho$ is defined as mass per unit volume.

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

Rearranging for mass $m$:

$$m = \rho \times V$$

**Step 2 — Volume of a rectangular plate**

A flat plate of length $L$, breadth $B$, and uniform thickness $t$ has volume:

$$V = L \times B \times t$$

Substituting:

$$m = \rho \times L \times B \times t$$

**Step 3 — Convert mass to weight in tonnes (the KTU convention)**

The shipbuilding industry universally uses **metric tonnes (t = 1000 kg)** as the weight unit. We therefore express the weight $W$ (in tonnes) as:

$$W = \frac{m}{1000} = \frac{\rho \times L \times B \times t}{1000}$$

Substituting the standard shipbuilding steel density $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$W = \frac{7850 \times L \times B \times t}{1000} = 7.85 \times L \times B \times t$$

This is the **master plate weight equation** used in every Indian shipyard estimation sheet.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Full Line-Item Cost Equation

**Step 1 — Start from the three-layer cost identity**

$$C_{line} = C_{MC} + C_{DLC} + C_{OH}$$

**Step 2 — Expand material cost with scrap credit adjustment**

The yard buys new steel at rate $C_{new}$ (₹/t), but recovers old steel at rate $C_{scrap}$ (₹/t). The net material cost per tonne is:

$$C_{MC,net} = C_{new} - R_{scrap} \times C_{scrap}$$

For the whole tonnage $W$:

$$C_{MC} = W \times (C_{new} - R_{scrap} \times C_{scrap})$$

**Step 3 — Expand direct labor cost**

With man-hour productivity $k$ (hr/t) and labour rate $R_L$ (₹/hr):

$$C_{DLC} = W \times k \times R_L$$

**Step 4 — Express overhead as a loading on (MC + DLC)**

$$C_{OH} = f_{OH} \times (C_{MC} + C_{DLC})$$

**Step 5 — Combine into the master line-item cost equation**

$$
C_{line} = (1 + f_{OH}) \times \left[ W \times (C_{new} - R_{scrap} \cdot C_{scrap}) + W \times k \times R_L \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Apply adjustment factors for non-standard conditions**

$$
C_{line}^{adj} = f_{pos} \times f_{grade} \times f_{curv} \times (1 + f_{OH}) \times W \times \left[ (C_{new} - R_{scrap} \cdot C_{scrap}) + k \cdot R_L \right]
$$

This is the **complete KTU ESE-ready estimation equation**. You may safely write this in the answer script to claim full marks in any 14-mark Part B estimation problem.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-mark style):**

> A bulk carrier enters dry-dock for its second Special Survey. The surveyor's report identifies a severely corroded port-side shell plate measuring 6.0 m × 2.5 m, with an as-built thickness of 18 mm requiring full renewal. The plate is in an **overhead** position and is of **mild steel** grade (no premium). Given:
> - Mild steel plate rate $C_{new} = ₹95{,}000/\text{t}$
> - Scrap rate $C_{scrap} = ₹45{,}000/\text{t}$
> - Scrap recovery factor $R_{scrap} = 0.75$
> - Productivity constant $k = 40 \text{ hr/t}$
> - Labour rate $R_L = ₹550/\text{hr}$
> - Overhead loading $f_{OH} = 0.60$
> - Position factor $f_{pos} = 1.20$ (overhead)
> - Curvature factor $f_{curv} = 1.10$ (slight curvature)
> - Grade factor $f_{grade} = 1.00$
>
> **Calculate the estimated line-item cost for renewing this plate.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate plate tonnage**

Using $W = 7.85 \times L \times B \times t$ with L = 6.0 m, B = 2.5 m, t = 0.018 m:

$$W = 7.85 \times 6.0 \times 2.5 \times 0.018$$

$$W = 7.85 \times 0.27 = 2.1195 \text{ t}$$

So the plate weighs approximately **2.12 tonnes**.

**Step 2 — Calculate net material cost per tonne**

$$C_{MC,net} = C_{new} - R_{scrap} \times C_{scrap} = 95{,}000 - (0.75 \times 45{,}000)$$

$$C_{MC,net} = 95{,}000 - 33{,}750 = ₹61{,}250/\text{t}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate direct labour cost per tonne**

$$C_{DLC,t} = k \times R_L = 40 \times 550 = ₹22{,}000/\text{t}$$

**Step 4 — Sum MC + DLC per tonne and apply overhead loading**

$$C_{OH,t} = f_{OH} \times (C_{MC,net} + C_{DLC,t}) = 0.60 \times (61{,}250 + 22{,}000)$$

$$C_{OH,t} = 0.60 \times 83{,}250 = ₹49{,}950/\text{t}$$

**Step 5 — Compute unadjusted line cost**

$$C_{line,unadj} = W \times (C_{MC,net} + C_{DLC,t} + C_{OH,t})$$

$$C_{line,unadj} = 2.1195 \times (61{,}250 + 22{,}000 + 49{,}950)$$

$$C_{line,unadj} = 2.1195 \times 1{,}33{,}200 = ₹2{,}82{,}316$$

**Step 6 — Apply position and curvature adjustment factors**

$$C_{line}^{adj} = f_{pos} \times f_{curv} \times f_{grade} \times C_{line,unadj}$$

$$C_{line}^{adj} = 1.20 \times 1.10 \times 1.00 \times 2{,}82{,}316$$

$$C_{line}^{adj} = 1.32 \times 2{,}82{,}316 = ₹3{,}72{,}657$$

**Final Answer (correct to nearest rupee):**

$$\boxed{C_{line}^{adj} \approx ₹3{,}72{,}657}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Structural Cost to Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix shows how the line-item cost in the worked example changes with each input parameter. This is the kind of "what-if" reasoning KTU examiners value in 14-mark answers.

| Parameter varied | Base value | New value | Resulting Cost (₹) | % Change |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Plate thickness (t) | 18 mm | 22 mm | ₹4,55,470 | +22.2% |
| Position | Overhead | Vertical | ₹3,19,419 | −14.3% |
| Grade | Mild steel | AH36 | ₹4,10,089 | +10.0% |
| Scrap recovery | 0.75 | 0.85 | ₹3,52,348 | −5.4% |
| Overhead loading | 0.60 | 0.40 | ₹3,17,002 | −14.9% |
| Labour productivity | 40 hr/t | 25 hr/t | ₹3,32,839 | −10.7% |

**Inference from the matrix:** Overhead loading and plate thickness dominate the cost variance, while scrap recovery has a comparatively muted effect. This is why yard owners aggressively negotiate dry-dock day rates and why surveyors always report as-built thickness, not nominal thickness, in their condition evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Structural Cost Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Surveyor Field Report] --> B[Defect Identification and Tagging]
    B --> C[Drawing Lookup - GA and Shell Expansion]
    C --> D[Geometric Measurement: L, B, t]
    D --> E[Weight Calculation W = 7.85 x L x B x t]
    E --> F{Steel Grade?}
    F -->|Mild Steel| G1[f_grade = 1.00]
    F -->|AH36 DH36 EH36| G2[f_grade = 1.20]
    G1 --> H{Position?}
    G2 --> H
    H -->|Flat| I1[f_pos = 1.00]
    H -->|Vertical| I2[f_pos = 1.10]
    H -->|Overhead| I3[f_pos = 1.20]
    I1 --> J{Curvature?}
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J -->|Flat Plate| K1[f_curv = 1.00]
    J -->|Single Curvature| K2[f_curv = 1.10]
    J -->|Double Curvature| K3[f_curv = 1.25]
    K1 --> L[Cost Layer 1: Material Cost MC]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M[Cost Layer 2: Direct Labor Cost DLC]
    M --> N[Cost Layer 3: Overhead Loading OH]
    N --> O[Apply Combined Adjustment Factors]
    O --> P[Final Line-Item Cost Entry in BOQ]
    P --> Q[Roll-up to Total Structural Cost]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Decomposition of Structural Cost Elements

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Structural Cost] --> B[Hull Shell Plates]
    A --> C[Deck Plates]
    A --> D[Internal Bulkheads]
    A --> E[Frames and Stiffeners]
    A --> F[Superstructure Steel]
    A --> G[Appendages Rudder Sternframe]
    A --> H[Foundations for Machinery]
    B --> B1[Port Side]
    B --> B2[Starboard Side]
    B --> B3[Bottom]
    C --> C1[Main Deck]
    C --> C2[Poop Deck]
    C --> C3[Forecastle Deck]
    E --> E1[Transverse Frames]
    E --> E2[Longitudinals]
    E --> E3[Webs and Girders]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Cost Adjustment Logic Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Base[Base Unit Rate in INR per tonne] --> Pos{Position of Work}
    Pos -->|Flat| P1[Multiplier 1.00]
    Pos -->|Vertical| P2[Multiplier 1.10]
    Pos -->|Overhead| P3[Multiplier 1.20]
    Base --> Grade{Steel Grade Required}
    Grade -->|MS| G1[Multiplier 1.00]
    Grade -->|HT| G2[Multiplier 1.20]
    Base --> Curv{Plate Curvature}
    Curv -->|Flat| C1[Multiplier 1.00]
    Curv -->|Curved| C2[Multiplier 1.10 to 1.25]
    P1 --> Final[Adjusted Unit Rate]
    P2 --> Final
    P3 --> Final
    G1 --> Final
    G2 --> Final
    C1 --> Final
    C2 --> Final
    Final --> Mult[Multiply by Tonnage W]
    Mult --> Output[Final Line Cost in INR]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Notes (Since Physical Drawings Are Not Feasible in Mermaid)

A physical "structural cost flow" diagram would normally consist of:

1. **A ship cross-section (mid-body)** with the following zones colour-coded:
   - **Bottom plate** (zone 1, flat → f_pos = 1.00)
   - **Side shell plate** (zone 2, vertical → f_pos = 1.10)
   - **Deck plate** (zone 3, overhead → f_pos = 1.20)
2. **A callout arrow** for each zone pointing to its standard rate card (₹/t).
3. **A central BOQ spreadsheet** receiving all three cost contributions.
4. **An overhead tank** at the top of the schematic symbolising the 40%–80% loading.

For KTU answer scripts, **draw a simple labelled sketch** of the ship cross-section with the three zones and the position factors annotated beside each. This fetches full marks under the "neat sketch" sub-credit of the question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define "structural cost" in the context of ship repair estimation. List its three primary components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Structural cost is the total monetary expenditure required to inspect, assess, renew, and reinstate the load-bearing and watertight structural members of a ship. Its three primary components are: **(i) Material Cost (MC)** — the procurement cost of new steel and welding consumables, net of scrap credit; **(ii) Direct Labor Cost (DLC)** — wages of cutters, fitters, welders, and grinders; and **(iii) Applied Overheads (OH)** — indirect costs such as dock hire, crane usage, supervision, and profit margin. (3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for the three components, or 1 + 2 for the three in one line.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
**State the standard shipbuilding steel density and write the formula for calculating the tonnage of a flat steel plate of length L, breadth B, and thickness t.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The standard density of mild shipbuilding steel is **ρ = 7.85 t/m³** (or equivalently 7850 kg/m³). The tonnage of a flat plate is given by:

$$W = 7.85 \times L \times B \times t$$

where L, B, and t are all in metres and W is in tonnes. (3 marks: 1 for density + 2 for the formula with unit explanation.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students lose 3–4 marks on Part B estimation questions because they (a) forget to deduct scrap credit from material cost, (b) omit one of the three cost layers, or (c) apply adjustment factors as percentages instead of dimensionless multipliers. **Always show your work in three clearly labelled layers and keep the factor tables on the side of the page for quick reference during the exam.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **three-layer cost architecture** used in ship structural cost estimation. State the typical range of values for the overhead loading factor in Indian shipyards. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A corroded bottom plate of a single-deck bulk carrier has the following dimensions: length 4.8 m, breadth 3.2 m, as-built thickness 20 mm. The plate is to be renewed in mild steel in a **flat (bottom)** position. The following data apply:
- Mild steel rate: ₹98,000/t
- Scrap rate: ₹42,000/t
- Scrap recovery factor: 0.75
- Productivity constant: 35 hr/t
- Labour rate: ₹600/hr
- Overhead loading: 0.55
- Position factor: 1.00, Grade factor: 1.00, Curvature factor: 1.10

Calculate the **estimated line-item cost** for this renewal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part Solution — Three-Layer Cost Architecture (7 marks):**

The three-layer architecture is the standard Indian shipyard convention for decomposing any structural cost line. **Layer 1 — Material Cost (MC):** This is the procurement cost of the new steel plates, rolled sections, and welding consumables (electrodes, wire, flux) entering the vessel. It is **reduced by the scrap credit** recovered from the old steel removed, which is typically **70%–80%** of the removed weight. **Layer 2 — Direct Labor Cost (DLC):** This is the wages paid to the actual tradesmen performing the cutting, edge preparation, fitting, welding, grinding, and NDT-stage handling. It is calculated as **man-hours × hourly rate**, with the man-hours computed from a productivity constant **k (25–50 hr/t)**. **Layer 3 — Applied Overheads (OH):** This is the indirect cost the yard loads to recover dry-dock hire, cranes, scaffolding, supervision, safety, insurance, and profit. In Indian shipyards the overhead loading factor $f_{OH}$ is typically **0.40 to 0.80** (i.e., 40%–80% of MC + DLC). A neat sketch of the three layers as a stack diagram should accompany the answer. (7 marks: 2 for naming + 2 for description + 1 for ranges + 2 for sketch.)

**(b) Part Solution — Numerical Estimation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Tonnage calculation**

$$W = 7.85 \times L \times B \times t = 7.85 \times 4.8 \times 3.2 \times 0.020$$

$$W = 7.85 \times 0.3072 = 2.4115 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2 — Net material cost per tonne**

$$C_{MC,net} = 98{,}000 - (0.75 \times 42{,}000) = 98{,}000 - 31{,}500 = ₹66{,}500/\text{t}$$

**Step 3 — Direct labour cost per tonne**

$$C_{DLC,t} = 35 \times 600 = ₹21{,}000/\text{t}$$

**Step 4 — Subtotal per tonne**

$$\text{Subtotal} = 66{,}500 + 21{,}000 = ₹87{,}500/\text{t}$$

**Step 5 — Overhead loading**

$$C_{OH,t} = 0.55 \times 87{,}500 = ₹48{,}125/\text{t}$$

**Step 6 — Total per tonne**

$$C_{t} = 87{,}500 + 48{,}125 = ₹1{,}35{,}625/\text{t}$$

**Step 7 — Unadjusted line cost**

$$C_{unadj} = 2.4115 \times 1{,}35{,}625 = ₹3{,}27{,}047$$

**Step 8 — Apply adjustment factors (position × grade × curvature)**

$$f_{total} = 1.00 \times 1.00 \times 1.10 = 1.10$$

**Step 9 — Final adjusted line cost**

$$C_{final} = 1.10 \times 3{,}27{,}047 = ₹3{,}59{,}752$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{C_{final} \approx ₹3{,}59{,}752}$$

(7 marks distributed as: 1 for tonnage + 1 for MC + 1 for DLC + 1 for OH + 1 for unadjusted cost + 1 for adjusted final cost + 1 for correct units and conclusion.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **adjustment factors** (position, grade, curvature) used in structural cost estimation. Justify why overheads are loaded as a percentage of (Material + Labor) and not as a fixed amount per tonne. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The superstructure deckhouse of a chemical tanker requires renewal of two side plates, each measuring 5.0 m × 1.8 m × 14 mm thick, in **AH36 grade steel** in a **vertical** position. Estimate the total cost given:
- AH36 plate rate: ₹1,20,000/t (already includes the 20% grade premium)
- Scrap rate: ₹48,000/t
- Scrap recovery factor: 0.80
- Productivity constant: 30 hr/t
- Labour rate: ₹650/hr
- Overhead loading: 0.50
- Position factor: 1.10, Grade factor: 1.00 (premium already in rate), Curvature factor: 1.00

Calculate the **total estimated cost** for both plates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part Solution — Adjustment Factors and Overhead Logic (7 marks):**

Adjustment factors are dimensionless multipliers that convert a **base unit rate** (₹/t) into a **site- and condition-specific unit rate**, reflecting the fact that the same tonne of steel costs the yard different amounts to install in different locations and grades. The **position factor** captures welding productivity loss: flat = 1.00, vertical = 1.10, overhead = 1.20, because welders deposit weld metal more slowly against gravity. The **grade factor** captures the premium for higher-tensile steel (AH36, DH36, EH36) needed for thicker plates per IACS UR S21. The **curvature factor** captures the additional cutting, rolling, and fitting effort for non-flat plates (1.10 for single curvature, up to 1.25 for double curvature like a stem or boss). Overheads are loaded as a **percentage of (MC + DLC)** rather than a fixed per-tonne amount because overheads scale with labour intensity, dock-day duration, and project complexity — all of which correlate with the labour content of the job, not the tonnage of steel alone. A small structural renewal of 0.5 t and a 50 t bow renewal will use very different yard resources even per tonne, hence the proportional loading. (7 marks: 3 for factors + 2 for overhead justification + 2 for examples.)

**(b) Part Solution — Numerical Estimation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Weight of one plate**

$$W_1 = 7.85 \times 5.0 \times 1.8 \times 0.014 = 7.85 \times 0.126 = 0.9891 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2 — Total weight of two plates**

$$W_{total} = 2 \times 0.9891 = 1.9782 \text{ t}$$

**Step 3 — Net material cost per tonne**

$$C_{MC,net} = 1{,}20{,}000 - (0.80 \times 48{,}000) = 1{,}20{,}000 - 38{,}400 = ₹81{,}600/\text{t}$$

**Step 4 — Direct labour cost per tonne**

$$C_{DLC,t} = 30 \times 650 = ₹19{,}500/\text{t}$$

**Step 5 — Subtotal per tonne**

$$\text{Subtotal} = 81{,}600 + 19{,}500 = ₹1{,}01{,}100/\text{t}$$

**Step 6 — Overhead loading**

$$C_{OH,t} = 0.50 \times 1{,}01{,}100 = ₹50{,}550/\text{t}$$

**Step 7 — Total per tonne including overhead**

$$C_{t} = 1{,}01{,}100 + 50{,}550 = ₹1{,}51{,}650/\text{t}$$

**Step 8 — Unadjusted total cost for both plates**

$$C_{unadj} = 1.9782 \times 1{,}51{,}650 = ₹2{,}99{,}766$$

**Step 9 — Apply adjustment factors**

$$f_{total} = 1.10 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 = 1.10$$

**Step 10 — Final adjusted cost**

$$C_{final} = 1.10 \times 2{,}99{,}766 = ₹3{,}29{,}743$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{C_{final} \approx ₹3{,}29{,}743}$$

(7 marks: 1 for tonnage × 2 plates + 1 for MC + 1 for DLC + 1 for OH + 1 for unadjusted total + 1 for final adjusted cost + 1 for correct unit and conclusion.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision points — print this and read twice before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ **Steel density ρ = 7.85 t/m³** is your single most important constant — write it first on every estimation answer page.
- ☐ The **master plate weight equation** is $W = 7.85 \times L \times B \times t$ — never forget it; it earns 1 mark instantly.
- ☐ Always **decompose into three layers**: Material (MC) → Labor (DLC) → Overhead (OH). Missing even one layer costs 2–3 marks.
- ☐ **Always deduct scrap credit** from material cost: $C_{MC,net} = C_{new} - R_{scrap} \times C_{scrap}$.
- ☐ **Default adjustment factors**: position (1.00 / 1.10 / 1.20), grade (1.00 / 1.20), curvature (1.00 / 1.10 / 1.25).
- ☐ **Productivity constant k = 25–50 hr/t** for ship structural renewal — use the value given in the problem, do not assume.
- ☐ **Indian yard overhead loading f_OH = 0.40 to 0.80** — quote the range in theory answers, the exact value in numerical problems.
- ☐ **Unit consistency check** before final answer: L, B, t in **metres**, W in **tonnes**, rate in **₹/t**, cost in **₹**. If units don't match, the answer is wrong even if the number is right.
- ☐ **Always apply adjustment factors at the very end** as a single multiplication, not piecemeal throughout — this makes the auditor's job easier and earns method marks.
- ☐ Remember the **"Hospital Bill" analogy** — survey report = diagnosis, structural cost = orthopaedic surgery bill, BOQ = itemised invoice.
- ☐ For 7-mark theory questions on adjustment factors, always **justify** the factor with a **physical reason** (e.g., "overhead position reduces welder productivity by ~20% due to gravity and restricted posture").
- ☐ For 7-mark numerical questions, **show all 9–10 steps** explicitly; the KTU board awards step marks liberally for clear working.
- ☐ **Final answer formatting**: round to the nearest rupee and present inside a box (using $\boxed{}$ in LaTeX or a bordered table cell) for the examiner's clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outfit Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Outfit Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Outfit Costs (Definition per KTU PENST861 2024 Syllabus):**
> Outfit costs represent the aggregate expenditure incurred in procuring, fabricating, installing, and commissioning all components, equipment, fittings, and systems that are fitted to a vessel **after** the basic hull structure has been erected and launched. These costs collectively cover every item required to make the hull a fully operational, habitable, and seaworthy ship, excluding only the cost of the raw steel hull plating and basic structural fabrication labour.

In formal ship cost engineering terminology (as adopted by international classification societies and KTU's Naval Architecture curriculum), the **Total Newbuilding Cost (TNC)** of a merchant ship is conventionally partitioned into three primary cost blocks:

$$\text{TNC} = C_{\text{Hull Steel}} + C_{\text{Machinery}} + C_{\text{Outfit}}$$

The **Outfit Cost** ($C_{\text{Outfit}}$) itself is a composite of multiple sub-categories and is the focus of this study note.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you have purchased an empty, locked house shell (the brick walls, roof, and foundation only) for **₹50 lakhs**. Before you can actually *live* in it, you must additionally spend money on:

- Doors, windows, plumbing, electrical wiring, sanitary fittings
- Kitchen appliances, wardrobes, floor tiles, paint
- AC units, geysers, security systems
- Furniture, light fixtures, modular fittings
- Landscaping, compound wall gates

If you add up **all of these "non-brick-wall" expenses**, you will likely find they equal or even exceed the cost of the basic shell. **Outfit Costs in shipbuilding are exactly this** — the "furnishing and fitting" cost of turning an empty steel hull into a fully functional floating vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** For a modern merchant vessel, **outfit costs typically constitute 50–60% of the total newbuilding price (TNC)**. The bare steel hull is often the *minority* cost component — a counterintuitive but absolutely critical fact for ship cost estimation.

### 1.3 Boundary Definition — What Is, and What Is NOT, Outfit

| **INCLUDED in Outfit Costs** | **EXCLUDED from Outfit Costs** |
|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Anchors, chains, mooring & towing equipment | Hull steel plates & sections |
| Deck fittings (bollards, fairleads, capstans) | Hull structural fabrication labour |
| Lifeboats, life rafts, lifejackets, MES systems | Welding consumables for primary structure |
| Fire-fighting systems, pumps, monitors | Main propulsion engine (sometimes segregated) |
| Navigation & communication electronics | Shaft line, propellers (sometimes segregated) |
| Electrical generators, switchboards, cabling | Design and engineering fees (capital cost) |
| Piping systems (bilge, ballast, fuel, fire) | Yard overheads and profit (sometimes) |
| HVAC, refrigeration, sanitary systems | Launching and dry-docking expenses |
| Interior joinery, insulation, paint, decor | Project financing interest |
| Auxiliary machinery (boilers, pumps, compressors) | Classification society fees (sometimes) |
| Aviation & weapons outfit (naval vessels) | Crew wages during construction |

### 1.4 Standard Cost-Share Benchmarks (Industry Reference Values)

For a typical **handysize bulk carrier (~32,000 DWT)** and a **Panamax bulk carrier (~75,000 DWT)** as reference, the empirical split is approximately:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Hull Steel}} &\approx 25\% \text{ of TNC} \\
C_{\text{Machinery}} &\approx 25\% \text{ of TNC} \\
C_{\text{Outfit}} &\approx 50\% \text{ of TNC}
\end{aligned}$$

For **warships and naval auxiliaries**, outfit costs are even higher due to weapons, electronics, and combat systems, often reaching **60–70% of TNC**.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Currency Convention:** All costs in this module are referenced in **Million US Dollars (M USD)** or as a **percentage of Total Newbuilding Cost (TNC)**.
- **Weight Reference Basis:** Outfit items are conventionally quoted on a **per Light Ship Tonne (LST)** basis, with one LST = **1,000 kg**.
- **Labour Hour Conversion:** 1 man-year ≈ **1,800 productive man-hours** in shipyard accounting.
- **Steel Price Benchmark (KTU Reference Value):** Mild steel hull plate ≈ **800–1,200 USD/tonne** (volatile, indexed to global commodity markets).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Outfit Costs

The outfit cost is a **multi-level hierarchical structure**. At Level 1, it is divided into major systems; at Level 2, each system breaks into sub-systems; at Level 3, into equipment and bulk items.

**Level 1 — Major System Groups (5–7 categories):**

1. **Hull Outfit (HO)** — Deck fittings, ground tackle, mooring, lifesaving, firefighting, paint, insulation, joinery.
2. **Propulsion Machinery (PM)** — Main engine, reduction gearbox, shaft line, propellers.
3. **Auxiliary Machinery (AM)** — Generators, boilers, pumps, compressors, heat exchangers, distillers.
4. **Electrical Outfit (EO)** — Switchboards, cabling, lighting, batteries, automation.
5. **Electronic & Navigation (EN)** — Radar, ECDIS, GMDSS, communication, gyrocompass, automation.
6. **Hull-Borne Engineering Services (HBE)** — Piping, HVAC, fire main, sanitary, freshwater.
7. **Special Outfit (SO)** — Aviation facilities, weapons systems, replenishment-at-sea gear (naval vessels).

### 2.2 The Outfit Cost Estimation Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Outfit Costs Are Estimated Separately:**
> Outfit items are procured from **hundreds of specialised vendors worldwide** (engine makers in Korea/Japan, navigation electronics from Europe, lifesaving from Norway, paint from global chemical majors). Because these items arrive at the yard in fragmented lots over the build period, **top-down lump-sum estimation is unreliable**. KTU examiners therefore expect students to demonstrate a **systematic, bottom-up methodology**.

**The KTU-Standard 5-Stage Estimation Procedure:**

1. **Stage 1 — Quantity Take-Off (QTO):** Generate a bill of materials listing every outfit item with quantity, unit, and specification.
2. **Stage 2 — Unit Rate Enquiry:** Apply vendor catalogue prices or historical yard rates to each line item.
3. **Stage 3 — Aggregation by Sub-System:** Sum line-item costs within each sub-system.
4. **Stage 4 — Add Installation Labour:** Multiply man-hour estimates by yard labour rates, then add to material cost.
5. **Stage 5 — Risk & Contingency Loading:** Apply a contingency factor (typically **5–15%**) for unforeseen items, price escalation, and design changes.

### 2.3 The Core Outfit Cost Formula

The fundamental equation governing outfit cost estimation is:

$$C_{\text{Outfit}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ (Q_i \times U_i) + (H_i \times R_{\text{labour}}) \right] \times (1 + f_c)$$

Where each variable carries the following physical interpretation:

- $Q_i$ = Quantity of outfit item *i* (units, tonnes, metres, as appropriate)
- $U_i$ = Unit material cost of item *i* (USD per unit)
- $H_i$ = Installation man-hours for item *i*
- $R_{\text{labour}}$ = Yard labour rate (USD per man-hour)
- $f_c$ = Contingency factor (dimensionless fraction)
- $n$ = Total number of outfit line items

### 2.4 Cost-Weight Ratio (CWR) Method

For preliminary-stage estimation (when detailed QTO is unavailable), the **Cost-Weight Ratio (CWR)** method is universally applied:

$$C_{\text{Outfit}} = \text{LST}_{\text{outfit}} \times \text{CWR}_{\text{outfit}}$$

Where $\text{LST}_{\text{outfit}}$ is the outfit weight in tonnes, and $\text{CWR}_{\text{outfit}}$ is the historical outfit cost per tonne (USD/tonne).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Relationship** | **Variables Explained** | **Typical Units** | **Application Stage** |
|------|----------------------------|-------------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $C_{\text{TNC}} = C_{\text{Hull}} + C_{\text{Mach}} + C_{\text{Outfit}}$ | Total newbuilding cost split | Million USD | All stages |
| 2 | $C_{\text{Outfit}} = \sum (Q_i U_i + H_i R) (1+f_c)$ | Bottom-up summation | USD | Detailed stage |
| 3 | $C_{\text{Outfit}} = \text{LST} \times \text{CWR}$ | Cost-Weight Ratio method | USD/tonne | Preliminary stage |
| 4 | $\%C_{\text{Outfit}} = \frac{C_{\text{Outfit}}}{C_{\text{TNC}}} \times 100$ | Outfit share of TNC | Percentage | Concept stage |
| 5 | $\text{CWR}_2 = \text{CWR}_1 \times \left(\frac{W_2}{W_1}\right)^{-0.3}$ | CWR scaling with size | Dimensionless exponent | Comparative |
| 6 | $C_{\text{Outfit,eq}} = f(D, V, P, R)$ | Functional unit method | USD | Conceptual design |
| 7 | $t_{\text{build}} = k \cdot \text{LST}^{0.4}$ | Build time scaling | Months | Project planning |
| 8 | $C_{\text{labor}} = H_{\text{total}} \times R_{\text{hour}}$ | Labour cost component | USD | Cost build-up |
| 9 | $C_{\text{escalated}} = C_{\text{base}} \times (1+e)^n$ | Price escalation factor | Dimensionless | Multi-year contracts |
| 10 | $\text{Man-hours/tonne} = a + b/W$ | Specific man-hour curve | h/tonne | Productivity studies |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Formula 5 (the **0.3 power-law scaling**) is a favourite KTU theory question. Memorise the exponent and the negative sign — outfit cost per tonne *decreases* as ship size increases (economy of scale).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyard Bidding:** Outfit cost estimation drives competitive tender pricing in shipbuilding contracts.
- **Loan & Insurance Valuation:** Banks and P&I clubs require outfit cost schedules for mortgage valuation.
- **Repair Cost Engineering:** During ship repair, outfit disassembly/replacement costs are estimated using the same framework.
- **Design Optimisation:** Designers use outfit cost feedback loops to choose between aluminium vs. steel superstructures, or between hydraulic vs. electric deck machinery.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** SOLAS, MARPOL, and Load Line conventions mandate specific outfit items — their costs are non-negotiable baseline.

### 2.7 The "Cost Significance" Concept

Not all outfit items carry equal cost weight. A classic KTU insight is the **Pareto Principle** applied to outfit costs:

- The **top 10 outfit items** typically account for **~70%** of total outfit cost.
- The **remaining hundreds of items** collectively account for **~30%**.

This means a ship estimator must identify and price the **top 10–20 high-value items meticulously**, while applying catalogue rates to the long tail of small items.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Outfit Cost as a Percentage of TNC

**Given:**
- A Panamax bulk carrier has hull steel cost = USD 18 M.
- Machinery cost = USD 22 M.
- Outfit cost = USD 35 M.

**Find:** The outfit cost as a percentage of total newbuilding cost (TNC).

**Step 1 — Compute the total newbuilding cost** by summing all three major cost blocks:

$$C_{\text{TNC}} = C_{\text{Hull}} + C_{\text{Machinery}} + C_{\text{Outfit}}$$

$$C_{\text{TNC}} = 18 + 22 + 35$$

$$C_{\text{TNC}} = 75 \text{ M USD}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the outfit share** using the percentage equation from the formula sheet:

$$\%C_{\text{Outfit}} = \frac{C_{\text{Outfit}}}{C_{\text{TNC}}} \times 100$$

$$\%C_{\text{Outfit}} = \frac{35}{75} \times 100$$

$$\%C_{\text{Outfit}} = 46.67\%$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** For this Panamax bulker, outfit accounts for nearly half (46.67%) of the total newbuilding price — confirming the KTU benchmark of 50%.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost-Weight Ratio (CWR) Method for a Tanker

**Given:**
- A chemical tanker of **12,000 DWT** has a light ship weight (LSW) = **5,800 tonnes**.
- The outfit weight fraction = **22%** of LSW.
- Historical outfit CWR for similar tankers = **USD 22,000 per tonne** (year 2023).

**Find:** The outfit cost in USD.

**Step 1 — Compute the outfit weight** as 22% of the light ship weight:

$$\text{LST}_{\text{outfit}} = 0.22 \times \text{LSW}$$

$$\text{LST}_{\text{outfit}} = 0.22 \times 5{,}800$$

$$\text{LST}_{\text{outfit}} = 1{,}276 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CWR formula** from the cheat sheet:

$$C_{\text{Outfit}} = \text{LST}_{\text{outfit}} \times \text{CWR}_{\text{outfit}}$$

$$C_{\text{Outfit}} = 1{,}276 \times 22{,}000$$

$$C_{\text{Outfit}} = 28{,}072{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 3 — Express in million USD:**

$$C_{\text{Outfit}} \approx 28.07 \text{ M USD}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cost Escalation Over a Multi-Year Contract

**Given:**
- Contract signed in Year 0 at outfit cost = USD 25 M.
- Estimated annual escalation rate $e = 4.5\%$ per year.
- Build duration = 3 years (mid-point delivery at end of Year 2).

**Find:** The escalated outfit cost at delivery.

**Step 1 — Identify the escalation equation** from the formula sheet:

$$C_{\text{escalated}} = C_{\text{base}} \times (1+e)^n$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values** with $n = 2$ (escalation compounds over 2 years from signing to delivery):

$$C_{\text{escalated}} = 25 \times (1 + 0.045)^2$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the compound factor:**

$$(1.045)^2 = 1.092025$$

**Step 4 — Compute the escalated cost:**

$$C_{\text{escalated}} = 25 \times 1.092025$$

$$C_{\text{escalated}} = 27.30 \text{ M USD}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The yard/owner must budget an **additional USD 2.30 M** beyond the base contract figure to absorb 2 years of inflation on outfit items.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Comparative Estimation Using the Power-Law Scaling

**Given:**
- Reference ship A: $\text{LST}_1 = 8{,}000$ tonnes, $C_{\text{Outfit,1}} = $ USD 120 M.
- New ship B: $\text{LST}_2 = 20{,}000$ tonnes.
- Scaling exponent $x = -0.3$ (outfit CWR decreases with size).

**Find:** Outfit cost of Ship B using the power-law scaling formula.

**Step 1 — Compute the CWR of Ship A:**

$$\text{CWR}_1 = \frac{C_{\text{Outfit,1}}}{\text{LST}_1} = \frac{120 \times 10^6}{8{,}000} = 15{,}000 \text{ USD/tonne}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the power-law scaling to obtain CWR of Ship B:**

$$\text{CWR}_2 = \text{CWR}_1 \times \left(\frac{\text{LST}_2}{\text{LST}_1}\right)^{-0.3}$$

$$\text{CWR}_2 = 15{,}000 \times \left(\frac{20{,}000}{8{,}000}\right)^{-0.3}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the size ratio:**

$$\frac{20{,}000}{8{,}000} = 2.5$$

**Step 4 — Apply the negative exponent:**

$$(2.5)^{-0.3} = \frac{1}{(2.5)^{0.3}}$$

$$(2.5)^{0.3} = e^{0.3 \times \ln(2.5)} = e^{0.3 \times 0.9163} = e^{0.2749} = 1.3166$$

$$\therefore (2.5)^{-0.3} = \frac{1}{1.3166} = 0.7595$$

**Step 5 — Compute CWR for Ship B:**

$$\text{CWR}_2 = 15{,}000 \times 0.7595 = 11{,}393 \text{ USD/tonne}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the outfit cost of Ship B:**

$$C_{\text{Outfit,2}} = \text{CWR}_2 \times \text{LST}_2$$

$$C_{\text{Outfit,2}} = 11{,}393 \times 20{,}000$$

$$C_{\text{Outfit,2}} = 227.86 \text{ M USD}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** Although Ship B is 2.5× heavier, its outfit cost is only 1.9× that of Ship A — confirming **economy of scale** in outfit cost per tonne.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Detailed QTO-Based Outfit Estimation for Hull Outfit

**Given:** A small general cargo vessel requires the following hull outfit items:

| **Item** | **Quantity** | **Unit Material Cost (USD)** | **Man-Hours per Unit** |
|----------|-------------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| Anchors (stockless) | 3 | 12,000 | 40 |
| Anchor chain (grade U2, 52 mm dia) | 550 m | 85/m | 3 h/m |
| Mooring winches | 2 | 95,000 | 280 |
| Lifeboats (30-person, enclosed) | 2 | 145,000 | 150 |
| Liferafts (20-person) | 4 | 8,500 | 25 |
| Fire pumps (centrifugal) | 2 | 18,000 | 60 |
| Paint (anti-fouling + primer) | 18,000 L | 22/L | 0.05 h/L |

Labour rate = USD 35/hour. Contingency factor = 10%.

**Find:** Total hull outfit cost.

**Step 1 — Compute material cost per line item** using $M_i = Q_i \times U_i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{anchors}} &= 3 \times 12{,}000 = 36{,}000 \\
M_{\text{chain}} &= 550 \times 85 = 46{,}750 \\
M_{\text{winches}} &= 2 \times 95{,}000 = 190{,}000 \\
M_{\text{lifeboats}} &= 2 \times 145{,}000 = 290{,}000 \\
M_{\text{liferafts}} &= 4 \times 8{,}500 = 34{,}000 \\
M_{\text{fire pumps}} &= 2 \times 18{,}000 = 36{,}000 \\
M_{\text{paint}} &= 18{,}000 \times 22 = 396{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Sum all material costs:**

$$\sum M_i = 36{,}000 + 46{,}750 + 190{,}000 + 290{,}000 + 34{,}000 + 36{,}000 + 396{,}000$$

$$\sum M_i = 1{,}028{,}750 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total installation man-hours** using $H_i = Q_i \times h_i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{anchors}} &= 3 \times 40 = 120 \\
H_{\text{chain}} &= 550 \times 3 = 1{,}650 \\
H_{\text{winches}} &= 2 \times 280 = 560 \\
H_{\text{lifeboats}} &= 2 \times 150 = 300 \\
H_{\text{liferafts}} &= 4 \times 25 = 100 \\
H_{\text{fire pumps}} &= 2 \times 60 = 120 \\
H_{\text{paint}} &= 18{,}000 \times 0.05 = 900
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Sum all man-hours:**

$$\sum H_i = 120 + 1{,}650 + 560 + 300 + 100 + 120 + 900 = 3{,}750 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Compute total labour cost:**

$$C_{\text{labor}} = \sum H_i \times R = 3{,}750 \times 35 = 131{,}250 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the subtotal (material + labour):**

$$C_{\text{sub}} = \sum M_i + C_{\text{labor}} = 1{,}028{,}750 + 131{,}250 = 1{,}160{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 7 — Apply contingency factor:**

$$C_{\text{Outfit,HO}} = C_{\text{sub}} \times (1 + f_c) = 1{,}160{,}000 \times 1.10 = 1{,}276{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation:** The hull outfit alone for this small general cargo ship costs **USD 1.276 million**, with material cost (88.6%) dominating labour cost (11.4%) — a typical ratio for outfit work.

---

### 3.6 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Estimation Method

| **Estimation Method** | **Data Required** | **Accuracy** | **Project Stage** | **Time Required** | **Cost to Perform** |
|------------------------|--------------------|---------------|--------------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Percentage of TNC** | Only similar-ship TNC reference | ±30% | Concept / feasibility | 1 hour | Negligible |
| **CWR Method** | Light ship weight + CWR data | ±20% | Preliminary design | 1 day | Low |
| **Functional Unit** | Vessel function (DWT, pax, TEU) | ±15–20% | Contract design | 3–5 days | Low |
| **Comparative (Power-Law)** | Reference ship + scaling law | ±15% | Tender preparation | 2–3 days | Low-medium |
| **Detailed QTO (Bottom-Up)** | Full specification + vendor quotes | ±5–8% | Production stage | 2–4 weeks | High |
| **Vendor Quotation-Based** | Actual RFQ responses | ±2–5% | Post-contract execution | 1–3 months | Very high |
