## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**"Define outfit costs in shipbuilding. List any four categories of outfit items with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Outfit costs refer to the aggregate expenditure on all items fitted to the vessel after the basic hull structure is erected, encompassing everything required to make the hull a fully operational and habitable ship, excluding only raw steel and primary structural fabrication.

> **Four Categories with Examples (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
>
> 1. **Hull Outfit:** Deck fittings — e.g., mooring winches, bollards, fairleads.
> 2. **Electrical Outfit:** Main switchboards, generators, cabling, lighting.
> 3. **Electronic & Navigation:** Radar, ECDIS, GMDSS communication suite.
> 4. **Auxiliary Machinery:** Pumps, boilers, compressors, heat exchangers, distillers.

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**"State the KTU-standard formula relating total newbuilding cost to its three primary cost blocks, and explain why outfit is often the largest single block."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Formula (1 mark):**
>
> $$C_{\text{TNC}} = C_{\text{Hull Steel}} + C_{\text{Machinery}} + C_{\text{Outfit}}$$

> **Explanation (2 marks):** Outfit is the largest block because it aggregates hundreds of vendor-supplied specialised systems (propulsion, electrical, electronics, piping, lifesaving, firefighting, paint, insulation, joinery) sourced globally. Unlike hull steel which is largely commodity-priced, outfit contains substantial engineering content, regulatory compliance costs (SOLAS, MARPOL, CLASS rules), and a large labour component for installation and commissioning — collectively driving the share to **45–60% of TNC** for merchant vessels and **60–70%** for warships.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A.1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*"With the aid of a clear hierarchical block diagram, classify and describe the major categories of outfit costs in a modern merchant vessel. Indicate the approximate percentage share of each category in the total outfit cost."*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*"A Handysize bulk carrier has the following outfit line items. Compute the total outfit cost using the bottom-up summation method, including a 10% contingency.*
*Labour rate = USD 30/hour.*

| **Item** | **Qty** | **Unit Cost (USD)** | **Man-Hours per Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mooring winches | 2 | 80,000 | 250 |
| Anchor chain (Grade U2) | 400 m | 75/m | 2.5 h/m |
| Lifeboats (25-person) | 2 | 130,000 | 140 |
| Liferafts | 4 | 7,500 | 20 |
| Bilge pumps | 3 | 6,500 | 45 |
| Navigation radar | 2 | 55,000 | 100 |
| Anti-fouling paint | 12,000 L | 20/L | 0.04 h/L |"

---

### **Model Answer for Question B-A.1**

#### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**Hierarchical Classification (5 marks):**

Outfit cost is decomposed into the following major categories:

1. **Hull Outfit (HO) — ~25–30% of outfit cost:**
   Includes deck fittings (winches, bollards, fairleads, capstans), ground tackle (anchors, chains, cables), lifesaving appliances (lifeboats, liferafts, lifejackets, MES), firefighting systems (pumps, monitors, extinguishers), insulation, joinery, and paint systems.

2. **Electrical Outfit (EO) — ~8–12% of outfit cost:**
   Includes main and emergency generators, main switchboard, distribution boards, cabling, lighting, batteries, and alarm/monitoring systems.

3. **Electronic & Navigation (EN) — ~10–15% of outfit cost:**
   Includes radar (X-band and S-band), ECDIS, GMDSS communication suite, gyrocompass, autopilot, GPS, AIS, echo sounder, speed log, and integrated bridge system.

4. **Hull-Borne Engineering Services (HBE) — ~15–20% of outfit cost:**
   Includes all piping systems (bilge, ballast, fuel, lube oil, fire main, seawater, freshwater, sanitary), HVAC, refrigeration, and freshwater generation.

5. **Auxiliary Machinery (AM) — ~15–20% of outfit cost:**
   Includes auxiliary boilers, air compressors, fuel oil purifiers, fresh water generators, sewage treatment plants, incinerators, and steering gear.

6. **Propulsion Machinery (PM) — ~10–15% of outfit cost (when included within outfit):**
   Includes main engine, reduction gearbox, shaft line, propellers, and main thrust bearing.

7. **Special Outfit (SO) — variable, naval only:**
   Aviation facilities (helideck, hangar, refuelling), weapons systems, RAS gear.

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 hierarchical decomposition flowchart.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Material Cost Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{winches}} &= 2 \times 80{,}000 = 160{,}000 \\
M_{\text{chain}} &= 400 \times 75 = 30{,}000 \\
M_{\text{lifeboats}} &= 2 \times 130{,}000 = 260{,}000 \\
M_{\text{liferafts}} &= 4 \times 7{,}500 = 30{,}000 \\
M_{\text{bilge pumps}} &= 3 \times 6{,}500 = 19{,}500 \\
M_{\text{radar}} &= 2 \times 55{,}000 = 110{,}000 \\
M_{\text{paint}} &= 12{,}000 \times 20 = 240{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Sum of material costs:**

$$\sum M_i = 160{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 260{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 19{,}500 + 110{,}000 + 240{,}000$$

$$\sum M_i = 849{,}500 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 2 — Man-Hour Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{winches}} &= 2 \times 250 = 500 \\
H_{\text{chain}} &= 400 \times 2.5 = 1{,}000 \\
H_{\text{lifeboats}} &= 2 \times 140 = 280 \\
H_{\text{liferafts}} &= 4 \times 20 = 80 \\
H_{\text{bilge pumps}} &= 3 \times 45 = 135 \\
H_{\text{radar}} &= 2 \times 100 = 200 \\
H_{\text{paint}} &= 12{,}000 \times 0.04 = 480
\end{aligned}$$

**Sum of man-hours:**

$$\sum H_i = 500 + 1{,}000 + 280 + 80 + 135 + 200 + 480 = 2{,}675 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Labour Cost (1 mark):**

$$C_{\text{labor}} = 2{,}675 \times 30 = 80{,}250 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 4 — Subtotal Before Contingency (1 mark):**

$$C_{\text{sub}} = 849{,}500 + 80{,}250 = 929{,}750 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 5 — Apply 10% Contingency (1 mark):**

$$C_{\text{Outfit,total}} = 929{,}750 \times 1.10 = 1{,}022{,}725 \text{ USD}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{Outfit,total}} \approx \text{USD } 1.023 \text{ Million}}$$

---

### **Question B-B.1 (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*"Explain the Cost-Weight Ratio (CWR) method for preliminary outfit cost estimation. A product tanker has a light ship weight of 9,500 tonnes. The outfit weight is 24% of LSW, and the outfit CWR for similar tankers is USD 18,500/tonne (2024 basis). Compute the total outfit cost."*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*"Discuss the impact of ship size on outfit CWR using the power-law relationship CWR2 = CWR1 × (LST2/LST1)^(−0.3). A reference vessel of 6,000 LST has outfit CWR of USD 17,000/tonne. Estimate the outfit cost for a new vessel of 25,000 LST, assuming the new vessel's outfit weight is 23% of LST."*

---

### **Model Answer for Question B-B.1**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Explanation of CWR Method (3 marks):**

The Cost-Weight Ratio (CWR) method is a preliminary-stage estimation technique used in ship cost engineering when detailed quantity take-offs are not yet available. It is based on the empirical observation that the **outfit cost per tonne of outfit weight** is relatively stable across similar ship types and sizes. The estimator multiplies the estimated outfit weight by a historically benchmarked CWR to obtain a first-order cost estimate.

**Advantages:** Fast, low data requirement, suitable for feasibility and concept design stages.
**Disadvantages:** Accuracy limited to ±20%; cannot capture design-specific variations, vendor-specific pricing, or regulatory-driven changes (e.g., new SOLAS requirements).

**Key formula:**

$$C_{\text{Outfit}} = \text{LST}_{\text{outfit}} \times \text{CWR}_{\text{outfit}}$$

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute outfit weight:**

$$\text{LST}_{\text{outfit}} = 0.24 \times 9{,}500 = 2{,}280 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CWR formula:**

$$C_{\text{Outfit}} = 2{,}280 \times 18{,}500$$

$$C_{\text{Outfit}} = 42{,}180{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 3 — Express in million USD:**

$$\boxed{C_{\text{Outfit}} = \text{USD } 42.18 \text{ Million}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Theoretical Discussion (3 marks):**

The power-law relationship $C_{\text{CWR,2}} = C_{\text{CWR,1}} \times \left(\frac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^{-0.3}$ captures the **economy of scale** in outfit costing. As ship size (LST) increases, the outfit cost per tonne **decreases** because:

- Fixed-quantity items (e.g., 1 radar, 1 switchboard, 1 steering gear) are spread over a larger platform.
- Bulk procurement (paint, piping, cabling) achieves volume discounts.
- Installation labour exhibits learning-curve benefits across repeated units (e.g., 8 identical pumps cost less per unit than 1).

The exponent **−0.3** is empirically derived from regression analysis of historical shipbuilding data; it is specific to outfit costs (different from hull steel, which uses a different exponent).

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the size ratio:**

$$\frac{L_2}{L_1} = \frac{25{,}000}{6{,}000} = 4.1667$$

**Step 2 — Apply the power-law scaling:**

$$(4.1667)^{-0.3} = \frac{1}{(4.1667)^{0.3}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponent:**

$$(4.1667)^{0.3} = e^{0.3 \times \ln(4.1667)} = e^{0.3 \times 1.4271} = e^{0.4281} = 1.5345$$

$$\therefore (4.1667)^{-0.3} = \frac{1}{1.5345} = 0.6517$$

**Step 4 — Compute the new CWR:**

$$\text{CWR}_2 = 17{,}000 \times 0.6517 = 11{,}079 \text{ USD/tonne}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new outfit weight:**

$$\text{LST}_{\text{outfit,2}} = 0.23 \times 25{,}000 = 5{,}750 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the outfit cost for the new vessel:**

$$C_{\text{Outfit,2}} = 5{,}750 \times 11{,}079$$

$$C_{\text{Outfit,2}} = 63{,}704{,}250 \text{ USD}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{Outfit,2}} \approx \text{USD } 63.70 \text{ Million}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the contingency factor:** Many students omit the (1 + $f_c$) term in the summation formula. KTU examiners allocate **1 dedicated mark** for this — losing it can drop your grade by one full letter.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing up tonnes and kg, or USD and million USD without clear conversion, is a **2-mark penalty** in numerical problems. Always declare units at every step.
> 3. **Confusing CWR with cost per DWT:** CWR is **cost per tonne of outfit weight** (not per DWT of ship). This is a common conceptual error worth **2–3 marks** in theory questions.
> 4. **Ignoring the negative sign in the power law:** Students often write $(L_2/L_1)^{0.3}$ instead of $(L_2/L_1)^{-0.3}$. The negative exponent is **the whole point** of the formula (economy of scale). This error yields a wrong answer that is **physically unrealistic** (predicts outfit cost per tonne *increases* with ship size).
> 5. **Omitting escalation in multi-year contracts:** For builds spanning 2–3 years, **always apply the escalation formula** $C_{\text{base}}(1+e)^n$. KTU frequently tests this in 14-mark problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Outfit Costs**

- ✅ **Outfit cost ≈ 50% of TNC** for merchant ships; up to **70%** for warships.
- ✅ **Three-block model:** $C_{\text{TNC}} = C_{\text{Hull}} + C_{\text{Machinery}} + C_{\text{Outfit}}$.
- ✅ **Outfit includes:** everything fitted after hull erection — hull outfit, electrical, electronic/navigation, piping/HVAC, auxiliary machinery, paint, insulation, joinery, lifesaving, firefighting.
- ✅ **Outfit excludes:** hull steel, primary structural fabrication, design fees, financing, profit.
- ✅ **Bottom-up formula:** $C_{\text{Outfit}} = \sum (Q_i U_i + H_i R)(1+f_c)$.
- ✅ **CWR formula:** $C_{\text{Outfit}} = \text{LST}_{\text{outfit}} \times \text{CWR}$.
- ✅ **Power-law scaling:** $\text{CWR}_2 = \text{CWR}_1 \times (L_2/L_1)^{-0.3}$ — economy of scale with ship size.
- ✅ **Escalation formula:** $C_{\text{escalated}} = C_{\text{base}} \times (1+e)^n$ — applies to multi-year contracts.
- ✅ **Typical contingency:** $f_c = 5\%\text{–}15\%$ on subtotal.
- ✅ **Typical labour content:** 1,800 man-hours = 1 man-year.
- ✅ **Pareto rule:** Top 10 items = ~70% of outfit cost.
- ✅ **Outfit weight fraction:** Typically **20–28%** of Light Ship Weight.
- ✅ **Estimation method selection:** Concept stage → Percentage or Functional; Preliminary → CWR or Power-law; Detailed → QTO with vendor quotes.
- ✅ **Reference vessel CWR (2024 basis):** USD **15,000–25,000/tonne** for merchant outfit.
- ✅ **Always convert units explicitly** — tonnes vs kg, USD vs M USD — in every numerical step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Labour Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Labour Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Labour Cost** in the context of ship repair, survey, and estimation refers to the **total monetary expenditure incurred by a shipyard, dry-dock facility, or marine workshop for the human workforce engaged in executing repair, maintenance, survey, and conversion works on a vessel.** It is a primary component of the overall repair cost and is computed as the product of the effective man-hours expended and the applicable labour rate (man-hour rate).

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology aligned with ship repair estimation practice, **Labour Cost** encompasses:

- **Direct Labour Cost** – Wages paid to workers physically engaged in the repair task (fitters, welders, plumbers, machinists, electricians, painters, riggers, etc.).
- **Indirect Labour Cost** – Salaries and wages of supervisory, administrative, planning, quality control, and storekeeping personnel supporting the direct workforce.
- **Payroll Burden / Social Charges** – Statutory and non-statutory fringe benefits such as provident fund, ESI (Employee State Insurance), gratuity, bonus, leave with wages, holiday pay, medical allowance, and insurance contributions.
- **Productivity-Adjusted Cost** – Effective cost after accounting for efficiency factors, idle time, learning curves, and overtime premiums.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of Labour Cost as the "human fuel bill" of a shipyard. Just as a car's running cost depends on (fuel price × distance) + (engineer's wages + driver's wages), a shipyard's labour bill depends on **(number of workers × hours worked × hourly rate × productivity factor)**. A ship being repaired is like a patient in a hospital — the surgeon (skilled worker) charges more per hour than a nurse's aide (helper), and a complex heart operation (e.g., main engine overhaul) takes far more skilled hours than a routine check-up (e.g., hull cleaning).

Imagine a pipe renewal job in the engine room:

- 1 fitter (₹200/hr) works for 8 hours → ₹1,600
- 1 helper (₹100/hr) works for 8 hours → ₹800
- Add 30% fringe benefits → ₹720
- **Total Labour Cost = ₹3,120** (before adding materials and overheads)

This is the essence of labour costing — quantifying *human effort* in monetary terms.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard man-hour per ton** (for ship repair estimation benchmarks): typically **80–120 man-hours/ton** of steel for general repairs.
- **Standard working hours**: **8 hours/day, 48 hours/week** as per Indian Factories Act.
- **Overtime premium rates**:
  - **1.5 ×** normal rate for work beyond 8 hours/day or 48 hours/week.
  - **2.0 ×** normal rate for work on weekly rest days and gazetted holidays.
- **Productivity factor** for ship repair: typically **0.6 – 0.85** (effective output vs. theoretical output).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 4):** Students must be able to (a) compute direct and indirect labour costs, (b) derive the effective man-hour rate, (c) apply fringe benefit multipliers, and (d) estimate labour costs for representative ship repair jobs such as plate renewal, pipe renewals, machinery overhauls, and survey-related works.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breakdown of Labour Cost Components

The total labour cost in ship repair can be decomposed into the following hierarchical structure:

1. **Gross Wage (Direct)**
   - Basic pay
   - Dearness allowance (DA)
   - House rent allowance (HRA)
   - Skill-based / trade-based increment

2. **Fringe Benefits / Payroll Burden**
   - Provident Fund (PF): 12% of basic + DA (employer contribution)
   - ESI: 3.25% of gross salary
   - Bonus & ex-gratia
   - Gratuity provision: ~4.16% of basic + DA
   - Leave with wages, holiday pay
   - Medical, canteen, transport subsidies

3. **Idle Time / Non-productive Hours**
   - Tool collection, drawing study, tea breaks, waiting for materials/cranes
   - Typically absorbed as a percentage add-on: **10–20%**

4. **Supervision & Indirect Labour**
   - Foremen, supervisors, QC inspectors, planners
   - Typically absorbed as: **15–25% of direct labour**

5. **Overheads (General)**
   - Yard administration, planning, accounts, HR
   - Typically absorbed as: **50–100% of direct labour**

### 2.2 Why Each Component Matters (The "Why" Behind the Math)

- **Why basic wage + DA?** — DA neutralises inflation; both are part of statutory wages.
- **Why 4.16% gratuity?** — It equals 15 days' wages per year of service = 15/365 = 4.11% ≈ 4.16% (rounded).
- **Why productivity factor?** — A worker on a ship rarely achieves 100% efficiency due to confined spaces, weather, waiting for crane support, etc.
- **Why supervision %?** — Without supervisors, no work gets coordinated; their cost is "shared" across all jobs.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula a student needs for KTU 2024 board-level questions on Labour Costs. Memorise these — they are repeatedly tested.

| **#** | **Formula Description** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Direct Labour Cost | $C_D = n \times h \times r$ | n = no. of workers, h = hours, r = hourly rate (₹/hr) |
| 2 | Effective Man-Hour Rate (with fringe) | $r_{eff} = r \times (1 + f_b)$ | $f_b$ = fringe benefit fraction (e.g., 0.30 for 30%) |
| 3 | Total Labour Cost (with fringe) | $C_L = C_D \times (1 + f_b)$ | ₹ |
| 4 | Overtime Premium (daily OT) | $C_{OT} = n \times h_{OT} \times r \times 1.5$ | For >8 hrs/day |
| 5 | Overtime Premium (weekly rest) | $C_{OT} = n \times h_{OT} \times r \times 2.0$ | For Sundays / holidays |
| 6 | Productivity-Adjusted Hours | $h_{eff} = \dfrac{h_{theoretical}}{\eta}$ | $\eta$ = productivity factor (0.6 – 0.85) |
| 7 | Cost with Productivity Factor | $C_P = n \times h_{eff} \times r_{eff}$ | ₹ |
| 8 | Total Job Cost (with overhead) | $C_{job} = C_L \times (1 + s) \times (1 + o)$ | s = supervision %, o = overhead % |
| 9 | Man-Hours per Ton (benchmark) | $M = 80 - 120 \; \text{man-hr/ton}$ | Steel renewal benchmark |
| 10 | Gratuity Provision | $G = 0.0416 \times (B + DA)$ | B = basic pay |
| 11 | Composite Labour Rate | $r_c = r \times (1 + f_b) \times (1 + s) \times (1 + o)$ | All-in rate per hour |
| 12 | Idle Time Factor | $C_{idle} = C_D \times f_i$ | $f_i$ = idle fraction (0.10 – 0.20) |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Quotation preparation:** Shipyards quote repair tenders using all-in labour rates.
- **Budget forecasting:** Shipowners budget dry-dock costs by estimating man-hours × rate.
- **Productivity benchmarking:** Comparing actual vs. standard man-hours identifies inefficient processes.
- **Contract negotiation:** Knowing the cost stack enables fair labour-only contracts.
- **Insurance claims:** Labour costs form a major head in hull & machinery insurance claim assessments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Composite Labour Rate

The **composite (all-in) labour rate** is the single figure a shipyard uses internally for cost control. It bundles basic wages, statutory fringe benefits, supervision, and yard overhead into one hourly figure.

**Step 1 — Start with the basic hourly rate (r):**
Let $r$ be the contractual basic hourly wage of a worker in ₹/hr.

**Step 2 — Add fringe benefit multiplier:**
Fringe benefits are usually expressed as a fraction $f_b$ of basic wage.

$$
r_1 = r \times (1 + f_b)
$$

**Step 3 — Add idle time multiplier:**
Idle time is absorbed as a fraction $f_i$ of productive cost.

$$
r_2 = r_1 \times (1 + f_i) = r \times (1 + f_b) \times (1 + f_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Add supervision multiplier:**
Supervision cost is a fraction $s$ of direct (productive) labour cost.

$$
r_3 = r_2 \times (1 + s) = r \times (1 + f_b) \times (1 + f_i) \times (1 + s)
$$

**Step 5 — Add general overhead multiplier:**
General yard overhead (administration, planning, stores, HR) is a fraction $o$ of total direct cost so far.

$$
r_c = r \times (1 + f_b) \times (1 + f_i) \times (1 + s) \times (1 + o)
$$

This is the **final composite labour rate** that fully loads basic wage with every indirect cost component.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Plate Renewal Labour Cost Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Estimate the total labour cost for renewing **20 tonnes** of hull plating in a dry dock. Given data:
> - Standard man-hours per ton = **100 man-hr/ton**
> - Crew composition per team: 4 fitters + 2 welders + 2 helpers
> - Fitter rate = ₹250/hr, Welder rate = ₹300/hr, Helper rate = ₹120/hr
> - Fringe benefits = 30% of basic
> - Supervision = 20% of direct cost
> - Yard overhead = 60% of direct cost
> - Productivity factor = 0.75 (i.e., only 75% efficient)

**Step 1 — Compute total standard man-hours required:**

$$
M_{std} = 20 \; \text{tons} \times 100 \; \frac{\text{man-hr}}{\text{ton}} = 2000 \; \text{man-hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Adjust for productivity:**

$$
M_{eff} = \frac{M_{std}}{\eta} = \frac{2000}{0.75} = 2666.67 \; \text{man-hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Weighted average hourly rate of one team (8 workers):**
One team consists of 4 fitters, 2 welders, 2 helpers = 8 workers.

$$
r_{team} = 4(250) + 2(300) + 2(120) = 1000 + 600 + 240 = 1840 \; \text{₹/hr per team}
$$

Average per worker:

$$
r_{avg} = \frac{1840}{8} = 230 \; \text{₹/hr per worker}
$$

**Step 4 — Direct labour cost (basic):**

$$
C_D = 2666.67 \times 230 = 613{,}333.33 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply fringe benefit multiplier (30%):**

$$
C_{FB} = 613{,}333.33 \times 1.30 = 797{,}333.33 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply supervision multiplier (20%):**

$$
C_{S} = 797{,}333.33 \times 1.20 = 956{,}800.00 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply yard overhead multiplier (60%):**

$$
C_{total} = 956{,}800.00 \times 1.60 = 1{,}530{,}880.00 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{C_{total} \approx ₹ \; 15.31 \; \text{Lakhs for 20-ton hull plate renewal labour}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cost Build-Up Summary

| **Cost Head** | **Multiplier** | **Cost (₹)** | **% of Total** |
|:---|:---:|---:|---:|
| Direct labour (basic) | 1.000 | 6,13,333 | 40.1% |
| Fringe benefits (30%) | +30% | 1,84,000 | 12.0% |
| Subtotal | 1.30 | 7,97,333 | 52.1% |
| Supervision (20%) | +20% | 1,59,467 | 10.4% |
| Subtotal | 1.56 | 9,56,800 | 62.5% |
| Yard overhead (60%) | +60% | 5,74,080 | 37.5% |
| **Grand Total** | **2.496** | **15,30,880** | **100.0%** |

### 3.4 Overtime Premium Calculation — Derivative Case

If the same job is required in **20 days** instead of an unconstrained schedule, and the team works **2 hours overtime per day**, then:

- Total days = 20
- Daily OT per worker = 2 hours
- Total OT hours per worker = $20 \times 2 = 40$ hours
- Total OT cost for 8 workers:

$$
C_{OT} = 8 \times 40 \times 230 \times 1.5 = ₹ \; 1{,}10{,}400
$$

This OT cost is **added on top** of the regular labour cost — it is not absorbed in the multipliers.

### 3.5 Sensitivity Insight

If the productivity factor drops from 0.75 to 0.60 (very inefficient conditions):

$$
M_{eff} = \frac{2000}{0.60} = 3333.33 \; \text{man-hours}
$$

Cost increase = $\dfrac{3333.33 - 2666.67}{2666.67} \times 100 = 25\%$ rise in total labour cost — a strong argument for improving site productivity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Labour Cost

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TOTAL LABOUR COST] --> B[DIRECT LABOUR COST]
    A --> C[INDIRECT LABOUR COST]
    A --> D[OVERHEAD COST]
    B --> B1[Basic Wage]
    B --> B2[Dearness Allowance]
    B --> B3[Skill Premium]
    C --> C1[Supervision Cost]
    C --> C2[QC Inspection]
    C --> C3[Planning and Stores]
    C --> C4[Idle Time]
    D --> D1[Yard Administration]
    D --> D2[Utilities and Rent]
    D --> D3[Insurance and Compliance]
```

### 4.2 Cost Build-Up Flow (Bottom-Up Multiplier Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Basic Hourly Wage R] --> P2[Apply Fringe Benefit Multiplier]
    P2 --> P3[Apply Idle Time Multiplier]
    P3 --> P4[Apply Supervision Multiplier]
    P4 --> P5[Apply Yard Overhead Multiplier]
    P5 --> P6[Composite Labour Rate RC]
    P6 --> P7[Multiply by Effective Man Hours]
    P7 --> P8[Final Loaded Labour Cost]
```

### 4.3 Man-Hour Estimation Workflow in Ship Repair

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q1[Identify Repair Scope] --> Q2[Refer Standard Man Hour Tables]
    Q2 --> Q3[Compute Standard Hours]
    Q3 --> Q4[Apply Productivity Factor]
    Q4 --> Q5[Effective Man Hours]
    Q5 --> Q6[Multiply by Composite Rate]
    Q6 --> Q7[Add Overtime Premium]
    Q7 --> Q8[Final Labour Cost]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Cost Stack Visualisation

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DIRECT[DIRECT LABOUR]
        D1[FITTERS]
        D2[WELDERS]
        D3[HELPERS]
        D4[MACHINISTS]
    end
    subgraph INDIRECT[INDIRECT LABOUR]
        I1[FOREMEN]
        I2[QC INSPECTORS]
        I3[PLANNERS]
    end
    subgraph FRINGE[FRINGE BENEFITS]
        F1[PF AND ESI]
        F2[GRATUITY]
        F3[LEAVE PAY]
    end
    subgraph OVERHEAD[YARD OVERHEAD]
        O1[ADMINISTRATION]
        O2[UTILITIES]
        O3[INSURANCE]
    end
    DIRECT --> TOTAL[TOTAL LABOUR COST]
    INDIRECT --> TOTAL
    FRINGE --> TOTAL
    OVERHEAD --> TOTAL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Labour Cost" as applied to ship repair estimation. List any four components that constitute labour cost in a shipyard.**

**Model Answer:**
Labour cost is the total monetary expenditure incurred by a shipyard for the human workforce engaged in executing repair, maintenance, survey, and conversion works on a vessel. It is the product of effective man-hours expended and the applicable labour rate.

**Four components:**
1. Direct labour wages (fitters, welders, helpers)
2. Indirect labour wages (supervisors, QC, planners)
3. Fringe benefits / payroll burden (PF, ESI, gratuity, bonus)
4. Yard overhead absorption (administration, utilities, insurance)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a "composite labour rate"? Why is it preferred over the basic hourly rate for ship repair estimation?**

**Model Answer:**
The composite labour rate is the all-in hourly rate obtained by loading the basic wage with fringe benefits, idle time, supervision, and yard overhead multipliers. It is preferred because:

- It represents the **true cost** of one man-hour to the yard, not just the worker's take-home.
- It enables **single-rate quotation** in tenders, simplifying cost estimation.
- It ensures that **indirect costs are recovered** — without it, the yard would run at a loss despite collecting worker wages.
- It facilitates **uniform comparison** between in-house and subcontracted labour.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q-A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat sketch or block diagram, explain the various components that make up the total labour cost in a ship repair organisation.

**(b)** A shipyard quotes a basic wage rate of **₹200/hour** for a fitter. If fringe benefits are **35%**, idle time is **15%**, supervision cost is **25%**, and yard overhead is **70%**, calculate:

  (i) The composite (all-in) labour rate per hour.
  (ii) The total labour cost for a job requiring **1,200 effective man-hours** at this rate.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Block Diagram of Labour Cost Components (7 marks)**

*(Draw the block diagram from Section 4.4 above. Components must include direct labour, indirect labour, fringe benefits, and overhead.)*

Detailed textual explanation:

- **Direct Labour** — fitters, welders, helpers, machinists, painters, electricians, riggers — physically perform the repair task.
- **Indirect Labour** — foremen, supervisors, QC inspectors, planners, storekeepers — coordinate and support the work.
- **Fringe Benefits** — PF (12%), ESI (3.25%), gratuity (4.16%), bonus, leave with wages — statutory and non-statutory add-ons.
- **Yard Overhead** — administration, HR, accounts, utilities (power, water, gas), insurance, depreciation of plant.

These are stacked and added progressively to obtain the total loaded labour cost.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Composite Labour Rate:**

Using the formula:

$$
r_c = r \times (1 + f_b) \times (1 + f_i) \times (1 + s) \times (1 + o)
$$

Substituting $r = 200$, $f_b = 0.35$, $f_i = 0.15$, $s = 0.25$, $o = 0.70$:

$$
r_c = 200 \times 1.35 \times 1.15 \times 1.25 \times 1.70
$$

Computing step by step:

$$
r_c = 200 \times 1.35 = 270.00
$$

$$
r_c = 270.00 \times 1.15 = 310.50
$$

$$
r_c = 310.50 \times 1.25 = 388.125
$$

$$
r_c = 388.125 \times 1.70 = 659.8125
$$

$$
\boxed{r_c \approx ₹ \; 659.81 \; \text{per hour}}
$$

**(ii) Total Labour Cost:**

$$
C_{total} = r_c \times H_{eff} = 659.81 \times 1200
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{total} = ₹ \; 7{,}91{,}775.00 \; (\approx ₹ \; 7.92 \; \text{Lakhs})}
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q-B.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the concept of "productivity factor" in ship repair labour estimation. How does it affect the effective man-hours and total labour cost?

**(b)** A pipe renewal job in the engine room requires **450 standard man-hours**. The crew consists of **2 fitters (₹220/hr each)** and **1 helper (₹130/hr)**. The fringe benefit rate is **28%**, supervision is **18%**, and yard overhead is **55%**. The productivity factor is **0.70**.

Calculate:
  (i) The effective man-hours required.
  (ii) The composite hourly rate of the crew.
  (iii) The total loaded labour cost for the job.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) Productivity Factor — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks)**

The **productivity factor ($\eta$)** is the ratio of effective working output to the theoretical standard output. It accounts for real-world inefficiencies in ship repair such as:

- Confined-space working conditions
- Adverse weather (rain, heat, humidity)
- Waiting time for materials, drawings, cranes, or access scaffolding
- Coordination delays between trades
- Safety briefings and permit-to-work procedures
- Worker fatigue and absenteeism

Mathematical effect:

$$
h_{eff} = \frac{h_{standard}}{\eta}
$$

If $\eta < 1$, the **effective man-hours inflate**, increasing total labour cost. A drop from $\eta = 0.85$ to $\eta = 0.65$ raises labour cost by $\approx 31\%$, severely impacting tender competitiveness. Shipyards therefore invest in better planning, prefabrication, and modular work to push $\eta$ closer to 1.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Effective man-hours:**

$$
H_{eff} = \frac{450}{0.70} = 642.86 \; \text{man-hours}
$$

**(ii) Composite hourly rate of the crew:**

Average hourly rate of the 3-person crew:

$$
r_{avg} = \frac{2(220) + 1(130)}{3} = \frac{440 + 130}{3} = \frac{570}{3} = 190 \; \text{₹/hr}
$$

Apply multipliers:

$$
r_c = 190 \times (1 + 0.28) \times (1 + 0.18) \times (1 + 0.55)
$$

$$
r_c = 190 \times 1.28 = 243.20
$$

$$
r_c = 243.20 \times 1.18 = 286.976
$$

$$
r_c = 286.976 \times 1.55 = 444.8128
$$

$$
\boxed{r_c \approx ₹ \; 444.81 \; \text{per crew-hour}}
$$

**(iii) Total loaded labour cost:**

$$
C_{total} = r_c \times H_{eff} = 444.81 \times 642.86
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{total} \approx ₹ \; 2{,}85{,}948 \; (\approx ₹ \; 2.86 \; \text{Lakhs})}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the productivity factor** — Students often compute cost using *standard* man-hours instead of *effective* man-hours. KTU examiners specifically deduct 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Multiplying percentages instead of using (1 + fraction)** — A common error is computing 35% as 35 instead of 0.35, then adding 200 + 35 = 235. The correct method is $200 \times 1.35 = 270$.
> 3. **Omitting idle time** — Idle time is a *separate* multiplier and must NOT be merged with fringe benefits.
> 4. **Unit mismatch** — Always state the final answer in ₹/hr for rates and ₹ (or Lakhs) for totals.
> 5. **Wrong O/T multiplier** — Use **1.5× for daily OT** and **2.0× for Sunday/holiday OT**. Marks are lost if reversed.
> 6. **Skipping block diagrams in part (a)** — A textual answer without the cost-component block diagram loses up to 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Labour Cost** = (Effective man-hours) × (Composite hourly rate).
- **Composite rate** = Basic × (1 + fringe) × (1 + idle) × (1 + supervision) × (1 + overhead).
- **Effective man-hours** = Standard man-hours ÷ Productivity factor ($\eta$).
- **Fringe benefits** typically include PF (12% of B+DA), ESI (~3.25%), gratuity (~4.16%), bonus, and leave pay.
- **Productivity factor** in ship repair ranges from **0.6 to 0.85** — never assume 1.0.
- **Overtime premium**: **1.5×** for >8 hrs/day, **2.0×** for weekly rest / holidays.
- **Standard benchmark** for steel renewal: **80–120 man-hr/ton**.
- **Crew composition** in ship repair typically: fitters, welders, helpers, machinists, painters, riggers.
- **Idle time fraction**: 10–20% of direct labour.
- **Supervision fraction**: 15–25% of direct labour.
- **Yard overhead fraction**: 50–100% of direct labour.
- **Always convert percentage to decimal** before multiplying: 30% = 0.30, not 30.
- **Block diagrams** showing the cost-stack hierarchy are mandatory for full marks in part-(a) of 14-mark questions.
- **Currency and units** must always be clearly stated in the final answer line.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machinery Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST861 - SHIP SURVEY, ESTIMATION AND REPAIR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machinery Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Machinery Costs** in ship repair estimation refer to the comprehensive financial expenditure incurred for the survey, maintenance, overhaul, repair, and replacement of all mechanical, electrical, and thermodynamic equipment installed aboard a vessel. These costs encompass the **Main Propulsion Plant**, **Auxiliary Machinery**, **Boilers and Steam Plant**, **Purifiers, Pumps, Compressors, Heat Exchangers, Shafting, Propellers**, and associated **Piping and Automation Systems**.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme guidelines under Module 4, machinery costs form the second-largest cost block (after hull costs) in any ship repair specification, typically contributing **30% to 45%** of the total repair bill for a mid-life special survey.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Machinery cost estimation is a core valuation skill tested in Part B (14-mark) questions. Students must demonstrate the ability to break down costs into *material, labor, overheads, and profit* and apply realistic unit rates.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a ship's machinery spaces as the **"human heart and circulatory system"** of the vessel. Just as a hospital bill for heart surgery breaks down into surgeon fees (labor), implant costs (materials), OT charges (overheads), and pharmacy (spares), a ship's machinery repair estimate is decomposed into:

- **Labor Cost** → Wages of fitters, turners, welders, electricians (akin to doctors and nurses).
- **Material Cost** → New crankshafts, bearings, pistons, valves (akin to implants and stents).
- **Spare Parts Cost** → Gaskets, seals, filters, consumables (akin to pharmacy items).
- **Overhead Cost** → Workshop rental, power, tools, supervision (akin to hospital infrastructure).
- **Profit Margin** → Shipyard's margin (akin to hospital's service charge).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Unit of Measurement:** In KTU board problems, machinery costs are commonly quoted **per Brake Horsepower (BHP)** for main engines and **per equipment number** for auxiliary machinery. The standard conversion is **1 BHP ≈ 0.746 kW**.

### 1.3 Classification of Ship Machinery

| Category | Examples | Typical Cost Weightage |
|----------|----------|------------------------|
| Main Propulsion | Main engine, reduction gear, shaft, propeller | 40–50% |
| Auxiliary Power | Diesel generators, emergency generators | 10–15% |
| Boilers & Steam Plant | Auxiliary boiler, exhaust gas boiler | 5–10% |
| Pumps & Compressors | Cooling pumps, lube oil pumps, air compressors | 8–12% |
| Purifiers & Heat Exchangers | FO purifier, LO cooler, FW cooler | 5–8% |
| Deck Machinery | Winches, windlasses, steering gear | 5–10% |
| Electrical & Automation | Switchboards, control systems, cabling | 5–8% |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Machinery Cost — The Five-Layer Model

Every line item in a machinery cost estimate can be decomposed into the following five layers:

1. **Direct Material Cost (MC)** – Cost of new equipment, spares, and replacement parts.
2. **Direct Labor Cost (LC)** – Wages paid to skilled workers (fitter, machinist, welder, rigger).
3. **Direct Expenses (DE)** – Subcontracted jobs, third-party services (e.g., turbocharger servicing by OEM).
4. **Overhead Cost (OC)** – Workshop rent, depreciation of tools, electricity, supervision salaries.
5. **Profit and Contingency (P&C)** – Shipyard markup (typically 10–15%) and contingency reserve (5–10%).

### 2.2 Theoretical Logic Behind Each Component

**Why material cost varies linearly with engine size:**
The mass of major components (crankshaft, bedplate, cylinder liners) scales approximately with BHP. Hence material cost ∝ BHP for like-for-like replacements.

**Why labor cost is non-linear:**
Disassembly and reassembly time does not scale linearly. For example, opening a 2-stroke main engine for piston overhaul may take 8 hours per cylinder, but reassembly including alignment may take 12 hours — so labor is best estimated using **standard man-hours per task** rather than per BHP.

**Why overhead is applied as a percentage:**
Overhead is a **fixed cost pool** distributed across all repair jobs. Standard shipyard practice applies **60% to 100% on direct labor** as overhead loading.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description | Unit |
|---------|-----------|-------------|------|
| Total Machinery Cost (TMC) | $\text{TMC} = \text{MC} + \text{LC} + \text{DE} + \text{OC} + (\text{P\&C})$ | Master summation formula | Currency (USD/INR) |
| Cost per BHP | $\text{C}_{\text{BHP}} = \dfrac{\text{TMC}_{\text{main engine}}}{\text{BHP}_{\text{rated}}}$ | Standard metric for propulsion | Currency/BHP |
| Overhead Loading | $\text{OC} = \alpha \times \text{LC}$ | $\alpha$ = overhead rate (0.6 to 1.0) | Currency |
| Profit Margin | $P = \beta \times (\text{MC} + \text{LC} + \text{OC})$ | $\beta$ = profit rate (0.10 to 0.15) | Currency |
| Contingency | $\text{Cont} = \gamma \times \text{Total Direct Cost}$ | $\gamma$ = 0.05 to 0.10 | Currency |
| Straight Line Depreciation | $D_{\text{SL}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{asset}} - S_{\text{scrap}}}{L_{\text{life}}}$ | Annual depreciation | Currency/year |
| Reducing Balance Depreciation | $D_{\text{RB}} = r \times \text{Book Value}$ | $r$ = depreciation rate (e.g., 0.15) | Currency/year |
| Cost Escalation (Index Method) | $C_{\text{present}} = C_{\text{past}} \times \dfrac{I_{\text{present}}}{I_{\text{past}}}$ | For inflation adjustment | Currency |
| Man-Hour Rate | $R_{\text{mh}} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Wage}}{\text{Productive Hours}}$ | Used to compute labor cost | Currency/hour |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exam problems, when the overhead rate is not specified, **default to 80% on direct labor**. When profit and contingency are not specified, **default to 12.5% combined** on direct cost.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Accurate machinery cost estimation is vital for:

- **Shipowners** – Budgeting for dry-dockings and Special Surveys (typically every 5 years).
- **Shipyards** – Quoting competitive tenders without underbidding.
- **Classification Societies** (IACS members like IRS, LR, DNV, BV) – Validating scope of survey-linked work.
- **Marine Insurance Companies (P&I Clubs)** – Settling average and claim disputes after casualties like main engine breakdowns.
- **Banks and Financiers** – Evaluating the residual value of vessels for loan collateral.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Machinery Cost from First Principles

Starting from the fundamental decomposition of a shipyard's costing methodology:

$$
\text{TMC} = \text{MC} + \text{LC} + \text{DE} + \text{OC} + \text{P\&C}
$$

Substituting the overhead definition $\text{OC} = \alpha \cdot \text{LC}$ and profit definition $P = \beta \cdot (\text{MC} + \text{LC} + \text{OC})$:

$$
\text{TMC} = \text{MC} + \text{LC} + \text{DE} + \alpha \cdot \text{LC} + \beta \cdot (\text{MC} + \text{LC} + \alpha \cdot \text{LC}) + \gamma \cdot (\text{MC} + \text{LC} + \text{DE} + \alpha \cdot \text{LC})
$$

Grouping terms:

$$
\text{TMC} = \text{MC}(1 + \beta + \gamma) + \text{LC}(1 + \alpha + \beta(1+\alpha) + \gamma(1+\alpha)) + \text{DE}(1 + \gamma)
$$

This expanded form is useful when KTU examiners ask for "isolating labor contribution" or "material contribution" to the total cost.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem Statement:** A shipyard estimates the following for overhauling a 12,000 BHP main engine during a Special Survey:

- Material cost (new pistons, bearings, gaskets) = **INR 1,80,00,000**
- Labor cost (estimated 2,400 man-hours at INR 800/hour) = **INR 19,20,000**
- Direct expenses (OEM servicing of turbocharger) = **INR 12,00,000**
- Overhead rate $\alpha$ = **80% on labor**
- Profit rate $\beta$ = **12% on (MC + LC + OC)**
- Contingency $\gamma$ = **8% on direct cost (MC + LC + DE + OC)**

**Step 1 — Compute Overhead:**

$$
\text{OC} = \alpha \times \text{LC} = 0.80 \times 19{,}20{,}000 = 15{,}36{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Direct Cost Pool:**

$$
\text{DC} = \text{MC} + \text{LC} + \text{DE} + \text{OC} = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 + 19{,}20{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}36{,}000 = 2{,}26{,}56{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Profit:**

$$
P = \beta \times (\text{MC} + \text{LC} + \text{OC}) = 0.12 \times (1{,}80{,}00{,}000 + 19{,}20{,}000 + 15{,}36{,}000)
$$

$$
P = 0.12 \times 2{,}14{,}56{,}000 = 25{,}74{,}720
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Contingency:**

$$
\text{Cont} = \gamma \times \text{DC} = 0.08 \times 2{,}26{,}56{,}000 = 18{,}12{,}480
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Total Machinery Cost:**

$$
\text{TMC} = 2{,}26{,}56{,}000 + 25{,}74{,}720 + 18{,}12{,}480 = 2{,}70{,}43{,}200
$$

**Step 6 — Cost per BHP:**

$$
C_{\text{BHP}} = \frac{2{,}70{,}43{,}200}{12{,}000} = 22{,}536.93 \ \text{INR/BHP}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Depreciation-Based Replacement Cost (Mid-Life Buyout)

When estimating whether to **repair or replace** a major machinery item, depreciation analysis is applied.

For straight-line depreciation:

$$
\text{Current Book Value} = C_{\text{asset}} - (L_{\text{elapsed}} \times D_{\text{SL}})
$$

For a main engine with $C_{\text{asset}}$ = INR 8 Crore, life $L$ = 20 years, elapsed $L_e$ = 12 years, scrap $S$ = 0.40 Crore:

$$
D_{\text{SL}} = \frac{8.00 - 0.40}{20} = 0.38 \ \text{Crore/year}
$$

$$
\text{Book Value} = 8.00 - (12 \times 0.38) = 8.00 - 4.56 = 3.44 \ \text{Crore}
$$

If the repair cost exceeds **60% of book value**, **replacement is recommended**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Repair vs Replacement Decision

| Parameter | Repair | Replace |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Cost Threshold | < 50% of replacement cost | > 70% of replacement cost |
| Downtime Impact | Short | Long |
| Class Survey Compliance | May need re-survey | Fresh certification |
| Residual Life Gain | 2–5 years | Full design life |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Machinery Cost Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Survey Report and Class Requirements] --> B[Identify Machinery Items in Scope]
    B --> C[Main Engine and Propulsion]
    B --> D[Auxiliary Machinery]
    B --> E[Boilers and Steam Plant]
    B --> F[Deck Machinery]
    C --> G[Quantity Take-Off: List Each Component]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Unit Rates for Material]
    G --> I[Estimate Man-Hours and Labor Cost]
    G --> J[Identify Subcontracted Services]
    H --> K[Sum Direct Material Cost]
    I --> L[Sum Direct Labor Cost]
    J --> M[Sum Direct Expenses]
    K --> N[Apply Overhead Loading]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Add Profit Margin]
    O --> P[Add Contingency Reserve]
    P --> Q[Final Machinery Cost Estimate]
    Q --> R[End: Report to Owner and Class]
```

### 4.2 Cost Component Hierarchy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TMC[Total Machinery Cost] --> DC[Direct Cost Block]
    TMC --> INDIRECT[Indirect Cost Block]
    DC --> MC[Material Cost]
    DC --> LC[Labor Cost]
    DC --> DE[Direct Expenses]
    INDIRECT --> OC[Overhead Cost]
    INDIRECT --> PC[Profit and Contingency]
    MC --> A1[Spares and Replacements]
    MC --> A2[Consumables and Gaskets]
    LC --> B1[Fitter Wages]
    LC --> B2[Welder Wages]
    LC --> B3[Electrician Wages]
    DE --> C1[OEM Services]
    DE --> C2[Third Party Calibration]
    OC --> D1[Workshop Rent]
    OC --> D2[Tool Depreciation]
    OC --> D3[Supervision Salary]
    PC --> E1[Profit Markup 10 to 15 percent]
    PC --> E2[Contingency 5 to 10 percent]
```

### 4.3 Classification of Survey-Driven Machinery Work

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Ship Survey Type] --> S1[Annual Survey]
    S --> S2[Intermediate Survey]
    S --> S3[Special Survey]
    S --> S4[Tailshaft Survey]
    S1 --> W1[Running Checks and General Inspection]
    S2 --> W2[Internal Inspection of Selected Equipment]
    S3 --> W3[Complete Overhaul of Main and Auxiliary Machinery]
    S4 --> W4[Propeller Shaft Withdrawal and Bearing Inspection]
    W3 --> M1[Main Engine Top Overhaul]
    W3 --> M2[Auxiliary Engine Overhaul]
    W3 --> M3[Boiler Internal Inspection]
    W3 --> M4[Purifier Overhaul]
    W3 --> M5[Pump and Compressor Servicing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The bulk of machinery cost (≈70%) is incurred during **Special Surveys**, which occur every 5 years. This is why shipowners budget annually — accumulating a **dry-docking reserve fund** equivalent to roughly **3–5% of the vessel's value** each year.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define the term "Machinery Cost" as applied in ship repair estimation. List any four categories of machinery that contribute to this cost.*

**Model Answer:**
Machinery cost in ship repair estimation refers to the total financial expenditure incurred for inspection, maintenance, overhaul, repair, and replacement of all mechanical, electrical, and thermodynamic equipment onboard a vessel. The four categories are: (i) Main propulsion machinery (main engine, reduction gear, shaft), (ii) Auxiliary machinery (generators, pumps, compressors), (iii) Boilers and steam plant, (iv) Deck machinery (winches, windlasses, steering gear).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the standard formula for computing the "Cost per BHP" of a main engine. Why is this metric useful for shipowners?*

**Model Answer:**
The cost per BHP is calculated as:

$$
C_{\text{BHP}} = \frac{\text{Total Repair Cost of Main Engine}}{\text{Rated Brake Horsepower}}
$$

This metric allows shipowners to **benchmark** repair costs across different vessels and engine makes, enabling **budget forecasting** and **vendor comparison**. It is industry standard because BHP is the most universally accepted indicator of engine size and capacity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

*(a)* With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **five-layer decomposition** of machinery cost in a ship repair estimate. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A shipyard estimates the following for a Special Survey machinery package of a 15,000 BHP bulk carrier:

- Material cost: INR 2,10,00,000
- Labor cost: INR 24,00,000 (based on 3,000 man-hours)
- Direct expenses (OEM turbocharger service + governor calibration): INR 15,00,000
- Overhead loading: 75% on direct labor
- Profit: 12% on (MC + LC + OC)
- Contingency: 8% on total direct cost

Calculate the **Total Machinery Cost** and the **Cost per BHP**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram and Section 2.1 textual explanation for the five-layer decomposition.*

The five layers are:
1. Direct Material Cost (MC)
2. Direct Labor Cost (LC)
3. Direct Expenses (DE)
4. Overhead Cost (OC)
5. Profit and Contingency (P&C)

Each layer serves a distinct economic purpose, and KTU examiners award marks for correctly naming and describing each.

**(b) Step-by-Step Calculation:**

*Step 1 — Overhead Cost:*

$$
\text{OC} = 0.75 \times 24{,}00{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000
$$

*Step 2 — Direct Cost Pool:*

$$
\text{DC} = 2{,}10{,}00{,}000 + 24{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}67{,}00{,}000
$$

*Step 3 — Profit:*

$$
P = 0.12 \times (2{,}10{,}00{,}000 + 24{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000) = 0.12 \times 2{,}52{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}24{,}000
$$

*Step 4 — Contingency:*

$$
\text{Cont} = 0.08 \times 2{,}67{,}00{,}000 = 21{,}36{,}000
$$

*Step 5 — Total Machinery Cost:*

$$
\text{TMC} = 2{,}67{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}24{,}000 + 21{,}36{,}000 = 3{,}18{,}60{,}000
$$

*Step 6 — Cost per BHP:*

$$
C_{\text{BHP}} = \frac{3{,}18{,}60{,}000}{15{,}000} = 2{,}124 \ \text{INR/BHP}
$$

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)**

*(a)* Discuss the factors that influence **machinery cost variation** between two similar vessels of the same class. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* The following data applies to the auxiliary machinery of a 10,000 DWT chemical tanker during intermediate survey:

| Item | Material Cost (INR) | Labor Hours | Rate (INR/hr) |
|------|---------------------|-------------|---------------|
| Auxiliary engine (3 nos) | 18,00,000 each | 600 each | 750 |
| FO purifiers (2 nos) | 4,50,000 each | 120 each | 750 |
| Pumps (cooling, lube, bilge — 6 nos) | 1,20,000 each | 40 each | 750 |
| Compressors (2 nos) | 3,00,000 each | 80 each | 750 |

Assume overhead = 70% on labor, profit = 10% on (MC + LC + OC), contingency = 7% on direct cost. Calculate total auxiliary machinery cost. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Factors influencing machinery cost variation:**

1. **Vessel age and survey cycle** — Older vessels require more extensive work.
2. **Hours of operation** — Higher running hours accelerate wear.
3. **Maintenance philosophy** — Preventive vs. breakdown-driven.
4. **Fuel quality history** — Poor fuel damages purifiers and fuel pumps.
5. **Trading pattern** — Tropical vs. temperate routes affect cooling systems.
6. **Class society scope** — Different societies have differing requirements.
7. **Geographic location of shipyard** — Labor rates vary by country.
8. **OEM agreements and spares availability** — Affects material pricing.

**(b) Step-by-Step Calculation:**

*Step 1 — Material Cost Total:*

$$
\text{MC} = (3 \times 18{,}00{,}000) + (2 \times 4{,}50{,}000) + (6 \times 1{,}20{,}000) + (2 \times 3{,}00{,}000)
$$

$$
\text{MC} = 54{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}20{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 76{,}20{,}000
$$

*Step 2 — Total Labor Hours and Labor Cost:*

$$
\text{Man-hours} = (3 \times 600) + (2 \times 120) + (6 \times 40) + (2 \times 80) = 1800 + 240 + 240 + 160 = 2{,}440 \ \text{hrs}
$$

$$
\text{LC} = 2{,}440 \times 750 = 18{,}30{,}000
$$

*Step 3 — Overhead Cost:*

$$
\text{OC} = 0.70 \times 18{,}30{,}000 = 12{,}81{,}000
$$

*Step 4 — Direct Cost Pool (no direct expenses given):*

$$
\text{DC} = 76{,}20{,}000 + 18{,}30{,}000 + 12{,}81{,}000 = 1{,}07{,}31{,}000
$$

*Step 5 — Profit:*

$$
P = 0.10 \times (76{,}20{,}000 + 18{,}30{,}000 + 12{,}81{,}000) = 0.10 \times 1{,}07{,}31{,}000 = 10{,}73{,}100
$$

*Step 6 — Contingency:*

$$
\text{Cont} = 0.07 \times 1{,}07{,}31{,}000 = 7{,}51{,}170
$$

*Step 7 — Total Auxiliary Machinery Cost:*

$$
\text{TMC}_{\text{aux}} = 1{,}07{,}31{,}000 + 10{,}73{,}100 + 7{,}51{,}170 = 1{,}25{,}55{,}270
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to multiply unit counts** (e.g., writing 18,00,000 instead of 54,00,000 for 3 engines). Always re-read the "nos" column.
> - **Overhead must be applied to labor only**, NOT to material. Writing $\text{OC} = 0.70 \times \text{MC}$ is a common error costing 2 marks.
> - **Profit base varies** — some problems specify "on direct cost" while others say "on (MC + LC + OC)". Read carefully.
> - **Cost per BHP is mandatory** in main engine problems but NOT in auxiliary machinery questions — know the context.
> - Always **carry forward intermediate values** to the next step instead of rounding prematurely.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Machinery cost = expenditure on mechanical/electrical/thermodynamic equipment repair.
- **Five-Layer Decomposition:** MC + LC + DE + OC + (P&C).
- **Master Formula:** $\text{TMC} = \text{MC} + \text{LC} + \text{DE} + \text{OC} + P + \text{Cont}$.
- **Cost per BHP:** Primary benchmark metric for main engine repairs.
- **Default Overhead Rate (KTU):** 60–100% on direct labor; use 80% if unspecified.
- **Default Profit Rate:** 10–15%; use 12% if unspecified.
- **Default Contingency Rate:** 5–10%; use 8% if unspecified.
- **Repair vs Replace Threshold:** If repair cost > 60% of book value, recommend replacement.
- **Straight Line Depreciation Formula:** $D = (C - S)/L$.
- **Survey Cycle:** Special Surveys drive 70% of machinery cost.
- **Vessel Cost Weightage:** Machinery contributes 30–45% of total repair bill.
- **Unit Conversion:** 1 BHP ≈ 0.746 kW; 1 metric ton = 1,000 kg.
- **Always check:** Whether profit is on (MC+LC+OC) or on full direct cost — the wording in the question decides.
- **Man-hour rate** = Annual wage ÷ Productive hours per year (typically 1,800–2,000 hours).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
